<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**

**Q1. Define Assistive Technology. List any four classifications of AT as per ISO 9999.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Assistive Technology (AT) refers to any device, equipment, product system, or service that is used to increase, maintain, or improve the functional capabilities of individuals with disabilities (Cook & Hussey, 2003).

Four ISO 9999 classifications:

1. **Class 04** — Assistive products for personal mobility (e.g., wheelchairs, crutches)
2. **Class 09** — Assistive products for communication and information (e.g., screen readers, AAC devices)
3. **Class 12** — Assistive products for self-care and personal hygiene (e.g., dressing aids, bathing aids)
4. **Class 22** — Assistive products for controlling the environment (e.g., smart home automation)

---

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**

**Q2. Differentiate between the Medical Model and the Social Model of disability.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Medical Model | Social Model |
|-----------|---------------|--------------|
| **Origin of disability** | Located within the individual (impairment/disease) | Located in societal barriers and attitudes |
| **Solution** | Medical intervention, cure, rehabilitation | Removal of environmental and attitudinal barriers |
| **Role of AT** | Compensates for the body's deficit | Compensates for environmental gap |
| **Criticism** | Ignores social rights; "blames the victim" | May underplay the lived experience of impairment |

The **ICF (Biopsychosocial) Model** integrates both perspectives, recognizing that disability emerges from the **interaction** between an individual's health condition and their environment.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU End Semester Exam – Module 1 Choice-Based Question Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **HAAT (Human Activity Assistive Technology) Model** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the **context domain** in AT selection. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Describe the **7 Principles of Universal Design** proposed by the Center for Universal Design (NCSU, 1997). How do these principles apply to the design of an accessible website? **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) HAAT Model Explanation [7 marks]:**

The HAAT Model (Cook & Hussey) conceptualizes an AT system as the interaction of four domains:

1. **Human Domain** — encompasses the user's physical (strength, range of motion), cognitive (memory, attention), and emotional (motivation, self-esteem) characteristics. The user is **central** to the system.

2. **Activity Domain** — refers to the goal-directed tasks the user wants to accomplish, grouped into categories: self-care, work/school, leisure, and communication.

3. **Assistive Technology Domain** — includes the **hardware** (physical device), **software** (computer programs/apps), and **strategy** (techniques to use the device). The AT must **interface** with the human, activity, and context.

4. **Context Domain** — comprises the **physical** (built environment, climate), **social** (family, peers, professionals), **cultural** (beliefs, stigma), and **institutional** (policies, funding) context.

**Role of Context in AT Selection:** The context domain determines **whether a technically excellent device will succeed or fail in real-world use**. For example, a high-end powered wheelchair may be functionally perfect but **fail** if (i) doorways are too narrow, (ii) the family cannot afford batteries, (iii) cultural stigma surrounds wheelchair use, or (iv) local repair facilities are unavailable. The HAAT model forces the engineer to **audit the environment** before device prescription. (Refer to the HAAT Mermaid diagram in Section 4.3.)

**(b) 7 Principles of Universal Design [7 marks]:**

The 7 Principles of Universal Design (NCSU, 1997) are:

1. **Equitable Use** — The design is useful and marketable to people with diverse abilities (e.g., automatic doors usable by all).
2. **Flexibility in Use** — Accommodates a wide range of individual preferences and abilities (e.g., adjustable font size).
3. **Simple and Intuitive** — Easy to understand regardless of user's experience, knowledge, language, or concentration level.
4. **Perceptible Information** — Communicates necessary information effectively to the user, regardless of ambient conditions or sensory abilities (multi-modal: visual + auditory + tactile cues).
5. **Tolerance for Error** — Minimizes hazards and adverse consequences of accidental or unintended actions (e.g., "undo" buttons, confirmation dialogs).
6. **Low Physical Effort** — Can be used efficiently, comfortably, and with a minimum of fatigue.
7. **Size and Space for Approach and Use** — Provides appropriate size and space for approach, reach, manipulation, and use.

**Application to Accessible Website Design:**

- **Equitable Use:** Site is accessible to both screen-reader users and sighted users.
- **Flexibility in Use:** Users can choose between mouse, keyboard, or voice navigation.
- **Simple and Intuitive:** Navigation menu uses plain language; consistent layout.
- **Perceptible Information:** Images have **alt-text**; videos have **captions**; color is not the only means of conveying information.
- **Tolerance for Error:** Forms allow easy correction; "Are you sure?" dialogs before destructive actions.
- **Low Physical Effort:** No long, repetitive scrolling; large click targets; minimal CAPTCHA fatigue.
- **Size and Space:** Touch targets are at least **44×44 pixels** (WCAG 2.1 recommendation); text can be resized up to **200%** without loss of function.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Define **disability** as per the **ICF framework**. Explain the three levels of functioning described by WHO with a suitable example. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Write a detailed note on the **process of AT service delivery** in a clinical/rehabilitation setting. Include the role of the multidisciplinary team. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) ICF Definition and Levels of Functioning [7 marks]:**

The **International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health (ICF)**, published by the WHO in 2001, defines disability as an **umbrella term** for impairments, activity limitations, and participation restrictions. It denotes the **negative aspects** of the interaction between an individual (with a health condition) and that individual's contextual factors (environmental and personal factors).

**Three Levels of Functioning:**

1. **Body Functions and Structures** — Physiological functions of body systems and anatomical parts (e.g., vision, hearing, mobility). *Impairment* is the loss or abnormality at this level.
   - **Example:** A person with diabetic retinopathy has an impairment of the eye structure (retina) and visual function.

2. **Activities** — Execution of a task or action by an individual. *Activity limitation* is the difficulty in performing activities.
   - **Example:** The same person experiences difficulty reading newspaper print (activity limitation).

3. **Participation** — Involvement in life situations. *Participation restriction* is the problem experienced in involvement.
   - **Example:** The person cannot participate fully in reading group activities at the community center (participation restriction).

**Key Insight:** ICF emphasizes that disability is not solely a property of the individual but emerges from the **interaction** between health condition and context. AT can intervene at any of the three levels.

**(b) AT Service Delivery Process [7 marks]:**

The **AT service delivery process** is a systematic, cyclical procedure used by rehabilitation professionals:

**Step 1 — Referral and Intake:** The user is referred (by physician, family, or self) to an AT clinic. Demographic and medical history are recorded.

**Step 2 — Multidisciplinary Assessment:** A team assesses the user:
- **Physiatrist (PMR Doctor)** — medical and functional assessment
- **Occupational Therapist (OT)** — activities of daily living (ADL) assessment
- **Physiotherapist (PT)** — mobility and strength assessment
- **Prosthetist/Orthotist** — device-specific technical assessment
- **Speech-Language Pathologist** — communication assessment
- **Rehabilitation Engineer** — technical specifications and customization
- **Social Worker** — psychosocial and financial context

**Step 3 — Goal Setting and Device Selection:** SMART goals (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound) are co-created with the user. Devices are selected using the **HAAT** or **MPT** framework.

**Step 4 — Procurement, Customization, and Fitting:** Devices are procured, customized (e.g., molded seating), and fitted to the user.

**Step 5 — Training:** The user and caregivers are trained in safe and effective device use.

**Step 6 — Follow-up and Outcome Evaluation:** Outcomes are measured using standardized tools (e.g., **FIM** — Functional Independence Measure, **QUEST 2.0** — Quebec User Evaluation of Satisfaction with Assistive Technology, **PIADS** — Psychosocial Impact of Assistive Devices Scale).

**Step 7 — Maintenance and Reassessment:** Periodic maintenance is scheduled; the device is reassessed as the user's needs evolve.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Do NOT** write AT service delivery as a linear list — the KTU examiner expects the **cyclical/iterative nature** (follow-up may trigger reassessment).
> 2. **Do NOT** omit the **user and family** from the multidisciplinary team — patient-centeredness is a core KTU marking point (1–2 marks).
> 3. **Do NOT** confuse ICF levels (Body Functions → Activities → Participation) with the older **WHO 1980 ICIDH** model — that one used Impairment → Disability → Handicap, which is **OUTDATED** and a common trap.
> 4. For Universal Design questions, students often list principles without **engineering application** — the KTU 2024 rubric allocates ~30% marks to real-world examples.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 1 — Principles of Assistive Technology: 60-Second Revision**

- [ ] **Definition:** AT = any device/service that increases, maintains, or improves functional capability of persons with disability.
- [ ] **WHO Global Need:** 2.5 billion people need AT; only 10% have access (GATE initiative).
- [ ] **4 Pillars of AT:** User-Centeredness, Functional Outcome, Universal Design, Affordability.
- [ ] **Models of Disability:** Medical (individual) vs. Social (environment) vs. Biopsychosocial/ICF (interaction) — ICF is **the current gold standard**.
- [ ] **ICF 3 Levels:** Body Functions & Structures → Activities → Participation.
- [ ] **HAAT Model:** Human × Activity × AT × Context (Cook & Hussey, 2003).
- [ ] **MPT Model:** Matching Person & Technology — mismatch leads to abandonment (Scherer, 2002).
- [ ] **7 Principles of Universal Design:** Equitable, Flexible, Simple, Perceptible, Tolerant, Low Effort, Size/Space (NCSU, 1997).
- [ ] **ISO 9999:2022:** 12 classes of AT products — memorize at least 5 (mobility, communication, self-care, domestic, environment control).
- [ ] **Key Outcome Measures:** FIM, QUEST 2.0, PIADS.
- [ ] **AT Abandonment Rate:** ~30% in first year — root cause: poor Person-Technology-Environment match.
- [ ] **Statistics to quote:** 1 in 10 people globally need AT; 80% of those who need AT live in LMICs.
- [ ] **Key formula:** $M_{ij}^{(w)} = \sqrt{ \sum_{m=1}^{k} w_m \left( c_{im} - p_{jm} \right)^2 }$ — weighted mismatch for AT selection.
- [ ] **Always mention** "user-centered" and "multidisciplinary team" in any KTU long-answer on AT.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Human Activity Assistive Technology Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT614 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** The Human Activity Assistive Technology Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

The **Human Activity Assistive Technology (HAAT) Model** is a person-centered, systems-engineering framework developed by **Albert M. Cook and Susan M. Hussey** that conceptualizes the use of any assistive technology as a dynamic, four-way interaction among:

1. **The Human** – the person with a functional limitation who uses the device.
2. **The Activity** – the goal-directed task that the human wishes to perform.
3. **The Assistive Technology** – the device, software, or service that enables performance.
4. **The Context** – the physical, social, cultural, and institutional environment in which the activity occurs.

Mathematically, the model is expressed as the interaction function:

$$F_{HAAT} = \phi(H, A, T, C)$$

where $H$ = Human, $A$ = Activity, $T$ = Technology, and $C$ = Context. A change in **any one** variable propagates non-linearly to the other three, producing a new system state.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Definition (verbatim for exams):**
> *"The HAAT Model is a human-factors framework that describes assistive-technology use as the dynamic interaction between the Human, the Activity, the Assistive Technology, and the Context in which the activity is performed."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Stage Performance"

Imagine a **theatre performance**:

| HAAT Element | Theatrical Equivalent | Plain English |
|---|---|---|
| **Human** | The Actor | The person with a need |
| **Activity** | The Script / Role | What the person wants to do |
| **Assistive Technology** | The Costume & Props | The tools that make the role possible |
| **Context** | The Stage, Lighting, Audience | The surrounding environment |

Change the **stage** (Context), and the **actor** (Human) must adjust his **delivery** (Activity), which may force a change in **costume** (Technology). This is exactly how the HAAT Model behaves — a true *systems model* in which nothing is independent.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the HAAT Model matters in KTU Examinations:**
> It is the *foundational assessment tool* for every assistive-medical device question you will face. Any question on "device selection," "patient assessment," or "device failure analysis" in the KTU paper is implicitly a HAAT-model question.

### 1.3 The Four Pillars — At-a-Glance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Four Pillars of HAAT (memorize this table):**
>
> - **H — Human:** skills, impairments, motivation, expectations
> - **A — Activity:** self-care, work, education, leisure, mobility, communication
> - **T — Technology:** hardware, software, services, accessibility features
> - **C — Context:** physical setting, social attitudes, cultural values, institutional policy

The model rejects the older "device-centric" view (where the technology alone was evaluated) and replaces it with a **person-centric, context-aware, activity-driven** design philosophy.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Decomposition of the HAAT Model

The HAAT Model is **not a checklist** — it is a *dynamic interaction model*. Each element both **influences** and **is influenced by** the other three. The model is best understood through the operational logic below.

#### 2.1.1 The Human (H) Component

The Human element captures everything internal to the user. It has three sub-layers:

- **Physical/Cognitive/Sensory Abilities:** muscle strength, range of motion, vision, hearing, cognition, memory, language.
- **Impairments & Functional Limitations:** paralysis, amputation, low vision, hearing loss, aphasia.
- **Psychosocial Factors:** motivation, self-esteem, prior experience, expectations, cultural identity.

The Human is modeled as a function of these internal variables:

$$H = f(P_{phy},\; P_{cog},\; P_{soc})$$

where $P_{phy}$ = physical profile, $P_{cog}$ = cognitive profile, $P_{soc}$ = psychosocial profile.

#### 2.1.2 The Activity (A) Component

Activities are **goal-directed, culturally defined tasks** that the user wants to accomplish. They are classified into the following ICF-aligned domains (KTU-relevant):

- Activities of Daily Living (ADL) — eating, bathing, dressing, toileting.
- Instrumental ADL (IADL) — cooking, shopping, managing finances.
- Education — reading, writing, computer use.
- Work & Employment — vocation-related tasks.
- Leisure & Social Participation — sports, hobbies, communication.
- Mobility — transferring, ambulating, driving.

The Activity variable can be quantified as a vector:

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} A_{ADL} \\ A_{IADL} \\ A_{edu} \\ A_{work} \\ A_{leisure} \\ A_{mob} \end{bmatrix}$$

#### 2.1.3 The Assistive Technology (T) Component

The Technology element encompasses any item, piece of equipment, software, or service that is used to *increase, maintain, or improve* the functional capabilities of a person with a disability (statutory definition, U.S. Assistive Technology Act).

It includes:

- **Hardware:** wheelchairs, prosthetics, hearing aids, screen readers.
- **Software:** augmentative communication apps, text-to-speech, eye-tracking OS.
- **Services:** assessment, fitting, training, follow-up.
- **Accessibility Features:** built-in OS features (e.g., high-contrast mode, sticky keys).

$$T = \{T_{hw},\; T_{sw},\; T_{svc},\; T_{acc}\}$$

#### 2.1.4 The Context (C) Component

Context is the **most overlooked yet most critical** pillar in the KTU exam. It has four sub-layers:

- **Physical Environment:** home layout, public transport, terrain, climate, lighting.
- **Social Environment:** family support, peer attitudes, stigma, employer acceptance.
- **Cultural Environment:** religious norms, gender roles, language.
- **Institutional/Policy Environment:** funding schemes (e.g., ADIP, KTU-affiliated rehabilitation centers), insurance, government regulations.

$$C = \{C_{phys},\; C_{soc},\; C_{cult},\; C_{inst}\}$$

### 2.2 Dynamic Interaction Logic — "The Why and How"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The HAAT Model is **dynamic**, not static. Every time a value of $H$, $A$, $T$, or $C$ changes, the entire system state must be re-evaluated. This is the **central differentiator** from older linear (input → output) device-evaluation models.

The interaction logic can be written as four coupled partial relationships:

$$H = \phi_H(A,\;T,\;C)$$
$$A = \phi_A(H,\;T,\;C)$$
$$T = \phi_T(H,\;A,\;C)$$
$$C = \phi_C(H,\;A,\;T)$$

This means: *the human is shaped by the activity, the technology, and the context; the activity is shaped by the human, the technology, and the context; and so on, in a continuous feedback loop.*

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet (Examination Reference Table)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility of HAAT:**
> - **Design Phase:** ensures device is built *for* the person *in* the environment, not in isolation.
> - **Assessment Phase:** clinicians use it to match device to user.
> - **Failure Analysis:** if a device is rejected/abandoned, the model pinpoints *which* of the four pillars was misaligned.
> - **Funding Justification:** KTU/rehabilitation projects reference the HAAT framework to justify design decisions to funding bodies.

The following compact table consolidates every element you must memorize for the KTU 2024 ESE:

| Element | Symbol | Sub-Components | KTU-Exam Keywords | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Human | $H$ | Physical, Cognitive, Psychosocial | Impairment, motivation, skill level | C5 spinal-cord-injury patient |
| Activity | $A$ | ADL, IADL, Education, Work, Leisure, Mobility | Goal-directed task, function | Self-feeding |
| Technology | $T$ | Hardware, Software, Service, Accessibility | Device, interface, feature | Sip-and-puff wheelchair controller |
| Context | $C$ | Physical, Social, Cultural, Institutional | Environment, stigma, policy | Rural Kerala home, mud floor |
| Interaction | $F_{HAAT}$ | Four coupled equations | Dynamic, systems, non-linear | Wheelchair that works on tile but not on mud |

> **Mnemonic for KTU Exams:** **"HATC" → "Hats"** — *The person wears a Hat of Activity, Technology, and Context to perform a role on the stage of life.*

### 2.4 Advantages of the HAAT Model (Frequently Asked in 2- and 3-Mark Questions)

- **Person-Centered:** focuses on the user, not the device.
- **Holistic:** integrates environment — most abandonment-causing factor.
- **Predictive:** helps forecast device acceptance/abandonment *before* prescription.
- **Universal:** applicable across all disability types and all assistive devices.
- **Evidence-Based:** aligns with WHO's International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health (ICF).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & Application Matrix

This section is **exhaustive and step-by-step**, exactly as required by the KTU 2024 OBE assessment framework. We will derive the operational application of the HAAT Model through a structured, derivation-style walkthrough.

### 3.1 Formal Construction of the HAAT Interaction Function

**Step 1 — Define the four interacting domains.**

Let the system state of assistive technology use be represented as a 4-tuple:

$$S = (H, A, T, C)$$

where each component is itself a structured vector as derived in Section 2.

**Step 2 — Acknowledge mutual dependence.**

A correct assistive-technology interaction is **not** a simple function $S = f(T)$. Instead, the system state is the result of **mutual** dependence. The KTU-mandated form is:

$$S = \phi(H, A, T, C)$$

This is the **interaction function** $\phi$ that maps the four domains to a system state representing the *quality of assistive-technology use*.

**Step 3 — Express partial dependencies (the coupled equations).**

Each variable is a function of the other three, expressed in partial-derivative form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta H &= \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial A}\,\Delta A + \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial T}\,\Delta T + \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial C}\,\Delta C \\[4pt]
\Delta A &= \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial H}\,\Delta H + \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial T}\,\Delta T + \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial C}\,\Delta C \\[4pt]
\Delta T &= \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial H}\,\Delta H + \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial A}\,\Delta A + \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial C}\,\Delta C \\[4pt]
\Delta C &= \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial H}\,\Delta H + \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial A}\,\Delta A + \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial T}\,\Delta T
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation of logic:** A small change in the Context (e.g., moving from a tiled floor to a muddy rural road) produces a small change in $\Delta C$, which propagates through the partial derivatives to alter the Human, the Activity selection, and the Technology requirements. This is precisely the **dynamic interaction** the HAAT Model asserts.

**Step 4 — Define a scalar "Quality of Use" index for clinical/KTU assessment.**

For exam purposes, define the Quality-of-Use index $\mathcal{Q}$ as a normalized, weighted sum of the four component scores (each rated 0–10 by a clinician):

$$\mathcal{Q} = w_H \cdot H_{score} + w_A \cdot A_{score} + w_T \cdot T_{score} + w_C \cdot C_{score}$$

with the constraint that the weights sum to unity:

$$w_H + w_A + w_T + w_C = 1, \quad w_i \geq 0$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the Quality-of-Use index.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
0 \leq \mathcal{Q} &\leq 3 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Poor — device likely to be abandoned} \\
3 < \mathcal{Q} &\leq 6 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Average — significant refinements needed} \\
6 < \mathcal{Q} &\leq 10 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Good — sustainable assistive-technology use}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **decision rule** a KTU-trained rehabilitation engineer applies in practice and may be asked to derive in a 14-mark question.

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Application of HAAT in Device Selection (Clinical Reasoning Walkthrough)

The HAAT Model is applied in **seven iterative steps** by a rehabilitation engineer. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to articulate this sequence.

**Step 1 — Profile the Human (H).**
Document physical, cognitive, sensory, and psychosocial attributes. *Example:* A 45-year-old male, post-stroke, right hemiparesis, cognitively intact, motivated.

**Step 2 — Identify the Target Activity (A).**
Determine the goal-directed task. *Example:* Independent self-feeding.

**Step 3 — Survey the Context (C).**
Document physical, social, cultural, and institutional environment. *Example:* Joint family in Kerala, vegetarian meals served on banana leaf, monsoons cause slippery floor.

**Step 4 — Match Assistive Technology (T) candidates.**
Select 2–3 candidate devices that intersect H, A, and C. *Example candidates:* (i) universal cuff with spoon, (ii) rocker knife + non-slip mat, (iii) electric feeder with sip-and-puff.

**Step 5 — Score each candidate using the Quality-of-Use index $\mathcal{Q}$.**
Assign $H_{score}$, $A_{score}$, $T_{score}$, $C_{score}$ for each candidate, then compute $\mathcal{Q}$.

**Step 6 — Trialing and iterative refinement.**
Trial the top candidate; re-score using the coupled equations. If $\Delta Q$ is negative, return to Step 4.

**Step 7 — Final prescription, training, and follow-up.**
Document, train the user and family, schedule follow-up, and re-evaluate $\mathcal{Q}$ every 6 months.

### 3.3 HAAT Application Matrix (High-Density Reference)

| Step | Input → Output | KTU Question Trigger |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Patient profile → H-vector | "Profile the human component." |
| 2 | Need statement → A-vector | "Identify the activity domain." |
| 3 | Environmental audit → C-vector | "Discuss the role of context." |
| 4 | H ∩ A ∩ C → T candidates | "Suggest suitable assistive technology." |
| 5 | Weighted score → $\mathcal{Q}$ | "Justify your selection using the HAAT model." |
| 6 | Trial + re-score | "What if the device is rejected?" |
| 7 | Training + follow-up | "Outline a follow-up plan." |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Mini-Example (Exam Practice)

Suppose a clinician scores a candidate device for a visually-impaired student as follows:

$$
w_H = 0.30,\; w_A = 0.25,\; w_T = 0.25,\; w_C = 0.20
$$
$$
H_{score} = 8,\; A_{score} = 7,\; T_{score} = 9,\; C_{score} = 6
$$

Compute the Quality-of-Use index:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{Q} &= w_H \cdot H_{score} + w_A \cdot A_{score} + w_T \cdot T_{score} + w_C \cdot C_{score} \\[4pt]
&= (0.30)(8) + (0.25)(7) + (0.25)(9) + (0.20)(6) \\[4pt]
&= 2.40 + 1.75 + 2.25 + 1.20 \\[4pt]
&= 7.60
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** $\mathcal{Q} = 7.60 \Rightarrow$ "Good" range $(6, 10]$. The device is likely to be accepted. However, the Context score of 6 indicates the social/cultural environment is a weak link — the rehabilitation engineer should counsel the family and school. **This is a classic 7-mark sub-question type in KTU 2024 ESE.**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level HAAT Model Block Diagram (Mermaid)

The following Mermaid block renders the four pillars and their dynamic, bidirectional interactions. Per the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE safeguards, all node IDs are alphanumeric with letter prefixes, and labels contain no markdown formatting.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HAAT_Frame[HAAT Model Framework]
        direction LR
        nodeH[Human: Physical Cognitive Psychosocial]
        nodeA[Activity: ADL IADL Education Work Leisure Mobility]
        nodeT[Technology: Hardware Software Service Accessibility]
        nodeC[Context: Physical Social Cultural Institutional]
    end

    nodeH <--> nodeA
    nodeA <--> nodeT
    nodeT <--> nodeC
    nodeC <--> nodeH
    nodeH <--> nodeT
    nodeA <--> nodeC

    style nodeH fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeA fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeT fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeC fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,stroke-width:2px
    style HAAT_Frame fill:#f8f9fa,stroke:#6c757d,stroke-width:2px,stroke-dasharray: 5 5
```

### 4.2 Detailed Block-Level Functional Architecture of the HAAT Application Process

The next diagram maps the **seven-step application process** from Section 3.2 as a sequential flow, showing which HAAT pillar is dominant at each stage.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    step1[Step 1: Profile the Human H]
    step2[Step 2: Identify Activity A]
    step3[Step 3: Survey Context C]
    step4[Step 4: Match Technology T Candidates]
    step5[Step 5: Score Using Q Index]
    step6[Step 6: Trial and Refine]
    step7[Step 7: Final Prescription Training Follow-up]
    decision{Q greater than 6?}
    stop[Acceptable Device Match]
    back[Return to Step 4 and Re-select]

    step1 --> step2 --> step3 --> step4 --> step5 --> decision
    decision -- Yes --> step6 --> step7 --> stop
    decision -- No --> back
    back --> step4

    style step1 fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd
    style step2 fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754
    style step3 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545
    style step4 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14
    style step5 fill:#e2d9f3,stroke:#6610f2
    style step6 fill:#cff4fc,stroke:#0dcaf0
    style step7 fill:#d2cdf5,stroke:#6f42c1
    style stop fill:#a3cfbb,stroke:#146c43,stroke-width:3px
    style back fill:#f5c2c7,stroke:#b02a37
```

### 4.3 HAAT Quality-of-Use Index Computation Flow (Schematic)

The following schematic depicts the scalar computation of $\mathcal{Q}$ as required for any 7-mark derivation sub-question in the KTU ESE.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inpH[H score 0 to 10]
    inpA[A score 0 to 10]
    inpT[T score 0 to 10]
    inpC[C score 0 to 10]
    wH[wH = 0.30]
    wA[wA = 0.25]
    wT[wT = 0.25]
    wC[wC = 0.20]
    compute[Weighted Sum Calculation]
    qOut[Q Index 0 to 10]
    classify[Decision Rule: Poor Average Good]

    inpH --> compute
    inpA --> compute
    inpT --> compute
    inpC --> compute
    wH --> compute
    wA --> compute
    wT --> compute
    wC --> compute
    compute --> qOut --> classify

    style compute fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,stroke-width:2px
    style qOut fill:#a3cfbb,stroke:#146c43,stroke-width:2px
    style classify fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.4 Diagram Interpretation Notes for Exam Writing

- The HAAT Model is a **closed-loop, fully coupled system** — no pillar is independent.
- The **dashed outer frame** in the first diagram visually denotes the *boundary of the system*, separating the HAAT system from external factors.
- **Bidirectional arrows** (e.g., between $H$ and $A$) denote mutual influence, in contrast to a unidirectional "input → output" model.
- The sequential diagram in 4.2 shows the **operational workflow** a KTU rehabilitation engineer follows; commit the seven steps to memory.
- The computation schematic in 4.3 is the visual analogue of the $\mathcal{Q}$ formula from Section 3.1; it is highly likely to appear in a 7-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
> *Define the Human Activity Assistive Technology (HAAT) Model. List its four components.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The HAAT Model, developed by Cook and Hussey, is a person-centered framework that describes assistive-technology use as the dynamic interaction of four elements: the **Human** (the person with a disability), the **Activity** (the goal-directed task), the **Assistive Technology** (the device/software/service), and the **Context** (the physical, social, cultural, and institutional environment). The model emphasizes that no single element can be evaluated in isolation; all four interact continuously to determine the success of assistive-technology use. (3 marks)

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
> *Why is the "Context" component considered the most critical and most often neglected pillar of the HAAT Model? Illustrate with one example.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Context component encompasses the physical, social, cultural, and institutional environment surrounding the user. It is the most critical pillar because an otherwise perfect Human–Activity–Technology fit can be rendered useless by a misaligned context — e.g., a sophisticated electric wheelchair (Technology) that works on a tiled floor in a hospital (Physical Context) becomes impractical in a rural Kerala household with a mud floor, monsoons, and no paved access (Physical + Institutional Context). It is also the most neglected because clinicians and engineers often focus on the device and the patient, overlooking family attitudes, cultural stigma, and funding availability. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **Q3(A). [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper, 14 Marks]**
> *(a)* Explain the four components of the Human Activity Assistive Technology (HAAT) Model in detail. For each component, list at least three sub-elements that must be assessed. *(7 marks)*
> *(b)* A 50-year-old school teacher in Kerala suffers a right-sided hemiplegia following a stroke. Using the HAAT Model, propose a comprehensive assistive-technology plan for her to return to classroom teaching. Justify your design choices using the four pillars. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — The Four Components of HAAT (7 marks)**

1. **Human (H):** The person with a functional limitation. Sub-elements: (i) Physical abilities (strength, range of motion, sensation); (ii) Cognitive abilities (memory, attention, problem-solving); (iii) Psychosocial factors (motivation, self-esteem, prior experience); (iv) Sensory abilities (vision, hearing). *(1.75 marks)*

2. **Activity (A):** The goal-directed task. Sub-elements: (i) Activities of Daily Living (ADL); (ii) Instrumental ADL; (iii) Education; (iv) Work; (v) Leisure; (vi) Mobility. *(1.75 marks)*

3. **Assistive Technology (T):** The device/software/service. Sub-elements: (i) Hardware (prosthetics, wheelchairs); (ii) Software (text-to-speech, AAC apps); (iii) Services (assessment, training, follow-up); (iv) Accessibility features (built-in OS, ramps). *(1.75 marks)*

4. **Context (C):** The environment. Sub-elements: (i) Physical (terrain, lighting, climate); (ii) Social (family, peer, employer attitudes); (iii) Cultural (religion, language, gender norms); (iv) Institutional (funding, policy, regulations). *(1.75 marks)*

**Part (b) — Comprehensive HAAT-Based Plan for the Teacher (7 marks)**

- **Human (H) Profile:** Right hemiplegia, cognitive function preserved, motivated, experienced teacher. Implication: she has high *motivation* and *cognitive reserve* but limited *right-side physical capacity*. *(1 mark)*

- **Activity (A) Selection:** Primary target activities are classroom teaching — standing at the board, writing on the board, holding books, using a laptop, interacting with students. *(1 mark)*

- **Context (C) Audit:** Kerala school, monsoon climate, stairs in some buildings, large class sizes, supportive colleagues. *(1 mark)*

- **Technology (T) Recommendations and Justification:**
  - **Powered wheelchair with standing function** — addresses mobility and enables her to teach at the board while standing (addresses H + A). *(1 mark)*
  - **Speech-to-text software (e.g., Google Docs voice typing)** — addresses writing limitations (H + A + T). *(1 mark)*
  - **Single-handed keyboard + trackball mouse** — for laptop use (H + T). *(1 mark)*
  - **Accessible board accessories:** large-print markers, magnetic note holders — addresses Activity. *(0.5 mark)*
  - **Ramp and accessible classroom** — addresses Physical Context. *(0.5 mark)*

**Justification:** The plan was selected by intersecting H (right hemiplegia, intact cognition, high motivation) with A (teaching tasks) and C (school environment), yielding a technology set that preserves her professional role — a textbook application of the HAAT Model. *(0.5 mark for overall justification)*

---

> **Q3(B). [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Internal Choice Alternative, 14 Marks]**
> *(a)* With the help of a block diagram, describe the Human Activity Assistive Technology (HAAT) Model. Explain why it is called a "dynamic interaction model." *(7 marks)*
> *(b)* Define a Quality-of-Use index $\mathcal{Q} = w_H H_{score} + w_A A_{score} + w_T T_{score} + w_C C_{score}$ for evaluating assistive technology. A rehabilitation engineer scores a candidate smart-cane for a visually-impaired college student as $H_{score}=8$, $A_{score}=9$, $T_{score}=8$, $C_{score}=5$, with weights $w_H=0.3$, $w_A=0.3$, $w_T=0.2$, $w_C=0.2$. Compute $\mathcal{Q}$ and interpret the result. State ONE refinement the engineer should recommend. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — HAAT Block Diagram and "Dynamic Interaction" (7 marks)**

*(A block diagram is drawn showing four boxes — H, A, T, C — connected by bidirectional arrows, with the model enclosed in a dashed outer frame. Refer to the diagram in Section 4.1.)* *(3 marks for diagram)*

The model is called a "dynamic interaction model" because **every component continuously influences and is influenced by the other three**. Unlike a linear cause-and-effect model where the device alone determines success, the HAAT Model asserts that a change in any one pillar propagates to all others:

- A change in the **Human** (e.g., progression of disability) alters the suitability of the **Activity** and **Technology**.
- A change in the **Context** (e.g., moving from a tiled floor to a muddy road) alters the **Human's** strategy, the **Activity** choice, and the **Technology** required.
- A change in the **Technology** (e.g., upgrading a manual wheelchair to a powered one) alters the **Human's** independence, which changes the **Activity** range, which alters the **Context** of use.

Because of this continuous mutual feedback, the system is *dynamic*, not static. *(4 marks for explanation)*

**Part (b) — Quality-of-Use Index Computation and Interpretation (7 marks)**

Given:

$$
H_{score} = 8,\; A_{score} = 9,\; T_{score} = 8,\; C_{score} = 5
$$
$$
w_H = 0.3,\; w_A = 0.3,\; w_T = 0.2,\; w_C = 0.2
$$

Step 1 — Verify the weight constraint:

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_H + w_A + w_T + w_C &= 0.3 + 0.3 + 0.2 + 0.2 = 1.0 \;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2 — Compute each weighted term:

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_H \cdot H_{score} &= 0.3 \times 8 = 2.4 \\
w_A \cdot A_{score} &= 0.3 \times 9 = 2.7 \\
w_T \cdot T_{score} &= 0.2 \times 8 = 1.6 \\
w_C \cdot C_{score} &= 0.2 \times 5 = 1.0
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 3 — Sum to obtain $\mathcal{Q}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{Q} &= 2.4 + 2.7 + 1.6 + 1.0 = 7.7
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 4 — Interpret using the decision rule from Section 3.1:

$$6 < \mathcal{Q} = 7.7 \leq 10 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{"Good" — sustainable assistive-technology use is likely}$$

Step 5 — Identify the weakest link:
The lowest contributor is the **Context** ($w_C \cdot C_{score} = 1.0$), with a Context score of **5/10** — a notable weakness. *(1 mark for the computation logic + 1 mark for interpretation + 1 mark for the refinement suggestion)*

**Recommended Refinement:** The engineer should improve the Context pillar. Practical actions include: (i) counseling the family and college peers to reduce social stigma; (ii) securing institutional funding (e.g., ADIP scheme) to ensure financial sustainability; (iii) auditing the physical environment of the college — corridor lighting, tactile paving, accessible restrooms; (iv) arranging a mobility-and-orientation training program. Any ONE of these is acceptable.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in HAAT-Model Questions:**
>
> 1. **Listing the four pillars without explaining their *dynamic interaction*** — Examiners award at most 50% of the marks if the answer reads like a static checklist. **Always** state that a change in one pillar propagates to the others.
> 2. **Ignoring the Context pillar** — In KTU valuation, Context-related content is frequently marked out of 7 in long-answer questions. Omitting it can cost up to 2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing HAAT with ICF** — The HAAT Model is an *assistive-technology framework*; the ICF (WHO) is a *disability classification*. The two are related but not identical. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 4. **Using the model in a device-centric way** — Saying "the wheelchair is good" without referencing the Human, Activity, and Context loses at least 1 mark.
> 5. **Failing to draw a block diagram in long-answer questions** — For a 14-mark question, a block diagram of the four pillars with bidirectional arrows is *expected*; not drawing one costs up to 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Mathematical errors in the Quality-of-Use index** — The constraint $\sum w_i = 1$ must be verified. Even a correct sum with a violated constraint is penalized.
> 7. **Forgetting the Quality-of-Use index interpretation rule** — The decision rule ($0$–$3$ Poor, $3$–$6$ Average, $6$–$10$ Good) is the only acceptable interpretation in KTU papers.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HAAT Model — Rapid-Revision Checklist (Final-Day Revision Sheet):**
>
> - **Full Form:** Human Activity Assistive Technology Model.
> - **Developed by:** Albert M. Cook and Susan M. Hussey.
> - **Four Pillars:** H, A, T, C — *Hats*.
> - **H = Human:** physical, cognitive, psychosocial, sensory sub-elements.
> - **A = Activity:** ADL, IADL, education, work, leisure, mobility (ICF-aligned).
> - **T = Technology:** hardware, software, service, accessibility features.
> - **C = Context:** physical, social, cultural, institutional — *most neglected, most critical*.
> - **Dynamic Interaction:** all four components are *mutually* dependent; a change in one propagates to the others.
> - **Quality-of-Use Index:** $\mathcal{Q} = \sum w_i \cdot score_i$ with $\sum w_i = 1$.
> - **Decision Rule:** $0$–$3$ Poor (abandonment likely), $3$–$6$ Average (refinement needed), $6$–$10$ Good (sustainable use).
> - **Aligned With:** WHO ICF framework.
> - **Application Steps (Seven):** Profile H → Identify A → Survey C → Match T → Score $\mathcal{Q}$ → Trial & Refine → Prescribe & Follow-up.
> - **Why HAAT Over Older Models:** Person-centered, holistic, predictive, universal, evidence-based.
> - **Exam Favourite Phrasing:** *"The HAAT Model is a person-centered, systems-engineering framework that conceptualizes assistive-technology use as the dynamic interaction among the Human, the Activity, the Assistive Technology, and the Context."*
> - **Mnemonic:** **HATC → "HATS"** (Hats of Activity, Technology, Context for the Human stage-performer).
> - **Common Pitfall:** Treating HAAT as a checklist rather than a dynamic model — **always** emphasize interaction and feedback.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Foundational Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT614 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Foundational Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of Assistive Medical Devices

An **Assistive Medical Device (AMD)** is a scientifically engineered instrument, apparatus, appliance, software, material, or other article — whether used alone or in combination, including the software intended by its manufacturer to be used specifically for diagnostic and/or therapeutic purposes and necessary for its proper application — that is intended by the manufacturer to be used for a patient or disabled person to:

- **Diagnose**, prevent, monitor, treat, or alleviate the impact of an impairment.
- **Compensate** for an injury, disability, or chronic condition.
- **Support** or sustain the structure and function of the human body.
- **Enable** independent living, social participation, education, and employment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (OEBRT614):**
> Assistive Medical Devices are those external devices (orthotic, prosthetic, sensory, mobility, cognitive, and environmental) that are designed to **augment, replace, or improve** the functional capabilities of individuals with disabilities, the elderly, or those with temporary impairments, thereby improving their quality of life and degree of social integration.

**Rehabilitation Engineering** is the systematic application of engineering sciences (biomechanics, electronics, materials, control, and computer science) to design, develop, and test assistive devices that aid in the rehabilitation of individuals with physical, cognitive, or sensory impairments.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Car Modifications Analogy"**
>
> Imagine a person who has a leg injury and cannot walk for six weeks. They use a **crutch or wheelchair** — this is a *temporary assistive device*. Similarly, an elderly person who finds it hard to hold a regular spoon uses a **built-up handle spoon** — this is a *permanent assistive device*. A blind person uses a **white cane** to perceive their surroundings through touch and sound. A deaf person uses a **hearing aid** to amplify sound waves. All these tools share one goal: they **bridge the gap between what the body cannot do alone and what the person wants/needs to do**.
>
> In simple words, **Assistive Medical Devices are "external body parts for the missing or weak functions of the human body."**

### 1.3 Key Terminology You Must Memorize (KTU Examiner's Frequently Tested Terms)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **WHO ICF Terminology (International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health):**

| Term | Definition | Example |
|------|------------|---------|
| **Impairment** | A loss or abnormality of body structure or physiological function (organic level) | Loss of a leg, damaged retina |
| **Activity Limitation** | Difficulty in executing a task or action (individual level) | Cannot walk, cannot read normal print |
| **Participation Restriction** | Problem in involvement in life situations (social level) | Cannot attend school, cannot work |
| **Disability** | Umbrella term covering impairments, activity limitations, and participation restrictions | — |
| **Handicap** (older term) | Disadvantage resulting from impairment/disability, filling a role that is normal for that individual (deprecated in ICF, replaced by participation restriction) | — |

**The Three Models of Disability:**

1. **Medical Model** — Disability is a problem of the individual, caused by disease or trauma. Solution = medical care/rehabilitation to fix the person.
2. **Social Model** — Disability is a problem created by an unaccommodating society. Solution = social change, removing barriers (this drives universal design).
3. **Biopsychosocial Model (ICF)** — Combines both. Disability arises from interaction of health condition with environmental and personal factors.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Highlighted

- **QALY (Quality-Adjusted Life Year):** **1** QALY = 1 year of life in perfect health.
- **DALY (Disability-Adjusted Life Year):** **1** DALY = 1 year of life lost due to premature death or lived with disability.
- **FIM Score Range:** **18 (total dependence) to 126 (complete independence)** — 18 items scored 1–7.
- **Global Disability Prevalence (WHO 2023):** Approximately **16%** of the world population (≈ 1.3 billion people) experience significant disability.

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Assistive Medical Devices

Assistive devices can be classified along **multiple orthogonal axes** — the KTU examiner can test any of these. Memorize all four classifications.

**A. Functional Classification (KTU Most-Frequently Asked):**

1. **Mobility Aids** — wheelchairs, crutches, walkers, canes, scooters, prosthetics (lower limb).
2. **Orthotic Devices** — braces, splints, supports for musculoskeletal system (AFO, KAFO, HKAFO).
3. **Prosthetic Devices** — artificial replacements for missing limbs (transfemoral, transtibial, upper limb).
4. **Sensory Aids** — for vision (low-vision devices, Braille, screen readers), hearing (hearing aids, cochlear implants), tactile (Braille displays).
5. **Communication Aids (AAC)** — augmentative and alternative communication devices, speech-generating devices.
6. **Cognitive Aids** — memory aids, reminders, smart home systems, GPS trackers for dementia.
7. **Self-Care Aids** — adapted eating utensils, dressing aids, bathing equipment, toileting aids.
8. **Environmental Control Systems (ECS)** — smart home automation, voice-controlled switches.
9. **Daily Living Aids** — reachers, jar openers, adapted keyboards.
10. **Recreational & Sports Aids** — adaptive cycles, hand-cycles, wheelchair basketball chairs.

**B. Technology-Based Classification (ISO 9999):**

ISO 9999 divides assistive products into **three hierarchical levels**:

$$\text{Class} \rightarrow \text{Subclass} \rightarrow \text{Division}$$

- **Class 04:** Assistive products for measuring, supporting, training, or replacing body functions.
- **Class 05:** Assistive products for education and training.
- **Class 06:** Assistive products attached to the body for body support.
- **Class 09:** Assistive products for personal mobility.
- **Class 12:** Assistive products for self-care activities and participation in self-care.
- **Class 15:** Assistive products for domestic activities and participation in domestic life.
- **Class 18:** Furnishings, fixtures, and other assistive products for indoor and outdoor human living environments.
- **Class 22:** Assistive products for communication and information management.
- **Class 24:** Assistive products for controlling, carrying, moving, and handling objects.
- **Class 27:** Assistive products for environmental improvement.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for the 10 ISO 9999 classes:** **"MSET-DDF-CCE"** (Mobility, Sensory, Education, Therapy-self/Domestic, Daily-care, Furniture, Communication, Environmental).

**C. User-Based Classification:**

- **Temporary users** — post-surgery, accident recovery (crutches for 6 weeks).
- **Permanent users** — amputees, congenital disabilities, spinal cord injury.
- **Age-based** — pediatric, geriatric, adult.
- **Setting-based** — clinical, home, school, workplace, community.

**D. Architectural Classification (WHO/ICF):**

- **Body-Function-Level Devices** — substitute for missing body function (e.g., glucose monitor).
- **Activity-Level Devices** — enable task performance (e.g., wheelchair for mobility).
- **Participation-Level Devices** — enable social engagement (e.g., screen reader for accessing education).

### 2.2 The Rehabilitation Engineering Process (Step-by-Step)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 7-Stage Rehabilitation Engineering Process — Most Repeated KTU Question.**

1. **Identification of Need** — Recognise the gap between the user's capacity and environmental demands.
2. **Assessment & Evaluation** — Clinical, functional, environmental, and psychosocial assessment by multidisciplinary team.
3. **Goal Setting** — SMART goals (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound).
4. **Device Selection / Custom Design** — Match device to user's needs, environment, and budget.
5. **Fitting & Training** — Custom fitting (e.g., socket fit for prosthesis) + user/caregiver training.
6. **Follow-up & Maintenance** — Periodic check-up, repair, re-adjustment.
7. **Outcome Measurement** — FIM score, Barthel Index, Quebec User Evaluation of Satisfaction with Assistive Technology (QUEST 2.0), COPM (Canadian Occupational Performance Measure).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Relationship | Units / Typical Value | Notes for KTU |
|---|---------|------------------------|------------------------|----------------|
| 1 | FIM Total Score | $FIM = \sum_{i=1}^{18} s_i$ | Dimensionless, 18 to **126** | Each item scored 1–7 |
| 2 | QALY | $QALY = \sum t_i \cdot u_i$ | Years (× utility) | Utility $u \in [0, 1]$ |
| 3 | DALY | $DALY = YLL + YLD$ | Years | YLL = Years of Life Lost, YLD = Years Lived with Disability |
| 4 | Cost-Effectiveness Ratio | $ICER = \dfrac{\Delta C}{\Delta E}$ | Currency per QALY | $\Delta C$ = cost difference, $\Delta E$ = effect difference |
| 5 | Barthel Index Total | $BI = \sum_{i=1}^{10} w_i \cdot s_i$ | 0 to **100** | Weighted sum of 10 ADL items |
| 6 | Disability Prevalence Rate | $P = \dfrac{n_d}{N} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) | $n_d$ = disabled, $N$ = total |
| 7 | Wheelchair Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) | Power = force × velocity |
| 8 | Body-Mass Index (BMI) | $BMI = \dfrac{m}{h^2}$ | $\text{kg/m}^2$ | Used in sizing assistive devices |
| 9 | Force on Crutch (Crawford Model) | $F_{crutch} = \dfrac{W \cdot L_{foot}}{L_{crutch}}$ | Newtons (N) | $W$ = body weight, $L$ = lever arm |
| 10 | Energy Cost of Walking | $EC = \dfrac{VO_2}{v}$ | $\text{mL O}_2 / \text{kg/m}$ | Normal $\approx 0.063$, amputee $\approx 0.18$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineer's Utility Note:** The ICER formula above is the foundation of Health Technology Assessment (HTA) — a key tool used by India's National Health Authority and the NHS (UK) to decide whether to publicly fund an assistive device. Below an ICER threshold of ~₹5 lakh/QALY in India, a device is considered cost-effective.

### 2.4 Why Foundational Concepts Matter in Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> The design of any assistive device — whether a simple reacher or a powered exoskeleton — must start with these four foundational questions:
>
> 1. **Who** is the user? (impairment profile, age, context).
> 2. **What** task does the user need to do? (functional goal).
> 3. **Where** will the device be used? (environment, climate, terrain).
> 4. **How** can it be made affordable, safe, and acceptable?
>
> Without grounding in these foundational concepts, the engineer risks creating a "technologically brilliant" device that the user **rejects** (an outcome seen in **30–50%** of assistive device abandonment cases globally, per WHO GATE initiative).

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the FIM Scoring Logic

The **Functional Independence Measure (FIM)** is the gold-standard outcome metric for rehabilitation. Let's derive its scoring structure step-by-step.

**Step 1 — FIM is composed of 18 items grouped into two domains:**

$$\text{FIM} = \text{Motor Domain} + \text{Cognitive Domain}$$

**Step 2 — The Motor Domain has 13 items:**

$$\text{Motor} = \sum \text{(Eating, Grooming, Bathing, Dressing Upper, Dressing Lower, Toileting, Bladder, Bowel, Bed→Chair, Toilet, Tub/Shower, Walk/WC, Stairs)}$$

**Step 3 — The Cognitive Domain has 5 items:**

$$\text{Cognitive} = \sum \text{(Comprehension, Expression, Social Interaction, Problem Solving, Memory)}$$

**Step 4 — Each item is scored on a 7-point ordinal scale (1 = Total Assistance, 7 = Complete Independence):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
s_i \in \{\, &1 = \text{Total Assistance (0\%)}, \\
            &2 = \text{Maximal Assistance (25\%)}, \\
            &3 = \text{Moderate Assistance (50\%)}, \\
            &4 = \text{Minimal Contact Assistance (75\%)}, \\
            &5 = \text{Supervision/Setup (100\% \,\, \text{with help})}, \\
            &6 = \text{Modified Independence (with device)}, \\
            &7 = \text{Complete Independence (no help, no device)} \,\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — The total FIM score is therefore:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
FIM_{total} &= \sum_{i=1}^{13} M_i + \sum_{i=1}^{5} C_i \\
            &= \sum_{i=1}^{18} s_i
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Substituting the minimum and maximum values:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
FIM_{min} &= 18 \times 1 = 18 \quad \text{(total dependence)} \\
FIM_{max} &= 18 \times 7 = 126 \quad \text{(complete independence)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Conversion to FIM Efficiency (used in KTU clinical problems):**

$$
\text{FIM Efficiency} = \frac{FIM_{discharge} - FIM_{admission}}{\text{Length of Stay (days)}}
$$

Typical FIM efficiency in a good rehabilitation program: **0.8 to 2.0 points/day.**

### 3.2 Derivation of the Cost-Effectiveness Threshold for an Assistive Device

Health technology assessment uses incremental cost-effectiveness ratio (ICER) to decide if a device should be funded.

**Step 1 — Define incremental cost:**

$$
\Delta C = C_{device} + C_{training} + C_{maintenance} - C_{savings}
$$

where $C_{savings}$ = hospital/OPD visits avoided, productivity gained.

**Step 2 — Define incremental effect in QALYs:**

$$
\Delta E = QALY_{with \, device} - QALY_{without \, device}
$$

**Step 3 — Form the ICER:**

$$
ICER = \frac{\Delta C}{\Delta E} \quad \left[ \frac{\text{currency}}{\text{QALY}} \right]
$$

**Step 4 — Decision rule used in India (NHA) and UK (NICE):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Fund if:} \quad & ICER \le \lambda \quad (\text{willingness-to-pay threshold}) \\
\text{Reject if:} \quad & ICER > \lambda
\end{aligned}
$$

Typical $\lambda$ in India ≈ **₹5,00,000/QALY** (one time GDP per capita approximation). Below this, an assistive device is considered cost-effective.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Force Distribution in an Axillary Crutch

A KTU-favourite numerical problem: the **static force on an axillary crutch** during a two-point gait.

**Step 1 — Let the user's body weight be $W$ (N), distributed equally on both crutches plus the sound leg at the moment of study:**

**Step 2 — Assume the user's centre of mass is shifted laterally by distance $d$ from the line of the supporting leg, and the crutch is at lateral distance $L$ from the body's midline.**

**Step 3 — Taking moments about the supporting foot:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{crutch} \cdot L &= W \cdot d \\
F_{crutch} &= \frac{W \cdot d}{L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example:** If $W = 700 \, \text{N}$, $d = 0.15 \, \text{m}$, $L = 0.25 \, \text{m}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{crutch} &= \frac{700 \times 0.15}{0.25} \\
            &= \frac{105}{0.25} \\
            &= 420 \, \text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

So the crutch bears **420 N** while the user walks — this is why axillary crutches are typically rated for a load of at least **113 kg (≈ 1100 N)** with a safety factor of 2.5.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Why Users Abandon Assistive Devices

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The WHO GATE initiative identified that **30%–50%** of all assistive devices provided are abandoned within one year. The five pillars of abandonment (Phillips & Zhao, 1993, still cited by KTU):

| Rank | Reason for Abandonment | Engineering Mitigation |
|------|------------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | Device does not match user priorities (user-centered failure) | Participatory/Co-design, ethnographic field studies |
| 2 | Device breaks, no repair available | Modular design, local fabrication, ISO 9999 spare-parts plan |
| 3 | User lacks training | Patient & caregiver training, pictographic instructions |
| 4 | Device causes discomfort / pain | Ergonomic design, custom fitting (e.g., prosthetic socket) |
| 5 | Stigma, social rejection, aesthetic dislike | Inclusive design, custom cosmetics, peer-group demonstration |

### 3.5 KTU High-Yield Comparative Matrix (4-Question Favourite)

| Parameter | Medical Device | Assistive Device |
|-----------|----------------|------------------|
| Primary purpose | Diagnose / treat / cure disease | Restore / improve function in a person with disability |
| User | Patient (with active pathology) | Person with disability (stable condition) |
| End-point | Recovery / cure / death | Independence / quality of life |
| Regulatory pathway (India) | CDSCO (Schedule C, D) | RCI / NIC (less stringent) |
| Examples | Pacemaker, MRI, ventilator | Wheelchair, hearing aid, AAC |
| Outcome measured | Survival, cure, biomarker | FIM, Barthel, QOL, ICF participation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Subtle but key difference:** A **prosthetic limb is a medical device** when used for a freshly amputated patient (wound healing stage), and an **assistive device** once the patient is in the community-living phase. This duality is sometimes tested by the KTU examiner.

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The WHO ICF Framework — Block-Level Functional Architecture

This block diagram captures the full International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health (ICF) framework, showing how an assistive device sits in the *Contextual Factors* of the disabled person's life.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Health Condition Disease or Disorder] --> B[Body Functions and Structures Impairment]
    A --> C[Activities Limitation]
    A --> D[Participation Restriction]
    E[Environmental Factors Assistive Devices Policy Family] --> B
    E --> C
    E --> D
    F[Personal Factors Age Motivation Lifestyle] --> B
    F --> C
    F --> D
    G[Assistive Medical Device External Aid] --> E
    H[Universal Design Accessible Environment] --> E
    I[Rehabilitation Service PT OT PROSTHETIST] --> E
    J[Outcome FIM Score Barthel Index QUEST Quality of Life] --> D
    D --> J
    C --> J
    B --> J
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The disabled person's **Health Condition** (left) gives rise to Impairment, Activity Limitation, and Participation Restriction.
- **Environmental Factors** (top-right block) act on all three. This block contains **Assistive Medical Devices**, Universal Design, and Rehabilitation Services.
- **Personal Factors** also moderate the outcome.
- The system is closed by the **Outcome** block, which loops back to validate the intervention's effectiveness.

### 4.2 The Rehabilitation Engineering Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1 Identify Need Referral] --> S2[Step 2 Multidisciplinary Assessment]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 SMART Goal Setting]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Device Selection or Custom Design]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Fabrication and Fitting]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 User and Caregiver Training]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 Follow up Maintenance Outcome Measure]
    S7 --> Q{Outcome Acceptable}
    Q -- Yes --> END1[Device Continued Reassessment Yearly]
    Q -- No --> S2
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Cycle 2]**
**Define the term "Assistive Medical Device" as per WHO. List any four categories of assistive devices with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Definition (1.5 marks):*
As per WHO, an **Assistive Medical Device** is any external product (including devices, equipment, instruments, and software) specially designed or adapted to support individuals with disabilities, the elderly, or those with chronic conditions, in performing tasks that they would otherwise be unable to do, thereby improving their functional capability and social participation.

*Four categories (1.5 marks — 0.5 each, with example):*

| # | Category | Example |
|---|----------|---------|
| 1 | Mobility aids | Manual wheelchair / axillary crutch |
| 2 | Sensory aids | Behind-the-ear digital hearing aid |
| 3 | Communication aids (AAC) | Speech-generating device for a person with cerebral palsy |
| 4 | Self-care aids | Built-up-handle spoon for an arthritis patient |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]**
**Differentiate between the Medical Model and the Social Model of disability. State the framework proposed by WHO that reconciles both.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Medical Model (1 mark):* Disability is viewed as a **problem of the individual**, caused directly by disease, trauma, or other health condition. The solution is medical intervention aimed at **cure, treatment, or rehabilitation of the individual** to bring them closer to "normal."

*Social Model (1 mark):* Disability is viewed as a **problem created by society** through environmental, attitudinal, and architectural barriers. The individual with impairment is disabled by an unaccommodating environment. The solution is **social change, accessibility, and universal design.**

*Reconciling framework (1 mark):* WHO's **International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health (ICF), 2001** adopts a **Biopsychosocial Model** that integrates the medical and social models, recognizing that disability emerges from the interaction of a health condition with environmental and personal contextual factors.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]

**(a) (7 marks)** Explain the WHO ICF framework in detail. With a suitable diagram, describe how an assistive device is positioned in the framework and how it interacts with body function, activity, and participation domains.

**(b) (7 marks)** Discuss the five major reasons for abandonment of assistive devices as identified by the WHO GATE initiative. For each reason, suggest one engineering or design strategy to mitigate it.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A (a):**

The **International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health (ICF)** was adopted by the 54th World Health Assembly in May 2001. It replaced the older ICIDH (1980) and is the global standard for describing and classifying health and disability at both individual and population levels.

**1. Core components of ICF (3 marks):**
- **Body Functions and Structures** — physiological and anatomical parts (e.g., limb, eye, ear).
- **Activities** — execution of a task by an individual (e.g., walking, reading).
- **Participation** — involvement in life situations (e.g., attending school, working).
- **Contextual Factors:**
  - **Environmental factors** — physical, social, attitudinal environment (where assistive devices sit).
  - **Personal factors** — age, gender, motivation, lifestyle (not classified by ICF but acknowledged).

**2. Position of Assistive Device in the framework (2 marks):**
An assistive device is an **Environmental Factor** (specifically a **Product and Technology** facilitator, ICF code e1). It acts as a *facilitator* that:
- **Supports body function** by providing external power/structure (e.g., powered wheelchair supports mobility function).
- **Reduces activity limitation** by enabling the user to perform a task (e.g., hearing aid enables conversation).
- **Removes participation restriction** by allowing access to education, work, and community (e.g., screen reader allows a blind student to use a computer).

**3. Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to the Mermaid block diagram drawn in **Section 4.1** of these notes. The student is expected to redraw it on paper.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A (b):**

The **WHO Global Cooperation on Assistive Technology (GATE)** initiative, in partnership with academic literature (Phillips & Zhao, 1993; Wessels et al., 2003), identified the following five major reasons for device abandonment (1.4 marks each, 7 total):

| # | Reason for Abandonment | Mitigation Strategy |
|---|------------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | **User-priority mismatch** — device does what the engineer thought was important, not what the user wants | **Participatory design / co-design** workshops with end users; ethnographic field studies before specification |
| 2 | **Breakdown and poor after-sales** — device fails, no local repair | **Modular design** with replaceable subassemblies; local fabrication; service-network contracts; ISO 13485 quality systems |
| 3 | **Inadequate training** — user/caregiver does not know how to use device | Structured **training programme** with pictographic manuals, follow-up home visits, peer-trainer model |
| 4 | **Discomfort and physical fit issues** — e.g., poorly fitting prosthetic socket | **Custom-fitted sockets** using CAD/CAM, pressure-mapping, iterative fitting trials; ergonomic anthropometric data |
| 5 | **Stigma, aesthetics, social rejection** — bulky, ugly, or culturally inappropriate | **Inclusive design**, customizable cosmetic covers, sleek modern industrial design, peer-group demonstration |

The **engineering takeaway:** the *technically best* device is not the *most adopted* device. The user-centered and context-aware perspective of rehabilitation engineering is what determines real-world success.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]

**(a) (7 marks)** Describe the ISO 9999 classification of assistive products. List at least six classes with one example each. Explain how this classification is useful for procurement, reimbursement, and policy.

**(b) (7 marks)** With a suitable derivation, define the Functional Independence Measure (FIM). A 55-year-old male patient was admitted with a FIM score of 36 and discharged after 30 days with a FIM score of 90. Calculate the FIM efficiency and comment on the rehabilitation outcome.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B (a):**

**1. ISO 9999 — definition and structure (2 marks):**

ISO 9999 is the **international standard** published by the International Organization for Standardization titled *"Assistive products for persons with disability — Classification and terminology"*. It is a **three-level hierarchical classification**:
- **Class** (broadest)
- **Subclass**
- **Division** (most specific)

Each assistive product is given a unique code. The latest edition (ISO 9999:2022) contains **10 classes, 130 subclasses, and over 780 divisions**.

**2. Six classes with examples (3 marks — 0.5 each):**

| Class | Title | Example |
|-------|-------|---------|
| 04 | Assistive products for measuring, supporting, training, or replacing body functions | Glucose monitor, hearing aid |
| 05 | Assistive products for education and training | Braille slate, adapted abacus |
| 06 | Orthoses and prosthetics (body support) | AFO (Ankle Foot Orthosis) |
| 09 | Assistive products for personal mobility | Manual wheelchair, lower-limb prosthesis |
| 12 | Assistive products for self-care | Adapted eating utensils, shower chair |
| 22 | Assistive products for communication and information | Screen reader, AAC device |

**3. Utility of ISO 9999 (2 marks):**
- **Procurement** — Hospitals, government schemes (e.g., ADIP scheme of India), and NGOs use ISO 9999 codes to specify the exact product to be purchased, avoiding ambiguity.
- **Reimbursement** — Insurance companies and government payers (e.g., CGHS, ESI) match ISO 9999 codes to reimbursement tariffs, ensuring standardised payment.
- **Policy & research** — Helps in tracking assistive technology needs, supply gaps, and unmet needs at national and global level (used by WHO GATE and ATscale Global Partnership for Assistive Technology).
- **Trade and customs** — Enables correct HS-code-based import duty classification.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B (b):**

**1. FIM — definition and derivation (3 marks):**

The **Functional Independence Measure (FIM)** is an 18-item, 7-level ordinal outcome measure developed in the 1980s by a task force of the American Congress of Rehabilitation Medicine and the American Academy of Physical Medicine and Rehabilitation. It assesses the **burden of care** — i.e., how much assistance a person needs to perform basic activities of daily living (ADL).

**Structure (as derived in Section 3.1):**

$$FIM = \sum_{i=1}^{18} s_i \quad ; \quad s_i \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7\}$$

- **Motor domain:** 13 items.
- **Cognitive domain:** 5 items.
- **Total range:** **18 to 126**.

**2. Numerical calculation (3 marks):**

Given:
- $FIM_{admission} = 36$
- $FIM_{discharge} = 90$
- Length of stay (LOS) = 30 days

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FIM Efficiency} &= \frac{FIM_{discharge} - FIM_{admission}}{LOS} \\
                      &= \frac{90 - 36}{30} \\
                      &= \frac{54}{30} \\
                      &= 1.8 \quad \text{points per day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**3. Comment on the outcome (1 mark):**

An FIM efficiency of **1.8 points/day** is considered **very good** in a typical inpatient rehabilitation setting. A value above 1.0 is generally taken as the threshold of an effective rehabilitation program; values between 1.5 and 2.5 are characteristic of well-managed stroke, traumatic brain injury, or post-amputation rehabilitation. The patient has moved from a *moderate/maximal assistance* category to *modified independence*, indicating a successful rehabilitation outcome.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 places KTU students lose marks on Foundational Concepts:**
>
> 1. **Confusing ICF with ICIDH.** ICIDH (1980) used *Impairment–Disability–Handicap*; ICF (2001) replaced it with *Body Functions/Structures–Activities–Participation* and added **Contextual Factors**. Writing the older terminology in a 2024 exam loses 0.5–1 mark.
> 2. **Stating ISO 9999 has only 4 or 5 classes.** The current edition has **10 classes**. Outdated answer = -1 mark.
> 3. **Forgetting to mention contextual factors** in the ICF diagram. The diagram must include *Environmental Factors* (where the device lives) and *Personal Factors* — examiners specifically check this.
> 4. **In FIM calculation, swapping admission and discharge values.** If the answer comes out negative, it is almost always an error in $FIM_{discharge} - FIM_{admission}$.
> 5. **Giving only technical reasons for abandonment** and missing the **social/aesthetic/stigma** factors. Rehabilitation Engineering is as much a *humanitarian discipline* as a technical one — examiners expect 1 full mark on user-centered design.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Module 1 — Foundational Concepts | Last-Minute Revision**

- ☐ **WHO definition** of Assistive Medical Device: external aid to restore, augment, or compensate for impaired function; improve participation.
- ☐ **Rehabilitation Engineering** = systematic application of engineering to rehabilitation.
- ☐ **ICF framework** = Biopsychosocial model. Four components: Body Functions/Structures, Activities, Participation, Contextual Factors (Environmental + Personal).
- ☐ **Impairment ≠ Disability ≠ Handicap.** Impairment (body level) → Activity Limitation (person level) → Participation Restriction (social level).
- ☐ **ICIDH (1980)** is **old** — do NOT use in 2024 scheme answers. Use **ICF (2001)**.
- ☐ **ISO 9999** = **10 classes, 3 levels** (Class / Subclass / Division). Latest edition **2022**.
- ☐ **Classification of AMD:** Functional, Technology-based (ISO 9999), User-based, Architectural.
- ☐ **FIM** = 18 items, scored 1–7 each; total range **18 to 126**. **FIM Efficiency = (FIM_out − FIM_in) / LOS** in days.
- ☐ **Barthel Index** = 10 ADL items, total **0 to 100**.
- ☐ **QALY** = utility × time. **DALY = YLL + YLD**.
- ☐ **ICER** = ΔCost / ΔQALY. Threshold in India ≈ ₹5 lakh/QALY.
- ☐ **WHO GATE initiative** identified 5 reasons for **30–50% abandonment** of assistive devices: priority mismatch, breakdown, lack of training, discomfort, stigma.
- ☐ **Rehabilitation Engineering Process** = 7 steps: Identify → Assess → Set Goals → Select/Design → Fabricate/Fit → Train → Follow-up & Outcome.
- ☐ **Stakeholders** = User, Family, Clinician (PMR/PT/OT), Rehabilitation Engineer, Manufacturer, Regulator (FDA/CDSCO/CE), Payer (Insurance/Govt).
- ☐ **Universal Design principles** (Ronald Mace, 1997): Equitable use, Flexibility, Simple/Intuitive, Perceptible information, Tolerance for error, Low effort, Size/space for approach.
- ☐ **Difference between Medical Device vs Assistive Device** — same product can be both at different stages of the user journey.
- ☐ **Disability prevalence globally ≈ 16%** (WHO 2023) — > **1.3 billion** people.
- ☐ The **engineer must think user-first**, not technology-first. This is the single most-tested philosophy in KTU Module 1.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application of HAAT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBRT614 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Application of HAAT # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 The HAAT Model — Formal Academic Definition The **Human Activity Assistive Technology (HAAT) Model** is an outcome-based, person-centered theoretical framework developed by **Albert M. Cook and Janice M. Hussey** (first published in their seminal textbook *Assistive Technologies: Principles and Practice*, Mosby/Elsevier). It is formally defined as: > **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):** > *"The HAAT Model is a holistic, human-factors engineering framework that conceptualizes the Assistive Technology (AT) system as an inseparable, dynamic, and bidirectional interaction between four core components: the **Human** (user with functional limitations), the **Activity** (the meaningful task or life-role to be performed), the **Assistive Technology** (the device, software, or service that bridges the gap), and the **Context** (the physical, social, cultural, and institutional environment in which the system operates)."* In the KTU 2024 scheme (NEP 2020 aligned), the HAAT model is treated as the **foundational assessment schema** upon which all subsequent AT selection, prescription, and outcome-measurement logic rests. ## 1.2 The Four Pillars of HAAT | Pillar | KTU Acronym | Core Entity | Key Question It Answers | |---|---|---|---| | **Human** | H | Person with disability / functional limitation | *WHO is the user?* | | **Activity** | A | Meaningful task (ADL, IADL, education, work) | *WHAT does the user want to do?* | | **Assistive Technology** | AT | Device, software, or service | *HOW is the gap bridged?* | | **Context** | C | Physical, social, institutional, cultural setting | *WHERE and WHEN does it happen?* | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board-Examiner Focus:** Unlike the older **medical model** (which centers on the *impairment*) or the **social model** (which centers on *environment*), the HAAT model uniquely focuses on the **Activity** as the central outcome. This is the *Activity-Limitation* approach adopted by the WHO ICF (International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health). ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bridge Builder" Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy (Bridge Builder):** > Imagine a person (**H**uman) standing on the near bank of a river. On the far bank lies a treasure chest labeled "My Daily Life" (**A**ctivity). The river is wide, swift, and dangerous — it represents the *functional limitation* (e.g., paralysis, low vision, cognitive deficit). The **Assistive Technology** is the *bridge* we engineer across the river. However, the bridge is useless if it lands in a swamp (**Context**) or in the wrong country with different road rules (**Social/Cultural Context**). > > **HAAT Application** = Designing the *right* bridge (AT), for the *right* person (Human), to reach the *right* destination (Activity), placed in the *right* terrain (Context).
