This is exactly how KTU expects you to think during case-study questions: never prescribe the device first — **start with the Activity**. ## 1.4 Why HAAT is the Gold-Standard in KTU Curriculum - **Multidisciplinary Acceptance**: Used by Occupational Therapists (OTs), Physiotherapists (PTs), Rehabilitation Engineers, Prosthetists, and Biomedical Engineers alike. - **Outcome-Based Alignment**: Maps directly onto KTU Course Outcomes (CO1: *Understand the human factors and frameworks underlying AT design*). - **Universal Applicability**: Can model low-tech (reachers, canes) to high-tech (robotic exoskeletons, AI-driven AAC) devices. - **Contextual Sensitivity**: The inclusion of the Context pillar prevents the classic "shelf-ware" phenomenon (where expensive devices are abandoned because they don't fit the user's home/work environment). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Decomposition of the HAAT System — The Four Quadrants The HAAT system is best understood as a **closed-loop, dynamic control system** (analogous to a human-factors feedback loop). The KTU examiner expects you to describe each component in terms of its *inputs*, *processes*, and *outputs*. ### 2.1.1 The Human (H) Component The Human is not a passive "patient" but an *active agent* with intrinsic capacities and limitations. **Intrinsic Sub-factors:** - **Sensory Functions:** Vision, hearing, somatosensation, proprioception, vestibular, pain. - **Motor Functions:** Strength, range of motion (ROM), tone (spasticity/rigidity), coordination, dexterity, endurance. - **Cognitive Functions:** Memory (short-term, long-term, working), attention, executive function, problem-solving, learning style. - **Affective/Social Factors:** Motivation, self-efficacy, family support, stigma tolerance, cultural beliefs about disability. - **Anthropometry:** Body dimensions, weight, height, reach envelopes (critical for wheelchair prescription). ### 2.1.2 The Activity (A) Component Activities are classified using the WHO ICF taxonomy: $$A_{total} = A_{ADL} \cup A_{IADL} \cup A_{Edu} \cup A_{Work} \cup A_{Leisure} \cup A_{Social}$$ Where: - $A_{ADL}$ = Activities of Daily Living (feeding, dressing, toileting, bathing, grooming, mobility, continence) - $A_{IADL}$ = Instrumental ADL (cooking, shopping, finance management, medication management) - $A_{Edu}$ = Education-specific tasks (writing, reading, computer use) - $A_{Work}$ = Vocational tasks - $A_{Leisure}$ = Recreation, sports, hobbies - $A_{Social}$ = Communication, social participation > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Tip:** When asked to "apply HAAT," always list at least 3 *specific* activities (not vague "daily life"). Example: "eating with a spoon," "typing a 500-word document," "transferring from bed to wheelchair." ### 2.1.3 The Assistive Technology (AT) Component Following the **ISO 9999:2022** classification, AT is categorized into: $$AT = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} AT_i \quad \text{where } AT_i \in \{ \text{Aids for } \ldots \}$$ Common classes relevant to KTU Module 1: - **Aids for personal mobility** (wheelchairs, walkers, canes, powered scooters) - **Aids for daily living** (reachers, adapted cutlery, dressing aids) - **Aids for communication** (AAC devices, hearing aids, text-to-speech) - **Aids for manipulation** (prosthetics, orthotics, robotic arms) - **Aids for sensory substitution** (Braille displays, screen readers, tactile maps) - **Aids for cognitive function** (memory aids, GPS way-finders, reminder apps) - **Information and communication technology (ICT) aids** (adaptive keyboards, eye-gaze trackers) ### 2.1.4 The Context (C) Component Cook and Hussey divide context into four nested layers, often visualized as concentric circles: $$C = \{C_{physical}, C_{social}, C_{cultural}, C_{institutional}\}$$ | Context Layer | Definition | Example Impact on AT | |---|---|---| | **Physical Context** | Built environment, lighting, noise, terrain | A power wheelchair needs a lift in a 2-storey house | | **Social Context** | Family, peers, caregivers, attitudes | A cochlear implant's success depends on family encouragement | | **Cultural Context** | Beliefs, stigma, religion, language | A prosthetic limb may be refused if viewed as "impure" | | **Institutional Context** | Policies, funding, legal, healthcare systems | Insurance reimbursement determines whether AT reaches the user | ## 2.2 The HAAT Output Equation (KTU High-Yield Framework) The success of an AT intervention can be formally expressed as: $$\text{Outcome} = f(H, A, AT, C) \cdot \text{Acceptance}$$ Where: - $f(H, A, AT, C)$ = the functional *match score* between the four pillars (a multi-dimensional fit function). - $\text{Acceptance}$ = a binary or continuous variable (1 = adopted long-term, 0 = abandoned). This is the famous **"HAAT Acceptance Factor"** often tested in KTU exams. > **Real-world engineering utility:** This model directly informs the **User-Centered Design (UCD)** process in ISO 9241-210, ensuring that biomedical device engineers do not design in isolation but co-design with the end-user. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & High-Yield Concept Table | Concept / Symbol | Description | Unit / Type | KTU Exam Frequency | |---|---|---|---| | **HAAT** | Human Activity Assistive Technology Model | Conceptual framework | ★★★★★ | | **ICF** | International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health (WHO) | WHO Standard | ★★★★★ | | **ISO 9999** | International classification of assistive products | ISO Standard | ★★★★ | | **MATCH-ACC** | Matching Person & Technology (a related model) | Assessment tool | ★★★ | | **Shelf-ware** | AT that is acquired but never used | Phenomenon | ★★★★ | | **UCD** | User-Centered Design (ISO 9241-210) | Design process | ★★★ | | **Activity Analysis** | Breaking down a task into steps, demands, and context | Clinical method | ★★★★★ | | **C3PO Framework** | Client, Context, Competency, Characteristics, Price, Outcome (device selection) | Selection algorithm | ★★★ | | **ROI in AT** | Return on Investment (cost of AT vs. caregiver burden reduction) | Economic metric | ★★ | | **QUEST 2.0** | Quebec User Evaluation of Satisfaction with Assistive Technology | Outcome measure | ★★★ | > [!NOTE] > **Critical KTU Note:** The model is sometimes abbreviated **"HATC"** or **"HAAT"** interchangeably in Indian textbook adaptations. Both refer to the same Cook & Hussey framework. The KTU syllabus specifically uses **"HAAT"**. ## 2.4 Why This Matters in Real Biomedical Engineering - **R&D Phase:** HAAT guides the design brief. Engineers must answer: "For whom, doing what, in what setting, and how does my device interface with their body?" - **Procurement Phase:** Hospital biomeds use HAAT to justify AT purchases to administration. - **Clinical Phase:** Therapists use HAAT to match patients to devices, reducing abandonment rates (which globally range from **20% to 70%** for mobility and vision AT). - **Policy Phase:** WHO's *GATE initiative* (Global Cooperation on Assistive Technology) uses HAAT-aligned logic for national AT policy. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Application of the HAAT Model (The KTU Case-Analysis Procedure) ## 3.1 The Standardized HAAT Application Algorithm (7-Step Process) The KTU board examiner expects a **systematic, reproducible 7-step process** whenever the question reads *"Apply HAAT to..."* or *"Using the HAAT model, evaluate..."*. This algorithm is the de-facto KTU answer template. **Step 1: Identify and Profile the Human (H)** List sensory, motor, cognitive, and affective capacities. Include diagnosis and *functional* (not just medical) limitations. **Step 2: Define the Target Activity (A)** Specify the exact task. Apply **Activity Analysis**: break it into sub-steps, identify the *physical, cognitive, and sensory demands* of each sub-step. **Step 3: Analyze the Context (C)** Map the four context layers for the activity. **Step 4: Identify the Gap (Mismatch Function)** $$Gap = Demand(A) - Capacity(H) \big\vert_{C}$$ The gap is what the AT must fill. **Step 5: Select Candidate AT(s)** Generate 2-3 device options. **Step 6: Evaluate Match & Predict Acceptance** For each candidate, score: $f_i(H, A, AT_i, C)$. Predict Acceptance based on prior user preference, training availability, aesthetics, and cost. **Step 7: Trial, Train, Follow-up (Iterate)** The HAAT model is a **closed-loop system**. Re-evaluate at 1, 3, 6 months. ## 3.2 Worked-Out Case Application (Sample KTU-style Walkthrough) > **Case Vignette:** *Ravi, a 32-year-old male, suffered a C5–C6 spinal cord injury 6 months ago. He is currently in a rehabilitation hospital in Kerala. He is a software engineer. He wants to return to his job at a Bengaluru IT firm within 3 months. He lives in a 2-storey rented house with his wife and elderly mother.* ### Step 1 — Human Profile (H) - **Diagnosis:** C5–C6 complete spinal cord injury (ASIA A). - **Sensory:** Intact above C6; loss of sensation and motor control below C6 (triceps absent, wrist extension preserved). - **Motor:** No voluntary finger movement; **tenodesis grasp** present; **shoulder and elbow flexion** preserved; trunk control poor. - **Cognitive/Affective:** Intact cognition, high motivation, signs of mild depression (PHQ-9 score = 8). - **Anthropometry:** Height 175 cm, weight 70 kg. ### Step 2 — Activity Analysis (A) Target activity: *"Write a 500-line Python program and attend a daily 4-hour stand-up meeting on Zoom."* Sub-steps & demands: | Sub-step | Physical Demand | Cognitive Demand | Sensory Demand | |---|---|---|---| | Type code | Fine motor finger flexion | High (syntax logic) | Visual (screen) | | Use mouse | Fine motor | Low | Visual | | Speak in meeting | Breath control, phonation | High (real-time Q&A) | Auditory | | Sit upright 4 hr | Trunk stability, pressure relief | Moderate | Proprioceptive | | Self-toilet | Transfer, clothing management | Low | — | ### Step 3 — Context (C) - **Physical:** Bengaluru office is wheelchair-accessible (ramps, accessible washrooms). Home has stairs to bedroom and bathroom. - **Social:** Supportive wife; elderly mother has knee pain and cannot lift him. Employer open to remote work. - **Cultural:** Family from rural Kerala, initially hesitant about "electric wheelchair," views it as "showing off the disability." - **Institutional:** ADIP scheme (Assistance to Disabled Persons for Purchase/Fitting of Aids/Appliances) provides partial funding. Insurer covers up to ₹2,50,000 for mobility AT. ### Step 4 — Gap Identification $$Gap = \{ \text{Finger movement, Trunk stability, Mobility (stairs)}, \text{Stamina} \}$$ ### Step 5 — Candidate AT Selection (AT) | Gap | AT Option 1 | AT Option 2 | AT Option 3 | |---|---|---|---| | Finger movement | **Voice-to-code (AI)** (e.g., Serenade, GitHub Copilot Voice) | **Mouth-stick keyboard** | **Sip-and-puff + on-screen keyboard** | | Trunk stability | **Custom-molded backrest cushion** | **Power tilt-in-space wheelchair** | **Corset TLSO brace** | | Mobility | **Power wheelchair (rear-wheel drive)** | **Smart power wheelchair with stair-climber** | Manual lightweight wheelchair (inadequate) | | Stamina | **Pressure-relief timer/alarm app** | **Ergonomic office chair with lateral supports** | Scheduled 30-min breaks | ### Step 6 — Match & Acceptance - **Voice-to-code** has highest *Activity-specificity* but low acceptance initially (cultural hesitation toward "talking to a computer" in front of colleagues). - **Power wheelchair with stair-climber** scores high on physical match but is **cost-prohibitive** (~₹18 lakh) vs. institutional cap. - **Mouth-stick keyboard** is low-tech, low-cost, but causes fatigue over 4-hour meetings. **Best combination:** Power wheelchair (rear-wheel drive) + Custom-molded backrest + Sip-and-puff with on-screen keyboard (for coding) + Voice-to-text (for meetings) + Pressure-relief smartwatch. ### Step 7 — Trial, Train, Follow-up - 1-month trial of sip-and-puff at rehab center. - 3-month home visit: assess home modifications (install stairlift using ADIP top-up). - 6-month workplace ergonomics audit. ## 3.3 Quantitative Activity-Demand Formula (KTU High-Yield) The *physical demand* of an activity can be expressed as a vector: $$\vec{D}_A = (D_{strength}, D_{ROM}, D_{endurance}, D_{coordination}, D_{sensory})$$ The *human capacity* vector is: $$\vec{C}_H = (C_{strength}, C_{ROM}, C_{endurance}, C_{coordination}, C_{sensory})$$ The **AT must supply the residual demand vector** $\vec{R}$: $$\vec{R} = \max(\vec{D}_A - \vec{C}_H, \vec{0})$$ This formula clarifies *why* no single AT fits all activities: it must be matched component-wise to the residual demand vector. ## 3.4 Common Application Errors (KTU Examiner's Pitfalls) - **Error 1:** Prescribing the AT before defining the Activity. *(Mark deduction: 2-3 marks)* - **Error 2:** Ignoring the Context pillar. *(Mark deduction: 1-2 marks)* - **Error 3:** Treating "Human" as a passive patient. *(Mark deduction: 1-2 marks)* - **Error 4:** Not considering Acceptance / abandonment factors. *(Mark deduction: 1 mark)* # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The HAAT Model — Master Diagram (Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture) The following Mermaid diagram renders the canonical HAAT model with the four pillars and their bidirectional interactions, including the central Activity loop and the Context-influence ring. ```mermaid flowchart TB H1["HUMAN
Sensory, Motor, Cognitive,
Affective, Anthropometry"] A1["ACTIVITY
ADL, IADL, Education,
Work, Leisure, Social"] AT1["ASSISTIVE TECHNOLOGY
Device, Software, Service
ISO 9999 Classes"] C1["CONTEXT
Physical, Social,
Cultural, Institutional"] H1 -- "Capacity inputs to" --> A1 A1 -- "Activity demands placed on" --> H1 H1 -- "Drives prescription of" --> AT1 AT1 -- "Reshapes capacity of" --> H1 A1 -- "Defines functional goal for" --> AT1 AT1 -- "Enables or modifies" --> A1 C1 -- "Modulates feasibility of" --> A1 C1 -- "Determines acceptance of" --> AT1 C1 -- "Affects performance of" --> H1 H1 -- "Negotiates within" --> C1 H1 ~~~ Core[" "] A1 ~~~ Core AT1 ~~~ Core C1 ~~~ Core ``` ## 4.2 The 7-Step HAAT Application Algorithm (Mermaid Flowchart) ```mermaid flowchart TD S1["STEP 1: Profile the Human H
Sensory Motor Cognitive Affective"] S2["STEP 2: Define Target Activity A
Activity Analysis sub steps demands"] S3["STEP 3: Map Context C
Physical Social Cultural Institutional"] S4["STEP 4: Compute Gap
Gap = Demand minus Capacity"] S5["STEP 5: Select Candidate ATs
2 to 3 options per gap"] S6["STEP 6: Match and Predict Acceptance
Score f of H A AT C times Acceptance"] S7["STEP 7: Trial Train Follow up
Re-evaluate at 1 3 6 months"] S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 S7 -. "Iterate if mismatch" .-> S5 ``` ## 4.3 Functional Mapping of AT to Human Sub-systems (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph HUMAN_SUBSYSTEMS SE["Sensory Sub system
Vision Hearing Touch"] MO["Motor Sub system
Strength ROM Tone"] CO["Cognitive Sub system
Memory Attention Executive"] end subgraph AT_BRIDGES AT_V["AT for Vision
Screen readers Braille CCTV"] AT_H["AT for Hearing
Hearing aids Captioning"] AT_M["AT for Motor
Wheelchair Prosthetics Exoskeleton"] AT_C["AT for Cognitive
Memory apps GPS Reminders"] end SE -- "Vision loss" --> AT_V SE -- "Hearing loss" --> AT_H MO -- "Motor limitation" --> AT_M CO -- "Cognitive limitation" --> AT_C AT_V --> OUT["Activity Outcome
Reading Communication Mobility"] AT_H --> OUT AT_M --> OUT AT_C --> OUT ``` ## 4.4 The Context Layer Ring (Mermaid Concentric View) ```mermaid flowchart TB INST["Institutional Context
Policies Funding Legal"] CUL["Cultural Context
Beliefs Stigma Religion Language"] SOC["Social Context
Family Peers Caregivers Attitudes"] PHY["Physical Context
Built environment Lighting Noise Terrain"] INST --> CUL CUL --> SOC SOC --> PHY PHY --> HAAT_CORE["HAAT CORE
H plus A plus AT"] HAAT_CORE -. "Direct modulation" .-> PHY HAAT_CORE -. "Direct modulation" .-> SOC HAAT_CORE -. "Direct modulation" .-> CUL HAAT_CORE -. "Direct modulation" .-> INST ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagrams:** In a KTU answer, you are expected to *describe* the diagram in 3-4 lines and then apply it. Do NOT just reproduce the diagram without explanation. Marks are awarded for *interpretation*, not mere copying. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1.** Define the HAAT model. List its four components. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Human Activity Assistive Technology (HAAT) Model**, proposed by Cook and Hussey, is a person-centered framework for designing, selecting, and evaluating assistive technology. It conceptualizes the AT system as an interactive whole comprising four components: 1. **Human (H)** — the user with sensory, motor, cognitive, and affective characteristics. 2. **Activity (A)** — the meaningful task the user wishes to perform (ADL, IADL, education, work, leisure). 3. **Assistive Technology (AT)** — the device, software, or service that bridges the gap between human capacity and activity demand. 4. **Context (C)** — the physical, social, cultural, and institutional environment that modulates the entire system. *(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for listing the four components with one-line description each.)* --- ### **Q2.** Differentiate between the medical model and the HAAT model of disability. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Medical Model | HAAT Model | |---|---|---| | **Focus** | The impairment (in the person) | The activity (the desired outcome) | | **Goal** | Cure / reduce impairment | Enable activity despite impairment | | **Human role** | Passive patient | Active agent & co-designer | | **Context** | Ignored | Central pillar | | **Outcome measure** | Symptom reduction | Functional participation | | **Approach** | Top-down, clinician-driven | Bottom-up, person-centered | HAAT aligns with the **WHO ICF (biopsychosocial model)**, whereas the medical model aligns with the older **ICIDH (impairment-disability-handicap)** classification. --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1, KTU 2024 Scheme]** > > **(a)** Describe in detail the four components of the HAAT model with suitable examples for each. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Apply the HAAT model to evaluate the assistive technology needs of a **65-year-old retired school teacher with severe age-related macular degeneration (AMD) and moderate bilateral hearing loss, living alone in a single-storey apartment in Kochi**. Recommend a comprehensive AT plan. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 marks **1. Human (H) — 2 marks** Describe sensory (vision ↓↓, hearing ↓), motor (preserved), cognitive (intact, mild slowing), affective (risk of social isolation, depression), anthropometry (frail, female, ~155 cm). **2. Activity (A) — 2 marks** - ADL: self-feeding, dressing, toileting, medication management. - IADL: cooking, finances, phone calls, grocery. - Leisure: reading, listening to radio/music, temple visits, conversing with grandchildren. **3. Assistive Technology (AT) — 2 marks** - Vision: magnifier (optical/CCTV), large-print books, talking books, high-contrast labels, audio labels (Talking Tactile Tablet). - Hearing: behind-the-ear digital hearing aids, personal amplifier, captioned telephone. - Multi-modal: smartphone with VoiceOver / TalkBack + hearing-aid coupling (MFi/ASHA). **4. Context (C) — 1 mark** Physical: well-lit single-storey, but uneven tile flooring. Social: lives alone, daughter in Bengaluru. Cultural: devout Hindu, attends daily puja. Institutional: RPWD Act 2016 benefits, ADIP scheme funding. --- #### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 marks **Step 1 — Profile H:** Diagnosis: bilateral wet AMD (treated with anti-VEGF injections), bilateral moderate presbycusis. Visual acuity 6/60, contrast sensitivity ↓, glare sensitivity ↑. Pure-tone average 50 dB HL. Lives alone → high fall risk. **Step 2 — Activity:** Priority target = *safe self-medication + communication with daughter + continued leisure reading*. **Step 3 — Context:** Single-storey apartment, stair-free, but low lighting and tile flooring. Daughter supportive (remote). Cultural: must recite Sanskrit slokas daily. Institutional: eligible for ADIP, free hearing aids under NPPCD. **Step 4 — Gap:** Vision (distance & near) + hearing (speech in noise) + social isolation. **Step 5 — AT Prescription Table (3 marks):** | Activity | Prescribed AT | Rationale | |---|---|---| | Reading newspaper | **Desktop CCTV (e.g., Optelec ClearView)** at 20X | Compensates central scotoma | | Self-medication | **Audio-label dispenser (Talking Rx)** + large-print label | Avoids confusion | | Phone calls with daughter | **Captioned telephone (CapTel)** + paired hearing aids | Combats hearing loss | | Daily puja | **Audio Sanskrit library + tactile prayer counter** | Cultural & spiritual continuity | | Fall prevention | **High-contrast tape on floor edges + smart night-light** | Contextual safety | | Social contact | **Smartphone with TalkBack + WhatsApp voice notes** | Maintains IADL communication | **Step 6 — Match & Acceptance:** All devices are low-to-moderate cost (₹5,000–₹60,000), culturally neutral, and within ADIP/NPPCD coverage. High predicted acceptance due to familiar interfaces. **Step 7 — Trial & Follow-up:** Trial CCTV for 1 week; hearing-aid fitting with real-ear measurement; 1-month home visit by district rehabilitation worker. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1, KTU 2024 Scheme]** > > **(a)** Explain the concept of *Context* in the HAAT model. Discuss how each of the four layers of context can make or break an AT intervention. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A **7-year-old child with cerebral palsy (GMFCS Level IV) is about to enter Class 2 in a mainstream government school in Thrissur**. Using the HAAT model, design a complete AT intervention plan covering mobility, communication, classroom participation, and toilet access. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 marks **Definition (1 mark):** Context in HAAT is the multi-layered environment in which the human-activity-technology triad operates. It has four nested layers. **Four Layers (4 marks — 1 each):** 1. **Physical Context:** Built environment — ramps, door widths, lighting, noise. *Example:* A ₹5 lakh power wheelchair is useless if the school gate has a 4-inch step. 2. **Social Context:** Family, peers, caregiver attitudes. *Example:* A child with AAC is more likely to succeed when classmates are taught "buddy" inclusion. 3. **Cultural Context:** Beliefs about disability. *Example:* In some communities, a hearing aid is seen as a "shame marker"; uptake requires community sensitisation. 4. **Institutional Context:** Policies, funding, legal frameworks. *Example:* The RPWD Act 2016 mandates accessible schools; the Rights of Children with Disabilities mandates inclusive education. **Why context makes/breaks AT (2 marks):** - Globally, **30–70% of AT is abandoned** within 1 year. The #1 cited reason in WHO surveys is **contextual mismatch** (not device failure). - HAAT's unique contribution: it forces the assessor to *unbundle* context and address each layer systematically before prescription. --- #### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 marks **Step 1 — Human (H):** 7-year-old, spastic quadriplegia (CP), GMFCS IV (self-mobility with severe limitations; may use powered mobility). Cognition: age-appropriate (assumed). Speech: dysarthric, uses ~20 single words. Sensation: intact. Affective: shy, prone to teasing anxiety. **Step 2 — Activity:** Toileting, classroom writing, peer communication, attending assembly. **Step 3 — Context:** - Physical: Government school, ground-floor classroom OK, but no accessible toilet; playground has gravel. - Social: Mother is primary caregiver; class teacher is untrained in disability; 35 students per class. - Cultural: Local community supportive but unfamiliar with "special needs." - Institutional: Kerala State Education Board permits scribe; Samagra Shiksha Abhiyan funds AT; accessible India Campaign (Sugamya Bharat Abhiyan) provides school retrofit. **Step 4 — Gap:** Mobility, communication, fine-motor writing, toilet independence. **Step 5 — AT Prescription (3 marks):** | Domain | Prescribed AT | Rationale | |---|---|---| | **Mobility** | **Lightweight manual wheelchair (foldable, anti-tipper)** with growth kit; transition to power wheelchair at age 9 | HAAT: child grows; school terrain | | **Classroom seating** | **Adjustable posture chair with lateral trunk supports + footrest** | GMFCS IV needs postural control | | **Writing** | **Adapted pencil grip + slant board + smartpen with voice-to-text (e.g., Livescribe)** | Compensates fine-motor deficit | | **Communication** | **AAC — Picture Exchange System (PECS) Level 1 + Proloquo2Go app on iPad** | Bridges dysarthria | | **Toilet** | **Commode chair with footrest + grab bars; school accessible-toilet retrofit** | Restores dignity & ADL independence | **Step 6 — Acceptance Predictors (1 mark):** - Mobility AT acceptance: high (mother motivated). - AAC acceptance: medium (teasing risk) → need peer-buddy program. - Toilet AT: low initially → school staff training mandatory. **Step 7 — Follow-up (1 mark):** - Monthly OT visits; teacher-training workshop; 6-month reassessment of AAC competence; transition to powered mobility at 9 years. --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in HAAT Application Questions:** > 1. **Writing only the definition of HAAT (2-3 marks) and skipping application (5+ marks).** KTU *always* tests application, not mere recall. > 2. **Prescribing AT before defining the Activity.** This is the #1 reason for losing 2-3 marks. Always start with the *task*, not the *tool*. > 3. **Ignoring the Context pillar.** Even if you have the perfect device, if you don't mention cultural, social, or institutional context, expect a 1-2 mark deduction. > 4. **Forgetting Acceptance / abandonment factors.** KTU specifically tests whether you understand that *the best AT on paper* may fail in real life. > 5. **Using vague terms like "wheelchair" or "hearing aid" without specifying type, model, features, or settings.** A powered wheelchair with tilt-in-space is *very* different from a basic transport chair. > 6. **Not stating the source / framework explicitly.** Always begin your answer with: *"Applying the HAAT model (Cook & Hussey)..."* — this earns the framework-identification mark. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **HAAT = Human + Activity + Assistive Technology + Context** > - **Cook & Hussey** — originators of the model. > - **Activity is the center** — never start with the device. > - **Context has 4 layers:** Physical, Social, Cultural, Institutional. > - **The "shelf-ware" problem** — 30–70% AT abandonment; HAAT is the antidote. > - **Always include Acceptance** as an outcome factor. > - **Use the 7-step algorithm** in case-study questions. > - **Cross-link with WHO ICF** for full KTU marks. > - **Outcome = f(H, A, AT, C) × Acceptance**. > - **Classify devices using ISO 9999** wherever possible. > - **Always state the framework name** explicitly in the opening line of your answer.

topics.title: Universal Design Functional Framework for Assistive Technologies: Hard and Soft Technologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT614 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Universal Design Functional Framework for Assistive Technologies: Hard and Soft Technologies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Universal Design (UD) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Design (UD)** is the design of products, environments, programmes, and services in such a way that they can be used by **all people**, to the greatest extent possible, **without the need for adaptation or specialized design**. The term was coined by architect **Ronald L. Mace** (Center for Universal Design, North Carolina State University, 1997).

In the context of Assistive Medical Devices, Universal Design transcends the traditional medical model (where a device is built *after* a disability is identified) and adopts a **social model** where the environment — not the person — is what must be modified.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Universal Design is a foundational pillar of Module 1. Students must remember the **7 Principles of Universal Design** verbatim, as Part A questions (3 marks) frequently test direct recall.

### 1.2 The 7 Principles of Universal Design (Mace, 1997)

1. **Equitable Use** — Useful and marketable to people with diverse abilities.
2. **Flexibility in Use** — Accommodates a wide range of individual preferences and abilities.
3. **Simple and Intuitive Use** — Easy to understand, regardless of user's experience or knowledge.
4. **Perceptible Information** — Communicates necessary information effectively to the user.
5. **Tolerance for Error** — Minimizes hazards and adverse consequences of accidental actions.
6. **Low Physical Effort** — Can be used efficiently, comfortably, and with minimum fatigue.
7. **Size and Space for Approach and Use** — Appropriate size and space for approach, reach, manipulation, and use.

### 1.3 Functional Framework for Assistive Technologies (AT)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assistive Technology (AT)** is defined by the *Assistive Technology Act of 2004 (US, Public Law 108-364)* as: *"any item, piece of equipment, or product system, whether acquired commercially, modified, or customized, that is used to increase, maintain, or improve functional capabilities of individuals with disabilities."*

The **Functional Framework** is a structural approach that classifies AT based on the **human function** it supports (mobility, communication, cognition, sensory, etc.) rather than on the disability diagnosis.

### 1.4 Hard Technologies vs. Soft Technologies — The Core Dichotomy

| Aspect | Hard Technology | Soft Technology |
|---|---|---|
| **Form** | Tangible, physical artefact | Intangible, knowledge/process-based |
| **Examples** | Wheelchair, cochlear implant, smart cane | Rehabilitation training, accessibility policy, software |
| **Lifespan** | Limited by wear, battery, calibration | Evolves with research and pedagogy |
| **Engineered by** | Biomedical / Mechanical engineers | Therapists, educators, software developers |
| **KTU focus area** | Module 2–4 (devices) | Module 1 (framework) |

### 1.5 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Think of Universal Design like a **modern restaurant kitchen**.
> - **Hard Technologies** = the *oven, knives, and refrigerators* — the physical tools that do the actual cooking.
> - **Soft Technologies** = the *recipes, training of the chef, and workflow (mise-en-place)* — the knowledge, process, and software that make the tools effective.
> - A wheelchair (hard) is useless without the right seating assessment, training, and inclusive policy (soft) — exactly why a *Functional Framework* must integrate both.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Functional Framework — Hierarchical Layers

The framework operates across **four conceptual layers**, often illustrated as a pyramid (see SECTION_4):

**Layer 1 — Human Function (Bottom of Pyramid)**
- The user's intrinsic need: mobility, communication, vision, hearing, cognition, self-care.
- Driven by the **International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health (ICF)** by WHO (2001).

**Layer 2 — Activity & Participation Goals**
- Translating function into real-world tasks: walking to a bus stop, reading a textbook, eating independently.

**Layer 3 — Assistive Technology Intervention**
- Selection of either a Hard or Soft Technology solution, or a hybrid combination.

**Layer 4 — Universal Design Environment (Top)**
- The societal and built environment in which the user operates — designed *inclusively from the start*.

### 2.2 Hard Technologies — Detailed Classification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Hard technologies in assistive medical devices are **physical, engineered artefacts**. They are typically classified by the **human function** they augment.

**A. Mobility Aids**
- Manual wheelchairs, powered wheelchairs, smart canes, exoskeletons, prosthetics, orthotics.
- **Key engineering metrics:** load capacity (kg), range (km), battery life (hours), turning radius (m).

**B. Sensory Aids**
- **Visual:** Screen readers, refreshable Braille displays, smart glasses (e.g., OrCam), white canes.
- **Auditory:** Hearing aids, cochlear implants, induction loops, alerting devices.

**C. Communication Aids (AAC — Augmentative and Alternative Communication)**
- Picture Exchange Systems (PECS), speech-generating devices (SGDs), eye-tracking AAC.

**D. Cognitive Aids**
- Memory phones, GPS wayfinders, medication dispensers with alarms.

**E. Daily Living (ADL) Aids**
- Adaptive cutlery, dressing sticks, reachers, shower chairs, robotic feeding arms.

### 2.3 Soft Technologies — Detailed Classification

> [!NOTE]
> Soft technologies are **non-physical enablers**. They include software, methodologies, services, and policies that ensure Hard Technologies are deployed effectively.

**A. Rehabilitation Methodologies**
- Physical therapy protocols, occupational therapy frameworks, speech-language pathology approaches.

**B. Software / Digital Platforms**
- Screen reader algorithms (JAWS, NVDA), eye-tracking firmware, AI-based sign language translators, mobile health (mHealth) applications.

**C. Training & Capacity Building**
- User training programs, caregiver education, clinician certification (e.g., RESNA — Rehabilitation Engineering and Assistive Technology Society of North America).

**D. Policy & Service Delivery**
- Accessibility standards (ADA, Section 508, RPwD Act 2016 of India), insurance reimbursement models, AT assessment protocols (HAAT, SETT).

**E. Assessment Frameworks**
- **HAAT Model** (Human, Activity, Assistive Technology) — Cook & Hussey.
- **SETT Framework** (Student, Environment, Tasks, Tools) — Joy Zabala.
- **Matching Person and Technology (MPT)** — Marcia Scherer.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Symbol | Unit | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| Number of design principles (UD) | N | dimensionless | **7** (Mace, 1997) |
| Battery capacity | C | mAh or Wh | Energy storage of powered devices |
| Device load capacity | L | kg (newton) | Maximum user weight supported |
| Range of mobility device | R | km | Distance per charge/use |
| ISO 9999 classification code | — | alphanumeric | Standardized AT taxonomy |
| WHO ICF domains | — | — | Body Function, Activity, Participation |
| Effective dose (radiation/imaging) | — | — | Not applicable to framework-level |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For Module 1 (Functional Framework), the "formulas" are **conceptual models and taxonomies**, not numerical equations. Memorize the **7 UD Principles**, **HAAT Model components**, and the **Hard vs. Soft Technology dichotomy**.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Functional Framework is used in:
- **Medical device R&D** (designing inclusive products from inception).
- **Hospital AT procurement** (tendering and selection processes).
- **Disaster response** (designing inclusive evacuation equipment).
- **Smart city planning** (Universal Design in public infrastructure).
- **Telerehabilitation** (blending soft technologies like video therapy with hard sensors).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Universal Design Process (Mathematical Mapping)

The Universal Design Process can be modelled as an **iterative optimization function**. Let us define:

$$
U_d = f(P, E, A, T, C)
$$

Where:
- $U_d$ = Universal Design index (higher = more inclusive)
- $P$ = Population diversity factor
- $E$ = Environment variability
- $A$ = Accessibility coefficient
- $T$ = Technology compatibility
- $C$ = Cost-effectiveness ratio

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Identify population diversity factor } P &= \frac{N_{users}}{N_{design-base}} \\
\text{Step 2: Quantify environment variability } E &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot v_i \\
\text{Step 3: Compute accessibility } A &= \frac{\text{Affected users served}}{\text{Total affected users}} \\
\text{Step 4: Determine technology compatibility } T &= \frac{\text{Hard + Soft integration score}}{\text{Max possible score}} \\
\text{Step 5: Cost-effectiveness } C &= \frac{\text{Lifetime benefit (QALY gained)}}{\text{Lifetime cost (INR/USD)}} \\
\text{Final aggregation: } U_d &= \alpha P + \beta E + \gamma A + \delta T + \epsilon C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation of steps:**
- Step 1 measures how broadly the design serves beyond the original target population.
- Step 2 weights each environmental variable (lighting, noise, terrain) by importance $w_i$.
- Step 3 is the core accessibility metric, ranging from 0 to 1.
- Step 4 measures how well hard and soft technologies integrate (e.g., a powered wheelchair + mobile app = high T).
- Step 5 normalizes monetary cost against health benefit in **Quality-Adjusted Life Years (QALY)** — a standard metric in medical device evaluation.
- $\alpha, \beta, \gamma, \delta, \epsilon$ are stakeholder-assigned weights summing to 1.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Matching Hard and Soft Technologies

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is a **derivation-style mapping** showing how a single hard technology requires multiple soft technologies for full functionality — a common KTU exam application question.

| Hard Technology (Device) | Required Soft Technology 1 | Required Soft Technology 2 | Required Soft Technology 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cochlear implant | Audiological mapping software | Auditory-verbal therapy (AVT) | Insurance / RPwD Act policy |
| Powered wheelchair | Joystick calibration algorithm | Occupational therapy training | Accessible built environment (ramps) |
| Smart cane (ultrasonic) | Obstacle-detection firmware | Orientation & mobility (O&M) training | Municipal tactile paving policy |
| Insulin pump | Closed-loop control algorithm | Diabetes self-management education | Endocrinologist teleconsultation |
| AAC speech device | Voice-output software | Speech-language pathology | Inclusive school policy |

### 3.3 Quantitative AT Selection Logic

Consider the **Matching Person and Technology (MPT)** evaluation by Scherer. A clinician scores three components, each on a scale of 1 to 5:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{score} &= M_{user} + M_{tech} + M_{env} \\
\text{where} \quad 3 &\leq M_{score} \leq 15
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{score} \geq 12 &\Rightarrow \text{Strong match — proceed with device selection} \\
9 \leq M_{score} &< 12 \Rightarrow \text{Moderate match — pilot trial required} \\
M_{score} &< 9 \Rightarrow \text{Poor match — re-evaluate or select alternative}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **decision rule** derived from the MPT framework — a high-yield concept for KTU 14-mark problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Universal Design Functional Framework — Hierarchical Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User with Functional Need] --> B[ICF Classification Body Function Activity Participation]
    B --> C[Assess Activity Limitation]
    C --> D{AT Intervention Required?}
    D -- No --> E[Universal Design Environment sufficient]
    D -- Yes --> F[Select Technology Type]
    F --> G1[Hard Technology Physical Device]
    F --> G2[Soft Technology Software Methodology Policy]
    G1 --> H1[Wheelchair Prosthesis Cochlear Implant Smart Cane]
    G2 --> H2[Training App Accessibility Policy AT Assessment]
    H1 --> I[Integration Verification]
    H2 --> I
    I --> J{User Outcome Improved?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Deploy and Monitor]
    J -- No --> L[Iterate and Reassess]
    K --> M[Functional Independence Achieved]
    L --> C
```

### 4.2 Hard vs. Soft Technology Integration Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HARD["HARD TECHNOLOGIES PHYSICAL LAYER"]
        HA[Mobility Devices]
        HB[Sensory Devices]
        HC[Communication Devices]
        HD[Cognitive Devices]
    end
    subgraph SOFT["SOFT TECHNOLOGIES INTANGIBLE LAYER"]
        SA[Software & Firmware]
        SB[Rehabilitation Training]
        SC[Policy & Standards]
        SD[Assessment Framework]
    end
    subgraph OUT["USER OUTCOME"]
        OU[Functional Independence Quality of Life Social Inclusion]
    end
    HA --> SA
    HB --> SA
    HC --> SA
    HD --> SA
    HA --> SB
    HB --> SB
    HC --> SB
    HD --> SB
    SA --> OU
    SB --> OU
    SC --> OU
    SD --> OU
```

### 4.3 The 7 Principles of Universal Design — Conceptual Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    UD[Universal Design - Mace 1997] --> P1[1 Equitable Use]
    UD --> P2[2 Flexibility in Use]
    UD --> P3[3 Simple and Intuitive]
    UD --> P4[4 Perceptible Information]
    UD --> P5[5 Tolerance for Error]
    UD --> P6[6 Low Physical Effort]
    UD --> P7[7 Size and Space for Approach]
    P1 --> EX1[Same device for all users]
    P2 --> EX2[Adjustable or adaptable]
    P3 --> EX3[No instructions needed]
    P4 --> EX4[Multimodal feedback]
    P5 --> EX5[Fail-safe mechanisms]
    P6 --> EX6[Minimal fatigue]
    P7 --> EX7[Wheelchair accessible dimensions]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Set A]**
*Define Universal Design. List any four principles of Universal Design.*

**Model Answer:**
Universal Design, coined by Ronald L. Mace (1997), is the design of products and environments to be usable by all people, to the greatest extent possible, without the need for adaptation or specialized design.
Four principles (out of 7):
1. Equitable Use
2. Flexibility in Use
3. Simple and Intuitive Use
4. Perceptible Information
*(Any four accepted; full 3 marks require definition + 4 principles.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**
*Differentiate between Hard Technology and Soft Technology in the context of Assistive Medical Devices. Give two examples of each.*

**Model Answer:**
Hard Technology refers to physical, tangible assistive devices such as wheelchairs, cochlear implants, prosthetic limbs, and smart canes. They are engineered artefacts with measurable specifications.
Soft Technology refers to intangible enablers such as rehabilitation training programs, screen-reader software, accessibility policies, and AT assessment frameworks (e.g., HAAT, MPT).
*(2 marks for differentiation + 1 mark for examples.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students commonly **omit the 7 UD Principles** when asked to "explain Universal Design" — this costs **3 of 14 marks** instantly.
> - When asked to differentiate Hard vs. Soft, students often give only physical definitions and **forget the integration aspect** (how both must work together). Always include at least one example of *integration*.
> - For MPT/HAAT questions, **state the model name, creator, and all components** — partial naming is treated as a 50% answer.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**
**(a)** Explain the Functional Framework for Assistive Technologies with a neat block diagram. Discuss how the WHO-ICF model integrates with this framework. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Describe in detail the **HAAT Model** (Human, Activity, Assistive Technology) by Cook and Hussey. How does it guide the selection of an assistive device for a user? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Functional Framework with ICF Integration (7 marks):**

The Functional Framework for Assistive Technologies is a structured approach that classifies AT based on the human function it supports rather than the diagnosis. It consists of four layers:

1. **Human Function Layer** — Identifies the user's intrinsic need (mobility, communication, cognition, sensory). This maps directly to the WHO-ICF (2001) **Body Functions and Structures** domain.
2. **Activity and Participation Layer** — Translates function into real-life tasks (e.g., walking, reading, eating), corresponding to ICF's **Activities and Participation** domain.
3. **AT Intervention Layer** — Selection of Hard Technology (device) and/or Soft Technology (training, software, policy).
4. **Universal Design Environment Layer** — Inclusive societal and built environment (ICF **Environmental Factors** domain).

**ICF Integration:** The WHO International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health (ICF) provides the **biopsychosocial model** that bridges medical and social perspectives. The Functional Framework uses ICF codes (e.g., b235 Vestibular functions, d450 Walking) to standardize AT prescription globally.

*(Block diagram as in SECTION 4.1, 2 marks reserved for diagram.)*

**(b) HAAT Model by Cook and Hussey (7 marks):**

The **HAAT Model** (Human, Activity, Assistive Technology) was proposed by **Albert M. Cook and Susan M. Hussey** in their textbook *Assistive Technologies: Principles and Practice* (1995, subsequent editions).

**Four Components:**
- **H — Human:** The user — physical, cognitive, sensory, and emotional characteristics.
- **A — Activity:** The goal-directed task the user wants to perform (ADL, IADL, education, work, leisure).
- **AT — Assistive Technology:** The device or service that bridges the gap between human and activity.
- **Context (sometimes 4th element):** The physical, social, and cultural environment.

**Selection Process (5 steps):**
1. Assess the **Human** (abilities, limitations, preferences).
2. Define the target **Activity** with the user.
3. Identify the **gap** between human ability and activity demand.
4. Select the **AT** that minimizes the gap (lowest-tech-first principle).
5. **Train, evaluate, and iterate** based on user feedback.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]**
**(a)** Discuss the **7 Principles of Universal Design** proposed by Ronald L. Mace. Illustrate each principle with one example from assistive medical devices. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** With a suitable case study, explain how **Hard Technology and Soft Technology** are integrated to provide a complete assistive solution for a person with severe visual impairment. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) The 7 Principles of Universal Design (7 marks):**

| # | Principle | Assistive Medical Device Example |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Equitable Use | A wheelchair-accessible MRI scanner used by both ambulatory and non-ambulatory patients. |
| 2 | Flexibility in Use | Adjustable-height hospital beds for patients of varied heights and care needs. |
| 3 | Simple and Intuitive | A one-button fall-alert pendant for elderly users with cognitive decline. |
| 4 | Perceptible Information | A digital glucometer with audio readout + large display for diabetic patients with retinopathy. |
| 5 | Tolerance for Error | An infusion pump with audible alarms and automatic shut-off on occlusion. |
| 6 | Low Physical Effort | A power-assisted wheelchair with joystick control, requiring minimal upper-body strength. |
| 7 | Size and Space | A bathroom with 1.5 m turning radius for wheelchair maneuverability (ADA standard). |

*(1 mark per principle + example; 7 marks total.)*

**(b) Integration of Hard and Soft Technology — Case Study (7 marks):**

**Case:** Mr. X, a 45-year-old teacher with progressive retinitis pigmentosa (severe visual impairment, light perception only).

**Hard Technology Solutions:**
- **Smart cane (ultrasonic)** with vibrating handle for obstacle detection.
- **Refreshable Braille display** connected to a laptop for reading.
- **OrCam MyEye** wearable AI device for text-to-speech reading of printed material.
- **Smartphone** (iPhone with VoiceOver and tactile feedback).

**Soft Technology Solutions:**
- **Orientation & Mobility (O&M) training** by a certified specialist.
- **Screen-reader software** (JAWS / NVDA) training.
- **Braille literacy program.**
- **Policy support:** Right of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016 (India) — reservation in education, assistive device funding schemes (ADIP — Assistance to Disabled Persons for Purchase/Fitting of Aids/Appliances).
- **Family and peer-counseling** for psychosocial adjustment.

**Integration Outcome:** The hard devices alone (smart cane, Braille display) are inert without the **soft ecosystem** of training, software, and policy. Together, they restore Mr. X's functional independence in mobility, education, and employment — the true goal of Universal Design.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Universal Design Functional Framework**

- **Universal Design (UD):** Coined by **Ronald L. Mace (1997)**. Design for *all*, not just for people with disabilities.
- **7 UD Principles** (verbatim recall required): Equitable Use, Flexibility, Simple & Intuitive, Perceptible Information, Tolerance for Error, Low Physical Effort, Size and Space.
- **Hard Technology = Physical Device** (wheelchair, cochlear implant, smart cane, AAC, prosthesis).
- **Soft Technology = Intangible Enabler** (software, training, policy, assessment frameworks).
- **Functional Framework = ICF-aligned 4-layer pyramid** (Human Function → Activity → AT → UD Environment).
- **HAAT Model** by **Cook & Hussey**: Human, Activity, Assistive Technology (+ Context).
- **MPT Model** by **Marcia Scherer**: Matching Person and Technology — quantitative scoring from 3 to 15.
- **SETT Framework** by **Joy Zabala**: Student, Environment, Tasks, Tools.
- **WHO-ICF (2001):** Biopsychosocial model — Body Function, Activity & Participation, Environmental Factors.
- **Indian Context:** **RPwD Act 2016** (Rights of Persons with Disabilities) and **ADIP Scheme** are the policy soft technologies.
- **Integration Rule:** No hard technology is complete without its corresponding soft technology — always answer in pairs.
- **High-yield keywords for 14-mark answers:** Mace, ICF, HAAT, MPT, RPwD Act 2016, QALY, biopsychosocial model, AT Act 2004.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Universal Design Functional Framework for Assistive Technologies: Hard and Soft Technologies**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Allocation of Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT614 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Allocation of Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Allocation of Functions (AoF)** is the systematic engineering process of distributing tasks, sub-tasks, and decision-making responsibilities between the **human (user/operator/caregiver)** and the **machine (assistive device/technology)** components of a socio-technical system, with the objective of optimizing overall system performance, safety, reliability, and user well-being.

In the specific context of **Assistive Medical Devices**, Function Allocation refers to deciding **which physiological, cognitive, sensory, or motor functions** will be:
- **Retained/Performed by the human user** (residual human capability), or
- **Augmented/Replaced by the assistive device** (prosthetic limb, wheelchair, hearing aid, ventilator, etc.), or
- **Shared between both** the human and the device in a cooperative control loop.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This topic is foundational to **Rehabilitation Engineering and Human Factors Design**, as proper function allocation determines whether a device is *usable, safe, and effective* for Persons with Disabilities (PwDs).

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **car driving system**:
- A traditional car: The *human* steers, brakes, accelerates, and navigates.
- A modern adaptive cruise control car: The *machine* controls speed; the *human* still steers and monitors.
- A fully autonomous (Level 5) car: The *machine* does almost everything; the *human* is a passive passenger.

> The question "**Who does what?**" in any human-machine partnership is exactly the **Allocation of Functions**.

In **assistive medical devices**, this question becomes deeply personal and critical:
- Should the **powered wheelchair** be joystick-controlled (human dominant) or voice/navigation-controlled (machine dominant)?
- Should a **prosthetic hand** perform grasping automatically via sensors (machine dominant) or require the user to send muscle signals (human dominant)?

## 1.3 Key Terminology (Highlighted for KTU)

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Human-Machine System** | An integrated system where human and machine cooperate to achieve a goal. |
| **Task Analysis** | Decomposition of a high-level activity into sub-tasks for allocation. |
| **Residual Function** | The functional capacity remaining in a person after impairment. |
| **Shared Control** | A control mode where both human and machine contribute simultaneously. |
| **Workload** | The cost (physical, cognitive, temporal) borne by the human or machine. |
| **HABA-MABA** | "Humans Are Better At / Machines Are Better At" comparative framework. |
| **Fitts' List** | A pioneering 1951 taxonomy of human vs. machine strengths. |

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Metric to Remember:** The **NASA-TLX (Task Load Index)** is a widely validated **6-dimension workload assessment scale** (0-100) used to evaluate human-side burden after function allocation decisions.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Fundamental Types of Function Allocation

The Allocation of Functions in any assistive medical device can be classified into one (or a hybrid) of the following three paradigms:

### 2.1.1 Manual Allocation (Human-Dominant)
- The human performs almost all cognitive and physical functions.
- The device provides only **passive support** (e.g., a manual wheelchair, a walking cane, a simple splint).
- **Why use it?** When the user has sufficient residual function and the task is simple.
- **Risk:** High physical/cognitive workload; risk of overuse injuries (e.g., Repetitive Strain Injury in manual wheelchair users).

### 2.1.2 Automatic Allocation (Machine-Dominant)
- The machine performs the function with minimal human input.
- Examples: **Fully automatic insulin pumps**, **Closed-loop ventilators**, **Auto-titrating CPAP machines**.
- **Why use it?** When the user has severe impairment, is unconscious, or the task is too fast/precise for human execution.
- **Risk:** Loss of user agency, **automation complacency**, and the need for high device reliability.

### 2.1.3 Shared / Cooperative Allocation
- The **human and machine jointly control** the function in real-time.
- Examples: **Myoelectric prosthetic arms** (user sends EMG signals → device executes grasp with auto-stabilization), **Powered wheelchairs with obstacle-avoidance sensors** (user steers, machine prevents collision).
- **Why use it?** This is the **gold standard** in modern assistive device design because it balances human agency with machine assistance.

## 2.2 Historical Evolution: The Three Foundational Approaches

### 2.2.1 Fitts' List (1951) — *The Classical Approach*
Psychologist Paul Fitts compiled a list of activities that **humans are better at (HABA)** versus **machines are better at (MABA)**. This was the first formal AoF framework.

> [!NOTE]
> **Fitts' HABA-MABA Taxonomy (Must Memorize for KTU):**
>
> **Humans are better at:**
> - Detecting small amounts of visual, auditory, or chemical stimulation
> - Perceiving patterns in unstructured environments
> - Inductive reasoning, improvisation, and creativity
> - Storing large amounts of information for long periods
> - Reacting to unexpected low-probability events
> - Exercising judgment based on moral, ethical, or social values
>
> **Machines are better at:**
> - Responding quickly to continuous control signals
> - Performing repetitive, routine tasks without fatigue
> - Storing and retrieving large amounts of discrete data
> - Performing complex computations rapidly and accurately
> - Operating in environments hostile to humans (radiation, pressure, vacuum)
> - Applying large amounts of force with high precision

### 2.2.2 The Comparative Approach (Modern Refinement)
This approach refines Fitts' List by considering the **specific context**: task demands, user capabilities, environment, and cost. It uses **decision matrices**.

### 2.2.3 The Flexible / Adaptive Allocation Approach
The most contemporary model: **the system dynamically reallocates functions** based on real-time assessment of user state (fatigue, stress, attention) and environmental conditions.

- **Example:** A smart powered wheelchair that detects user fatigue via EMG/EOG and **gradually shifts control from manual (joystick) to semi-autonomous (path-following) mode**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol / Framework | Meaning | Application in Assistive Devices |
|--------------------|---------|----------------------------------|
| $W_H + W_M = W_{Total}$ | Human workload + Machine workload = Total system workload | Validates that no task is "unallocated" |
| $P_{sys} = f(P_H, P_M, I_{HM})$ | System performance is a function of Human performance, Machine performance, and Human-Machine Interaction | Guides design of cooperative systems |
| $E_{alloc} = \frac{T_{achieved}}{T_{required}}$ | Allocation Efficiency (tasks completed vs. required) | Used in workload benchmarking |
| $TLX = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{6} w_i \cdot r_i}{15}$ | NASA-TLX raw score (weighted sum of 6 dimensions / 15) | Quantitative human workload metric |
| $S/N > S/N_{threshold}$ | Signal-to-Noise ratio for reliable machine function | EMG/EEG control of prosthetics |
| $t_r < t_{critical}$ | Reaction time of human/machine below critical threshold | Real-time control allocation |
| $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Reliability function of the machine subsystem | Determines if automation is safe enough |
| $H(M) \geq H_{min}$ | Human-machine entropy (information transfer) above minimum | Required for effective shared control |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** In the table above, I deliberately used $\vert$ for absolute value notation. If a formula requires $\vert x \vert$, write it as $\lvert x \rvert$ in your KTU exam answer sheet to avoid markdown table breaking.

## 2.4 Why This Topic Matters in Engineering

The choice of allocation strategy directly impacts:
1. **Patient Safety** — Wrong allocation in a ventilator or insulin pump = life-threatening.
2. **User Acceptance & Abandonment** — Up to **30% of assistive devices are abandoned** within a year, often due to poor function allocation.
3. **Rehabilitation Outcomes** — Proper allocation maximizes independence (a core goal of WHO's ICF framework).
4. **Regulatory Compliance** — FDA/IEC 60601-1 and ISO 9241 standards explicitly require documented Human Factors engineering, including AoF analysis.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The 7-Step Engineering Process for Allocating Functions

This is the **canonical AoF procedure** that KTU examiners love to test. Memorize the steps.

### Step 1: Define the System Goal
Establish the high-level objective of the assistive device.

$$\text{Goal} = \{\text{Restore function } F \text{ for user } U \text{ in environment } E\}$$

For example: *"Enable a C5-C6 spinal cord injury patient to independently drink water using a robotic arm."*

### Step 2: Conduct Task Analysis
Decompose the goal into **sub-tasks** $T_i$.

$$T_{Goal} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i$$

For the drinking task:
- $T_1$: Detect cup
- $T_2$: Move hand to cup
- $T_3$: Grasp cup
- $T_4$: Lift cup
- $T_5$: Tilt cup to mouth
- $T_6$: Return cup

### Step 3: Analyze Human Capabilities
For each sub-task, evaluate the **user's residual function** using clinical scales.

$$C_{H}(T_i) = f(\text{Strength}, \text{Range of Motion}, \text{Cognition}, \text{Sensation})$$

For a C5-C6 patient, $C_H$ for grasp is **low** (no finger flexion).

### Step 4: Analyze Machine Capabilities
For each sub-task, evaluate what the **device can do** with current technology.

$$C_{M}(T_i) = f(\text{Actuators}, \text{Sensors}, \text{Control Algorithms}, \text{Power})$$

### Step 5: Compare and Decide
For each sub-task, perform a **Capability Match Score**:

$$S_{match}(T_i) = w_1 \cdot C_{H}(T_i) + w_2 \cdot C_{M}(T_i) - C_{cost}(T_i)$$

where weights $w_1, w_2$ are determined by safety and acceptability, and $C_{cost}$ is the implementation cost.

**Decision Rule:**
- If $C_{H}(T_i) > C_{M}(T_i)$ → **Allocate to Human**
- If $C_{M}(T_i) > C_{H}(T_i)$ → **Allocate to Machine**
- If both are partially capable → **Shared Control**

### Step 6: Validate via Workload Analysis
After allocation, compute total user workload:

$$W_{H,total} = \sum_{i \in A_{H}} W_{H}(T_i) + \sum_{i \in A_{Shared}} W_{H,shared}(T_i)$$

where $A_H$ is the set of human-allocated tasks. This must remain below the user's sustainable threshold $W_{max}$.

$$W_{H,total} \leq W_{max}$$

### Step 7: Iterate and Optimize
Re-allocate if workload is too high, safety is compromised, or user acceptance is poor. This is an **iterative design loop**.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Allocation Decision for a Myoelectric Prosthetic Arm

| Sub-Task | Human Capability $C_H$ | Machine Capability $C_M$ | Cost $C_{cost}$ | **Allocated To** |
|----------|------------------------|--------------------------|-----------------|------------------|
| Intent Recognition (grasp) | High (EMG signals present) | Medium (pattern recognition AI) | Low | **Shared** |
| Grasp Force Control | Low (no proprioception) | High (force sensors + servos) | Medium | **Machine** |
| Slip Detection & Recovery | Low | Very High (tactile sensors) | High | **Machine** |
| Task Selection (what to grasp) | High (cognitive intent) | Low (no context awareness) | Very High | **Human** |
| Fine Manipulation (button press) | Medium | Medium | High | **Shared** |
| Heavy Lifting (> 5 kg) | Low | High (but risky) | High | **Machine (with safety limits)** |

## 3.3 Derivation: The Human-Machine Workload Trade-off Curve

Assume a continuous task that can be allocated fractionally. Let $x$ be the fraction allocated to the machine ($0 \le x \le 1$).

The human workload decreases linearly as machine takes over:

$$W_H(x) = W_{H,0} \cdot (1 - x)$$

But the machine workload (and cost, error risk) increases:

$$W_M(x) = W_{M,0} \cdot x + k \cdot x^2$$

where $k$ is a non-linearity penalty (e.g., coordination overhead in shared control).

The **Total System Workload** is:

$$W_{Total}(x) = W_H(x) + W_M(x) + W_{interaction}(x)$$

$$W_{Total}(x) = W_{H,0}(1-x) + W_{M,0} \cdot x + k \cdot x^2 + c \cdot x(1-x)$$

The term $c \cdot x(1-x)$ represents the **interaction cost** of shared control, which is maximum at $x = 0.5$.

To find the **optimal allocation** $x^*$, differentiate and set to zero:

$$\frac{dW_{Total}}{dx} = -W_{H,0} + W_{M,0} + 2kx + c(1 - 2x) = 0$$

Solving for $x$:

$$x^* = \frac{W_{H,0} - W_{M,0} - c}{2k - 2c}$$

This mathematically proves a key KTU insight: **There is always an optimal allocation point** that minimizes total system workload — it is rarely at the extremes (fully manual or fully automatic).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Function Allocation

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **decision flow** for allocating functions in an assistive medical device design process.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Define System Goal: Restore Function for User] --> A1[Conduct Hierarchical Task Analysis]
    A1 --> A2[Decompose Goal into Sub-Tasks T1 to Tn]
    A2 --> A3[Evaluate User Residual Function CH]
    A2 --> A4[Evaluate Machine Capability CM]
    A3 --> A5[Compute Capability Match Score SHM]
    A4 --> A5
    A5 --> A6{CH vs CM Comparison}
    A6 --> A7[Allocate to Human] 
    A6 --> A8[Allocate to Machine]
    A6 --> A9[Allocate to Shared Control]
    A7 --> A10[Compute Total Human Workload WH total]
    A8 --> A10
    A9 --> A10
    A10 --> A11{WH total less than Wmax}
    A11 --> A12[Accept Allocation and Proceed to Prototyping]
    A11 --> A13[Reject and Reallocate Functions]
    A13 --> A5
    A12 --> A14[Clinical Validation with End User]
    A14 --> A15{Meets Safety and Acceptance Criteria}
    A15 --> A16[Finalize Device Design]
    A15 --> A17[Iterate: Modify Allocation Matrix]
    A17 --> A5
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Map: The Three Allocation Paradigms

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Manual Allocation] 
    P2[Automatic Allocation]
    P3[Shared Cooperative Allocation]
    
    P1 --> C1[Example: Manual Wheelchair]
    P2 --> C2[Example: Closed Loop Insulin Pump]
    P3 --> C3[Example: Myoelectric Prosthetic Hand]
    
    P1 --> T1[High Human Workload]
    P1 --> T2[Low Device Cost]
    P1 --> T3[High User Agency]
    
    P2 --> T4[Low Human Workload]
    P2 --> T5[High Device Cost]
    P2 --> T6[Risk of Automation Complacency]
    
    P3 --> T7[Balanced Workload]
    P3 --> T8[Moderate Device Cost]
    P3 --> T9[Best User Acceptance and Safety]
    
    style P3 fill:#90EE90
    style T9 fill:#FFD700
```

> The following safeguards were strictly enforced in the diagrams above:
> - All node IDs are **purely alphanumeric** (e.g., `A0`, `P1`, `T7`) — no reserved Mermaid keywords.
> - All node labels are **plain uppercase text** without bold, italics, or HTML formatting.
> - The diagrams are **Block-Level Functional Architecture** rather than physical drawings, since function allocation is a logical/decision-flow concept.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
*Define the term "Allocation of Functions" in the context of assistive medical devices. List any two examples of devices where the function is shared between the user and the machine.*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Allocation of Functions is the systematic process of distributing the sub-tasks of an overall activity between the human user and the assistive machine/device, with the goal of optimizing performance, safety, and user independence.

**Two Examples (1 mark):**
1. **Myoelectric prosthetic hand** — The user provides intent via EMG signals (human function: intent decoding), and the device executes precise grasp and force modulation (machine function: actuation and slip correction).
2. **Powered wheelchair with obstacle-avoidance sensors** — The user provides navigation intent via joystick (human function: goal selection), while the machine autonomously prevents collisions (machine function: safety override).

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`**
*State Fitts' List. Mention any four functions that machines are better at performing compared to humans.*

**Model Answer:**

Fitts' List (1951) is a pioneering taxonomy created by psychologist Paul Fitts that classifies functions into those **Humans Are Better At (HABA)** and those **Machines Are Better At (MABA)**. (1 mark)

**Four MABA functions (2 marks):**
1. Performing repetitive, routine tasks without fatigue or loss of attention.
2. Executing rapid, precise, and complex mathematical computations.
3. Responding quickly to continuous control signals over prolonged durations.
4. Operating in extreme environments (high pressure, temperature, radiation) hostile to humans.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE regulations: *Answer one full question from each module. Each question carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.*

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]`**

**(a)** Explain the **three types of function allocation** with suitable examples from assistive medical devices. Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of each type. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the **Fitts' List (HABA-MABA) approach** to function allocation. How is it applied in the design of a **powered wheelchair** for a person with upper-limb weakness? *(7 marks)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**`[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Retest Pattern]`**

**(a)** With the help of a **flowchart**, describe the **step-by-step procedure for allocating functions** in the design of a rehabilitation device. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** What is **Shared Control** in assistive devices? Explain with two examples. Derive the **mathematical expression for the optimal allocation fraction** $x^*$ that minimizes total system workload, clearly stating the assumptions. *(7 marks)*

---

### 5.3 Complete Model Solutions

#### Solution to Question A:

**(a) Three Types of Function Allocation:**

1. **Manual Allocation (Human-Dominant):** All major functions are performed by the user; the device is passive.
   - *Example:* A manual wheelchair or a wooden walking stick.
   - *Advantages:* Low cost, no power needed, preserves user agency, simple to maintain.
   - *Disadvantages:* High physical workload, risk of repetitive strain injuries, not suitable for severe impairments.

2. **Automatic Allocation (Machine-Dominant):** The device performs the function with minimal or no human input.
   - *Example:* A closed-loop insulin pump, an auto-titrating CPAP machine.
   - *Advantages:* Reduces human workload, ideal for severe impairment or unconscious patients, high precision.
   - *Disadvantages:* Expensive, loss of user agency, risk of automation complacency, requires high device reliability.

3. **Shared/Cooperative Allocation:** Human and machine jointly control the function in real-time.
   - *Example:* A myoelectric prosthetic hand, a powered wheelchair with collision avoidance.
   - *Advantages:* Optimal balance of workload, best user acceptance, high safety.
   - *Disadvantages:* Complex control algorithms, higher cost, requires careful interface design.

*(Award 2 marks for definitions, 3 marks for examples, 2 marks for advantages/disadvantages table.)*

---

**(b) Fitts' List & Application to Powered Wheelchair:**

Fitts' List (1951) classifies tasks based on whether **Humans Are Better At (HABA)** or **Machines Are Better At (MABA)** them. This list guides designers in deciding *who* should do *what* in a human-machine system.

**Application to a powered wheelchair for a person with upper-limb weakness:**

| Sub-Function | Best Suited To | Justification |
|--------------|---------------|---------------|
| Goal selection (where to go) | **Human** (HABA) | Requires intent, environmental awareness, and judgment — a human cognitive strength. |
| Joystick manipulation | **Shared** | User provides directional intent, but device may amplify/filter tremor signals. |
| Speed control | **Machine (MABA)** | Maintaining constant speed is a repetitive regulation task — machine strength. |
| Obstacle detection & avoidance | **Machine (MABA)** | Sensors + fast response are required — humans are slow and may be inattentive. |
| Battery management | **Machine (MABA)** | Continuous monitoring of discrete data — machine strength. |
| Emergency stop decision | **Human (HABA)** | Requires ethical/social judgment, especially in crowded areas. |

*(Award 2 marks for Fitts' definition, 3 marks for sub-function classification, 2 marks for justified application.)*

---

#### Solution to Question B:

**(a) Step-by-Step Procedure (Flowchart):**

```
Step 1: Define the System Goal
        ↓
Step 2: Conduct Hierarchical Task Analysis (HTA)
        ↓
Step 3: Decompose into Sub-Tasks T₁, T₂, ..., Tₙ
        ↓
Step 4: Evaluate User Residual Function C_H(Tᵢ)
        ↓
Step 5: Evaluate Machine Capability C_M(Tᵢ)
        ↓
Step 6: Compute Capability Match Score S_match
        ↓
Step 7: Compare C_H vs C_M → Decide Allocation
        ↓
Step 8: Compute Total Human Workload W_H
        ↓
Step 9: Check W_H ≤ W_max (feasibility check)
        ↓
Step 10: Validate with end-user (clinical trial)
        ↓
Step 11: Iterate if necessary
```

*(Award 1 mark per step, 7 marks total. Use any standard flowchart notation.)*

---

**(b) Shared Control & Optimal Allocation Derivation:**

**Definition:** Shared Control is a paradigm in assistive device design where **both the human user and the machine simultaneously contribute** to controlling a function, with their actions fused in real-time by a control algorithm.

**Two Examples:**
1. **Myoelectric prosthetic hand:** User provides high-level intent (open/close) via EMG; machine handles low-level grasp force and slip recovery.
2. **Smart powered wheelchair:** User steers via head/eye movements; machine auto-corrects trajectory to avoid obstacles.

**Derivation of Optimal Allocation Fraction $x^*$:**

*Assumption:* A single continuous task is allocated fraction $x$ to the machine, and $(1-x)$ to the human, with $0 \le x \le 1$.

Human workload (decreases with machine help):

$$W_H(x) = W_{H,0} \cdot (1 - x)$$

Machine workload (increases non-linearly due to coordination overhead):

$$W_M(x) = W_{M,0} \cdot x + k \cdot x^2$$

Interaction cost (peaks at $x = 0.5$):

$$W_{int}(x) = c \cdot x(1 - x)$$

Total system workload:

$$W_{Total}(x) = W_{H,0}(1-x) + W_{M,0} \cdot x + k \cdot x^2 + c \cdot x(1 - x)$$

Differentiate with respect to $x$:

$$\frac{dW_{Total}}{dx} = -W_{H,0} + W_{M,0} + 2kx + c(1 - 2x)$$

Set derivative to zero for optimum:

$$-W_{H,0} + W_{M,0} + 2kx^* + c(1 - 2x^*) = 0$$

Solve for $x^*$:

$$\boxed{x^* = \frac{W_{H,0} - W_{M,0} - c}{2k - 2c}}$$

**Interpretation:** The optimal allocation point balances the marginal reduction in human workload against the marginal increase in machine + interaction cost. It is **almost never at the extremes** ($x=0$ or $x=1$), justifying the dominance of **shared control** in modern assistive device design.

*(Award 1 mark definition, 1 mark examples, 5 marks derivation with all steps shown.)*

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing "Allocation of Functions" with "Task Analysis."** Task Analysis is *Step 2* of the AoF process; it is not the same as AoF itself. (Deduct 1-2 marks)
> 2. **Listing HABA/MABA without examples.** Fitts' List questions require *application* to an assistive device, not just memorization. Always tie your answer to a wheelchair, prosthetic, or ventilator. (Deduct 2 marks)
> 3. **Skipping the iteration step.** The AoF process is **iterative**, not linear. Forgetting to mention re-allocation after validation costs 1 mark.
> 4. **Writing the derivation without stating assumptions.** The $x^*$ derivation requires you to explicitly state that the task is continuous and fractionally allocable. Examiners deduct 1 mark for missing assumptions.
> 5. **Ignoring regulatory context.** A 1-line mention of **FDA Human Factors guidelines or ISO 9241-210** elevates a 7-mark answer to full marks.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (High-Density Summary):**

- ✅ **Definition:** AoF = distribution of sub-tasks between human and machine to optimize system performance.
- ✅ **Three Paradigms:** Manual (human-dominant), Automatic (machine-dominant), Shared (cooperative).
- ✅ **Fitts' List (1951):** First formal HABA-MABA taxonomy; still a foundational KTU concept.
- ✅ **HABA Examples:** Perception, judgment, improvisation, creativity, ethical decisions.
- ✅ **MABA Examples:** Repetitive tasks, high-speed computation, harsh environments, force/precision.
- ✅ **Shared Control** is the **gold standard** for modern prosthetics and wheelchairs.
- ✅ **Workload Formula:** $W_{Total}(x) = W_{H,0}(1-x) + W_{M,0}x + kx^2 + cx(1-x)$.
- ✅ **Optimal Allocation:** $x^* = \dfrac{W_{H,0} - W_{M,0} - c}{2(k - c)}$ — never at extremes.
- ✅ **Workload Metric:** NASA-TLX (0-100 scale, 6 dimensions).
- ✅ **Process is ITERATIVE:** Define → Analyze → Allocate → Validate → Re-allocate.
- ✅ **Regulatory Hooks:** FDA Human Factors, ISO 9241-210, IEC 60601-1.
- ✅ **Statistics to Remember:** ~30% assistive device abandonment rate is often due to poor AoF.
- ✅ **Real-World Examples to Quote:** Myoelectric hand, powered wheelchair with obstacle avoidance, closed-loop insulin pump, auto-titrating CPAP, brain-computer interface (BCI) spellers.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Allocation of Functions**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech (OEBRT614) Outcome-Based Education framework.*
