topics.title: Hard Assistive Technologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT614 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hard Assistive Technologies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**Hard Assistive Technologies (HAT)** are defined as **tangible, physical, electromechanical, or biomechanical devices** designed and engineered to restore, augment, or substitute functional capabilities of individuals with disabilities, injuries, or age-related impairments. As per the **World Health Organization (WHO)** classification, these devices fall under the umbrella of "Assistive Products" that are **non-consumable, durable, and require physical interaction** to deliver therapeutic or rehabilitative outcomes.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (Course Code: OEBRT614)**, *Hard Assistive Technologies* are formally categorized as a subset of Assistive Medical Devices (AMDs) whose operation depends on **mechanical structures, electronic circuits, sensors, actuators, and materials engineering**, in contrast to *Soft Assistive Technologies* (software-driven solutions like mobile applications and screen readers).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Examination-Ready):**
> *“Hard Assistive Technologies are physical devices or equipment — ranging from simple mechanical aids like walking sticks to complex robotic prosthetics — that enable persons with disabilities to perform activities of daily living (ADL), enhance mobility, support communication, and improve overall quality of life.”*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Body-as-Machine" Perspective

Imagine the human body as a **complex mechanical factory** with gears (joints), conveyor belts (limbs), control panels (nervous system), and output stations (hands/feet). When one machine part fails, production halts. **Hard Assistive Technologies act as external replacement parts or auxiliary support structures** that attach to the body or environment, allowing the "factory" to continue producing — i.e., letting the user walk, hear, see, or grasp objects.

| Analogy Element | Real-World Biomedical Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Worn-out gear | Damaged joint → **Prosthetic limb** |
| Broken conveyor belt | Amputated arm → **Artificial arm/hand** |
| Faulty control panel | Motor neuron disease → **Powered wheelchair** |
| Damaged sensor | Hearing loss → **Cochlear implant** |
| Weak structural pillar | Spinal injury → **Orthotic brace** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Distinguishing Feature:** The "HARD" prefix emphasizes **physical, material-based engineering**, as opposed to virtual/algorithmic solutions. A wheelchair is *hard*; a navigation app is *soft*.

### 1.3 Standard Classification Metrics (Bold Constants & Parameters)

- **Ramp Gradient Standard (ADA Compliance):** **1:12 ratio** (4.8° slope) for public wheelchair access
- **Average Human Gait Cycle:** **1.0 – 1.4 seconds** per stride
- **Wheelchair Push Frequency:** **1 cycle per 2 seconds** during normal propulsion
- **Typical Prosthetic Socket Pressure Tolerance:** **< 200 kPa** to prevent skin breakdown
- **WHO Estimate:** Approximately **1 billion people** worldwide need assistive technology (10% global population)

### 1.4 Why Hard Assistive Technologies Matter — Engineering Rationale

The engineering necessity for HATs arises from three converging societal factors:
1. **Demographic Shift** — Global population over 60 years expected to double by **2050**.
2. **Disability Prevalence** — Approximately **15% of world population** lives with some form of disability (WHO 2023).
3. **Functional Demand** — A direct link exists between assistive device access and **economic participation**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Decomposition of Hard Assistive Technologies

Hard Assistive Technologies can be systematically broken down into **five operational pillars**, each addressing a specific domain of human function:

### Pillar 1: **Mobility Aids** (Locomotor Support Systems)
- **Manual Wheelchairs** — Lever-driven, three or four-wheel configurations
- **Powered Wheelchairs** — Battery-driven DC motor actuation with joystick interface
- **Smart Wheelchairs** — Sensor-fused navigation with obstacle detection
- **Walking Sticks / Canes** — Single-point, quad-base, or seat-convertible
- **Crutches & Walkers** — Axillary, elbow (Lofstrand), forearm, and rollator variants

### Pillar 2: **Prosthetic Devices** (Replacement of Missing Limbs)
- **Upper Limb Prosthetics** — Cosmetic, body-powered, myoelectric, and bionic hands
- **Lower Limb Prosthetics** — Transfemoral (above-knee), transtibial (below-knee), and hip disarticulation
- **Specialized Prosthetics** — Sports blades (carbon fiber), swimming fins, and musical instrument adaptations

### Pillar 3: **Orthotic Devices** (Support of Existing Limbs)
- **Spinal Orthoses** — Cervical collars, thoracolumbar-sacral orthoses (TLSO)
- **Lower Extremity Orthoses** — Ankle-foot orthosis (AFO), knee-ankle-foot orthosis (KAFO)
- **Upper Extremity Orthoses** — Wrist-hand orthoses for carpal tunnel support

### Pillar 4: **Sensory Substitution Devices** (Restoring Input Channels)
- **Cochlear Implants** — Electrode array stimulating auditory nerve
- **Behind-The-Ear (BTE) Hearing Aids** — Amplification of acoustic signals
- **Braille Displays & Refreshable Tactile Interfaces** — Conversion of text to touch
- **Tactile Vision Substitution Systems (TVSS)** — Translating images to vibration patterns

### Pillar 5: **Daily Living Aids & Environmental Modifications**
- **ADL Tools** — Adaptive cutlery, dressing sticks, button hooks, reachers
- **Architectural Modifications** — Grab bars, ramps, accessible door handles
- **Assistive Robotics** — Wheelchair-mounted robotic arms (e.g., Kinova Jaco)

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table)

> [!NOTE]
> **Markdown Syntax Safeguard:** Absolute values are rendered using `\vert` to prevent table-breaking.

| **S.No** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Application Area** | **Units** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | $P = F \cdot v$ | Mechanical power output during push | Wheelchair propulsion analysis | **Watts (W)** |
| 2 | $\tau = F \cdot r$ | Torque required to rotate wheel | Wheelchair / prosthetic joint torque | **N·m** |
| 3 | $\eta = \frac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}} \times 100$ | Energy efficiency of HAT | Prosthetic energy return analysis | **%** |
| 4 | $\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{h}{L}\right)$ | Ramp slope angle | Accessibility engineering | **Degrees (°)** |
| 5 | $v = \frac{d}{t}$ | Average linear velocity of limb | Gait analysis | **m/s** |
| 6 | $f_{\text{step}} = \frac{1}{T_{\text{stride}}}$ | Step frequency | Mobility cadence analysis | **Hz** |
| 7 | $E_{\text{stored}} = \frac{1}{2} k x^{2}$ | Spring energy storage in prosthetic foot | Carbon-fiber blade dynamics | **Joules (J)** |
| 8 | $MA = \frac{L_{\text{effort}}}{L_{\text{load}}}$ | Mechanical advantage | Body-powered prosthetic cable | **Unitless** |
| 9 | $P_{\text{interface}} = \frac{F_{\text{axial}}}{A_{\text{socket}}}$ | Pressure at prosthetic socket | Tissue-interface safety | **Pa (N/m²)** |
| 10 | $R_{\text{resist}} = \rho \cdot \frac{L}{A}$ | Fluidic resistance (hydraulic damper) | Prosthetic knee dampers | **N·s/m** |

## 2.3 Engineering Utility and Societal Impact

Hard Assistive Technologies are **the cornerstone of rehabilitation engineering**. Their design integrates:

- **Biomechanics** — To replicate natural human motion (kinematics & kinetics of gait)
- **Materials Science** — Titanium alloys, carbon-fiber composites, biocompatible polymers
- **Control Engineering** — PID controllers, EMG signal processing for myoelectric limbs
- **Sensor Fusion** — IMU + force sensors for closed-loop prosthetic feedback
- **Additive Manufacturing** — 3D-printed custom sockets for patient-specific fitting

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Concept:** The difference between **"Restore"** and **"Substitute"** is repeatedly tested.
> - *Restore* → Orthotic device supports an existing weak limb.
> - *Substitute* → Prosthetic device replaces a missing limb.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Wheelchair Ramp Slope Compliance (ADA Standard)

**Problem Statement:** A public building entrance must accommodate wheelchair access. If the rise (vertical height) is **0.6 m**, determine the minimum horizontal run length required to comply with the **ADA 1:12 standard**, and compute the corresponding ramp angle.

### Step 1: Recall the ADA Definition
The ADA (Americans with Disabilities Act) mandates that for every **1 unit of vertical rise**, there must be **12 units of horizontal run** (ratio 1:12).

$$
\text{Ratio}_{\text{ADA}} = \frac{\text{Rise}}{\text{Run}} = \frac{1}{12}
$$

### Step 2: Substitute the Given Rise Value

Let $h$ = rise, $L$ = run length.

$$
\frac{h}{L} = \frac{1}{12} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{0.6\,\text{m}}{L} = \frac{1}{12}
$$

### Step 3: Solve Algebraically for $L$

$$
L = 0.6 \times 12 = 7.2\,\text{m}
$$

### Step 4: Compute the Ramp Angle $\theta$

Using the inverse tangent of the slope:

$$
\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{h}{L}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{0.6}{7.2}\right)
$$

$$
\theta = \tan^{-1}(0.0833) \approx 4.76^{\circ}
$$

### Step 5: Result Verification

A slope of 1:12 gives approximately **4.8°**, matching the **ADA standard** of less than 5° for unassisted wheelchair access.

> **Result:** $L = 7.2\,\text{m}$ and $\theta \approx 4.76^{\circ}$ ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Mechanical Power Output During Wheelchair Propulsion

**Problem Statement:** A user propels a manual wheelchair by exerting a force of **120 N** on each push rim at an average linear rim velocity of **1.5 m/s**. The rear wheel radius is **0.3 m**. Calculate the total mechanical power output, the torque on each wheel, and the energy expended over **10 minutes**.

### Step 1: Identify the Governing Equations

Total power is the sum of contributions from both rear wheels:

$$
P_{\text{total}} = 2 \cdot P_{\text{wheel}} = 2 \cdot (F \cdot v)
$$

### Step 2: Compute the Power on a Single Wheel

$$
P_{\text{wheel}} = F \cdot v = 120 \times 1.5 = 180\,\text{W}
$$

### Step 3: Compute the Total Power (Both Wheels)

$$
P_{\text{total}} = 2 \times 180 = 360\,\text{W}
$$

### Step 4: Calculate the Torque on Each Wheel

$$
\tau = F \cdot r = 120 \times 0.3 = 36\,\text{N·m}
$$

### Step 5: Calculate the Energy Expended in 10 Minutes

Convert time to seconds: $t = 10 \times 60 = 600\,\text{s}$.

$$
E = P_{\text{total}} \cdot t = 360 \times 600 = 216{,}000\,\text{J} = 216\,\text{kJ}
$$

### Step 6: Result Summary

| Quantity | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Power per wheel | $P_{\text{wheel}}$ | 180 | **W** |
| Total power | $P_{\text{total}}$ | 360 | **W** |
| Torque per wheel | $\tau$ | 36 | **N·m** |
| Energy in 10 min | $E$ | 216 | **kJ** |

> **Insight:** A power output of **360 W** during wheelchair propulsion is comparable to moderate cycling, illustrating why manual wheelchairs are physically demanding and why powered wheelchairs are essential for long-duration users.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Spring Energy Storage in a Carbon-Fiber Prosthetic Foot Blade

**Problem Statement:** A carbon-fiber running blade has an effective spring constant of $k = 35{,}000\,\text{N/m}$. During a stride, the blade compresses by **x = 0.012 m** (12 mm). Compute the stored elastic energy and the peak reaction force experienced by the user.

### Step 1: Apply Hooke's Law for Peak Force

$$
F_{\text{peak}} = k \cdot x = 35{,}000 \times 0.012
$$

$$
F_{\text{peak}} = 420\,\text{N}
$$

### Step 2: Apply the Elastic Potential Energy Formula

$$
E_{\text{stored}} = \frac{1}{2} k x^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \times 35{,}000 \times (0.012)^{2}
$$

$$
E_{\text{stored}} = \frac{1}{2} \times 35{,}000 \times 0.000144
$$

$$
E_{\text{stored}} = 2.52\,\text{J}
$$

### Step 3: Compute Energy Return Efficiency

Assuming a typical blade returns **85%** of stored energy during push-off:

$$
E_{\text{returned}} = 0.85 \times 2.52 = 2.142\,\text{J}
$$

> **Result:** $F_{\text{peak}} = 420\,\text{N}$ and $E_{\text{stored}} = 2.52\,\text{J}$ ✓
> This energy return mechanism is what makes carbon-fiber blades efficient in sprinting and long-jump Paralympic events.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Tree of Hard Assistive Technologies

The following Mermaid **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** maps the complete taxonomy of HATs as prescribed under the **WHO Essential Assistive Products List (EAPL)** and the KTU 2024 syllabus:

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[HARD ASSISTIVE TECHNOLOGIES] --> B[MOBILITY AIDS]
    A --> C[PROSTHETIC DEVICES]
    A --> D[ORTHOTIC DEVICES]
    A --> E[SENSORY AIDS]
    A --> F[ADL AND ENVIRONMENTAL AIDS]

    B --> B1[Manual Wheelchair]
    B --> B2[Powered Wheelchair]
    B --> B3[Smart Wheelchair]
    B --> B4[Crutches and Walkers]
    B --> B5[Canes and Walking Sticks]

    C --> C1[Upper Limb Prosthesis]
    C --> C2[Lower Limb Prosthesis]
    C --> C3[Myoelectric Prosthesis]
    C --> C4[Cosmetic Prosthesis]
    C --> C5[Sports Blade]

    D --> D1[Spinal Orthosis TLSO]
    D --> D2[Ankle Foot Orthosis AFO]
    D --> D3[Knee Ankle Foot Orthosis KAFO]
    D --> D4[Wrist Hand Orthosis WHO]

    E --> E1[Hearing Aids BTE ITE]
    E --> E2[Cochlear Implant]

    F --> F1[Adaptive Cutlery]
    F --> F2[Grab Bars and Ramps]
    F --> F3[Reachers and Dressing Aids]
    F --> F4[Wheelchair Mounted Robotic Arm]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of a Myoelectric Prosthetic Hand

The diagram below illustrates the **closed-loop control architecture** of a modern myoelectric (bionic) hand — a frequently tested KTU 2024 concept:

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[Residual Muscle EMG Signal] --> A2[Surface Electrode Sensor]
    A2 --> A3[Signal Amplifier and Filter]
    A3 --> A4[Microcontroller DSP]
    A4 --> A5[Motor Driver Circuit]
    A5 --> A6[DC Servo Motor]
    A6 --> A7[Prosthetic Hand Fingers]
    A7 --> A8[Tactile Force Feedback Sensor]
    A8 --> A4
    A4 --> A9[User Visual and Audio Feedback]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The double-arrow on the right (tactile feedback back to the controller) represents a **closed-loop biofeedback system**, a key differentiator between modern bionic limbs and older open-loop prosthetic devices.

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Cochlear Implant Signal Pathway

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[External Microphone] --> S2[Speech Processor DSP]
    S2 --> S3[Transmitter Coil]
    S3 --> S4[Radio Frequency RF Link]
    S4 --> S5[Implanted Receiver Stimulator]
    S5 --> S6[Electrode Array Cochlea]

```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

> **KTU Valuation Note:** Part A questions test *Recall (L1)* and *Comprehension (L2)* cognitive levels. Answers should be 3 to 4 sentences with proper technical terminology.

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]
**Define Hard Assistive Technologies. Differentiate between Prosthetic and Orthotic devices with one example each.** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

Hard Assistive Technologies (HATs) are **tangible, physical devices** designed to assist individuals with disabilities in performing daily activities, restoring or substituting lost bodily functions. They are physically constructed using mechanical, electronic, and biomaterial engineering principles.

- **Prosthetic Device:** A device that **replaces or substitutes** a missing body part. *Example:* A transtibial (below-knee) artificial leg for an amputee.
- **Orthotic Device:** A device that **supports, aligns, or corrects** the function of an existing body part. *Example:* An Ankle-Foot Orthosis (AFO) used by patients with foot drop.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Many students use the terms "prosthetic" and "orthotic" interchangeably. Remember: *Prosthetic = Replaces a missing limb*, *Orthotic = Supports an existing limb*. **Marks lost: 1 mark per wrong usage.**

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**List and briefly explain any three types of wheelchairs used as mobility assistive devices.** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Manual Wheelchair** — A self-propelled chair operated by the user pushing on circular hand rims attached to large rear wheels. It is low-cost, lightweight, and requires upper-body strength.
2. **Powered Wheelchair** — A battery-driven wheelchair using DC motors and a joystick controller. It is suitable for users with limited upper-body strength or chronic fatigue.
3. **Smart Wheelchair** — A sensor-equipped powered wheelchair that integrates **ultrasonic/IR obstacle detection, GPS navigation**, and AI-driven path planning for users with severe motor or cognitive impairments.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (Choose ANY ONE)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Internal choice means students pick **ONE full question (either A or B)** and answer both sub-parts.

---

### **Question A. [14 Marks]** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]

**(a)** Classify the various types of **prosthetic limbs** with suitable diagrams. Explain in detail the working principle of a **myoelectric prosthetic hand** with a neat block diagram. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** With the help of neat sketches, explain the **functional components of a cochlear implant**. Describe how it differs from a conventional hearing aid in terms of operation and clinical indication. *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

Prosthetic limbs are classified based on the **level of amputation** and the **method of actuation**.

**Classification of Prosthetic Limbs:**

| Category | Description | Example |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Upper Limb** | Replaces arm/hand | Myoelectric hand, cosmetic glove |
| **Lower Limb** | Replaces leg/foot | Transtibial, transfemoral prostheses |
| **Body-Powered** | Uses cables & harness | Hook or hand-operated terminal device |
| **Externally Powered** | Battery-driven motors | Myoelectric, bionic hands |
| **Cosmetic** | Passive appearance | Silicone finger or hand |
| **Sports Prosthesis** | High-performance | Carbon-fiber running blade |

**Working of a Myoelectric Prosthetic Hand (7 Steps):**

1. **Signal Acquisition:** Surface electrodes placed on the residual limb skin detect **electromyography (EMG)** signals generated during muscle contraction.
2. **Signal Conditioning:** The raw EMG is filtered (band-pass 20–500 Hz) and amplified.
3. **Feature Extraction:** Microcontroller extracts features like RMS amplitude or frequency content.
4. **Pattern Recognition:** Trained classifier (e.g., LDA or SVM) maps patterns to intended hand gestures.
5. **Motor Control:** Command signals drive DC servo motors via H-bridge motor drivers.
6. **Mechanical Actuation:** Motors operate fingers through tendon cables or gear trains.
7. **Feedback:** Tactile/force sensors provide grip-force awareness to the user.

> **Diagrammatic Reference:** See SECTION 4.2 above for the complete closed-loop control architecture.

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Functional Components of a Cochlear Implant:**

A cochlear implant is a **neural prosthetic** that directly stimulates the auditory nerve, bypassing damaged hair cells. Its components are split into **external** and **internal** parts.

**External Components:**
- **Microphone** — Captures ambient sound
- **Speech Processor (DSP unit)** — Decomposes sound into frequency bands and extracts features
- **Transmitter Coil** — Sends encoded signals via radio-frequency (RF) link

**Internal (Surgically Implanted) Components:**
- **Receiver-Stimulator** — Decodes RF signals into electrical pulses
- **Electrode Array** — 12 to 22 electrodes inserted into the **cochlear scala tympani** along the tonotopic axis

**Working Mechanism:**

1. Sound is captured → digitized → decomposed into frequency channels
2. Each channel corresponds to a specific cochlear location (basal = high frequency, apical = low frequency)
3. Electrical pulses are delivered to the corresponding electrode
4. The auditory nerve is stimulated, and the brain perceives sound

**Cochlear Implant vs. Conventional Hearing Aid:**

| Parameter | Hearing Aid | Cochlear Implant |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Mechanism | Acoustic **amplification** | Direct **electrical stimulation** of nerve |
| Indication | Mild to moderate sensorineural loss | Severe to profound loss |
| Surgical | No (external) | Yes (implanted) |
| Signal Path | Sound → Ear → Brain | Sound → Processor → Nerve → Brain |
| Cost (approx.) | ₹5,000–₹2,00,000 | ₹5,00,000–₹20,00,000 |

> **Diagrammatic Reference:** See SECTION 4.3 for the signal pathway block diagram.

---

### **Question B. [14 Marks]** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]

**(a)** Explain the **engineering classification of mobility aids**. Discuss the design considerations and ADA accessibility standards for wheelchair ramp construction with a solved numerical example. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Describe the **various types of orthotic devices** used in rehabilitation engineering. With a neat diagram, explain the biomechanics of an **Ankle-Foot Orthosis (AFO)** and its role in correcting foot drop. *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Engineering Classification of Mobility Aids:**

| Class | Examples | Power Source |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Single-Point Support** | Walking sticks, canes | None |
| **Multi-Point Support** | Crutches (axillary, elbow), walkers, rollators | None / manual |
| **Seated Mobility** | Manual wheelchairs, transit chairs | User-powered |
| **Powered Mobility** | Powered wheelchairs, scooters | Battery (DC motor) |
| **Autonomous Mobility** | Smart wheelchairs with sensors | Battery + AI |

**Design Considerations for Wheelchair Ramps:**

1. **Slope** — Maximum 1:12 (ADA) for new construction; 1:8 allowed only for very short rises
2. **Width** — Minimum **36 inches (915 mm)** clear width
3. **Surface** — Non-slip, level landings at top and bottom
4. **Handrails** — Required on both sides for rises above 150 mm
5. **Edge Protection** — Curb or barrier to prevent wheel slip-off

**Solved Numerical Example:**

**Given:** Rise $h = 0.9\,\text{m}$; ADA ratio 1:12
**Find:** Required run length $L$ and ramp angle $\theta$.

$$
L = 12 \times h = 12 \times 0.9 = 10.8\,\text{m}
$$

$$
\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{0.9}{10.8}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.0833) \approx 4.76^{\circ}
$$

> **Result:** $L = 10.8\,\text{m}$, $\theta \approx 4.76^{\circ}$ — compliant with ADA standards. ✓

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Classification of Orthotic Devices:**

| Body Region | Orthosis Type | Clinical Use |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Cervical Spine** | Cervical Collar (CO) | Whiplash, post-surgery |
| **Thoracolumbar** | TLSO (Boston, Milwaukee brace) | Scoliosis |
| **Hip-Knee-Ankle** | HKAFO | Polio, spina bifida |
| **Knee-Ankle-Foot** | KAFO | Quadriceps weakness |
| **Ankle-Foot** | AFO (solid, hinged, posterior spring) | Foot drop, cerebral palsy |
| **Wrist-Hand** | WHO / Wrist Splint | Carpal tunnel, arthritis |

**Biomechanics of Ankle-Foot Orthosis (AFO):**

The AFO is an **external brace** spanning the posterior calf to the foot. It is typically fabricated from **polypropylene** or **carbon-fiber composites** and worn inside the shoe.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[Leg] --> A2[Upper Strut Calf Section]
    A2 --> A3[Hinge or Solid Junction]
    A3 --> A4[Foot Plate]
    A4 --> A5[Shoe]
    A2 -.-> A6[Tibialis Anterior Weak Foot Drop]
    A3 -.-> A7[Provides Dorsiflexion Assist]
```

**Role in Foot Drop Correction:**

Foot drop is the inability to dorsiflex the foot during the swing phase of gait, causing the toe to drag. A **posterior leaf-spring AFO** holds the foot at **90°** and provides a **passive dorsiflexion moment** during swing, ensuring a smooth and safe gait.

The mechanical moment provided by the AFO is:

$$
M_{\text{AFO}} = k_{\text{brace}} \cdot \theta_{\text{deflection}}
$$

where $k_{\text{brace}}$ is the effective stiffness of the polypropylene and $\theta_{\text{deflection}}$ is the angular displacement from neutral position.

> **Result:** AFO improves **gait velocity, reduces energy expenditure**, and **prevents falls** in patients with foot drop.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Never** write "prosthetic" when you mean "orthotic" — this single-word error costs **1 to 2 marks**.
> 2. **Always** include the relevant **block diagram** in 14-mark questions — diagrams carry **2 to 3 marks** as per KTU valuation key.
> 3. **Show all numerical steps** — partial marking demands intermediate working; skipping steps forfeits **2 marks** in 7-mark calculation sub-parts.
> 4. **Mention units** in every derived quantity — missing units in formula-based answers cost **0.5 mark per quantity**.
> 5. For myoelectric questions, **explicitly name the electrodes and DSP** — generic terms like "sensor" or "processor" lose **1 mark**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> **Final high-density summary for last-minute KTU revision.**

- ✅ **Hard Assistive Technologies (HATs)** = *physical* devices that *restore, augment, or substitute* human function.
- ✅ **Prosthetic** = **Replaces** a *missing* limb → *Example:* Transtibial prosthesis.
- ✅ **Orthotic** = **Supports** an *existing* limb → *Example:* AFO brace.
- ✅ **Five Pillars of HAT:** Mobility Aids, Prosthetics, Orthotics, Sensory Aids, ADL Aids.
- ✅ **Myoelectric Prosthetic Hand** operates on **EMG (Electromyography)** signals detected from residual muscles.
- ✅ **Cochlear Implant** bypasses damaged hair cells and **directly stimulates the auditory nerve** through an electrode array.
- ✅ **ADA Ramp Standard:** Slope ratio = **1:12** (≈ 4.8°).
- ✅ **Powered Wheelchair** uses **DC servo motors + joystick control**.
- ✅ **Carbon-fiber prosthetic blades** store and return elastic energy using Hooke's Law: $E = \frac{1}{2}kx^{2}$.
- ✅ **Mechanical Power Formula:** $P = F \cdot v$ — used to analyze wheelchair propulsion.
- ✅ **WHO Estimate:** ~1 billion people globally need assistive products.
- ✅ **Difference between "Restore" and "Substitute"** is a guaranteed KTU exam question every semester.
- ✅ **Always draw a block diagram** for any 14-mark question on cochlear implants, myoelectric limbs, or powered wheelchairs.
- ✅ **Mechanical advantage** of body-powered prosthesis: $MA = L_{\text{effort}} / L_{\text{load}}$.
- ✅ **Pressure at prosthetic socket** must remain below **200 kPa** to prevent skin breakdown.
- ✅ **Tactile Braille displays** and **TVSS (Tactile Vision Substitution Systems)** are examples of sensory substitution HATs.
- ✅ **Smart wheelchairs** integrate **sensors (ultrasonic, IR, IMU) + AI** for autonomous navigation.
- ✅ **Hydraulic dampers** in prosthetic knees use **fluidic resistance** $R = \rho L / A$ to control swing-phase velocity.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Soft Assistive Technologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT614 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Soft Assistive Technologies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🩺 Module 1 — Soft Assistive Technologies

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Soft Assistive Technologies (SAT)** are a class of compliant, intrinsically safe medical and rehabilitative devices fabricated predominantly from **low-modulus elastomers, textiles, gels, and flexible polymers** (typically with Young's modulus **E ≈ 10 kPa – 1 MPa**), which employ **continuum-bodied mechanisms** to provide physical support, force augmentation, or motion assistance to humans, while maintaining intrinsic conformity to biological tissues.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, *Soft Assistive Technologies* span the sub-domains of:

1. **Soft Robotics in Rehabilitation** — Compliant actuators, grippers, and exoskeletons.
2. **Soft Exosuits (Wearable Soft Robots)** — Textile-based assistive garments for upper/lower limbs.
3. **Soft Prosthetic Sockets & Liners** — Conformal interfaces for amputee prostheses.
4. **Soft Continuum Manipulators** — Flexible probes for minimally invasive surgery and endoscopy.
5. **Soft Orthoses** — Braces and supports for patients with neuromuscular disorders.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition Builder)

> [!NOTE]
> **"Imagine the difference between a rigid steel rod and a soft rubber tube used as a handle."**
> When you grip a rigid steel rod, your hand is forced to adopt a *fixed geometry* dictated by the handle. Pressure is concentrated at bony prominences, often causing discomfort or injury. Now consider a soft rubber tube — it **deforms to match the natural shape of your hand**, distributes pressure uniformly across a larger contact area, and feels *intuitively safer*.
>
> **Soft Assistive Technologies operate on the exact same principle.** Instead of imposing a rigid kinematic structure on the human body, they *adapt* to the body's natural anatomy, providing assistance *with* the body rather than *against* it. The compliance of the device is a *functional design feature*, not a limitation.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

| Parameter | Typical Range (Soft Devices) | Clinical Significance |
|-----------|------------------------------|------------------------|
| Young's Modulus (E) | **10 kPa – 1 MPa** | Must match soft tissue (E ≈ 0.5–100 kPa) |
| Operating Pressure | **50 – 250 kPa** | Below tissue damage threshold |
| Safe Contact Pressure | **< 4.3 kPa (30 mmHg)** | Capillary closure limit |
| Actuator Strain | **> 100 %** | Required for natural motion range |
| Bandwidth | **0.5 – 5 Hz** | Matches human voluntary motion |
| Power Density | **0.1 – 10 W/kg** | Comparable to skeletal muscle (~50 W/kg max) |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Concept for KTU Exams:** Always emphasize the *intrinsic safety* and *anthropomorphic conformity* of soft devices as the **primary engineering advantages** when answering design questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Foundational Operational Principles

Soft assistive devices derive their function from the **synergy of three engineering pillars**:

1. **Material Compliance** — Hyperelastic elastomers (Silicone Ecoflex, PDMS, Polyurethane) with non-linear stress-strain response characterized by hyperelastic models (Mooney-Rivlin, Ogden, Yeoh).
2. **Compliant Actuation** — Distributed force generation through fluidic, thermal, or chemical means, eliminating the need for discrete joints.
3. **Continuum Mechanics Framework** — The body of the device is treated as a *continuous deformable medium* with infinite degrees of freedom (DoF), in contrast to rigid robots with finite discrete DoF.

### 2.2 Hierarchical Design Logic

- **Step 1 — Anatomical Mapping:** The target body region (e.g., finger, elbow, knee) is kinematically analyzed to determine desired degrees of freedom and range of motion (RoM).
- **Step 2 — Material Selection:** Soft elastomers are chosen based on Shore-A hardness, tear strength, biocompatibility (ISO 10993), and fatigue life.
- **Step 3 — Actuator Topology:** Designers select between **pneumatic (PAMs, SPAs)**, **hydraulic**, **shape memory alloy (SMA)**, **electroactive polymer (EAP)**, or **tendon-driven textile** architectures.
- **Step 4 — Sensor Integration:** Soft/stretchable sensors (liquid metal channels, conductive elastomers, fabric electrodes) are embedded to provide proprioceptive feedback.
- **Step 5 — Control Strategy:** Pressure-position or EMG-triggered closed-loop control maintains synchronous assistance with user intent.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table summarizes all essential equations for Module 1 problem-solving. Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ instead of the pipe symbol in any in-table expressions to preserve markdown syntax.**

| # | Equation | Description / Application | Units |
|---|----------|---------------------------|-------|
| 1 | $F_{McK} = \dfrac{\pi D_0^{\,2}\, P}{4}\,(3\cos^{2}\alpha - 1)$ | **McKibben Muscle Contractile Force** — pulling force generated by a pressurized braided bladder (Chou–Hannaford model) | N |
| 2 | $V_{McK} = \dfrac{\pi D_0^{\,2}\, L_0}{4}\,\dfrac{\sin^{2}\alpha_0}{\sin^{2}\alpha}$ | **McKibben Volume Conservation** — relationship between length, diameter and braid angle for an incompressible bladder | m³ |
| 3 | $\kappa = \dfrac{P \cdot A \cdot r_{c}}{E \cdot I}$ | **Soft Pneumatic Bending Curvature** — κ of an elastomeric SPA (analogous to Euler–Bernoulli beam) | m⁻¹ |
| 4 | $M = P \cdot A \cdot r_{c}$ | **Bending Moment** generated by pressurized chamber offset from neutral axis | N·m |
| 5 | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A_0}\,\lambda$ | **Cauchy Stress** in hyperelastic material under stretch ratio λ | Pa |
| 6 | $\lambda = 1 + \varepsilon$ | **Stretch Ratio** definition (engineering strain ε) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $W = C_{10}(I_1 - 3) + C_{01}(I_2 - 3)$ | **Mooney–Rivlin Strain Energy** — for moderate strains (≤ 100 %) | J/m³ |
| 8 | $W = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \dfrac{2\mu_i}{\alpha_i^{2}}\left(\lambda_1^{\alpha_i}+\lambda_2^{\alpha_i}+\lambda_3^{\alpha_i}-3\right)$ | **Ogden Strain Energy** — for large strains (≥ 100 %) | J/m³ |
| 9 | $P_{safe} = \dfrac{F_{contact}}{A_{contact}} < 4.3\;\text{kPa}$ | **Clinical Safety Pressure Threshold** (capillary perfusion limit) | Pa |
| 10 | $\tau_{muscle} = \dfrac{F_{assist}\, r_{lever}}{I_{joint}}$ | **Effective Joint Torque Augmentation** by an exosuit | N·m |
| 11 | $\eta = \dfrac{W_{output}}{W_{input}} = \dfrac{\int F\,dL}{\int P\,dV}$ | **Pneumatic Muscle Efficiency** — useful mechanical work / pneumatic input work | dimensionless |
| 12 | $t_{response} \approx \dfrac{V_{chamber}}{Q_{valve}}$ | **Pneumatic Response Time** — chamber volume ÷ valve flow rate | s |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Rehabilitation Medicine:** Soft exosuits (e.g., *Harvard Biodesign Lab's Soft Exosuit*) augment post-stroke gait by ~30 % metabolic reduction.
- **Prosthetics:** Soft silicone liners in transtibial prostheses reduce pressure-sore incidence by ~65 % compared to rigid sockets.
- **Surgical Robotics:** Continuum manipulators (e.g., *Octopus-inspired endoscopes*) access curved anatomical pathways with minimal invasiveness.
- **Daily Living Aids:** Soft robotic grippers assist patients with arthritis or spinal cord injuries in Activities of Daily Living (ADL).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always cite **clinical safety pressure < 4.3 kPa** and **material biocompatibility (ISO 10993)** when justifying design choices in 14-mark questions.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: McKibben Pneumatic Artificial Muscle Force

The **McKibben muscle** is the canonical soft actuator in KTU syllabus. We now derive its contractile force from the **principle of virtual work** applied to an incompressible hyperelastic bladder constrained by an inextensible braided sleeve.

### 3.1.1 Geometric Setup

Let:
- $D_0$ = initial (unpressurized) bladder diameter (m)
- $L_0$ = initial bladder length (m)
- $D, L$ = instantaneous diameter and length under pressure $P$
- $\alpha_0, \alpha$ = initial and instantaneous braid-helix angle (angle between fiber and muscle axis)
- $n$ = number of fibers in the braid (dimensionless)

**Geometric relationship between braid angle, diameter, and length** (derived from helical fiber geometry of length $b$ wound at angle $\alpha$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= \dfrac{n\,b}{\pi}\sin\alpha \\
L &= n\,b\cos\alpha
\end{aligned}
$$

From these two relations, eliminate $n b$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{D}{L} &= \dfrac{\sin\alpha}{\cos\alpha} = \tan\alpha \\
\Rightarrow \quad D &= L\,\tan\alpha
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.2 Volume Conservation (Incompressibility)

The elastomeric bladder is treated as **incompressible**, so its volume is preserved:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4}\,L = \dfrac{\pi D_0^{\,2}}{4}\,L_0 \\[4pt]
\dfrac{\pi (L\tan\alpha)^{2}}{4}\,L &= \dfrac{\pi D_0^{\,2}}{4}\,L_0 \\[4pt]
L^{3}\,\tan^{2}\alpha &= D_0^{\,2}\,L_0
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.3 Application of the Principle of Virtual Work

The **first law of thermodynamics** for a quasi-static pneumatic muscle equates the *pneumatic input work* to the *sum of useful mechanical work output* and *stored elastic energy*. Under the common assumption that **braid fibers are inextensible** (no elastic energy is stored in the sleeve), the relation simplifies to:

$$
P\,dV = F\,dL
$$

Therefore the contractile force is:

$$
F = P\,\dfrac{dV}{dL}
$$

### 3.1.4 Computing dV/dL

From the volume conservation equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= \dfrac{\pi}{4}\,L^{3}\,\tan^{2}\alpha \\[4pt]
\dfrac{dV}{dL} &= \dfrac{\pi}{4}\,\bigl[3L^{2}\tan^{2}\alpha + L^{3}\cdot 2\tan\alpha\cdot \sec^{2}\alpha\,\dfrac{d\alpha}{dL}\bigr]
\end{aligned}
$$

To evaluate $\dfrac{d\alpha}{dL}$, we differentiate the geometric identity $L \tan\alpha = D_0\sqrt{L_0/L}$ (from Step 3.1.2):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan\alpha + L\,\sec^{2}\alpha\,\dfrac{d\alpha}{dL} &= -\dfrac{D_0}{2}\,\sqrt{L_0}\,L^{-3/2} \\[4pt]
L\,\sec^{2}\alpha\,\dfrac{d\alpha}{dL} &= -\dfrac{D_0}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{L_0}{L^{3}}} - \tan\alpha
\end{aligned}
$$

After algebraic manipulation (substituting back and simplifying, the detailed algebra is provided in advanced texts), the **final compact form** is:

$$
\boxed{\,F_{McK} = \dfrac{\pi D_0^{\,2}\,P}{4}\,(3\cos^{2}\alpha - 1)\,}
$$

### 3.1.5 Physical Interpretation of the Result

| Term | Physical Meaning | Engineering Insight |
|------|------------------|---------------------|
| $\dfrac{\pi D_0^{\,2}}{4}$ | Initial cross-sectional area of bladder | Larger diameter → higher force |
| $P$ | Internal gauge pressure | Force scales linearly with pressure |
| $(3\cos^{2}\alpha - 1)$ | Geometric amplification factor | **Requires $\alpha > 54.7°$ for positive force output** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Design Pitfall:** If the braid angle $\alpha < \arccos(1/\sqrt{3}) \approx 54.7°$, the geometric term becomes *negative*, and the muscle *pushes* rather than *pulls*. Most commercial McKibben muscles use $\alpha_0 \in [60°, 75°]$ to ensure a positive contractile force throughout operation.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Soft Pneumatic Bending Actuator (SPBA) Curvature

A **Soft Pneumatic Bending Actuator** consists of an elastomeric body with one or more embedded air chambers, typically offset from the neutral axis to induce bending upon pressurization.

### 3.2.1 Equivalent Beam Model

Treat the elastomeric body as a **Euler–Bernoulli beam** with bending stiffness $EI$ and a pressurized chamber of cross-sectional area $A$ at eccentricity $r_c$ from the neutral axis.

**Pressure-induced bending moment** (force × moment arm):

$$
M = P \cdot A \cdot r_c
$$

**Moment–curvature relation** (Euler–Bernoulli):

$$
M = E \cdot I \cdot \kappa
$$

### 3.2.2 Equating the Two Expressions

$$
\begin{aligned}
E \cdot I \cdot \kappa &= P \cdot A \cdot r_c \\[4pt]
\Rightarrow \quad \kappa &= \dfrac{P \cdot A \cdot r_c}{E \cdot I}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.3 Bending Angle for an Actuator of Length L

$$
\theta_{bend} = \kappa \cdot L = \dfrac{P \cdot A \cdot r_c \cdot L}{E \cdot I}
$$

### 3.2.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A soft pneumatic bending actuator has $E = 200\,\text{kPa}$, $I = 5\times 10^{-9}\,\text{m}^{4}$, $A = 80\,\text{mm}^{2} = 8\times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^{2}$, $r_c = 4\,\text{mm} = 4\times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}$, and $L = 80\,\text{mm} = 0.08\,\text{m}$. Find the curvature and tip bending angle when $P = 100\,\text{kPa}$.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\kappa &= \dfrac{(100\times 10^{3})\,(8\times 10^{-5})\,(4\times 10^{-3})}{(200\times 10^{3})\,(5\times 10^{-9})} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{100{,}000 \times 8\times 10^{-5} \times 4\times 10^{-3}}{200{,}000 \times 5\times 10^{-9}} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{32 \times 10^{-3}}{1.0} = 32\;\text{m}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\theta_{bend} = \kappa \cdot L = 32 \times 0.08 = 2.56\;\text{rad} \approx 146.7^{\circ}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This high bending angle explains why soft pneumatic bending actuators are ideally suited for **finger-rehabilitation gloves**, where each finger joint may require up to 90–110° of flexion.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Soft vs. Rigid Assistive Devices

| Parameter | Soft Assistive Device | Rigid Assistive Device | KTU Preference |
|-----------|----------------------|------------------------|----------------|
| Intrinsic Safety | **Inherently safe** (compliance absorbs impacts) | Requires active control + sensors for safety | Soft ✓ |
| Degrees of Freedom | Infinite (continuum) | Finite (discrete joints) | Soft ✓ |
| Payload-to-weight | Low (1:5 typical) | High (5:1 typical) | Rigid ✓ |
| Precision | Lower (~5–10 mm) | High (< 0.1 mm) | Rigid ✓ |
| Fabrication | Molding / 3D printing | CNC machining / assembly | Soft ✓ |
| Cost | Moderate | High (motors, reducers) | Soft ✓ |
| Patient Acceptance | High (textile-like) | Low (exoskeleton stigma) | Soft ✓ |
| Power Source | Often pneumatic (compressor needed) | Onboard batteries (portable) | Rigid ✓ |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
**Define Soft Robotics. List any three advantages of soft robotic assistive devices over conventional rigid exoskeletons in rehabilitation applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Soft Robotics is a sub-field of robotics that constructs machines primarily from **compliant, elastomeric materials** (with Young's modulus comparable to biological tissues, typically **10 kPa – 1 MPa**), employing **continuum-bodied, distributed mechanisms** rather than discrete rigid links and joints.

> **Three Advantages (2 marks):**
> 1. **Intrinsic Safety:** The compliant body passively conforms to human anatomy and absorbs impact energy, eliminating the need for complex active safety controllers.
> 2. **Anthropomorphic Conformity:** Distributes contact pressure uniformly across biological surfaces, preventing pressure ulcers and improving patient comfort.
> 3. **Reduced Mechanical Complexity:** Eliminates discrete joints, bearings, and gear reducers, resulting in lower cost, lighter weight, and silent operation.
> 4. *(Any other valid point: e.g., better patient acceptance due to textile-like appearance)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**
**What is a McKibben Pneumatic Artificial Muscle (PAM)? Explain its working principle in two sentences.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** A McKibben PAM is a contractile soft actuator consisting of an **inner elastomeric (rubber) bladder** enclosed within a **double-helically braided nylon sleeve**, which converts pneumatic (compressed air) energy into axial contraction force.

> **Working Principle (2 marks):** When compressed air is injected into the bladder, internal pressure causes radial expansion. The braided sleeve, however, constrains this radial expansion and, due to its helical geometry, forces the muscle to **contract axially** (shorten in length), thereby producing a **pulling force** along its longitudinal axis proportional to the input pressure and dependent on the **initial braid angle α** (must exceed 54.7° for force generation).

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - In 14-mark questions, **forgetting to draw a labelled diagram** typically costs **2 marks** immediately (KTU strictly enforces "neat diagram" instruction).
> - In derivations, **skipping intermediate algebraic steps** (e.g., "differentiating and simplifying...") costs the **methodology mark**; always show every line.
> - For design questions, **failing to mention ISO 10993 biocompatibility or the 4.3 kPa capillary pressure threshold** is a frequent reason students lose the "clinical relevance" mark (1–2 marks).
> - Always state **assumptions** (e.g., incompressibility of bladder, inextensibility of braid) explicitly at the start of derivations.

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **construction, working, and characteristics** of **Pneumatic Artificial Muscles (PAMs)** used in soft assistive devices. Use a neat labelled diagram and discuss at least **four performance characteristics** (force, stroke, efficiency, bandwidth).

**Model Answer:**

**Construction (2 marks):**
A McKibben-type PAM consists of:
- **Inner Bladder:** A thin-walled cylindrical elastomer (latex or silicone rubber) that holds the pressurized fluid. It must be flexible, airtight, and highly extensible.
- **Braided Sleeve:** A double-helical mesh of inextensible fibers (typically nylon or polyester) wound over the bladder at a controlled helix angle α₀ (commonly 60°–75°). The fibers are bonded at the ends to rigid fittings.
- **End Fittings:** Rigid threaded connectors that anchor the bladder and braid, and provide the air inlet port.

**Working (2 marks):**
- On pressurization, the bladder tries to expand radially. The inextensible braid converts this radial expansion into **axial contraction** (by helical geometry).
- The actuator shortens along its axis, generating a **pulling (contractile) force** $F$ at its free end.
- On depressurization, an external spring or antagonistic muscle pair restores the original length (most PAMs are *single-acting*).

**Four Performance Characteristics (3 marks):**

| # | Characteristic | Typical Value | Comment |
|---|----------------|---------------|---------|
| 1 | Contractile force | 100 – 3000 N | Linear with pressure; depends on α |
| 2 | Contraction stroke | 25 – 35 % of rest length | Limited by braid geometry |
| 3 | Energy efficiency | 30 – 50 % | Lower than electric motors due to compression of air |
| 4 | Bandwidth | 1 – 3 Hz | Limited by valve response and chamber volume |

**Labelled Diagram (essential, must include):** cross-section of bladder + braid, air inlet, end fittings, axial direction arrow, and braid angle α.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **design, fabrication, and clinical applications** of **soft pneumatic rehabilitation gloves** for post-stroke hand therapy.

**Model Answer:**

**Design (2 marks):**
- **Topology:** Five elastomeric finger actuators (one per digit) plus a thumb abductor/ flexor, all bonded to a textile glove substrate.
- **Bending Principle:** Each finger uses an asymmetric chamber design so that pressurization causes flexion (closing the hand) — curvature κ = (P·A·r_c)/(E·I).
- **Actuation:** 5–10 bar compressed air delivered through a wearable miniature compressor or portable tank.
- **Sensors:** Liquid-metal soft strain sensors (e.g., Galinstan-filled microchannels) on the dorsal side of each finger provide angular position feedback; surface EMG electrodes on the forearm detect residual voluntary EMG signals.

**Fabrication (2 marks):**
1. **Mould Preparation:** 3D-printed (SLA/FDM) positive moulds of each finger segment.
2. **Casting:** Ecoflex 00-30 (Shore-A ≈ 30) silicone is poured and degassed under vacuum.
3. **Curing:** Room-temperature cure for 4 hours, demoulding yields the elastomeric body.
4. **Sealing:** Silicone-based bonding of the half-moulds using plasma surface treatment.
5. **Integration:** Bonding to a breathable textile glove with snap-fit pneumatic connectors.

**Clinical Applications (2 marks):**
- **Post-Stroke Rehabilitation:** Provides repetitive task-oriented hand opening/closing, promoting neuroplasticity via mirror therapy integration.
- **Cerebral Palsy Management:** Assists children with spastic hands in ADLs (eating, writing).
- **Spinal Cord Injury (C4–C6):** Restores partial grip function for patients with preserved forearm EMG signals.
- **Rheumatoid Arthritis:** Reduces joint loading during object manipulation.

**Safety Considerations (1 mark):** Operating pressure capped at 1.5 bar; contact pressure maintained < 4.3 kPa; biocompatibility certified per **ISO 10993**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a **neat block diagram**, explain the architecture and working of a **soft textile exosuit for lower-limb gait assistance**. Discuss the role of **IMU-based intention detection** and **cable-tendon actuation**.

**Model Answer:**

**Architecture (2 marks):**
A lower-limb soft exosuit consists of:
- **Textile garment** worn on the thigh, calf, and hip segments, with **Bowden cable actuators** routed along the lateral side of the leg.
- **Two (or more) electric motors with cable spools** mounted on a hip belt (off-board mass placement to reduce leg inertia).
- **Bowden cables** transmit force from the motors to **soft textile anchors** placed proximal and distal to the joint.
- **IMU sensors** (one on shank, one on thigh) measure segmental angles and angular velocities in the sagittal plane.
- **Central controller** (typically a microcontroller or SBC) implements a **finite-state machine (FSM)** that switches between *stance* and *swing* gait phases based on heel-strike / toe-off detection.

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
- During the **stance phase**, the motor pulls the cable to generate an **ankle plantarflexion torque** (aiding push-off), reducing the metabolic cost of walking by 20–35 %.
- During the **swing phase**, the motor is disengaged to allow free leg motion.
- Forces are transmitted *only through soft textile anchors*, avoiding rigid cuffs and the associated discomfort.

**IMU-Based Intention Detection (1.5 marks):**
- Angular velocity of the shank (measured by gyroscope) is used to detect **toe-off** (peak positive angular velocity) and **heel-strike** (peak negative angular velocity).
- This allows **~30 ms latency** from intention detection to actuator response, which is sufficient for natural gait cadence (~1 Hz).

**Cable-Tendon Actuation Advantages (1.5 marks):**
- High force transmission across joints using **flexible, non-compressible Bowden cables**.
- Distal placement of soft anchors enables **conformal load transfer** without restricting muscle bulging.
- Remote motor placement reduces the **effective inertia** carried by the wearer.
- Limitations: cable friction losses (~10–20 %), hysteresis, and need for anchor load paths along anatomical lines.

**Labelled Diagram (essential):** Wearable garment + IMU placements + cable routing + motor location + gait phase indicator.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** **Derive the contractile force equation** for a **McKibben Pneumatic Artificial Muscle** starting from the principle of virtual work. Clearly state all assumptions and define each variable.

**Model Answer:**

> **Assumptions (1 mark):**
> 1. The inner bladder is **incompressible** ($V = \text{constant}$).
> 2. The braided sleeve is **inextensible** (fibres have constant length $b$).
> 3. The process is **quasi-static** (no inertial effects).
> 4. **No friction** between bladder and braid.
> 5. **Uniform pressure** $P$ inside the bladder.

> **Variables (1 mark):**
> $D_0, L_0$ = initial diameter and length; $D, L$ = current values; $\alpha_0, \alpha$ = initial and current braid angle; $P$ = gauge pressure; $V$ = bladder volume; $F$ = contractile force; $n$ = number of braid fibres; $b$ = fibre length.

> **Geometric Relations (1 mark):** From helical fibre geometry:
> $$D = L\tan\alpha \qquad ; \qquad V = \frac{\pi D^{2} L}{4} = \frac{\pi L^{3}\tan^{2}\alpha}{4}$$

> **Volume Conservation (1 mark):** $L^{3}\tan^{2}\alpha = D_0^{2} L_0$ (constant).

> **Principle of Virtual Work (2 marks):** For quasi-static isobaric process with no elastic energy storage in the braid, the pneumatic input work equals mechanical output:
> $$P\,dV = F\,dL \quad \Rightarrow \quad F = P\,\frac{dV}{dL}$$

> **Differentiation and Substitution (1 mark):** Differentiating $V$ w.r.t. $L$ and eliminating $D$ using the geometric identity $D = L\tan\alpha$ yields after algebraic manipulation:
> $$\boxed{\,F = \frac{\pi D_0^{2}\,P}{4}\,(3\cos^{2}\alpha - 1)\,}$$

> **Physical Interpretation (additional mark for clear statement):** Force is positive only when $\alpha > \arccos(1/\sqrt{3}) \approx 54.7°$. For typical $\alpha_0 = 70°$, the geometric factor equals $3\cos^{2}(70°) - 1 \approx 0.35$, so for $D_0 = 20\,\text{mm}$ and $P = 200\,\text{kPa}$, $F \approx 110\,\text{N}$ — well suited for finger/limb assistance.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Save this list and review 30 minutes before the KTU exam.**

- ⭐ **Soft Assistive Devices** use **low-modulus elastomers** ($E \approx 10\,\text{kPa} - 1\,\text{MPa}$) for **inherent safety and anthropomorphic conformity**.
- ⭐ The **McKibben Muscle** is the canonical soft actuator — **Force**: $F = (\pi D_0^2 P/4)(3\cos^2\alpha - 1)$.
- ⭐ **Braid angle threshold:** $\alpha > 54.7°$ for **positive contractile force** (memorize this value!).
- ⭐ **Soft Pneumatic Bending Actuator** curvature: $\kappa = P \cdot A \cdot r_c / (E \cdot I)$.
- ⭐ Clinical safety contact pressure: **< 4.3 kPa** (capillary closure); **biocompatibility per ISO 10993**.
- ⭐ **Volume conservation** in McKibben: $L^3 \tan^2\alpha = D_0^2 L_0$ — used in derivations.
- ⭐ **Four key soft actuator types:** Pneumatic (PAMs, SPAs), Hydraulic, SMA, EAP — know one application each.
- ⭐ **Soft exosuits** use **IMU + cable-tendon** actuation with off-board motors — metabolic cost reduction 20–35 % during gait.
- ⭐ **Advantages of soft over rigid:** intrinsic safety, infinite DoF, conformal contact, low cost, high patient acceptance.
- ⭐ **Disadvantages of soft over rigid:** lower force density, need for compressor/pump, lower precision, lower bandwidth.
- ⭐ **Hyperelastic material models:** Mooney–Rivlin (moderate strains), Ogden (large strains).
- ⭐ **Rehabilitation glove design:** 5 fingers, asymmetric chambers for flexion, Ecoflex 00-30, soft strain sensors for feedback.
- ⭐ Always draw **layered block diagrams** in 14-mark questions — earn the "diagram" marks.
- ⭐ Always **state assumptions** in derivations — earn the "methodology" marks.
- ⭐ Mention **clinical relevance** (post-stroke, SCI, cerebral palsy, arthritis) in design answers.
- ⭐ KTU weightage: Module 1 typically contributes **15–20 %** of the full syllabus; expect 1 Part-A (3 marks) and 1 Part-B (14 marks) question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Technology Options
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBRT614 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Technology Options # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is "Technology Options" in Assistive Medical Devices? ### Formal KTU Definition **Technology Options** in the context of Assistive Medical Devices (AMD) refers to the comprehensive spectrum of engineering methodologies, hardware architectures, software platforms, materials, and human-machine interface strategies that can be employed to design, develop, and deploy devices aimed at restoring, augmenting, or substituting lost physiological or cognitive function in individuals with disabilities, chronic conditions, or age-related impairments. According to the KTU 2024 OEBRT614 syllabus framework, technology options span the entire **Assistive Technology (AT) stack** — from low-tech mechanical aids to high-tech AI-driven neuroprosthetic systems. ### Classification by WHO/ICF Framework The **World Health Organization (WHO)** and the **International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health (ICF)** categorize assistive technology into six major functional domains: | Domain | Example Devices | |---|---| | Mobility | Wheelchairs, walkers, prosthetics, orthoses | | Vision | Screen readers, Braille displays, smart canes | | Hearing | Hearing aids, cochlear implants, alerting devices | | Communication | AAC devices, speech-generating devices | | Cognition | Memory aids, organizational software, reminders | | Self-care/Environment | Adaptive utensils, smart home controls | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The term "Technology Options" is not a single technology — it is a **decision matrix** that an assistive technology engineer must consult when matching a user's functional limitation to an appropriate engineering solution. The choice depends on user need, environment, cost, and acceptability. --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Swiss Army Knife" of Healthcare Imagine you are a carpenter, and a client needs a tool to hammer a nail. You have many options: - A simple **stone** (low-tech) - A **wooden mallet** (mid-tech) - A **pneumatic nail gun** (high-tech) All three are "technology options" to solve the same problem. The *right* choice depends on the user's strength, skill, budget, and environment. In the same way, an assistive technology engineer evaluating a person with mobility impairment must choose between: - A **simple cane** (low-tech) - A **manual wheelchair** (mid-tech) - A **powered exoskeleton** (high-tech) Each "technology option" sits on a complexity-vs-accessibility spectrum. > [!NOTE] > **Core Principle:** The **principle of the least complex effective technology** states that the simplest, most reliable, and most affordable technology that meets the user's functional goal should be preferred. This is a cornerstone of the KTU assistive devices design philosophy. --- ## 1.3 The Three Tiers of Technology Options ### Tier 1: **No-Tech / Low-Tech** - No electronic components, no power source required. - Examples: **walking canes, reachers, magnifying glasses, communication boards, simple splints**. - **Cost:** Low. **Reliability:** Very high. **Maintenance:** Minimal. ### Tier 2: **Mid-Tech** - Battery-powered, often microprocessor-based, but not networked or AI-driven. - Examples: **battery-operated hearing aids, electric wheelchairs, electronic AAC devices, Braille notetakers**. - **Cost:** Moderate. **Reliability:** High. **Maintenance:** Periodic. ### Tier 3: **High-Tech** - Computer-controlled, networked, AI-enhanced, possibly with biosignal input. - Examples: **Cochlear implants, powered exoskeletons, brain-computer interfaces (BCI), smart prosthetics with haptic feedback, robotic-assisted therapy devices**. - **Cost:** High. **Reliability:** Moderate. **Maintenance:** Specialized. > [!TIP] > A common KTU exam pitfall: students often describe *high-tech = best*. In assistive technology, this is **not always true**. Cultural acceptability, maintenance infrastructure, and cost in low-resource settings often make low-tech solutions the *clinically optimal* choice. --- ## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics in Assistive Technology Options - **ISO 9999:2022** — International classification of assistive products (replaces the older EN ISO 9999). - **WHO Priority Assistive Products List (APL)** — 50 essential assistive products identified by the WHO. - **ATscale Global Benchmark** — Global cost benchmarks for AT. - **Universal Design (UD)** principles — Ron Mace's 7 Principles. - **Accessibility, Durability, Affordability, Acceptability, Availability** — the **"5 A's"** of appropriate assistive technology. All of the above are **bolded** as they are frequently asked in KTU exams. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Technology Selection Decision Framework The choice of an assistive technology option is governed by a **Multi-Criteria Decision Analysis (MCDA)**. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to know the structural framework for matching a technology option to a user need. ### 2.1.1 The HAAT Model (Human, Activity, Assistive Technology) Proposed by **Cook and Polgar**, the **HAAT model** is the dominant theoretical framework in assistive technology. The model can be expressed structurally as: $$ \text{Assistive Technology Outcome} = f(H, A, AT, C) $$ Where: - $H$ = Human (user's physical, cognitive, sensory abilities) - $A$ = Activity (task to be performed) - $AT$ = Assistive Technology (the device/system) - $C$ = Context (physical, social, cultural, institutional environment) > [!NOTE] > This is **not a strict mathematical equation** but a structural model. The function $f(\cdot)$ represents the dynamic interaction between these four domains. Misuse of this equation in a KTU numerical problem would result in mark deduction. ### 2.1.2 The MPT (Matching Person and Technology) Model A complementary framework by **Scherer** focuses on user subjective factors: - User's goals and preferences - User's prior experience with technology - User's temperament (e.g., persistence, adaptability) - Environmental supports and barriers The matching equation is conceptualized as: $$ M_{success} = \frac{(U_{fit} \times E_{support})}{(C_{barrier} + T_{complexity})} $$ Where: - $M_{success}$ = Match success index - $U_{fit}$ = User-technology fit - $E_{support}$ = Environmental support - $C_{barrier}$ = Contextual barrier - $T_{complexity}$ = Technology complexity > [!WARNING] > The MPT equation is **conceptual**, not a deterministic formula. KTU answers should describe the relationship qualitatively and use the equation as an illustrative structure. --- ## 2.2 Technology Options by Functional Domain — A Detailed Matrix ### 2.2.1 Mobility Technology Options | Sub-Category | Technology Option | User Need | |---|---|---| | Ambulatory aids | Cane, crutch, walker | Mild balance/gait deficit | | Manual wheelchairs | Self-propelled, attendant-propelled | Unable to ambulate, upper limb strength preserved | | Powered wheelchairs | Joystick, sip-and-puff, head-control | Cannot self-propel manual chair | | Standing devices | Manual stander, power stander | Pressure relief, bone density | | Exoskeletons | Lower-extremity powered orthosis | Spinal cord injury, stroke rehabilitation | | Prosthetics | Body-powered, myoelectric, osseointegrated | Limb amputation | ### 2.2.2 Sensory Technology Options (Vision) | Technology Option | Working Principle | Cognitive Load | |---|---|---| | Optical magnifier | Refractive lens enlargement | Low | | CCTV/video magnifier | Electronic magnification | Low | | Screen reader (software) | Text-to-speech (TTS) engine | Medium | | Refreshable Braille display | Piezoelectric pin actuation | Low | | Smart cane (ultrasonic) | Sonar echo-location | Low | | AI object recognition (e.g., OrCam) | Computer vision + ML | Low for user, High in processing | | Retinal implant / Bionic eye | Electrical stimulation of retina/visual cortex | High | ### 2.2.3 Hearing Technology Options - **Conventional Hearing Aid** — air-conduction amplifier, 3 classes: BTE, ITE, CIC. - **Bone-Anchored Hearing Aid (BAHA)** — percutaneous abutment, bone conduction. - **Middle Ear Implant (MEI)** — vibratory transducer on ossicular chain. - **Cochlear Implant (CI)** — direct electrical stimulation of the cochlear nerve. - **Auditory Brainstem Implant (ABI)** — stimulation at the cochlear nucleus. - **Assistive Listening Devices (ALD)** — FM, infrared, induction loop systems. ### 2.2.4 Communication Technology Options (AAC) | AAC Type | Technology Option | |---|---| | Unaided AAC | Gestures, sign language, facial expressions | | Low-tech aided AAC | Paper-based communication boards, Picture Exchange System (PECS) | | Mid-tech aided AAC | Single-message speech buttons, static display SGDs | | High-tech aided AAC | Dynamic display SGDs (e.g., Tobii Dynavox), eye-gaze AAC | --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > All formulas below are **KTU exam-relevant** for analytical/numerical problems on technology option selection. | Equation / Concept | Description | Variables & Units | KTU Use Case | |---|---|---|---| | $P = \frac{V^2}{R}$ | Electrical power dissipation | $P$ in W, $V$ in V, $R$ in $\Omega$ | Battery life estimation for powered wheelchair | | $E = P \times t$ | Energy stored in battery | $E$ in Wh, $t$ in h | Operating range calculation | | $C_{rate} = \frac{I_{discharge}}{Q_{nominal}}$ | Battery C-rate | dimensionless | Battery sizing for portable device | | $SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | Signal-to-noise ratio | $V$ in volts | Audio amplifier design for hearing aid | | $Gain_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}}\right)$ | Amplifier voltage gain | dimensionless | Hearing aid gain specification | | $f_{c} = \frac{1}{2 \pi RC}$ | Filter cutoff frequency | $f_c$ in Hz, $R$ in $\Omega$, $C$ in F | Band-pass filter for biosignal | | $R_{contact} = \frac{\rho L}{A}$ | Electrical resistance of electrode | $\rho$ in $\Omega \cdot m$ | Skin-electrode interface for myoelectric prosthetic | | $T = \frac{1}{f_{s}}$ | Sampling period (digitization) | $T$ in s, $f_s$ in Hz | EMG signal acquisition system | | $E_{stim} = V_{stim} \cdot I_{stim} \cdot t_{pulse}$ | Energy per stimulation pulse | $E$ in $\mu J$, $t_{pulse}$ in $\mu s$ | FES device design | | $Z_{th} = \frac{1}{h \cdot A}$ | Thermal resistance | $h$ in W/m²K | Heat dissipation in wearable devices | | $F_{max} = \mu \cdot N$ | Friction force limit | $F$ in N, $\mu$ dimensionless, $N$ in N | Wheelchair braking / grip | | $Torque = F \cdot r$ | Mechanical torque | $F$ in N, $r$ in m | Motor selection for prosthetic hand | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Strict markdown rule:** In KTU notes, the absolute value or "such that" symbol inside tables is written as `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking table syntax. Example: $R \vert_{skin}$ instead of $R \mid skin$. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Powered wheelchair motor sizing** uses the $F = ma$ and $Torque = F \cdot r$ relationships to determine the required wattage. - **Cochlear implant electrode design** is fundamentally a trade-off between the number of stimulation channels (fidelity) and current spread (channel separation). - **Myoelectric prosthetic control** uses the surface EMG signal amplitude (rectified, smoothed) to drive a DC motor. - **Exoskeleton battery sizing** uses the $E = P \cdot t$ relationship — a typical lower-extremity exoskeleton consumes **150–500 W**, hence battery life is **2–4 hours** per charge. --- ## 2.5 The "5 A's" of Appropriate Technology Options (KTU High-Yield) > [!NOTE] > This is a frequently asked KTU Part A question. Memorize the 5 A's. 1. **Available** — accessible in the user's geography. 2. **Affordable** — economically viable for the user/family/healthcare system. 3. **Accessible** — usable by the target user without stigma. 4. **Appropriate** — matches the user's functional and cultural needs. 5. **Acceptable** — the user and community find it acceptable. All five A's are **bolded** as they are KTU-emphasis terms. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Battery Life of a Powered Wheelchair (Energy Method) A powered wheelchair is to be operated by a user with a spinal cord injury. The wheelchair's drive motors consume an average power of **180 W** during normal indoor use, and the on-board electronics (controller, lighting, sensors) consume an additional **20 W**. The battery pack is rated at **24 V, 60 Ah** (lead-acid type, with a usable depth-of-discharge of 80%). **Goal:** Estimate the operating time per full charge, and the usable range if the wheelchair travels at an average speed of **6 km/h**. --- ### Step 1: Compute the total energy capacity of the battery pack The nominal energy capacity is computed using: $$ E_{nominal} = V_{nom} \cdot Q_{nom} $$ Substituting the values: $$ E_{nominal} = 24 \, V \times 60 \, Ah $$ $$ E_{nominal} = 1440 \, Wh $$ The unit **Wh** is shorthand for **Watt-hours**, which is the product of voltage (V), current (A), and time (h). In SI base units, $1 \, Wh = 3600 \, J$. ### Step 2: Apply the Depth-of-Discharge (DoD) factor Lead-acid batteries cannot be safely discharged below 80% of their rated capacity without damage. So the *usable* energy is: $$ E_{usable} = E_{nominal} \times DoD $$ $$ E_{usable} = 1440 \, Wh \times 0.80 $$ $$ E_{usable} = 1152 \, Wh $$ ### Step 3: Compute the total average power draw $$ P_{total} = P_{motors} + P_{electronics} $$ $$ P_{total} = 180 \, W + 20 \, W $$ $$ P_{total} = 200 \, W $$ ### Step 4: Estimate the operating time Using the energy-time-power relation: $$ E = P \cdot t \quad \Rightarrow \quad t = \frac{E_{usable}}{P_{total}} $$ $$ t = \frac{1152 \, Wh}{200 \, W} $$ $$ t = 5.76 \, h $$ So, the wheelchair can operate for **approximately 5.76 hours** on a full charge. ### Step 5: Estimate the range $$ D = v \cdot t $$ $$ D = 6 \, \frac{km}{h} \times 5.76 \, h $$ $$ D = 34.56 \, km $$ So, the usable range is approximately **34.56 km** per full charge. --- > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering sanity check:** Real-world factors — inclines, user weight, headwind, battery aging — reduce this range by **20–40%**. KTU answers that report the theoretical range without mentioning these factors may lose partial marks. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Cutoff Frequency of a Pre-emphasis Filter in a Hearing Aid A behind-the-ear (BTE) hearing aid must filter out low-frequency ambient noise below **500 Hz** (e.g., air conditioners, traffic rumble). The filter is implemented as a single-pole RC high-pass filter. **Given:** $C = 47 \, nF$ (a standard surface-mount capacitor value). **Goal:** Determine the required resistance $R$ such that the cutoff frequency is exactly **500 Hz**. --- ### Step 1: Recall the high-pass RC filter cutoff frequency $$ f_{c} = \frac{1}{2 \pi R C} $$ ### Step 2: Solve for R algebraically $$ R = \frac{1}{2 \pi f_{c} C} $$ ### Step 3: Substitute numerical values $$ R = \frac{1}{2 \pi \times 500 \, Hz \times 47 \times 10^{-9} \, F} $$ $$ R = \frac{1}{2 \times 3.14159 \times 500 \times 47 \times 10^{-9}} $$ $$ R = \frac{1}{1.476 \times 10^{-4}} $$ $$ R \approx 6775 \, \Omega $$ ### Step 4: Select a standard resistor value The nearest E24 standard value is **6.8 k$\Omega$**. This produces a slightly different actual cutoff: $$ f_{c,actual} = \frac{1}{2 \pi \times 6800 \times 47 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 498 \, Hz $$ > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters for assistive technology:** The 500 Hz cutoff is critical because **speech intelligibility** in hearing-impaired users drops sharply when low-frequency noise is not filtered — a **5 dB SNR improvement** at low frequencies can yield a **20–30% speech recognition gain** in noise. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Myoelectric Prosthetic Hand — Grip Force and Torque A myoelectric prosthetic hand has three fingers driven by a single DC motor. The motor is connected via a spur gear with a reduction ratio of **1:40**. The motor produces a maximum stall torque of **50 mN·m**. The output pulley radius is **15 mm**. **Goal:** Compute the maximum grip force at the fingertip. --- ### Step 1: Output torque after gear reduction $$ \tau_{out} = \tau_{motor} \times N $$ $$ \tau_{out} = 50 \times 10^{-3} \, N \cdot m \times 40 $$ $$ \tau_{out} = 2.0 \, N \cdot m $$ ### Step 2: Convert to force using the lever arm $$ F = \frac{\tau_{out}}{r} $$ $$ F = \frac{2.0 \, N \cdot m}{15 \times 10^{-3} \, m} $$ $$ F = 133.33 \, N $$ ### Step 3: Compare to physiological grip strength Typical human grip strength is **200–300 N** for an adult male. The prosthetic hand at **133 N** falls short of full physiological strength but is sufficient for **activities of daily living (ADLs)** like holding a cup or turning a doorknob. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choosing Among Three Technology Options > [!NOTE] > This matrix is a KTU-favored question type: "Compare three technology options for [impairment] across [criteria]." | Criterion | Cane (Low-tech) | Powered Wheelchair (Mid-tech) | Exoskeleton (High-tech) | |---|---|---|---| | Cost | Very low | Moderate–High | Very high | | User strength required | High | None | Low | | Maintenance | Minimal | Periodic (battery, tires) | Specialized technician | | Energy source | None | Battery | Battery (heavy) | | Terrain range | All | Smooth surfaces | Limited | | Skill to use | Low | Low | High | | Cultural acceptability | High | Moderate | Low–Moderate | | Suitability for upper-limb strength | Must be high | Not required | Not required | | Stigma | Low | Moderate | Variable | | Suitability for quadriplegics | Unsuitable | Highly suitable | Highly suitable | > [!TIP] > **KTU exam technique:** A common "Compare and justify" question asks which technology option is *most appropriate* for a given user profile. Always tie your answer to the **5 A's** framework. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: The HAAT Model Block Architecture This diagram maps the four interacting components of the **HAAT Model** (Cook & Polgar), which is the foundational framework for selecting technology options in assistive medical devices. ```mermaid flowchart LR H[Human
Physical, Cognitive, Sensory
User Profile] A[Activity
Task to be Performed
ADL, IADL, Work, Leisure] AT[Assistive Technology
Device or System
Low, Mid, or High-Tech] C[Context
Environment, Culture, Support
Physical and Social] H <-->|user-device fit| AT A <-->|task-device fit| AT C <-->|env-device fit| AT H <-->|person-activity match| A A <-->|activity-context| C H <-->|person-context| C AT --> OUT1[Functional Outcome
Improved Participation] H --> OUT1 A --> OUT1 C --> OUT1 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The AT block sits at the center, but it does **not** determine the outcome alone. The four-way interaction determines whether the technology option *succeeds* in a real user context. --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Technology Option Tiers and Their Sub-Domains ```mermaid flowchart TB TOP[Technology Options for
Assistive Medical Devices] TOP --> T1[Tier 1 Low-Tech] TOP --> T2[Tier 2 Mid-Tech] TOP --> T3[Tier 3 High-Tech] T1 --> T1A[No Power Required] T1 --> T1B[Mechanical Aids] T1 --> T1C[Paper-Based AAC] T1A --> T1A1[Walking Cane, Reacher, Splint] T1B --> T1B1[Manual Wheelchair] T1C --> T1C1[Picture Boards, PECS] T2 --> T2A[Battery Powered] T2 --> T2B[Microprocessor Based] T2 --> T2C[Single-Function Devices] T2A --> T2A1[Hearing Aid, Electric Wheelchair] T2B --> T2B1[Speech Generating Device] T2C --> T2C1[Screen Reader Software] T3 --> T3A[AI and Networked] T3 --> T3B[Biosignal Driven] T3 --> T3C[Implantable] T3A --> T3A1[Smart Prosthetic, AI Vision Aid] T3B --> T3B1[Myoelectric Arm, BCI] T3C --> T3C1[Cochlear Implant, Retinal Prosthesis] ``` --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Functional Block Architecture of a Myoelectric Prosthetic Arm ```mermaid flowchart LR EMG1[Surface EMG Electrodes
Residual Limb] EMG2[Differential Amplifier
Gain 1000x] EMG3[Bandpass Filter
20 Hz to 500 Hz] EMG4[Rectifier and Smoother] EMG5[ADC
Sampling 1 kHz] EMG6[Microcontroller
Pattern Classifier] EMG7[Motor Driver H-Bridge] EMG8[DC Gear Motor] EMG9[Prosthetic Hand Fingers] EMG10[Battery Pack Li-ion 7.4 V] EMG1 --> EMG2 --> EMG3 --> EMG4 --> EMG5 --> EMG6 --> EMG7 --> EMG8 --> EMG9 EMG10 --> EMG7 EMG10 --> EMG6 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > This block diagram is the standard **KTU block-level functional architecture** for any signal-driven assistive device. Memorize the signal flow: **Acquisition → Amplification → Filtering → Rectification → Digitization → Classification → Actuation**. --- ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Decision Flowchart for Selecting a Mobility Technology Option ```mermaid flowchart TD START[User Requires Mobility Aid] Q1{User Can Ambulate
with Assist?} Q2{Upper Limb
Strength Adequate?} Q3{Environment
Indoor Only?} Q4{Functional Goal
Therapy or ADL?} START --> Q1 Q1 -->|Yes| O1[Recommend Cane or Walker] Q1 -->|No| Q2 Q2 -->|Yes| O2[Recommend Manual Wheelchair] Q2 -->|No| Q3 Q3 -->|Yes| O3[Recommend Powered Wheelchair] Q3 -->|No| Q4 Q4 -->|Therapy| O4[Recommend Robotic Gait Trainer] Q4 -->|ADL| O5[Recommend Powered Exoskeleton] O1 --> CK[Verify 5 A's] O2 --> CK O3 --> CK O4 --> CK O5 --> CK CK --> DONE[Final Device Selection] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. Define "Technology Options" in the context of assistive medical devices. List the three tiers of technology options with one example for each. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** Technology Options refer to the complete spectrum of engineering solutions — from simple mechanical aids to complex AI-driven neuroprosthetic systems — that can be used to restore, augment, or substitute lost function in a person with disability or chronic impairment. The three tiers are: - **Low-tech (No-tech tier):** Devices that require no external power source. *Example:* Walking cane, reacher, paper-based communication board. - **Mid-tech (Battery-powered / Microprocessor tier):** Devices that use simple electronics and battery power. *Example:* Electric wheelchair, body-powered hearing aid, single-message speech device. - **High-tech (Computer-controlled / AI tier):** Devices with advanced computation, biosignal input, or implantable components. *Example:* Myoelectric prosthetic arm, cochlear implant, brain-computer interface. > [!TIP] > For full marks, always cite a *specific clinical population* for each example (e.g., "low-tech cane for a person with mild hemiparesis post-stroke"). --- ### Q2. State and briefly explain the "5 A's" of appropriate assistive technology selection. `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]` **Model Answer:** The **5 A's** are the criteria used to judge whether a chosen technology option is appropriate for a user: 1. **Available** — The device must be physically accessible in the user's geographic region (e.g., a cochlear implant is unavailable in many rural areas of low-income countries). 2. **Affordable** — The cost must be within the user's or healthcare system's economic means (e.g., a powered exoskeleton costing ₹20 lakhs is not affordable for the average Indian user). 3. **Accessible** — The user must be able to physically, cognitively, and sensorially operate the device. 4. **Appropriate** — The device must match the user's clinical need, body dimensions, and functional goals. 5. **Acceptable** — The device must be culturally and socially acceptable to the user and their community (e.g., a hearing aid may carry social stigma in some cultures). --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- ### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks **"Compare and contrast the technology options available for providing mobility assistance to a person with spinal cord injury at the T6 level. Discuss the engineering principles underlying each option."** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks:** Classify the technology options into three categories and discuss the engineering principles of manual and powered wheelchairs. **Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks:** Discuss powered exoskeletons, including their actuation, control, power management, and limitations. --- #### Solution to Sub-part (a): **Classification of Mobility Technology Options for T6 SCI:** For a person with thoracic-level (T6) spinal cord injury, lower limb function is lost, but upper limb function is largely preserved.
