The technology options include: **(i) Manual Wheelchair (Mid-tech)** - Engineering principle: Rolling friction is minimized using precision bearings and pneumatic tires; lever arms on the hand-rim multiply the user's input torque. - Self-propulsion torque: $T = F \cdot r$, where $F$ is the user-applied force on the hand-rim and $r$ is the hand-rim radius (typically 25 cm). - Cost: ₹5,000 – ₹50,000 in India. - Limitations: Upper-limb overuse injuries (carpal tunnel syndrome, rotator cuff tears); not suitable for long distances or steep inclines. **(ii) Powered Wheelchair (Mid-to-High tech)** - Engineering principle: A 24V DC motor (or two, one per wheel) drives the wheels through a gearbox. Control is via a joystick that maps $x$-axis (forward/back) and $y$-axis (left/right) inputs to motor PWM duty cycles. - Battery: Lead-acid or Li-ion, typically 24V, 35–75 Ah. - Operating time (computed earlier): **5.76 h** for 200W average draw on a 1440 Wh pack. - Control electronics: Microcontroller-based with PID closed-loop speed control. - Cost: ₹1,00,000 – ₹5,00,000. **(iii) Powered Exoskeleton (High-tech) — discussed in part (b).** > [!WARNING] > **Common student error:** Students often confuse "powered wheelchair" with "powered exoskeleton." A wheelchair is a *vehicle*; an exoskeleton is *worn on the body* and provides posture support and ambulation. Mark deduction if these are conflated. --- #### Solution to Sub-part (b): **Powered Lower-Extremity Exoskeleton (High-tech Mobility Option):** - **Actuation principle:** Brushless DC motors at the hip and knee joints, geared to provide torque. Typical joint torque: **40–80 Nm** at the knee, **20–50 Nm** at the hip. - **Control system:** Uses **IMU sensors** (gyroscope + accelerometer) to detect the user's trunk tilt. A forward trunk tilt intent triggers the swing-phase actuation of the leg. - **Crutch interface:** Most exoskeletons (e.g., ReWalk, Ekso GT) require forearm crutches for balance and to trigger the gait cycle. - **Power management:** A typical exoskeleton consumes **150–500 W** continuously. Battery is usually a **24V, 10–20 Ah Li-ion pack**, giving **2–4 hours** of continuous walking. - **Computation:** Sampling rate for IMU and joint encoders is **100–500 Hz**, processed by an embedded ARM Cortex-M4/M7 microcontroller. - **Limitations:** 1. High cost (₹15–₹50 lakhs). 2. Battery life is short. 3. Cannot navigate stairs (most models). 4. Requires trained operator and supervised environment. 5. Not suitable for users with severe spasticity, contractures, or osteoporosis. - **Clinical utility:** Effective for **gait retraining** in incomplete SCI, and provides **psychological benefit** (eye-level interaction, improved self-image). --- ### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks **"Explain the technology options available for individuals with severe to profound hearing loss. Discuss the engineering principles of conventional hearing aids and cochlear implants, with a comparison of their indications and limitations."** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks:** Discuss the engineering principles of conventional air-conduction hearing aids and bone-anchored hearing systems. **Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks:** Discuss the cochlear implant, including electrode design, speech processing strategy, and clinical indications. --- #### Solution to Sub-part (a): **Conventional Air-Conduction Hearing Aid (Mid-tech):** - **Working principle:** Sound is captured by a microphone (typically a MEMS microphone, sensitivity −42 dBV/Pa). The signal is pre-amplified, passed through a band-pass filter (typically 200 Hz – 8 kHz), and then through a multi-band compressor (WDRC — Wide Dynamic Range Compression) that maps the wide dynamic range of normal hearing (~120 dB) into the narrowed dynamic range of the impaired ear. - **Output stage:** A Class-D receiver (balanced armature or dynamic) delivers amplified sound into the ear canal via a custom earmold. - **Gain specification:** $Gain_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}}\right)$, typically 30–70 dB. - **Power:** Battery (zinc-air, size 312 or 675) provides 5–14 days of use. - **Limitation:** Ineffective for severe-profound hearing loss (> 90 dB HL) due to **recruitment** and **distortion** in the damaged cochlea. **Bone-Anchored Hearing Aid (BAHA) — Osseointegrated:** - **Working principle:** A percutaneous titanium abutment is surgically implanted into the temporal bone. Sound is converted to mechanical vibration by an external processor, transmitted through the abutment, and stimulates the cochlea directly via bone conduction — **bypassing the outer and middle ear**. - **Indication:** Conductive or mixed hearing loss, chronic otitis media, congenital aural atresia. - **Limitation:** Surgical risk (skin reaction, implant loss); not suitable for profound sensorineural loss. --- #### Solution to Sub-part (b): **Cochlear Implant (CI) — High-tech Hearing Option:** - **Working principle:** Sound is captured by an external microphone, processed by a speech processor, and converted into a coded electrical signal. This is transmitted via a radio-frequency link across the skin to an implanted receiver-stimulator. Electrical pulses are delivered to an electrode array threaded into the **scala tympani** of the cochlea. - **Electrode design:** Typically 12–22 electrodes along an array 20–30 mm long. Each electrode stimulates a different tonotopic region of the cochlea, with the **base** responding to high frequencies and the **apex** to low frequencies. - **Speech processing strategies:** - **CIS (Continuous Interleaved Sampling):** Pulses delivered sequentially, avoiding channel interaction. - **ACE (Advanced Combination Encoder):** Selects only the N highest-amplitude channels per frame, conserving power. - **FSP (Fine Structure Processing):** Encodes temporal fine structure for better music and tonal language perception. - **Energy per stimulation pulse:** $E_{stim} = V_{stim} \cdot I_{stim} \cdot t_{pulse}$. Typical values: $V_{stim} \approx 1-8 \, V$, $I_{stim} \approx 100-1000 \, \mu A$, $t_{pulse} \approx 25-100 \, \mu s$, giving $E_{stim}$ in the **μJ range**. - **Clinical indication:** Bilateral severe-to-profound sensorineural hearing loss (> 90 dB HL) with limited benefit from hearing aids. - **Limitations:** 1. Surgical risk (facial nerve damage, meningitis). 2. Expensive (₹5–₹15 lakhs in India). 3. Requires intensive auditory-verbal therapy post-implantation. 4. Variable outcomes depending on age at implantation, duration of deafness, and neural survival. 5. No regeneration of hair cells — current spread limits frequency resolution. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often write that cochlear implants "restore normal hearing." This is **incorrect**. CI provides a *useful representation* of sound for speech understanding but does not replicate the fine frequency and temporal resolution of a healthy cochlea. Marks are deducted for this misconception. --- ## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU students lose marks in "Technology Options" questions:** > > 1. **Conflating "low-tech" with "primitive."** Low-tech solutions are not inferior; they are often the most appropriate option in low-resource settings. > 2. **Omitting user-context factors.** Describing a device without referencing user need, environment, or the 5 A's loses marks in application questions. > 3. **Confusing similar devices.** e.g., BTE hearing aid vs. cochlear implant; manual vs. powered wheelchair; body-powered vs. myoelectric prosthetic. > 4. **Skipping engineering principles.** A "compare and contrast" question demands discussion of *how* the device works, not just what it does. > 5. **Writing "restores normal function."** No current assistive device fully restores normal function. Use phrases like "improves functional capacity" or "partially compensates for lost function." > 6. **Forgetting clinical indications.** Always state *who* the device is for, not just *what* it is. > 7. **Ignoring ethical / social issues.** Cultural acceptability, affordability, and the digital divide are all in-scope for KTU 2024. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist** - **Definition:** Technology Options = the full range of engineering solutions from low-tech to high-tech for AT needs. - **Three Tiers:** Low-tech (no power), Mid-tech (battery/microprocessor), High-tech (AI/implantable/networked). - **HAAT Model:** Human, Activity, Assistive Technology, Context — all four interact dynamically. - **MPT Model:** User-Technology-Environment matching, conceptual equation: $M_{success} = \frac{(U_{fit} \times E_{support})}{(C_{barrier} + T_{complexity})}$. - **5 A's:** Available, Affordable, Accessible, Appropriate, Acceptable. - **WHO 6 Domains:** Mobility, Vision, Hearing, Communication, Cognition, Self-care. - **Mobility options:** Cane → Walker → Manual WC → Powered WC → Exoskeleton. - **Vision options:** Optical magnifier → CCTV → Screen reader → Braille display → AI vision aid → Retinal implant. - **Hearing options:** Hearing aid → BAHA → MEI → CI → ABI. - **Communication (AAC) options:** Unaided → Low-tech aided → Mid-tech SGD → High-tech eye-gaze AAC. - **Battery sizing:** $E = V \cdot Q \cdot DoD$, $t = E / P$. - **Filter design:** $f_c = \frac{1}{2 \pi R C}$. - **Motor sizing:** $\tau_{out} = \tau_{motor} \times N$, $F = \tau / r$. - **SNR formula:** $SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(V_s / V_n)$. - **CI energy:** $E_{stim} = V \cdot I \cdot t_{pulse}$. - **Universal Design:** 7 principles by Ron Mace — equitable use, flexibility, simple/intuitive, perceptible info, tolerance for error, low effort, size/space. - **ISO 9999:2022** is the international classification of assistive products. - **WHO Priority APL** = 50 essential assistive products. - **KTU rule:** Always tie technology choice to the user, not to the device. > **End of Module 1, Topic: Technology Options**

topics.title: Anatomic Sites for Control of AT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT614 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Anatomic Sites for Control of AT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Anatomic Sites for Control of Assistive Technology (AT)** refer to the specific, physiologically accessible locations on the human body that serve as **signal sources** (bioelectric, biomechanical, or bioacoustic) or **mechanical coupling points** for commanding, steering, or operating an assistive device.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, these sites are classified by the **type of physiological variable** they generate and the **degree of disability** they accommodate. The valid signal modalities include:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> An anatomic control site is any residue of voluntary motor function, sensory pathway, or autonomic process that can be reliably transduced into a deterministic control input (voltage, switch closure, pneumatic pressure, or digital command) for an AT device such as a wheelchair, prosthetic limb, communication aid, or environmental controller.

| Modality Class | Anatomic Site | Typical Physiologic Signal |
|----------------|---------------|----------------------------|
| Bioelectric | Cerebral cortex | EEG (Electroencephalogram) |
| Bioelectric | Extraocular muscles | EOG (Electrooculogram) |
| Bioelectric | Skeletal muscles | EMG (Electromyogram) |
| Bioelectric | Retina / Visual cortex | ERP (Event-Related Potential) |
| Biomechanical | Head, neck, shoulder, tongue | Mechanical displacement / Force |
| Biomechanical | Residual limbs | Socket-mediated force / motion |
| Bioacoustic | Larynx, vocal tract | Voice / Speech |
| Biochemical | Skin (sweat), exhaled air | GSR, CO₂, VOC |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Your Body as a Living Remote Control**
> 
> Imagine your TV remote is broken and you must build a *new* remote using **your own body** as the controller. The "buttons" of this remote are the **anatomic sites** — for instance:
> - **Bite down (jaw muscles)** → channel up
> - **Raise an eyebrow (frontalis muscle)** → mute
> - **Whistle (larynx + oral cavity)** → voice activation
> 
> An engineer designing assistive technology must therefore scan the user's body for *any remaining controllable tissue* and assign it a digital function. This scan is the study of **anatomic sites for control of AT**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physiological Constants

- **EMG amplitude** (surface): typically **50 µV to 5 mV** RMS
- **EEG amplitude**: typically **10 µV to 100 µV** (peak-to-peak)
- **EOG amplitude**: **5–20 µV per degree** of eye rotation (corneo-retinal standing potential ≈ **0.4–1.0 mV**)
- **Saccade duration**: **30–80 ms**
- **Voice fundamental frequency**: **85–180 Hz** (adult male), **165–255 Hz** (adult female)
- **Tongue force**: up to **~10 N** comfortably sustained
- **Breath pressure (sip-and-puff)**: **±5 to ±10 cm H₂O** for binary, **±2 to ±15 cm H₂O** for proportional
- **Residual-limb EMG crosstalk**: ideally **< 10 %** of adjacent muscle activity

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification Logic

The selection of an anatomic control site follows a strict **clinical-engineering cascade**:

1. **Preserve the most natural site first** — i.e., the limb or organ the user *already* intended to use (e.g., a hand amputee should ideally use residual forearm muscles, not the contralateral shoulder).
2. **Fall back to a more proximal or alternative site** when the natural site is unusable (e.g., high-level quadriplegia → shoulder, head, or tongue).
3. **Fall back to a bioelectric/autonomic signal** when mechanical sites are exhausted (locked-in syndrome → EEG, EOG).
4. **Apply multimodal fusion** when a single site is unreliable (combine EMG + EOG + switch).

## 2.2 Why Each Site Is Chosen — The Engineering Rationale

| Site | Why Chosen | Limitation |
|------|-----------|------------|
| Forearm/wrist muscles (EMG) | High signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), intuitive grasp mapping | Not available above-elbow amputation |
| Shoulder (3-position switch) | Strong, slow-twitch, low fatigue | Coarse control only |
| Head (mechanical pylon or accelerometer) | Almost always intact in SCI | Cannot be used while reclined |
| Tongue (inductive/optical) | Highly dexterous, hidden, fatigue-resistant | Requires intra-oral retainer; unsuitable for ventilated users |
| EOG / Eye tracking | Works in ALS / late-stage MD | Lighting-sensitive; not for nystagmus patients |
| EEG (motor imagery) | Fully non-muscular; works in locked-in syndrome | Low information transfer rate (~25 bits/min); training-intensive |
| Voice | Hands-free, high bandwidth | Dysarthria users excluded; noisy environments |
| Sip-and-puff | Reliable in high-level SCI | Only 2–4 discrete commands |

## 2.3 Signal Conditioning Block — Common to All Bioelectric Sites

$$
x(t) \xrightarrow{\text{Amplify}} A \cdot x(t) \xrightarrow{\text{Band-pass}} x_f(t) \xrightarrow{\text{ADC}} x[n] \xrightarrow{\text{Feature extract}} \mathbf{f} \xrightarrow{\text{Classifier}} y
$$

where:
- $A \approx 1000\text{–}10{,}000$ for EMG, $\approx 10{,}000\text{–}100{,}000$ for EEG
- Band-pass: **20–500 Hz** (EMG), **0.5–40 Hz** (EEG), **0.1–15 Hz** (EOG)
- $y$ is the discrete control command

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Value/Unit |
|---|---------|---------|--------------------|
| 1 | $V_{\text{RMS}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T}\int_0^T v^2(t)\,dt}$ | EMG / bioelectric signal energy | 50 µV – 5 mV |
| 2 | $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$ | Nyquist sampling theorem | $f_s = 1\text{ kHz}$ (EMG), $256\text{ Hz}$ (EEG) |
| 3 | $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{\text{signal}}}{V_{\text{noise}}}\right)$ | Signal quality metric | **> 20 dB** desired |
| 4 | $\theta_{\text{eye}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{EOG}}}{k_{\text{EOG}}}$ | Eye angle from EOG voltage | $k_{\text{EOG}} \approx 20\ \mu\text{V/deg}$ |
| 5 | $P_{\text{breath}} = \rho g h$ | Sip-and-puff pressure from height | $h$ in cm H₂O |
| 6 | $\tau = RC$ | High-pass / low-pass time constant | $\tau_{\text{EMG,HP}} = 0.01\text{ s}$ |
| 7 | $F_{\text{control}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{commands}}}{T_{\text{task}}}$ | Information throughput (bits/min) | **0–25 bpm** EEG, **60–120 bpm** multimodal |
| 8 | $\eta_{\text{crosstalk}} = \dfrac{\text{RMS}_{\text{adjacent}}}{\text{RMS}_{\text{primary}}} \times 100\%$ | Channel isolation quality | **< 10 %** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas appear in KTU ESE problems related to **prosthetic controller design**, **BCI bit-rate estimation**, and **sip-and-puff calibration**. Master the SNR, RMS, and Nyquist equations — they are recurrent.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — RMS Voltage of an EMG Signal

A surface EMG signal $v(t)$ is treated as a zero-mean, band-limited, stochastic process with Gaussian amplitude distribution.

**Step 1.** Define the instantaneous power across a load resistor $R$:

$$
p(t) = \dfrac{v^2(t)}{R}
$$

**Step 2.** The average power over a window of duration $T$ is:

$$
\bar{p} = \dfrac{1}{T}\int_0^T \dfrac{v^2(t)}{R}\,dt
$$

**Step 3.** Equate this to the power delivered by an equivalent DC voltage $V_{\text{RMS}}$:

$$
\dfrac{V_{\text{RMS}}^2}{R} = \dfrac{1}{T}\int_0^T \dfrac{v^2(t)}{R}\,dt
$$

**Step 4.** Cancel $R$ on both sides:

$$
V_{\text{RMS}}^2 = \dfrac{1}{T}\int_0^T v^2(t)\,dt
$$

**Step 5.** Take the positive square root (voltage is non-negative):

$$
V_{\text{RMS}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T}\int_0^T v^2(t)\,dt}
$$

**Step 6.** In discrete (sampled) form, for $N$ samples acquired at sampling rate $f_s$:

$$
V_{\text{RMS}} \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{n=1}^{N} v^2[n]},\qquad T = \dfrac{N}{f_s}
$$

**Numerical sanity check:** For a typical biceps EMG of amplitude $v_{\text{peak}} = 1\text{ mV}$ (sinusoidal approximation):

$$
V_{\text{RMS}} = \dfrac{v_{\text{peak}}}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{1\text{ mV}}{1.414} \approx 0.707\text{ mV}
$$

which lies inside the expected **50 µV – 5 mV** clinical range.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Sampling Rate Requirement for EEG

The EEG signal carries clinically relevant information up to the gamma band ($\approx 40\text{ Hz}$ in standard clinical recordings, up to $100\text{ Hz}$ in research).

**Step 1.** Identify the maximum useful frequency component:

$$
f_{\max} = 40\text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 2.** Apply the Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem:

$$
f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}
$$

**Step 3.** Substitute:

$$
f_s \geq 2 \times 40\text{ Hz} = 80\text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 4.** Add the engineering safety margin (anti-aliasing filter roll-off):

$$
f_s^{\text{practical}} = 2.5 \times f_{\max} \approx 100\text{ Hz (minimum)}
$$

**Step 5.** Standard KTU convention:

$$
\boxed{\,f_s = 256\text{ Hz (clinical EEG)},\quad f_s = 512\text{ Hz (research-grade)}\,}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Eye Angle from EOG Voltage

The eye acts as a dipole: the cornea is positive relative to the retina by a standing potential $V_0 \approx 0.4\text{–}1.0\text{ mV}$. Skin electrodes placed left/right of the eye capture the horizontal component.

**Step 1.** The recorded EOG voltage is proportional to the sine of the eye-rotation angle:

$$
V_{\text{EOG}} = k \sin(\theta_{\text{eye}})
$$

where $k$ is the corneo-retinal gain in µV/deg.

**Step 2.** For small saccades ($\theta_{\text{eye}} < 20°$), $\sin(\theta) \approx \theta$ in radians:

$$
V_{\text{EOG}} \approx k \cdot \theta_{\text{eye}}
$$

**Step 3.** Solve for the eye angle:

$$
\theta_{\text{eye}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{EOG}}}{k}
$$

**Step 4.** Substitute the standard KTU value $k \approx 20\ \mu\text{V/deg}$:

$$
\theta_{\text{eye}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{EOG}}}{20\ \mu\text{V/deg}}
$$

**Numerical example:** If $V_{\text{EOG}} = 200\ \mu\text{V}$,

$$
\theta_{\text{eye}} = \dfrac{200}{20} = 10° \text{ (horizontal saccade)}
$$

This derivation is the **foundation of EOG-based wheelchair control** for ALS patients.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Anatomic Site

| User Condition | Rank-1 Site | Rank-2 Site | Rank-3 Site | Avoid |
|----------------|-------------|-------------|-------------|-------|
| Below-elbow amputee | Residual forearm EMG | Contralateral hand switch | — | EEG (overkill) |
| Above-elbow amputee | Shoulder switch + biceps/triceps EMG | 3-axis inertial shoulder | Tongue | EOG |
| High SCI (C4 intact) | Sip-and-puff | Head array switch | Chin joystick | Hand EMG |
| Locked-in syndrome | EEG (motor imagery) | EOG | Auditory ERP (P300) | Mechanical switches |
| Cerebral palsy (athetosis) | Head pointer / IR switch | EMG gating | Voice | Direct EOG (artifact) |
| ALS (advanced) | EOG + single switch | Brain–computer interface | — | Voice, sip-puff |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Block Diagram — Anatomic Sites → AT Control Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Human Body] --> B[Anatomic Site Selection]
    B --> C{Signal Type}
    C --> D[Bioelectric - EMG EEG EOG]
    C --> E[Biomechanical - Head Shoulder Tongue Residual Limb]
    C --> F[Bioacoustic - Voice Speech]
    C --> G[Autonomic - Breath Skin]
    D --> H[Electrodes + Amplifier]
    E --> I[Mechanical Transducer - Switch Accelerometer Encoder]
    F --> J[Microphone + DSP]
    G --> K[Pressure Sensor GSR Sensor]
    H --> L[Band-pass Filter]
    L --> M[ADC Sampler]
    I --> N[Signal Conditioning]
    J --> N
    K --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[Feature Extraction - RMS MAV Zero-crossing]
    O --> P[Classifier - LDA SVM Neural Net Threshold]
    P --> Q[AT Device Command]
    Q --> R[Wheelchair]
    Q --> S[Prosthetic Limb]
    Q --> T[Communication AAC]
    Q --> U[Environmental Control ECU]
```

## 4.2 Sub-Diagram — EMG-Based Prosthetic Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Brain Motor Command] --> A2[Residual Muscle]
    A2 --> A3[Surface Electrode Pair]
    A3 --> A4[Differential Amplifier Gain 1000]
    A4 --> A5[Band-pass 20 to 500 Hz]
    A5 --> A6[Rectifier and Smoother]
    A6 --> A7[ADC at 1 kHz]
    A7 --> A8[Microcontroller]
    A8 --> A9[Prosthetic Motor Driver]
    A9 --> A10[Prosthetic Hand or Wrist]
    A10 --> A11[Force Feedback optional]
    A11 --> A2
```

## 4.3 Sub-Diagram — BCI Pipeline for Locked-In Patient

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Visual Stimulus] --> B2[Retina] --> B3[Visual Cortex]
    B3 --> B4[EEG Electrode Array 8 to 64 channel]
    B4 --> B5[Differential Amplifier]
    B5 --> B6[Band-pass 0.5 to 40 Hz]
    B6 --> B7[ADC 256 Hz]
    B7 --> B8[Feature - P300 or Motor Imagery]
    B8 --> B9[LDA Classifier]
    B9 --> B10[Speller or Wheelchair Command]
```

## 4.4 Sub-Diagram — Sip-and-Puff Pneumatic Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1[User Mouth] --> C2[Hard Inhale]
    C2 --> C3[Pressure Transducer Negative cmH2O]
    C1 --> C4[Hard Exhale]
    C4 --> C5[Pressure Transducer Positive cmH2O]
    C3 --> C6[Threshold Comparator]
    C5 --> C6
    C6 --> C7[Soft Sip Soft Puff = 4 Quadrant Proportional]
    C7 --> C8[Wheelchair Direction]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any three anatomic sites commonly used for controlling assistive devices in persons with high-level spinal cord injury (C1–C4). For each site, name the corresponding transducer.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per site + transducer pair):**

1. **Sip-and-puff / oral cavity** → pressure transducer (binary or proportional pneumatic switch).
2. **Head motion (neck flexion/extension, lateral tilt)** → mercury tilt switch or 3-axis MEMS accelerometer mounted on headrest.
3. **Shoulder elevation / depression (via trapezius or deltoid remnant EMG)** → surface EMG electrodes.

*(Alternative valid: voice (microphone), tongue (inductive/optical palatal sensor), EOG (periorbital electrodes).)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the corneo-retinal standing potential and state its typical magnitude. Why is this relevant to assistive device control?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **corneo-retinal standing potential** is the small DC voltage that exists between the **positive cornea** and the **negative retina** of the eyeball, generated by the metabolic activity of the retinal pigment epithelium.

- **Magnitude:** approximately **0.4 to 1.0 mV** (typical value used in KTU: ~0.5 mV).
- **Relevance:** Because the eye rotates within this static dipole field, skin electrodes placed around the orbit can record the **Electrooculogram (EOG)**, whose voltage is proportional to eye angle ($\approx 20\ \mu\text{V}$ per degree). This signal is exploited to drive **EOG-based wheelchairs and communication aids** for users with ALS or locked-in syndrome who retain ocular motility.

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **EMG-based control of a myoelectric prosthetic hand**. Identify the anatomic sites from which the EMG signals are picked up for a below-elbow amputee. State typical signal amplitude and the recommended sampling frequency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **RMS value of the EMG signal** and compute the SNR (in dB) of a prosthetic controller where $V_{\text{signal}} = 1.2\text{ mV (RMS)}$ and $V_{\text{noise}} = 30\ \mu\text{V (RMS)}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

1. **Anatomic sites (2 marks):** For a below-elbow amputee, surface EMG is recorded from the **residual forearm flexor** (e.g., flexor carpi radialis, flexor digitorum superficialis) and **extensor** compartments — these two muscle groups retain voluntary control and naturally map to *hand-close* and *hand-open* intent. For an above-elbow amputee, the **biceps** and **triceps** are used.

2. **Block diagram (3 marks):**
$$\text{Muscle} \rightarrow \text{Surface electrodes (Ag/AgCl)} \rightarrow \text{Instrumentation amplifier (gain} \approx 1000\text{)} \rightarrow \text{Band-pass filter (20–500 Hz)} \rightarrow \text{Rectifier + smoother} \rightarrow \text{ADC} \rightarrow \text{Microcontroller} \rightarrow \text{Motor driver} \rightarrow \text{Prosthetic hand}$$

3. **Signal specs (2 marks):**
   - Amplitude: **50 µV to 5 mV (RMS)**
   - Sampling frequency: **≥ 1 kHz** (Nyquist with safety margin for 500 Hz upper cutoff)

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Derivation (5 marks):**

$$
V_{\text{RMS}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T}\int_0^T v^2(t)\,dt}
$$

(Show the derivation steps as in Section 3.1 above — define instantaneous power, average power, equate to DC equivalent, cancel $R$, take square root.)

**Numerical computation (2 marks):**

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{\text{signal}}}{V_{\text{noise}}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1.2 \times 10^{-3}}{30 \times 10^{-6}}\right)
$$

$$
= 20 \log_{10}(40) = 20 \times 1.602 = \boxed{32.04\text{ dB}}
$$

This is well above the **20 dB threshold** for reliable prosthetic control.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — *Alternative choice*

**(a)** Explain the principle of **EOG-based control of an electric wheelchair** for a person with advanced ALS. Identify the anatomic sites, the governing equation, and the typical signal range. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **sip-and-puff system** is used by a C3 quadriplegic to control a powered wheelchair. The hard puff threshold is set at **+8 cm H₂O** and hard sip at **−8 cm H₂O**, with a dead band of **±1 cm H₂O**. Determine the **four logical commands** generated and justify, with a block diagram, why this anatomic site is preferred over a hand-operated joystick for this user. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

1. **Anatomic sites (2 marks):** Skin electrodes placed at the **left outer canthus, right outer canthus** (for horizontal) and **above/below the eye** (for vertical) capture the EOG. The corneo-retinal dipole of the eye acts as the source.

2. **Governing equation (3 marks):**
$$
V_{\text{EOG}} = k \sin(\theta_{\text{eye}}), \quad \theta_{\text{eye}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{EOG}}}{k},\quad k \approx 20\ \mu\text{V/deg}
$$

3. **Signal range and control mapping (2 marks):**
   - $V_{\text{EOG}}$: **100–500 µV** for moderate saccades
   - Eye-left → wheelchair-left; eye-right → wheelchair-right; blink → stop; double-blink → horn

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

1. **Four commands (4 marks):**
   - $P > +8\text{ cm H}_2\text{O}$ (hard puff) → **Forward**
   - $P < -8\text{ cm H}_2\text{O}$ (hard sip) → **Reverse**
   - $+1 < P < +8\text{ cm H}_2\text{O}$ (soft puff) → **Right turn**
   - $-8 < P < -1\text{ cm H}_2\text{O}$ (soft sip) → **Left turn**
   - $-1 \leq P \leq +1\text{ cm H}_2\text{O}$ → **Neutral / dead band**

2. **Justification with block diagram (3 marks):**

$$
\text{Mouth} \rightarrow \text{Pneumatic tube} \rightarrow \text{Pressure transducer} \rightarrow \text{Dead-band logic} \rightarrow \text{Wheelchair motor controller}
$$

**Why preferred:** A C3 quadriplegic retains **diaphragmatic and oral muscle control** (phrenic nerve originates C3–C5) but has lost all hand/arm function. Sip-and-puff exploits *voluntary breath*, which is the most distal voluntary effector still under cortical command. A hand joystick is **physically inaccessible** at this level.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Marks Lost**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in SNR / RMS calculations (−1 to −2 marks).
> 2. **Citing wrong amplitude range** for EEG (it's *µV*, not mV — examiners deduct heavily).
> 3. **Confusing EOG sites** (periorbital skin electrodes) with EEG sites (scalp / cortex) — site identification is a separate marking point.
> 4. **Skipping the dead-band explanation** in sip-and-puff questions — full 2 marks are reserved for this.
> 5. **Not stating Nyquist with safety margin** — pure 2·f_max loses a partial credit in ESE.
> 6. **Using programming code (Python / MATLAB scripts)** in the answer sheet for non-CS subjects — **strictly prohibited**; write equations only.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [ ] An **anatomic control site** = any body location whose physiologic output (electric, mechanical, acoustic, pneumatic) is reliably transduced into an AT command.
- [ ] **EMG sites:** residual forearm (below-elbow), biceps/triceps (above-elbow), shoulder muscles (high SCI); amplitude **50 µV – 5 mV**; band-pass **20–500 Hz**.
- [ ] **EEG sites:** scalp (motor cortex, visual cortex); amplitude **10–100 µV**; band-pass **0.5–40 Hz**; sample at **≥ 256 Hz**.
- [ ] **EOG sites:** periorbital skin electrodes; governed by corneo-retinal standing potential **0.4–1.0 mV**; sensitivity **≈ 20 µV/deg**.
- [ ] **Mechanical sites:** head (tilt switch / IMU), shoulder (3-position switch), tongue (intra-oral inductive / Hall sensor), chin (mini-joystick).
- [ ] **Sip-and-puff:** pressure thresholds **±8 cm H₂O** (binary) or proportional range **±2 to ±15 cm H₂O**; ideal for **C1–C4 SCI**.
- [ ] **Voice sites:** laryngeal microphone; fundamental frequency **85–180 Hz (male)** / **165–255 Hz (female)**; unsuitable for dysarthria.
- [ ] **Decision cascade:** preserve natural limb → fall back to proximal → fall back to bioelectric → fuse multimodal.
- [ ] **Key formulas (must memorize):** $V_{\text{RMS}} = \sqrt{\tfrac{1}{T}\int v^2 dt}$, $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$, $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 20\log_{10}(V_s/V_n)$, $\theta_{\text{eye}} = V_{\text{EOG}}/k$.
- [ ] **Bit-rate benchmark:** EEG-BCI ≈ **25 bits/min**; multimodal EMG + switch ≈ **60–120 bits/min**; able-bodied joystick ≈ **600 bits/min**.
- [ ] **Clinical SNR threshold:** **> 20 dB** for reliable AT control.
- [ ] **Cross-modality crosstalk:** **< 10 %** is the engineering target.
- [ ] **Always draw the block diagram** in any 7-mark sub-question — it carries **2–3 marks** by itself.
- [ ] **No code snippets** in the answer sheet for this course — only equations, tables, and diagrams.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Elements of the Human/Technology Interface
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT614 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Elements of the Human/Technology Interface

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

The **Human/Technology Interface (HTI)** in the context of assistive medical devices refers to the complete bidirectional communication system that enables information exchange between a human user (with sensory, motor, or cognitive impairment) and a technological system (prosthetic, orthotic, wheelchair, communication aid, or rehabilitative device). It is formally defined as the integrated assembly of **sensors, signal conditioning circuits, controllers, actuators, and feedback displays** that transduces physiological intent into machine action and conversely, conveys machine status to the user.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Core):**
> The Human-Technology Interface consists of five functional elements: **(1) Biological Signal Source**, **(2) Sensor/Transducer**, **(3) Signal Processor**, **(4) Controller**, and **(5) Actuator with Feedback Display**. The interface is considered *effective* when signal fidelity, latency, and cognitive load are all minimized below the user's perceptual threshold.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car. Your hands on the steering wheel are **sensors** detecting your intent. The steering column, power steering pump, and ECU form the **signal processor and controller**. The wheels turning are the **actuators**. The road feel transmitted back through the steering wheel is **feedback**. The dashboard showing speed and RPM is the **display**.

Now, imagine the car is your own body and one of your arms is missing. The Human/Technology Interface is the entire chain — from the nerves that still "fire" in your residual limb, to the electrodes on the skin, to the microcontroller, to the motors in a robotic hand, to the vibration buzzers that tell you "you are gripping too hard." It is a **translator** that converts biological intent into mechanical action.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:** The interface is NEVER just one device. It is a *chain*. A failure at any link (electrode, amplifier, classifier, or actuator) causes the entire assistive device to fail in the eyes of the user.

### 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics

The quality of any HTI is measured by:

- **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR):** Target $\geq 20$ dB for reliable biosignal classification.
- **Latency:** End-to-end delay must be $\leq 300$ ms to feel "natural" to the user.
- **Classification Accuracy:** $\geq 95\%$ for clinical-grade intent recognition.
- **Bit Rate (for communication BCIs):** Measured in **bits/min**, target $\geq 60$ bits/min.
- **Power Consumption:** Wearable interfaces target $< 500$ mW.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Functional Elements — Structured Logic Breakdown

**Element 1 — Biological Signal Source**
- *What it is:* A measurable biopotential or physical variable generated by the human body.
- *Why it matters:* It is the *raw information* the device interprets. Without it, no assistive command can be issued.
- *Common types:* Electromyogram (EMG), Electroencephalogram (EEG), Electrooculogram (EOG), Electrocardiogram (ECG), mechanomyogram (MMG), and residual limb force/torque.

**Element 2 — Sensor / Transducer**
- *What it is:* A hardware component that converts a biological variable into an electrical signal.
- *Why it matters:* It is the *primary transducer* at the body side. Selection dictates what can be measured.
- *Common types:* Surface electrodes (Ag/AgCl), dry-contact electrodes, needle electrodes, force-sensitive resistors (FSR), accelerometers, gyroscopes, and inductive position sensors.

**Element 3 — Signal Conditioning & Processor**
- *What it is:* Analog front-end (amplification, filtering) followed by digital feature extraction and classification.
- *Why it matters:* Biosignals are tiny (microvolts to millivolts) and buried in noise. Without this stage, the controller receives garbage.
- *Typical stages:* Instrumentation amplifier (gain 1000×), band-pass filter (e.g., 20–500 Hz for EMG, 0.5–40 Hz for EEG), 50/60 Hz notch filter, ADC (≥12-bit, ≥1 kS/s for EMG), feature extraction (RMS, MAV, zero-crossings, AR coefficients), and classifier (LDA, SVM, k-NN, or neural network).

**Element 4 — Controller**
- *What it is:* The decision-making unit (microcontroller, DSP, or FPGA) that maps classified intent to actuator commands.
- *Why it matters:* It is the *brain* of the device. It enforces safety limits, mode switching, and state machines.
- *Functions:* Trajectory generation, impedance/admittance control, closed-loop feedback law execution, and emergency stop.

**Element 5 — Actuator with Feedback Display**
- *What it is:* The motor/piezo/pneumatic element that produces motion, plus the display/haptic system that informs the user.
- *Why it matters:* Without it, the classified intent goes nowhere. Without feedback, the user cannot tell if their command was correctly interpreted.
- *Actuator types:* DC servomotors, brushless DC motors, pneumatic muscles, shape-memory alloys, and voice coils.
- *Feedback types:* Vibrotactile motors, electrotactile stimulation, visual LCD/LED arrays, auditory cues, and force reflection.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** A well-designed HTI keeps the *forward path* (intent → action) under **150 ms** and the *feedback path* (action → perception) under **200 ms**, summing to a total round-trip latency well below the human reflex threshold of ~250 ms.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Parameter | Symbolic Form | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Electrode-skin impedance | $Z_{skin} = R_s \parallel \frac{1}{j\omega C_d}$ | $\vert Z_{skin} \vert \approx 5\text{ k}\Omega$ at 1 Hz (gel); $\geq 100\text{ k}\Omega$ (dry) |
| 2 | Common-Mode Rejection Ratio | $CMRR = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)$ | $\geq 80$ dB (clinical) |
| 3 | Skin preparation resistance reduction | $R_{wet} \approx 0.1 \, R_{dry}$ | Up to $10\times$ lower |
| 4 | EMG amplitude (surface) | $V_{RMS} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T v^2(t)\, dt}$ | $50\ \mu\text{V}$ to $5$ mV |
| 5 | Shannon-Hartley Theorem (BCI bit rate) | $I = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)$ | bits / second |
| 6 | Nyquist Sampling Theorem | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | $f_s \geq 1000$ Hz for EMG |
| 7 | Closed-loop transfer function | $T(s) = \frac{C(s)G(s)}{1 + C(s)G(s)}$ | Stability: $\vert T(j\omega) \vert < 1$ at crossover |
| 8 | Steady-state error (unity feedback) | $e_{ss} = \lim_{s\to 0} s \cdot \frac{R(s)}{1 + C(s)G(s)}$ | $0$ for Type-1 system |
| 9 | Force feedback gain | $F_{fb} = K_{fb} \, (x_{ref} - x_{actual})$ | N (Newtons) |
| 10 | Weber's Law (just-noticeable difference) | $\frac{\Delta I}{I} = k$ (constant) | $k \approx 0.08$ for vibrotactile intensity |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symbol Reminder for Tables:** All absolute-value / magnitude expressions above use `\vert` (not `|`) to preserve markdown table integrity — a common KTU answer-script formatting error.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The HTI framework is not academic — it is the design template used in commercial systems such as the **DEKA LUKE Arm** (3rd-gen prosthetic), **C-Leg** (Ottobock microprocessor knee), **Independence Drive** (EEG wheelchair), and **Argus II Retinal Prosthesis** (epiretinal electrode array). Every regulatory submission to the US FDA or EU MDR must demonstrate traceable performance across all five elements.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Effective SNR at the Analog Front-End

The signal measured by a surface electrode is the superposition of the true biosignal, environmental interference, and intrinsic amplifier noise. Let us derive the effective SNR delivered to the classifier.

**Step 1 — Define Signal and Noise Components**

Let $v_{bio}(t)$ be the true biological signal (e.g., EMG during voluntary contraction), $v_{line}(t)$ be the 50 Hz power-line interference coupled through the body, $v_{motion}(t)$ be motion artefact from electrode movement, and $n_{amp}(t)$ be the amplifier's intrinsic noise. The total measured voltage is:

$$
v_m(t) = v_{bio}(t) + v_{line}(t) + v_{motion}(t) + n_{amp}(t)
$$

**Step 2 — Express the Line Interference in Closed Form**

Assume the line interference is a sinusoid of amplitude $A_L$ and frequency $f_L$:

$$
v_{line}(t) = A_L \sin(2\pi f_L t)
$$

The right-leg driver (driven-right-leg circuit) produces an inverted common-mode feedback $v_{RLD}(t) = -G_{cm}\, v_{cm}(t)$ that cancels this term at the body, where $v_{cm}(t)$ is the common-mode voltage and $G_{cm}$ is the common-mode feedback gain (typically 40 dB).

**Step 3 — Apply the Notch Filter Transfer Function**

After amplification, a second-order notch filter centered at $f_L$ has transfer function:

$$
H_{notch}(s) = \frac{s^2 + \omega_L^2}{s^2 + B s + \omega_L^2}
$$

where $\omega_L = 2\pi f_L$ and $B$ is the bandwidth of the notch. For an ideal notch, $H_{notch}(j\omega_L) = 0$, eliminating the 50 Hz component.

**Step 4 — Compute the Output Noise Power**

After filtering, the residual noise power spectral density is approximately white over the band of interest, so the integrated noise power is:

$$
P_n = \int_{f_1}^{f_2} S_n(f) \, df \approx S_0 \, (f_2 - f_1)
$$

where $f_1, f_2$ are the filter band-edges, and $S_0$ is the noise floor (units: V²/Hz).

**Step 5 — Compute Effective SNR**

$$
SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{signal}}{P_n}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{signal}^{2}}{S_0\,(f_2 - f_1)}\right)
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** For a clinical EMG system, $V_{signal} \approx 1$ mV (RMS), $S_0 \approx 10^{-14}$ V²/Hz, and $(f_2 - f_1) = 480$ Hz, giving:

$$
SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{(10^{-3})^2}{10^{-14} \cdot 480}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(2.08 \times 10^{5}) \approx 53\ \text{dB}
$$

This satisfies the $\geq 20$ dB clinical threshold by a wide margin.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Classification Bit-Rate for a Communication BCI

**Step 1 — Shannon's Channel Capacity**

$$
I = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \quad \text{(bits/second)}
$$

**Step 2 — Substituting BCI Parameters**

For a P300 speller, effective bandwidth $B \approx 20$ Hz (slow cortical potential sampling), and SNR $\approx 0.5$ (signal is buried in EEG noise). Substituting:

$$
I = 20 \log_2(1 + 0.5) = 20 \log_2(1.5)
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical Evaluation**

Since $\log_2(1.5) = \frac{\ln(1.5)}{\ln 2} = \frac{0.4055}{0.6931} \approx 0.585$:

$$
I = 20 \times 0.585 = 11.7\ \text{bits/s} \approx 702\ \text{bits/min}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Letters per Minute**

Assuming 5 bits per character (English alphabet has 26 letters, $\log_2 26 \approx 4.7$ bits/letter):

$$
\text{LPM} = \frac{702}{4.7} \approx 149\ \text{letters/min}
$$

This is comparable to the KTU examiner's handwritten typing speed and a major achievement for a non-invasive BCI.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Interface Modality

| User Profile | Recommended HTI Modality | Sensor Type | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Upper-limb amputee (transradial) | EMG pattern recognition | Surface EMG electrodes | Residual muscles still produce strong, classifiable signals |
| High-level spinal cord injury (C4) | EEG-based BCI | Dry EEG cap | Only cortical signals are accessible |
| Locked-in patient | EOG + EEG hybrid | Peri-ocular electrodes | Eye movements remain voluntary |
| Visually impaired | Vibrotactile / audio feedback | Voice-coil tactors | Substitute for missing visual channel |
| Hearing impaired | Vibrotactile alerting | Wrist-worn pager | Converts audio cues to touch |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of the Human/Technology Interface

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Biological Signal Source] --> B[Sensor / Transducer]
    B --> C[Analog Front-End Amplifier]
    C --> D[Band-Pass and Notch Filter]
    D --> E[Analog to Digital Converter]
    E --> F[Feature Extraction Module]
    F --> G[Classifier Engine]
    G --> H[Microcontroller Controller]
    H --> I[Actuator Driver]
    I --> J[Mechanical Actuator Output]
    J --> K[Human User]
    K --> L[Feedback Display]
    L --> K
    H --> L
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **forward path** flows from `A` (muscle / brain) through signal acquisition (`B → E`), intelligence (`F → G`), decision (`H`), and mechanical action (`I → J`).
- The **feedback path** flows from `J` (actuator output) back to the user via `L` (display/tactor), allowing the user to perceive and correct their input.
- The closed loop `K → A → ... → J → L → K` is what makes the device an *interface* rather than a one-way controller.

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — Closed-Loop Prosthetic Hand

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph USER_ZONE[USER BIOLOGICAL ZONE]
        U1[Residual Limb Muscles]
        U2[Peripheral Nerves]
        U3[Somatosensory Cortex]
    end

    subgraph INTERFACE_ZONE[HTI INTERFACE ZONE]
        I1[Surface Electrode Array]
        I2[Instrumentation Amplifier]
        I3[Digital Signal Processor]
        I4[Intent Classifier]
        I5[Embedded Controller]
        I6[Vibrotactile Feedback Driver]
    end

    subgraph DEVICE_ZONE[ASSISTIVE DEVICE ZONE]
        D1[Motor Driver H-Bridge]
        D2[Servo Motor Array]
        D3[Mechanical Fingers]
        D4[Force Sensitive Resistors]
    end

    U1 --> I1
    I1 --> I2
    I2 --> I3
    I3 --> I4
    I4 --> I5
    I5 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
    D3 --> K[User Grasps Object]
    K --> D4
    D4 --> I5
    I5 --> I6
    I6 --> U3
```

**Reading the Architecture:**
- `USER_ZONE` contains the biological sources — muscles generate EMG, nerves carry afferent signals, and the cortex perceives feedback.
- `INTERFACE_ZONE` is the engineered bridge; every block here must be optimized for low latency.
- `DEVICE_ZONE` is the mechanical world that interacts with the environment.
- The bottom feedback chain (`D4 → I5 → I6 → U3`) closes the loop by encoding grip force as vibration pulses that the cortex can perceive.

### 4.3 Signal Flow Stages vs. Latency Budget

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Biosignal Generation] -->|5 ms| B[Electrode Pickup]
    B -->|2 ms| C[Amplification and Filtering]
    C -->|1 ms| D[ADC Sampling]
    D -->|3 ms| E[Feature Extraction]
    E -->|2 ms| F[Classification]
    F -->|1 ms| G[Controller Decision]
    G -->|5 ms| H[Motor Command]
    H -->|30 ms| I[Mechanical Motion]
    I -->|20 ms| J[Force Sensor Reading]
    J -->|10 ms| K[Vibrotactile Stimulus]
    K -->|40 ms| L[User Perception]
```

**Reading the Latency Diagram:** The total round-trip latency sums to approximately **119 ms**, comfortably below the 250 ms human reflex threshold. Students should learn to break down *every* system into its constituent delays — KTU board questions frequently ask for this analysis.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

List and briefly define the five functional elements of a Human/Technology Interface used in assistive medical devices.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Biological Signal Source:** The physiological signal (EMG, EEG, EOG, etc.) generated by the user's body that encodes intent. *(0.5 mark)*
2. **Sensor/Transducer:** The electrode or physical sensor that converts the biological variable into an electrical signal. *(0.5 mark)*
3. **Signal Processor:** The analog and digital circuitry (amplifier, filter, ADC, feature extractor) that conditions and digitizes the signal. *(1 mark)*
4. **Controller:** The microcontroller / DSP that interprets classified intent and issues actuator commands, including safety logic. *(0.5 mark)*
5. **Actuator with Feedback Display:** The motor/pneumatic/piezo element that produces motion, plus the visual/tactile/auditory display that closes the loop to the user. *(0.5 mark)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

What is the typical latency budget for a clinically acceptable Human/Technology Interface, and why is this value important?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The end-to-end latency (from intent generation to feedback perception) must be **≤ 300 ms**, with the forward path ideally $\leq 150$ ms and the feedback path $\leq 200$ ms. *(2 marks)* This is critical because the human stretch reflex operates in 200–250 ms; any longer delay is perceived as "sluggish" by the user, breaks the sense of embodiment, and leads to device rejection in clinical practice. *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the complete signal chain of a myoelectric prosthetic hand. Identify the role of each block and the key performance parameters. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the effective Signal-to-Noise Ratio at the output of a two-stage EMG acquisition system that uses an instrumentation amplifier with gain $G = 1000$, followed by a band-pass filter (20–500 Hz) and a 50 Hz notch filter. Assume a residual noise spectral density of $S_0 = 5 \times 10^{-15}$ V²/Hz and a typical EMG RMS amplitude of 1.5 mV. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram and Roles (7 marks):**

The myoelectric prosthetic hand signal chain consists of the following blocks (1 mark per block, 1 mark for parameters):

1. **Residual Limb Muscles** — generate the EMG signal during voluntary contraction (50 µV to 5 mV).
2. **Surface Ag/AgCl Electrodes** — transduce ionic currents in the skin to electron flow in the wire; target $\vert Z_{skin} \vert < 10$ kΩ.
3. **Instrumentation Amplifier (INA128 or AD620)** — provides differential gain of 1000× with CMRR $\geq 80$ dB to reject common-mode 50 Hz interference.
4. **Band-Pass Filter (20–500 Hz)** — removes DC offset, motion artefact, and high-frequency noise; preserves the EMG energy band.
5. **50 Hz Notch Filter (Q=8)** — eliminates residual power-line interference.
6. **ADC (12-bit, 1 kS/s)** — samples the conditioned analog signal into digital values; sample rate follows Nyquist ($f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$).
7. **Feature Extractor (RMS, MAV, ZC, WL)** — reduces each window to a feature vector.
8. **Classifier (LDA, SVM, or ANN)** — assigns the feature vector to one of N grip classes.
9. **Embedded Controller (ARM Cortex-M4)** — selects motor commands, enforces safety limits, and runs the closed-loop state machine.
10. **H-Bridge Motor Driver + DC Servo Motors** — actuate the fingers.

**Key Performance Parameters:** Classification accuracy $\geq 95\%$, latency $\leq 150$ ms (forward path), SNR $\geq 20$ dB, power consumption $\leq 500$ mW. *(1 mark)*

---

**(b) SNR Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify the signal power (1 mark):**

$$
V_{signal}^{2} = (1.5 \times 10^{-3})^2 = 2.25 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{V}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the equivalent noise bandwidth after filtering (2 marks):**

The band-pass filter is 20–500 Hz, so:

$$
B_{BPF} = 500 - 20 = 480\ \text{Hz}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute total noise power (2 marks):**

$$
P_n = S_0 \cdot B_{BPF} = 5 \times 10^{-15} \cdot 480 = 2.4 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{V}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Compute SNR in linear scale and then in dB (2 marks):**

$$
SNR_{linear} = \frac{2.25 \times 10^{-6}}{2.4 \times 10^{-12}} = 9.375 \times 10^{5}
$$

$$
SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(9.375 \times 10^{5}) = 10 \cdot (5 + \log_{10} 9.375)
$$

Since $\log_{10}(9.375) \approx 0.972$:

$$
SNR_{dB} \approx 10 \cdot (5 + 0.972) = 59.72\ \text{dB}
$$

**Conclusion:** The system achieves an SNR of approximately **59.7 dB**, far exceeding the 20 dB clinical threshold and ensuring robust classifier performance.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Define the Human/Technology Interface. Explain the difference between EEG-based and EMG-based assistive interfaces, citing two clinical applications of each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A P300-based Brain-Computer Interface has effective bandwidth $B = 15$ Hz and SNR $S/N = 0.8$. Calculate the channel capacity in bits/s and the equivalent letters per minute (assuming 5 bits per letter). Comment on whether this meets the clinical threshold for a communication prosthesis. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Definition and Comparison (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** The Human/Technology Interface is the integrated hardware-software chain that mediates bidirectional information flow between a human user (typically with impairment) and an assistive device, comprising sensors, signal processors, controllers, actuators, and feedback displays.

**Differences (3 marks):**

| Aspect | EEG-Based Interface | EMG-Based Interface |
|---|---|---|
| Signal Source | Cortical (brain) electrical activity | Muscle electrical activity |
| Acquisition | Scalp electrodes (non-invasive) | Surface/needle electrodes on muscle |
| Signal Amplitude | 5–100 µV | 50 µV to 5 mV |
| Bandwidth | 0.5–40 Hz | 20–500 Hz |
| User Group | Locked-in, ALS, high SCI | Limb amputee, motor neuropathy |
| Time Resolution | Slow (P300, SSVEP) | Fast (real-time motion intent) |

**Clinical Applications (2 marks):**
- **EEG-based:** (i) P300 speller for locked-in patients, (ii) SSVEP wheelchair for ALS users.
- **EMG-based:** (i) Myoelectric prosthetic hand for transradial amputees, (ii) Functional Electrical Stimulation (FES) for foot-drop in stroke patients.

---

**(b) Channel Capacity Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Apply Shannon-Hartley Theorem (2 marks):**

$$
I = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) = 15 \log_2(1 + 0.8)
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Logarithm (2 marks):**

$$
\log_2(1.8) = \frac{\ln(1.8)}{\ln 2} = \frac{0.5878}{0.6931} \approx 0.848
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Channel Capacity (1 mark):**

$$
I = 15 \times 0.848 = 12.72\ \text{bits/s} \approx 763\ \text{bits/min}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Letters per Minute (1 mark):**

$$
LPM = \frac{763}{5} \approx 152\ \text{letters/min}
$$

**Step 5 — Clinical Threshold Comment (1 mark):** A clinically viable communication BCI should achieve $\geq 5$ letters/min to be considered useful. At 152 letters/min, this P300 speller **far exceeds the clinical threshold** and approaches the typing speed of a non-impaired user on a smartphone keyboard (~200 letters/min), making it highly suitable for clinical deployment.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing the Interface with a single device.** The HTI is a *chain* — if you describe only the sensor or only the actuator, you will lose 3–4 marks immediately. Always list all five elements.
> 2. **Using `|` for absolute value in markdown/PDF tables.** This breaks the table layout. Always use `\vert` or `\mid`.
> 3. **Skipping units in derivations.** KTU examiners explicitly deduct 0.5 marks for missing units (V, Hz, dB, mV, etc.).
> 4. **Forgetting the closed-loop feedback path.** A purely forward-path description is incomplete. Always mention at least one feedback modality (visual, vibrotactile, electrotactile, auditory, or force reflection).
> 5. **Confusing "latency" and "reaction time."** Latency is the *device* delay; reaction time is the *human* delay (~200 ms). They are additive but not identical.
> 6. **Writing SNR as a ratio without converting to dB** when the question asks for dB. Always give both forms if unsure.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **The HTI is a five-element chain:** Biological Source → Sensor → Signal Processor → Controller → Actuator + Feedback.
- **The interface is bidirectional** — both forward (intent → action) and feedback (action → perception) paths are mandatory.
- **Electrode-skin impedance** is the first bottleneck; target $\vert Z \vert < 10$ kΩ at 1 Hz for wet electrodes.
- **CMRR** of the instrumentation amplifier must be $\geq 80$ dB for clinical EMG.
- **Nyquist sampling** demands $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$; for EMG, $f_s \geq 1000$ Hz is standard.
- **End-to-end latency** must be $\leq 300$ ms for user acceptance; the 250 ms reflex threshold is the hard upper limit.
- **SNR target** for clinical biosignal classification is $\geq 20$ dB; well-designed systems achieve 40–60 dB.
- **Feature extraction** (RMS, MAV, ZC, WL, AR coefficients) reduces data dimensionality before classification.
- **Classifiers** in commercial prosthetics are dominated by **LDA** (simplicity) and **ANN/ SVM** (accuracy).
- **Shannon's theorem** sets the upper bound for BCI bit-rate; $I = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$.
- **Feedback modalities** include vibrotactile (most common in prosthetics), electrotactile, visual, and auditory.
- **Closed-loop stability** requires the transfer function $T(s) = \frac{C(s)G(s)}{1 + C(s)G(s)}$ to have all poles in the left-half plane (Bode/Nyquist criteria).
- **Clinical thresholds:** $\geq 5$ letters/min for communication BCIs, $\geq 95\%$ classification accuracy for prosthetic BCIs.
- **Safety:** Every controller must include hardware and software emergency-stop, current limiting, and watchdog timer.
- **Regulatory:** All clinically deployed HTIs must comply with IEC 60601-1 (medical electrical equipment safety) and ISO 14708 (implants).
- **KTU favourite question pattern:** "Explain the Human/Technology Interface with a block diagram" — always present the diagram **and** the five-element description, never just one.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Speech
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBRT614 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Speech ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition of Speech **Speech** is a complex, time-varying **acoustic pressure wave** produced by the coordinated physiological activity of the human vocal apparatus (respiratory, laryngeal, and articulatory subsystems) and shaped by the resonant cavities of the **vocal tract**. From an engineering perspective, speech is treated as a **non-stationary quasi-periodic biomedical signal** carrying linguistic, paralinguistic, and emotional information between a speaker and a listener. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Speech is the acoustic manifestation of language, generated when a modulated air stream from the lungs is shaped by the **vocal folds (source)** and filtered by the **vocal tract (filter)**. The study of speech in assistive medical devices focuses on: (i) compensating for **speech impairments**, (ii) restoring communication via **prosthetic speech devices**, and (iii) enabling **alternative communication (AAC)**. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, the study of "Speech" within *Assistive Medical Devices* focuses on three engineering pillars: | Pillar | Engineering Focus | Clinical Application | |---|---|---| | **Speech Production Modeling** | Source-Filter theory, Formant estimation | Understanding dysphonia, dysarthria | | **Speech Synthesis (TTS)** | Articulatory / Concatenative / HMM-based synthesis | Text-to-speech for ALS, locked-in patients | | **Speech Recognition (ASR)** | Feature extraction (MFCC), HMM, Deep Learning | Voice-controlled wheelchairs, smart homes | ### 1.2 The Human Speech Production Mechanism Speech is produced by a coordinated three-tier system: 1. **Respiratory System (Power Supply):** The **lungs** and **diaphragm** generate the expiratory airflow that acts as the *energy source* (analogous to a power amplifier's DC supply). 2. **Laryngeal System (Sound Source):** The **vocal folds** (within the **larynx**) oscillate to convert airflow into a periodic or aperiodic acoustic signal — the *excitation source*. 3. **Articulatory System (Filter/Modulator):** The **pharynx, oral cavity, nasal cavity, tongue, lips, and teeth** act as a variable acoustic filter that shapes the spectral envelope of the source signal. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy — "The Musical Instrument":** > Think of speech production exactly like playing a **violin**: > - The **bow moving across the string** is analogous to the **lungs pushing air** through the vocal folds. > - The **violin string vibrating** is the **vocal folds** producing the fundamental buzz. > - The **violin's wooden body** is the **vocal tract**, whose shape (moved by the fingers/articulators) amplifies certain harmonics (formants) and dampens others. > > When the violin player's fingers move, the *same string vibration* produces entirely *different notes*. Similarly, when you change the shape of your mouth/tongue, the *same vocal-fold buzz* produces different vowels (e.g., "ah" vs "ee"). ### 1.4 Types of Speech Sounds (Phonetics) | Category | Manner of Production | Examples | |---|---|---| | **Voiced** | Vocal folds vibrate; periodic source | /a/, /i/, /u/, /m/, /n/ | | **Unvoiced** | Vocal folds inactive; aperiodic/noise source | /s/, /f/, /sh/, /p/, /t/ | | **Plosive (Stop)** | Complete closure then sudden release | /p/, /b/, /t/, /d/, /k/, /g/ | | **Fricative** | Turbulent airflow through narrow constriction | /s/, /z/, /f/, /v/ | | **Nasal** | Airflow diverted through nasal cavity | /m/, /n/, /ng/ | | **Vowel** | Open vocal tract; formant-dominated | /a/, /e/, /i/, /o/, /u/ | ### 1.5 Standard Metrics & Constants for Speech Signals > [!IMPORTANT] > **Industry-Standard Sampling & Frequency Parameters:** > - **Fundamental frequency (F₀)** of adult speech: **85 Hz – 180 Hz (males)**, **165 Hz – 255 Hz (females)**, **250 Hz – 400 Hz (children)**. > - **Speech bandwidth:** approximately **300 Hz to 3400 Hz** (narrowband, telephony) and **50 Hz to 8000 Hz** (wideband, high-fidelity). > - **Standard sampling rate:** **16 kHz** (telephony/ASR) or **44.1 kHz** (high-fidelity). > - **Speed of sound in air (c):** **343 m/s** at 20 °C. > - **Standard Frame length for analysis:** **20 ms – 30 ms** (stationarity window). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Source-Filter Model of Speech Production (Fant, 1960) The **Source-Filter Model**, formalized by Gunnar Fant, is the cornerstone theoretical framework for speech production. It **linearizes** and **decouples** the speech signal into two mathematically independent components: $$ s(t) = e(t) \ast h(t) $$ Where: - $s(t)$ = radiated speech pressure wave at the lips/nostrils - $e(t)$ = excitation (glottal) source waveform - $h(t)$ = impulse response of the vocal tract filter - $\ast$ = convolution operator In the **frequency domain**, this decouples into simple multiplication: $$ S(f) = E(f) \cdot H(f) $$ This separation is the *engineering foundation* of all modern speech codecs, vocoders, and text-to-speech engines. ### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of Speech Production **Step 1 — Air Pressure Generation (Power Stage):** The diaphragm contracts, increasing intra-pulmonic pressure $P_L$ (typically 5–15 cm H₂O during conversational speech). This creates a quasi-steady expiratory airflow $U$ through the glottis. **Step 2 — Glottal Excitation (Source Stage):** The vocal folds, controlled by the **cricothyroid** and **thyroarytenoid** muscles, undergo **phonation**: - Voiced sounds → **periodic** glottal pulse train with period $T_0 = 1/F_0$. - Unvoiced sounds → **aperiodic noise** generated at constrictions (turbulent flow, Reynolds number > 2000). **Step 3 — Vocal Tract Filtering (Filter Stage):** The vocal tract (length ≈ **17.5 cm** in adult males) acts as a non-uniform acoustic tube closed at one end (glottis) and open/closed at the other (lips/nostrils). It exhibits **resonances** (formants) that selectively amplify frequency bands. **Step 4 — Radiation at the Lips (Radiation Stage):** The lips act as a high-pass radiator, modeled as a differentiation operator. The final pressure wave $s(t)$ is what we record as the speech signal. ### 2.3 Why and How — Engineering Rationale > [!NOTE] > **Why does the source-filter model matter for assistive devices?** > - It allows **independent diagnosis**: clinicians can determine if a disorder is *source-related* (e.g., vocal fold paralysis → low F₀) or *filter-related* (e.g., cleft palate → distorted formants). > - It enables **substitution**: an electrolarynx provides the source, while the user's intact vocal tract still acts as the filter — preserving intelligibility. > - It powers **TTS engines** (e.g., formant synthesis, HTS, Tacotron) where source and filter parameters are explicitly generated. ### 2.4 Acoustic Theory of the Vocal Tract — Closed-Open Tube Modeling the vocal tract as a uniform tube of length $L = 17.5$ cm, closed at the glottis and open at the lips, the **resonant (formant) frequencies** are given by: $$ F_n = \frac{(2n - 1)\,c}{4L}, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \dots $$ For a typical adult male vocal tract: $$ F_1 = \frac{c}{4L} \approx \frac{343}{4 \times 0.175} \approx 490 \text{ Hz} $$ $$ F_2 = \frac{3c}{4L} \approx 3 \times 490 \approx 1470 \text{ Hz} $$ $$ F_3 = \frac{5c}{4L} \approx 5 \times 490 \approx 2450 \text{ Hz} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Vowel Identification by Formants:** $F_1$ and $F_2$ are sufficient to identify virtually all vowels. For example, /i/ (as in "see") has low $F_1$ (~270 Hz) and high $F_2$ (~2300 Hz), while /u/ (as in "boot") has low $F_1$ (~300 Hz) and low $F_2$ (~870 Hz). ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Source-Filter Model (time) | $s(t) = e(t) \ast h(t)$ | — | Speech synthesis, vocoders | | 2 | Source-Filter Model (freq.) | $S(f) = E(f) \cdot H(f)$ | — | Spectral analysis | | 3 | Formant frequency (closed-open tube) | $F_n = (2n-1)\,c / (4L)$ | Hz | Vowel classification | | 4 | Fundamental frequency | $F_0 = 1/T_0$ | Hz | Pitch detection, prosody | | 5 | Nyquist Sampling Criterion | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Hz | ADC design for speech | | 6 | Standard sampling rate | $f_s = 16\,\text{kHz}$ (telephony) | Hz | Codec / ASR systems | | 7 | Frame energy | $E = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x^2(n)$ | — | VAD (Voice Activity Detection) | | 8 | ZCR (Zero Crossing Rate) | $Z = \tfrac{1}{2(N-1)} \sum \vert \text{sgn}(x_n) - \text{sgn}(x_{n-1}) \vert$ | — | Voiced/Unvoiced classification | | 9 | Short-Time Energy | $E_n = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} [x(m) \, w(n-m)]^2$ | — | Endpoint detection | | 10 | Linear Prediction (LPC) | $\hat{s}(n) = \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\, s(n-k)$ | — | Vocal tract modeling | | 11 | Sound Speed (air, 20°C) | $c = 343$ m/s | m/s | Acoustic calculations | | 12 | Speech Intensity (normal) | ~ **60 dB SPL** at 1 m | dB | Audiometric calibration | ### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Biomedical Engineering - **Electrolarynx Design:** A handheld buzzer substitutes the failed glottal source for laryngectomees; the patient's intact vocal tract continues to act as the filter, producing intelligible (though monotone) speech. - **Cochlear Implants:** Convert formant frequencies and temporal envelope cues into electrical stimulation patterns — directly leveraging the source-filter structure. - **Silent Speech Interfaces (SSI):** Use EMG, ultrasound tongue imaging, or permanent magnetic articulography (PMA) to reconstruct the filter parameters when the source is unavailable. - **Tracheoesophageal (TE) Speech:** Post-laryngectomy patients redirect lung airflow through a one-way valve (Provox, Blom-Singer) into the esophagus, restoring a usable source. - **AAC Devices (Augmentative & Alternative Communication):** Eye-tracking, brain-computer interfaces (BCI), or single-switch scanning generate synthetic speech via TTS engines. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Formant Frequencies of a Uniform Closed-Open Vocal Tract **Goal:** Derive the resonant frequencies of a uniform tube of length $L$, closed at the glottal end ($x = 0$) and open at the lip end ($x = L$). **Step 1: Establish the Acoustic Wave Equation.** For plane-wave propagation in a lossless tube, the pressure $p(x,t)$ satisfies the 1-D wave equation: $$ \frac{\partial^{2} p(x,t)}{\partial x^{2}} = \frac{1}{c^{2}} \frac{\partial^{2} p(x,t)}{\partial t^{2}} $$ **Step 2: Apply the Method of Separation of Variables.** Assume a separable harmonic solution of the form: $$ p(x,t) = X(x) \, T(t) = A \cos(kx) \cos(\omega t) $$ where $k = \omega / c$ is the wavenumber, $\omega = 2\pi f$ is the angular frequency, and $c$ is the speed of sound. **Step 3: Apply the Closed-End Boundary Condition.** A rigid closed end (at the glottis, $x = 0$) requires zero volume velocity, which means the **pressure antinode** occurs here: $$ \left. \frac{\partial p}{\partial x} \right|_{x=0} = 0 $$ Substituting $p(x,t) = A \cos(kx) \cos(\omega t)$: $$ \frac{\partial p}{\partial x} = -A k \sin(kx) \cos(\omega t) \;\Big|_{x=0} = -A k \sin(0) \cos(\omega t) = 0 $$ This boundary condition is **automatically satisfied** for any $k$ (since $\sin(0) = 0$). Hence, $\cos(kx)$ is the correct spatial form. **Step 4: Apply the Open-End Boundary Condition.** At the open lip end ($x = L$), the pressure must equal atmospheric (assumed zero gauge), giving a **pressure node**: $$ p(L, t) = A \cos(kL) \cos(\omega t) = 0 \;\;\forall\,t $$ This requires: $$ \cos(kL) = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad kL = \frac{(2n - 1)\pi}{2}, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \dots $$ **Step 5: Solve for the Resonant Frequencies.** Since $k = 2\pi f / c$, we substitute: $$ \frac{2\pi f_n L}{c} = \frac{(2n - 1)\pi}{2} $$ Solving for $f_n$: $$ \boxed{\,F_n = \frac{(2n - 1)\,c}{4L}, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \dots\,} $$ **Step 6: Numerical Evaluation for an Adult Male Vocal Tract.** Take $L = 17.5$ cm $= 0.175$ m, $c = 343$ m/s, $n = 1, 2, 3$: $$ \begin{aligned} F_1 &= \frac{(2(1) - 1) \times 343}{4 \times 0.175} = \frac{1 \times 343}{0.700} = 490.0 \text{ Hz} \\[6pt] F_2 &= \frac{(2(2) - 1) \times 343}{4 \times 0.175} = \frac{3 \times 343}{0.700} = 1470.0 \text{ Hz} \\[6pt] F_3 &= \frac{(2(3) - 1) \times 343}{4 \times 0.175} = \frac{5 \times 343}{0.700} = 2450.0 \text{ Hz} \end{aligned} $$ These correspond closely to empirical formant measurements for the neutral vowel /ə/ (schwa). ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Minimum Sampling Rate for Speech (Nyquist Criterion) **Goal:** Determine the minimum sampling frequency $f_s$ required to digitize a speech signal with bandwidth $B$ without aliasing. **Step 1: State the Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem.** A bandlimited signal with maximum frequency $f_{max}$ can be perfectly reconstructed from its samples if and only if: $$ f_s \geq 2 \, f_{max} $$ **Step 2: Identify the Speech Bandwidth.** For high-fidelity wideband speech: $$ B = f_{max} - f_{min} \approx 8000 - 50 \approx 7950 \text{ Hz} $$ For narrowband (telephony): $$ B \approx 3400 - 300 = 3100 \text{ Hz} $$ **Step 3: Compute the Required Sampling Rate.** Wideband case: $$ f_s = 2 \times 8000 = 16{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 16 \text{ kHz} $$ Narrowband case: $$ f_s = 2 \times 4000 = 8000 \text{ Hz} = 8 \text{ kHz} $$ **Step 4: Apply an Anti-Aliasing Filter.** A low-pass anti-aliasing filter with cutoff at $f_s/2$ is inserted before the ADC to ensure no spectral content above Nyquist enters the sampler. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** The ITU-T G.711 standard (used in landline telephony) uses $f_s = 8$ kHz with 8-bit companded $\mu$-law PCM, yielding a 64 kbps bitrate — the basis of the public switched telephone network (PSTN). ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Linear Predictive Coding (LPC) of Speech **Goal:** Show how the vocal tract filter is estimated from the speech waveform using linear prediction. **Step 1: Formulate the Prediction Problem.** Given a speech sample $s(n)$, predict it as a linear combination of its past $p$ samples: $$ \hat{s}(n) = \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \, s(n - k) $$ **Step 2: Define the Prediction Error (Residual).** $$ e(n) = s(n) - \hat{s}(n) = s(n) - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \, s(n - k) $$ **Step 3: Minimize the Mean-Squared Error.** Minimize $E = \mathbb{E}[e^2(n)]$ with respect to each $a_k$: $$ \frac{\partial E}{\partial a_k} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \mathbb{E}\left[ e(n) \, s(n - k) \right] = 0, \quad k = 1, 2, \dots, p $$ This yields the **Yule-Walker equations**: $$ \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \, R(\vert i - k \vert) = R(i), \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, p $$ where $R(\tau) = \mathbb{E}[s(n) s(n+\tau)]$ is the autocorrelation of the speech frame. **Step 4: Solve Using the Levinson-Durbin Recursion** (an $O(p^2)$ algorithm), yielding coefficients $\{a_1, a_2, \dots, a_p\}$ that model the vocal tract transfer function: $$ H(z) = \frac{1}{1 - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k z^{-k}} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Key Point:** LPC is the mathematical engine behind formant-estimation, low-bitrate speech codecs (e.g., GSM, MELP, LPC-10e at 2.4 kbps), and classical speech recognition front-ends. ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Speech Disorder vs. Source/Filter Defect | Disorder | Clinical Name | Defective Stage | Source / Filter Impact | Typical Assistive Device | |---|---|---|---|---| | Aphonia (post-laryngectomy) | Loss of voice | **Source** | No glottal excitation | Electrolarynx, TEP valve, TTS | | Dysarthria | Slurred/articulatory weakness | **Filter (articulators)** | Distorted formants | AAC device, alphabet board | | Dysphonia | Hoarseness/breathiness | **Source** (vocal fold) | Irregular glottal pulses | Voice amplifier, therapy | | Stuttering | Fluency disorder | **Neuromotor timing** | Repetitions/prolongations | DAF (Delayed Auditory Feedback) device | | Broca's Aphasia | Non-fluent aphasia | **Neurolinguistic planning** | Agrammatic, effortful | AAC + language therapy apps | | Velopharyngeal Insufficiency | Hypernasality | **Filter (velum)** | Nasal coupling of formants | Speech bulb prosthesis, surgery | | Hearing Loss (profound) | Sensorineural | **Perception** | Cannot decode speech | Cochlear implant, hearing aid | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Human Speech Production — Functional Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Lungs and Diaphragm
Respiratory Power Source] --> B[Trachea
Airflow Conduit] B --> C[Larynx and Vocal Folds
Glottal Excitation Source] C --> D[Pharynx
Upper Vocal Tract Coupling] D --> E[Oral Cavity
Articulator Filter 1] D --> F[Nasal Cavity
Articulator Filter 2] E --> G[Tongue and Lips
Fine Articulation] F --> G G --> H[Radiated Sound Pressure Wave
s of t at Lips and Nostrils] H --> I[Acoustic Environment
Propagation Medium] I --> J[Listener Ear and Auditory System
Receiver] ``` ### 4.2 Source-Filter Model — Engineering Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Periodic Pulse Train
Voiced Excitation F0] --> M[Multiplier / Adder] B[Noise Generator
Unvoiced Excitation] --> M M --> F[ Vocal Tract Filter
H of z via LPC or Formants] F --> R[Lip Radiation Filter
High Pass Differentiator] R --> OUT[Speech Signal s of t] style M fill:#ffd6a5 style F fill:#caffbf style R fill:#bdb2ff ``` ### 4.3 Text-to-Speech (TTS) System — Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD IN[Input Text] --> T1[Text Normalization
Expand numbers abbreviations] T1 --> T2[Phonetic Transcription
Grapheme to Phoneme G2P] T2 --> T3[Prosody Prediction
Pitch Duration Energy] T3 --> T4[Acoustic Model
Concatenative or Neural Tacotron] T4 --> T5[Vocoder
Griffin Lim or WaveNet] T5 --> OUT[Synthesized Speech Waveform] style IN fill:#e0fbfc style T4 fill:#ffadad style T5 fill:#ffd6a5 style OUT fill:#caffbf ``` ### 4.4 Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC) Device — Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR INPUT[User Input
Switch Eye Gaze BCI EMG] --> CTRL[Access Method Controller] CTRL --> SEL[Symbol or Letter Selection
Encoding Minspeak or Rate Enhancement] SEL --> STORE[Message Storage Buffer] STORE --> TTS[Embedded TTS Engine
Multilingual] TTS --> AMP[Audio Amplifier] AMP --> SPK[Loudspeaker Output] TTS --> DISP[Text Display Screen] style INPUT fill:#ffadad style TTS fill:#caffbf style SPK fill:#bdb2ff ``` ### 4.5 Cochlear Implant — Speech Processing Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR MIC[Microphone Array] --> AGC[Automatic Gain Control] AGC --> BPF[Bandpass Filter Bank
22 to 64 Channels] BPF --> ENVL[Envelope Detector and Smoothing] ENVL --> CIS[Continuous Interleaved Sampling CIS Strategy] CIS --> COD[RF Encoder and Coil Transmission] COD --> IMPL[Implanted Receiver and Electrode Array in Cochlea] IMPL --> AUDITORY[Auditory Nerve Stimulation] style MIC fill:#fdffb6 style CIS fill:#caffbf style IMPL fill:#ffadad ``` ### 4.6 Electrolarynx — Functional Block Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR BAT[Battery Power Supply 9V] --> OSC[Piezomechanical Oscillator
Fixed F0 approx 100 to 200 Hz] OSC --> INTENS[Intensity Control Potentiometer] INTENS >> TRANSD[Transducer Diaphragm Held Against Neck] TRANSD -.Couples Vibration.-> TISSUE[Neck Tissue] TISSUE -.Vocal Tract Filter.-> AIR[Acoustic Speech Output at Mouth] style BAT fill:#caffbf style OSC fill:#ffadad style TRANSD fill:#bdb2ff ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** *With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the Source-Filter model of speech production.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Source-Filter model (Gunnar Fant, 1960) decouples speech production into an **excitation source** and a **vocal tract filter**, related as: $$ s(t) = e(t) \ast h(t) \;\;\text{or equivalently}\;\; S(f) = E(f) \cdot H(f) $$ - **Source $e(t)$:** Glottal pulse train (voiced) at fundamental frequency $F_0$ or noise (unvoiced). - **Filter $h(t)$:** Vocal tract impulse response, characterized by formant frequencies $F_1, F_2, F_3 \dots$ - **Radiation:** High-pass differentiation at the lips. - **Block diagram:** Source → Vocal Tract Filter → Radiation → Output Speech. *(Mark Split: 1.5 marks for theory + 1.5 marks for diagram/equation.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** *List any three speech parameters used in clinical assessment of voice and state their typical normal values in adult males.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Fundamental Frequency ($F_0$):** ~ **85–180 Hz** in adult males. 2. **Jitter (Frequency Perturbation):** Cycle-to-cycle variation in $F_0$, normal < **1%** (≈0.5–1.0%). 3. **Shimmer (Amplitude Perturbation):** Cycle-to-cycle variation in amplitude, normal < **0.5 dB** (≈0.2–0.5 dB). 4. *(Bonus)* **Harmonics-to-Noise Ratio (HNR):** Normal > **20 dB**. --- ### Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) --- **Q3.
