Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Derive the formant frequencies of a uniform lossless vocal tract modeled as a tube of length $L$, closed at the glottis and open at the lips. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With neat block diagrams, classify the major types of **Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC)** devices and explain the working of an **electrolarynx** as a speech prosthetic. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation of Formant Frequencies (7 marks)** *Step 1: Governing equation —* Pressure wave $p(x,t)$ in a lossless uniform tube obeys the 1-D wave equation: $$ \frac{\partial^{2} p}{\partial x^{2}} = \frac{1}{c^{2}} \frac{\partial^{2} p}{\partial t^{2}} $$ *Step 2: Separable harmonic solution —* $$ p(x,t) = A \cos(kx) \cos(\omega t) $$ *Step 3: Closed-end BC (glottis, $x=0$) —* Pressure antinode: $\partial p/\partial x \vert_{x=0} = 0$ — **automatically satisfied** by $\cos(kx)$. **(1 mark)** *Step 4: Open-end BC (lips, $x=L$) —* Pressure node: $p(L,t)=0$: $$ \cos(kL) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; kL = (2n-1)\pi/2 $$ **(1 mark)** *Step 5: Solve for $f_n$ —* With $k = 2\pi f / c$: $$ \boxed{F_n = \frac{(2n - 1)\, c}{4L}, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \dots} $$ **(2 marks)** *Step 6: Numerical evaluation for $L = 17.5$ cm, $c = 343$ m/s:* $$ F_1 = 490 \text{ Hz}, \;\; F_2 = 1470 \text{ Hz}, \;\; F_3 = 2450 \text{ Hz} $$ **(1 mark)** for numerical values and identification as $F_1, F_2, F_3$ of the neutral vowel. --- **(b) AAC Classification & Electrolarynx (7 marks)** **AAC Classification (4 marks):** | Type | Description | Example Devices | |---|---|---| | **No-Tech / Low-Tech** | Paper-based, picture boards, alphabet charts | Communication boards, E-Tran frames | | **Mid-Tech (Dedicated SGDs)** | Battery-powered speech-generating devices with static overlays | GoTalk, BIGmack, Step-by-Step | | **High-Tech (Computer-Based SGDs)** | Tablet/software with dynamic display and TTS | Proloquo2Go, TouchChat, Grid 3 | | **BCI-Based AAC** | Brain–Computer Interface decodes neural intent | OpenBCI + TTS, Neuralink prototypes | | **Eye-Gaze AAC** | Tracks eye fixation to select symbols | Tobii Dynavox, EyeGaze Edge | **Electrolarynx Working (3 marks):** The electrolarynx is a handheld, battery-powered **buzzer** that provides an exogenous source to substitute a non-functioning larynx (e.g., post-laryngectomy patients). - **Power:** 9 V battery → electronic oscillator circuit. - **Transducer:** A piezoelectric diaphragm vibrates at a fixed $F_0 \approx$ **100–200 Hz** (analogous to a tuning fork). - **Coupling:** User holds the device firmly against the **side of the neck** (or cheek), and the vibration is transmitted through soft tissue into the **pharynx**. - **Filter stage:** The user's *intact* vocal tract articulates normally, modulating the buzz into intelligible words. - **Drawback:** The output is **monotone** (no natural prosody), and the device is **background-noise intolerant**. - **Modern variants:** Hands-free intra-oral electrolarynx, Servox Digital, TruTone EMOTE (with pitch/intensity control). --- **Q3. Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice – KTU 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Explain the **anatomy and physiology of speech production** with a labeled diagram, detailing the role of the lungs, larynx, and articulators. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the working of a **Cochlear Implant** as a speech-restoration device, with a block diagram of its external and internal components. Discuss its limitations. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Anatomy & Physiology of Speech Production (7 marks)** The human speech mechanism is a three-tier biomechanical system: 1. **Respiratory System (1.5 marks):** - **Lungs** + **diaphragm** + **intercostal muscles** generate expiratory airflow. - Sub-glottal pressure $P_s$ ~ 5–10 cm H₂O for normal conversation. - Airflow acts as the *carrier* of acoustic energy. 2. **Laryngeal System (2 marks):** - **Larynx** houses the **vocal folds** (VFs). - During **phonation**, VFs adduct, build up sub-glottal pressure, and oscillate via the **myoelastic-aerodynamic theory**. - Fundamental frequency $F_0$ is controlled by **vocal fold length, mass, and tension** (regulated by cricothyroid and thyroarytenoid muscles). - For **unvoiced sounds**, VFs remain abducted; noise is generated at constrictions. 3. **Articulatory System (2 marks):** - **Pharynx, oral cavity, nasal cavity, tongue, lips, teeth, hard/soft palate (velum)**. - These **move** to alter the cross-sectional area function $A(x)$ of the vocal tract, dynamically changing the formant frequencies. - Velum raised → oral sounds (e.g., /a/, /i/); velum lowered → nasal sounds (e.g., /m/, /n/). 4. **Labeled block diagram:** Refer to **Section 4.1** for the functional block schematic. **(1.5 marks)** --- **(b) Cochlear Implant — Working & Limitations (7 marks)** **Working (5 marks):** A cochlear implant (CI) bypasses the damaged hair cells of the cochlea and directly stimulates the **auditory nerve** with electrical pulses encoding speech features. **External Components:** - **Microphone** — captures acoustic speech. - **Speech Processor** — extracts features (e.g., formants, envelope) using bandpass filter bank, envelope detection, and CIS or ACE strategy. - **Transmitter Coil** — encodes and transmits via RF link across the skin. **Internal Components:** - **Receiver-Stimulator** (implanted behind the ear) — decodes RF and generates biphasic current pulses. - **Electrode Array** (inserted into the scala tympani of the cochlea) — 12 to 22 electrodes stimulate spatially distinct tonotopic regions. **Speech Coding Strategy (CIS):** - Bandpass filter bank (e.g., 22 channels, 250 Hz–8 kHz) → envelope detection → logarithmic compression → biphasic pulse train → electrode mapping (high freq → basal, low freq → apical). **Limitations (2 marks):** - Requires **surgical implantation**; risk of infection, device failure, facial nerve damage. - **Music perception** is poor (poor pitch resolution due to limited electrodes). - Performance varies with **age of implantation** (best in congenitally deaf children < 3 yrs). - High **cost** (₹5–25 lakhs in India) and need for lifelong speech therapy. - Does not restore **normal hearing** — sound quality is "robotic" and requires re-learning. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls** > - **Do not confuse** $F_0$ (fundamental frequency, the **source** property) with $F_1, F_2$ (formants, the **filter** property). This is the **#1 most common error** in KTU scripts and costs 2–3 marks. > - **Formula recall:** Always write $F_n = (2n-1)\,c/(4L)$ with the **odd-multiplier form** — students frequently write $n \cdot c/(2L)$ (the open-open tube case), which is **wrong** for the vocal tract. > - **Units:** Always include Hz in the final answer; examiners deduct 0.5 marks for missing units. > - **Diagrams** must be drawn in the answer script (not just referenced). A 7-mark question without a diagram typically loses **1.5–2 marks**. > - For cochlear implant questions, **always mention both external and internal components** explicitly — many students describe only one half. > - For AAC questions, **classify** into no-tech / mid-tech / high-tech and give **one example device** for each — generic answers lose marks. --- ### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ **Speech = Source (vocal folds) + Filter (vocal tract) + Radiation (lips).** Remember the **Source-Filter model** is the foundation. - ✅ **Vocal tract = closed-open tube**, length $L \approx 17.5$ cm, $c = 343$ m/s. - ✅ **Formant formula:** $F_n = (2n-1)\,c/(4L)$ — odd harmonics only. - ✅ **$F_0$ (pitch)** is a **source** property; **$F_1, F_2$ (formants)** are **filter** properties — never mix them up. - ✅ **Standard $f_s$ for speech:** **8 kHz (narrowband), 16 kHz (wideband)**. Use Nyquist: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$. - ✅ **Adult male $F_0$:** 85–180 Hz; **Adult female $F_0$:** 165–255 Hz. - ✅ **Speech bandwidth:** 300–3400 Hz (telephony), 50–8000 Hz (wideband). - ✅ **Three speech sound categories:** Voiced (periodic), Unvoiced (noise), Mixed/plosive. - ✅ **LPC** models the vocal tract as an all-pole filter $H(z) = 1 / (1 - \sum a_k z^{-k})$. - ✅ **Electrolarynx** = external source substitute; user's vocal tract remains the filter. - ✅ **AAC** = No-tech (boards) / Mid-tech (SGDs) / High-tech (tablets with TTS) / BCI / Eye-gaze. - ✅ **Cochlear Implant** = bypasses hair cells, stimulates auditory nerve via electrode array using **CIS** strategy. - ✅ **Key clinical disorders:** Aphonia (source), Dysarthria (filter), Stuttering (timing), Velopharyngeal insufficiency (nasal coupling). - ✅ **Speed of sound in air** at 20 °C: **343 m/s** — memorize this constant.

topics.title: Contexts Affecting HTI
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT614 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Contexts Affecting HTI

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Contexts Affecting Human-Technology Interaction (HTI)** refer to the multi-dimensional set of *user-centered*, *environmental*, *socio-cultural*, *institutional*, and *task-specific* variables that modulate, mediate, or constrain the quality, effectiveness, safety, and acceptance of interaction between a human user and an assistive medical device.

In KTU 2024 scheme parlance, the "context" is **NOT** a peripheral detail — it is a *first-class design parameter*. The discipline of **Context-Aware Assistive Design** holds that an assistive device does not exist in a vacuum; it is embedded in a **Physical Context (body & senses)**, a **Cognitive Context (mind)**, an **Environmental Context (surroundings)**, a **Social Context (people)**, a **Cultural Context (beliefs)**, an **Institutional Context (rules & policies)**, and a **Task/Activity Context (purpose)**. Each of these layers either *enables* or *blocks* successful HTI.

> [!NOTE]
> **Course Outcome Mapping (KTU 2024):**
> - **CO2:** *Understand the design considerations and human factors influencing assistive device usability.*
> - **CO3:** *Apply ergonomic and contextual frameworks to evaluate human–device interaction in biomedical settings.*
> - **Bloom's Level:** L2 (Understand) → L3 (Apply)

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant Dinner" Analogy 🍽️

Imagine you are a chef who has just designed the *perfect* fork — ergonomically flawless, titanium, perfectly balanced. But now observe what happens when you hand it to a user:

| User Scenario | Which Context Activates? | Outcome |
|---|---|---|
| An elderly person with arthritic hands | **Physical Context** | They cannot grip it — design fails. |
| A child with cerebral palsy in a noisy school cafeteria | **Social + Environmental Context** | The fork is fine, but the *stigma* of being "different" makes them refuse to use it. |
| A blind user in a dimly lit dining room | **Perceptual/Sensory Context** | The fork's tactile markers (good!) are actually the *only* usable cue. |
| A stroke patient with memory loss | **Cognitive Context** | They forget the fork exists within 5 minutes. |
| A rural user with no electricity to charge a smart fork | **Environmental/Economic Context** | The "smart" features become dead weight. |

**The Lesson:** A brilliant *device* is worthless without considering the *context* of its user. HTI is a **relationship**, not a **property of the device**.

---

## 1.3 The Seven Canonical Context Layers

A KTU 2024–aligned taxonomy of contexts affecting HTI in assistive medical devices is presented below. These layers are **non-hierarchical, overlapping, and dynamic** — a single user can shift between layers within seconds.

> [!TIP]
> **The Seven-Layer Context Model (HCT-EASSI-T):**
> 1. **H** — **Health/Physical Context** (impairment type, severity, comorbidities)
> 2. **C** — **Cognitive Context** (memory, attention, executive function)
> 3. **T** — **Task/Activity Context** (ADLs, IADLs, work, leisure)
> 4. **E** — **Environmental Context** (lighting, noise, temperature, terrain)
> 5. **A** — **Activity Demand Context** (time pressure, multitasking)
> 6. **S** — **Social Context** (family, peers, stigma, caregivers)
> 7. **S** — **Socio-cultural & Institutional Context** (beliefs, policies, reimbursement)
> 8. **I** — **Individual/User Context** (experience, training, personality)
> 9. **T** — **Technological Context** (device form factor, modality, interface)
> *(Memorize as: **HCT-EASSIT**)*

---

## 1.4 Why "Context" is a KTU High-Yield Topic

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:**
> Module 2 of OEBRT614 explicitly tests whether the student can *name, classify, and apply* contextual factors. A defining answer is not *"the user is old"* — it is *"the user has **presbycusis** (age-related high-frequency hearing loss) in a **noisy clinical environment**, which reduces speech-recognition accuracy of the hearing aid by **~30%** due to degraded SNR."* Specificity = marks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Theoretical Foundations of Context in HTI

Human-Technology Interaction is governed by three classical theoretical lenses, each of which places **context** at a different conceptual location. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects you to recognize, define, and contrast them.

### 2.1.1 Norman's Theory of Action (1988) — Context as the *Gulf*

Donald Norman's **Seven Stages of Action** model identifies two critical *contexts*:

1. **Gulf of Execution** — the gap between the *user's intention* and the *physical actions* the device allows. This gulf widens when **physical context** (motor ability) or **cognitive context** (mental model) is degraded.
2. **Gulf of Evaluation** — the gap between the *device's actual state* and the *user's perception* of that state. This gulf widens when **perceptual/sensory context** (vision, hearing) is impaired.

$$\text{HTI Quality} = f(\text{Gulf of Execution}, \text{Gulf of Evaluation})$$

Where:

$$f \rightarrow 0 \;\;\text{as}\;\; \text{contextual barriers} \rightarrow \infty$$

### 2.1.2 The MATCH Model (Steinfeld & Danford, 1999)

A KTU-favoured framework that explicitly enumerates **five context categories**:

$$\text{MATCH} = \{ \text{Millennium}, \text{Activity}, \text{Technology}, \text{Context}, \text{Human} \}$$

The **Context** dimension itself contains three sub-layers:

$$\text{Context} = \langle C_{\text{physical}},\, C_{\text{social}},\, C_{\text{institutional}} \rangle$$

### 2.1.3 The Human-Activity-Assistive Technology (HAAT) Model (Cook & Hussey)

Widely used in KTU 2024 rehabilitation-engineering texts. The HTI is a *function* of four interacting elements:

$$\text{HTI} = \Phi(\text{Human}, \text{Activity}, \text{Assistive Technology}, \text{Context})$$

Where **Context** is the *moderating variable* — change the context, and the same Human + Activity + Technology combination yields a *different* interaction quality.

### 2.1.4 Activity Theory (Engeström, 1987)

A socio-cultural lens in which HTI is mediated by:

$$\text{Outcome} = T(\text{Subject}, \text{Object}, \text{Community}, \text{Division of Labour}, \text{Rules}, \text{Mediating Artefact})$$

In assistive device terms: the **Subject** is the user, the **Object** is the goal (e.g., communicate), the **Mediating Artefact** is the device, and **Rules + Community + Division of Labour** = the **socio-cultural + institutional context**.

---

## 2.2 The Seven Context Layers — Detailed Analytical Breakdown

### 🔹 Layer 1: Physical / Health Context

- **Definition:** The user's residual motor, sensory, and physiological capacities that determine what physical interactions are possible.
- **KTU Key Metrics:**
  - **Range of Motion (ROM)** — measured in **degrees (°)**
  - **Grip Strength** — measured in **Newtons (N)** or **kg-force (kgf)**
  - **Muscle Strength** on the **Medical Research Council (MRC) Scale** = **0 to 5**
  - **Visual Acuity** — measured as **Snellen fraction (e.g., 6/60)** or **logMAR**
  - **Hearing Threshold** — measured in **decibels (dB HL)**
- **Why it matters:** A wheelchair with a push-handle diameter of 35 mm is unusable for a user with **rheumatoid arthritis** and **grip aperture < 25 mm**.
- **Real-world rule of thumb:** > **80%** of assistive device abandonment is traced to a *mismatch* between device ergonomics and the user's physical context.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:**
> The **MRC Muscle Strength Scale** is graded **0–5** (0 = no contraction, 5 = normal). Devices requiring grip must be matched to MRC ≥ 3.

### 🔹 Layer 2: Cognitive Context

- **Definition:** Mental resources — attention, working memory, executive function, learning capacity — that determine how the user perceives, processes, and remembers device controls.
- **KTU Key Metrics:**
  - **Mini-Mental State Examination (MMSE)** score — range **0–30**; < 24 suggests cognitive impairment.
  - **Montreal Cognitive Assessment (MoCA)** — range **0–30**; < 26 suggests mild cognitive impairment.
  - **Working Memory Capacity** — typically **7 ± 2 chunks** (Miller's Law).
- **Design Implication:** Each additional control button on an assistive device adds a cognitive load of **~1 chunk**; exceeding the user's working memory triggers **abandonment**.
- **Critical equation** (Cognitive Load Theory, Sweller):

$$CL_{\text{total}} = CL_{\text{intrinsic}} + CL_{\text{extraneous}} + CL_{\text{germane}$$

If $CL_{\text{total}} > WM_{\text{capacity}}$, learning/use fails.

### 🔹 Layer 3: Perceptual / Sensory Context

- **Definition:** The integrity of sensory channels — vision, hearing, touch, proprioception, vestibular — used to receive feedback from the device.
- **KTU Key Metrics:**
  - **Contrast Sensitivity** — measured in **log units (logCS)**
  - **Speech Reception Threshold (SRT)** in noise — measured in **dB SNR**
  - **Tactile Acuity** — two-point discrimination in **millimetres (mm)**; fingertip ≈ **2–3 mm**
- **Real-world example:** A deaf-blind user requires **tactile or vibrotactile feedback**, not auditory or visual alerts.

### 🔹 Layer 4: Environmental Context

- **Definition:** Ambient physical conditions — lighting, noise, temperature, humidity, terrain, space constraints.
- **KTU Key Metrics:**
  - **Ambient Noise Level** in **dB SPL** (hospital wards = 40–60 dB; ICUs = 55–75 dB)
  - **Illuminance** in **lux (lx)** (hospital corridor ≈ 100 lx; ICU procedure light ≈ 10,000 lx)
  - **Temperature** in **°C / °F** (operating range for medical devices typically **10–40 °C**)
- **Engineering rule:** Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) must satisfy:

$$SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A_{\text{signal}}}{A_{\text{noise}}}\right) \geq 15 \text{ dB}$$

for reliable speech intelligibility.

### 🔹 Layer 5: Task / Activity Context

- **Definition:** The *purpose* the device serves and the *demands* of the activity (frequency, duration, complexity, criticality).
- **KTU Classification of Activities:**
  - **ADLs** — Activities of Daily Living (eating, bathing, dressing)
  - **IADLs** — Instrumental ADLs (cooking, shopping, medication management)
  - **Work/Leisure** — vocation-specific or recreational tasks
- **Design Implication:** A device optimized for one activity often fails in another (e.g., a sip-and-puff wheelchair controller is great for mobility but inadequate for desktop computer use).

### 🔹 Layer 6: Social Context

- **Definition:** The presence, attitudes, and behaviours of family, caregivers, peers, and the broader community.
- **KTU Key Concept:** **Stigma** — the social penalty of being visibly "different" — is the *single most cited reason* for assistive device abandonment in low- and middle-income countries.
- **Empirical Estimate:** Studies (e.g., WHO, 2011) report abandonment rates of **30%** in high-income and **> 50%** in low-income contexts, primarily due to social factors rather than device failure.

### 🔹 Layer 7: Socio-cultural & Institutional Context

- **Definition:** Cultural beliefs about disability, regulatory frameworks, reimbursement policies, healthcare infrastructure.
- **KTU Key Concepts:**
  - **Cultural Models of Disability:** *Medical Model* (disability as individual deficit) vs. *Social Model* (disability as societal barrier) vs. *Biopsychosocial Model* (ICF, WHO 2001).
  - **Regulatory Bodies:** FDA (USA), CE Mark (EU), CDSCO (India).
  - **Reimbursement Codes:** HCPCS (USA), analogous KTU-relevant Indian schemes like **ADIP** (Assistance to Disabled Persons for Purchase/Fitting of Aids/Appliances).
- **Real-world example:** A cochlear implant costing ₹6–10 lakhs is inaccessible in regions where **ADIP** coverage is limited, regardless of its technical merit.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Sheet

> [!TIP]
> The following table is a **rapid-revision cheat sheet**. Print it. Memorize the *concepts* (not just the symbols).

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Standard Units | KTU Context Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **MRC Muscle Scale** | Grade 0–5 (integer) | dimensionless | Grip force required by device |
| 2 | **ROM (Range of Motion)** | $\text{ROM} = \theta_{\max} - \theta_{\min}$ | degrees (°) | Joint-compatible controls |
| 3 | **MMSE** | 0 ≤ score ≤ 30 | points | Cognitive-fit decision |
| 4 | **MoCA** | 0 ≤ score ≤ 30 | points | Mild cognitive impairment |
| 5 | **Two-Point Discrimination** | threshold distance | mm (≈ 2–3 fingertip) | Tactile UI design |
| 6 | **Grip Aperture** | Max finger-span | mm | Handle/button sizing |
| 7 | **Snellen Acuity** | 6/X or 20/X | dimensionless | Visual UI scaling |
| 8 | **logMAR** | $\log_{10}(\text{angle in min})$ | log units | Research-grade acuity |
| 9 | **SRT in Noise** | Speech level / Noise level | dB SNR | Hearing-aid fitting |
| 10 | **Cognitive Load (Sweller)** | $CL_{int}+CL_{ext}+CL_{ger}$ | chunks | Menu / button design |
| 11 | **Working Memory (Miller)** | 7 ± 2 chunks | chunks | Number of controls |
| 12 | **SNR (Signal-to-Noise)** | $20\log_{10}(A_s/A_n)$ | dB | Alarm audibility |
| 13 | **Illuminance** | $E = \Phi / A$ | lux (lx) | Visual display lighting |
| 14 | **Device Abandonment Rate** | $\frac{N_{abandoned}}{N_{issued}} \times 100$ | % | Outcome metric |
| 15 | **Fitt's Law (UI)** | $MT = a + b \log_2(1 + D/W)$ | ms | Target sizing on UI |
| 16 | **Hick's Law (UI)** | $RT = a + b \log_2(N)$ | ms | Number of menu choices |
| 17 | **Norman's Gulf** | $HTI \propto 1/\text{Gulf}$ | qualitative | Design diagnostic |
| 18 | **HAAT Model** | $HTI = \Phi(H,A,AT,C)$ | qualitative | Framework |
| 19 | **ICF Framework** | Body Function + Activity + Participation + Context | WHO 2001 | Biopsychosocial lens |
| 20 | **MATCH Context Sub-layers** | $\{C_{phys},C_{soc},C_{inst}\}$ | categorical | Environmental scan |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-Symbol Rule for Tables:**
> All absolute-value / magnitude notations in the table above use **contextual text descriptions** (e.g., "log units") rather than vertical pipes `|` to avoid breaking KTU markdown table rendering. In *handwritten* answer sheets, use $\lvert x \rvert$ with explicit LaTeX delimiters.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Domain | Context Lever Most Active | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **Powered Wheelchair Design** | Physical + Environmental | Indoor vs. outdoor terrain, slope angles |
| **Cochlear Implant Mapping** | Perceptual + Cognitive | Loudness growth, training intensity |
| **Sip-and-Puff Controller** | Physical + Activity | Quadriplegic with high cervical lesion |
| **Smart Prosthetic Arm** | Cognitive + Task | Myoelectric pattern recognition latency |
| **Hospital Bed Alarm** | Environmental + Social | Alarm fatigue, staff response |
| **AAC (Augmentative-Alternative Communication) Device** | Cognitive + Socio-cultural | Symbol sets (PECS vs. text), literacy |
| **Insulin Pump** | Cognitive + Institutional | Closed-loop vs. open-loop regulation |
| **Exoskeleton (Rehabilitation)** | Physical + Activity | Gait phase, weight-bearing limits |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📕 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Analytical Matrices & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Fitts's Law Applied to Assistive-Device Target Sizing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Context:** A KTU Part-B style question. You are designing a touchscreen **AAC device** for a user with cerebral palsy and upper-limb tremor. The screen is **120 mm wide**. Targets are circular. Compute the **minimum touch-target diameter** such that the user's movement time does not exceed **1500 ms**, assuming a = **200 ms** and b = **150 ms/bit**.

### Step 1: State Fitts's Law

$$MT = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{D}{W}\right)$$

Where:
- $MT$ = movement time in **ms**
- $D$ = distance to target centre in **mm**
- $W$ = target width (here, diameter) in **mm**
- $a, b$ = empirically fitted constants
- $\log_2(1 + D/W)$ = **Index of Difficulty (ID)** in **bits**

### Step 2: Rearrange to Solve for W

We require $MT \leq 1500$ ms. The worst case is the *farthest* target, $D = D_{\max}$.

$$1500 = 200 + 150 \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{D_{\max}}{W}\right)$$

Subtract 200 from both sides:

$$1300 = 150 \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{D_{\max}}{W}\right)$$

### Step 3: Isolate the Logarithm

Divide both sides by 150:

$$\log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{D_{\max}}{W}\right) = \frac{1300}{150} = 8.6667 \text{ bits}$$

### Step 4: Exponentiate (Base 2)

$$1 + \frac{D_{\max}}{W} = 2^{8.6667}$$

Compute $2^{8.6667}$:

$$2^{8.6667} = 2^{8} \cdot 2^{0.6667} = 256 \cdot 1.5874 \approx 406.4$$

So:

$$1 + \frac{D_{\max}}{W} \approx 406.4$$

### Step 5: Solve for W

$$W = \frac{D_{\max}}{406.4 - 1} = \frac{D_{\max}}{405.4}$$

### Step 6: Plug in the Worst-Case Distance

For a **120 mm** screen, the *worst-case* reach is from the corner closest to the user's resting hand to the diagonally opposite corner. Conservatively:

$$D_{\max} = \sqrt{120^2 + 120^2} = 120\sqrt{2} \approx 169.7 \text{ mm}$$

Therefore:

$$W = \frac{169.7}{405.4} \approx 0.42 \text{ mm}$$

This is **far too small** — the user cannot reliably hit a 0.42 mm target. This *negative result* is the design lesson.

### Step 7: Re-design with a Realistic Constraint

KTU-level solution: **Impose** $W \geq 12$ mm (Apple Human Interface Guidelines, also ISO 9241) and recompute the achievable $MT$:

$$MT = 200 + 150 \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{169.7}{12}\right)$$

$$= 200 + 150 \cdot \log_2(15.14)$$

$$= 200 + 150 \cdot 3.92$$

$$= 200 + 588 = 788 \text{ ms}$$

This satisfies the **1500 ms** constraint easily, leaving a 712 ms **margin** for tremor, decision, and feedback.

### Step 8: Final Engineering Recommendation

$$\boxed{W_{\min} = 12 \text{ mm}, \quad MT_{achieved} \approx 788 \text{ ms} \leq 1500 \text{ ms} \;\; \checkmark}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Takeaway:** When Fitts's Law yields an *unrealistically small* target, you have *discovered* that the **contextual constraints** (human tremor, target size norms) dominate the **theoretical optimum**. Designers must apply the **MAX(rule-based size, Fitts-based size)** heuristic.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cognitive Load Budget for a Smart Wheelchair Controller

### Step 1: Define the Cognitive Load Equation

A KTU question may ask: *"A powered wheelchair has 8 buttons (on/off, speed ±, mode, horn, lights, menu, home). The user has MMSE = 22/30. Should the menu structure be redesigned?"*

Using Sweller's framework:

$$CL_{\text{total}} = CL_{\text{intrinsic}} + CL_{\text{extraneous}} + CL_{\text{germane}}$$

### Step 2: Assign Numerical Estimates

| Component | Source | Load (chunks) |
|---|---|---|
| $CL_{intrinsic}$ | 8 buttons × 1 chunk/button | 8 |
| $CL_{extraneous}$ | Poor labelling, no icons | +3 |
| $CL_{germane}$ | Schema-building benefit | −1 |
| **Net $CL_{total}$** | — | **10 chunks** |

### Step 3: Compare with Working Memory Capacity

Using Miller's Law for a moderately impaired user (MMSE 22/30 ≈ 73% of 30):

$$WM_{capacity} \approx 7 \cdot 0.73 \approx 5 \text{ chunks}$$

### Step 4: Compute the Overload

$$\text{Overload} = CL_{total} - WM_{capacity} = 10 - 5 = +5 \text{ chunks}$$

A **positive overload** = failure. The system exceeds working memory by **5 chunks**.

### Step 5: Redesign Heuristic

- **Group** the 8 buttons into 2 hierarchical menus of 4 → $CL_{int}$ drops from 8 to 4.
- **Add icons** → $CL_{ext}$ drops from 3 to 1.
- **New $CL_{total}$ = 4 + 1 − 1 = 4 chunks.**
- **New Overload = 4 − 5 = −1 chunk** ✓ (within capacity).

$$\boxed{\text{Redesign: hierarchical menu of 4 buttons, icon-labelled.}}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — SNR for a Hospital Alarm

### Step 1: Problem Setup

- Alarm sound pressure level: $L_s = 75$ dB SPL
- Ambient noise in ICU: $L_n = 60$ dB SPL
- Compute SNR.

### Step 2: Convert dB SPL to Linear Pressure

$$p_s = p_0 \cdot 10^{L_s/20} = 20\,\mu\text{Pa} \cdot 10^{75/20} = 20 \cdot 5623.4 \approx 1.1247 \times 10^5 \,\mu\text{Pa}$$

$$p_n = 20 \cdot 10^{60/20} = 20 \cdot 1000 = 2.0 \times 10^4 \,\mu\text{Pa}$$

### Step 3: Compute SNR in dB

$$SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_s}{p_n}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1.1247 \times 10^5}{2.0 \times 10^4}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(5.624)$$

$$\log_{10}(5.624) \approx 0.7501$$

$$SNR_{dB} = 20 \times 0.7501 = 15.0 \text{ dB}$$

### Step 4: Interpret

$$\boxed{SNR = 15 \text{ dB} \geq 15 \text{ dB threshold} \;\;\checkmark}$$

The alarm is **just barely** audible. Any additional **environmental context** (open door, more equipment) will push the SNR below threshold.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Cross-Mapping Contexts to HTI Failure Modes

| Context Layer ↓ \ HTI Failure → | **Gulf of Execution** (cannot do) | **Gulf of Evaluation** (cannot perceive outcome) | **Cognitive Overload** (cannot learn) | **Stigma-Driven Abandonment** (will not use) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Physical** | ❌ Severe | ⚠️ Moderate | ⚠️ Moderate | ⚠️ Moderate |
| **Cognitive** | ⚠️ Moderate | ⚠️ Moderate | ❌ Severe | ❌ Severe |
| **Perceptual** | — | ❌ Severe | ⚠️ Moderate | ⚠️ Moderate |
| **Environmental** | ⚠️ Moderate | ❌ Severe | ⚠️ Moderate | — |
| **Task/Activity** | ⚠️ Moderate | ⚠️ Moderate | ⚠️ Moderate | ⚠️ Moderate |
| **Social** | — | — | — | ❌ Severe |
| **Socio-cultural / Institutional** | ⚠️ Moderate | — | — | ❌ Severe |

> **Legend:** ❌ Severe impact | ⚠️ Moderate impact | — Negligible

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Matrix:** The **Physical** context most directly widens the *Gulf of Execution* (you physically cannot press the button), while the **Social** and **Institutional** contexts are the *primary drivers* of abandonment. This matrix is itself a **high-yield KTU diagram** — sketch it in exams.

---

## 3.5 KTU-Style Numerical Checklist (Verify, Don't Skip)

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Grip aperture for a typical user:} \quad & A \geq 25 \text{ mm} \\
\text{Standard cane length rule:} \quad & L_{cane} = 0.45 \times H_{user} \text{ (elbow at 15°)} \\
\text{Wheelchair seat depth:} \quad & D_{seat} = H_{popliteal} - 25 \text{ mm} \\
\text{Prosthetic socket comfort:} \quad & P_{interface} \leq 200 \text{ kPa} \text{ (tissue tolerance)} \\
\text{Cane load capacity:} \quad & F_{cane} \geq 0.25 \times m_{user} \times g \\
\text{Hearing aid gain rule:} \quad & G_{max} = \frac{1}{2} \text{ (User's Uncomfortable Level - Threshold)}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unit reminders (mandatory in KTU answer sheets):**
> - $g = 9.81$ **m/s²**
> - $p_0 = 20$ **μPa** (reference sound pressure)
> - $A_{weight} = 1$ **m²** (or specify) for dB SPL
> - Always **state the unit** of your answer — losing a unit = losing **½ to 1 mark**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📙 Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Seven-Layer Context Model (HCT-EASSIT)

```mermaid
graph TD
    userH[USER Human Core]
    ctxHealth[CONTEXT 1 Physical Health<br/>ROM Grip Strength MRC Sensory Loss]
    ctxCog[CONTEXT 2 Cognitive<br/>MMSE MoCA Working Memory Attention]
    ctxTask[CONTEXT 3 Task Activity<br/>ADL IADL Work Leisure]
    ctxEnv[CONTEXT 4 Environmental<br/>Light Noise Temp Terrain Space]
    ctxAct[CONTEXT 5 Activity Demand<br/>Time Pressure Multitasking Criticality]
    ctxSoc1[CONTEXT 6 Social<br/>Family Caregiver Peer Stigma]
    ctxSoc2[CONTEXT 7 Sociocultural Institutional<br/>Beliefs Policies Regulation Reimbursement]
    ctxInd[CONTEXT 8 Individual User<br/>Experience Training Personality]
    ctxTech[CONTEXT 9 Technological<br/>Form Factor Modality Interface]

    dev[ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICE]
    hti((HTI QUALITY<br/>Outcome))

    userH --> ctxHealth
    userH --> ctxCog
    userH --> ctxInd
    ctxTask --> dev
    ctxAct --> dev
    ctxEnv --> dev
    ctxSoc1 --> dev
    ctxSoc2 --> dev
    ctxTech --> dev
    ctxHealth --> dev
    ctxCog --> dev
    ctxInd --> dev
    dev --> hti
    ctxSoc1 --> hti
    ctxSoc2 --> hti
    ctxEnv --> hti
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** The **user** is the source of the inner contexts (Physical, Cognitive, Individual). The **device** is the convergence node. The **outer contexts** (Task, Activity, Environmental, Social, Sociocultural) surround the device. **HTI Quality** is the *emergent* outcome of all nine contexts interacting with the device.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Norman's Gulf of Evaluation & Execution with Context

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    intent[USER INTENTION] -->|Gulf of Execution| action[PHYSICAL ACTION]
    action --> device[DEVICE STATE CHANGE]
    device -->|Gulf of Evaluation| perception[USER PERCEPTION]
    perception --> interpret[USER INTERPRETATION]
    interpret -->|compare to goal| intent

    ctxPhys[Physical Context WIDENS Gulf of Execution]
    ctxPerc[Perceptual Context WIDENS Gulf of Evaluation]
    ctxCog[Cognitive Context AFFECTS Interpretation]

    ctxPhys -.-> action
    ctxPerc -.-> perception
    ctxCog -.-> interpret

    classDef ctxBox fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#7d6608,color:#000
    class ctxPhys,ctxPerc,ctxCog ctxBox
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Caption Tip:** When you reproduce this in an exam, label each *gulf* with the *context* that widens it. Examiners award marks for **explicit linkage**, not just drawing the box.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — HTI Decision Flowchart for a Clinician/Designer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([New Assistive Device Requirement]) --> q1{Is the user's PHYSICAL<br/>context documented?}
    q1 -- No --> a1[STOP Conduct Physical Assessment<br/>ROM Grip Strength MRC Sensory]
    q1 -- Yes --> q2{Is the user's COGNITIVE<br/>context screened?}
    q2 -- No --> a2[STOP Conduct MMSE MoCA<br/>Working Memory Test]
    q2 -- Yes --> q3{Is the ENVIRONMENT<br/>of use mapped?}
    q3 -- No --> a3[STOP Conduct Site Visit<br/>Light Noise Space]
    q3 -- Yes --> q4{Is the TASK<br/>of use clear?}
    q4 -- No --> a4[STOP Task Analysis<br/>ADL IADL Work]
    q4 -- Yes --> q5{Is the SOCIAL<br/>context addressed?}
    q5 -- No --> a5[STOP Stakeholder Consultation<br/>Family Caregiver]
    q5 -- Yes --> q6{Are INSTITUTIONAL<br/>constraints met?}
    q6 -- No --> a6[STOP Regulatory Check<br/>FDA CE CDSCO Reimbursement]
    q6 -- Yes --> design[PROCEED to HTI Design]
    design --> pilot[Pilot Test with Real User]
    pilot --> abandon[Monitor Abandonment Rate]
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Fitts's Law Geometric Intuition (Adapted for Tremor)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    startF[User with Tremor] --> dist{Distance D to target}
    dist -->|Larger D| hardL[HIGHER Index of Difficulty]
    dist -->|Smaller D| easyL[LOWER Index of Difficulty]

    hardL --> sizeW{Width W of target}
    easyL --> sizeW

    sizeW -->|Larger W| lowerID[ID DECREASES]
    sizeW -->|Smaller W| higherID[ID INCREASES]

    lowerID --> okMT[Movement Time within 1500 ms]
    higherID --> failMT[Movement Time EXCEEDS 1500 ms]

    okMT -->|Adopt| final[Recommend 12 mm targets]
    failMT -->|Redesign| final
```

---

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Context-Aware Assistive System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SENSE[PERCEPTION LAYER]
        s1[Biosensors ECG EMG GSR]
        s2[Ambient Sensors Light Noise Temperature]
        s3[Context Classifier Machine Learning]
    end

    subgraph DECIDE[DECISION LAYER]
        d1[User Model Module<br/>Cognitive Physical Profile]
        d2[Task Model Module<br/>Activity Recognition]
        d3[Adaptation Engine<br/>Rule Based + AI]
    end

    subgraph ACT[ACTUATION LAYER]
        a1[Display Visual UI]
        a2[Haptic Vibrotactile Motors]
        a3[Audio Speaker]
        a4[Motorized Actuator Wheelchair Prosthetic]
    end

    subgraph FB[FEEDBACK LOOP]
        f1[User Response]
        f2[Outcome Sensor]
    end

    s1 --> s3
    s2 --> s3
    s3 --> d1
    s3 --> d2
    d1 --> d3
    d2 --> d3
    d3 --> a1
    d3 --> a2
    d3 --> a3
    d3 --> a4
    a1 --> f1
    a2 --> f1
    a3 --> f1
    a4 --> f1
    f1 --> f2
    f2 --> s3
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Architecture:** A *context-aware* assistive system is a **closed-loop cyber-physical system**. The *Perception Layer* senses both the **user's state** (biosensors) and the **environment** (ambient sensors). The *Decision Layer* contains a *User Model* (cognitive/physical profile) and a *Task Model* (activity recognition) — these are the **contextual encoders**. The *Actuation Layer* then chooses the **optimal output modality** (visual, haptic, audio, motorized) for the *current* context. The *Feedback Loop* closes the loop by monitoring the user's response and outcome.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# 📒 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define "context" in Human-Technology Interaction. List the three sub-categories of context in the MATCH model.*

**Model Answer (≈ 80–100 words):**

In Human-Technology Interaction (HTI), "context" refers to the set of **physical, social, and institutional** conditions surrounding the user, the device, and the activity, which influence the quality of interaction.

The **MATCH model** (Steinfeld & Danford, 1999) identifies three sub-categories of context:

1. **Physical Context** — built environment, terrain, lighting, noise, temperature.
2. **Social Context** — presence of family, caregivers, peers, social attitudes, stigma.
3. **Institutional Context** — policies, regulations, reimbursement, organizational culture.

> **[3 Marks Breakdown]:** Definition 1M + 3 sub-categories 1M + naming MATCH 1M.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Supplementary]**
*What is the "Gulf of Execution" and "Gulf of Evaluation" in Norman's model? Name ONE context that widens each gulf.*

**Model Answer:**

**Gulf of Execution:** The gap between the **user's intention/goal** and the **physical actions** required to operate the device. *Widened by:* **Physical Context** (e.g., motor impairment, reduced grip strength).

**Gulf of Evaluation:** The gap between the **device's actual state** and the **user's perception/understanding** of that state. *Widened by:* **Perceptual Context** (e.g., visual impairment, hearing loss, cognitive deficit).

> **[3 Marks Breakdown]:** Gulf of Execution + context 1M | Gulf of Evaluation + context 1M | Example 1M.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, ESE Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> For 14-mark questions, students typically lose marks in three places: (1) **no context-specific examples** (e.g., answering "the user is disabled" instead of "the user has **bilateral below-elbow amputation with 6/60 visual acuity in a rural Indian household**"); (2) **no metric or unit** (e.g., writing "the button is small" without giving a millimetre value or comparing to **Fitts's Law**); (3) **confusing layers** (e.g., mixing up *cognitive* and *perceptual* context). Define. Quantify. Exemplify. Cross-link.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Main]**

> **(a)** *With a neat block diagram, explain the **HAAT (Human-Activity-Assistive Technology) Model** and discuss how the **Context** element influences HTI in each of its three forms. [7 Marks]*
>
> **(b)** *A 65-year-old retired teacher with **bilateral severe presbycusis (high-frequency hearing loss above 3 kHz)**, **mild cognitive impairment (MoCA = 23)**, and **rheumatoid arthritis affecting hand grip (MRC = 3/5)** is to be fitted with a **hearing aid with a multi-button remote control**. Identify the **three primary context layers** that will most affect HTI and propose **two design modifications per layer** to mitigate failure. [7 Marks]*

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.A(a) [7 Marks]**

**1. HAAT Model Definition [1 Mark]**
The HAAT model (Cook & Hussey, 1995) describes assistive technology as a system of four interacting elements:
**H**uman, **A**ctivity, **A**ssistive **T**echnology, and **C**ontext.

$$HTI = \Phi(H, A, AT, C)$$

**2. Block Diagram [2 Marks]**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H[Human<br/>Skills Abilities Limitations] -->|interacts with| AT[Assistive Technology<br/>Device Software]
    A[Activity<br/>Goal Purpose Task] -->|performed via| AT
    C[Context<br/>Physical Social Institutional Cultural] -->|moderates all| H
    C -->|moderates all| A
    C -->|moderates all| AT
    AT -->|outcome| O[Participation Inclusion]
    H --> O
    A --> O
```

**3. Three Forms of Context [3 Marks]**

| Form | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Physical** | Built & natural environment | Stairs, narrow doorways, lighting |
| **Social** | People, attitudes, stigma | Caregiver assistance, peer pressure |
| **Institutional** | Policies, regulations, funding | ADIP scheme, insurance, FDA approval |

**4. Influence on HTI [1 Mark]**
Context is the **moderating variable**. The same Human + Activity + AT combination yields *different* HTI outcomes in different contexts.

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.A(b) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1: Identify the Three Primary Context Layers [3 Marks]**

| Context Layer | User's Specific Deficit | HTI Risk |
|---|---|---|
| **Perceptual/Sensory** | Presbycusis > 3 kHz | Cannot hear high-frequency beeps, alerts, voice prompts |
| **Cognitive** | MoCA = 23 (mild impairment) | Forgets button mapping; slow learning |
| **Physical** | MRC = 3/5 grip | Cannot press small/flush buttons |

**Step 2: Two Design Modifications per Layer [4 Marks = 1.33 × 3, round to 4]**

| Layer | Modification 1 | Modification 2 |
|---|---|---|
| **Perceptual** | Replace high-frequency beeps (≥ 3 kHz) with **vibrotactile alerts** on the wrist. | Add **visual LED ring** on the remote; pair with smartphone app showing status text. |
| **Cognitive** | Reduce number of buttons from 8 → **4**, each labelled with a **single high-contrast icon + word**. | Add **audio narration of each button** in the user's mother tongue on press. |
| **Physical** | Increase button diameter from 8 mm → **15 mm**; recess flush with surface for accidental-press protection. | Add **oversized rocker switch** for volume that requires only a *push*, not a *pinch*. |

**Step 3: Final Recommendation [0.5 Mark]**
A **multi-modal, hierarchical, ergonomically oversized** remote control with **vibrotactile, visual, and audio redundancy** would maximize HTI quality for this user profile.

> **[7 Marks Breakdown]:** Identification 3 × 0.5M = 1.5M | Mods 6 × 0.5M = 3M | Justification 1.5M | Recommendation 0.5M → conservatively awarded.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Supplementary]**

> **(a)** *Differentiate between the **Medical Model**, the **Social Model**, and the **Biopsychosocial Model (ICF)** of disability. State which model most comprehensively explains "contexts affecting HTI" and justify. [7 Marks]*
>
> **(b)** *A rural Indian school for the deaf is deploying **smart classroom hearing-assistive FM systems**. Apply the **seven-layer HCT-EASSIT context model** to predict the **three most likely failure modes** and recommend **one mitigation per failure mode**. [7 Marks]*

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.B(a) [7 Marks]**

**Comparison Table [5 Marks]**

| Aspect | Medical Model | Social Model | Biopsychosocial (ICF, WHO 2001) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Disability located in** | The individual's body | Society's barriers | Interaction of body + society + context |
| **Problem framed as** | Pathology, deficit to cure | Discrimination, barriers to remove | Multi-dimensional functioning |
| **Solution** | Treatment, rehabilitation, devices | Accessibility laws, inclusive design | Combined medical + social + environmental |
| **Role of context** | Minimal | Recognized | **Central — explicit Context factor** |
| **Critique** | Ignores social barriers | Ignores biological realities | Most comprehensive |
| **Origin** | 19th–20th c. medicine | 1970s UK disability rights movement | WHO, 2001 |

**Justification for Most Comprehensive [2 Marks]**
The **Biopsychosocial Model (ICF)** is the most comprehensive because it **explicitly incorporates Context** as a *first-class construct*, alongside Body Function, Activity, and Participation. The Medical Model **ignores context**; the Social Model **ignores the body**. Only ICF unites both — and is therefore the most useful lens for analyzing "contexts affecting HTI."

> **[7 Marks Breakdown]:** Table 5M (≈ 0.7M/cell) + Justification 2M.

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.B(b) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1: Map the HCT-EASSIT Layers to the Deployment Scenario [1 Mark]**

The user is a **deaf student in a rural Indian classroom**. Mapping:

- **H (Health)**: Severe-profound hearing loss; may have additional visual or cognitive comorbidities.
- **C (Cognitive)**: Varied literacy; may be in regional-language medium.
- **T (Task)**: Listening to teacher, participating in class discussion.
- **E (Environmental)**: Rural school — irregular power, dusty, hot, open windows.
- **A (Activity Demand)**: Sustained attention across 6–8 periods/day.
- **S (Social 1)**: Peers may tease; teachers may not be trained.
- **S (Social 2)**: ADIP funding cycles; CDSCO device approval.
- **I (Individual)**: First-time user of FM technology.
- **T (Technological)**: FM transmitter, receivers, batteries.

**Step 2: Predict the Three Most Likely Failure Modes [3 Marks]**

| # | Failure Mode | Layer(s) | Why |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Power/battery failure** in rural setting | Environmental + Technological | Irregular electricity; rechargeable batteries degrade in heat (40 °C+). |
| 2 | **Social stigma / refusal to wear receivers** | Social | Visible FM receiver marks child as "different"; peer teasing. |
| 3 | **Teacher unfamiliarity with transmitter operation** | Social (institutional) + Individual | Insufficient training; complex UI; no technical support staff in rural schools. |

**Step 3: One Mitigation per Failure Mode [3 Marks]**

| Failure | Mitigation |
|---|---|
| **Power/battery** | Equip schools with **solar-charging stations** for receivers; design devices to operate on **standard AA cells** available locally. |
| **Stigma** | Make receivers **discreet** (ear-level BTE-style); run **peer-awareness campaigns**; allow students to *personalize* their devices (colour, stickers). |
| **Teacher training** | Provide **3-day on-site training**; produce **regional-language video tutorials**; appoint a **single trained "resource teacher"** per cluster of 5 schools. |

> **[7 Marks Breakdown]:** Layer-mapping 1M | 3 Failures 3 × 0.5M = 1.5M | Justifications 3 × 0.5M = 1.5M | Mitigations 3 × 1M = 3M.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — "Contexts Affecting HTI"**

### ✅ Definitions
- ☐ **Context** = the *physical + social + institutional* conditions surrounding user, device, and activity.
- ☐ **HTI Quality** is *not* a property of the device — it is an *emergent* property of the **user × device × context** system.
- ☐ **Gulf of Execution** (intention → action) ↔ **widened by Physical Context**.
- ☐ **Gulf of Evaluation** (state → perception) ↔ **widened by Perceptual Context**.

### ✅ Models & Frameworks (Memorize All)
- ☐ **HAAT** (Cook & Hussey): Human, Activity, Assistive Technology, Context.
- ☐ **MATCH** (Steinfeld & Danford): Context has 3 sub-layers — *Physical, Social, Institutional*.
- ☐ **Activity Theory** (Engeström): Subject, Object, Community, Rules, Division of Labour, Mediating Artefact.
- ☐ **Norman's Gulf Model**: 7 stages of action; 2 gulfs.
- ☐ **ICF** (WHO 2001): Body Function + Activity + Participation + **Contextual Factors** (Environmental + Personal).

### ✅ The HCT-EASSIT Layers (9)
1. **H** — Physical/Health (ROM, Grip, MRC, Sensory Loss)
2. **C** — Cognitive (MMSE, MoCA, Working Memory)
3. **T** — Task/Activity (ADL, IADL, Work, Leisure)
4. **E** — Environmental (Light lx, Noise dB SPL, Temp °C, Terrain)
5. **A** — Activity Demand (Time, Multitasking, Criticality)
6. **S** — Social (Family, Peers, Stigma, Caregivers)
7. **S** — Sociocultural & Institutional (Beliefs, FDA, CE, CDSCO, ADIP)
8. **I** — Individual User (Experience, Training, Personality)
9. **T** — Technological (Form Factor, Modality, Interface)

### ✅ Key Numbers (Burn into Memory 🔥)
- ☐ **MMSE**: 0–30; < 24 = cognitive impairment
- ☐ **MoCA**: 0–30; < 26 = mild cognitive impairment
- ☐ **MRC Scale**: 0–5 (integer)
- ☐ **Miller's Law**: 7 ± 2 chunks
- ☐ **Fitts's Law**: $MT = a + b \log_2(1 + D/W)$
- ☐ **Hick's Law**: $RT = a + b \log_2(N)$
- ☐ **Sweller's CL**: $CL_{int} + CL_{ext} + CL_{ger}$
- ☐ **SNR threshold**: ≥ 15 dB for intelligibility
- ☐ **Touch target minimum**: ≥ 12 mm (ISO 9241)
- ☐ **Abandonment rate**: 30% (high-income), > 50% (low-income) — **social context** is the biggest driver.
- ☐ **Reference sound pressure**: $p_0 = 20$ μPa
- ☐ **Acceleration due to gravity**: $g = 9.81$ m/s²

### ✅ Common Pitfalls (Examiners Penalize)
- ☐ ❌ Writing "context" without **naming the layer** (Physical? Cognitive? Social?).
- ☐ ❌ Forgetting to **include units** (lux, dB SPL, mm, °C, kgf).
- ☐ ❌ Confusing **Perceptual** (vision/hearing) with **Cognitive** (memory/attention).
- ☐ ❌ Confusing **Social** (people, stigma) with **Institutional** (policies, regulations).
- ☐ ❌ Failing to **map context to design modification** — always close the loop with a *recommendation*.
- ☐ ❌ Skipping the **Indian-context examples** (ADIP, CDSCO, rural deployment) when the question is KTU-Indian.

### ✅ Golden Rule for KTU Answers
$$\boxed{\text{Define} \rightarrow \text{Quantify} \rightarrow \text{Exemplify} \rightarrow \text{Recommend} \rightarrow \text{Map-to-Indian-Context}}$$

> 🎯 **Final Exam Mantra:**
> *"In OEBRT614, an answer without a **context** is an answer without a **context**."*
> Every assistive device is *used*, not just *designed*. The context is where use lives.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
