<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Functional Movement Evaluation – Core Foundations

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Functional Movement Evaluation (FME)** is the systematic, quantitative and qualitative assessment of human movement patterns—encompassing **kinematics** (motion descriptors), **kinetics** (forces and moments), and **physiological effort**—used to characterize an individual's mobility, balance, strength, and coordination for clinical diagnosis, rehabilitation planning, prescription of assistive devices, and outcome measurement.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, FME forms the **assessment backbone** of rehabilitation engineering. Before any assistive medical device (prosthesis, orthosis, walker, wheelchair, or functional electrical stimulator) is prescribed, the clinician/engineer must objectively *quantify* how the patient moves and where the deficit lies.

### Decomposition of the Definition

| Sub-Element | Engineering Meaning | Measurement Domain |
|---|---|---|
| **Kinematics** | Position, velocity, acceleration of body segments | Temporal-spatial, joint angles |
| **Kinetics** | Forces, torques, power at joints | Newton (N), Nm, Watt |
| **Physiological effort** | Muscle activation, energy cost | Surface EMG (mV), VO₂ (mL/kg/min) |
| **Functional outcome** | Ability to perform ADLs | FIM, Barthel Index (0–100 scale) |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Vehicle Diagnostic" View

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a car brought to a service center.** A mechanic does not randomly replace parts. First, the car is put on a **roller dynamometer** to measure wheel speed, the **OBD-II port** is read for sensor data (force/pressure), and a **test driver** notes unusual noises or pulling. Only after this *evaluation* is a part replaced or modified.

A human patient works the same way:

- The **roller dynamometer** $\rightarrow$ **gait laboratory walkway / instrumented treadmill**
- The **OBD-II sensor stream** $\rightarrow$ **EMG, force plates, motion-capture markers**
- The **test driver report** $\rightarrow$ **clinical observational scales** (e.g., Berg Balance, TUG)

The *output* of FME is a **functional profile**—a structured data record that guides the prescription of assistive devices.

### Why FME Matters in Assistive Device Design

1. **Baseline quantification** – establishes a starting deficit level (e.g., gait speed, ROM).
2. **Device justification** – provides objective evidence to insurance/regulatory bodies.
3. **Fit verification** – ensures the orthosis/prosthesis restores symmetric kinematics.
4. **Outcome tracking** – post-device FME validates improvement (Δ gait speed, Δ energy cost).

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!TIP]
> **Key reference values used throughout FME (Normative Adult, walking):**
> - **Self-selected walking speed:** $v \approx 1.30\ \text{m/s}$ (±0.20)
> - **Cadence:** $\approx 110\ \text{steps/min}$ (±10)
> - **Stride length:** $\approx 1.41\ \text{m}$
> - **Stance:Swing ratio:** $\approx 60:40$
> - **Single-limb support:** $\approx 40\%$ of gait cycle
> - **Vertical Ground Reaction Force (vGRF):** $1.0$ to $1.2$ × body weight during stance
> - **Energy cost of walking:** $\approx 0.80\ \text{mL O}_2/\text{kg/m}$ in healthy adults
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Taxonomy of Functional Movement Evaluation

FME methods form a **three-tier pyramid** from simplest (cheapest) to most complex (highest fidelity):

### Tier 1 — Clinical Observational Scales (Subjective)

- **Visual Gait Analysis** – trained clinician observes walking pattern, rates deviations on ordinal scales.
- **Functional Independence Measure (FIM)** – 18-item, 7-level scoring (1 = total assist, 7 = complete independence); total range **18 to 126**.
- **Berg Balance Scale (BBS)** – 14 functional balance tasks, each 0–4; max score **56**; $<45$ indicates fall risk.
- **Timed Up and Go (TUG)** – rise from chair, walk 3 m, return, sit; cutoff $> 12\ \text{s}$ indicates mobility deficit.

### Tier 2 — Instrumented Non-Optical Methods

- **Goniometry** – measures joint Range of Motion (ROM); uses a **universal goniometer** aligned to anatomical landmarks.
- **Manual Muscle Testing (MMT)** – grades 0/1/2/3/4/5 (Oxford scale) representing 0–100% of normal strength.
- **Dynamometry** – hand-held or isokinetic dynamometer measures peak torque (Nm).
- **Surface Electromyography (sEMG)** – records muscle activation amplitude and timing.
- **Force Platforms / Pressure Insoles** – measure Ground Reaction Forces (GRF) and Center of Pressure (CoP).

### Tier 3 — Advanced Motion Capture & Modeling

- **Optoelectronic systems** (Vicon, Qualisys) – passive/active markers; sub-millimeter accuracy.
- **Inertial Measurement Units (IMUs)** – accelerometers + gyroscopes; portable, wearable.
- **Inverse dynamics** – computes net joint moments/muscle forces from kinematics + GRF + segment inertial properties.
- **Computational musculoskeletal modeling** (OpenSim, AnyBody) – predicts individual muscle forces.

---

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step in FME

| Step | Why It Matters | Clinical Output |
|---|---|---|
| Define the **task** (walk, sit-to-stand, reach) | Standardizes evaluation across visits | Task-specific score |
| Record **kinematics** | Identifies compensation patterns (e.g., hip hiking, circumduction) | Joint-angle waveforms |
| Record **kinetics** | Reveals loading at joints (knee adduction moment in OA) | Joint moments in Nm/kg |
| Record **EMG** | Confirms muscle activation timing (e.g., tibialis anterior in drop foot) | Activation envelopes |
| Compute **energy cost** | Quantifies *efficiency* of gait | mL O₂/kg/m |
| Synthesize into **clinical score** | Communicates to non-engineering team | FIM, BBS, TUG |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is your **one-stop reference** for every equation you will be examined on. Memorize units as much as the symbols.

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $v = \dfrac{d}{t}$ | Walking speed = distance / time | $\text{m/s}$ |
| 2 | $\text{Cadence} = \dfrac{\text{steps}}{\text{time}}$ | Steps per minute | $\text{steps/min}$ |
| 3 | $\text{Stride length} = 2 \times \text{step length}$ | One gait cycle distance | $\text{m}$ |
| 4 | $\text{ROM} = \theta_{\max} - \theta_{\min}$ | Joint range of motion | degrees (°) |
| 5 | $\tau = I \alpha$ | Joint torque = moment of inertia × angular acceleration | $\text{Nm}$ |
| 6 | $\text{vGRF}_{\text{peak}} \approx 1.2 \, W$ | Peak vertical ground reaction force | $\text{N}$ |
| 7 | $E_{\text{cost}} = \dfrac{\text{VO}_2}{v}$ | Energy cost of walking | $\text{mL O}_2/\text{kg/m}$ |
| 8 | $\text{FIM score} \in [18,\,126]$ | Functional independence total | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\text{BBS} \in [0,\,56]$ | Berg Balance total | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\text{TUG}_\text{cutoff} = 12\ \text{s}$ | Fall-risk threshold for elderly | $\text{s}$ |
| 11 | $f_{\text{Nyquist}} \geq 2 f_{\max}$ | Minimum sampling rate for EMG/motion | $\text{Hz}$ |
| 12 | $M_{\text{joint}} = \sum_i \left( r_i \times F_i \right) - I_i \alpha_i$ | Inverse dynamics net joint moment | $\text{Nm}$ |
| 13 | $\text{CoP}_x = \dfrac{\sum F_i \, x_i}{\sum F_i}$ | Center of pressure (x-axis) | $\text{m}$ |
| 14 | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi}{\text{stride period}}$ | Gait cycle angular frequency | $\text{rad/s}$ |
| 15 | $\text{Symmetry Index} = \dfrac{\vert X_L - X_R \vert}{0.5(X_L + X_R)} \times 100\%$ | Left-right asymmetry | \% |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering tip:** When solving KTU numericals, always convert body weight $W$ (kg) to force in Newtons using $F = m g$, where $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Prosthetic socket design:** Inverse-dynamics-derived hip and knee moments dictate load-bearing capacity of the socket.
- **Orthotic knee joints (KAFO):** FME reveals varus/valgus thrust and guides stance-phase lock vs. swing-phase free decisions.
- **Powered exoskeletons:** Real-time FME (IMU + EMG) drives actuation controllers; $\text{vGRF}$ feedback enables terrain-adaptive impedance.
- **Wheelchair prescription:** TUG, 6-minute walk, and propulsion kinetics decide whether a manual or powered chair is appropriate.
- **Fall-prevention devices:** BBS and TUG scores feed risk algorithms inside smart canes and hip-protector airbags.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stride Length and Cadence from a Measured Walk

A subject walks a **10-metre straight walkway** with **two force plates embedded mid-way**. Five complete gait cycles are recorded. The data summary is:

- Average cycle duration $T = 1.10\ \text{s}$
- Number of steps in 60 s = 110
- Distance walked in 30 s = 39 m

We need to compute **walking speed $v$**, **cadence**, and **stride length**.

### Step-by-step Working

**Step 1 – Compute walking speed $v$.**

$$
v = \dfrac{d}{t} = \dfrac{39\ \text{m}}{30\ \text{s}}
$$

$$
v = 1.30\ \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 2 – Compute cadence.**

$$
\text{Cadence} = \dfrac{\text{steps}}{\text{time}} = \dfrac{110\ \text{steps}}{1\ \text{min}}
$$

$$
\text{Cadence} = 110\ \text{steps/min}
$$

**Step 3 – Compute step length, then stride length.**

Step length is the distance covered in one step; in 1 min there are 110 steps and the distance covered is $v \times 60$:

$$
\text{Step length} = \dfrac{v \times 60\ \text{s}}{\text{Cadence}} = \dfrac{1.30 \times 60}{110}
$$

$$
\text{Step length} = \dfrac{78.0}{110} = 0.709\ \text{m}
$$

**Step 4 – Compute stride length.**

$$
\text{Stride length} = 2 \times \text{Step length} = 2 \times 0.709
$$

$$
\text{Stride length} = 1.418\ \text{m}
$$

**Step 5 – Verification using $T$:**

$$
v = \dfrac{\text{Stride length}}{T} = \dfrac{1.418}{1.10} = 1.289\ \text{m/s} \approx 1.30\ \text{m/s} \ \checkmark
$$

All values are consistent and fall within the **normative adult range**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Symmetry Index of a Hemiplegic Patient

A patient post-stroke has the following peak knee-extension moments during stance:

- Affected (left) limb: $M_L = 25\ \text{Nm}$
- Unaffected (right) limb: $M_R = 55\ \text{Nm}$

Calculate the **Symmetry Index (SI)** and interpret the result.

### Step-by-step Working

**Step 1 – Recall the Symmetry Index formula.**

$$
\text{SI} = \dfrac{\vert M_L - M_R \vert}{0.5\,(M_L + M_R)} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 2 – Substitute the values.**

$$
\text{SI} = \dfrac{\vert 25 - 55 \vert}{0.5\,(25 + 55)} \times 100\% = \dfrac{30}{40} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{SI} = 75\%
$$

**Step 3 – Interpretation.**

| SI Range | Interpretation |
|---|---|
| $0$ to $\leq 10\%$ | Normal symmetry |
| $> 10\%$ to $25\%$ | Mild asymmetry |
| $> 25\%$ to $50\%$ | Moderate asymmetry |
| $> 50\%$ | **Severe asymmetry** ← our patient |

> [!WARNING]
> An SI of **75 %** indicates **severe asymmetry**. This justifies prescribing an assistive device such as a **KAFO** or **functional electrical stimulation** of the quadriceps to restore loading symmetry.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Inverse Dynamics to Find Ankle Plantarflexion Moment

A 70 kg subject has the following during late stance:
- Shank mass $m_s = 4.5\ \text{kg}$, foot mass $m_f = 1.2\ \text{kg}$
- Shank length $l_s = 0.44\ \text{m}$, foot length $l_f = 0.26\ \text{m}$
- $\text{GRF}_v = 750\ \text{N}$ acting at CoP located $0.18\ \text{m}$ anterior to ankle joint
- Segment angular accelerations negligible ($\alpha_i \approx 0$)

Find the **net ankle plantarflexion moment**.

### Step-by-step Working

**Step 1 – Write the inverse-dynamics moment equation for the ankle.**

$$
M_{\text{ankle}} = \sum_i \left( r_i \times F_i \right) - I_i \alpha_i
$$

Since $\alpha_i \approx 0$, the inertial term drops.

**Step 2 – Identify the dominant moment contributor (GRF).**

The vertical GRF acts $0.18\ \text{m}$ anterior to the ankle, creating a **plantarflexion moment**.

$$
M_{\text{ankle, GRF}} = \text{GRF}_v \times d_{\text{GRF}} = 750 \times 0.18
$$

$$
M_{\text{ankle, GRF}} = 135\ \text{Nm}
$$

**Step 3 – Subtract gravitational moments of distal segments.**

The foot's weight ($m_f g$) acts at its centre of mass, about $0.13\ \text{m}$ anterior to the ankle:

$$
M_{g,\text{foot}} = m_f g \times 0.13 = 1.2 \times 9.81 \times 0.13
$$

$$
M_{g,\text{foot}} = 1.53\ \text{Nm} \quad (\text{plantarflexion})
$$

**Step 4 – Combine.**

$$
M_{\text{ankle,net}} = 135 + 1.53 \approx 136.5\ \text{Nm}
$$

This is the **net internal ankle plantarflexion moment** that the gastrocnemius–soleus must generate in late stance—critical when designing an **ankle-foot orthosis (AFO)** with energy-storing features.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right FME Tool

| Clinical Question | Best Tool | Output | Marks Insight (KTU) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Can the patient walk safely? | TUG | Time in s | $> 12$ s = fall risk |
| Is the balance impaired? | Berg Balance | 0–56 score | $< 45$ = fall risk |
| What is the joint ROM? | Goniometer | Degrees | Normal knee flexion ≈ 0–135° |
| What is the muscle strength? | MMT or dynamometer | 0–5 grade / Nm | Grade $\leq 3$ = device candidate |
| Is gait symmetric? | Force plates + SI calc | SI % | $> 10$ % = asymmetry |
| Which muscles activate abnormally? | sEMG | Activation timing | Pre-activation in spasticity |
| What is energy expenditure? | Cosmed K4 / indirect calorimetry | mL O₂/kg/m | Stroke patients ≈ 2× normal |
| How do they walk outdoors? | IMU wearable | Stride variability | High CV = fall risk |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an FME System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Patient Performs Functional Task] --> B[Task Definition Module]
    B --> C[Sensor Suite]
    
    C --> C1[Optical Motion Capture]
    C --> C2[Force Platforms]
    C --> C3[Surface EMG Electrodes]
    C --> C4[Inertial Measurement Units]
    C --> C5[Goniometers]
    
    C1 --> D[Data Acquisition Unit]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
    
    D --> E[Signal Conditioning]
    E --> F1[Kinematic Processor]
    E --> F2[Kinetic Processor]
    E --> F3[EMG Analyzer]
    E --> F4[Energy Cost Calculator]
    
    F1 --> G[Clinical Report Generator]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    
    G --> H[Functional Profile Database]
    H --> I[Assistive Device Prescription Module]
    I --> J1[Prosthesis Design]
    I --> J2[Orthosis Design]
    I --> J3[Wheelchair Selection]
    I --> J4[FES Controller Tuning]
    
    J1 --> K[Outcome Re-Evaluation Loop]
    J2 --> K
    J3 --> K
    J4 --> K
    K --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The outer loop (`K → A`) represents the **iterative nature of FME**—the patient is re-tested after device prescription, and the data feeds back to refine the prescription. This is precisely the **Plan–Do–Check–Act (PDCA)** cycle KTU examiners love to test.

## 4.2 FME Evaluation Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Patient Presents with Mobility Deficit] --> S1{Self Report: ADL Difficulty?}
    S1 -- No --> S2[Observation Only]
    S1 -- Yes --> S3{Sit-to-Stand Possible?}
    
    S3 -- No --> S4[Wheelchair Candidate]
    S4 --> S4a[Powered vs Manual via FIM, Arm Strength]
    
    S3 -- Yes --> S5{Static Balance OK?}
    S5 -- No --> S6[Berg Balance Scale Test]
    S6 --> S6a{Scoreless Than 45}
    S6a -- Yes --> S7[Prescribe Walker or Cane plus Balance Therapy]
    
    S5 -- Yes --> S8[Timed Up and Go Test]
    S8 --> S8a{Time Greater Than 12s}
    S8a -- Yes --> S9[Fall Risk Confirmed plus Full Gait Lab Evaluation]
    S8a -- No --> S10[Monitor Every 6 Months]
    
    S9 --> S11[Instrumented Gait Analysis]
    S11 --> S11a[Kinematics plus Kinetics plus EMG]
    S11a --> S12[Identify Specific Deficit]
    S12 --> S12a[Orthosis Prosthosis FES Decision]
```

## 4.3 Sensor-to-Parameter Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Optical Markers] --> Q1[Joint Angles]
    P2[Force Plates] --> Q2[GRF and CoP]
    P3[sEMG] --> Q3[Muscle Activation Timing]
    P4[IMU] --> Q4[Accelerations and Angular Velocity]
    P5[Goniometer] --> Q5[Static ROM]
    P6[Gas Analyzer] --> Q6[VO2 and Energy Cost]
    
    Q1 --> R[Integrated Functional Profile]
    Q2 --> R
    Q3 --> R
    Q4 --> R
    Q5 --> R
    Q6 --> R
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define Functional Movement Evaluation. List any four clinical tools used for FME.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** FME is the systematic assessment of human movement encompassing kinematics, kinetics and physiological effort, used for clinical diagnosis, rehabilitation planning, prescription of assistive devices, and outcome measurement.
- **Four clinical tools (1.5 marks – any four):**
  1. Goniometer (ROM)
  2. Manual Muscle Testing (MMT)
  3. Berg Balance Scale
  4. Timed Up and Go (TUG)
  5. Functional Independence Measure (FIM)
  6. Surface EMG

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is the significance of the Symmetry Index (SI) in gait evaluation? State the threshold value for clinical asymmetry.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Significance (2 marks):** SI quantifies left–right asymmetry in any gait parameter (step length, stance time, joint moment). It identifies compensatory gait patterns post-stroke, post-amputation, or in osteoarthritis, guiding whether assistive devices (cane, orthosis, prosthesis alignment) are required.
- **Threshold (1 mark):** An SI of **> 10 %** indicates clinically meaningful asymmetry.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently lose 2–3 marks by **(a)** omitting units in final answers, **(b)** failing to interpret numerical results (e.g., not stating *what* the SI value means clinically), and **(c)** skipping the **why** behind selecting a particular FME tool. Always end a numerical answer with an *interpretation sentence*.

---

### Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – Model Question]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of an instrumented gait analysis laboratory. Describe the role of each component. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 65 kg subject walks on an instrumented walkway. Peak vertical GRF during right stance is **780 N**, applied 0.16 m anterior to the ankle. Foot mass = 1.1 kg, foot CoM located 0.12 m anterior to ankle. Find the net ankle plantarflexion moment in late stance. Comment on its relevance to AFO prescription. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) (7 marks)

**Block diagram (3 marks):**

```
Patient → Marker placement protocol
       ↓
Motion Capture Cameras (6–12)
       ↓
Force Platforms (2–4 in walkway)
       ↓
EMG electrodes (8–16 channels)
       ↓
Data Acquisition + Synchronization
       ↓
Processing Workstation
       ↓
Kinematic + Kinetic + EMG outputs
       ↓
Clinical Report → Device Prescription
```

**Component roles (4 marks – 1 mark each):**

1. **Cameras** – track retro-reflective markers placed on anatomical landmarks; reconstruct 3-D positions at 100–200 Hz.
2. **Force platforms** – record 3-D GRF and CoP at 1000 Hz.
3. **EMG system** – amplifies and digitizes muscle electrical activity (typically 1–10 kHz sampling).
4. **Acquisition + sync hardware** – time-aligns all signals with a common trigger.

#### Model Solution — (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 – Vertical GRF moment (2 marks):**

$$
M_{\text{GRF}} = 780 \times 0.16 = 124.8\ \text{Nm}
$$

**Step 2 – Gravitational moment of foot (1.5 marks):**

$$
M_{g,\text{foot}} = 1.1 \times 9.81 \times 0.12 = 1.295\ \text{Nm}
$$

**Step 3 – Net ankle moment (1.5 marks):**

$$
M_{\text{ankle,net}} = 124.8 + 1.295 \approx 126.1\ \text{Nm}
$$

**Step 4 – Clinical interpretation (2 marks):**

A plantarflexion moment of **~126 Nm** in late stance is required for push-off. In patients with weak plantarflexors (e.g., post-polio, stroke), this moment cannot be generated, leading to reduced push-off and slower gait. A **rigid or posterior-leaf-spring AFO** can store energy during mid-stance and release it at push-off, partially substituting for the lost plantarflexor moment — directly informing AFO stiffness selection.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – Model Question]

**(a)** Describe the Timed Up and Go (TUG) test, Berg Balance Scale, and Functional Independence Measure (FIM). For each, state scoring range, what it measures, and the clinical cutoff indicating mobility deficit. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A patient walks 40 m in 32 s. Stride length is 1.20 m. Calculate walking speed, cadence, and symmetry index if right step length is 0.65 m and left is 0.55 m. Comment on whether an assistive device is indicated. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) (7 marks)

| Test (1 mark each) | Scoring Range (0.5 mark) | What it measures (1 mark each) | Clinical Cutoff (0.5 mark each) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **TUG** | Time in seconds (lower = better) | Functional mobility; sit, walk 3 m, return, sit | $> 12$ s = fall risk |
| **Berg Balance** | 0–56 (higher = better) | Static and dynamic balance via 14 tasks | $< 45$ = fall risk |
| **FIM** | 18–126 (higher = better) | Burden of care across 18 ADL items | $< 72$ = moderate dependency |

*(Marks distributed: 1 + 1 + 1 for descriptions = 3, 0.5 × 3 for ranges = 1.5, 1 × 3 for what measured = 3 → redistributed as above for clarity, summing to 7.)*

#### Model Solution — (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 – Walking speed (1.5 marks):**

$$
v = \dfrac{40\ \text{m}}{32\ \text{s}} = 1.25\ \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 2 – Cadence (1.5 marks):**

$$
\text{Step length} = \dfrac{\text{Stride length}}{2} = \dfrac{1.20}{2} = 0.60\ \text{m}
$$

Number of steps taken in 32 s:

$$
n_{\text{steps}} = \dfrac{40}{0.60} = 66.67\ \text{steps}
$$

$$
\text{Cadence} = \dfrac{66.67 \times 60}{32} = 125\ \text{steps/min}
$$

**Step 3 – Symmetry Index (2 marks):**

$$
\text{SI} = \dfrac{\vert 0.65 - 0.55 \vert}{0.5\,(0.65 + 0.55)} \times 100\% = \dfrac{0.10}{0.60} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{SI} = 16.67\%
$$

**Step 4 – Interpretation (2 marks):**

A walking speed of 1.25 m/s is within normal range. However, the SI of **16.67 %** indicates **mild-to-moderate asymmetry**. This alone does not demand an assistive device, but if the patient reports pain, fatigue, or fall history, a **single-point cane on the longer (right) step side** is indicated to offload the shorter (left) limb.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-revision checklist — read this the night before the exam.**

- ✅ **FME = Kinematics + Kinetics + Physiological Effort + Functional Scoring**.
- ✅ Always state **units** (m/s, Nm, mL O₂/kg/m, %) in numerical answers.
- ✅ **vGRF peak ≈ 1.2 × body weight** during normal walking — committed to memory.
- ✅ **TUG > 12 s → fall risk**; **BBS < 45 → fall risk**; **FIM < 72 → moderate dependency**.
- ✅ **Symmetry Index > 10 % = clinically asymmetric**; **> 50 % = severe**.
- ✅ Sampling rate must obey **Nyquist**: $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$ — important for EMG ($\geq 1$ kHz) and motion capture ($\geq 60$ Hz for walking).
- ✅ **Inverse dynamics** moment = $\sum r \times F - I\alpha$ — applied distally-to-proximally.
- ✅ Clinical FME tools fall into **3 tiers**: observational → instrumented → motion-lab.
- ✅ FME is an **iterative loop** — re-evaluate after every device prescription (PDCA cycle).
- ✅ For assistive-device justification, prefer **quantitative** outputs (gait speed, SI, joint moments) over subjective reports.
- ✅ Never skip the **interpretation step** — KTU examiners reward clinical insight with 1–2 marks even when the math is correct.
- ✅ Walking speed, cadence, and stride length are **inter-dependent**: $v = \text{stride length} \times \text{cadence}/120$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characteristics of Control Interfaces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT614 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Characteristics of Control Interfaces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Control Interface (KTU 2024 Definition):** A *control interface* in an assistive medical device is the human–machine boundary that captures, transduces, and encodes a user's voluntary physiological or physical signal into a deterministic command set that drives the actuator of a prosthetic, orthotic, wheelchair, or rehabilitative system.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** (OEBRT614, Module 2), the **characteristics of control interfaces** refer to the measurable qualitative and quantitative attributes that govern how effectively, efficiently, comfortably, and safely a user (often with a disability) can operate an assistive device. These characteristics determine the *usability*, *bandwidth*, *intuitiveness*, and *clinical viability* of the interface.

The principal characteristics classified by KTU are:

| # | Characteristic | Domain |
|---|----------------|--------|
| 1 | **Sensitivity** | Signal acquisition |
| 2 | **Specificity** | Signal discrimination |
| 3 | **Latency / Response Time** | Real-time control |
| 4 | **Accuracy / Classification Rate** | Command fidelity |
| 5 | **Information Transfer Rate (ITR)** | Throughput |
| 6 | **User Fatigue / Ergonomics** | Long-term usability |
| 7 | **Reliability & Robustness** | Clinical safety |
| 8 | **Learnability & Intuitiveness** | Cognitive load |

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Universal TV Remote":** Imagine your TV remote has 100 buttons but only two actually work. Now imagine you must press those two buttons **100 times per minute**, without looking, using your *elbow*. That is essentially the challenge an assistive-device user faces. The **control interface** is the equivalent of the remote's *buttons + signal + the TV's receiver*. Its *characteristics* tell us how good that remote is — how fast (latency), how many channels (bandwidth), how often you must press (fatigue), and how reliably it ignores you bumping the couch (noise immunity).

A more technical analogy: think of the control interface as the **steering wheel of a car**.
- A **good** steering wheel (high-quality interface) is responsive, requires little force, and gives precise directional control.
- A **bad** one is sluggish, requires huge arm movement, and wobbles on its own (noise, drift, low specificity).

In assistive technology, the *user* has limited physical/physiological "bandwidth" — so the interface must **amplify intent** without introducing error, fatigue, or delay.

## 1.3 Why These Characteristics Matter in KTU Context

The KTU 2024 syllabus (NEP 2020 aligned) emphasizes **Outcome-Based Education (OBE)**. For this topic, the relevant Course Outcomes (COs) typically assess whether the student can:
- Identify and compare different control-interface modalities.
- Evaluate the suitability of an interface for a given disability profile.
- Quantitatively justify interface selection using standard performance metrics.

Therefore, the characteristics are not abstract — they are the **engineering metrics** that allow a biomedical engineer to *objectively* choose between, say, an EMG-based prosthetic controller and an EEG-based brain–computer interface for a given patient.

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** A frequently tested KTU concept is the *trade-off triangle* — **Speed vs. Accuracy vs. Fatigue**. No single interface wins on all three. Expect a 14-mark question asking you to compare two interfaces using this triangle.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of Each Characteristic

### 2.1.1 Sensitivity
- **Definition:** The *minimum input magnitude* the interface can reliably detect above its noise floor.
- **Why it matters:** A user with weak muscle signals (e.g., post-stroke) generates low-amplitude EMG. A sensitive interface extracts usable commands from weak signals.
- **How it's quantified:** Minimum Detectable Signal (MDS) in microvolts (µV) for bioelectric interfaces.

### 2.1.2 Specificity
- **Definition:** The interface's ability to *distinguish* between intended and unintended (noise, cross-talk, voluntary + involuntary) signals.
- **Why it matters:** Specificity prevents false activations — e.g., an EMG-controlled hand must NOT open when the user merely shifts posture.
- **How it's quantified:** Selectivity Index or **Classification Accuracy** in %.

### 2.1.3 Latency (Response Time)
- **Definition:** End-to-end delay between the user generating a command signal and the actuator responding.
- **Why it matters:** Latency > ~300 ms in real-time control (e.g., powered wheelchair) becomes unsafe.
- **How it's quantified:** $T_{latency} = T_{acquire} + T_{process} + T_{actuate}$ in milliseconds (ms).

### 2.1.4 Accuracy
- **Definition:** Percentage of correct command classifications out of total trials.
- **Why it matters:** Determines clinical reliability. Below ~85% accuracy, the user reverts to slower compensatory strategies.
- **How it's quantified:** $A = \frac{N_{correct}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$.

### 2.1.5 Information Transfer Rate (ITR) — *Most-Tested KTU Formula*
- **Definition:** The effective *bit rate* of communication between user and device, measured in **bits per minute (bpm)** or **bits per trial (bpt)**.
- **Why it matters:** A wheelchair needs only ~5–8 bpm; a dexterous prosthetic hand needs >30 bpm. ITR normalizes the speed-accuracy trade-off.
- **How it's quantified:** Wolpaw's canonical equation (see Section 3).

### 2.1.6 User Fatigue
- **Definition:** The physical/cognitive exertion imposed on the user during sustained operation.
- **Why it matters:** High fatigue = abandonment of device. Interfaces like sip-and-puff require sustained respiratory effort.
- **How it's quantified:** Borg Scale (subjective) or EMG median-frequency drift (objective).

### 2.1.7 Reliability & Robustness
- **Definition:** Performance consistency across environments (sweat, temperature, electrode displacement, electromagnetic interference).
- **Why it matters:** Clinical-grade devices must perform across a 24-hour window without recalibration.

### 2.1.8 Learnability & Intuitiveness
- **Definition:** Time/training required for a naive user to achieve proficiency.
- **Why it matters:** Pediatric and elderly users require highly intuitive mappings (e.g., forward lean → forward motion).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize this table.** Every KTU 2024 question on this topic references at least one of these formulas.

| # | Formula / Metric | Expression | Unit | Used For |
|---|------------------|------------|------|----------|
| 1 | **Accuracy** | $A = \frac{N_{c}}{N_{t}} \times 100$ | % | Classifier performance |
| 2 | **Sensitivity Index (d′)** | $d' = \frac{\mu_{s} - \mu_{n}}{\sigma_{n}}$ | dimensionless | Signal detection theory |
| 3 | **SNR** | $SNR = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{s}}{P_{n}}\right)$ | dB | Noise immunity |
| 4 | **Weber's Fraction (JND)** | $k = \frac{\Delta I}{I}$ | dimensionless | Tactile/force threshold |
| 5 | **Latency** | $T_{L} = T_{a} + T_{p} + T_{act}$ | ms (milliseconds) | Real-time control |
| 6 | **ITR (Wolpaw)** | $B = V \left[ \log_{2}N + P \log_{2}P + (1-P) \log_{2}\left(\frac{1-P}{N-1}\right) \right]$ | bits/min | Throughput |
| 7 | **Fitts' Law (Index of Difficulty)** | $ID = \log_{2}\left(\frac{2A}{W}\right)$ | bits | Target acquisition time |
| 8 | **Movement Time (Fitts)** | $MT = a + b \cdot ID$ | ms | Ergonomic throughput |
| 9 | **Nyquist Sampling** | $f_{s} \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Hz | Digitization limit |
| 10 | **EMG Bandwidth** | $20 \text{ Hz} \leq f \leq 500 \text{ Hz}$ | Hz | Bioelectric design |

> **Where:**
> - $N_{c}$ = correct classifications, $N_{t}$ = total trials
> - $\mu_{s}, \mu_{n}$ = mean signal and noise; $\sigma_{n}$ = noise std. deviation
> - $P_{s}, P_{n}$ = signal and noise power
> - $V$ = selections per minute, $N$ = number of classes, $P$ = classification accuracy (as fraction)
> - $A$ = target distance, $W$ = target width (Fitts' Law)
> - $f_{s}$ = sampling frequency, $f_{max}$ = highest signal frequency

## 2.3 Engineering Utility

In **clinical rehabilitation engineering**, these characteristics are the *acceptance criteria* in the procurement workflow:

- A **myoelectric hand** for a below-elbow amputee is selected primarily on **Accuracy** and **Latency**.
- A **P300-based BCI** for an ALS-locked-in patient is selected on **ITR** and **Robustness**.
- A **sip-and-puff wheelchair** for a high-cervical SCI patient is selected on **Fatigue** and **Reliability**.

The KTU 2024 module explicitly links this to the **Matching Person & Technology (MPT)** model — interface characteristics must match user capability, device function, and environmental context.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Information Transfer Rate (Wolpaw's Equation)

This is the **single most important derivation** for KTU 2024. It is derived from Shannon's channel-capacity theorem applied to a discrete-selection interface (e.g., a BCI spelling matrix or a wheelchair controller with N directions).

### 3.1.1 Starting Point — Shannon Channel Capacity

$$
C = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} p(j \mid i) \cdot p(i) \cdot \log_{2}\left(\frac{p(j \mid i)}{\sum_{k} p(j \mid k) \cdot p(k)}\right)
$$

Where:
- $N$ = total number of possible classes (commands)
- $p(i)$ = prior probability of user intending class $i$
- $p(j \mid i)$ = probability that the system outputs class $j$ when the user intended $i$

### 3.1.2 Simplifying Assumptions (KTU Standard)

1. **Uniform prior:** All $N$ classes are equally likely, so $p(i) = \frac{1}{N}$.
2. **Symmetric confusion matrix:** Errors are equally distributed among the $(N-1)$ wrong classes.
3. **Diagonal accuracy:** $p(i \mid i) = P$ (the classification accuracy as a fraction).
4. **Off-diagonal accuracy:** $p(j \mid i) = \frac{1-P}{N-1}$ for $j \neq i$.

### 3.1.3 Plugging In

Substituting into the Shannon equation:

$$
B = \log_{2}(N) + P \log_{2}(P) + (1-P) \log_{2}\left(\frac{1-P}{N-1}\right)
$$

This is the **ITR per selection (in bits)**. To convert to **bits per minute**, multiply by the selection rate $V$ (selections per minute):

$$
B_{bpm} = V \cdot \left[ \log_{2}(N) + P \log_{2}(P) + (1-P) \log_{2}\left(\frac{1-P}{N-1}\right) \right]
$$

This is the **Wolpaw ITR formula** universally used in BCI literature and in KTU Module 2.

### 3.1.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

> **Problem:** A P300 speller has $N = 36$ characters, an average accuracy $P = 0.90$ (i.e., 90%), and the user averages $V = 5$ selections/min. Compute the ITR.

**Step 1 — Compute the per-selection terms.**

$$
\log_{2}(36) = \log_{2}(36) \approx 5.1699 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
P \log_{2}(P) = 0.90 \times \log_{2}(0.90) = 0.90 \times (-0.1520) = -0.1368 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
\frac{1-P}{N-1} = \frac{0.10}{35} \approx 0.002857
$$

$$
\log_{2}(0.002857) \approx -8.4510
$$

$$
(1-P) \log_{2}\left(\frac{1-P}{N-1}\right) = 0.10 \times (-8.4510) = -0.8451 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2 — Sum the bracketed terms.**

$$
B = 5.1699 + (-0.1368) + (-0.8451) = 4.1880 \text{ bits/selection}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by the selection rate.**

$$
B_{bpm} = 5 \times 4.1880 = 20.94 \text{ bits/min}
$$

> **Result:** $\boxed{B_{bpm} \approx 20.94 \text{ bits/min}}$ — a typical P300-speller performance.

### 3.1.5 Limiting Cases (Examiner Loves These)

- **Perfect accuracy** ($P = 1$): the error terms vanish, $B = V \log_{2}(N)$.
- **Chance accuracy** ($P = \frac{1}{N}$): $B = 0$ bits/min — no information conveyed.
- **Binary interface** ($N = 2$): reduces to $B = V \left[ 1 + P\log_{2}P + (1-P)\log_{2}(1-P) \right]$ (binary entropy form).

## 3.2 Derivation: Fitts' Law (Target Acquisition Time)

Fitts' Law models the **speed-accuracy trade-off** for pointing/selection tasks — directly applicable to interfaces like head-tracking, eye-tracking, and joystick control.

### 3.2.1 Statement

$$
MT = a + b \cdot \log_{2}\left(\frac{2A}{W}\right)
$$

Where:
- $MT$ = movement time (ms)
- $A$ = distance to target
- $W$ = target width (tolerance)
- $a, b$ = empirically fitted constants
- $ID = \log_{2}\left(\frac{2A}{W}\right)$ = **Index of Difficulty (bits)**

### 3.2.2 Sample Calculation

> **Problem:** A powered wheelchair user must reach a doorway toggle located $A = 0.5$ m away, with effective target width $W = 0.05$ m. For a sip-and-puff interface, $a = 200$ ms, $b = 150$ ms/bit. Find $MT$.

**Step 1 — Compute ID.**

$$
ID = \log_{2}\left(\frac{2 \times 0.5}{0.05}\right) = \log_{2}(20) \approx 4.3219 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute MT.**

$$
MT = 200 + 150 \times 4.3219 = 200 + 648.29 = 848.29 \text{ ms}
$$

> **Result:** $\boxed{MT \approx 848 \text{ ms}}$ — useful for designing target-acceptance windows in wheelchair controllers.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Common Control Interfaces

| Interface Modality | Sensitivity | Specificity | Latency (ms) | Typical Accuracy | ITR (bpm) | Fatigue | Best For |
|--------------------|:-----------:|:-----------:|:------------:|:----------------:|:---------:|:-------:|----------|
| **Mechanical Switch** | High | Very High | <50 | ~99% | High | Low | Discrete commands (call bell) |
| **Joystick (Proportional)** | High | Medium | 50–100 | ~95% | High | Low | Power wheelchair, prosthetic arm |
| **Sip-and-Puff** | High | Medium | 100–200 | ~90% | Low–Medium | Medium | High-cervical SCI users |
| **EMG (Myoelectric)** | Medium | Medium | 100–250 | 85–95% | Medium | Low | Prosthetic hand, virtual cursor |
| **EOG (Electro-oculogram)** | High | High | 100–300 | 80–90% | Low | Low | Eye-motion assistive devices |
| **EEG / BCI (P300, Motor Imagery)** | Low–Medium | Low–Medium | 500–2000 | 70–90% | 5–30 | High | Locked-in, severe paralysis |
| **Voice / Speech Recognition** | High | Medium (noisy env.) | 200–500 | 85–95% | Medium | Medium | Smart-home control, AAC |
| **Head Tracking (IMU)** | High | High | 50–100 | ~95% | High | Low | Wheelchair, computer access |
| **Tongue / Oral Control** | High | High | 100–200 | ~90% | High | Medium | High-cervical SCI |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Hint:** Notice the inverse relationship between *Information Throughput* and *Robustness/Accessibility*. EEG-BCI gives the most *severe-disability accessibility* but the *lowest ITR* and the *highest fatigue*. A KTU 14-mark question will expect you to discuss this trade-off explicitly.

## 3.4 Sensitivity Index (d′) — Signal Detection Theory

For binary classification (e.g., "intent" vs. "no intent"):

$$
d' = \frac{\mu_{s} - \mu_{n}}{\sigma_{n}}
$$

A higher $d'$ means the interface can better separate signal from noise.

**Worked Example:** A myoelectric classifier's "rest" class has $\mu_{n} = 0$ mV, $\sigma_{n} = 5$ µV, and the "flexion" class mean is $\mu_{s} = 25$ µV. Compute $d'$.

$$
d' = \frac{25 - 0}{5} = 5.0
$$

A $d' = 5$ is considered *excellent* discrimination (corresponding to >99% correct in a Gaussian-equivalent model).

## 4.2 The Speed–Accuracy–Fatigue Trade-off Triangle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Sp[Speed Low Latency]:::corner
    Ac[High Accuracy]:::corner

    Sp --- Ac

    EMGJ[EMG]:::good --> Mid1[Balanced Tradeoff]:::neutral
    Joy[Proportional Joystick]:::good --> Mid1
    Mech[Mechanical Switch]:::good --> Mid1
    Mid1 --> Sp
    Mid1 --> Ac
    Mid1 --> Ft

    EEG[EEG BCI]:::poor --> Mid2[Speed Poor Accuracy Low Fatigue High]:::neutral

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** No single control modality sits at the centroid. EMG, joysticks, and mechanical switches cluster near the *balanced* centroid. EEG-based BCIs and sip-and-puff systems sacrifice speed and (in EEG's case) accuracy to gain **accessibility for users with severe paralysis** — a critical clinical trade-off.

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Generic Bioelectric Control Interface

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User Physiological Source Muscle Brain Eye]:::src --> B[Electrodes Sensors Transducers]:::stage
    B --> C[Front End Amplifier INA]:::stage
    C --> D[Bandpass Filter 20 to 500 Hz]:::stage
    D --> E[ADC Sampling at 1 kHz]:::stage
    E --> F[Feature Extraction RMS MAV WL]:::stage
    F --> G[Classifier LDA SVM ANN]:::stage
    G --> H[Command Decoder Mapping]:::stage
    H --> I[Actuator Prosthesis Wheelchair]:::act
    I --> J[Feedback Visual Tactile]:::fb

    J -.-> A

    classDef src fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef stage fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef act fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef fb fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
```

**Block Interpretation:**
1. **Source** – muscle, brain, or eye emits bioelectric activity.
2. **Electrodes/Sensors** – transduce ionic currents into electronic voltages (for bioelectric) or motion into digital data (for IMU).
3. **Front-End Amplifier** – typically an instrumentation amplifier (e.g., INA128) with high CMRR > 100 dB.
4. **Bandpass Filter** – removes DC offset, motion artifact, and high-frequency EMI.
5. **ADC** – samples per Nyquist ($f_{s} \geq 2 f_{max}$).
6. **Feature Extraction** – RMS, MAV, Waveform Length, Zero Crossings.
7. **Classifier** – LDA (linear), SVM (kernel), or ANN (deep) assigns class.
8. **Decoder** – maps class to actuator command.
9. **Feedback** – closes the human-in-the-loop control.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define "Information Transfer Rate (ITR)" in the context of an assistive control interface. List any two factors on which ITR depends.**

**Model Answer:**
The Information Transfer Rate (ITR) is a quantitative measure of the effective data throughput of a control interface, expressed in **bits per minute (bpm)**. It characterizes how much *user intent* can be reliably communicated to the device per unit time, integrating both the *speed* of selection and the *accuracy* of classification.

**Two factors on which ITR depends:**
1. The **number of distinct classes/commands** ($N$) the interface can produce.
2. The **classification accuracy** ($P$) of the interface, i.e., the probability of correct command detection.

(Also accepted: selection rate $V$, or signal-to-noise ratio of the input.)

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the two factors. Avoid confusing ITR with bandwidth in Hz.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is meant by "specificity" of a control interface? Why is high specificity important in a myoelectric prosthetic hand?**

**Model Answer:**
**Specificity** is the ability of a control interface to correctly identify and isolate the *intended* user command while rejecting *unintended* signals such as noise, cross-talk, or involuntary muscle activity.

**Importance in a myoelectric prosthetic hand:**
A prosthetic hand has multiple degrees of freedom (e.g., hand open, close, wrist rotation, pinch). If the interface has poor specificity, the hand may *open* when the user only intends a *wrist rotation*, leading to dropped objects, frustration, and ultimately *device abandonment*. High specificity ensures that each intended motion is mapped unambiguously to one actuator command, which is critical for both safety and user confidence.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for clinical relevance. Practical example scores higher than generic answers.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **functional architecture of a bioelectric (EMG-based) control interface** used in assistive medical devices. Explain the role of each block in determining the interface's overall characteristics. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Information Transfer Rate (ITR)** formula used in evaluating assistive control interfaces, starting from Shannon's channel-capacity principle. A 4-class EEG-based BCI speller achieves 80% accuracy at 6 selections/min. Compute its ITR in bits per minute. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Block Diagram:** *(Refer to Section 4.3 Mermaid figure — Functional Block Diagram — Generic Bioelectric Control Interface.)*

**Block-by-block explanation:**

1. **Source (Physiological Signal):** Voluntary muscle contraction generates motor-unit action potentials, typically 50 µV–5 mV in amplitude, in the 20–500 Hz band. The amplitude and frequency content define the *upper bound* on sensitivity.

2. **Electrodes:** Surface or intramuscular electrodes transduce ionic current into electronic voltage. Electrode–skin impedance (typically < 5 kΩ for gel electrodes) determines *noise pickup* and *motion-artifact sensitivity*.

3. **Front-End Amplifier:** A high-CMRR (> 100 dB) instrumentation amplifier (e.g., INA128) provides 60–80 dB of gain. Determines *common-mode noise rejection* and *baseline input range*.

4. **Bandpass Filter:** Typically 20 Hz–500 Hz. Removes DC offset, baseline wander, and high-frequency EMI. Defines *specificity* by attenuating non-physiological signals.

5. **ADC & Sampling:** Sampling at $\geq 1$ kHz per Nyquist. Resolution (12–16 bit) defines *quantization noise floor*, directly impacting *sensitivity*.

6. **Feature Extraction:** Time-domain features (RMS, MAV, Waveform Length, Zero Crossings) or frequency-domain features (mean/median frequency). Determines *class separability*.

7. **Classifier:** LDA, SVM, or ANN maps features to commands. Determines *accuracy* and *latency*.

8. **Command Decoder & Actuator:** Maps class label to actuator pulse-width modulation, velocity, or position command. Determines *real-time latency*.

9. **Feedback Loop:** Visual, auditory, or tactile feedback to the user, closing the human-in-the-loop. Determines *learnability* and *user fatigue*.

**Connecting to characteristics:** Sensitivity is governed by stages 1–2; specificity by stages 3–4; latency by stages 3–7; accuracy by stages 5–7; fatigue by stages 7 and 9.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Derivation of ITR** — *(Refer to Section 3.1 of these notes for the full step-by-step derivation.)*

Recap of the final formula:

$$
B = V \left[ \log_{2}N + P \log_{2}P + (1-P) \log_{2}\left(\frac{1-P}{N-1}\right) \right]
$$

**Given Numerical Problem:**
- $N = 4$ classes
- $P = 0.80$ (accuracy)
- $V = 6$ selections/min

**Step 1 — Compute $\log_{2}(4)$.**

$$
\log_{2}(4) = 2.0000 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $P \log_{2}(P)$.**

$$
\log_{2}(0.80) = \frac{\ln(0.80)}{\ln(2)} = \frac{-0.2231}{0.6931} \approx -0.3219
$$

$$
P \log_{2}P = 0.80 \times (-0.3219) = -0.2575 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the off-diagonal term.**

$$
\frac{1-P}{N-1} = \frac{0.20}{3} \approx 0.06667
$$

$$
\log_{2}(0.06667) = \frac{\ln(0.06667)}{\ln(2)} = \frac{-2.7081}{0.6931} \approx -3.9069
$$

$$
(1-P) \log_{2}\left(\frac{1-P}{N-1}\right) = 0.20 \times (-3.9069) = -0.7814 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum the bracketed terms.**

$$
B = 2.0000 - 0.2575 - 0.7814 = 0.9611 \text{ bits/selection}
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by selection rate.**

$$
B_{bpm} = 6 \times 0.9611 = 5.7666 \text{ bits/min}
$$

> **Final Answer:** $\boxed{B_{bpm} \approx 5.77 \text{ bits/min}}$

This is a low ITR, characteristic of motor-imagery BCIs; it explains why such interfaces are typically used for *discrete* selection (e.g., yes/no communication) rather than continuous dexterous control.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Alternative]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast **EMG-based** and **EEG-based** control interfaces for assistive medical devices, with respect to the following characteristics: (i) sensitivity, (ii) specificity, (iii) latency, (iv) user fatigue, and (v) information transfer rate. Justify which is more suitable for (A) a below-elbow amputee and (B) a fully locked-in ALS patient. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **Fitts' Law** as applied to the design of control interfaces. A user must select targets on a wheelchair-mounted touchscreen. The targets are 30 mm wide and located 90 mm from the starting point. If the empirical constants are $a = 250$ ms and $b = 100$ ms/bit, compute the movement time and the throughput in bits/second. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Comparative Table (mandatory for full marks):**

| Characteristic | EMG Interface | EEG Interface |
|----------------|---------------|---------------|
| **Sensitivity** | Medium (depends on muscle bulk) | Low (µV signals buried in noise) |
| **Specificity** | Medium (cross-talk, fatigue) | Low–Medium (high variability) |
| **Latency** | 100–250 ms | 500–2000 ms |
| **User Fatigue** | Low (natural muscle use) | High (concentration required) |
| **ITR** | 20–60 bpm | 5–30 bpm |
| **Setup Time** | Low | Very High (gel electrodes, calibration) |
| **Cost** | Low–Medium | High |

**Justification:**

**(A) Below-elbow amputee:** The **EMG interface** is more suitable. The residual forearm muscles generate strong, well-localized EMG signals, and the user has intact cognition and visual feedback. Low fatigue and high ITR enable dexterous multi-grip prosthetic control.

**(B) Fully locked-in ALS patient:** The **EEG interface** is the only viable option, because voluntary muscle activity (and hence EMG) is absent. Although the ITR is lower and setup is demanding, EEG-based BCIs (P300, motor imagery) allow communication and environmental control without any muscular involvement.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently write *"EMG is always better than EEG."* This is incorrect in the locked-in context. The KTU key demands that you *contextualize* the answer to the patient's residual function. Marks are lost when you fail to justify the choice with respect to the specific disability profile.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Fitts' Law Explanation:**

Fitts' Law is a predictive model of human motor performance that quantifies the **trade-off between speed and accuracy** in a pointing/selection task. It states that the **movement time (MT)** required to acquire a target is a linear function of the **Index of Difficulty (ID)**, where ID is the logarithmic ratio of the target distance to the target width.

$$
MT = a + b \cdot ID
$$

$$
ID = \log_{2}\left(\frac{2A}{W}\right)
$$

**Engineering utility:** Used in interface design to predict the time a user will need to select a control (e.g., a button on a wheelchair joystick panel), enabling designers to choose appropriate target sizes and spacings to minimize selection time and error.

**Given Numerical Problem:**
- Target width $W = 30$ mm
- Distance $A = 90$ mm
- $a = 250$ ms, $b = 100$ ms/bit

**Step 1 — Compute ID.**

$$
ID = \log_{2}\left(\frac{2 \times 90}{30}\right) = \log_{2}(6)
$$

$$
\log_{2}(6) = \frac{\ln(6)}{\ln(2)} = \frac{1.7918}{0.6931} \approx 2.585 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute MT.**

$$
MT = 250 + 100 \times 2.585 = 250 + 258.5 = 508.5 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Throughput.**

Throughput $TP$ is defined as ID/MT in bits/second:

$$
TP = \frac{ID}{MT} = \frac{2.585 \text{ bits}}{508.5 \text{ ms}} = \frac{2.585}{0.5085 \text{ s}} \approx 5.085 \text{ bits/s}
$$

> **Final Answer:** $\boxed{MT \approx 508.5 \text{ ms}, \quad TP \approx 5.08 \text{ bits/s}}$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** A common mistake is using the formula $ID = \log_{2}(A/W)$ (without the factor 2). The KTU standard — derived from the Shannon formulation — uses $ID = \log_{2}(2A/W)$ (MacKenzie's corrected form). Writing the wrong form will cost 2 of the 7 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — Characteristics of Control Interfaces**

- **Eight core characteristics:** Sensitivity, Specificity, Latency, Accuracy, ITR, User Fatigue, Reliability, Learnability.
- **Master formula:** **Wolpaw's ITR** — $B = V [\log_{2}N + P \log_{2}P + (1-P)\log_{2}(\frac{1-P}{N-1})]$ — applied for BCI/BCI-speller evaluation.
- **Limiting cases of ITR:** $P = 1 \Rightarrow B = V \log_{2}N$; $P = \frac{1}{N} \Rightarrow B = 0$.
- **Fitts' Law:** $MT = a + b \log_{2}(2A/W)$ — applies to target acquisition design.
- **Speed–Accuracy–Fatigue trade-off:** No interface wins on all three; EMG and joysticks are *balanced*; EEG-BCI sacrifices speed/accuracy for *accessibility*.
- **Sensitivity index d′:** $d' = (\mu_{s} - \mu_{n}) / \sigma_{n}$; $d' = 5$ implies near-perfect discrimination.
- **SNR in dB:** $SNR = 10 \log_{10}(P_{s}/P_{n})$.
- **EMG bandwidth:** **20–500 Hz** (memorize the limits).
- **Nyquist criterion:** Sample at $f_{s} \geq 2 f_{max}$.
- **Clinical mapping principle:** *Interface choice must match patient residual function* — EMG for amputees, sip-and-puff for high-cervical SCI, EEG-BCI for fully locked-in patients.
- **Common examiner traps:** (1) using Fitts' Law without the factor 2; (2) computing ITR in bits/trial but reporting it as bits/min; (3) saying "EMG is better than EEG" without contextualizing the disability.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CI for Direct and Indirect Selection - Keyboards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBRT614 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** CI for Direct and Indirect Selection - Keyboards ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Computer Interface (CI) in the Context of Assistive Technology In assistive technology, a **Computer Interface (CI)** refers to the hardware/software layer that translates a user's physical intent (a movement, a breath, a blink, a switch press) into a logical command that the operating system or application software can understand. For users with motor, cognitive, or sensory impairments, the standard mouse-and-keyboard combination is often inaccessible, so specialized Human–Computer Interface (HCI) pathways are engineered. Selection methods in assistive CIs are classified along a fundamental axis: **Direct Selection** versus **Indirect Selection**. ### 1.2 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Direct Selection (KTU Definition):** > A selection technique in which the user indicates the desired target **simultaneously and spatially**, in a single intentional action, by pointing to or physically contacting the target item. *Example:* typing on a QWERTY keyboard, pressing a switch mapped to a letter, touching an on-screen key. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Indirect Selection (KTU Definition):** > A selection technique in which the target item is **highlighted, scanned, or otherwise presented** to the user sequentially, and the user confirms the highlighted item through a secondary action (e.g., a switch press, a sip-and-puff, a dwell timeout). *Example:* row-column scanning keyboards, automatic linear scanning of an on-screen keyboard. > [!NOTE] > **Keyboards in AT Context:** A *keyboard* is a matrix-arranged set of discrete input transducers (keys) that, when actuated, generate a unique digital code sent to the host computer. In AT, keyboards are repurposed or redesigned so that they can be operated by users with limited dexterity, vision, or cognitive bandwidth. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are in a restaurant and the menu is written in a language you cannot read. - **Direct Selection** is like pointing your finger at the picture of the dish you want. The waiter instantly knows what you chose. There is *one* action: *point-and-tap*. - **Indirect Selection** is like having the waiter read the entire menu aloud, item by item, while you shout "STOP!" when he reaches the dish. There are *two* actions: *listen/scan* and *confirm*. A standard QWERTY keyboard is a direct-selection device: you reach for the "A" and you press "A." A scanning keyboard is an indirect-selection device: the system highlights key after key, and you press a single switch when the correct key is illuminated. ### 1.4 Standard Metrics (Bolded for Recall) - **Typing Rate:** measured in **Words Per Minute (WPM)** — benchmark: 40 WPM is considered functional typing speed. - **Keystroke Latency:** typically **< 50 ms** for USB HID keyboards. - **Scanning Rate:** measured in **items/second** or **seconds/item**; common AT setting is **0.5 s to 2.0 s per item**. - **Activation Force:** force required to register a key, typically **0.45 N to 0.80 N** for standard, **0.20 N to 0.35 N** for low-force AT keyboards. - **Key Travel:** the distance the keycap moves, typically **2 mm to 4 mm** for standard, **1 mm to 1.5 mm** for short-travel AT variants. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Taxonomy of Keyboards Used in Assistive Technology Keyboards for AT are classified along three orthogonal axes: **(a) selection method**, **(b) physical layout**, and **(c) actuation mechanism**. #### A. Direct-Selection Keyboards 1. **Standard QWERTY Keyboard** — 104/105 keys, full travel. Used by users with intact fine motor control but with low vision (paired with large-print keycaps) or single-hand use (paired with keyguards). 2. **Expanded / Oversized Keyboards** — e.g., **BigKeys LX**, key size **25 mm × 25 mm** (vs. standard 19 mm). Reduces targeting errors for users with tremor, arthritis, or cognitive disabilities. 3. **Mini / Compact Keyboards** — reduced footprint, used where range-of-motion is restricted (e.g., **grip-palm** users, wheelchair tray mounting). 4. **Chord Keyboards** — fewer keys; multiple keys pressed simultaneously to generate a character. Example: **BAT (Chordic) Keyboard** with 7 keys generates 128 combinations. Used by stenographers and by users with single-hand access. 5. **On-Screen / Virtual Keyboards** — software-rendered; selected by mouse, touch, head-tracking, or eye-tracking pointer. 6. **Morse-Code Keyboards** — two-switch (dot/dash) input; very high efficiency for users with severe motor impairment. #### B. Indirect-Selection (Scanning) Keyboards 1. **Row-Column Scanning** — highlights one row at a time; switch press selects the row, then highlights one key in the row; switch press selects the key. Two-step, **logarithmic search**. 2. **Linear (Sequential) Scanning** — highlights one key after another in a fixed order. Simple, but **linear time** complexity. 3. **Block / Group Scanning** — divides the keyboard into quadrants; user narrows down hierarchically. Reduces scan steps. 4. **Directed Scanning** — two switches (e.g., sip-and-puff) move the highlight left/right; a third switch confirms. ### 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step (Keyboard Matrix Scanning) > [!NOTE] > Even when discussing AT, the *internals* of a keyboard are governed by the same **matrix scanning algorithm** found in embedded systems. Understanding this is a high-yield KTU topic. A typical **m × n** key matrix is scanned by a microcontroller as follows: 1. **Initialization:** All row lines are driven **HIGH**; all column lines are configured as inputs with **pull-down resistors**. 2. **Row Drive:** The controller drives **Row 0 LOW** (others remain HIGH). It then reads every column. 3. **Key-Debounce:** A reading is considered valid only if it remains stable for the **debounce interval** (typically **5 ms to 15 ms**). This prevents false triggers due to mechanical contact bounce. 4. **Roll-Over Handling:** If more than one key in a row is pressed, the controller applies an **n-key roll-over (NKRO)** or **2-key roll-over (2KRO)** policy. 5. **Iteration:** The controller proceeds to Row 1, Row 2, …, Row m-1, completing one full scan frame. 6. **Frame Rate:** A complete scan must occur in **< 10 ms** to satisfy the **USB HID 125 Hz polling rate** (i.e., 8 ms per frame). ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table summarizes the key engineering formulas for keyboard-based AT systems. **No pipe `|` symbols** are used inside table cells — vertical bars in math use `\vert` or `\mid` as required by the LaTeX engine. | # | Quantity | Formula | Description | Typical Units | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Scan time per frame | $T_{\text{scan}} = m \cdot n \cdot T_{\text{clk}}$ | Time to poll an $m \times n$ matrix at clock period $T_{\text{clk}}$ | seconds (s) | | 2 | Min. frame period (USB) | $T_{\text{frame}} = \dfrac{1}{f_{\text{pol}}}$ | Polling frequency $f_{\text{pol}} = 125$ Hz for USB HID | s | | 3 | Indirect selection throughput | $R = \dfrac{1}{N \cdot T_{\text{item}} + T_{\text{sel}}}$ | Items per second for a sequential scan of $N$ items, dwell $T_{\text{item}}$, selection time $T_{\text{sel}}$ | selections/s | | 4 | Words per minute (scan) | $\text{WPM} = \dfrac{60}{N_{\text{char}} \cdot T_{\text{item}} + T_{\text{sel}}}$ | Equivalent typing speed for scanned entry | WPM | | 5 | Debounce constant | $N_{\text{deb}} = \dfrac{T_{\text{deb}}}{T_{\text{scan}}}$ | Number of consecutive identical readings required | integer | | 6 | Fitts' Law (key targeting) | $T_{\text{move}} = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W} + 1\right)$ | Time to move from one key to another; $D$ = distance, $W$ = key width, $a,b$ = empirical constants (s) | s | | 7 | Required scanning items (row–col) | $N_{\text{steps}} = m + n$ | Two-stage row-column scan of $m$ rows and $n$ cols | integer | | 8 | Linear scan steps (full keyboard) | $N_{\text{steps}} = m \cdot n$ | One-by-one scan of every key | integer | | 9 | Average scan steps (block scan, $k$ blocks) | $N_{\text{avg}} = \log_k(m \cdot n)$ | Hierarchical narrowing | integer | | 10 | Keystroke force vs. error trade-off | $E \propto \dfrac{1}{F^k},\; k>0$ | Empirical — error $E$ decreases as activation force $F$ increases | dimensionless | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Rehabilitation Engineering:** Selecting between direct and indirect keyboards determines whether a patient with ALS, cerebral palsy, or spinal cord injury can communicate independently. Clinical intake evaluations (e.g., the **QUEST 2.0** questionnaire) explicitly rate the *ease of use* of the keyboard. - **Embedded Product Design:** The matrix-scanning algorithm is reused in industrial keypads, elevator panels, and microwave ovens — understanding it via the AT lens makes the same firmware skill cross-transferable. - **Standards Compliance:** AT keyboards must satisfy **ISO 9241-410** (display-screen keyboard ergonomics) and **Section 508** (US federal accessibility). KTU expects awareness of these specifications. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Minimum Scan Frame Time for an $m \times n$ Keyboard Matrix **Problem:** A designer chooses an $8 \times 16$ keyboard matrix (8 rows, 16 columns = 128 keys). The microcontroller's I/O port read/write takes $T_{\text{clk}} = 2 \,\mu\text{s}$. Determine whether the matrix can be scanned within the USB HID frame budget of $T_{\text{frame}} = 8$ ms. **Step 1 — Identify the scan time formula.** Each key position requires the controller to: (a) drive one row LOW, and (b) read all $n$ columns. Across all $m$ rows, the total number of polled key positions is $m \cdot n$. $$T_{\text{scan}} = m \cdot n \cdot T_{\text{clk}}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the given values.** $$T_{\text{scan}} = 8 \cdot 16 \cdot 2 \,\mu\text{s} = 256 \,\mu\text{s}$$ **Step 3 — Compare to the USB HID frame budget.** $$T_{\text{scan}} = 256 \,\mu\text{s} \;\ll\; T_{\text{frame}} = 8\,000 \,\mu\text{s}$$ **Step 4 — Conclude.** The scan consumes only **3.2 %** of the available frame time, leaving a huge margin for debounce, USB stack handling, and power-saving sleep cycles. **Conclusion:** The $8 \times 16$ matrix is comfortably scan-compatible with USB HID 125 Hz polling. ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Throughput of a Row-Column Scanning Keyboard **Problem:** A user with quadriplegia operates an on-screen keyboard with $m = 6$ rows and $n = 10$ columns (full QWERTY plus digits = 60 keys, but each key occupies one row–column cell). Dwell time per item $T_{\text{item}} = 1.0$ s, switch-press confirmation time $T_{\text{sel}} = 0.3$ s. Compute the typing speed in WPM, assuming an average English word of 5 characters. **Step 1 — Total scan time per character.** Row-column scanning requires the user to wait for the *correct row* to be highlighted and press the switch, then wait for the *correct column* within that row and press again. The expected number of scan items traversed is half the row count plus half the column count, plus the dwell overhead. $$T_{\text{char}} = \frac{m}{2} \cdot T_{\text{item}} + T_{\text{sel}} + \frac{n}{2} \cdot T_{\text{item}} + T_{\text{sel}}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute values.** $$T_{\text{char}} = \frac{6}{2} \cdot 1.0 + 0.3 + \frac{10}{2} \cdot 1.0 + 0.3 = 3.0 + 0.3 + 5.0 + 0.3 = 8.6 \text{ s}$$ **Step 3 — Convert to WPM.** $$\text{WPM} = \frac{60}{T_{\text{char}} \cdot 5} = \frac{60}{8.6 \cdot 5} = \frac{60}{43.0} \approx 1.40 \text{ WPM}$$ **Step 4 — Interpret.** A row-column scanner with these settings produces a typing rate of approximately **1.4 WPM**, which is at the lower bound of functional communication. KTU clinical benchmarks classify this as *basic interactive communication* — enough for yes/no responses and short phrases, but inadequate for fluent conversation. ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Comparing Linear, Row-Column, and Block Scanning **Problem:** Using the same 60-key keyboard, compare the *average* number of switch presses and the *average* selection time for three scanning strategies. Use $T_{\text{item}} = 0.8$ s, $T_{\text{sel}} = 0.2$ s. **Step A — Linear scan (one by one).** The user waits, on average, half of 60 items to be scanned before pressing, then waits for confirmation. $$N_{\text{avg, linear}} = \frac{60}{2} = 30 \text{ items scanned}$$ $$T_{\text{linear}} = 30 \cdot 0.8 + 0.2 = 24.2 \text{ s}$$ **Step B — Row-Column scan (6 rows × 10 cols).** $$N_{\text{avg, RC}} = \frac{6}{2} + \frac{10}{2} = 3 + 5 = 8 \text{ items scanned}$$ $$T_{\text{RC}} = 8 \cdot 0.8 + 2 \cdot 0.2 = 6.4 + 0.4 = 6.8 \text{ s}$$ **Step C — Block scan with 3 hierarchical levels (e.g., 4 → 5 → 3).** $$N_{\text{avg, block}} = \log_4(60) \approx 2.95 \text{ levels}$$ Approximated to the next switch presses: $$T_{\text{block}} \approx 3 \cdot 0.8 + 3 \cdot 0.2 = 3.0 \text{ s}$$ **Step D — Comparative table.** | Strategy | Avg. items scanned | Switch presses | Total time (s) | WPM (5-char words) | |---|---|---|---|---| | Linear | 30 | 1 | 24.2 | 0.50 | | Row-Column | 8 | 2 | 6.8 | 1.76 | | Block (3-level) | 3 | 3 | 3.0 | 4.00 | > [!NOTE] > The trade-off is clear: **fewer scan items require more switch presses per character**. For users with reliable, fast switch actuation, row-column scanning is the KTU-recommended clinical default. For users with extremely slow or effortful switches, block scanning maximizes words-per-minute at the cost of cognitive load. ### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Debounce Count from Scan Period **Problem:** A designer wants a debounce interval $T_{\text{deb}} = 10$ ms. The scan time per frame is $T_{\text{scan}} = 1$ ms. How many identical consecutive readings are required to confirm a key press? **Step 1 — Use the formula.** $$N_{\text{deb}} = \frac{T_{\text{deb}}}{T_{\text{scan}}} = \frac{10 \text{ ms}}{1 \text{ ms}} = 10$$ **Step 2 — Interpretation.** The firmware must observe the same key low for **10 successive scan frames** before publishing a key-down event. This filters out the mechanical bounce of metal-dome or membrane switches, whose bounce duration rarely exceeds **5–8 ms** in quality keyboards. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Keyboard as an AT Computer Interface ```mermaid flowchart LR A[User with Motor
or Sensory Impairment] --> B[Physical Access Channel
Hand, Headstick, Switch, Sip-Puff, Eye-Gaze] B --> C[Keyboard Subsystem
Direct or Scanning] C --> D[Microcontroller
Matrix Scan + Debounce] D --> E[Encoder
Scan Code to HID] E --> F[USB HID Driver
OS Level] F --> G[Application Software
Word Processor, AAC] G --> H[Output to User
Screen, Speech Synth, Braille] H -.Feedback Loop.-> A style A fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8 style C fill:#FFF3CD,stroke:#F4B400 style D fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#D93025 style G fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#188038 ``` ### 4.2 Decision Flow — Direct vs. Indirect Selection on a Keyboard ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([User Assessment]) --> Q1{Can user reach
and press the
target key directly?} Q1 -- YES --> Q2{Is fine motor
precision intact?} Q1 -- NO --> Indirect[Indirect Selection] Q2 -- YES --> Q3{Needs layout
adaptation?} Q2 -- NO --> Keyguard[Use Keyguard +
Expanded Keys] Q3 -- YES --> AltLayout[Dvorak / ABC /
Mini Keyboard] Q3 -- NO --> Standard[Standard QWERTY
with Keycaps] Indirect --> ScanType{Switch actuation
reliable?} ScanType -- YES --> RC[Row-Column Scan] ScanType -- NO --> Block[Block / Group Scan] ScanType -- NO2[Two reliable switches] --> Directed[Directed Scan] style Standard fill:#E6F4EA style AltLayout fill:#E6F4EA style Keyguard fill:#E6F4EA style RC fill:#FCE8E6 style Block fill:#FCE8E6 style Directed fill:#FCE8E6 style Indirect fill:#FFF3CD ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid — Keyboard Matrix Scan Process ```mermaid flowchart TD Init([Initialize: All Rows HIGH]) --> Row0[Drive Row 0 LOW] Row0 --> ReadCols[Read all Columns] ReadCols --> Debounce{Stable for
N_deb frames?} Debounce -- NO --> Row0 Debounce -- YES --> Publish[Publish HID Key-Down
if newly pressed] Publish --> Keyup{All keys
released in row?} Keyup -- NO --> ReadCols Keyup -- YES --> NextRow[Next Row] NextRow --> Done{All m rows
scanned?} Done -- NO --> Row0 Done -- YES --> FrameEnd[Frame Complete
Wait 8 ms] FrameEnd --> Init style Init fill:#E8F0FE style Publish fill:#E6F4EA style FrameEnd fill:#FCE8E6 ``` ### 4.4 Information Flow — Comparison of the Three Scan Strategies ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Linear[Linear Sequential Scan] L1[Item 1] --> L2[Item 2] --> L3[Item 3] --> L4[Item 60] --> Lsel[Switch Press] end subgraph RowCol[Row-Column Scan] R1[Row 1..6] --> Rsel1[Switch 1] --> R2[Within Row: Col 1..10] --> Rsel2[Switch 2] end subgraph Block[Block Hierarchical Scan] B1[Block 1..4] --> Bsel1[Switch 1] --> B2[Sub-Block] --> Bsel2[Switch 2] --> B3[Item] --> Bsel3[Switch 3] end style Lsel fill:#FCE8E6 style Rsel1 fill:#FFF3CD style Rsel2 fill:#FFF3CD style Bsel1 fill:#E6F4EA style Bsel2 fill:#E6F4EA style Bsel3 fill:#E6F4EA ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!NOTE] > The following questions are calibrated to the KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern: **Part A (3 marks, ~50 words)** and **Part B (14 marks, with internal choice, sub-parts of 7+7)**. --- ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]** *Differentiate between direct selection and indirect selection in the context of assistive computer interfaces. Give one example of an input device for each.* **Model Answer (≈ 50 words):** Direct selection allows the user to point to and choose the target in a **single, simultaneous action** (e.g., a finger-press on a QWERTY key or a touch on an on-screen keyboard). Indirect selection presents targets **sequentially** (scanning), and the user confirms the highlighted item with a **separate switch action** (e.g., row-column scanning with a single switch). The first is faster but requires fine motor control; the second suits users with severe motor limitation. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Paper]** *List four types of keyboards commonly used in assistive technology and state the user population each best serves.* **Model Answer (≈ 55 words):** (i) **Expanded/oversized keyboards** — users with tremor, arthritis, or low vision. (ii) **Mini/compact keyboards** — users with limited reach (e.g., wheelchair tray). (iii) **Chord keyboards (e.g., BAT)** — single-hand users with good fine motor control. (iv) **On-screen scanning keyboards** — users with severe motor impairment who operate a single switch. Each keyboard is selected after a clinical assessment of the user's *range of motion, dexterity, vision, and cognitive load tolerance*. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- #### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Series I]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **internal architecture of a computer interface keyboard** used in assistive technology, including the role of the microcontroller, the key matrix, the debounce logic, and the HID encoder. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A row-column scanning keyboard has **m = 5 rows and n = 12 columns** (full QWERTY layout). The dwell time per scanned item is **T_item = 0.75 s**, and the switch-press confirmation time is **T_sel = 0.25 s**. (i) Calculate the **average time to select one character** through row-column scanning. (ii) Calculate the **equivalent typing speed in WPM**, assuming 5 characters per word. (iii) If the same keyboard were to be operated by **linear sequential scanning**, what would be the new average selection time and WPM? Compare the two strategies. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution — Part (a):** 1. **Block Diagram** (drawn on answer sheet, validated against Section 4.1 of these notes): - Key matrix (5 × 12) → Microcontroller → Debounce filter → Scan-code → HID encoder → USB/HID driver → Host OS. 2. **Key matrix:** A grid of $m$ row wires and $n$ column wires; at each intersection sits a normally-open SPST switch. Only one wire is driven LOW at a time; columns are read. 3. **Microcontroller role:** Sequentially drives rows LOW, samples columns, builds the *scan code* (e.g., scan-set 2 for IBM keyboards). 4. **Debounce logic:** A counter increments only if the same key state is observed for $N_{\text{deb}}$ consecutive scans; otherwise the counter is reset. This filters mechanical bounce. 5. **HID encoder:** Translates the scan code to a USB HID usage code (e.g., 'A' → 0x04) and assembles an 8-byte HID report. 6. **OS layer:** The USB HID driver parses the report and places the character in the OS keyboard buffer, making it available to any application. 7. **AT-specific addition:** For indirect-selection keyboards, an additional *scanning control module* resides between the matrix and the HID encoder; it generates the timed highlight pattern and only emits a HID key-down when the user switch is activated. **Model Solution — Part (b):** **(i) Row-column average selection time:** $$T_{\text{RC}} = \frac{m}{2} \cdot T_{\text{item}} + T_{\text{sel}} + \frac{n}{2} \cdot T_{\text{item}} + T_{\text{sel}}$$ $$T_{\text{RC}} = \frac{5}{2} \cdot 0.75 + 0.25 + \frac{12}{2} \cdot 0.75 + 0.25$$ $$T_{\text{RC}} = 1.875 + 0.25 + 4.5 + 0.25 = 6.875 \text{ s per character}$$ **(ii) WPM (row-column):** $$\text{WPM}_{\text{RC}} = \frac{60}{T_{\text{RC}} \cdot 5} = \frac{60}{6.875 \cdot 5} = \frac{60}{34.375} \approx 1.75 \text{ WPM}$$ **(iii) Linear scan average selection time and WPM:** The user must wait, on average, half of $m \cdot n$ items. $$N_{\text{avg, linear}} = \frac{5 \cdot 12}{2} = 30 \text{ items}$$ $$T_{\text{linear}} = 30 \cdot 0.75 + 0.25 = 22.75 \text{ s per character}$$ $$\text{WPM}_{\text{linear}} = \frac{60}{22.75 \cdot 5} = \frac{60}{113.75} \approx 0.53 \text{ WPM}$$ **Comparison:** Row-column scanning is approximately **3.3× faster** than linear scanning (1.75 vs. 0.53 WPM) because it replaces a linear search with a logarithmic two-stage search, dramatically reducing the expected dwell overhead. The trade-off is one extra switch press per character. For a user with a reliable, low-latency switch, row-column scanning is clinically preferred. --- #### Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative* **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Series II]** **(a)** Explain the **classification of assistive keyboards** based on selection method, layout, and actuation mechanism. For each class, cite **two commercial product examples** and the **target user population**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A clinical engineer is asked to recommend a keyboard for a **14-year-old student with cerebral palsy** who has *unreliable grip*, *high tremor*, but *good cognitive function* and *intact vision*. Compare **three plausible keyboard options** using the Fitts' Law framework, listing the engineering trade-offs. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution — Part (a):** **I. By Selection Method** - **Direct-Selection Keyboards:** Standard QWERTY (Logitech K120, Apple Magic Keyboard), Expanded (BigKeys LX, KinderBoard), Mini (Logitech K400, Periboard-332). Suits users with intact fine motor control and adequate range of motion. - **Indirect-Selection (Scanning) Keyboards:** Row-column (ClickToScan on-screen keyboard, Keys-U-See with single-switch interface), Block (Smartbox Grid Player, ProxTalker). Suits users with severe motor limitation operating a single switch. **II. By Layout** - **Standard QWERTY** (104–105 keys) — most software familiarity. - **Alphabetical/ABC** (e.g., KinderBoard) — for users with cognitive disability, dyslexia, or those learning the alphabet. - **Dvorak** — reduces finger travel distance; for users with single-hand access or RSI. - **Chord** (e.g., BAT 7-key, Infogrip Chord) — for users with one functional hand. **III. By Actuation Mechanism** - **Mechanical** (e.g., Cherry MX switches, KeyGuard) — durable, tactile feedback, high activation force (≈ 0.45–0.80 N). - **Membrane** (e.g., most consumer keyboards) — soft, low force (≈ 0.25 N), quieter, less precise. - **Capacitive/Touch** (e.g., iPad on-screen, Sensitive Touch Membrane) — zero force, but susceptible to ghosting. - **Pneumatic (Sip-and-Puff)** — for users with no hand/arm control; sip = one switch, puff = the other. **Model Solution — Part (b):** Three plausible options for the 14-year-old: 1. **Expanded keyboard (BigKeys LX, ~25 mm key pitch) with a keyguard.** - *Fitts' Law implication:* Larger $W$ → smaller $\log_2(D/W+1)$ → faster targeting. Activation force ≈ 0.25 N (low). - *Trade-off:* Bulky footprint; not portable between classrooms. 2. **Standard QWERTY with keyguard and key-locator stickers.** - *Fitts' Law implication:* Smaller $W$ (≈ 19 mm) → larger index of difficulty → more errors. - *Trade-off:* Familiar layout (so cognitive load is low) but tremor will cause adjacent-key errors. 3. **On-screen scanning keyboard with a single adaptive switch (e.g., finger switch mounted on a wrist strap).** - *Fitts' Law implication:* Targeting cost → 0 (no aiming required). Time governed by Equation (3) of the formula sheet. - *Trade-off:* Lowest motor demand but also the lowest WPM; risk of social stigma in a school setting. **Engineering Recommendation:** Given *good cognitive function* and *intact vision* in a 14-year-old who values peer integration, the **expanded keyboard with a keyguard** is the most balanced choice: it preserves the standard QWERTY layout (familiarity), reduces the Fitts' Law index of difficulty, and is socially acceptable in a classroom. The scanning keyboard should be retained as a *backup* for fatigue days. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (CI for Direct & Indirect Selection — Keyboards):** > 1. **Confusing "scanning" with "switching"** — *scanning* is the *presentation strategy* of the targets; *switching* is the *actuation mechanism*.
