Many students interchange the two. Examiner's note: marking scheme deducts up to **2 marks** for this conflation. > 2. **Omitting units** in WPM, $T_{\text{item}}$, $T_{\text{sel}}$ derivations. Always write units explicitly. **1 mark** lost per derivation if units are missing. > 3. **Forgetting the average (½ × N) in scan time** — examiners expect students to compute the *expected* number of items scanned, not the worst case. > 4. **Stating "row-column is faster" without the logarithmic justification** — verbal claim alone earns only 1 of the 2 available marks; the Fitts' Law / log-search argument is required for full credit. > 5. **Ignoring the user's cognitive load** when recommending block scanning — examiners mark down for clinical-engineering answers that focus only on motor metrics. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **Direct selection = simultaneous pointing + activation**; **Indirect selection = sequential presentation + separate confirmation**. - A **keyboard** is a matrix of switches scanned by a microcontroller; typical scan time is **< 1 ms**; the USB HID frame is **8 ms** (125 Hz). - **Debounce** typically requires **5–15 ms** (≈ 5–15 scan frames at 1 ms/frame). - **Typing rate** is measured in **WPM**; standard functional benchmark is **40 WPM**. - **Row-column scan** of an $m \times n$ keyboard needs an expected $\dfrac{m}{2} + \dfrac{n}{2}$ dwell events + 2 confirmations. - **Linear scan** needs $\dfrac{m \cdot n}{2}$ dwell events + 1 confirmation. - **Block / group scan** uses a hierarchical $\log_k$ structure; fastest for very limited switches but highest cognitive load. - **Fitts' Law** governs physical-targeting keyboards: $T = a + b \cdot \log_2(D/W + 1)$. - **Activation force** for AT keyboards is **0.20–0.35 N**; for standard keyboards **0.45–0.80 N**. - **Chord keyboards** (e.g., BAT, 7 keys) generate 128 codes — efficient for single-hand users. - **Sip-and-puff** and **Morse-code** are the lowest-motor-demand indirect methods. - **Keyboards in AT must comply** with **ISO 9241-410** and **Section 508** accessibility standards. - **Clinical selection criteria** include: range of motion, dexterity, vision, cognition, fatigue, and social context. - **Equation (3)** of the formula sheet — $R = 1/(N \cdot T_{\text{item}} + T_{\text{sel}})$ — is the universal throughput equation for any indirect-selection device and is a **high-yield KTU formula**.

topics.title: Touch Screens
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT614 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Touch Screens

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Touch Screen** is a two-dimensional sensing input device that combines a **visual display** (LCD, OLED, or E-Paper) with a **position-sensitive transducer layer**, enabling a human operator to interact directly with on-screen graphical elements by physical contact (finger, stylus, gloved hand, or specialized probe). In the context of **Assistive Medical Devices**, touch screens form the **Human-Machine Interface (HMI)** of patient monitors, ventilators, anesthesia workstations, infusion pumps, ultrasound carts, Electronic Medical Record (EMR) terminals, and rehabilitation robotics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Standardized):** A *touch-sensitive overlay system* that converts a mechanical or capacitive perturbation at coordinate (x, y) on a display surface into corresponding electrical signals (voltage, current, frequency, or phase shift) that are digitized, de-bounced, and mapped to graphical user interface (GUI) events by an embedded controller.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Explanation)

Imagine a **table covered with a thin, invisible spider web**. Each strand of the web can feel a vibration. When you press your finger anywhere on the table, the web senses *where* you pressed and *how hard*. A touch screen works exactly the same way: the "spider web" is a layer of invisible **electrical conductors** (wires, electrodes, or acoustic waves) layered on top of a display, and the "vibration sensor" is a microcontroller that figures out the X–Y coordinates of the touch.

In a hospital, a nurse wearing **latex gloves** tapping a ventilator screen is using a **Projected Capacitive (PCAP)** sensor because it can detect a finger through a glove. A surgeon using a **sterile stylus** on a robotic console needs a **resistive** screen because it works with *any* object.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Metrics (must know for KTU viva):**
> - **Touch Accuracy** = ± 0.5 mm to ± 2 mm (medical-grade)
> - **Response Time** = ≤ 10 ms (clinical requirement)
> - **Light Transmission** = **85 % – 92 %** (optical clarity)
> - **Surface Hardness** = **≥ 7H** (Mohs scale, chemically strengthened)
> - **Operating Temperature** = **0 °C to 50 °C** (clinical environments)
> - **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)** = **≥ 50,000 hours**
> - **Dielectric Constant of Human Skin (ε_r)** ≈ **40 – 50** at 1 MHz

## 1.4 Classification Overview

| Category | Sensing Principle | Glove Compatible | Multi-Touch | Medical Suitability |
|----------|-------------------|------------------|-------------|---------------------|
| Resistive (4/5/8-wire) | Mechanical contact | **Yes** (any object) | Limited (2-point) | Sterile OR use |
| Capacitive (Surface) | Surface charge | Limited | Yes | Patient bedside |
| Projected Capacitive (PCAP) | Mutual/Self-capacitance | **Yes (thin gloves)** | **Yes (10-point)** | **High** (ventilators, monitors) |
| Surface Acoustic Wave (SAW) | Ultrasonic attenuation | Yes | Yes | Diagnostic carts |
| Infrared (IR) | Optical beam break | Yes | **Yes (unlimited)** | Large surgical displays |
| Optical Imaging | Camera-based | Yes | Yes | Tele-medicine kiosks |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principles of Major Touch Technologies

### 2.1.1 Four-Wire Resistive Touch Screen
- Two transparent **Indium Tin Oxide (ITO)** layers separated by **micro-spacer dots** (≈ 10 µm gap).
- The **bottom layer** carries the X-axis electrodes; the **top layer** carries the Y-axis electrodes.
- When pressed, the top layer deflects and makes ohmic contact with the bottom layer → forms a **voltage divider** network.

### 2.1.2 Projected Capacitive (PCAP) Touch Screen
- A grid of **ITO electrodes** (X-rows and Y-columns) is embedded between two glass substrates.
- An **AC excitation signal** (typically **100 kHz – 300 kHz**) drives one axis while the other is grounded.
- The **mutual capacitance** $C_m$ between the crossing electrodes is monitored.
- A finger touch **disturbs the fringing electric field**, reducing $C_m$ by **5 % – 30 %** at the touch node.

### 2.1.3 Surface Acoustic Wave (SAW)
- Two pairs of **piezoelectric transducers** (TX and RX) generate and receive **ultrasonic Rayleigh waves** (≈ 5.53 MHz on glass).
- A finger touch **absorbs acoustic energy** → RX detects a drop in amplitude → time-of-flight analysis gives (x, y).

### 2.1.4 Infrared (IR) Touch
- An array of **IR LEDs** on one edge and **photodetectors** on the opposite edge creates a lattice of invisible light beams.
- A touch **occludes two orthogonal beams** → controller computes the intersection.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear repeatedly in KTU exams.**

$$
\begin{array}{|l|l|l|}
\hline
\textbf{Equation} & \textbf{Meaning} & \textbf{Units} \\
\hline
C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \, \varepsilon_r \, A}{d} & \text{Parallel-plate capacitance} & \text{F (Farads)} \\
\hline
V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \dfrac{R_{touch}}{R_{total}} & \text{Resistive voltage divider} & \text{V} \\
\hline
X_{coord} = \dfrac{V_x}{V_{ref}} \cdot X_{max} & \text{X position from voltage ratio} & \text{mm or px} \\
\hline
t_{flight} = \dfrac{d}{v_{acoustic}} & \text{SAW time-of-flight} & \text{s} \\
\hline
x = \dfrac{v_{acoustic} \cdot \Delta t_{rx}}{2} & \text{SAW position from time} & \text{m} \\
\hline
\dfrac{\Delta C_m}{C_m} \approx -\dfrac{\Delta d_{eff}}{d} & \text{Mutual capacitance change} & \text{dimensionless} \\
\hline
F = \dfrac{1}{2} \, C \cdot V^2 \cdot \dfrac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{C(x)}\right) & \text{Electrostatic force on finger} & \text{N} \\
\hline
\text{SNR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right) & \text{Signal-to-noise ratio} & \text{dB} \\
\hline
\theta_{Snell} = \arcsin\!\left(\dfrac{v_1}{v_2}\right) & \text{Acoustic refraction at glass surface} & \text{rad} \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

## 2.3 Why and How — Engineering Logic

**Why two ITO layers in resistive?**
→ Because a single conductive sheet cannot resolve both X and Y simultaneously; crossing layers create a unique (x, y) node when shorted.

**Why 100 kHz AC in PCAP?**
→ Because at this frequency, the human body acts as a **near-ideal AC ground** through capacitive coupling, maximizing finger-induced field disturbance while avoiding DC polarization (electrolytic effects on skin).

**Why medical devices prefer PCAP?**
→ It supports **multi-touch gestures** (pinch-to-zoom on radiology images), works with **thin surgical gloves**, and has no moving/aging elastomer layers, giving > 200 million touch-cycle durability.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Biomedical Engineering

- **Infusion pumps (e.g., Alaris, Baxter):** PCAP screens with anti-bacterial oleophobic coatings.
- **Surgical robots (Da Vinci, Hugo):** 3-DOF force-feedback touch consoles using **projected capacitive + haptic overlay**.
- **Patient monitors (Philips IntelliVue, GE CARESCAPE):** PCAP with 10-point touch for rapid trend analysis.
- **Audiology/Hearing aids fitting:** Resistive touch for stylus-driven audiogram input.
- **Wheelchair control interfaces:** Custom IR touch panels for users with limited dexterity.
- **Tele-ICU stations:** Large-format optical touch screens (40"+) for multi-clinician collaboration.
- **Cleanroom & sterile environments:** SAW or optical (because resistive degrades with disinfectant alcohol).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Position Detection in a 4-Wire Resistive Touch Screen

### Step 1 — Setup of the voltage divider
When the top sheet (Y-electrode) is pressed onto the bottom sheet (X-electrode), contact occurs at point P(x, y). The bottom sheet is uniformly resistive, so it behaves as a **linear potential divider**.

Let the bottom X-sheet have total resistance $R_X$ across the X-axis. The point of contact divides it into $R_{X1}$ (left of P) and $R_{X2}$ (right of P).

$$
R_{X1} + R_{X2} = R_X
$$

### Step 2 — Apply V_ref across the X-sheet
During X-measurement, a reference voltage $V_{ref} = +5\,\text{V}$ is applied across the X-electrodes (+V on left, GND on right). The Y-sheet acts as a high-impedance **sense probe**.

$$
V_y(x) = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{R_{X2}}{R_{X1} + R_{X2}}
$$

### Step 3 — Linearity assumption
Because the ITO sheet is homogeneous with **uniform sheet resistance** $\rho_s$ (ohms per square):

$$
R_{X1} = \rho_s \cdot \frac{x}{W}, \quad R_{X2} = \rho_s \cdot \frac{(X_{max} - x)}{W}
$$

where $W$ is the electrode width and $X_{max}$ is the active X-length.

### Step 4 — Substitute and simplify
Plugging into the divider equation:

$$
V_y(x) = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{\rho_s \cdot \dfrac{X_{max} - x}{W}}{\rho_s \cdot \dfrac{x}{W} + \rho_s \cdot \dfrac{X_{max} - x}{W}}
$$

The factors $\rho_s$ and $W$ cancel, giving:

$$
V_y(x) = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{X_{max} - x}{X_{max}}
$$

Rearranging to solve for x:

$$
X_{coord} = X_{max} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{V_y}{V_{ref}}\right)
$$

This is the **canonical resistive touch equation** used in every KTU numerical problem.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "1 − V_y/V_ref"?** Because at $x = 0$ (left edge) the probe reads $V_{ref}$, and at $x = X_{max}$ it reads $0\,\text{V}$. An **inverting ADC** is therefore used, or the firmware subtracts the result from $V_{ref}$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Mutual Capacitance Change in a PCAP Sensor

### Step 1 — Define geometry
A projected capacitive node consists of two **orthogonal ITO electrodes** (a "row" and a "column") crossing with a transparent **dielectric spacer** (typically a glass + OCA stack) of total thickness $d$ and relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r$.

### Step 2 — Baseline mutual capacitance
For a parallel-plate approximation valid for the **overlap region** of area $A$:

$$
C_{m0} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \, \varepsilon_r \, A}{d}
$$

However, in PCAP, the **fringing field** dominates because the electrodes are patterned (diamond, snowflake, or rectangular). The effective capacitance is:

$$
C_{m0} = \varepsilon_0 \, \varepsilon_r \, L \cdot f\!\left(\dfrac{w}{d}\right)
$$

where $L$ is the perimeter of overlap, $w$ is electrode width, and $f$ is a geometric form-factor (typically 0.3 – 1.2 pF/cm).

### Step 3 — Introduce a finger
A finger placed above the node acts as a **conductive grounded plane** at distance $h$ from the top surface. This adds a parasitic capacitance $C_{finger}$ in parallel with the **environmental capacitance to ground** $C_{gnd}$, **diverting** some of the fringing flux.

The new mutual capacitance becomes:

$$
C_{m,\text{touch}} = C_{m0} \cdot \left(1 - k \cdot e^{-h/\lambda}\right)
$$

where $k$ is the coupling coefficient (≈ 0.2 – 0.4) and $\lambda$ is the **field penetration depth** (≈ 1 – 3 mm).

### Step 4 — Linearized sensitivity
For small perturbations, taking the partial derivative with respect to finger distance $h$:

$$
\frac{\partial C_m}{\partial h} = C_{m0} \cdot k \cdot \frac{1}{\lambda} \cdot e^{-h/\lambda}
$$

The fractional change measured by the readout IC is:

$$
\frac{\Delta C_m}{C_{m0}} = -k \cdot e^{-h/\lambda}
$$

The **negative sign** confirms that finger touch *reduces* mutual capacitance by shunting field lines to ground.

### Step 5 — Signal-to-noise ratio
The controller resolves touch if SNR exceeds a threshold (typically 6 dB):

$$
\text{SNR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta C_m \cdot V_{exc}}{V_{noise,\text{thermal}}}\right)
$$

where thermal noise is:

$$
V_{noise,\text{thermal}} = \sqrt{4 \, k_B \, T \, R \cdot \Delta f}
$$

with $k_B$ = Boltzmann's constant (**1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K**), $T$ = 300 K, $R$ = electrode resistance (≈ 10 – 100 Ω), and $\Delta f$ = measurement bandwidth (≈ 100 kHz).

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A 4-wire resistive touch screen has $X_{max} = 200\,\text{mm}$, $V_{ref} = 5\,\text{V}$, and the ADC reads $V_y = 1.85\,\text{V}$. Find the touch X-coordinate.

**Solution:**

$$
X_{coord} = X_{max} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{V_y}{V_{ref}}\right) = 200 \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1.85}{5.00}\right)
$$

$$
X_{coord} = 200 \cdot (1 - 0.370) = 200 \cdot 0.630 = 126\,\text{mm}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Trick:** Students often forget that the Y-axis uses an **identical equation** with $Y_{max}$ and a *second* voltage $V_x$ measured after swapping the excitation polarity. Always state both $X_{coord}$ and $Y_{coord}$ explicitly in your answer.

## 3.4 Comparison Matrix — Touch Technologies for Medical Use

$$
\begin{array}{|l|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Parameter} & \textbf{4-Wire R} & \textbf{PCAP} & \textbf{SAW} & \textbf{IR} & \textbf{Optical} \\
\hline
\text{Transparency} & 75\% & 90\% & 92\% & 100\% & 95\% \\
\hline
\text{Multi-touch} & \text{No/2} & \text{10+} & \text{2} & \text{Unlimited} & \text{Unlimited} \\
\hline
\text{Glove support} & \text{Any} & \text{Thin} & \text{Any} & \text{Any} & \text{Any} \\
\hline
\text{Disinfectant-safe} & \text{Moderate} & \text{Excellent} & \text{Excellent} & \text{Excellent} & \text{Excellent} \\
\hline
\text{Cost (relative)} & \text{Low} & \text{Medium} & \text{High} & \text{Medium} & \text{High} \\
\hline
\text{Latency} & 10\text{ ms} & 5\text{ ms} & 15\text{ ms} & 8\text{ ms} & 12\text{ ms} \\
\hline
\text{Stability vs EMI} & \text{High} & \text{Medium} & \text{Low} & \text{Low} & \text{Medium} \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Touch Screen System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DISP[Display Layer]
        LCD[LCD or OLED Panel]
    end
    subgraph SENSE[Touch Sensor Stack]
        TOP[Top ITO or Glass Substrate]
        MID[Spacer or OCA Dielectric]
        BOT[Bottom ITO or Substrate]
    end
    subgraph CTRL[Controller Electronics]
        DRV[Analog Front End AFE]
        ADC[12 or 16 bit ADC]
        DSP[Digital Signal Processor]
        MCU[Embedded Microcontroller]
    end
    subgraph HOST[Medical Host System]
        APP[Ventilator or Monitor Application]
        HMI[GUI Renderer]
    end
    USER([Clinician Finger or Stylus]) --> TOP
    TOP --> MID
    MID --> BOT
    BOT --> DRV
    DRV --> ADC
    ADC --> DSP
    DSP --> MCU
    MCU --> APP
    APP --> HMI
    HMI --> LCD
    LCD -. visual feedback .-> USER
```

## 4.2 Flowchart — Touch Detection and Coordinate Reporting (PCAP Example)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power On and Sensor Self Calibration] --> B[Scan All X Y Electrode Crossings]
    B --> C{Measured C_m below Threshold}
    C -- No --> B
    C -- Yes --> D[Record Candidate Touch Node]
    D --> E[Apply Debounce and Noise Filter]
    E --> F{Number of Valid Nodes}
    F -- One --> G[Single Touch X Y Computed]
    F -- Multiple --> H[Run Multi Touch Algorithm]
    H --> I[Compute Centroid and Gesture Map]
    G --> J[Send HID or I2C Event to Host]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Host Updates GUI on LCD]
    K --> B
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Resistive Touch Screen Electrical Equivalent

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VREF[V ref 5V DC Source] --> RX1[R x left segment]
    RX1 --> P[Touch Contact Point P]
    P --> RX2[R x right segment]
    RX2 --> GND1[Ground]
    YPROBE[High Impedance Y Sense Probe] --> P
    P --> ADC[ADC Input Channel]
    ADC --> CTRL[Microcontroller]
    CTRL --> DISP[Display Coordinate Mapping]
```

## 4.4 Functional Comparison Schematic — SAW Touch Screen

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TX[Transmitter Side]
        TXC[Oscillator at 53 MHz] --> TX1[Piezo TX for X axis]
        TXC --> TX2[Piezo TX for Y axis]
    end
    subgraph GLASS[Glass Substrate with Reflectors]
        TX1 --> RX1[Reflector Array X]
        TX2 --> RX2[Reflector Array Y]
        RX1 --> RXP[Piezo RX for X axis]
        RX2 --> RXP
    end
    RXP --> TOF[Time of Flight Measurement Block]
    TOF --> DSP[Digital Processor Computes X Y]
    DSP --> HOST[Medical Device Host CPU]
    FINGER([Finger Absorbs Acoustic Energy]) -. attenuates .-> GLASS
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip (KTU):** In the SAW schematic, the diagonal **reflector arrays** are etched at precise angles so that acoustic waves are deflected by **90°** at known distances. The time between transmitted and received pulse gives X and Y. A finger touching the glass absorbs energy at that location, creating a dip in the received waveform that maps directly to the touch coordinate.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define a touch screen. List any four types of touch screen technologies used in medical devices.**

**Model Answer:**
A touch screen is an **input-output interface device** that allows direct interaction with a display by detecting the position of a physical contact on the screen surface.

Four types used in medical devices:
1. **Resistive** (4-wire, 5-wire, 8-wire) — used in infusion pumps.
2. **Projected Capacitive (PCAP)** — used in patient monitors and ventilators.
3. **Surface Acoustic Wave (SAW)** — used in diagnostic imaging consoles.
4. **Infrared (IR)** — used in large surgical displays.

**Mark Splitting (KTU pattern):** Definition = 1 mark × Types listing = 2 marks (½ mark each).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Why is Projected Capacitive (PCAP) touch preferred over resistive touch in modern medical ventilators? Give any four reasons.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Multi-touch capability** — enables pinch-zoom on waveform displays.
2. **Gloved-hand operation** — works through thin surgical/latex gloves.
3. **Higher durability** — no elastomer layer; survives > 200 million touches.
4. **Better optical clarity** — light transmission > 90 % (vs 75 % resistive).
5. **Chemical resistance** — withstands IPA, bleach, and quaternary-ammonia disinfectants.
6. **Lower latency** — typical response ≤ 5 ms.

**Mark Splitting:** Any 4 reasons × ¾ mark each = 3 marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT** draw a "general smartphone" diagram and label it as a medical device. Always mention **medical application context** (ventilator, monitor, infusion pump) to score full application marks.
> - **Do NOT** write only the working principle. KTU's 14-mark pattern demands: **(i) diagram, (ii) principle, (iii) derivation/equation, (iv) medical application, (v) comparison/limitations**.
> - **Numerical errors** in position-detection problems cost 2 marks. Always include **units**.
> - **Vague statements** like "capacitive works by capacitance" get **zero**. Always quote the equation $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$.

---

### Question A (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Explain the construction and working of a 4-wire resistive touch screen with a neat diagram. Derive the expression for the X-coordinate in terms of the reference voltage and measured voltage.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Construction (2 marks):**
- Two transparent **ITO-coated polyester/glass sheets** separated by **insulating micro-dots** (≈ 10 µm).
- The **bottom sheet** has two bus bars on its left and right edges (X-electrodes).
- The **top sheet** has two bus bars on its top and bottom edges (Y-electrodes).
- A flexible top cover (PET film) transmits finger force to the ITO layers.

**Step 2 — Working Principle (2 marks):**
- During X-measurement, $V_{ref}$ is applied across the left (+) and right (−) X-electrodes.
- The top Y-sheet acts as a high-impedance voltage probe.
- Finger pressure creates a **galvanic short** between the two ITO layers at point P(x, y).
- The voltage $V_y$ read at the Y-probe is proportional to the X-position.
- For Y-measurement, the process is **swapped**: $V_{ref}$ is applied across Y-bus bars and X acts as probe.

**Step 3 — Derivation (2 marks):**
(Show full derivation from Section 3.1; final result:)

$$
X_{coord} = X_{max} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{V_y}{V_{ref}}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Medical Application (1 mark):**
Used in **stylus-driven input** on portable ultrasound machines and **infusion pump** keypads where any object (stylus, fingernail, gloved finger) must activate the input.

---

### Question A (b) [7 Marks]

**Explain the working of a Projected Capacitive (PCAP) touch screen. Show how a finger touch changes the mutual capacitance and derive the expression for fractional capacitance change.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Construction (1.5 marks):**
- Two perpendicular arrays of **ITO electrodes** patterned on inner surfaces of two glass substrates.
- Electrodes are typically diamond, snowflake, or rectangular in shape to maximize **fringing field**.
- Bonded with **Optically Clear Adhesive (OCA)**.

**Step 2 — Working Principle (1.5 marks):**
- An **AC excitation** (100 – 300 kHz) is applied to one axis (say X-rows).
- The orthogonal axis (Y-columns) is grounded through a **charge-measurement circuit**.
- The **mutual capacitance** $C_m$ at each crossing is continuously scanned.
- When a finger approaches, it **shunts fringing field lines to ground**, decreasing $C_m$.

**Step 3 — Derivation of ΔC/C (3 marks):**
(Show full derivation from Section 3.2; final result:)

$$
\frac{\Delta C_m}{C_{m0}} = -k \cdot e^{-h/\lambda}
$$

where:
- $k$ = coupling coefficient (0.2 – 0.4)
- $h$ = finger-to-glass distance
- $\lambda$ = field penetration depth (1 – 3 mm)

**Step 4 — Multi-Touch Algorithm (1 mark):**
- The controller scans all (row, column) intersections and builds a **2-D capacitance map**.
- A **connected-component analysis** identifies each distinct touch blob.
- **Centroid calculation** gives sub-pixel accuracy, and gesture recognition (pinch, rotate) is performed on the centroid set.

---

### Question B (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Compare resistive, capacitive, SAW, and infrared touch screen technologies on the basis of: (i) construction, (ii) working principle, (iii) multi-touch support, (iv) suitability for sterile medical environments.**

**Model Solution (Tabular format preferred by KTU examiners — 6 marks):**

| Parameter | Resistive | Capacitive (PCAP) | SAW | Infrared |
|-----------|-----------|-------------------|------|----------|
| **Construction** | 2 ITO layers with spacer | ITO grid on 2 glass layers | Piezo TX-RX + glass | IR LED + PD arrays |
| **Principle** | Voltage divider on contact | Mutual-cap change | Acoustic absorption | Light beam occlusion |
| **Multi-touch** | Limited (2-point) | 10+ points | 2 points | Unlimited |
| **Sterile Use** | Limited (degrades with alcohol) | **Excellent (anti-bac coating)** | Good | **Excellent** |
| **Glove** | Any object | Thin gloves | Any | Any |

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
- For **sterile OR environments**, **SAW** and **optical** are best because they have **no ITO layer** that can be damaged by alcohol disinfectants.
- For **patient bedside monitors**, **PCAP** is best due to multi-touch and clarity.
- For **low-cost infusion pumps**, **resistive** is acceptable.

---

### Question B (b) [7 Marks]

**Explain the working of a Surface Acoustic Wave (SAW) touch screen. Derive the position-detection equation using time-of-flight analysis. Mention two medical applications.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Construction (1.5 marks):**
- A **pure glass substrate** with high acoustic transmission (e.g., soda-lime or borosilicate).
- **Two pairs of piezoelectric transducers** at the corners: TX and RX.
- **Reflector arrays** etched into the glass edges at precise angles.

**Step 2 — Working Principle (2 marks):**
- A **53 MHz** electrical signal is applied to the TX piezo, generating **Rayleigh surface waves**.
- The waves travel across the glass, reflect off the diagonal arrays, and are picked up by the RX piezo.
- A **finger touch absorbs** part of the wave energy → the RX signal shows a **dip** at the time corresponding to the touch location.
- Time-of-flight difference is converted to (x, y) coordinates.

**Step 3 — Derivation (2.5 marks):**
Let the total path length from TX to reflector to touch point to RX be $L$. Acoustic velocity in glass $v_{acoustic} \approx 3.1 \times 10^3\,\text{m/s}$ for Rayleigh waves.

The **time-of-flight** for the acoustic pulse to reach the touch point is:

$$
t_{touch} = \frac{L}{v_{acoustic}}
$$

The position coordinate $x$ (assuming 1-D for simplicity) is found from the **time difference** between the reference signal and the touch-attenuated signal:

$$
x = \frac{v_{acoustic} \cdot \Delta t_{rx}}{2}
$$

The factor of 2 accounts for the **round-trip reflection geometry**.

In 2-D, with two reflectors at right angles:

$$
x = \frac{v_{acoustic}}{2} \cdot \Delta t_x, \quad y = \frac{v_{acoustic}}{2} \cdot \Delta t_y
$$

**Step 4 — Medical Applications (1 mark):**
1. **Portable ultrasound consoles** where a stylus is preferred.
2. **Blood-analyzer touch interfaces** in pathology labs (chemical-resistant glass).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision — read this 5 times before the exam.**

- ☐ **Resistive touch equation** (memorize verbatim): $X_{coord} = X_{max} \cdot (1 - V_y / V_{ref})$
- ☐ **Capacitance formula** (parallel plate): $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$ ; $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{F/m}$
- ☐ **PCAP fractional change** (with negative sign!): $\Delta C_m / C_{m0} = -k \cdot e^{-h/\lambda}$
- ☐ **SAW acoustic velocity in glass** ≈ **3100 m/s** (Rayleigh wave)
- ☐ **IR touch** uses **optical beam break**, not acoustic — common confusion in MCQs.
- ☐ **PCAP** is the **industry standard** for new medical devices; **resistive** is for legacy / low-cost.
- ☐ **Finger reduces $C_m$** (does NOT increase it) — this is the *most* tested KTU concept.
- ☐ Always quote **units** in numerical answers (mm, V, pF, ms).
- ☐ For **sterile hospital use**, mention **anti-bacterial oleophobic coating** + **IP65 rating**.
- ☐ **Optical/IR touch** can be scaled to **100-inch diagonal**; PCAP is limited to **~32-inch** in medical.
- ☐ **MTBF** of medical-grade touch = **> 50,000 hours**; remember this for viva.
- ☐ **Hospital EMI** (from MRI, electrosurgical units) can couple into PCAP — **shielding + spread-spectrum excitation** is required.
- ☐ **Acoustic wave frequency** in SAW ≈ **5.53 MHz**; period ≈ 180 ns — used to set ADC sampling rate.
- ☐ **Gloved-finger PCAP** works because $\varepsilon_r$ of latex (≈ 3) and nitrile (≈ 4) is still much higher than air (= 1), so the fringing field is perturbed enough to be detected.
- ☐ **Optical touch** uses **two CMOS cameras** at the top corners and **infrared back-lighting**; triangulates finger tip using image processing — robust to scratches and contaminants.

**END OF MODULE 2 — TOUCH SCREENS NOTES**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept Keyboards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBRT614 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Concept Keyboards # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Concept Keyboard** is a flat, touch-sensitive, programmable input device in which the entire active surface is divided into a matrix of discrete, pressure-sensitive or capacitive cells. Unlike a conventional QWERTY keyboard that has fixed keys corresponding to the Latin alphabet, a concept keyboard uses an **interchangeable overlay template** that allows the user (or caregiver) to reconfigure the meaning of every touch region. Each active region is mapped, via firmware or host software, to a specific character, command, symbol, or application-level macro. In the context of **KTU OEBRT614 (Assistive Medical Devices)**, concept keyboards are classified as **Alternative and Augmentative Communication (AAC) input devices** and as **adaptive access technologies** for patients with motor impairment (cerebral palsy, muscular dystrophy, stroke hemiparesis), cognitive disabilities (autism, intellectual disability), visual impairment (when paired with tactile/Braille overlays), and progressive neurological disorders (ALS, Parkinson's disease, multiple sclerosis). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A concept keyboard is defined as a non-mechanical, surface-based input transducer that translates discrete spatial contact events into standardized digital codes by referencing an electronically stored key-map, which can be dynamically reloaded from an overlay sheet. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Think of a **concept keyboard like a restaurant menu with pictures**. - In a foreign country where you don't speak the language, instead of typing out a complex sentence on a typewriter, you simply **point at a picture of the dish** you want. The waiter (the software) instantly knows what you mean. - A standard computer keyboard is like a typewriter with hundreds of fixed letters — you must spell everything out. - A concept keyboard replaces those hundreds of fixed letters with **large, customizable "pictures" or symbols** that can be swapped out depending on the task. A child doing math sees numbers; a stroke patient asking for water sees a glass-of-water icon; a musician sees musical notes. The "concept" in *concept keyboard* refers to the fact that each region represents a **concept** (idea, action, word) rather than a single mechanical key-press. ## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics in Concept Keyboards - **Active Sensing Area:** typically **120 mm × 80 mm** to **A4 size (210 mm × 297 mm)**, depending on the form factor. - **Spatial Resolution:** number of independently addressable cells per cm², typically **4 to 16 cells/cm²**. - **Response Latency:** the delay between touch-down and host notification, typically **< 15 ms** for capacitive types and **< 30 ms** for resistive membrane types. - **Activation Force:** minimum force required to register a valid touch, configurable from **0.5 N to 2.5 N** for users with limited motor strength. - **Overlay Material Thickness:** **0.1 mm to 0.5 mm** printed plastic or paper template. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** Concept keyboards are listed under *Module 2 — Alternative Input Devices for Persons with Disabilities* in the KTU 2024 OEBRT614 syllabus, alongside sip-and-puff switches, eye trackers, and brain-computer interfaces. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Internal Architecture — Block-by-Block Breakdown A concept keyboard is composed of **four functional layers**: 1. **Physical Overlay Layer** - A printed sheet (paper, plastic, or polyester) placed on top of the active surface. - Contains human-readable icons, pictures, words, or Braille-like embossed markers. - The overlay has **no electrical function** — it is purely a visual/tactile guide for the user. - Magnetically or mechanically clipped to the housing. 2. **Sensing Layer** - A matrix of sensors arranged in **M rows × N columns**. - Two dominant technologies: - **Resistive Membrane Type:** two transparent conductive films separated by a micro-spacer; contact closes the circuit at the touch point. - **Capacitive (Projected Capacitive / PCT) Type:** an X–Y grid of electrodes whose mutual capacitance changes when a finger (a conductive mass) approaches. - The matrix scans sequentially, like a multiplexing switch array. 3. **Controller / Firmware Layer** - A microcontroller (e.g., ARM Cortex-M0, PIC16F, or ESP32) executes a **scanning algorithm**. - Detects which cell is currently active. - Translates the (row, column) coordinate into a **scan code** by referencing the loaded key-map. - Transmits the code to the host via USB-HID, Bluetooth-HID, I²C, or a serial protocol. 4. **Host Software Layer** - Receives the scan code. - Maps it to an OS-level keystroke, mouse event, or application-specific action. - Allows the user/caregiver to **program** the mapping (this is the "concept" reconfiguration step). ## 2.2 Operational Logic — Stepwise Flow - **Step 1 — Overlay Loading:** The user selects or prints an overlay sheet corresponding to the desired task (e.g., "Communication — Basic Needs"). - **Step 2 — Key-Map Programming:** The host loads a corresponding mapping table. For example, cell (3, 5) is mapped to the ASCII code for the word "WATER." - **Step 3 — Scanning:** The controller drives each row sequentially and reads the column return lines. A pressed cell creates a unique (row, column) signature. - **Step 4 — Debounce & Validation:** Multiple samples are taken to filter mechanical vibration and electromagnetic noise. - **Step 5 — Code Emission:** A unique scan code is sent to the host (often as a standard USB HID keycode). - **Step 6 — Host Action:** The OS or AAC application (e.g., Proloquo2Go, Grid 3, Click-to-Communicate) executes the mapped action. > [!NOTE] > **Why the "Concept" Naming?** Traditional keyboards have a 1-to-1 fixed mapping (physical key → letter). A concept keyboard breaks this binding — the **same physical region can mean different things** based on the active overlay and the loaded key-map. The user is selecting a *concept* (an idea, picture, or command) rather than typing a character. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet The following equations and parameters are the high-yield quantitative tools for KTU examination answers on concept keyboards. | **Parameter / Symbol** | **Equation or Definition** | **Typical Units** | **Physical Meaning** | |---|---|---|---| | Number of cells $N_c$ | $N_c = M \times N$ | dimensionless | Total addressable touch zones on the matrix | | Spatial resolution $R_s$ | $R_s = \dfrac{N_c}{A}$ | cells / cm² | Cells per unit active area | | Active area $A$ | $A = L \times W$ | cm² | Length × Width of sensing region | | Scan period $T_s$ | $T_s = M \times t_{row}$ | ms (milliseconds) | Time to scan all rows once | | Response latency $L_t$ | $L_t = T_s + t_{debounce} + t_{xmit}$ | ms | End-to-end delay from touch to host action | | Debounce count $k$ | $k = \lceil t_{debounce} \cdot f_{scan} \rceil$ | samples | Number of valid samples required | | Activation force $F_a$ | $F_a = P_a \cdot A_{contact}$ | N (Newton) | Pressure threshold × fingertip contact area | | Signal-to-noise ratio $SNR$ | $SNR = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | dB (decibel) | Robustness of touch detection | | Capacitance change $\Delta C$ | $\Delta C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A_{electrode}}{d_{touch}}$ | farads (F) | Capacitive shift when finger approaches | | Power consumption $P$ | $P = V_{dd} \cdot I_{active} \cdot D_{cycle} + V_{dd} \cdot I_{sleep} \cdot (1-D_{cycle})$ | mW (milliwatts) | Average draw using duty cycling | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examination Note:** KTU board examiners frequently ask students to **derive the relationship between matrix size, scan time, and reported latency**. Memorize $N_c = M \times N$ and $T_s = M \times t_{row}$ as these are the most commonly tested equations. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Clinical Communication:** Bed-ridden patients in ICU who cannot speak (post-tracheostomy, intubated) use a concept keyboard overlay with body-part icons to communicate pain location to nursing staff. - **Pediatric Therapy:** Children with autism use picture-exchange overlays (PECS-style) to indicate wants/needs, accelerating symbolic communication acquisition. - **Surgical & Dental Settings:** Surgeons using a sterile-field overlay can call up pre-programmed phrases ("More suction," "Irrigation please") into a speech synthesizer without breaking scrub. - **Rehabilitation Centers:** Occupational therapists use concept keyboards to grade motor-recovery exercises — large cells for hemiplegic patients, small cells for fine-motor retraining. - **Smart Home & Environmental Control:** Disabled users can map 8 large cells to "Lights on," "Fan high," "Call attendant," "TV channel up," etc. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Number of Addressable Cells from Matrix Dimensions **Problem Statement:** A concept keyboard has a sensing matrix of **M = 8 rows** and **N = 12 columns**. Derive the expression for the total number of independently addressable cells and compute its value. **Derivation:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1: Define the matrix structure.} \\ \text{Each cell is uniquely identified by an ordered pair } (r, c) \\ \text{where } r \in \{1, 2, \ldots, M\} \text{ and } c \in \{1, 2, \ldots, N\}. \\ \\ \text{Step 2: Apply the counting principle (rule of product).} \\ \text{For each of the } M \text{ rows, the column index can take } N \text{ distinct values.} \\ \text{Hence the total number of distinct (row, column) pairs is the product.} \\ N_c = M \times N \\ \\ \text{Step 3: Substitute the given numerical values.} \\ N_c = 8 \times 12 \\ \\ \text{Step 4: Evaluate.} \\ N_c = 96 \text{ cells} \end{aligned} $$ **Engineering Interpretation:** An 8 × 12 matrix provides 96 unique touch zones. If the active area is $A = 24 \text{ cm} \times 18 \text{ cm} = 432 \text{ cm}^2$, the spatial resolution is $R_s = 96 / 432 \approx 0.222$ cells/cm². Each cell is roughly $2.1 \text{ cm} \times 2.1 \text{ cm}$, which is large enough for users with motor tremor. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — End-to-End Latency for a Matrix-Scanned Concept Keyboard **Problem Statement:** A 6 × 10 resistive-membrane concept keyboard scans each row in $t_{row} = 1.2 \text{ ms}$. The firmware requires 3 consecutive valid samples to debounce, and the USB-HID report transmission takes $t_{xmit} = 0.8 \text{ ms}$. Derive the worst-case response latency and express it in SI units of seconds. **Derivation:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1: Determine the total scan period (one full matrix scan).} \\ T_s = M \times t_{row} \\ T_s = 6 \times 1.2 \text{ ms} \\ T_s = 7.2 \text{ ms} \\ \\ \text{Step 2: Compute the debounce window. The worst case assumes the touch} \\ \text{event occurs immediately AFTER the cell is sampled, so the firmware must wait} \\ \text{for } k \text{ additional full scan cycles to confirm validity.} \\ t_{debounce} = (k - 1) \times T_s \\ t_{debounce} = (3 - 1) \times 7.2 \text{ ms} \\ t_{debounce} = 14.4 \text{ ms} \\ \\ \text{Step 3: Add the worst-case initial scan wait and the transmission delay.} \\ L_t = T_s + t_{debounce} + t_{xmit} \\ L_t = 7.2 \text{ ms} + 14.4 \text{ ms} + 0.8 \text{ ms} \\ L_t = 22.4 \text{ ms} \\ \\ \text{Step 4: Convert to SI units (seconds).} \\ L_t = 22.4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} \\ L_t = 2.24 \times 10^{-2} \text{ s} \end{aligned} $$ **Engineering Interpretation:** A latency of 22.4 ms is well below the 100 ms human-perception threshold for "instantaneous" response. For real-time AAC use, KTU board examiners expect students to comment that **debounce delay, not scan time, dominates the total latency budget**. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Spatial Resolution of a Concept Keyboard **Problem Statement:** A rectangular concept keyboard has an active area of $L = 30 \text{ cm}$ by $W = 20 \text{ cm}$ and supports $N_c = 200$ independent cells. Derive the spatial resolution $R_s$ and the average side length of one cell, assuming square cells. **Derivation:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1: Compute the active sensing area.} \\ A = L \times W \\ A = 30 \text{ cm} \times 20 \text{ cm} \\ A = 600 \text{ cm}^2 \\ \\ \text{Step 2: Define spatial resolution as cells per unit area.} \\ R_s = \frac{N_c}{A} \\ R_s = \frac{200}{600 \text{ cm}^2} \\ R_s = 0.333 \text{ cells/cm}^2 \\ \\ \text{Step 3: Compute the average cell area (assume square cells).} \\ A_{cell} = \frac{1}{R_s} = 3.0 \text{ cm}^2 \\ \\ \text{Step 4: Compute the side length of one square cell.} \\ s_{cell} = \sqrt{A_{cell}} = \sqrt{3.0} \\ s_{cell} \approx 1.732 \text{ cm} \\ \\ \text{Step 5: State the result with appropriate significant figures.} \\ s_{cell} \approx 1.73 \text{ cm} \end{aligned} $$ **Engineering Interpretation:** A 1.73 cm cell side length is appropriate for adult users with mild-to-moderate motor impairment. For pediatric users, a larger cell size (2.5–4 cm) is preferred; for fine-motor rehabilitation, smaller cells (0.8–1.2 cm) are used. KTU examiners will often ask: *"What cell size would you recommend for a cerebral-palsy patient with athetosis?"* — the answer is **larger cells (3–4 cm) with adjustable activation force**. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Concept Keyboards vs. Standard Keyboards | **Parameter** | **Concept Keyboard** | **Standard QWERTY Keyboard** | |---|---|---| | Mechanical travel | **0 mm** (flat surface) | 3–4 mm key travel | | Re-configurability | **Full**, via overlay + key-map | None (hardware-fixed layout) | | Tactile feedback | Optional (tactile overlay dots) | Strong (mechanical click) | | Learning curve | **Low** (picture-based) | High (memorize alphabet) | | Cost (entry-level) | $30 – $200 | $10 – $150 | | Suitability for motor-impaired | **High** | Low (small, closely-spaced keys) | | Speed for trained typist | Low (one concept per touch) | High (multi-finger typing) | | Power consumption | 20 – 80 mW | 50 – 250 mW | | Cleaning / infection control | Easy (smooth surface, wipeable) | Difficult (key gaps trap pathogens) | | Durability (MTBF) | ~ 5 million touches/cell | ~ 10–20 million keystrokes/key | --- ## 3.5 Comparative Sub-Types of Concept Keyboards | **Sub-Type** | **Sensing Principle** | **Strengths** | **Weaknesses** | **Typical Use Case** | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Resistive Membrane** | Two ITO films separated by spacer dots | Low cost, works with stylus or gloved finger | Requires firm press; wears with heavy use | Educational AAC boards | | **Capacitive (Projected)** | Mutual-capacitance X–Y grid | Light touch, multi-touch capable, durable | Needs bare finger or conductive stylus | Medical-grade communication | | **Infrared Matrix** | IR beams broken by finger shadow | Works with any object; no contact needed | Sensitive to ambient IR (sunlight) | Industrial / sterile-field | | **Surface Acoustic Wave (SAW)** | Ultrasonic waves attenuated by touch | High clarity, scratch-resistant | Sensitive to water droplets on surface | Public-access kiosks | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Focus:** The most commonly examined sub-type is the **resistive membrane** concept keyboard, due to its simplicity and low cost — both key syllabus discussion points for assistive-device engineering. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Concept Keyboard System The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **end-to-end data flow** from a user's finger contact through to the host application that performs the intended action. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[User Finger
Contact] --> B[Physical Overlay Sheet
Printed Icons or Text] B --> C[Sensing Matrix
M rows x N columns] C --> D[Row Driver
Multiplexer] D --> E[Column Reader
Sense Amplifier] E --> F[Analog to Digital
Converter] F --> G[Microcontroller
Firmware Engine] G --> H[Debounce
and Validation] H --> I[Scan Code
Lookup Table] I --> J[Host Interface
USB HID or Bluetooth] J --> K[Operating System
Input Pipeline] K --> L[AAC Application
Speech Synthesizer or
Word Processor] L --> M[Action Output
Spoken Word or Text] subgraph sensorLayer [Sensing Layer] C D E end subgraph controlLayer [Control Layer] F G H I end subgraph hostLayer [Host Layer] J K L end ``` **Reading the Diagram:** - The user touches a region on the **overlay sheet** — this is purely a visual aid. - The **sensing matrix** below detects the contact coordinate. - The **row driver** activates one row at a time while the **column reader** checks for the resulting signal. - The **microcontroller** converts the analog reading into a digital (row, column) pair, then **validates** it with debounce. - The validated pair is matched against the **scan code lookup table** (the "key-map"). - The resulting code is **transmitted** to the host over USB or Bluetooth. - The host's **OS input pipeline** routes the event to the active **AAC application**, which performs the final action (e.g., speaks a word or types a sentence). --- ## 4.2 State Machine of a Concept Keyboard Cell This diagram shows the **internal state transitions** of a single cell in the matrix — a frequent KTU viva question. ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Idle Idle --> Sampling: Scan cycle begins Sampling --> Pressed: Touch detected
and validated Sampling --> Idle: No touch or
noise rejected Pressed --> Held: Touch maintained
across cycles Held --> Held: Debounce
confirms Held --> Released: Touch removed
or force falls
below threshold Released --> Idle: Cool-down
period elapsed Released --> Pressed: New touch
within window Idle --> [*]: Power off ``` **Reading the State Diagram:** - A cell is normally **Idle** (no contact). - On every scan cycle, the firmware enters **Sampling** mode for that cell. - If a valid touch is detected and passes debounce, the cell moves to the **Pressed** state. - As long as the touch is maintained, the cell remains in the **Held** state. - When the touch ends, the cell enters the **Released** state for a short cool-down period before returning to **Idle**. - This prevents **phantom re-triggers** from finger tremor or sticky overlays. --- ## 4.3 Comparison Schematic — How a Concept Keyboard Differs from a Membrane Keypad ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph stdKbd [Standard Membrane Keypad] S1[Fixed Printed Legend
ABC or 123] --> S2[Single Carbon Pill
Per Key] S2 --> S3[Fixed Scan Code
Hardwired in PCB] end subgraph cncKbd [Concept Keyboard] C1[Removable Overlay
User Can Change Picture] --> C2[Sparse Matrix Grid
M x N Cells] C2 --> C3[Programmable Key-Map
Stored in Firmware EEPROM] C3 --> C4[Host Re-maps Code
To Any Application Action] end stdKbd -.vs.-> cncKbd ``` **Reading the Comparison:** - A standard membrane keypad is **physically hardwired** — the legend and the function are inseparable. - A concept keyboard **decouples the legend from the function**: the overlay can be changed at will, and the firmware's key-map can be reloaded to assign new meanings to the same physical cells. - This is the foundational concept KTU examiners test when they ask: *"Why is a concept keyboard considered a flexible AAC input device?"* # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, OEBRT614]** Define a **concept keyboard**. List **three** clinical applications of concept keyboards in assistive medical-device practice. **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **concept keyboard** is a flat, touch-sensitive input device in which the active surface is divided into a matrix of discrete, addressable cells, each of which can be dynamically mapped (via a software key-map and a printed overlay) to a specific character, command, symbol, or application-level macro. Unlike a conventional QWERTY keyboard, the user is not constrained to a fixed layout — the meaning of each touch region is **re-defined by the active overlay and its corresponding key-map**. **Three clinical applications:** 1. **Post-operative communication in ICU:** Intubated or tracheostomized patients use a picture-based concept keyboard overlay to indicate pain location, request water, or call the nurse without speaking. 2. **Pediatric autism therapy:** Children with autism spectrum disorder (ASD) use PECS-style concept keyboard overlays to develop symbolic communication skills. 3. **Surgical sterile-field control:** Operating surgeons use a sterile-barrier concept keyboard overlay to issue pre-programmed voice commands (e.g., "More suction," "Irrigation please") without breaking the surgical scrub. 4. *(Optional credit point)* Environmental control for patients with quadriplegia, allowing them to operate lights, fans, bed position, and nurse-call systems through large-cell concept keyboards. *(Marks distribution: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for naming the three applications, 1 mark for correct clinical context — examiner's discretion.)* --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, OEBRT614]** Differentiate between a **resistive-membrane** concept keyboard and a **capacitive (projected)** concept keyboard. State **one advantage** and **one disadvantage** of each. **Model Answer (3 marks):** | **Parameter** | **Resistive Membrane** | **Capacitive (Projected)** | |---|---|---| | **Working principle** | Two ITO-coated films separated by micro-spacer dots touch each other under finger pressure, closing an electrical circuit at the contact point. | An X–Y grid of electrodes measures the change in mutual capacitance caused by the conductive mass of an approaching finger. | | **Activation force** | Requires firm pressure (~ 0.8 – 1.5 N). | Detects light touch (~ 0.1 – 0.3 N). | | **Stylus compatibility** | Works with **any** object (stylus, gloved finger). | Requires a **conductive** object (bare finger or active stylus). | | **Multi-touch** | Generally not supported. | Supported in most modern designs. | | **Durability** | Moderate; film wears with heavy pressure. | High; no mechanical contact. | | **Cost** | Lower. | Higher. | - **Resistive advantage:** Works with gloved hand or stylus — important in clinical/infection-control settings. - **Resistive disadvantage:** Requires firm press; not suitable for users with severe motor weakness. - **Capacitive advantage:** Light touch, multi-touch capable, longer operational life. - **Capacitive disadvantage:** Does not work with standard non-conductive gloves; higher unit cost. *(Marks: 1.5 for correct working principles of both types, 1.5 for advantages/disadvantages — examiner allocates.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Question Choices (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- ### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** With the help of a **neat block diagram**, describe the working of a **resistive-membrane concept keyboard** as an assistive input device. Explain the role of the overlay, the sensing matrix, the controller, and the host software in achieving flexible input. **[7 marks]** **(b)** A concept keyboard is to be designed for **pediatric patients with cerebral palsy**. The active area is $L = 25 \text{ cm}$ and $W = 20 \text{ cm}$. The design specification requires square cells of side length $s = 2.5 \text{ cm}$. Compute the **number of rows and columns** in the sensing matrix, the **total number of addressable cells**, and the **spatial resolution**. Justify why this cell size is appropriate for cerebral-palsy patients. **[7 marks]** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Working of a Resistive-Membrane Concept Keyboard (7 marks):** A resistive-membrane concept keyboard consists of **four functional blocks**: 1. **Overlay Sheet (0.5 mark):** A printed paper or polyester sheet clipped onto the device. It displays user-friendly icons or text corresponding to the cells beneath. The overlay carries **no electrical function** — it is purely a cognitive aid that helps the user identify the meaning of each region. 2. **Sensing Matrix (1.5 marks):** Two thin transparent films coated with Indium-Tin-Oxide (ITO) are arranged orthogonally — one set of parallel horizontal strips (rows) on the top film, one set of parallel vertical strips (columns) on the bottom film. They are separated by a micro-spacer dot pattern (~ 0.1 mm gap). When the user presses a region with a finger, the two films contact at that (row, column) intersection, closing a circuit. The matrix is scanned row-by-row by a multiplexer; the column with a measurable return signal identifies the active cell. 3. **Controller / Firmware (2 marks):** A microcontroller executes a scan loop that: - Selects each row sequentially via the row driver. - Reads the column-sense lines through an analog-to-digital converter. - Applies **software debounce** (typically 3–5 consecutive valid samples). - Looks up the (row, column) coordinate in a **key-map table** stored in EEPROM or flash memory. - Emits the corresponding **scan code** (e.g., a USB HID keycode) over USB or Bluetooth. 4. **Host Software (2 marks):** The host computer (or tablet running an AAC app) receives the scan code and routes it to the active application. Crucially, the host allows the **caregiver or therapist to reprogram the key-map** at any time — this is what makes the device a *concept* keyboard. For example, the same physical cell (3, 2) might mean "Apple" during a mealtime task and "Red" during a color-learning task. The overlay image and the key-map change together. 5. **Output Action (1 mark):** The host application performs the mapped action: types a word, speaks a phrase through a speech synthesizer, opens a file, sends an environmental-control command, or triggers a switch. --- **(b) Numerical Design for Pediatric Cerebral-Palsy Patients (7 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1: Active area calculation.} \\ A = L \times W = 25 \text{ cm} \times 20 \text{ cm} = 500 \text{ cm}^2 \\ \\ \text{Step 2: Number of columns along the length.} \\ N = \frac{L}{s} = \frac{25 \text{ cm}}{2.5 \text{ cm}} = 10 \text{ columns} \\ \\ \text{Step 3: Number of rows along the width.} \\ M = \frac{W}{s} = \frac{20 \text{ cm}}{2.5 \text{ cm}} = 8 \text{ rows} \\ \\ \text{Step 4: Total number of addressable cells.} \\ N_c = M \times N = 8 \times 10 = 80 \text{ cells} \\ \\ \text{Step 5: Spatial resolution.} \\ R_s = \frac{N_c}{A} = \frac{80}{500 \text{ cm}^2} = 0.16 \text{ cells/cm}^2 \end{aligned} $$ **Justification (3 marks of the 7):** - A 2.5 cm square cell is **large enough to be activated by a closed fist, palm, or elbow** — important for children with cerebral palsy who may have involuntary athetoid movements, spasticity, or limited fine-motor control. - The cell is **small enough to provide 80 distinct concepts**, sufficient for a basic communication board (food, drink, family members, feelings, common verbs). - A **low spatial resolution (0.16 cells/cm²)** reduces accidental activation from tremor and is recommended by occupational-therapy guidelines for CP patients with the Gross Motor Function Classification System (GMFCS) levels III–V. --- ### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Compare **concept keyboards** with **standard QWERTY keyboards** as assistive input devices. Discuss **five parameters** of comparison and explain, with justification, why concept keyboards are preferred for users with severe motor impairment. **[7 marks]** **(b)** A 6 × 8 matrix concept keyboard scans each row in $t_{row} = 0.9 \text{ ms}$. The firmware requires $k = 4$ consecutive valid samples for debounce, and the Bluetooth-HID transmission delay is $t_{xmit} = 1.5 \text{ ms}$. Compute the **worst-case response latency** and compare it with the **100 ms human-perception threshold** for "real-time" response. **[7 marks]** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Comparison and Justification (7 marks):** | **Parameter** | **Concept Keyboard** | **Standard QWERTY Keyboard** | **Why This Matters for Motor Impairment** | |---|---|---|---| | **Cell / Key Size** | Large, customizable (1.5 – 5 cm) | Small, fixed (~ 1.4 cm) | Large cells accommodate tremor, athetosis, and limited reach. | | **Activation Force** | Adjustable (0.5 – 2.5 N) | Fixed (~ 0.5 – 0.7 N) | Lower or tunable force is required by weak or spastic muscles. | | **Layout Customization** | Full, via overlay and key-map | None — QWERTY fixed | Allows picture-based or symbol-based layouts that match the user's cognition. | | **Mechanical Travel** | 0 mm (flat surface) | 3 – 4 mm | Reduced travel decreases muscular effort and joint stress. | | **Learning Curve** | Low — picture / icon based | High — requires alphabet literacy | Accessible to pre-literate children and cognitively impaired adults. | | **Hygiene & Infection Control** | Easy to wipe, smooth surface | Difficult — gaps trap pathogens | Critical for hospital and home-care settings. | | **Cost (entry-level)** | Moderate | Low to moderate | Trade-off balanced by clinical need. | **Justification (2 marks embedded):** Concept keyboards are preferred for users with severe motor impairment because they **(i)** remove the need for fine finger movements, **(ii)** support picture/symbol communication accessible to non-literate users, **(iii)** can be reconfigured per task, and **(iv)** allow adjustable activation force — features that a QWERTY keyboard physically cannot offer. --- **(b) Latency Calculation (7 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1: Scan period for one full matrix pass.} \\ T_s = M \times t_{row} \\ T_s = 6 \times 0.9 \text{ ms} \\ T_s = 5.4 \text{ ms} \\ \\ \text{Step 2: Debounce window. The touch event may occur just after a row is sampled,} \\ \text{so the firmware needs } (k-1) \text{ additional full scans to confirm validity.} \\ t_{debounce} = (k - 1) \times T_s \\ t_{debounce} = (4 - 1) \times 5.4 \text{ ms} \\ t_{debounce} = 16.2 \text{ ms} \\ \\ \text{Step 3: Add worst-case scan wait, debounce, and transmission delay.} \\ L_t = T_s + t_{debounce} + t_{xmit} \\ L_t = 5.4 \text{ ms} + 16.2 \text{ ms} + 1.5 \text{ ms} \\ L_t = 23.1 \text{ ms} \\ \\ \text{Step 4: Convert to seconds and compare to the 100 ms threshold.} \\ L_t = 23.1 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 2.31 \times 10^{-2} \text{ s} \\ \\ \text{Step 5: Compare to threshold.} \\ \frac{L_t}{100 \text{ ms}} = \frac{23.1}{100} = 0.231 = 23.1\% \text{ of the threshold} \end{aligned} $$ **Comparison and Conclusion (1 mark embedded):** The worst-case latency of **23.1 ms** is approximately **23% of the 100 ms real-time perception threshold**, meaning the device is perceived as **instantaneous** by the user. This confirms that the design is suitable for real-time AAC applications such as turn-taking conversation. The dominant delay is the **debounce window (16.2 ms = 70% of total latency)**, so reducing the debounce count $k$ from 4 to 2 would cut total latency to roughly 12 ms — though at the cost of increased susceptibility to noise and accidental touches. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > - **Mistake 1:** Writing the definition of a concept keyboard as if it were a *membrane keypad*. A membrane keypad has a fixed key legend; a concept keyboard does not. Examiners deduct **1 to 2 marks** for this conflation. > - **Mistake 2:** Forgetting to convert **ms to seconds** in latency calculations. KTU 2024 Scheme specifies SI units in the final answer; the marker can deduct **0.5 mark** for unit inconsistency. > - **Mistake 3:** In numerical questions, students often assume the touch happens at the *start* of a scan cycle, which underestimates latency. The **worst case** must be assumed: the touch occurs *just after* a row has been scanned, so the firmware must wait one full scan period plus $(k-1)$ additional debounce periods. > - **Mistake 4:** Confusing **spatial resolution** (cells per cm²) with **matrix size** (M × N). Examiners explicitly look for $R_s = N_c / A$ as a distinct quantity. > - **Mistake 5:** Writing the formula $T_s = N \times t_{row}$ instead of $T_s = M \times t_{row}$. The row driver selects one of $M$ rows per step, not $N$ columns. Marks lost: typically **1 mark**. > - **Mistake 6:** Failing to justify the choice of cell size for clinical populations. KTU board examiners now award partial marks for **clinical-reasoning justification** (e.g., why 2.5 cm cells for CP patients). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Concept Keyboards** - **Definition (must memorize verbatim):** A flat, touch-sensitive, programmable input device with an M × N sensing matrix and an interchangeable overlay, where each cell is mapped to a character/command/symbol via a software key-map. - **Four functional blocks:** Overlay → Sensing Matrix → Controller/Firmware → Host Software. - **Two main sensing technologies:** Resistive membrane (pressure-based) and Projected Capacitive (PCT, finger-based). - **Core formulas to memorize:** - $N_c = M \times N$ - $T_s = M \times t_{row}$ - $L_t = T_s + (k-1) \cdot T_s + t_{xmit}$ - $R_s = N_c / A$ - $A_{cell} = 1 / R_s$ - **Cell-size design rule of thumb:** - Severe motor impairment / pediatric → 2.5 – 4 cm cells - Moderate motor impairment / adult → 1.5 – 2.5 cm cells - Fine-motor retraining → 0.8 – 1.2 cm cells - **Three clinical applications (always have 3 ready):** ICU communication, autism PECS therapy, surgical sterile-field voice control. - **Activation force range:** 0.5 N (light) to 2.5 N (firm) — adjustable to user. - **Real-time threshold:** Latency must be **< 100 ms** for perceived "instantaneous" response. Most modern designs achieve **< 30 ms**. - **Why "Concept"?** The same physical cell can mean different things in different contexts — the **overlay + key-map** pair defines the *concept*. - **Comparison shortcut:** Concept keyboards win on **flexibility, accessibility, and clinical customizability**; standard keyboards win on **typing speed and familiarity for able-bodied users**. - **MTBF typical value:** ~ 5 million touches per cell for resistive; > 10 million for capacitive. - **Viva-favorite question:** *"Why is debounce needed in a concept keyboard, and what happens if it is omitted?"* — Answer: Debounce filters mechanical vibration, electrical noise, and finger tremor. Without it, a single intentional touch can register as multiple events ("key bounce"), causing the AAC app to repeat or mis-interpret commands.

topics.title: Eye-Controlled Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT614 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Eye-Controlled Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

An **Eye-Controlled System (ECS)** is a class of Human-Machine Interface (HMI) assistive technology that decodes voluntary ocular (eye) kinematics — such as gaze direction, blink rate, and saccadic motion — into control signals for external electromechanical devices. In the KTU 2024 syllabus context (OEBRT614), these systems are categorised under *augmentative and alternative communication (AAC)* devices engineered for patients with **locked-in syndrome (LIS)**, **amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS)**, **spinal cord injury (SCI)**, **cerebral palsy (CP)**, and severe **muscular dystrophy**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):**
> *Eye-Controlled Systems are non-invasive (or minimally invasive) bioelectric acquisition platforms that exploit the corneo-retinal standing potential of the human eye to derive real-time gaze coordinates, which are subsequently mapped to a finite set of device commands.*

The dominant transduction modalities recognised in the KTU curriculum are:
1. **Electrooculography (EOG)** — measures corneo-retinal potential via skin electrodes.
2. **Infra-Red Oculography (IROG / IR-OG)** — measures reflected IR light from the sclera/iris boundary.
3. **Video-Oculography (VOG)** — uses CMOS cameras + computer-vision algorithms.

> [!NOTE]
> The **corneo-retinal standing potential** is the *Standing Potential Difference (SPD)*, normally **0.4 mV to 1.0 mV** between the electrically positive cornea and the electrically negative retina. This is the fundamental bioelectric source of the EOG signal.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Biological Joystick"

Imagine your eyes as a **living, self-powered joystick** that you have been using since birth. The cornea (front of the eye) acts like a tiny positive electrical pole, while the retina (back) acts like the negative pole — a miniature battery inside your eyeball. Now imagine that whenever you glance **left, right, up, or down**, this tiny battery physically *rotates* inside your head. Skin electrodes stuck around your eyes can "listen" to this rotation the way a stethoscope listens to a heartbeat.

The voltage difference picked up by the electrodes changes predictably with the direction you are looking. A small electronic circuit (signal amplifier) magnifies this whisper-quiet signal (typically **15–200 µV per degree** of eye rotation), and a microcontroller translates it into keystrokes, wheelchair direction, or a mouse-cursor position.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Use Case:** Stephen Hawking, who had ALS, used a similar (infrared-based) cheek-switch and later gaze-tracking system to author his books and deliver lectures. Modern systems like *Tobii Dynavox* and *EyeGaze Edge* allow patients to type up to **60 words per minute** using only their eyes.

### 1.3 Classification Hierarchy (KTU 2024 Taxonomy)

| Modality | Physical Principle | Sampling Rate | Spatial Accuracy | Invasiveness |
|----------|-------------------|---------------|------------------|--------------|
| **EOG** | Bioelectric (corneo-retinal SPD) | 100 – 1000 Hz | 1°–2° | Non-invasive (surface electrodes) |
| **IROG** | Optical (IR reflectance) | 50 – 500 Hz | 0.5°–1° | Non-invasive (eye-glasses mounted) |
| **VOG** | Optical (camera + CV) | 30 – 120 Hz | 0.25°–0.5° | Non-invasive (remote camera) |
| **Scleral Search Coil** | Electromagnetic induction | 1000+ Hz | 0.1° | Invasive (contact lens coil) |

> [!TIP]
> For KTU exams, **EOG** is the most-tested modality because it has the cleanest derivable equations, deterministic electrode placement, and the lowest device cost. Focus your revision there.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Dipole Model of the Human Eye

The KTU-preferred theoretical framework treats the eyeball as an **electric dipole** embedded in a conductive medium (the peri-orbital tissue, with resistivity ρ ≈ **200–500 Ω·cm**).

- The **cornea** is modelled as the **positive pole** (anode).
- The **retina** is modelled as the **negative pole** (cathode).
- The **dipole moment vector** $\vec{p}$ is aligned along the visual axis, with magnitude:

$$\vec{p} = Q \cdot \vec{d}$$

where $Q$ is the equivalent charge and $\vec{d}$ is the dipole arm length (approximately the eye's axial length, ~**24 mm**).

> [!NOTE]
> The Standing Potential Difference is maintained by the **retinal pigment epithelium (RPE)** which actively pumps ions. It is therefore NOT generated by neural activity — it is a *steady DC* (or slowly varying DC) potential present in both light and dark adapted states.

### 2.2 Derivation of the EOG Signal — Physical Setup

For a horizontal eye movement, electrodes are placed at the **lateral canthi** (outer corners) of both eyes. The voltage picked up between the *right* ($V_R$) and *left* ($V_L$) electrodes is:

$$V_{EOG,h} = V_R - V_L$$

When the eye rotates by an angle $\theta$ from the primary (straight-ahead) position, the projection of the dipole onto the horizontal electrode axis changes. The first-order approximation yields:

$$V_{EOG,h}(\theta) \approx k_h \cdot \sin(\theta)$$

where $k_h$ is a subject-specific gain constant in the range of **5–20 µV/degree** (or ~**200–700 µV** total for ±30° gaze).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For small angles ($|\theta| < 20°$), the small-angle approximation holds: $\sin(\theta) \approx \theta$ (in radians). This is what makes the EOG signal *linearly* proportional to gaze angle within the central visual field — the region we care about for assistive HMI.

### 2.3 Equivalent-Circuit Model (KTU Favourite)

The skin–electrode interface is modelled as a **parallel RC network** in series with a voltage source (the dipole projection) and a load resistance $R_L$ (input impedance of the amplifier, ideally > **10 MΩ** to avoid loading errors).

The transfer function from skin potential to amplifier output is:

$$H(s) = \frac{R_L}{R_L + R_s + \dfrac{1}{sC_s}}$$

where $R_s$ is the skin-electrode contact resistance (typically **2–50 kΩ** with Ag/AgCl wet electrodes) and $C_s$ is the polarisation capacitance (**0.1–10 µF**).

### 2.4 Signal Characteristics

| Parameter | Value | KTU Significance |
|-----------|-------|------------------|
| **Signal amplitude** | 5 – 20 µV/° (horizontal), 10 – 30 µV/° (vertical) | Determines amplifier gain required |
| **DC offset** | 0.2 – 1.0 mV (eye-specific SPD) | Must be removed by HPF |
| **Bandwidth** | DC to ~30 Hz (saccades dominant) | Determines LPF cutoff |
| **Noise sources** | EMG (1–10 mV), 50 Hz line, baseline drift | Sets SNR and filter design |
| **Skin–electrode impedance** | < 5 kΩ (Ag/AgCl, gel-prepped) | Must be < 1/100 of amplifier input Z |
| **ADC resolution** | ≥ 12-bit (medical) / 16-bit (research) | Dynamic range requirement |

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Formula Sheet — Eye-Controlled Systems (Module 2)**

| # | Equation | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | $V_{EOG} = k \cdot \sin(\theta)$ | EOG voltage vs. gaze angle (exact) | V vs. rad |
| 2 | $V_{EOG} \approx k \cdot \theta$ | Small-angle linearisation ($\theta$ in rad) | V vs. rad |
| 3 | $\theta_{deg} = \dfrac{V_{EOG}}{k}$ | Gaze-angle estimation | degrees |
| 4 | $G_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}}\right)$ | Amplifier voltage gain (dB) | dB |
| 5 | $f_{c,HPF} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_1 C_1}$ | High-pass cutoff (drift removal) | Hz |
| 6 | $f_{c,LPF} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_2 C_2}$ | Low-pass cutoff (EMG/line noise) | Hz |
| 7 | $SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)$ | Signal-to-noise ratio | dB |
| 8 | $CMRR = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_{d}}{A_{cm}}\right)$ | Common-mode rejection ratio | dB |
| 9 | $V_{n,total} = \sqrt{V_{n,1}^{2} + V_{n,2}^{2} + V_{n,3}^{2}}$ | RMS noise summation (uncorrelated) | V |
| 10 | $\tau = R \cdot C$ | Skin–electrode RC time constant | s |

> [!WARNING]
> **No vertical pipes `\|` inside table cells.** The notation $\vert x \vert$ is rendered as $\vert x \vert$ using the $\vert$ LaTeX command. In your KTU answer scripts, prefer the absolute value bar drawn manually.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Eye-controlled systems have a tri-modal industrial footprint in the KTU Module-2 context:
1. **Medical Rehabilitation** — wheelchair navigation, prosthetic limb selection menus, spelling boards.
2. **Communication Aids** — augmentative typing interfaces (Tobii, EyeGaze, LC Technologies).
3. **Diagnostic Tool** — vestibular and oculomotor disorder screening (nystagmus detection).
4. **Consumer Electronics** — drowsiness detection in drivers, foveated rendering in VR headsets, smartphone face-unlock.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — EOG Voltage from the Dipole Projection

**Given:**
- Eye modelled as a dipole of moment $\vec{p}$ located at the eye's centre.
- Two electrodes placed symmetrically at the lateral canthi, separated by horizontal distance $2a$ (typically $2a \approx$ **6–8 cm**).
- The dipole axis makes an angle $\theta$ with the primary (forward) gaze direction.

**Step 1 — Write the dipole potential at an arbitrary field point.**

The electric potential of a dipole at a field point with position vector $\vec{r}$ (relative to the dipole centre) is:

$$V(\vec{r}) = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \dfrac{\vec{p} \cdot \hat{r}}{r^{2}}$$

**Step 2 — Specialise to the geometry of the two canthal electrodes.**

Let the right electrode be at $\vec{r}_R = (a, 0, 0)$ and the left electrode at $\vec{r}_L = (-a, 0, 0)$. The dipole moment is $\vec{p} = p(\cos\theta, \sin\theta, 0)$ for a horizontal eye rotation by $\theta$.

**Step 3 — Compute the potential at each electrode.**

The right electrode sees a projection along the +x axis; the left sees a projection along the −x axis:

$$V_R = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \dfrac{p \cos\theta}{a^{2}}$$

$$V_L = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \dfrac{p (-\cos\theta)}{a^{2}} = -\dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \dfrac{p \cos\theta}{a^{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Take the differential output.**

$$V_{EOG,h} = V_R - V_L = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \dfrac{2p \cos\theta}{a^{2}}$$

**Step 5 — Combine constants and recognise the result.**

Define the subject-specific gain $K_h = \dfrac{2p}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 a^{2}}$. Then:

$$V_{EOG,h}(\theta) = K_h \cdot \cos(\theta)$$

> [!NOTE]
> Note the **cosine** appears (not sine) because the dipole axis and the electrode line are *perpendicular* when the eye is in primary gaze. For vertical channels, the geometry flips the trigonometric function back to $\sin$.

**Step 6 — Apply small-angle approximation.**

For $\vert\theta\vert \le 20°$ (≈ 0.349 rad), $\cos(\theta) \approx 1 - \theta^{2}/2$. The near-constant DC term is removed by the HPF, and the residual quadratic term is negligible. Within ±10°, the system behaves as a *constant-amplitude* source whose **direction encodes** the gaze angle.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Gaze-Angle Estimation from Measured Voltage

**Given:** Measured $V_{EOG}$ and the calibrated gain $K_h$.

**Step 1 — Invert the trigonometric relation.**

$$\cos(\theta) = \dfrac{V_{EOG}}{K_h}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $\theta$ in degrees.**

$$\theta = \arccos\!\left(\dfrac{V_{EOG}}{K_h}\right) \quad [\text{radians}]$$

$$\theta_{deg} = \dfrac{180}{\pi} \arccos\!\left(\dfrac{V_{EOG}}{K_h}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Linearise for the central field.**

For small $\theta$ (and with the DC offset removed by the HPF so that $V_{EOG}$ represents the *change* in potential):

$$\theta_{deg} \approx \dfrac{V_{EOG}}{K_h} \cdot \dfrac{180}{\pi}$$

**Numerical example (KTU-style):**
Let $K_h = 600\ \mu V$ per $\pm 30°$ full scale. Suppose the measured signal is $V_{EOG} = 200\ \mu V$.

$$\theta = \dfrac{200}{600} \times 30° = 10° \quad \text{to the right}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Required Amplifier Gain

**Given:** EOG signal $V_{in} = 50\ \mu V$ (typical for 5° gaze shift), ADC full-scale $V_{FS} = 5\ V$, ADC resolution $N = 12$ bits.

**Step 1 — Compute the LSB of the ADC.**

$$V_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^{N}} = \dfrac{5}{4096} \approx 1.22\ \text{mV}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the required peak-to-peak gain.**

To map the ±200 µV EOG to roughly 80% of full scale (~±2 V):

$$A_v = \dfrac{2.0\ \text{V}}{200\ \mu V} = 10{,}000 \quad (\text{i.e., } 80\ \text{dB})$$

**Step 3 — Distribute the gain across stages.**

- **Instrumentation pre-amp (AD620 / INA128):** $A_{v1} = 100$ (40 dB)
- **Second-stage active LPF + gain:** $A_{v2} = 100$ (40 dB)
- **Total:** $A_{v,tot} = 10{,}000$ (80 dB) ✔

**Step 4 — Choose the HPF cutoff to suppress drift.**

A typical EOG system uses $f_{c,HPF} = 0.05\ \text{Hz}$ to eliminate electrode DC offset without distorting slow saccades. With $C_1 = 1\ \mu F$:

$$R_1 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f_c C_1} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (0.05)(1\times 10^{-6})} \approx 3.18\ \text{M}\Omega$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Two-Dimensional Gaze Reconstruction (4-Electrode Montage)

For a complete 2-D gaze tracker, four electrodes are used: top, bottom, left, right. The vertical channel:

$$V_{EOG,v} = V_T - V_B = K_v \cdot \sin(\phi)$$

where $\phi$ is the vertical eye angle (positive = looking up). The horizontal channel:

$$V_{EOG,h} = V_R - V_L = K_h \cdot \cos(\theta)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **gaze vector** on the screen plane is then:

$$\vec{g} = (x, y) = \left( K_h \cos\theta, \; K_v \sin\phi \right)$$

This vector is mapped to a screen coordinate via a pre-computed **calibration matrix** $\mathbf{M}$ (typically 9-parameter affine transform) determined by asking the user to look at 5–9 known on-screen targets.

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Blink-Detection Algorithm (Dwell-Free Control)

**Step 1 — Define the blink energy detector.**

A blink produces a large transient in $V_{EOG,v}$ (cornea moves towards the upper electrode, away from the lower one). The blink energy over a sliding window is:

$$E_{blink}(t) = \int_{t-W}^{t} V_{EOG,v}^{2}(\tau)\, d\tau$$

**Step 2 — Threshold decision.**

A blink is declared if:

$$E_{blink}(t) > E_{th} \quad \text{AND} \quad \dfrac{dV_{EOG,v}}{dt} > S_{th}$$

**Step 3 — Classify voluntary vs. involuntary blinks.**

Voluntary (intentional) blinks last **150–400 ms** with steep slope, while involuntary blinks last **100–150 ms** with gentler slope. A **width-and-slope classifier** separates the two with > 95 % accuracy in published assistive-device studies.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — EOG-Based Eye-Controlled Wheelchair

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Eye Dipole Source 0.4 to 1.0 mV SPD"] --> B["Skin Electrodes AgAgCl x4"]
    B --> C["INA128 Instrumentation Preamp Gain 100 CMRR 110 dB"]
    C --> D["Active High Pass Filter f0 0.05 Hz DC Drift Removal"]
    D --> E["Active Low Pass Filter f0 30 Hz EMG and Line Noise"]
    E --> F["Notch Filter 50 Hz Notch"]
    F --> G["ADS1115 16 bit ADC I2C"]
    G --> H["STM32 Microcontroller Feature Extraction"]
```
