```mermaid
    H --> I{"Decision Logic Gaze Direction Classification"}
    I --> J["Left Command"]
    I --> K["Right Command"]
    I --> L["Forward Command"]
    I --> M["Stop Command"]
    J --> N["Motor Driver BTS7960 PWM"]
    K --> N
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O["DC Geared Motors 24 V 250 W"]
    O --> P["Wheelchair Motion"]
    H --> Q["LCD Display Status Feedback"]
    H --> R["Buzzer Audible Alert"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Signal Processing Pipeline (Generic ECS)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Raw EOG Signal Acquisition"] --> S2["Preprocessing Bandpass 0.05 to 30 Hz"]
    S2 --> S3["Notch Filter 50 Hz Powerline"]
    S3 --> S4["Feature Extraction RMS Mean Slope"]
    S4 --> S5["Classification SVM or Threshold based"]
    S5 --> S6a["Cursor Control"]
    S5 --> S6b["Spelling Board Selection"]
    S5 --> S6c["Wheelchair Navigation"]
    S5 --> S6d["Appliance ON OFF Switch"]
    S1 -. DC Offset .-> S7["Baseline Tracking and Subtraction"]
    S7 --> S2
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — 2-D Gaze Reconstruction Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E1["Electrode Top"] --> M1["INA Channel V"]
    E2["Electrode Bottom"] --> M1
    E3["Electrode Right"] --> M2["INA Channel H"]
    E4["Electrode Left"] --> M2
    M1 --> AD1["ADC CH1 Vertical"]
    M2 --> AD2["ADC CH2 Horizontal"]
    AD1 --> DSP["DSP or MCU Calibration Matrix M"]
    AD2 --> DSP
    DSP --> SC["Screen Gaze Coordinate xy"]
    SC --> DW["Dwell Timer 800 ms"]
    DW --> SEL["Click Selection"]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Flowchart — Calibration Procedure (Standard 9-Point)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C0["System Power ON"] --> C1["Display 9 Calibration Targets Sequentially"]
    C1 --> C2["User Fixates Each Target for 1.5 s"]
    C2 --> C3["Record EOG Voltages Vx Vy"]
    C3 --> C4{"All 9 Targets Sampled"}
    C4 -->|No| C1
    C4 -->|Yes| C5["Compute Affine Matrix M via Least Squares"]
    C5 --> C6["Validation Phase 5 Random Targets"]
    C6 --> C7{"Mean Error below 1.5 deg"}
    C7 -->|No| C1
    C7 -->|Yes| C8["Save Calibration to EEPROM"]
    C8 --> C9["System Ready for Operation"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid:** Mermaid renders natively in most modern markdown viewers (GitHub, VS Code, Obsidian, Notion). The diagrams above visually map the **physiological source → electrodes → analog front end → digital processing → actuation** chain that examiners love to draw in viva voce.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

**Q1. Define Electrooculography (EOG) and state the bioelectric origin of the EOG signal. Mention the typical amplitude of the EOG signal in microvolts.**
*(Module 2 — CO2 — Bloom Level: Remember)*

**Model Answer:**
Electrooculography (EOG) is a non-invasive technique used to record the bioelectric potential changes that occur due to eye movements. The signal originates from the **standing potential difference (SPD)** of the eye, which is maintained by the retinal pigment epithelium (RPE). The cornea is electrically positive (≈ **+0.4 to +1.0 mV**) relative to the retina. When the eye rotates, this dipole orientation changes, producing a measurable voltage variation at surface electrodes placed around the orbit. The typical EOG signal amplitude is in the range of **5–20 µV per degree** of eye rotation, with a total useful signal of **0.1–1.0 mV** for ±30° of gaze. (3 marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2. List any three transduction techniques used in eye-controlled systems and compare their accuracy.**
*(Module 2 — CO2 — Bloom Level: Understand)*

**Model Answer:**
The three primary techniques are:

1. **Electrooculography (EOG):** Uses skin electrodes to record corneo-retinal SPD. Accuracy: **1°–2°**; non-invasive, low cost, robust to lighting.
2. **Infra-Red Oculography (IROG):** Uses IR LEDs and photodetectors mounted on glasses. Accuracy: **0.5°–1°**; sensitive to head movement.
3. **Video-Oculography (VOG):** Uses cameras and computer-vision to track pupil/iris. Accuracy: **0.25°–0.5°**; highest accuracy but computationally heavy and requires good lighting.

(3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Scheme]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an EOG-based eye-controlled wheelchair system. Discuss the role of the instrumentation amplifier, filters, and the microcontroller.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the relationship between the EOG voltage and the gaze angle using the dipole model of the eye. Show the small-angle linearisation and explain its significance for assistive applications.

#### **Model Solution to Q.A (a):**

An EOG-based eye-controlled wheelchair acquires the user's gaze direction via four surface electrodes (top, bottom, left, right canthi) connected to the corneo-retinal dipole.

**Block Diagram:** (Draw the Mermaid-style architecture from Section 4.1 in the answer book.)

**Stage 1 — Instrumentation Amplifier (INA128 / AD620):**
- Provides high **differential gain** (typically 100×) and high **CMRR (> 100 dB)** to reject 50 Hz mains interference that appears equally on both electrode leads.
- Provides high input impedance (> **10 GΩ**) to avoid loading the high-impedance skin–electrode interface.
- Conversion of the bipolar (±200 µV) EOG signal to a single-ended signal referenced to analog ground.

**Stage 2 — Active Filters:**
- **High-Pass Filter (HPF) at 0.05 Hz:** Removes the large DC offset from the standing potential and any slow baseline drift due to electrode polarisation.
- **Low-Pass Filter (LPF) at 30 Hz:** Removes high-frequency EMG contamination from facial muscles and broadband noise.
- **50 Hz Notch Filter:** Specifically attenuates powerline interference.

**Stage 3 — ADC and Microcontroller:**
- A 12-bit or 16-bit ADC digitises the filtered EOG.
- The microcontroller (e.g., STM32 / Arduino Mega) runs a **calibration algorithm** (least-squares fit to known targets) to convert voltages to gaze angles.
- A **decision logic** maps the gaze direction to one of four discrete commands: LEFT, RIGHT, FORWARD, STOP.
- The command is sent via a motor-driver IC (e.g., BTS7960, L298N) to drive two 24 V DC geared motors.
- A **dwell time** (e.g., 1 second) and **blink-detection** algorithm prevent accidental commands.

(7 marks distributed across diagram = 2, amplifier/filter = 3, MCU/decision = 2)

#### **Model Solution to Q.A (b):**

The eye is modelled as a dipole of moment $\vec{p}$ with the positive pole (cornea) and the negative pole (retina). Two electrodes are placed at the lateral canthi at a horizontal separation of $2a$ from the eye's centre.

The dipole moment vector makes an angle $\theta$ with the forward (primary) gaze direction. The projection of $\vec{p}$ along the horizontal axis is $p \cos\theta$. The potential at the right electrode is:

$$V_R = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \dfrac{p \cos\theta}{a^{2}}$$

and at the left electrode:

$$V_L = -\dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \dfrac{p \cos\theta}{a^{2}}$$

The differential EOG voltage is:

$$V_{EOG,h} = V_R - V_L = \dfrac{2p \cos\theta}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 a^{2}} = K_h \cos\theta$$

For small gaze angles ($\vert\theta\vert \le 20°$), $\cos\theta \approx 1$, so the residual term is approximately constant and is rejected by the HPF. After DC removal, the signal is:

$$V_{EOG,h}(\theta) \approx K_h \cdot 1 \quad \text{(DC only)}$$

For a *change* in gaze from $\theta_1$ to $\theta_2$:

$$\Delta V_{EOG,h} = K_h \left[\cos\theta_2 - \cos\theta_1\right] \approx K_h \cdot (\theta_1 - \theta_2) \quad \text{for small angles}$$

This **linear** mapping allows the microcontroller to estimate gaze angle directly from voltage without inverse-trigonometric computation, enabling real-time control of the wheelchair with minimal processing latency. (7 marks)

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working of an Infra-Red Oculography (IROG) based eye-tracking system. Compare its performance with the EOG system.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the signal processing chain for a video-oculography (VOG) system. Include the steps of pupil detection, glint tracking, and gaze estimation.

*(Full 7-mark model answers are expected to include block diagrams, comparison tables, and at least one numerical example. Refer to the KTU Formula Sheet in Section 2.5 for parameter values.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Traps:**
> 1. **Confusing EOG and EEG:** Students often mix up Electrooculography with Electroencephalography. EOG is a *DC* signal driven by the RPE, NOT neural activity. EEG records brain activity and is AC-dominated.
> 2. **Wrong trigonometric function:** The lateral canthus sees a $\cos\theta$ relationship (because the dipole axis is *perpendicular* to the inter-electrode axis in primary gaze). Many students write $V_{EOG} = K \sin\theta$ — this is **wrong** for horizontal channels.
> 3. **Forgetting HPF:** The DC component of the SPD (0.4–1.0 mV) is much larger than the gaze-related AC signal (50–200 µV). Without a proper HPF, the amplifier will saturate. KTU examiners check for this.
> 4. **Wrong units in gain calculation:** Gain is dimensionless; in dB it is $20 \log_{10}(A_v)$, NOT $10 \log_{10}$. Many students use the power formula by mistake.
> 5. **Ignoring calibration:** A "gaze-to-screen" mapping is *mandatory* in any VOG system. A 9-point affine calibration is the KTU-preferred answer.
> 6. **Not specifying the electrode type:** Always mention **Ag/AgCl pre-gelled** electrodes for EOG in your answers. The examiner is trained to look for this.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Eye-Controlled System (ECS):** An HMI that decodes eye movements into device commands for assistive applications.
- **Primary bioelectric source:** Corneo-retinal Standing Potential Difference (**0.4–1.0 mV**), generated by the RPE.
- **Three main modalities:** EOG (bioelectric), IROG (IR reflectance), VOG (camera + CV).
- **EOG signal amplitude:** **5–20 µV/°** (horizontal), **10–30 µV/°** (vertical).
- **Dipole model:** Eye ≈ dipole of moment $\vec{p}$; EOG voltage is the projection of $\vec{p}$ onto the electrode axis.
- **Key equation:** $V_{EOG,h} = K_h \cos\theta$ (exact) and $V_{EOG,h} \approx K_h \cdot \theta$ (small-angle).
- **Analog front-end:** Instrumentation amp (gain ~100, **CMRR > 100 dB**) → HPF (~0.05 Hz) → LPF (~30 Hz) → 50 Hz notch → ADC (≥ 12-bit).
- **Electrode placement:** 4-electrode montage (top, bottom, left, right) for 2-D gaze; Ag/AgCl wet electrodes preferred.
- **Calibration:** 5–9 point affine transform to map voltage space to screen coordinate space.
- **Blink detection:** RMS energy + slope classifier distinguishes voluntary (> 150 ms) from involuntary (~100 ms) blinks.
- **Clinical applications:** Wheelchair control, AAC typing, prosthetic selection, environmental control (TV, lights, call-bell).
- **Standards:** IEC 60601-1 (medical electrical equipment safety), ISO 9241 (ergonomics) — mention if KTU asks about regulatory aspects.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "EOG SPA":**
> **E**lectrodes (Ag/AgCl, 4-cantha) → **O**culometric signal (5–20 µV/°) → **G**aze-Command (LEFT/RIGHT/UP/DOWN/STOP).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tracking of Body Features
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT614 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Tracking of Body Features

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Tracking of Body Features** is a sub-discipline of biomedical engineering, computer vision, and human–computer interaction (HCI) that involves the real-time or near-real-time detection, identification, localization, and continuous monitoring of specific anatomical landmarks, joints, gestures, or motion patterns of the human body using an array of sensors, computational algorithms, and signal-processing techniques.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme – OEBRT614 (Assistive Medical Devices)**, body feature tracking is defined as:

> *"The systematic acquisition, processing, and interpretation of spatial, temporal, and kinematic data corresponding to predefined human body landmarks—such as the eyes, head, hands, fingers, limbs, torso, and gait cycle—using sensor-based or vision-based modalities, with the explicit goal of enabling, restoring, or augmenting physiological function in individuals with sensory, motor, or cognitive impairments."*

The standard taxonomy of body features tracked in assistive technology includes:

| Body Feature | Primary Use Case in Assistive Devices |
|---|---|
| **Eye (gaze / pupil / iris)** | Communication boards, wheelchair navigation |
| **Head (pose / orientation)** | Cursor control, sip-and-puff alternatives |
| **Hand & Fingers (skeletal joints)** | Prosthetic hand control, sign language interpretation |
| **Arm & Limbs (joint angles, kinematics)** | Rehabilitation robots, exoskeletons |
| **Torso / Posture (spine, shoulder)** | Fall detection, posture correction |
| **Gait (heel-strike, stride, cadence)** | Smart walkers, FES (Functional Electrical Stimulation) |
| **Face (facial Action Units – AU)** | Emotion-aware AAC devices |
| **Tongue / Oral cavity** | Tongue-drive wheelchairs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Body feature tracking forms the *perceptual front-end* of nearly every modern assistive system. Without robust tracking, downstream command inference (e.g., translating a hand gesture into a wheelchair command) is impossible. Therefore, tracking accuracy, latency, and robustness directly determine the **usability grade** of any assistive medical device.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are a coach standing at the edge of a swimming pool, watching a swimmer perform the butterfly stroke. You are not watching *the whole swimmer as a blob*; instead, your eyes automatically lock onto:
- The **angle of the elbows** (are they sweeping correctly?),
- The **rhythm of the kick** (is the dolphin kick synchronized?),
- The **head position** (is it bobbing at the right breath interval?).

Your brain is essentially *tracking discrete body features* of the swimmer in real time and inferring whether the technique is correct.

Now translate that same idea to a machine:
- A **camera** is the coach's "eyes."
- A **tracking algorithm** is the coach's "brain."
- The **body landmarks** (elbow, knee, eye, fingertip) are the "features" being tracked.
- The **decision made** (e.g., "stroke is incorrect, fire vibratory feedback") is the *assistive action*.

So, in plain English:

> **"Body feature tracking = teaching a machine to watch a specific part of the human body the way a sports coach watches an athlete — continuously, precisely, and meaningfully — so that the machine can help a disabled person do something they otherwise could not."**

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following **standardized performance metrics** are universally used to evaluate any body-tracking system. They are routinely tested in KTU lab viva and ESE (End Semester Examination):

- **Tracking Accuracy (Spatial Error):** typically **1–5 mm** for skeletal tracking and **0.5°–2°** for gaze/head pose.
- **Sampling Rate (Temporal Resolution):** **30 Hz to 240 Hz** (the latter for high-speed gait analysis).
- **Latency (End-to-End Delay):** ideally **< 100 ms** for real-time control, **< 20 ms** for closed-loop prosthetic control.
- **Degrees of Freedom (DoF):** a rigid body in 3D space has **6 DoF** (3 translational + 3 rotational).
- **Standard Anatomical Frame:** defined by the **ISB (International Society of Biomechanics)** recommendation using bony landmarks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why These Numbers Matter:** A latency of even 200 ms makes a prosthetic hand feel "sluggish and disconnected" to the user (the well-known *sense of agency* problem). Hence the **< 100 ms** target is non-negotiable in KTU-grade assistive device design.

### 1.4 Classification of Body Feature Tracking Systems

Body tracking systems are broadly classified along **three orthogonal axes**:

1. **By Sensor Modality:**
   - **Vision-based (optical)** — RGB cameras, depth sensors (e.g., Kinect, Intel RealSense), stereo rigs, event cameras, LiDAR.
   - **Inertial-based (non-optical)** — IMUs (Inertial Measurement Units) containing accelerometers, gyroscopes, magnetometers.
   - **Bioelectric-based** — EMG (electromyography), EEG (electroencephalography), EOG (electrooculography), ECG.
   - **Hybrid (sensor fusion)** — combines two or more modalities (e.g., vision + IMU).

2. **By Dimensionality:**
   - **2D tracking** (image plane) — used in mouse-cursor control, simple webcams.
   - **3D tracking** (world coordinates) — used in prosthetic control, robotic rehabilitation.

3. **By Application Domain:**
   - **Rehabilitation tracking** — stroke recovery, gait retraining.
   - **Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC)** — eye-gaze typing.
   - **Mobility control** — wheelchair command inference.
   - **Prosthetic command generation** — pattern recognition from EMG.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Pipeline of a Body Feature Tracker

Every body feature tracking system — whether it is a $5 webcam gaze tracker or a $50,000 Vicon motion-capture rig — funnels data through the same **four-stage pipeline**. KTU examiners frequently test this pipeline; memorizing it is high-yield.

**Stage 1 — Sensing (Data Acquisition):**
- A sensor (camera, IMU, EMG electrode) converts a physical quantity (light, acceleration, voltage) into an electrical signal.
- The signal is sampled at frequency $f_s$ (Hz) and digitized with $b$ bits of resolution.

**Stage 2 — Preprocessing (Noise Reduction & Normalization):**
- Filtering (low-pass, Kalman, median) removes sensor noise.
- Color-space conversion (RGB → grayscale or HSV) reduces computational load.
- Background subtraction isolates the moving subject.

**Stage 3 — Feature Detection & Tracking (The Core Stage):**
- The algorithm locates the body feature in each frame.
- A **detector** finds the feature in frame $t$.
- A **tracker** follows it from frame $t$ to frame $t+1$.

**Stage 4 — Interpretation & Actuation:**
- The tracked coordinates are mapped to a command (e.g., "move cursor to (x, y)" or "close prosthetic gripper").
- Feedback is sent to the user via haptics, audio, or visual display.

### 2.2 Mathematical Foundation of Feature Tracking

The core mathematical problem of body feature tracking can be formalized as follows:

Given an image (or signal) sequence $I_t$ indexed by discrete time $t \in \{1, 2, \dots, T\}$, the goal is to estimate the state vector $\mathbf{x}_t$ representing the location, orientation, and scale of the body feature:

$$\mathbf{x}_t = \begin{bmatrix} u_t \\ v_t \\ s_t \\ \theta_t \end{bmatrix}$$

where:
- $u_t, v_t$ = pixel coordinates (or 3D world coordinates for depth cameras).
- $s_t$ = scale (size of the feature window).
- $\theta_t$ = orientation angle (in radians).

**Two fundamental approaches** exist to estimate $\mathbf{x}_t$:

**Approach A — Generative (Model-Based) Tracking:**
The tracker has an internal model $M(\mathbf{x}_t)$ of the body feature. It searches for $\mathbf{x}_t$ that minimizes the difference between the model and the actual image region:

$$\mathbf{x}_t^{*} = \arg\min_{\mathbf{x}_t} \; \mathcal{L}\big(M(\mathbf{x}_t), \; I_t(\mathbf{x}_t)\big)$$

A common loss function is the **Sum of Squared Differences (SSD):**

$$\mathcal{L}_{SSD} = \sum_{(i,j) \in W} \big[ I_t(i,j) - M(\mathbf{x}_t, i, j) \big]^2$$

**Approach B — Discriminative (Data-Driven) Tracking:**
A classifier (e.g., CNN, Random Forest) is trained to directly predict $\mathbf{x}_t$ from image patches. Modern systems like **MediaPipe**, **OpenPose**, and **BlazePose** use this paradigm.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table is the **master cheat-sheet** for numerical problems. **Memorize every row.**

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units | Application / Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pinhole Camera Projection | $u = f \cdot \frac{X}{Z}, \quad v = f \cdot \frac{Y}{Z}$ | pixels (u, v), mm (X, Y, Z, f) | Maps 3D world point → 2D image point |
| 2 | Camera Intrinsic Matrix | $K = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & 0 & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | pixels | Calibration of any vision-based tracker |
| 3 | Euclidean Distance (tracking error) | $e = \sqrt{(u_p - u_g)^2 + (v_p - v_g)^2}$ | pixels (or mm after calibration) | Measures accuracy of tracked landmark vs ground truth |
| 4 | Optical Flow Constraint (Lucas–Kanade) | $I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0$ | dimensionless (gradients in image) | 2D motion estimation between consecutive frames |
| 5 | Kalman Filter Prediction | $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{t \vert t-1} = A \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{t-1 \vert t-1} + B \mathbf{u}_t$ | state-vector units | Fusing IMU + vision for smooth tracking |
| 6 | Kalman Filter Update | $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{t \vert t} = \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{t \vert t-1} + K_t ( \mathbf{z}_t - H \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{t \vert t-1} )$ | state-vector units | Correcting predicted state with sensor measurement |
| 7 | Kalman Gain | $K_t = P_{t \vert t-1} H^T (H P_{t \vert t-1} H^T + R)^{-1}$ | dimensionless | Weighting between prediction and measurement |
| 8 | Stride Length (gait) | $L = N \cdot h \cdot k$ | meters | Estimating distance per gait cycle (N = steps, h = height, k ≈ 0.4) |
| 9 | Gait Cadence | $C = \frac{60}{T_{step}}$ | steps/min | Frequency of foot strikes in gait tracking |
| 10 | Joint Angle from IMU | $\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{a_y}{a_z}\right)$ | degrees or radians | Tilt angle of a limb segment from accelerometer |
| 11 | Sampling Theorem Bound | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Hz | Minimum sampling rate to avoid aliasing |
| 12 | Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) | $RMSE = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} e_i^2}$ | same as $e$ | Gold-standard accuracy metric for trackers |
| 13 | EMG → Force (linear envelope) | $F(t) = a \cdot \text{RMS}(EMG) + b$ | Newtons (N) | Maps muscle activity to prosthetic grip force |
| 14 | Pupil-Corneal Reflection (gaze) | $g = \alpha (p - r)$ | screen coordinates (pixels) | Vector from glint to pupil center ≈ gaze direction |
| 15 | Skeletal Joint Rotation (Euler) | $R = R_z(\gamma) \cdot R_y(\beta) \cdot R_x(\alpha)$ | unitless rotation matrix | 3D pose of a limb segment |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Table Notation:** The vertical bars in formulas like $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{t \vert t-1}$ use `\vert` (or `\mid`) instead of `\|` to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax. When you write these in your exam answer sheet in LaTeX, you may freely use the standard `\|` notation.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Tracking of body features is **the central nervous system** of modern assistive medical devices. Without it, there can be no intelligent prosthetic, no autonomous wheelchair, no AAC device, no fall-prevention system. Concrete applications include:

- **Smart Prosthetic Limbs:** EMG sensors detect the user's intended muscle contraction pattern; the tracker decodes this into a specific hand grasp (e.g., *pinch*, *lateral*, *power*). Companies like *COVVI*, *Össur*, and *Open Bionics* ship such systems commercially.
- **Eye-Gaze AAC Devices:** Devices like *Tobii Dynavox* and *EyeGaze Edge* track the pupil + corneal reflection (glint) at **120 Hz** to allow a person with ALS to type up to 60 words per minute using only their eyes.
- **Rehabilitation Robots:** Devices like *Lokomat* and *Armeo Power* track joint angles of stroke patients to provide **assistance-as-needed** control.
- **Wheelchair Control via Head Tracking:** Wheelchairs from *Permobil* and *Sunrise Medical* use head-pose tracking as a hands-free navigation method for users with quadriplegia.
- **Fall Detection in Elderly Care:** Wearable IMU-based trackers (e.g., *Apple Watch*, *Philips Lifeline*) use trunk-tilt and acceleration thresholds to detect falls and auto-alert caregivers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation 1: The Pinhole Camera Model (from 3D World to 2D Image)

The pinhole model is the foundational equation for **every vision-based body tracker**. KTU examiners frequently expect students to derive or apply this. Let us derive it from first principles.

**Step 1 — Set up the geometry.**
Place the camera's optical center $O$ at the origin. The image plane is located at a distance $f$ (the *focal length*) in front of the lens. A 3D world point $\mathbf{P} = (X, Y, Z)^T$ is projected through $O$ onto the image plane at point $\mathbf{p} = (u, v)^T$.

**Step 2 — Use similar triangles.**
The ray from $\mathbf{P}$ to $O$ pierces the image plane. By the principle of similar triangles, the ratio of the image coordinates to the world coordinates equals the ratio of $f$ to $Z$:

$$\frac{u}{f} = \frac{X}{Z} \quad \Rightarrow \quad u = f \cdot \frac{X}{Z}$$

$$\frac{v}{f} = \frac{Y}{Z} \quad \Rightarrow \quad v = f \cdot \frac{Y}{Z}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to homogeneous coordinates and matrix form.**
To express this as a single linear matrix multiplication, we introduce homogeneous coordinates (append a "1" to vectors):

$$\begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \sim \begin{bmatrix} f & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & f & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

The symbol "$\sim$" means *equal up to a scalar multiple* (because the resulting third row gives the depth $Z$).

**Step 4 — Account for the principal point offset.**
The image origin is rarely exactly at the center of the sensor. We add offsets $c_x$ and $c_y$ (the principal point), and allow different focal lengths for $u$ and $v$ (because pixels are not necessarily square):

$$\begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \sim \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} f_x & 0 & c_x & 0 \\ 0 & f_y & c_y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}}_{K_{3 \times 4}} \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

This $3 \times 4$ matrix is the **camera intrinsic matrix**, denoted $K$. It is the heart of every vision-based body feature tracker.

**Step 5 — Why this matters for body tracking.**
Suppose we want to track a fingertip. We detect its 2D pixel location $(u, v)$ in the image. To recover the 3D world coordinate $(X, Y, Z)$ (which is what the prosthetic hand controller actually needs), we must invert this projection. With one camera, this is under-determined (one equation, three unknowns). Therefore, real body trackers use either:
- **Stereo cameras** (two cameras give two equations), or
- **Depth cameras** (the depth $Z$ is directly measured, removing one unknown).

> [!NOTE]
> **Pin-hole derivation is mandatory in KTU 2024 ESE Module 2.** Always draw the ray diagram and label $f$, $Z$, $X$, $Y$, $u$, $v$ when writing the answer.

### 3.2 Detailed Derivation 2: Lucas–Kanade Optical Flow

Optical flow is a **classical body feature tracking method** that estimates pixel-wise motion between two consecutive frames. The Lucas–Kanade (LK) method is the most widely taught variant in KTU biomedical engineering courses.

**Step 1 — Brightness Constancy Assumption.**
Assume the brightness (intensity) of a body feature point does not change between frames $t$ and $t+dt$:

$$I(x, y, t) = I(x + dx, \, y + dy, \, t + dt)$$

**Step 2 — Taylor Series Expansion.**
Expand the right-hand side using a first-order Taylor series around $(x, y, t)$:

$$I(x + dx, y + dy, t+dt) \approx I(x,y,t) + \frac{\partial I}{\partial x}dx + \frac{\partial I}{\partial y}dy + \frac{\partial I}{\partial t}dt$$

**Step 3 — Subtract and simplify.**
Subtracting $I(x,y,t)$ from both sides and dividing by $dt$:

$$\frac{\partial I}{\partial x}\frac{dx}{dt} + \frac{\partial I}{\partial y}\frac{dy}{dt} + \frac{\partial I}{\partial t} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Introduce optical flow notation.**
Let $u = dx/dt$ and $v = dy/dt$ (the 2D motion in the image plane). Let $I_x = \partial I/\partial x$, $I_y = \partial I/\partial y$, and $I_t = \partial I/\partial t$. The equation becomes:

$$I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0$$

This is the **Optical Flow Constraint Equation (OFCE)**.

**Step 5 — Apply the Lucas–Kanade assumption (constant flow in a small window).**
A single pixel gives one equation with two unknowns — *under-determined*. LK assumes the flow $(u, v)$ is constant in a small window $W$ of $n \times n$ pixels. Stacking the OFCE for all pixels in $W$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} I_{x,1} & I_{y,1} \\ I_{x,2} & I_{y,2} \\ \vdots & \vdots \\ I_{x,n^2} & I_{y,n^2} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \end{bmatrix} = - \begin{bmatrix} I_{t,1} \\ I_{t,2} \\ \vdots \\ I_{t,n^2} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 — Solve via the least-squares (pseudo-inverse) method.**

$$\begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \end{bmatrix} = - (A^T A)^{-1} A^T \mathbf{b}$$

where $A$ is the $n^2 \times 2$ spatial-gradient matrix and $\mathbf{b}$ is the temporal-gradient vector. This $(u, v)$ is the LK estimate of the body feature's instantaneous motion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** The OFCE is **one equation, two unknowns**; therefore, any single-pixel method fails. LK resolves this by windowing. The KTU exam often tests whether the student understands *why a window is needed*.

### 3.3 Detailed Derivation 3: Kalman Filter for Sensor Fusion in Body Tracking

Body trackers that fuse vision + IMU almost universally use a **Kalman Filter (KF)**. Let us derive the KF update step used in, for example, a head-tracking system.

**Step 1 — Define the state vector.**
The state of the tracked body feature at time $t$ is:

$$\mathbf{x}_t = \begin{bmatrix} p_t \\ \dot{p}_t \end{bmatrix}$$

where $p_t$ is the position (scalar or 3D) and $\dot{p}_t$ is the velocity.

**Step 2 — Write the prediction model (state transition).**
Assuming constant-velocity motion:

$$\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{t \vert t-1} = A \, \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{t-1 \vert t-1}$$

where

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & \Delta t \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Predict the covariance.**

$$P_{t \vert t-1} = A \, P_{t-1 \vert t-1} \, A^T + Q$$

where $Q$ is the process-noise covariance (modelling uncertainty in the constant-velocity assumption).

**Step 4 — Obtain a sensor measurement.**
The vision system reports a position $z_t$ (pixel coordinates converted to world coordinates). The measurement model is:

$$\mathbf{z}_t = H \, \mathbf{x}_t + \text{noise}$$

with $H = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$ (we only directly measure position, not velocity).

**Step 5 — Compute the Kalman Gain.**
The Kalman Gain $K_t$ determines how much weight to give the new measurement versus the prediction:

$$K_t = P_{t \vert t-1} H^T (H P_{t \vert t-1} H^T + R)^{-1}$$

where $R$ is the measurement-noise covariance.

**Step 6 — Update the state estimate.**

$$\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{t \vert t} = \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{t \vert t-1} + K_t (\mathbf{z}_t - H \, \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{t \vert t-1})$$

**Step 7 — Update the covariance.**

$$P_{t \vert t} = (I - K_t H) P_{t \vert t-1}$$

**Step 8 — Iterate.**
Return to Step 2 with the new state estimate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** When $R$ is large (noisy sensor), $K_t$ becomes small, and the filter trusts the IMU prediction more. When $R$ is small (clean sensor), $K_t$ becomes large, and the filter trusts the vision measurement more. This *adaptive weighting* is the core reason Kalman filters are indispensable in modern body trackers.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Algorithm Table

The following table is essential for KTU 14-mark questions asking you to "compare and contrast" body-tracking algorithms:

| Algorithm | Type | Strengths | Weaknesses | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Template Matching (SSD/NCC)** | Generative | Simple, deterministic | Fails under rotation, scale, occlusion | Static or slowly-moving faces |
| **Lucas–Kanade Optical Flow** | Generative | Computationally efficient | Brightness constancy assumption breaks under sudden light changes | Head/eye tracking in controlled lighting |
| **Mean-Shift / CAMShift** | Generative | Robust to scale changes | Slow convergence, drifts in clutter | Color-distinctive feature tracking |
| **Kalman / Particle Filter** | Generative + Probabilistic | Handles noise and occlusion via prediction | Requires motion model | Sensor fusion, predictive tracking |
| **HOG + SVM (Classical ML)** | Discriminative | Strong on rigid features (face, pedestrian) | Manual feature engineering | Pedestrian/face detection in older systems |
| **CNN-based (e.g., OpenPose)** | Discriminative (Deep) | Robust to pose, scale, occlusion | High compute, large training set | Full-body skeletal tracking |
| **MediaPipe BlazePose** | Discriminative (Real-time) | 30+ FPS on mobile, robust | Closed-source, Google ecosystem | Mobile AAC, AR rehabilitation |
| **Transformer-based (e.g., PoseFormer)** | Discriminative (Deep) | Captures long-range temporal context | Even higher compute | Video-based gait analysis |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1: High-Level Block Diagram of a Body Feature Tracking System

This is the canonical block diagram you should be able to draw from memory in any KTU exam. The mermaid version below is fully compatible with the strict identifier and formatting rules (no reserved keywords, no markdown inside node labels).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[USER BODY<br>FEATURE] --> B[SENSOR LAYER<br>RGB / Depth / IMU / EMG]
    B --> C[PREPROCESSING<br>Filtering and Bg Subtract]
    C --> D[FEATURE DETECTION<br>Landmark Localization]
    D --> E[FEATURE TRACKING<br>Optical Flow or Deep Net]
    E --> F[STATE ESTIMATION<br>Kalman or Particle Filter]
    F --> G[INTENT DECODER<br>Gesture to Command]
    G --> H[ACTUATOR<br>Prosthetic / Wheelchair / AAC]
    H --> I[USER FEEDBACK<br>Haptic / Audio / Visual]
    I --> A
```

**Explanation of Flow:**
- The **user's body feature** (e.g., a contracting bicep, a moving eye) is the input.
- The **sensor layer** converts the physical phenomenon into an electrical/digital signal.
- **Preprocessing** cleans the signal (removes noise, normalizes lighting).
- **Feature detection** localizes the body landmark in each frame.
- **Feature tracking** maintains the landmark identity across frames.
- **State estimation** smooths the trajectory and predicts future motion.
- **Intent decoding** translates the motion into a meaningful command.
- The **actuator** executes the command on the assistive device.
- **User feedback** closes the loop, allowing the user to confirm and refine the action.

### 4.2 Diagram 2: Sensor Modality Decision Tree

Use this decision tree in design viva or 14-mark "design a system" questions:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[NEW TRACKING<br>PROBLEM] --> Q1{Is the user<br>indoors?}
    Q1 -- YES --> Q2{Need 3D<br>coordinates?}
    Q1 -- NO --> Q3{Is the limb<br>in motion?}
    Q2 -- YES --> Q4[Use Depth<br>Camera Kinect]
    Q2 -- NO --> Q5[Use RGB Webcam<br>MediaPipe Hands]
    Q3 -- YES --> Q6[Use IMU on<br>the body segment]
    Q3 -- NO --> Q7[Use EMG<br>for muscle intent]
    Q4 --> End[FINAL SENSOR<br>SELECTION]
    Q5 --> End
    Q6 --> End
    Q7 --> End
```

### 4.3 Diagram 3: Skeletal Body Feature Hierarchy (Used in 3D Pose Tracking)

This is a block-level functional architecture showing the parent–child relationship between skeletal joints as used in modern pose trackers (OpenPose, BlazePose, Vicon):

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[PELVIS<br>ROOT JOINT] --> S[SPINE]
    S --> N[NECK]
    N --> H[HEAD]
    N --> LS[LEFT SHOULDER]
    N --> RS[RIGHT SHOULDER]
    LS --> LE[LEFT ELBOW]
    LE --> LW[LEFT WRIST]
    LW --> L1[LEFT THUMB]
    LW --> L2[LEFT INDEX]
    LW --> L3[LEFT MIDDLE]
    LW --> L4[LEFT RING]
    LW --> L5[LEFT PINKY]
    RS --> RE[RIGHT ELBOW]
    RE --> RW[RIGHT WRIST]
    RW --> R1[RIGHT THUMB]
    RW --> R2[RIGHT INDEX]
    RW --> R3[RIGHT MIDDLE]
    RW --> R4[RIGHT RING]
    RW --> R5[RIGHT PINKY]
    P --> LH[LEFT HIP]
    LH --> LK[LEFT KNEE]
    LK --> LA[LEFT ANKLE]
    LA --> LFO[LEFT FOOT]
    P --> RH[RIGHT HIP]
    RH --> RK[RIGHT KNEE]
    RK --> RA[RIGHT ANKLE]
    RA --> RFO[RIGHT FOOT]
```

### 4.4 Diagram 4: Closed-Loop Control Architecture for Prosthetic Hand

A body tracker in a prosthetic system forms the *forward path* of a classic feedback control loop. The mermaid version maps the interaction between the user, the tracker, and the prosthetic actuator.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    User[USER<br>Brain sends<br>muscle command] --> EMG[EMG SENSOR<br>reads muscle<br>electrical activity]
    EMG --> Track[BODY FEATURE<br>TRACKER<br>classifies intent]
    Track --> Cmd[GENERATE<br>GRIP COMMAND]
    Cmd --> Actuator[PROSTHETIC<br>ACTUATOR<br>moves fingers]
    Actuator --> Object[OBJECT<br>grasped]
    Object --> ForceS[FORCE SENSOR<br>in fingertips]
    ForceS --> FB[FEEDBACK<br>TO USER<br>vibration or audio]
    FB --> User
```

**Engineering Insight:** The "Body Feature Tracker" block is the *intelligence* of this loop. If it is wrong, the actuator is wrong. If it is slow, the feedback is meaningless. The KTU board examiner expects you to identify the tracker as the **single point of failure** in this architecture.

### 4.5 Diagram 5: Gaze Tracking Optical Geometry (Pupil + Glint Method)

For eye-gaze AAC systems, the geometry is fixed. The cornea acts as a convex mirror; an infrared LED produces a *glint* (specular reflection). The vector from the glint to the pupil center is mapped to a screen coordinate.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IR[IR LED<br>illuminates eye] --> Cornea[CORNEA<br>reflects glint]
    Cornea --> Pupil[PUPIL<br>absorbs IR]
    Camera[IR CAMERA<br>captures both] --> Det1[GLINT DETECTOR<br>locates bright spot]
    Camera --> Det2[PUPIL DETECTOR<br>locates dark center]
    Det1 --> Vec[VECTOR CALC<br>pupil minus glint]
    Det2 --> Vec
    Vec --> Map[CALIBRATION MAP<br>vector to screen xy]
    Map --> Cursor[SCREEN CURSOR<br>or AAC key]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Model Question Paper)]**
*Define "tracking of body features" in the context of assistive medical devices. List any four common body features that are tracked in modern assistive systems.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Tracking of body features is the real-time detection, identification, and continuous monitoring of anatomical landmarks of the human body — such as the eyes, head, hands, limbs, or gait — using sensors and computational algorithms, in order to extract control commands or feedback signals for assistive medical devices.

**Four common body features (0.5 marks each, total 2 marks; list any four):**
1. **Eye** (gaze, pupil, iris) — used in eye-gaze AAC and wheelchair control.
2. **Head** (pose, orientation) — used in hands-free cursor and wheelchair control.
3. **Hand and fingers** (skeletal joints, grasps) — used in prosthetic hand command decoding.
4. **Arm / leg** (joint angles, kinematics) — used in rehabilitation robotics and exoskeletons.
5. **Gait** (heel-strike, stride, cadence) — used in smart walkers and fall detection.

#### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Retrieved from Past Paper Archive)]**
*State the brightness-constancy assumption used in the Lucas–Kanade optical flow method. Write the optical flow constraint equation and explain why a single-pixel method fails to solve it.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Brightness-constancy assumption (1 mark):**
The intensity (brightness) of a body feature point remains constant as it moves between two consecutive image frames. Mathematically: $I(x, y, t) = I(x + dx, y + dy, t + dt)$.

**Optical flow constraint equation (1 mark):**
$$I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0$$

where $I_x, I_y$ are spatial image gradients, $I_t$ is the temporal gradient, and $(u, v)$ is the 2D motion vector (optical flow).

**Why a single pixel fails (1 mark):**
A single pixel provides **one scalar equation** in **two unknowns** $(u, v)$ — the system is under-determined and has infinitely many solutions. Therefore, the Lucas–Kanade method assumes the flow is locally constant within a small $n \times n$ window and solves the resulting over-determined system using least squares.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Many students **omit units** in numerical answers. Always write units (e.g., mm, pixels, Hz, m/s²). Marks are deducted for missing units.
> - In derivations, students often **skip the geometric setup step**. Always draw the ray diagram (or block diagram) before writing the first equation. Examiners award 1–2 marks for a clear figure.
> - In Kalman filter questions, students confuse the **prediction step** (prior) with the **update step** (posterior). Memorize the order: Predict → Measure → Update.
> - For "compare and contrast" questions, merely listing points without a conclusion is penalized. End with one or two summary sentences.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Adapted Model Paper)]**
*With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the four-stage pipeline of a body feature tracking system used in an assistive medical device. Discuss in detail the working of the Lucas–Kanade optical flow algorithm for tracking a moving hand, and derive the optical flow constraint equation from the brightness-constancy assumption.*

**Model Answer Structure (14 marks):**

**(a) Block Diagram and Pipeline Explanation — 7 marks**

The four-stage pipeline is:
1. **Sensing (Data Acquisition):** Cameras (RGB, depth, IR), IMUs, or bioelectric sensors convert physical phenomena into electrical signals. Example: an RGB-D camera captures the user's hand at 30 Hz.
2. **Preprocessing:** Median filtering, background subtraction, color-space conversion, and illumination normalization clean the raw signal.
3. **Feature Detection and Tracking:** A detector (e.g., a CNN-based hand landmark model like MediaPipe Hands) localizes 21 keypoints of the hand. A tracker (e.g., Lucas–Kanade or Kalman) maintains the identity of each keypoint across frames.
4. **Interpretation and Actuation:** The tracked coordinates are mapped to a command (e.g., "index finger extended = open prosthetic gripper"). The command is sent to the actuator (prosthetic motor, wheelchair controller, or AAC speech synthesizer).

A block diagram should accompany the answer, similar to the diagram in Section 4.1. (Award 4 marks for the four stages with one example each; 2 marks for the block diagram; 1 mark for explaining the prosthetic hand mapping.)

**(b) Lucas–Kanade Derivation — 7 marks**

(Use the derivation from Section 3.2 of these notes. The examiner expects the following explicit steps, with one mark per step:)

1. **Brightness-constancy assumption** — clearly stated (1 mark).
2. **First-order Taylor expansion** of $I(x+dx, y+dy, t+dt)$ (1 mark).
3. **Subtraction and simplification** to obtain $I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0$ (2 marks).
4. **Recognition of the under-determination problem** — one equation, two unknowns (1 mark).
5. **Application of the LK window assumption** — stacking equations for $n \times n$ pixels (1 mark).
6. **Least-squares pseudo-inverse solution** $\begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \end{bmatrix} = -(A^T A)^{-1} A^T \mathbf{b}$ (1 mark).

**Total: 7 marks for part (b).**

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Adapted Past Paper)]**
*Explain the pinhole camera model used in vision-based body feature tracking. Derive the projection equations relating a 3D world point to its 2D image coordinates, and discuss how the camera intrinsic matrix is calibrated. Briefly describe how a stereo or depth camera overcomes the limitations of a monocular camera for 3D body tracking in an assistive device such as a head-controlled wheelchair.*

**Model Answer Structure (14 marks):**

**(a) Pinhole Camera Model and Projection — 7 marks**

1. **Geometric setup** (1.5 marks): Camera optical center $O$ at origin; image plane at distance $f$ (focal length); 3D world point $\mathbf{P} = (X, Y, Z)^T$ projected to image point $\mathbf{p} = (u, v)^T$.
2. **Similar-triangle derivation** (2 marks): $u = f \cdot X/Z$ and $v = f \cdot Y/Z$. (Both equations must be explicitly shown.)
3. **Homogeneous matrix form** (1.5 marks): $3 \times 4$ intrinsic matrix $K$ with $f_x, f_y, c_x, c_y$.
4. **Calibration** (2 marks): Use a checkerboard pattern with known 3D corner positions. Solve a non-linear least-squares problem to find $K$ (and distortion coefficients). Mention OpenCV's `calibrateCamera()` or Zhang's method.

**(b) Stereo/Depth Camera and 3D Tracking — 7 marks**

1. **Monocular limitation** (1.5 marks): One camera gives one 2D image of a 3D world; depth $Z$ is lost, so 3D position of a body feature cannot be uniquely recovered.
2. **Stereo camera principle** (2.5 marks): Two cameras separated by a baseline $B$ observe the same point; disparity $d = u_L - u_R$ yields depth via $Z = f \cdot B / d$. 3D body landmark positions (e.g., head pose) can then be triangulated.
3. **Depth camera alternative** (2 marks): Active IR projection (e.g., Kinect, RealSense) directly measures $Z$ per pixel via time-of-flight or structured light, bypassing the disparity computation.
4. **Application to head-controlled wheelchair** (1 mark): The 3D head pose is mapped to (yaw, pitch, roll) commands that drive the wheelchair's left/right motors. Stereo/depth cameras are essential because a 2D-only estimate is insufficient to distinguish "lean forward = go forward" from "tilt head up = no movement."

**Total: 14 marks.**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

Use this as a **last-minute revision checklist** before walking into the KTU ESE hall.

- **Definition (1-liner):** Body feature tracking = real-time detection + monitoring of anatomical landmarks for assistive device control.
- **Three modality classes:** Vision-based, Inertial-based, Bioelectric-based. Hybrid sensor fusion (vision + IMU) is the gold standard.
- **The 4-stage pipeline:** Sensing → Preprocessing → Detection + Tracking → Interpretation + Actuation. Memorize this verbatim.
- **Pinhole projection:** $u = f \cdot X/Z$, $v = f \cdot Y/Z$. The intrinsic matrix $K$ is calibrated with a checkerboard.
- **Optical Flow (Lucas–Kanade):** Brightness constancy + Taylor expansion gives $I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0$. One equation, two unknowns → needs a window.
- **Kalman filter order:** Predict → Measure → Update. Kalman gain $K_t$ is dimensionless and acts as the trust-weight for the new measurement.
- **EMG → Force mapping:** Linear envelope: $F(t) = a \cdot \text{RMS(EMG)} + b$.
- **Gaze tracking formula:** $g = \alpha (p - r)$, where $p$ is pupil center and $r$ is corneal glint.
- **Sampling theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$. For human gait (~2 Hz), a 30 Hz camera is ample; for finger tremor (~12 Hz), you need 30+ Hz.
- **Standard metrics to remember:** Accuracy 1–5 mm, latency < 100 ms, sampling 30–240 Hz, RMSE as the gold-standard error metric.
- **Always include units in numerical answers** — the most common mark-deduction point in KTU 2024 ESE.
- **Draw the block diagram first** before writing equations — examiners award 1–2 marks for a clear figure.
- **Assistive device examples** you should be able to name cold:
  - Prosthetic hand: EMG-based pattern recognition.
  - Eye-gaze AAC: Tobii Dynavox.
  - Head-controlled wheelchair: Permobil, Sunrise Medical.
  - Rehabilitation robot: Lokomat, Armeo Power.
  - Fall-detection: Apple Watch, Philips Lifeline.
- **Software libraries** (know at least two): OpenCV, MediaPipe, OpenPose, Vicon, Kinect SDK.
- **Hardware** (know at least three): Microsoft Kinect, Intel RealSense, Tobii Eye Tracker, Myo Armband (EMG), XSens MVN (IMU motion capture).
- **Two big challenges to mention in any answer:** (1) occlusion (the body feature is hidden by another part of the body), and (2) drift (the tracker slowly loses the feature over time). Mentioning these in a viva earns you bonus credibility points.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Enhancing Keyboard Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT614 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Enhancing Keyboard Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Enhancing Keyboard Control** refers to the systematic set of hardware adaptations, software modifications, and accessibility configurations designed to enable users with motor impairments, physical disabilities, limited dexterity, tremors, or restricted range of motion to operate a standard QWERTY (or alternative layout) keyboard effectively. It is a foundational subdomain of **Assistive Technology (AT)** that falls under the broader category of *Alternative and Augmentative Input Devices* in Human-Computer Interaction (HCI) and rehabilitation engineering.

The discipline encompasses:
- **Hardware-level enhancements** → Keyguards, enlarged keys, reduced-travel switches, alternative keyboard layouts (e.g., Chord, Dvorak, Maltron).
- **Software-level enhancements** → Accessibility utilities (StickyKeys, FilterKeys, ToggleKeys, MouseKeys, BounceKeys, RepeatKeys, SlowKeys).
- **Predictive input systems** → Word prediction, word completion, abbreviation expansion (text expansion tools).
- **On-screen virtual keyboards** → Software-rendered keyboards operated via pointing devices, switches, or eye-tracking.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The KTU syllabus frames keyboard control enhancement as a *functional independence intervention*. It is not merely about speed — it is about ensuring **functional access** to computing for users with Cerebral Palsy, Muscular Dystrophy, Spinal Cord Injury (SCI), Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS), Arthritis, Stroke, and visual impairment.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a beautiful grand piano in front of you, but you can only press ONE key at a time using a single knuckle, your palm keeps shaking involuntarily, and your fingers cannot lift more than **2 mm** off the keys. The music (your intended output) is trapped inside you.

**Enhancing Keyboard Control** is the equivalent of:
- Replacing the piano's heavy keys with feather-touch switches (Hardware).
- Adding a guard plate with holes so your shaking palm only hits ONE key at a time — a **Keyguard** (Hardware).
- Turning a "two-key chord" (Ctrl+Alt+Del) into three sequential single presses via **StickyKeys** (Software).
- Showing you the next likely word so you type 4 letters instead of 12 — **Word Prediction** (Software).
- Putting the entire piano on a tablet screen so a single switch-tap cycles through the alphabet — **On-Screen Keyboard** (Virtual).

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:** Keyboard control enhancement = anything that lets a person with limited physical ability still produce text and commands on a computer reliably, accurately, and with reduced fatigue.

---

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Standard, Bolded)

- **Key Activation Force (KAF)** — measured in **grams (g)** or **centinewtons (cN)**. Standard: 50–80 g; Light-touch: 20–45 g.
- **Key Travel Distance** — measured in **millimeters (mm)**. Standard: 4 mm; Reduced: 1.5–2.5 mm.
- **Typing Rate (Words Per Minute, WPM)** — Standard able-bodied: **40–60 WPM**; With enhancements: 10–25 WPM is functional.
- **Error Rate (Keystrokes per Error, KSPE or EPK)** — Lower is better; keyguards and filters reduce this by 60–80%.
- **Keystroke Latency (ms)** — Time between intended keypress and registered character.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** A reduction of **10 g** in KAF can improve typing rate by **15–25%** for users with arthritis. A keyguard typically increases accuracy by **70%** for users with tremors but reduces speed by **20%**. The design trade-off is intentional — *accuracy > speed* in clinical AT.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Keyboard Control Enhancements

The KTU 2024 module organises enhancements into **four functional tiers**:

### Tier 1 — Hardware Modifications
- **Keyguards**: Acrylic/Perspex plates mounted above the keys with cut-out holes aligned to each keytop.
- **Enlarged-Key Keyboards**: Key size increased from 19 mm to 25–40 mm; reduced key count per area.
- **Trackball / Joystick-Integrated Keyboards**: Combine pointing and typing on one device.
- **Alternative Layouts**: Dvorak, Colemak, Chord keyboards (e.g., Twiddler, BAT — Braille and Talking keyboard).
- **Foot-Operated Switches**: USB foot pedals mapped to frequently used keys (Enter, Space, Ctrl).

### Tier 2 — Operating System Accessibility Features
- **StickyKeys**: Converts multi-key shortcuts (chords) into sequential key presses.
- **FilterKeys**: Includes four sub-features:
  - *SlowKeys* — accepts keystroke only if key is held for a configurable dwell time.
  - *BounceKeys* (Debounce) — ignores repeated keystrokes within a configurable rejection window.
  - *RepeatKeys* — adjusts the delay before a held key auto-repeats.
  - *ToggleKeys* — emits an audible tone when Caps Lock, Num Lock, Scroll Lock are toggled.
- **MouseKeys**: Numeric keypad arrows emulate mouse movement.
- **SerialKeys** (legacy): Routes input from a single switch to serial port.

### Tier 3 — Predictive Input & Word Completion
- **Word Prediction**: Top-N list of most likely next words, ordered by frequency + recency.
- **Word Completion**: Completes the current word after a configurable prefix length.
- **Abbreviation Expansion**: "btw" → "by the way" — reduces keystrokes for repeated phrases.
- **Frequency Models**: Zipf's law, n-gram language models.

### Tier 4 — On-Screen (Virtual) Keyboards
- Standard OS on-screen keyboard (Windows, macOS, iOS, Android).
- **Scanning Keyboards**: Single-switch scanning; cursor cycles through keys/groups.
- **QWERTY Virtual Keyboard with Row-Column Scanning**: Reduces 30+ keys to 6+5 = 11 scans.
- **Head-tracking / Eye-tracking driven virtual keyboards** (e.g., Tobii, Camera Mouse).

---

## 2.2 Operational Logic of StickyKeys (Step-wise)

StickyKeys works as a **state machine** with three states:

1. **LATCH STATE** — Modifier key (Ctrl, Alt, Shift, Win) is pressed and held virtually.
2. **LOCK STATE** — Modifier is toggled ON (double-tap); remains active until pressed again.
3. **RELEASE STATE** — Modifier key is pressed alongside a non-modifier, then both released.

**Decision Logic (Pseudo-flow, NOT code):**
- IF key pressed = modifier → set sticky flag TRUE for that modifier.
- IF key pressed = non-modifier AND sticky flag TRUE → execute chord, then clear flag.
- IF key pressed = modifier twice within 5 seconds → enter LOCK state.

---

## 2.3 Operational Logic of BounceKeys / Debounce

**BounceKeys** addresses *tremor-induced unintended repeated keystrokes*.

The system ignores any key event that occurs within a configurable **Debounce Window** $\Delta t$ after the previous *valid* event from the same key.

**Mathematical Representation:**

$$T_{\text{valid}} = \{ t \mid t - t_{\text{last}} > \Delta t_{\text{debounce}} \}$$

where:
- $t$ = timestamp of incoming keypress
- $t_{\text{last}}$ = timestamp of last accepted keypress of the same key
- $\Delta t_{\text{debounce}}$ = configurable rejection window, typically **0.3 s to 1.0 s**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Trade-off:** A larger $\Delta t_{\text{debounce}}$ reduces errors from tremors but slows down intentionally rapid typing. **0.5 seconds** is the clinical default recommended by the Rehabilitation Engineering and Assistive Technology Society of North America (**RESNA**).

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---------|----------------|----------------------|
| 1 | BounceKeys Debounce | $t - t_{\text{last}} > \Delta t_{\text{debounce}}$ | $\Delta t = 0.3$–$1.0$ s |
| 2 | SlowKeys Dwell | $t_{\text{release}} - t_{\text{press}} > t_{\text{dwell}}$ | $t_{\text{dwell}} = 0.5$–$2.0$ s |
| 3 | Word Prediction Keystroke Saving | $S\% = \dfrac{N_{\text{original}} - N_{\text{predicted}}}{N_{\text{original}}} \times 100$ | 30–55% saving |
| 4 | Row-Column Scan Steps | $S = R + C$ where $R$=rows, $C$=columns | 6 + 5 = 11 steps max |
| 5 | On-Screen Keyboard Selection (Linear Scan) | $S = N_{\text{keys}}$ | 101+ steps (full QWERTY) |
| 6 | Keystroke Reduction via Abbreviation | $R\% = \dfrac{K_{\text{full}} - K_{\text{abbrev}}}{K_{\text{full}}} \times 100$ | 50–80% |
| 7 | Typing Rate | $WPM = \dfrac{\text{Characters typed}}{5 \times T_{\text{minutes}}}$ | 10–60 WPM |
| 8 | Effective Access Time (EAT) | $EAT = T_{\text{select}} + T_{\text{confirm}}$ | 1–8 s per character (scanning) |
| 9 | Key Activation Force (KAF) | Direct specification | 20–80 g |
| 10 | Key Travel | Direct specification | 1.5–4.0 mm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** Row-Column scanning is mathematically optimal for single-switch scanning. For a QWERTY layout (10 columns × 4 rows), the maximum number of selections = $10 + 4 = 14$ steps, instead of the naive 40 single-switch steps. This is a **2.85× efficiency gain**.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Rehabilitation Engineering Clinics** — OT/PT assessment and prescription.
- **Workplace Accessibility Compliance** — Section 508 (USA), RPwD Act 2016 (India), EN 301 549 (EU).
- **Educational Inclusion** — Students with dyslexia, dyspraxia, cerebral palsy using laptops in mainstream schools.
- **Aging-in-Place** — Elderly users with arthritis or Parkinson's tremor.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Keystroke Savings from Word Prediction

**Problem Statement:** A user types the sentence **"the quick brown fox"** (3 words, 19 characters including spaces). The word predictor offers "the" after 't', "quick" after 'qu', and "fox" after 'fo'. The user must type the first 2 characters of each word and then select the prediction with 1 keystroke (Tab/Down+Enter). Derive the keystroke savings.

### Given Data
- Original sentence: **"the quick brown fox"** → character count $N_{\text{original}} = 19$
- Prediction trigger: **2-character prefix** per word
- Prediction selection cost: **1 keystroke**
- Words to predict: "the" (3), "quick" (5), "brown" (5), "fox" (3) — we apply to 3 of 4 words.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Identify how many characters are typed manually with prediction.
- "the" → user types "th" + 1 select = 3 keystrokes (instead of 3 typed fully).
- "quick" → user types "qu" + 1 select = 3 keystrokes (instead of 5).
- "brown" → typed fully (predictor may not have it first) = 5 keystrokes.
- "fox" → user types "fo" + 1 select = 3 keystrokes (instead of 3 — neutral here).

**Step 2:** Compute total predicted-mode keystrokes $N_{\text{predicted}}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{predicted}} &= 3 + 3 + 5 + 3 \\
&= 14 \text{ keystrokes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the Keystroke Saving formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S\% &= \frac{N_{\text{original}} - N_{\text{predicted}}}{N_{\text{original}}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{19 - 14}{19} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{5}{19} \times 100 \\
&\approx 26.3\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation.
- The user saved **5 keystrokes** in a 19-character sentence → **~26% reduction**.
- For longer sentences or higher-frequency word lists (top 10,000 words), savings of **40–55%** are clinically reported.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Row-Column Scanning Efficiency for QWERTY

**Problem Statement:** Compare the *number of switch activations* required to type the letter **'M'** (located in row 3, column 7) using (a) linear scanning and (b) row-column scanning of an on-screen keyboard with 40 keys arranged in 4 rows × 10 columns.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 (Linear Scan):** The system highlights each key in sequence. The cursor must travel from key 1 to key 31 (assuming alphabetical or QWERTY linear order: M is the 13th letter of alphabet but the 31st key in QWERTY physical order "Q,W,E,R,T,Y,U,I,O,P,A,S,D,F,G,H,J,K,L,Z,X,C,V,B,N,M").

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{linear}} &= \text{position of M in scan order} \\
&= 31 \text{ switch activations}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 (Row-Column Scan):** The user first selects the **row** containing M (row 3 of 4) → requires 3 switch activations (highlight cycles Row 1, Row 2, Row 3 → user presses to confirm). Then the user selects the **column** within that row (column 7 of 10) → requires 7 activations.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{RC}} &= R + C \\
&= 3 + 7 \\
&= 10 \text{ switch activations}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 (Efficiency Gain):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{N_{\text{linear}} - N_{\text{RC}}}{N_{\text{linear}}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{31 - 10}{31} \times 100 \\
&\approx 67.7\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 (Worst-Case Bound):** The maximum number of switch activations in row-column scan is bounded by the sum of the number of rows and columns, regardless of position:

$$
N_{\text{RC, max}} = R + C
$$

For a full QWERTY (3 rows × 10 columns):

$$
N_{\text{RC, max}} = 3 + 10 = 13
$$

For a 4×10 grid:

$$
N_{\text{RC, max}} = 4 + 10 = 14
$$

**Step 5 (Information-Theoretic Note):** The theoretical minimum number of binary decisions required to select one of $N$ keys is $\lceil \log_2 N \rceil$. For 40 keys:

$$
N_{\text{min}} = \lceil \log_2 40 \rceil = 6 \text{ binary decisions}
$$

Row-column scanning is **suboptimal** but **robust** for single-switch users who cannot perform parallel binary encoding.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Enhancement

| User Condition | Primary Impairment | Recommended Enhancement Stack |
|----------------|-------------------|-------------------------------|
| Cerebral Palsy (spastic) | Uncontrolled motor output | Keyguard + BounceKeys + StickyKeys + SlowKeys |
| Spinal Cord Injury (C4-C5) | Quadriplegia, no hand use | Sip-and-puff + Scanning on-screen keyboard |
| Muscular Dystrophy | Progressive weakness | Light-touch keyboard + Word prediction |
| Arthritis (severe) | Pain, reduced ROM | Enlarged keys + SlowKeys + ergonomic layout |
| Parkinson's disease | Tremor, rigidity | BounceKeys (1.0 s) + StickyKeys + Larger pointer |
| Blind / Low Vision | No visual feedback | Tactile keyguard + Tactile bumps on F, J + Screen reader |
| ALS (advanced) | Loss of speech + limb | Eye-tracking + On-screen keyboard + Word prediction |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Keyboard Enhancement System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    userInput[User Physical Input: Finger, Switch, SipPuff, Gaze] --> preProc[Pre-Processing Hardware Layer]
    preProc --> keyguard[Keyguard or Enlarged Key Surface]
    keyguard --> physKey[Physical Key Switch with Adjusted KAF]
    physKey --> osLayer[Operating System Input Layer]
    osLayer --> stickyKeys[StickyKeys Engine]
    osLayer --> filterKeys[FilterKeys Engine: Slow, Bounce, Repeat]
    osLayer --> mouseKeys[MouseKeys Engine]
    filterKeys --> debounce{Debounce Check: t minus tlast greater than threshold}
    debounce --> acceptKey[Accept Key]
    debounce --> rejectKey[Reject Key: Tremor or Bounce]
    stickyKeys --> modState{Modifier State Machine: Latch, Lock, Release}
    modState --> execChord[Execute Chord Command]
    modState --> clearMod[Clear Modifier Flag]
    acceptKey --> appLayer[Application Layer: Text Input Stream]
    execChord --> appLayer
    appLayer --> predEngine[Word Prediction Engine]
    predEngine --> wordList[Word List from Zipf and Ngram Model]
    wordList --> topN[Top N Predictions Display]
    topN --> userSelect[User Selects: Click, Scan, or Eye Gaze]
    userSelect --> finalText[Final Text Output]
    clearMod --> finalText
    rejectKey --> noOp[No Operation: Key Discarded]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid State Machine — StickyKeys Modifier Behaviour

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Latch: Modifier Key Pressed
    Latch --> Lock: Same Modifier Pressed Twice within 5 sec
    Latch --> ExecuteChord: Non-Modifier Key Pressed
    Lock --> Idle: Same Modifier Pressed to Release
    ExecuteChord --> Idle: Both Keys Released
    Lock --> ExecuteChord: Non-Modifier Key Pressed
    ExecuteChord --> Lock: Modifier Still Active
    Idle --> Idle: No Modifier or Non-Modifier Match
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Flow — Row-Column Scanning Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Single Switch Press Initiates Scan] --> rowScan[Highlight Row 1]
    rowScan --> rowPress{Switch Pressed?}
    rowPress --> noRow1[Move to Row 2]
    noRow1 --> row2Press{Switch Pressed?}
    row2Press --> noRow2[Move to Row 3]
    noRow2 --> row3Press{Switch Pressed?}
    row3Press --> targetRow[Selected Row: proceed to Column Scan]
    rowPress --> targetRow
    row2Press --> targetRow
    targetRow --> colScan[Highlight Column 1 within Row]
    colScan --> colPress{Switch Pressed?}
    colPress --> colNext[Move to Next Column]
    colNext --> colPress2{Switch Pressed?}
    colPress2 --> colNext2[Continue]
    colPress2 --> keySelected[Key at Intersection Selected]
    colPress --> keySelected
    keySelected --> output[Character Emitted to Text Stream]
    output --> start
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Hardware Keyguard Assembly

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    base[Keyboard Base: Standard QWERTY] --> switchMech[Membrane or Mechanical Switch Layer]
    switchMech --> keycaps[Standard Keycaps: 19mm pitch]
    keycaps --> mountPlate[Mounting Frame: Aluminium or 3D Printed]
    mountPlate --> acrylicGuard[Acrylic Keyguard Plate: 3 to 5 mm thickness]
    acrylicGuard --> cutouts[Precision Cutouts: Aligned to Each Keycap]
    cutouts --> userHand[User Hand or Stylus Enters Cutout]
    acrylicGuard -. blocks adjacent keys .-> keycaps
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** The acrylic keyguard physically blocks the user from pressing two keys simultaneously — directly addressing the *uncontrolled co-activation* common in spastic cerebral palsy. This is a hardware-level debounce.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define StickyKeys and explain its significance in assistive computing.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

StickyKeys is a software accessibility feature of the operating system that allows a user to press multi-key combination shortcuts (called *chords*, such as Ctrl+Alt+Delete or Ctrl+C) one key at a time, sequentially, instead of holding multiple keys simultaneously.

**Significance:**
1. **Enables chord execution** for users who can press only one key at a time (e.g., single-switch users).
2. **Reduces motor demand** — the user no longer needs to coordinate two or three fingers to hold keys down.
3. **Three states** — *Latch, Lock, Release* — provide flexible operation: Latch for one-time chords; Lock for repeated chords (e.g., holding Shift for many capital letters); Release clears the modifier.
4. **Built into major OS** — Windows (since Windows 95), macOS, GNOME, KDE — making it universally available without additional cost.

---

### **Question 2: What is a Keyguard? State two of its advantages and one disadvantage.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

A **Keyguard** is a rigid plate, typically made of clear acrylic (Perspex) or aluminium, with cut-out holes precisely aligned to each key of a keyboard, mounted approximately **3–5 mm above the keycap surface**.

**Advantages:**
1. **Prevents accidental adjacent-key presses** — critical for users with tremors, athetosis, or spastic movements.
2. **Provides a physical targeting guide** — users can rest their hands on the plate between keypresses, reducing fatigue.
3. Enables one-finger or single-switch typing with high accuracy.

**Disadvantage:**
1. **Reduces typing speed** by approximately **15–25%** because the user must precisely align fingers with the cutouts. Alternatively, it requires two-handed lift-off for rapid typists.

---

## 📘 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### ⭐ **Question A (14 Marks): StickyKeys & BounceKeys Deep Analysis**

`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat state diagram, explain the working of StickyKeys. List the three states and the trigger conditions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain BounceKeys with a mathematical formulation. A user has set a debounce window of $\Delta t_{\text{debounce}} = 0.6$ s. The following keystrokes of the letter 'S' were recorded: 10:00:00.000, 10:00:00.120, 10:00:00.250, 10:00:00.700, 10:00:01.500. Identify which keystrokes are accepted and which are rejected. **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ MODEL ANSWER — Question A

#### Part (a) — StickyKeys Working (7 marks)

**StickyKeys** is an accessibility utility that converts simultaneous multi-key shortcuts (chords) into sequential single-key presses. It is essential for users with **single-input capability** (one finger, one switch, sip-and-puff).

**The three states of the StickyKeys state machine are:**

1. **LATCH STATE** — Triggered when a modifier key (Ctrl, Alt, Shift, Win, or Fn) is pressed once. The modifier is virtually held down. The next non-modifier key is treated as part of a chord.

2. **LOCK STATE** — Triggered when the same modifier is pressed twice within 5 seconds. The modifier remains virtually active until the user explicitly presses it again to release. This is useful for typing multiple capital letters (Shift-Lock) or for repeated actions.

3. **RELEASE STATE** — When a non-modifier key is pressed while in LATCH or LOCK, the chord is executed and the modifier is automatically released (in LATCH mode). In LOCK mode, the modifier remains active for further chords.

**Trigger Conditions:**

- IF a modifier is pressed alone → enter LATCH.
- IF a modifier is pressed while another modifier is already in LATCH → both modifiers are virtually held (for chord like Ctrl+Alt).
- IF the same modifier is pressed twice within 5 s → enter LOCK.
- IF any non-modifier is pressed while in LATCH or LOCK → execute the chord and (in LATCH only) clear the modifier flag.

> *(Diagrammatic representation should be drawn — see Section 4.2 Mermaid state diagram for reference. 2 marks for diagram, 5 marks for textual explanation.)*

---

#### Part (b) — BounceKeys Mathematical Analysis (7 marks)

**BounceKeys (Debounce Filter)** is an accessibility feature that ignores unintended repeated keystrokes caused by tremors, muscle spasms, or motor incoordination. It uses a *debounce window* $\Delta t_{\text{debounce}}$.

**Mathematical Formulation:**

A keystroke at time $t$ is accepted IF and ONLY IF:

$$t - t_{\text{last\_accepted}} > \Delta t_{\text{debounce}}$$

Otherwise, it is rejected as a bounce / tremor artefact.

**Given:** $\Delta t_{\text{debounce}} = 0.6$ seconds.

**Recorded timestamps for key 'S':**

| # | Timestamp (HH:MM:SS.ms) | $t$ (seconds from first) | $t - t_{\text{last\_accepted}}$ | Decision |
|---|------------------------|--------------------------|-------------------------------|----------|
| 1 | 10:00:00.000 | 0.000 | — (no prior) | **ACCEPT** |
| 2 | 10:00:00.120 | 0.120 | $0.120 - 0.000 = 0.120$ s | $0.120 < 0.6$ → **REJECT** |
| 3 | 10:00:00.250 | 0.250 | $0.250 - 0.000 = 0.250$ s | $0.250 < 0.6$ → **REJECT** |
| 4 | 10:00:00.700 | 0.700 | $0.700 - 0.000 = 0.700$ s | $0.700 > 0.6$ → **ACCEPT** |
| 5 | 10:00:01.500 | 1.500 | $1.500 - 0.700 = 0.800$ s | $0.800 > 0.6$ → **ACCEPT** |

**Final accepted sequence:** Keystrokes **1, 4, and 5** are accepted → the user input is interpreted as **"S S S"** (three S characters).

**Rejected (bounce-suppressed):** Keystrokes 2 and 3.

**Conclusion:** The BounceKeys filter reduced 5 raw physical presses to 3 valid logical characters, eliminating 2 tremor-induced duplicates while preserving user intent.

---

### ⭐ **Question B (14 Marks): On-Screen Keyboard & Row-Column Scanning**

`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Compare **linear scanning** and **row-column scanning** of an on-screen virtual keyboard. Show mathematically which is more efficient for typing the letter **'P'** located in **row 1, column 10** of a 4-row × 10-column QWERTY layout (40 keys total). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **Word Prediction** and **Abbreviation Expansion** as keyboard enhancement techniques. A user types a 200-character paragraph and uses a word predictor that triggers after 2 characters, with each prediction selection costing 1 keystroke. If the predictor correctly predicts **35 words** out of **50 words** in the paragraph, calculate the keystroke savings. Assume an average word length of 5 characters. **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ MODEL ANSWER — Question B

#### Part (a) — Scanning Method Comparison (7 marks)

**Linear Scanning:** The on-screen cursor highlights each key one by one in sequence (e.g., Q → W → E → ...). To select key 'P' (10th key in top row of 40-key QWERTY in left-to-right physical order), the user must wait for the cursor to highlight all preceding keys and press the switch on 'P'.

**Row-Column Scanning:** The scan first iterates through rows. The user presses the switch when the desired row is highlighted. The scan then iterates through columns within that row. The user presses the switch when the desired column is highlighted.

**Calculation for letter 'P' (row 1, column 10):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{linear}} &= \text{position of P in scan order} = 10 \\
N_{\text{row-col}} &= R + C = 1 + 10 = 11
\end{aligned}
$$

For 'P' located at the **edge** of the grid, both methods require similar activations, but for letters in the middle of the keyboard (e.g., 'G' = row 2, column 5):

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{linear, G}} &= 20 \text{ (assuming 10 keys in row 1 + 5 keys into row 2)} \\
N_{\text{row-col, G}} &= 2 + 5 = 7
\end{aligned}
$$

**Worst-case comparison for the entire 40-key keyboard:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{linear, max}} &= 40 \\
N_{\text{row-col, max}} &= R + C = 4 + 10 = 14
\end{aligned}
$$

**Efficiency gain (worst case):**

$$
\eta = \frac{40 - 14}{40} \times 100 = 65\%
$$

**Conclusion:** Row-column scanning is **always equal to or better than** linear scanning. Its upper bound ($R + C$) is much smaller than $N_{\text{keys}}$, making it the standard in clinical scanning keyboards.

---

#### Part (b) — Word Prediction & Abbreviation Expansion with Calculation (7 marks)

**Word Prediction** is a software technique that anticipates the next word the user intends to type based on the partial text already entered. It uses statistical language models (Zipf's frequency distribution, n-gram models, or modern transformer-based AI). After the user types a configurable prefix (e.g., 2–3 characters), the system offers the top N (usually 5–8) most probable completions, and the user can select one with a single keystroke.

**Abbreviation Expansion** is a text-substitution utility where a short string (abbreviation) is automatically replaced with a longer pre-defined string. Example: typing "asap" expands to "as soon as possible", "omw" expands to "on my way".

**Given Calculation:**

- Paragraph length: $N_{\text{original}} = 200$ characters
- Average word length: 5 characters
- Total words in paragraph: $50$
- Predictions successful: $35$ out of $50$
- Each prediction selection: $1$ keystroke
- Trigger prefix: $2$ characters

**Step 1:** Compute original keystrokes (no enhancement).

$$
N_{\text{original}} = 200 \text{ keystrokes}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute keystrokes for predicted words.

For each of the 35 successfully predicted words, the user types only the 2-character prefix and then 1 keystroke to select the prediction = **3 keystrokes per predicted word**.

For the remaining $50 - 35 = 15$ unpredicted words, the user types the full 5 characters = **5 keystrokes per word**.

Space characters (49 of them) are still typed manually → 49 keystrokes.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{predicted}} &= (35 \times 3) + (15 \times 5) + 49 \\
&= 105 + 75 + 49 \\
&= 229 \text{ keystrokes}
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait — this appears *higher* than the original. This indicates that with a 2-character trigger and 5-character average word length, the technique is **NOT** beneficial unless the words are long. **Let us reconsider with longer words (e.g., 8-character average):**

**Re-calculated Scenario — Average word length = 8 characters:**

- Total keystrokes without prediction: $50 \times 8 + 49 = 449$ keystrokes.
- With prediction: $(35 \times 3) + (15 \times 8) + 49 = 105 + 120 + 49 = 274$ keystrokes.

**Savings:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S\% &= \frac{449 - 274}{449} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{175}{449} \times 100 \\
&\approx 38.97\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Word prediction becomes highly effective when average word length exceeds 6 characters. The **2-character trigger is optimal** because a 1-character trigger produces too many false candidates, and a 3+ character trigger reduces the prefix-saving benefit.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students forget to add the space character count (49 spaces) in the calculation → lose 1 mark.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Students assume word prediction *always* saves keystrokes, regardless of word length → incorrect. Savings emerge only when word length > (prefix + selection cost). Memorise the threshold: for prefix 2 + selection 1 = 3, savings begin when word length ≥ 4.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Confusing *Word Prediction* (next word) with *Word Completion* (end of current word). They are different — **KTU expects this distinction**.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Omitting the unit "%" in the final answer.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Enhancing Keyboard Control**

- ✅ **StickyKeys** = sequential chord execution. Three states: **Latch, Lock, Release**.
- ✅ **FilterKeys** = umbrella term with 4 sub-features: **SlowKeys, BounceKeys, RepeatKeys, ToggleKeys**.
- ✅ **BounceKeys Formula:** Accept IF $(t - t_{\text{last}}) > \Delta t_{\text{debounce}}$; typical $\Delta t$ = **0.5 s** (RESNA standard).
- ✅ **SlowKeys Formula:** Accept IF $(t_{\text{release}} - t_{\text{press}}) > t_{\text{dwell}}$; typical dwell = **0.5–2.0 s**.
- ✅ **MouseKeys** = numeric keypad acts as mouse; useful for users with hand impairment but good finger control.
- ✅ **Keyguard** = acrylic plate with cutouts; **reduces errors by ~70%**, **reduces speed by ~20%** — accuracy-favoured trade-off.
- ✅ **Word Prediction** = next-word suggestion; **typical savings 30–55%** for normal English text.
- ✅ **Word Completion** = completes *current* word (e.g., "med" → "medicine"). Different from prediction.
- ✅ **Abbreviation Expansion** = "btw" → "by the way"; savings 50–80% for repeated phrases.
- ✅ **Row-Column Scanning** = $N = R + C$ switch activations; better than linear $N$ for $N > R + C$.
