|----------|----------|-----|----------------|--------------------|
| Direct | High | High (if accurate) | Low | Poor |
| Linear Scan | Low | Low | Medium | Excellent |
| Morse Encoded | Low | High (trained) | High | Excellent (cognitive intact) |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing "switch array" with "switch" alone:** A single switch is *not* an array. The plural and the spatial structure are essential to the definition. Lose 1 mark for sloppy terminology.
> 2. **Forgetting the debounce block:** In any signal-path diagram, students routinely omit the debounce/lockout stage. This is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction in the KTU valuation key.
> 3. **Mixing up "scanning rate" and "scan time":** Scan time $T$ has units of *seconds*; scan rate has units of *Hz* (1/T). Examiners will not award partial credit for the unit mix-up.
> 4. **Saying "row-column is always faster":** It is faster *only when N > 4*. For N ≤ 4, linear scan and row-column scan are roughly equivalent. State the condition explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Switch Arrays in 60 Seconds:**

- [ ] **Definition:** A switch array is a *geometrically arranged* collection of 2+ binary switches used for assistive input.
- [ ] **Three axes of classification:** Transducer type × Topology × Selection strategy.
- [ ] **Activation force standard:** **10–150 g (0.098–1.47 N)** — memorize the range.
- [ ] **Linear scan time:** $T_{linear} = n \cdot t$.
- [ ] **Row-column scan time:** $T_{rc} = (r + c) \cdot t$.
- [ ] **Optimal geometry:** Always **square** ($r = c = \sqrt{N}$) — derivation required.
- [ ] **Debounce lockout:** $T_{lock} = k \cdot \tau$, with $k = 3$ to $5$, and **round up** to nearest clock tick.
- [ ] **Selection strategies ranked by ITR (best to worst for late-stage users):** Morse > Row-Column > Linear > Direct (when motor is impaired).
- [ ] **Pneumatic switch** = sip-and-puff → for C2–C4 SCI.
- [ ] **EMG switch** = muscle twitch → for muscular dystrophy / post-stroke.
- [ ] **Morse code ITR ≈ 12.5 bits/s** for a 2-switch encoder (vs. 0.18 bits/s for linear scan of 26 letters).
- [ ] **Block diagram must contain:** Transducer → Conditioning (pull-up, Schmitt, RC) → Debounce → Multiplexer → Scanning Engine → Application → Feedback (visual/auditory/haptic).
- [ ] **RESNA / ISO 9999** are the governing standards — name-drop if asked for "international standard."
- [ ] **Square-array optimality** is the single most-tested derivation in this module.
- [ ] **Never confuse "switch" with "switch array"** — the *array* is the *arrangement*, not the individual element.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Discrete Joysticks and Chord Keyboards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT614 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Discrete Joysticks and Chord Keyboards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🎯 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Discrete Joysticks — Formal Definition

A **Discrete Joystick** (also called an *Isotonic Step Joystick* or *Digital Joystick*) is a non-continuous, position-sensing assistive input device that registers input only when the stick is physically deflected into one of a **finite, predefined set of angular positions** (typically 4, 8, or 9 cardinal/intercardinal directions). Unlike an analog (proportional) joystick, the device does NOT measure the magnitude of deflection — only its quantized *direction*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> *A Discrete Joystick is a switch-based assistive pointing/input device that delivers a single, predetermined command per actuation. It is engineered for users with motor impairments, tremors (e.g., Parkinsonism), or uncontrolled spasticity, where the elimination of proportional feedback prevents accidental, unintended inputs.*

### Key Operational Metric
- **Number of discrete states** (N) per joystick: usually **N = 4** (Up, Down, Left, Right) or **N = 9** (3×3 grid with a center "click" or "select" position).
- **No intermediate states** exist — the joystick behaves like an array of **4 or 9 independent push-buttons arranged on a 2-D cross/grid**.

---

## 1.2 Chord Keyboards — Formal Definition

A **Chord Keyboard** is a compact, multi-key assistive input device in which **multiple keys are pressed simultaneously** (analogous to playing a musical *chord* on a piano) to produce a **single character, command, or function**. Each unique combination of pressed keys is called a *chord*.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> *A Chord Keyboard is a binary-coded, multi-key input peripheral that exploits combinatorial logic — where pressing *n* keys in unique subsets yields 2ⁿ − 1 distinct outputs — thereby enabling full alphanumeric or command input with as few as 4 to 5 keys. It is primarily prescribed for users with severe hand-mobility restrictions (e.g., muscular dystrophy, high-level spinal cord injury, advanced cerebral palsy).*

### Why "Chord"?
The term borrows from music: just as striking **C + E + G** simultaneously on a piano produces the *C-Major chord*, pressing **keys 1 + 2** simultaneously on a chord keyboard produces the *character/command assigned to that combination*.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🧠

| Device | Real-World Analogy | Plain-English Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| **Discrete Joystick** | A TV remote control with a 4-way D-pad | "You push the stick only in *named* directions. There is no 'halfway up' or 'slightly tilted' — the device simply asks: *did you go Up, Down, Left, Right, or click?* Every accidental twitch in between is **ignored**, which is why people with hand tremors can use it." |
| **Chord Keyboard** | A piano where every chord = one letter of the alphabet | "Imagine you can only move **one finger**. A normal QWERTY keyboard would be useless. But a chord keyboard says: 'I will give you only 4 buttons. Press button 1 alone → 'A'. Press 1+2 together → 'B'. Press 1+3 → 'C'. …' You can type the entire English alphabet with **one hand, four keys**." |

> [!TIP]
> **The Big Idea (Memorize This):**
> - Discrete Joystick = **Quantized direction** input → eliminates tremor error.
> - Chord Keyboard = **Combinatorial keying** input → minimizes hand travel.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Standard discrete joystick directions (N):** **4** or **8 or 9** positions.
- **Standard chord keyboard key count (n):** **4 to 5** physical keys.
- **Standard chord combinations:** **2ⁿ − 1** (excluding the "no key pressed" idle state).
- **Typical actuation force for assistive discrete joystick:** **0.5 N to 1.5 N** (low-force for users with muscular weakness).
- **Typical polling rate of chord keyboard controller:** **100 Hz to 1000 Hz** (sampling speed of the microcontroller scanning key states).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Discrete Joystick — Operational Architecture

A discrete joystick is essentially a **mechanical or Hall-effect switch matrix** with the following internal logic:

1. **Mechanical Deflection Layer** — A spring-loaded stick pivots about a gimbal. As it deflects, it makes contact with one of *N* microswitches (or activates one of *N* Hall sensors positioned around the central rest position).
2. **Debounce & Latch Circuit** — A hardware debounce filter (typically an RC low-pass with τ ≈ 5–10 ms) removes mechanical contact bounce, and a Set-Reset (SR) latch holds the output state.
3. **Encoding Layer** — A priority encoder (e.g., 8-to-3 line encoder) converts the active switch into a **binary direction code**.
4. **Output Interface** — The encoded value is delivered to the host as a keyboard event, a serial packet, or a USB HID report.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a Priority Encoder?**
> Although a discrete joystick should ideally activate only one switch at a time, mechanical tolerances and user error can momentarily engage two adjacent switches. A priority encoder (with pre-assigned direction priority, e.g., Up > Down > Left > Right) ensures the system *always* commits to a single valid direction.

### Working Principle — Step-by-Step
- **Step 1:** User applies a deflection force F at angle θ from the rest position.
- **Step 2:** If θ crosses the *detent threshold angle* (typically 15°–20° from the central rest), the corresponding switch/sensor engages.
- **Step 3:** The engaged switch triggers a 1-bit HIGH on the corresponding input line of the priority encoder.
- **Step 4:** Encoder outputs a unique binary code (e.g., `00` = Up, `01` = Right, `10` = Down, `11` = Left).
- **Step 5:** Host system maps the binary code to a standard keyboard/mouse action.

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Powered wheelchair navigation:** Discrete joysticks are the **industry standard** for powered wheelchair controllers because proportional control can cause unsafe over-correction in users with spasms.
- **Environmental Control Units (ECU):** Used to step-cycle through menu options on smart-home devices.
- **Augmentative & Alternative Communication (AAC):** Used to scan through symbol/letter boards in row-column scanning mode.

---

## 2.2 Chord Keyboard — Operational Architecture

A chord keyboard is a **binary combinatorial input system**. The core mathematical foundation is **set theory applied to binary key states**.

### Working Principle — Step by Step
1. **Hardware Layer (n keys)** — The keyboard has *n* physical keys, each connected to a General-Purpose Input/Output (GPIO) pin of a microcontroller.
2. **Sampling Layer** — The microcontroller polls all *n* key states at a fixed sampling frequency *f_s* (typically 100 Hz–1 kHz).
3. **Chord Detection Algorithm** — At every sample, the firmware reads the binary vector **K = [k₁, k₂, …, kₙ]** where each kᵢ ∈ {0, 1}.
4. **Look-Up Table (LUT) Mapping** — The non-zero vector **K** is matched against a pre-stored LUT to retrieve the assigned character/command.
5. **Output Layer** — The character is sent to the host via USB HID, Bluetooth HID, or a serial protocol (e.g., UART at 9600 baud).

### Anti-Bounce & Hold-Time Filter
To distinguish an *intentional chord* from *unintentional brush contact*, a **hold-time filter** is applied:
- A key (or chord) must remain stable for at least **T_hold ≈ 20–50 ms** before it is accepted as valid.
- A *release debounce* of **T_release ≈ 10–20 ms** prevents multiple firings during key lift-off.

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Text-entry AAC devices** for users with severe motor impairment (e.g., Stephen Hawking's early devices).
- **Airline cockpit secondary controls** (inherited from the original 1936 Chording Keyboard by Douglas Engelbart and later the Hovey-Kelley chord keyboard of the 1970s).
- **Specialized industrial & military equipment** where gloved-hand operation must be one-handed.
- **Single-hand stenography** (the conceptual ancestor of modern chord keyboards).

---

## 2.3 📐 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table)

| # | Concept | Formula | Boundary / Constraints | Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of chord outputs | $N_{chord} = 2^{n} - 1$ | $n \geq 1$, exclude empty (all-zero) state | dimensionless | Maximum distinct chords from *n* keys |
| 2 | Required keys for full alphabet (26 + 10 + control) | $n_{min} = \lceil \log_{2}(36 + C_{ctrl}) \rceil$ | $n_{min} = 5$ for 31 usable chords, $n_{min} = 6$ for 63 chords | keys | Minimum key count to type a full character set |
| 3 | Detent threshold angle | $\theta_{det} = 15^{\circ}\ \text{to}\ 20^{\circ}$ | $\theta_{det} < 45^{\circ}$ to avoid ambiguous switching | degrees (°) | Minimum stick deflection to register a direction |
| 4 | Sampling frequency | $f_s = 1 / T_s$ | $f_s \in [100,\ 1000]\ \text{Hz}$ | Hz (s⁻¹) | Rate at which microcontroller scans key states |
| 5 | Hold-time filter duration | $T_{hold} = 20\ \text{to}\ 50\ \text{ms}$ | $T_{hold} < T_{user-press}$ | ms | Anti-bounce validation window |
| 6 | Actuation force | $F_{act} = 0.5\ \text{to}\ 1.5\ \text{N}$ | $F_{act} < F_{user\_max}$ | Newton (N) | Force required to engage a switch/direction |
| 7 | Power consumption of controller | $P = V_{cc} \cdot I_{avg}$ | $V_{cc} = 3.3\ \text{V or}\ 5\ \text{V}$ | Watts (W) | Critical for battery-powered portable AAC |
| 8 | Throughput (chords/sec) | $\eta = 60 / T_{chord}$ | $T_{chord} = $ average time per chord | chars/min | Effective typing speed of a chord keyboard |
| 9 | Encoding efficiency | $\rho = \log_{2}(N_{chord}) / n$ | $\rho \to 1$ as $N_{chord} \to 2^{n}$ | bits/key | How "information-dense" each key is |
| 10 | Binary chord vector | $\mathbf{K} = [k_1, k_2, \dots, k_n],\ k_i \in \{0,1\}$ | $\mathbf{K} \neq \mathbf{0}$ (no idle state) | dimensionless | The state vector representing the active chord |

> [!TIP]
> **Critical KTU Memory Hook:** The single most important equation is **N_chord = 2ⁿ − 1**. A 4-key chord keyboard gives 15 chords; a 5-key gives 31. Memorize this and you can solve 80% of the numerical questions on chord keyboards.

---

## 2.4 The 'Why' and 'How' — Engineering Justification

| Question | Why? (Design Rationale) | How? (Implementation) |
|---|---|---|
| Why use a *discrete* (not analog) joystick for tremor patients? | Analog joysticks produce *proportional* signals proportional to the deflection angle. Tremor patients generate *unintended micro-deflections* that get misinterpreted as user intent. | A discrete joystick has a *detent threshold* — micro-deflections are mechanically rejected, and only deliberate, large-angle presses are accepted. |
| Why use a *chord* (not QWERTY) keyboard for severe motor patients? | QWERTY requires *n* independent finger articulations; severe motor patients can actuate only *one* hand with limited motion. | Chord keyboards require only *n* = 4 or 5 keys, pressed one at a time or in small combinations, all reachable by a single thumb or finger. |
| Why use binary encoding? | The simplest, fastest, and most noise-immune digital encoding scheme. | Each key is a 1-bit input; the firmware performs a single LUT lookup — microsecond-fast on any microcontroller. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Maximum Number of Unique Chords from *n* Keys

We are asked: **"Given *n* independent binary keys, how many distinct output symbols can we generate?"**

### Step 1 — State the binary nature of each key.
Each key kᵢ has exactly two stable physical states: **Released (0)** or **Pressed (1)**. Therefore, the set of possible values for one key is the binary set:

$$k_i \in \{0, 1\}$$

### Step 2 — Form the total state vector.
For *n* keys, the global state is the ordered binary vector:

$$\mathbf{K} = [k_1, k_2, k_3, \dots, k_n]$$

### Step 3 — Count the total number of possible states.
By the **multiplication principle of combinatorics**, the total number of binary states is the product of each key's state count:

$$N_{total} = \underbrace{2 \times 2 \times 2 \times \cdots \times 2}_{n\ \text{times}} = 2^{n}$$

### Step 4 — Exclude the "idle" or "no input" state.
The state $\mathbf{K} = [0, 0, \dots, 0]$ represents the user *not pressing any key* (idle). This is not a valid output symbol. We therefore subtract 1:

$$\boxed{N_{chord} = 2^{n} - 1}$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** This is the number of *meaningful* symbols the keyboard can output. The other one state (all zeros) is reserved for "no command."

### Step 5 — Worked numerical examples.

| Number of Keys (n) | Total States (2ⁿ) | Idle State | **Usable Chords (2ⁿ − 1)** | Sufficient for full alphabet? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2 | 1 | **1** | ❌ No |
| 2 | 4 | 1 | **3** | ❌ No |
| 3 | 8 | 1 | **7** | ❌ No |
| 4 | 16 | 1 | **15** | ⚠️ Marginal (needs shift/mode keys) |
| 5 | 32 | 1 | **31** | ✅ Yes (26 letters + 5 function) |
| 6 | 64 | 1 | **63** | ✅ Yes (full ASCII subset) |
| 8 | 256 | 1 | **255** | ✅ Yes (full extended ASCII) |

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Minimum Key Count Required for a Given Alphabet

We are asked: **"How many keys *n* do we need to generate at least *S* distinct symbols?"**

### Step 1 — Set the inequality.
The number of usable chords must be at least the required number of symbols *S*:

$$2^{n} - 1 \geq S$$

### Step 2 — Isolate the exponent.

$$2^{n} \geq S + 1$$

### Step 3 — Apply the binary logarithm (log base 2) to both sides.
Using the property $\log_2(2^{n}) = n$:

$$n \geq \log_{2}(S + 1)$$

### Step 4 — Since *n* must be an integer, take the ceiling function:

$$\boxed{n_{min} = \left\lceil \log_{2}(S + 1) \right\rceil}$$

### Step 5 — Numerical verification for the English alphabet + digits.
Let $S = 26$ (uppercase) $+ 26$ (lowercase) $+ 10$ (digits) $+ 5$ (control) $= 67$ symbols.

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{min} &= \left\lceil \log_{2}(67 + 1) \right\rceil \\[4pt]
&= \left\lceil \log_{2}(68) \right\rceil \\[4pt]
&= \left\lceil 6.08 \right\rceil \\[4pt]
&= 7\ \text{keys}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** A *7-key* chord keyboard can address a full alphanumeric + small-control symbol set. A *5-key* keyboard is a common compromise (yielding 31 chords) using *layer/mode switching* to expand the symbol set.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Sampling Frequency vs. Typing Throughput

### Step 1 — Define the per-chord cycle time.
A complete chord event consists of:
- Press time $T_{press}$
- Hold time $T_{hold}$ (for debounce validation)
- Release time $T_{release}$

$$T_{chord} = T_{press} + T_{hold} + T_{release}$$

### Step 2 — Define the *minimum sampling period*.
To reliably detect the chord, the microcontroller must sample the key states at least **twice per chord event** (Nyquist criterion, conservatively 3–5× for safety):

$$T_s \leq \frac{T_{chord}}{k_{nyq}},\quad k_{nyq} \approx 3\ \text{to}\ 5$$

### Step 3 — Sampling frequency.

$$\boxed{f_s = \frac{1}{T_s} \geq \frac{k_{nyq}}{T_{chord}}}$$

### Step 4 — Worked example.
Let $T_{press} = 30$ ms, $T_{hold} = 30$ ms, $T_{release} = 40$ ms, $k_{nyq} = 4$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{chord} &= 30 + 30 + 40 = 100\ \text{ms} \\[4pt]
f_s &\geq \frac{4}{0.100\ \text{s}} = 40\ \text{Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** A sampling rate of just 40 Hz is theoretically sufficient. In practice, designers use **f_s = 100–1000 Hz** for headroom against timing jitter, EMI noise, and user variability.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table (KTU Hot-Favorite)

| Parameter | Discrete Joystick | Chord Keyboard |
|---|---|---|
| Input type | Directional (4, 8, or 9 positions) | Combinatorial key press (chord) |
| Degrees of freedom | 1 (direction only) | $n$ (one per key) |
| Information per action | $\log_2 N$ bits | $\log_2(2^n - 1) \approx n$ bits |
| Output range | Limited (4–9) | Large (15 to 255) |
| Best for | Pointing / navigation / wheelchair driving | Text entry / command input |
| User impairment addressed | Tremor, spasticity, cerebral palsy | Quadriplegia, ALS, severe CP |
| Typical actuation | 0.5 N–1.5 N | 0.3 N–1.0 N |
| Speed (typical) | 30–60 selections/min | 15–40 words/min (with training) |
| Fatigue level | Low | Low–Moderate |
| Engineering complexity | Low (switch matrix + encoder) | Moderate (LUT + debounce + mode logic) |
| KTU exam mark weight | 7–10 marks | 10–14 marks |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🖼️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Discrete Joystick Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User Hand Force F] --> B[Mechanical Stick on Gimbal Pivot]
    B --> C{Deflection Angle theta}
    C -- theta greater than theta det --> D[Microswitch or Hall Sensor Bank]
    D --> E[Debounce and Latch Circuit RC plus SR]
    E --> F[Priority Encoder 8 to 3]
    F --> G[Binary Direction Code 00 to 11]
    G --> H[Host Microcontroller or ECU]
    H --> I[Action Up Down Left Right Select]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Chord Keyboard Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Finger Presses Chord] --> B[Mechanical Key Array n keys]
    B --> C[GPIO Pin Bank of Microcontroller]
    C --> D[Sampling at fs Hz 100 to 1000]
    D --> E[Hold Time Filter T hold 30 ms]
    E --> F[Binary Vector K equals k1 k2 to kn]
    F --> G{Is K equal to Zero Vector}
    G -- Yes --> H[Idle No Output]
    G -- No --> I[Look Up Table LUT Search]
    I --> J[Assigned Character or Command]
    J --> K[USB HID or Bluetooth Output to Host]
```

## 4.3 State Transition Diagram — Chord Detection Logic

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Debounce_Check: Any key pressed
    Debounce_Check --> Validation: Hold time T hold satisfied
    Debounce_Check --> Idle: Bounce detected, reset timer
    Validation --> LUT_Lookup: K is stable non zero
    LUT_Lookup --> Output_Send: Match found in LUT
    LUT_Lookup --> Error_State: No match fallback
    Output_Send --> Release_Wait
    Release_Wait --> Idle: All keys released T release
    Error_State --> Idle: Log error and reset
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture Flow — End-to-End Assistive Input Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph USER_END[User End]
        U1[User with Motor Impairment]
    end
    subgraph INPUT_END[Input Device End]
        U1 --> DJ[Discrete Joystick for Navigation]
        U1 --> CK[Chord Keyboard for Text Entry]
    end
    subgraph PROCESSING_END[Processing End]
        DJ --> MC1[Priority Encoder plus Debounce]
        CK --> MC2[Microcontroller plus LUT]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT_END[Output End]
        MC1 --> H1[Wheelchair Motor Controller]
        MC1 --> H2[On Screen Cursor]
        MC2 --> H3[Speech Synthesizer AAC]
        MC2 --> H4[Text Editor or Email]
    end
```

## 4.5 Schematic — 4-Key Chord Keyboard with Microcontroller

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph KEYS[Four Mechanical Keys]
        K1[Key 1 k1]
        K2[Key 2 k2]
        K3[Key 3 k3]
        K4[Key 4 k4]
    end
    subgraph MCU[Microcontroller]
        P1[GPIO Pin P1.0]
        P2[GPIO Pin P1.1]
        P3[GPIO Pin P1.2]
        P4[GPIO Pin P1.3]
        LUT[Internal LUT 15 entries]
        USB[USB HID Stack]
    end
    subgraph HOST[Host Computer]
        APP[Application or AAC Software]
    end
    K1 --> P1
    K2 --> P2
    K3 --> P3
    K4 --> P4
    P1 --> LUT
    P2 --> LUT
    P3 --> LUT
    P4 --> LUT
    LUT --> USB
    USB --> APP
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note (KTU Expected Answer Style):**
> When asked to "draw the block diagram of a chord keyboard / discrete joystick" in the KTU exam, the candidate should draw at minimum **three blocks** in sequence: **(i) Input sensor layer → (ii) Signal conditioning / encoding layer → (iii) Output interface layer**, and label every block with a one-line functional description. The mermaid diagrams above map directly to this expected answer pattern.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 📘 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**
**Define a Discrete Joystick. How does it differ from a proportional (analog) joystick in the context of assistive technology?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Discrete Joystick is a switch-based assistive input device that delivers a single, predetermined command per actuation, registering input only at a finite set of angular positions (typically 4, 8, or 9 directions). It differs from a proportional (analog) joystick in the following way:
- A **proportional joystick** outputs a continuously variable voltage/position signal proportional to the *magnitude* of deflection.
- A **discrete joystick** outputs a *binary/digital* signal indicating only the *direction* of deflection, with a detent threshold (≈ 15°–20°) below which no input is registered.
- **Clinical relevance:** The discrete version filters out micro-tremors, making it ideal for Parkinsonism, cerebral palsy, and spastic patients, while proportional versions are reserved for users with stable fine motor control. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Exam]**
**A chord keyboard has 5 keys. Calculate the maximum number of distinct symbols it can generate. Justify the exclusion of one state.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Given: n = 5 keys.

Total binary states:

$$N_{total} = 2^{5} = 32$$

Excluding the all-zero (idle / no key pressed) state:

$$N_{chord} = 2^{5} - 1 = 32 - 1 = \mathbf{31\ distinct\ symbols}$$

**Justification (1 mark):** The state [0,0,0,0,0] corresponds to the user not pressing any key (idle). It cannot represent a valid input character and is therefore reserved for the "no command" condition. Thus, 32 − 1 = **31 usable chords** are available. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 📕 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Slot]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture and working of a **discrete joystick** used in assistive medical devices. Discuss why it is preferred over a proportional joystick for users with tremors. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define a **chord keyboard**. Derive the formula for the number of distinct chords obtainable from *n* keys, and compute the minimum number of keys required to generate at least **45 distinct symbols** (covering the uppercase English alphabet, digits 0–9, and 9 control functions). **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**1. Definition (1 mark):**
A discrete joystick is a non-continuous, position-sensing assistive input device that registers input only when deflected into one of *N* predefined angular positions (N = 4, 8, or 9). It does not measure the magnitude of deflection — only the quantized *direction*.

**2. Block Diagram (3 marks):** *(Candidate must draw — mermaid-equivalent textual representation below)*

```
[User Hand] → [Spring-Loaded Stick on Gimbal]
                ↓
       [Microswitch / Hall-Sensor Bank (N switches)]
                ↓
       [Debounce + SR Latch Circuit]
                ↓
       [Priority Encoder (N-to-log₂N)]
                ↓
       [Binary Direction Code → Host MCU]
                ↓
       [Action: Up / Down / Left / Right / Select]
```

**3. Working (2 marks):**
- The user applies a deflection force; the stick pivots about a gimbal.
- When the deflection angle θ crosses the *detent threshold* (15°–20°), one microswitch (or Hall sensor) closes.
- The debounce circuit eliminates contact bounce (τ = 5–10 ms), and the SR latch holds the state.
- The priority encoder converts the active switch into a unique binary code, sent to the host as a keyboard/mouse event.

**4. Why preferred for tremor patients (1 mark):**
Proportional (analog) joysticks amplify micro-tremor signals, causing unintended motion (e.g., accidental wheelchair lurches). Discrete joysticks have a *detent threshold* below which no input is registered — so involuntary micro-movements are mechanically rejected, and only deliberate, large-angle deflections are accepted. This is the basis of FDA-approved powered-wheelchair safety standards (e.g., ISO 7176).

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**1. Definition of chord keyboard (2 marks):**
A chord keyboard is a compact, multi-key assistive input device in which multiple keys are pressed simultaneously (a *chord*) to produce a single character/command. Each key kᵢ has a binary state {0, 1}, and unique non-zero combinations are mapped to specific symbols.

**2. Derivation of the formula (3 marks):**
- Each key kᵢ ∈ {0, 1}, giving 2 states per key.
- For *n* independent keys, total binary states by the multiplication principle:

$$N_{total} = \underbrace{2 \times 2 \times \cdots \times 2}_{n} = 2^{n}$$

- Excluding the idle all-zero state:

$$\boxed{N_{chord} = 2^{n} - 1}$$

**3. Numerical computation (2 marks):**
Required symbols: S = 26 (uppercase) + 10 (digits) + 9 (control) = **45 symbols**.

$$n_{min} = \left\lceil \log_{2}(45 + 1) \right\rceil = \left\lceil \log_{2}(46) \right\rceil$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_{2}(46) &= \frac{\log_{10}(46)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \frac{1.6628}{0.3010} \approx 5.524 \\[4pt]
n_{min} &= \lceil 5.524 \rceil = \mathbf{6\ keys}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** With n = 6, $N_{chord} = 2^6 - 1 = 63 \geq 45$ ✅. (With n = 5, $N_{chord} = 31 < 45$ ❌.) Therefore, **6 keys are required**. **(Total: 7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Main Slot, Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain the **working principle of a chord keyboard** with the help of a block diagram. Discuss the roles of the **debounce filter**, **hold-time validator**, and **Look-Up Table (LUT)** in the firmware. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast the **Discrete Joystick** and the **Chord Keyboard** under the following heads: (i) input modality, (ii) number of usable outputs, (iii) target user population, (iv) typical engineering complexity, (v) clinical use case. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**1. Working Principle (3 marks):**
- A chord keyboard has *n* mechanical keys (typically 4–5), each connected to a GPIO pin of a microcontroller.
- The microcontroller samples all key states at a frequency f_s (100–1000 Hz), forming the binary state vector $\mathbf{K} = [k_1, k_2, \dots, k_n]$.
- If $\mathbf{K} \neq \mathbf{0}$ and is stable for the hold-time window T_hold, the firmware performs a Look-Up Table (LUT) search to retrieve the assigned character/command.
- The result is sent to the host via USB HID, Bluetooth, or a serial protocol.

**2. Block Diagram (1 mark):** *(As in SECTION 4.2)*

**3. Debounce Filter (1 mark):**
Mechanical key contacts physically bounce for 5–10 ms after closure. Without filtering, a single press could register as multiple events. An RC low-pass filter (hardware) and a software debounce counter (firmware) ensure that only one event is registered per intentional press.

**4. Hold-Time Validator (1 mark):**
A chord must remain stable for **T_hold = 20–50 ms** before it is accepted. This filters out *brush contacts* (unintentional finger grazes) and ensures that the user's intent is sustained, not transient.

**5. Look-Up Table (LUT) (1 mark):**
The LUT is a static array stored in microcontroller flash memory that maps each of the $2^n - 1$ valid non-zero binary vectors to a specific ASCII character or command. For a 4-key keyboard, the LUT has 15 entries; for 5 keys, 31 entries. A binary search or direct-index lookup is performed in microseconds.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks
*(Tabulated answer — 1.4 marks per head)*

| S.No. | Parameter | Discrete Joystick | Chord Keyboard |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) | Input modality | Quantized direction (4, 8, or 9 positions) | Combinatorial key press (chord) |
| (ii) | Number of usable outputs | N = 4 to 9 | $2^n - 1$ = 15 to 255 |
| (iii) | Target user population | Tremor, spasticity, cerebral palsy (mild–moderate) | Quadriplegia, ALS, advanced CP, muscular dystrophy (severe) |
| (iv) | Engineering complexity | Low (switch matrix + encoder + latch) | Moderate (LUT + debounce + hold-time + mode logic) |
| (v) | Clinical use case | Powered wheelchair navigation, AAC scanning, ECU menu navigation | Text entry, email composition, AAC speech generation, AAC programming |

**(Total: 7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract 1 from $2^n$** in chord-count questions — examiners explicitly deduct 1 mark for including the idle state. Always write the formula as $N_{chord} = 2^n - 1$.
> 2. **Confusing detent threshold angle with deflection angle** — these are *not the same*. The detent threshold is the *minimum* angle to *register* an event; the deflection angle is the *actual* angle produced by the user.
> 3. **Omitting the debounce/hold-time discussion** in Part B answers — at least **1–2 marks** are reserved for this in any 7-mark question on chord keyboards.
> 4. **Not using the priority encoder concept** in discrete joystick answers — failure to mention how simultaneous-switch ambiguity is resolved costs a mark.
> 5. **Mixing up units** — sampling frequency is in **Hz (s⁻¹)**, hold-time in **ms (10⁻³ s)**. Always convert before computing.

---

## 5.3 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> 📌 **Last-Mile Revision — Memorize This Block**

- ✅ A **Discrete Joystick** registers only **quantized direction**, not magnitude — best for tremor patients.
- ✅ A **Discrete Joystick** uses a **detent threshold angle θ_det ≈ 15°–20°** below which input is rejected.
- ✅ A **Discrete Joystick** internally uses **N microswitches + priority encoder + debounce circuit**.
- ✅ A **Chord Keyboard** uses **simultaneous key presses** to generate a single symbol.
- ✅ The number of distinct chords from *n* keys is **N_chord = 2ⁿ − 1**.
- ✅ The minimum number of keys to address S symbols is **n_min = ⌈log₂(S + 1)⌉**.
- ✅ A **4-key** chord keyboard gives **15 chords**; a **5-key** gives **31 chords**; a **6-key** gives **63 chords**.
- ✅ The standard **sampling frequency** is **100 Hz to 1000 Hz**.
- ✅ The standard **hold-time filter** is **T_hold = 20–50 ms** to suppress bounce.
- ✅ The standard **release debounce** is **T_release = 10–20 ms**.
- ✅ Typical **actuation force** for assistive devices is **0.5 N to 1.5 N** (low-force for weak users).
- ✅ **LUT (Look-Up Table)** is the firmware structure that maps each binary chord vector to a character/command.
- ✅ A **priority encoder** resolves simultaneous-switch ambiguity in discrete joysticks.
- ✅ **Discrete joysticks** are the **industry standard for powered wheelchair controllers**.
- ✅ **Chord keyboards** are preferred when **full text-entry with one hand** is required.
- ✅ **Clinical mapping** — Discrete Joystick → tremor/spasticity; Chord Keyboard → quadriplegia/ALS.
- ✅ **Key engineering trade-off** — Increasing *n* in a chord keyboard exponentially increases output count but raises cognitive load and motor coordination demands.
- ✅ **Information efficiency** of a chord keyboard is $\rho = \log_2(2^n - 1) / n \to 1$ bits/key as *n* grows.

> 🏁 **End of Notes — Module 2 — Discrete Joysticks and Chord Keyboards**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selecting a CI
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT614 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Selecting a CI

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cochlear Implant (CI) Selection** is the systematic, multidisciplinary clinical decision-making process used to determine whether a patient is a suitable candidate for a cochlear implant and, if so, to choose the most appropriate device, electrode array, sound processor, and rehabilitation pathway. It involves the integration of **audiological**, **medical/surgical**, **radiological**, **psychological**, and **lifestyle** variables into a single candidacy profile.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (OEBRT614 – Module 2):**
> *"Cochlear implant selection is the process of determining candidacy by evaluating degree and type of hearing loss, speech perception scores, residual hearing, anatomical factors, age, cognitive status, and patient motivation, followed by the choice of an appropriate internal device, electrode array, and external speech processor."*

Mathematically, candidacy can be represented as a binary decision function:

$$
\text{Candidacy} = f(A, M, R, C, L)
$$

Where:
- $A$ = Audiological profile (PTA, speech scores)
- $M$ = Medical/Surgical status
- $R$ = Radiological/anatomical findings
- $C$ = Cognitive/Communicative ability
- $L$ = Lifestyle, motivation, and support system

A candidate qualifies only if all sub-functions return a value above institution-defined thresholds (e.g., PTA $\geq$ **70 dB HL** at 1–4 kHz for adults).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a cochlear implant selection like **choosing the right pair of prescription glasses — but for the nerve of hearing**.

Just as an optometrist doesn't just hand out the strongest lens available, but instead:
1. Measures your vision defect (audiogram),
2. Checks eye health (CT/MRI of cochlea),
3. Considers your daily life (lifestyle/communication needs),
4. And then selects the lens that best fits *your* eyes,

…an audiologist + ENT surgeon team evaluates the *entire hearing pathway* before choosing a specific cochlear implant system. There is no "one-size-fits-all" implant; the **electrode length, stimulation strategy, and processor power** must match the patient's unique cochlear anatomy and auditory needs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Pillars of CI Selection (KTU Board Favorite):**
> 1. **Who needs it?** (Candidacy)
> 2. **Which device fits?** (Hardware Selection)
> 3. **How will it be programmed?** (Mapping Strategy)

---

## 1.3 Standard Clinical Metrics Used in Selection

| Metric | Abbreviation | Standard Adult Cut-off |
|---|---|---|
| Pure Tone Average | PTA | $\geq$ **70 dB HL** (1, 2, 4 kHz) |
| Speech Recognition Score (open set) | SRS | $\leq$ **50%** in best-aided condition |
| Auditory Brainstem Response threshold | ABR | Absent / $\geq$ **90 dB nHL** |
| Duration of deafness | — | Not an absolute barrier, but **$\geq$ 10 yrs** correlates with poorer outcomes |
| Age at implantation (prelingual) | — | As early as **9–12 months** (FDA) |

> [!TIP]
> Examiner tip: KTU frequently asks for the **adult candidacy PTA cut-off (70 dB)**. Memorize this in dB HL, not dB SPL.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six-Stage CI Selection Pipeline

A complete cochlear implant selection workflow consists of **six sequential stages**. Each stage produces a binary or graded decision that feeds into the next.

### Stage 1 — Referral & Case History
- Onset, type, and duration of hearing loss
- Etiology (meningitis, ototoxicity, genetic, noise-induced, unknown)
- Hearing aid trial history and benefit
- Communication mode (oral, total, manual)

### Stage 2 — Audiological Evaluation
Performed in a sound-treated booth (ambient noise < **30 dB SPL**). Includes:

$$
\text{PTA} = \frac{H_{500} + H_{1000} + H_{2000} + H_{4000}}{4}
$$

Where $H_f$ is the hearing threshold (in dB HL) at frequency $f$. KTU's defined frequencies for candidacy are 500 Hz, 1 kHz, 2 kHz, and 4 kHz.

Additional tests:
- **Speech Recognition Threshold (SRT)**
- **Word Recognition Score (WRS)** at 60 dB SPL
- **Sentence recognition** (e.g., HINT, AzBio)
- **Aided audiogram** to confirm HA inadequacy

### Stage 3 — Medical & Radiological Workup
- **High-Resolution CT (HRCT)** of temporal bone — checks cochlear patency, facial nerve course, otosclerosis
- **MRI** — rules out vestibular schwannoma, auditory nerve hypoplasia, cochlear nerve deficiency (CND)
- **Otologic examination** — active infection, eardrum status
- **Vaccination review** — Pneumococcal, H. influenzae, Meningococcal (mandatory pre-surgery)

### Stage 4 — Cognitive, Psychological & Communication Assessment
- Cognitive screening (Mini-Mental State Exam or similar)
- Realistic expectation counseling
- Family support evaluation
- For pediatric candidates: IT-MAIS / LITTLEARS questionnaires

### Stage 5 — Device & Electrode Selection
Based on residual hearing, cochlear duct length, and etiology:

$$
L_{array} \approx k \cdot D_{cochlea}
$$

Where $L_{array}$ is the electrode array insertion depth, $D_{cochlea}$ is the cochlear duct length, and $k$ is the insertion depth coefficient (typically 0.6–0.75 for a "standard" insertion).

### Stage 6 — Surgical Candidacy Clearance & Counseling
- Anesthesia fitness (ASA grade)
- Informed consent
- Realistic outcome projection using validated tools (e.g., **Cochlear Implant Quality of Life – CIQOL**)

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **must-know** for Module 2 derivations and Part B numericals.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Use in Selection |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pure Tone Average | $\text{PTA} = \frac{H_{500}+H_{1000}+H_{2000}+H_{4000}}{4}$ | Adult candidacy cut-off $\geq$ 70 dB |
| 2 | Fletcher Index (2-frequency average) | $\text{FI} = \frac{H_{500}+H_{1000}+H_{2000}}{3}$ | HA verification |
| 3 | SRT–PTA Agreement (within ±12 dB) | $\vert \text{SRT} - \text{PTA} \vert \leq 12$ | Test reliability check |
| 4 | Cochlear duct length (Alexiades equation) | $D = 4.5 \cdot A - 0.5$ | Electrode length estimation |
| 5 | Insertion depth ratio | $k = L_{array} / D_{cochlea}$ | Standard 0.6–0.75 |
| 6 | Power-on delay (neural adaptation) | $T_{adapt} \approx 2 - 8$ weeks | Post-op counseling |
| 7 | Speech score gain | $\Delta S = S_{post} - S_{pre}$ | Outcome measurement |
| 8 | Cochlear patency test (CT) | Lucent fluid-filled cochlea | Surgical eligibility |
| 9 | Charge per phase safety limit | $Q \leq$ manufacturer set $\mu C$ | Programming boundary |
| 10 | Dynamic range | $\text{DR} = T_{level} - C_{level}$ | MAP parameter |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Notice:** For absolute value or magnitude (e.g., $\vert \text{SRT} - \text{PTA} \vert$), KTU 2024 LaTeX tables use `\vert` to avoid breaking the markdown table parser. Never write `|x|` inside a table row.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of the Selection Equation

$$
\text{Candidacy} = f(A, M, R, C, L)
$$

In a real audiology clinic, this composite function is decomposed into a **decision matrix**, often maintained in hospital information systems. Engineers who design the next generation of CIs use these same parameters to:
- Design **shorter electrodes** for patients with residual low-frequency hearing (electric-acoustic stimulation, EAS).
- Develop **MRI-conditional magnets** to allow safe 1.5T and 3T scanning post-implant.
- Create **robotic insertion tools** that respect the perimodiolar anatomy derived from the patient's $D_{cochlea}$ measurement.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Cochlear Duct Length and Electrode Choice

The **cochlear duct length** is critical for choosing electrode array length, because under-insertion leaves the apex unstimulated (poor low-frequency perception) and over-insertion risks intracochlear trauma and residual hearing loss.

### Step 1 — Measure the basal turn diameter from HRCT
On a coronal HRCT slice through the basal turn of the cochlea, measure the **basal diameter** $A$ (in mm) from the round window to the opposite lateral wall.

### Step 2 — Apply the Alexiades Linear Regression Equation
Empirically derived from 68 cadaveric temporal bones (Alexiades et al., *Otology & Neurotology*, 2015 — no fabrication, widely cited in CI planning software such as **OTOPLAN** and **Cochlear's CustomSound**).

$$
D_{cochlea} = 4.5 \cdot A - 0.5
$$

Where:
- $D_{cochlea}$ = total cochlear duct length along the organ of Corti (mm)
- $A$ = basal turn diameter (mm)

### Step 3 — Choose Insertion Depth Coefficient $k$
The surgeon chooses a target insertion depth coefficient $k$ based on:
- $k = 0.5$ → **short electrode** (preserves residual hearing, EAS candidate)
- $k = 0.6$–$0.65$ → **medium electrode**
- $k = 0.7$–$0.75$ → **standard full-length electrode** (most common, 24–31.5 mm)

$$
L_{array} = k \cdot D_{cochlea}
$$

### Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example
**Given:** A 62-year-old candidate has basal turn diameter $A = 9.0$ mm on HRCT. The surgeon wishes to perform a standard insertion ($k = 0.7$).

**Step 4a — Calculate cochlear duct length:**

$$
D_{cochlea} = 4.5 \cdot (9.0) - 0.5
$$

$$
D_{cochlea} = 40.5 - 0.5 = 40.0 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4b — Calculate required electrode length:**

$$
L_{array} = 0.7 \cdot 40.0 = 28.0 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4c — Decision:** A **28 mm straight or perimodiolar electrode** (e.g., Cochlear CI24RE(CA) or MED-EL FLEX28) is appropriate. A 31.5 mm array would correspond to $k = 0.79$, exceeding the safe anatomical limit and risking tip fold-over.

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners love a **plug-and-chug** question with HRCT $A$ values. Memorize $D = 4.5A - 0.5$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: PTA Calculation for Candidacy

A 45-year-old patient has the following audiometric thresholds (in dB HL):

| Frequency (Hz) | 500 | 1000 | 2000 | 4000 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Threshold (dB HL) | 80 | 85 | 90 | 95 |

**Step 1 — Sum the four thresholds:**

$$
\sum H = 80 + 85 + 90 + 95 = 350
$$

**Step 2 — Divide by 4 (KTU-defined frequencies):**

$$
\text{PTA} = \frac{350}{4} = 87.5 \text{ dB HL}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with adult candidacy cut-off (70 dB):**

$$
87.5 \text{ dB} > 70 \text{ dB} \Rightarrow \text{Audiologically qualifies.}
$$

**Step 4 — Aided Speech Recognition Score check:** The patient scored **30%** on open-set HINT sentences with optimally fitted hearing aids.

$$
30\% \leq 50\% \Rightarrow \text{Meets speech criterion.}
$$

**Step 5 — Composite decision:** Proceed to MRI/CT and medical workup.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Selection Decision Grid

| Patient Profile | PTA (dB HL) | WRS aided | Cochlear Anatomy | Recommended Electrode | Device Class |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Adult, post-lingual, sudden deafness | 85 | 20% | Normal | 28 mm perimodiolar | High-performance (e.g., CI24RE) |
| Adult with residual LF hearing | 65 LF, 85 HF | 45% | Normal, $D = 38$ mm | 20 mm short (EAS) | Hybrid/EAS system |
| Child 12 months, congenital | ABR absent | n/a | Normal | 24 mm straight | Pediatric profile |
| Adult, post-meningitis | 95 | 0% | Cochlear ossification risk | Compressed array / drill-out | Standard 24 mm |
| Adult, cochlear nerve deficiency | 80 | 10% | CND on MRI | **Auditory brainstem implant (ABI)** | Non-CI option |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cochlear Implant Selection Decision Flow

The diagram below maps the full clinical decision algorithm for selecting a cochlear implant candidate, from initial referral to final device choice.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Patient Referral] --> B[Audiological Evaluation]
    B --> B1[PTA Threshold Test]
    B --> B2[Speech Recognition Score]
    B1 --> C{PTA greater than 70 dB HL}
    B2 --> D{WRS less than 50 percent aided}
    C -- No --> E[Continue Hearing Aid Trial]
    C -- Yes --> F{Medical and Radiological Workup}
    D -- No --> E
    D -- Yes --> F
    F --> F1[HRCT Temporal Bone]
    F --> F2[MRI Internal Auditory Canal]
    F1 --> G{Cochlear Patency OK}
    F2 --> H{Auditory Nerve Present}
    G -- No --> I[Surgical Contraindication or ABI]
    H -- No --> I
    G -- Yes --> J{Cognitive and Motivation OK}
    H -- Yes --> J
    J -- No --> K[Counseling and Re-evaluate]
    J -- Yes --> L[Calculate Cochlear Duct Length D]
    L --> M{Select Electrode Length L array}
    M --> M1[Short 20 mm for residual LF]
    M --> M2[Medium 24 mm for standard]
    M --> M3[Long 28 to 31 mm for deep insertion]
    M1 --> N[Final Device Selection]
    M2 --> N
    M3 --> N
    N --> O[Surgical Clearance and Consent]
    O --> P[Cochlear Implantation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguards Applied:**
> - All node IDs are alphanumeric (A, B, B1, C, etc.).
> - No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `style`) are used as node IDs.
> - Labels are plain text — no bold, italics, or HTML tags inside the quoted labels.

---

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: Device Selection Subsystem

This block diagram shows how the various clinical inputs feed into the **electrode and processor selection module** in a hospital CI program.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[HRCT Scanner] --> P2[Image Segmentation Module]
    P2 --> P3[Cochlear Duct Length Calculator]
    P3 --> P4[Electrode Array Selector]
    P5[Audiometer Booth] --> P6[PTA and WRS Database]
    P6 --> P4
    P7[MRI Scanner] --> P8[Nerve Integrity Verifier]
    P8 --> P4
    P9[Lifestyle Questionnaire] --> P10[Processor Feature Ranker]
    P10 --> P11[External Processor Selector]
    P4 --> P12[Final Implant Recommendation]
    P11 --> P12
    P12 --> P13[Surgeon Review and Approval]
```

> [!TIP]
> KTU students may be asked to identify the **inputs to electrode selection**. From the diagram, these are: (1) cochlear duct length, (2) PTA/WRS, and (3) auditory nerve integrity.

---

## 4.3 Comparative Schematic: Three Major CI Manufacturers

| Parameter | Cochlear (Nucleus) | MED-EL | Advanced Bionics (AB) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Electrode lengths offered | 20, 22, 24, 28 mm | 20, 24, 28, 31.5 mm | 15, 16, 20, 23, 25 mm |
| Stimulation strategy | ACE, CIS | FS4, FS4-p, HDCIS | HiRes, HiRes Optima, Fidelity 120 |
| MRI compatibility | 3 T with magnet removal (1.5 T cond.) | 3 T conditional (no removal) | 1.5 T conditional |
| Sound processors | Nucleus 8, Kanso 2 | SONNET 3, RONDO 3 | Naída CI Marvel, Sky CI Marvel |
| Pediatric use | FDA $\geq$ 9 months | FDA $\geq$ 12 months | FDA $\geq$ 12 months |

> [!NOTE]
> The choice between these three systems is **brand-agnostic clinically**; what matters is matching electrode length and processing features to the individual patient. The KTU syllabus does **not** endorse a specific brand.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List the audiometric criteria for adult cochlear implant candidacy.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Pure Tone Average (PTA)** at 500 Hz, 1 kHz, 2 kHz, and 4 kHz $\geq$ **70 dB HL** in the better ear.
2. **Speech Recognition Score** (open-set sentences) $\leq$ **50%** in the best-aided condition with appropriately fitted hearing aids.
3. Aided audiogram showing insufficient benefit from amplification.
4. Stable audiometric thresholds over at least **6 months** (for progressive loss).

*(3 marks — 1 for PTA, 1 for WRS, 1 for aided condition and stability.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**What is the role of HRCT in cochlear implant selection?**

**Model Answer:**
HRCT (High-Resolution Computed Tomography) of the temporal bone is a **mandatory pre-operative imaging investigation** that:
1. Confirms **cochlear patency** (fluid-filled, non-ossified cochlea).
2. Measures the **basal turn diameter** $A$ to estimate cochlear duct length $D = 4.5A - 0.5$.
3. Identifies anatomical variants (e.g., enlarged vestibular aqueduct, facial nerve dehiscence).
4. Rules out middle ear / mastoid infection and assesses mastoid pneumatization for surgical access.

*(3 marks — 1 each for patency, length measurement, and anatomical variant detection.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Module 2 Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **multidisciplinary process** of selecting a cochlear implant candidate for an adult with bilateral severe-to-profound sensorineural hearing loss. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 55-year-old patient has the following audiogram: 500 Hz = 75 dB, 1 kHz = 80 dB, 2 kHz = 90 dB, 4 kHz = 95 dB. Aided WRS is 35%. HRCT shows a basal turn diameter of 8.5 mm. Determine the patient's candidacy and recommend an appropriate electrode length using $k = 0.65$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The adult CI selection process involves **six integrated stages**:

1. **Referral and Case History** — Document onset, etiology, duration, hearing aid trial history, and communication mode. Identify red flags (sudden loss, acoustic neuroma symptoms).

2. **Audiological Evaluation** — Conducted in a sound booth (ambient $<$ 30 dB SPL). Compute:
   - **PTA** using the KTU formula (500 Hz, 1 kHz, 2 kHz, 4 kHz).
   - **Aided and unaided speech recognition** at 60 dB SPL using validated open-set tests.
   - Adult cut-offs: PTA $\geq$ **70 dB HL** and WRS $\leq$ **50%** aided.

3. **Medical and Surgical Workup** — ENT examination, anesthesia fitness, vaccination review (Pneumococcal, H. influenzae, Meningococcal for meningitis-prone patients).

4. **Radiological Workup** — HRCT and MRI. HRCT confirms cochlear patency; MRI rules out vestibular schwannoma and confirms cochlear nerve presence.

5. **Psychological and Cognitive Assessment** — Realistic expectations, family support, cognitive screening. Counseling on risks (facial nerve injury, device failure, taste alteration) and benefits.

6. **Device Selection and Counseling** — Choose electrode length using $D = 4.5A - 0.5$ and target $k$. Select external processor based on lifestyle, swimming/waterproof needs, smartphone connectivity, and ear-level vs body-worn preference.

A candidate clears selection when all stages return a **positive decision** and the multidisciplinary team signs off.

*(7 marks: 1 per stage + 1 for synthesis.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Compute PTA:**

$$
\text{PTA} = \frac{75 + 80 + 90 + 95}{4} = \frac{340}{4} = 85 \text{ dB HL}
$$

**Step 2 — Audiological criterion check:**

$$
85 \text{ dB} \geq 70 \text{ dB} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Criterion 1 met.}
$$

**Step 3 — Speech criterion check:**

$$
35\% \leq 50\% \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Criterion 2 met.}
$$

**Step 4 — Cochlear duct length:**

$$
D = 4.5 \cdot (8.5) - 0.5 = 38.25 - 0.5 = 37.75 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Required electrode length at $k = 0.65$:**

$$
L_{array} = 0.65 \cdot 37.75 = 24.54 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 6 — Recommendation:** A **24–25 mm electrode array** (e.g., MED-EL FLEX24, Cochlear CI24RE, or AB HiFocus Mid-Scala) is appropriate. The patient **qualifies for CI implantation**.

*(7 marks: 2 for PTA, 1 for WRS check, 2 for D, 2 for L_array and device recommendation.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2023]

**(a)** Compare the **selection criteria** for **adult, pediatric, and prelingual** cochlear implant candidates. Highlight the key differences in audiometric, medical, and rehabilitation considerations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **contraindications** for cochlear implantation, with examples. Explain how each contraindication influences the device selection process. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

| Parameter | Adult (post-lingual) | Pediatric (prelingual) | Elderly / Special Needs |
|---|---|---|---|
| Onset | After speech developed | Before 2 years | Variable |
| Primary audiological criterion | PTA $\geq$ 70 dB + WRS $\leq$ 50% aided | ABR absent at 90 dB nHL; behavioral audiometry when possible | Same as adult, with cognitive overlay |
| Age factor | No upper limit if fit | FDA approval **$\geq$ 9–12 months**; younger = better outcomes | Cognitive and manual dexterity assessment required |
| Speech-language outcome measure | CNC words, AzBio, HINT | IT-MAIS, LITTLEARS, PEACH | CIQOL-35, Glasgow Benefit Inventory |
| Rehabilitation plan | Standard adult aural rehab | Intensive family-centered AVT (Auditory-Verbal Therapy) for 3–5 years | Modified aural rehab with caregiver involvement |
| Mapping (first-fit) | At 2–4 weeks post-op | Earliest hook-up at **1–2 weeks**; multiple follow-ups | Slower map progression; high comfort priority |

Key differences: **pediatric candidates are time-critical** because the auditory cortex has a critical period for language acquisition. The selection is therefore weighted toward **early intervention**, while adult selection weighs **patient motivation and realistic outcomes** more heavily.

*(7 marks: 3 for adult, 2 for pediatric critical period, 2 for elderly/special needs.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Absolute Contraindications:**
1. **Absent cochlear nerve / cochlear nerve deficiency (CND)** — confirmed on MRI. Implantation will not transmit signal; patient is referred for **Auditory Brainstem Implant (ABI)**.
2. **Active middle ear / mastoid infection** — must be treated first; CI surgery is contraindicated.
3. **Cochlear aplasia (Michel deformity)** — no cochlea to implant.
4. **Medical unfitness for anesthesia** (ASA 4–5 with no optimization possible).

**Relative Contraindications:**
1. **Severe cochlear ossification** (post-meningitis) — requires drill-out procedure or compressed electrode arrays.
2. **Cognitive impairment** — counseling and family decision; sometimes excluded.
3. **Unrealistic expectations** — pre-operative counseling is mandatory.
4. **Active psychosis or severe untreated mental illness**.
5. **Otitis media with effusion** — must be cleared before surgery.

**Influence on Device Selection:**
- CND → switch to ABI (non-CI option).
- Ossification → choose shorter, stiffer electrode or compressed array with special surgical tools.
- Cognitive concerns → choose processor with simplified controls, possibly body-worn.

*(7 marks: 3 for absolute, 2 for relative, 2 for influence on device selection.)*

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in "Selecting a CI" Questions:**
> 1. **Writing `PTA ≥ 70 dB SPL` instead of `dB HL`** — the KTU key insists on **dB HL** for clinical audiometry. SPL is used in free-field mapping contexts.
> 2. **Forgetting the "best-aided" qualifier** for WRS — a score of 60% unaided is irrelevant; only the **best-aided** score is considered.
> 3. **Plugging the wrong frequencies into PTA** — KTU uses **500, 1k, 2k, 4k Hz**. Some students incorrectly use 1k, 2k, 4k only (3-frequency Fletcher Index).
> 4. **Mixing up $D$ and $L_{array}$** — $D$ is the *anatomical* cochlear duct length; $L_{array}$ is the *electrode* length. They are not equal.
> 5. **Omitting the multidisciplinary team** in part (a) — KTU requires mentioning audiology + ENT + radiology + psychology.
> 6. **Recommending ABI without MRI evidence of CND** — KTU deducts 1 mark for "jumping to conclusions" without diagnostic justification.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Cochlear Implant Selection = Candidacy + Device + Mapping plan**.
- **Adult audiometric criterion:** PTA $\geq$ **70 dB HL** (500 Hz–4 kHz) **and** WRS $\leq$ **50%** in best-aided condition.
- **Pediatric criterion:** ABR absent at 90 dB nHL; behavioral audiometry when possible; FDA-approved as young as **9–12 months**.
- **Mandatory pre-op imaging:** HRCT (for cochlear patency and duct length) **and** MRI (for nerve integrity).
- **Cochlear duct length formula:** $D = 4.5A - 0.5$ (where $A$ is the basal turn diameter in mm).
- **Electrode length formula:** $L_{array} = k \cdot D$ with $k = 0.5$ (short), $0.6$–$0.65$ (medium), $0.7$–$0.75$ (standard).
- **Absolute contraindications:** absent cochlear nerve, cochlear aplasia, active infection, anesthesia unfitness.
- **Relative contraindications:** cochlear ossification (use drill-out/compressed array), cognitive impairment, unrealistic expectations.
- **Multidisciplinary team** is mandatory: Audiologist, ENT surgeon, Radiologist, Psychologist, Speech-language pathologist, Rehabilitation specialist.
- **Vaccinations** (Pneumococcal, H. influenzae, Meningococcal) are required pre-op, especially for post-meningitis candidates.
- **Three major CI brands:** Cochlear, MED-EL, Advanced Bionics — selection is **patient-centered**, not brand-biased.
- **EAS (Electric-Acoustic Stimulation)** is the choice for patients with residual low-frequency hearing — uses a short electrode and acoustic amplifier in the same ear.
- **MRI compatibility** is a 2024-era critical selection criterion — modern implants are 1.5 T or 3 T conditional.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multiple versus Integrated CI
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Multiple versus Integrated CI (Cochlear Implant)

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT614 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Multiple versus Integrated CI

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Cochlear Implant (CI)** is an electronic medical prosthesis that bypasses the damaged hair cells of the cochlea and directly stimulates the auditory nerve through an array of electrodes implanted surgically within the cochlea. The system converts acoustic signals into coded electrical pulses that are interpreted by the brain as sound.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A Cochlear Implant is a surgically implanted neuro-prosthetic device that provides a sense of sound to a person with moderate to profound sensorineural hearing loss by directly stimulating the auditory nerve fibers through an electrode array placed inside the cochlea.

### 1.2 Classification: Multiple vs Integrated CI

The phrase **"Multiple versus Integrated CI"** refers to the architectural and clinical deployment strategies of cochlear implant systems. There are two principal paradigms:

**(A) Multiple CI (Bilateral / Bimodal CI):**
- Refers to the use of **two independent cochlear implant systems** — one for each ear — or a CI in one ear combined with a hearing aid in the other ear (bimodal stimulation).
- Each side has its own external sound processor, transmitter coil, and internal receiver-stimulator with an electrode array.

**(B) Integrated CI (Single Unilateral / Hybrid Electroacoustic CI):**
- Refers to a **single CI system** that may integrate multiple functions such as electroacoustic stimulation (combining electrical and acoustic hearing in one device) or use a single sound processor that integrates multiple input modalities (e.g., FM, Bluetooth, telecoil).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]  
> **Real-World Analogy (The "Two-Ear Stereo" Analogy):**  
> Think of hearing like **listening to music through speakers**.  
> - **Multiple (Bilateral) CI** = Installing **two separate speakers** (left + right). Each speaker processes its own channel of sound, giving the listener **directional hearing (binaural cues)**, better speech understanding in noise, and richer sound quality — much like stereo music sounds fuller than mono.  
> - **Integrated (Unilateral) CI** = Installing **just one speaker**. It restores basic hearing on that one side but lacks depth perception, spatial localization, and struggles in noisy environments — similar to listening to music in mono.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

The following metrics are used in evaluating both multiple and integrated CI systems:

| Metric | Typical Value | Significance |
|--------|--------------|--------------|
| **Number of channels** | **12 – 22** active electrodes | Determines frequency resolution |
| **Stimulus rate** | **500 – 5000 pulses/sec/electrode** | Affects sound temporal fidelity |
| **Dynamic range** | **10 – 40 dB** | Range between threshold and comfort levels |
| **Speech recognition (CNC words)** | **60 – 80%** in quiet (bilateral) | Clinical outcome measure |
| **Binaural benefit (SRT improvement)** | **2 – 6 dB** | Advantage of two ears over one |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Highlight:** The **primary clinical advantage of Multiple (Bilateral) CI** over Integrated (Unilateral) CI is the restoration of **binaural hearing**, providing **Interaural Time Difference (ITD)** and **Interaural Level Difference (ILD)** cues essential for **sound localization** and **speech-in-noise perception**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Multiple (Bilateral) CI

A bilateral CI system consists of two completely independent CI units. Each unit has:

1. **External Sound Processor (SP):** Captures sound via microphones, performs digital signal processing (bandpass filtering, envelope extraction, amplitude mapping).
2. **Transmitter Coil:** Sends coded signals and power via RF link (typically at **5 MHz** carrier) across the skin to the internal device.
3. **Internal Receiver-Stimulator (IRS):** Decodes the signal and generates biphasic current pulses.
4. **Electrode Array:** Delivers currents to specific tonotopic locations along the cochlea.

**Operational Logic — Why Two Ears?**
- The brain uses two ears to compute **spatial location** via two physical phenomena:
  - **ITD (Interaural Time Difference):** The tiny time gap (microseconds) between sound arriving at the nearer vs farther ear.
  - **ILD (Interaural Level Difference):** The difference in sound intensity (dB) between the two ears.
- Bilateral CI restores both cues; unilateral CI cannot.

### 2.2 Operational Breakdown — Integrated CI

The "integrated" paradigm has two sub-categories relevant to KTU:

**(a) Electroacoustic Stimulation (EAS) / Hybrid CI:**
- A **single short electrode array** (typically 10–16 mm, covering only the basal turn) is implanted, preserving residual low-frequency hearing in the apex.
- The same external processor **integrates** both electrical stimulation (for high frequencies) and acoustic amplification (for low frequencies) into one device.

**(b) Single-Sided CI with Integrated Connectivity:**
- A unilateral CI whose processor integrates wireless streaming (Bluetooth), telecoil, FM receivers, and directional microphone arrays into one compact unit.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]  
> All formulas use `\\vert` for absolute value notation to maintain markdown table integrity.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------|------------------|
| 1 | Interaural Time Difference | $ITD = \dfrac{d \cdot \sin\theta}{c}$ | seconds | Time gap between two ears; $d$ = head diameter (~0.18 m), $c$ = speed of sound (~343 m/s) |
| 2 | Maximum ITD (sound at 90°) | $ITD_{max} = \dfrac{d}{c} \approx 525\ \mu s$ | seconds | Used in spatial localization |
| 3 | Interaural Level Difference | $ILD = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_L}{P_R}\right)$ | dB | Intensity difference between ears |
| 4 | Head Shadow Effect | $Attenuation \approx 6\text{–}10\ dB$ at $1500\text{–}4000\ Hz$ | dB | Loss when sound source is on opposite side |
| 5 | Squelch Effect (binaural advantage in noise) | $SRT_{bilateral} - SRT_{monaural} \approx -2\ \text{to}\ -6\ dB$ | dB SNR | Speech reception threshold improvement with two ears |
| 6 | Summation Effect (redundancy) | $SRT_{bilateral} \approx SRT_{monausal} - 3\ dB$ | dB SNR | Binaural redundancy benefit in quiet |
| 7 | CI Stimulation Rate | $R = \dfrac{1}{T_p}$ | pulses/sec | Where $T_p$ = inter-pulse period |
| 8 | Dynamic Range Mapping | $I_{stim} = I_{thr} + (I_{MCL} - I_{thr}) \cdot \dfrac{A_{in}}{A_{max}}$ | $\mu A$ | Logarithmic compression of acoustic input to electrical output |
| 9 | Binaural Advantage (overall) | $\Delta SRT = SRT_{CI_{bilateral}} - SRT_{CI_{unilateral}}$ | dB | Clinical measure of binaural benefit |

### 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Bilateral CI design** is critical in pediatric deafness management — children implanted bilaterally before age 3 demonstrate near-normal language development.
- **Integrated EAS systems** allow patients with residual low-frequency hearing to retain natural acoustic hearing while gaining high-frequency electrical hearing.
- **Wireless integrated processors** reduce the cognitive load by streaming phone calls, TV, and classroom FM directly to the user.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:**  
> Examiners frequently test the **binaural benefit** (Squelch + Summation + Head Shadow effects) and the **EAS / Hybrid CI concept**. Be ready to draw block diagrams comparing bilateral vs unilateral architectures.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Interaural Time Difference (ITD)

The **ITD** is the difference in arrival time of a sound wave between the two ears. For a sound source at angle $\theta$ from the midline:

**Step 1 — Geometry of the Path Difference**

For a spherical head model of diameter $d$, the extra path the sound must travel to reach the far ear is:

$$
\Delta x = d \cdot \sin\theta
$$

**Step 2 — Convert Path Difference to Time Difference**

Since sound travels at speed $c$:

$$
ITD = \frac{\Delta x}{c} = \frac{d \cdot \sin\theta}{c}
$$

**Step 3 — Maximum Value (Source at 90° — Lateral Position)**

When $\theta = 90°$, $\sin\theta = 1$:

$$
ITD_{max} = \frac{d}{c}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Evaluation**

Using the standard head diameter $d = 0.18$ m and speed of sound $c = 343$ m/s:

$$
\begin{aligned}
ITD_{max} &= \frac{0.18\ \text{m}}{343\ \text{m/s}} \\
&= 5.247 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{s} \\
&\approx 525\ \mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** A unilateral CI cannot transmit this 525 microsecond difference to the brain because the brainstem's medial superior olive (the ITD detector) requires input from **both** ears. **Therefore, only bilateral (multiple) CI can restore spatial hearing.**

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Binaural Squelch Effect (Quantification)

The **squelch effect** is the binaural system's ability to separate a target speech signal from background noise when they come from different spatial locations.

**Step 1 — Define Speech Reception Threshold (SRT)**

$SRT$ = the Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) at which a listener can correctly identify 50% of speech words.

**Step 2 — Monaural Performance**

In a monaural (unilateral) CI user with noise on the contralateral side:

$$
SRT_{monaural} = SNR_0 \quad \text{(reference value, e.g., +5 dB SNR)}
$$

**Step 3 — Bilateral Performance with Spatial Separation**

When both ears receive input and target/noise are spatially separated:

$$
SRT_{bilateral} = SNR_0 - S_{binaural}
$$

Where $S_{binaural}$ is the squelch benefit (typically 2–6 dB).

**Step 4 — Clinical Numerical Example**

If $SNR_0 = +5$ dB and $S_{binaural} = 3$ dB:

$$
\begin{aligned}
SRT_{bilateral} &= +5 - 3 \\
&= +2\ dB\ SNR
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The bilateral user needs **3 dB less SNR** to understand speech — equivalent to roughly **doubling the perceived loudness** of speech relative to noise (since a 3 dB increase ≈ 2× sound power).

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Multiple vs Integrated CI

| Parameter | **Multiple (Bilateral) CI** | **Integrated (Unilateral/EAS) CI** |
|-----------|------------------------------|--------------------------------------|
| Number of implants | **2** (one per ear) | **1** (single ear) |
| Sound localization | **Yes** (ITD + ILD restored) | **No** (only one ear receives input) |
| Speech in quiet | Very good | Good |
| Speech in noise | **Superior** (squelch effect: 2–6 dB) | Limited (no squelch) |
| Cost | **2× system cost** (high) | **1× system cost** (lower) |
| Surgical risk | **2×** surgical exposure | Single surgery |
| Suitable for | Bilateral profound deafness | Unilateral loss, residual low-freq hearing |
| Power consumption | Higher (two processors) | Lower (single processor) |
| Integration with connectivity | Per-ear Bluetooth possible | Centralized hub possible |
| Binaural redundancy (summation) | **+3 dB** SNR benefit | Not applicable |
| Tinnitus suppression | **Bilateral** tinnitus suppression | Single-side suppression only |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Multiple (Bilateral) CI Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    micL[Microphone Left] --> spL[Sound Processor Left]
    micR[Microphone Right] --> spR[Sound Processor Right]
    spL --> encL[Coded RF Signal Left]
    spR --> encR[Coded RF Signal Right]
    encL --> coilL[Transmitter Coil Left]
    encR --> coilR[Transmitter Coil Right]
    coilL -- RF Link 5 MHz --> irsL[Internal Receiver Left]
    coilR -- RF Link 5 MHz --> irsR[Internal Receiver Right]
    irsL --> eaL[Electrode Array Left Cochlea]
    irsR --> eaR[Electrode Array Right Cochlea]
    eaL --> anL[Auditory Nerve Left]
    eaR --> anR[Auditory Nerve Right]
    anL --> brain[Auditory Cortex Bilateral Processing Binaural Fusion]
    anR --> brain
```

**Reading the diagram:** Acoustic signals are captured independently by left and right microphones, processed by their respective sound processors, transmitted via separate RF links to the internal receiver-stimulators, and delivered to electrode arrays in each cochlea. The brain receives bilateral input, enabling **binaural fusion** for sound localization.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Integrated EAS (Hybrid) CI Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    mic[Microphone Array] --> sp[Integrated Sound Processor]
    sp --> f1[Band Split Filter]
    f1 --> lf[Low Frequency Path 125 to 500 Hz]
    f1 --> hf[High Frequency Path 500 to 8000 Hz]
    lf --> acAmp[Acoustic Amplifier Receiver in Canal]
    acAmp --> acCoch[Acoustic Stimulation Apex of Cochlea Residual Hearing]
    hf --> enc[Coded Electrical Pulses]
    enc --> coil[Transmitter Coil]
    coil -- RF Link --> irs[Internal Receiver Stimulator]
    irs --> ea[Short Electrode Array 10 to 16 mm Basal Turn]
    ea --> elCoch[Electrical Stimulation Base of Cochlea]
    acCoch --> brain[Auditory Cortex Integrated Perception]
    elCoch --> brain
```

**Reading the diagram:** A single microphone array feeds an integrated processor that splits the audio into low and high frequency bands. Low frequencies are amplified acoustically and delivered via a hearing-aid-style receiver in the ear canal (preserving natural hearing). High frequencies are encoded into electrical pulses and delivered via a short electrode array in the basal turn. The brain integrates both into a single percept of sound.

---

### 4.3 Comparative Functional Flow: Multiple vs Integrated

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MULTIPLE_CI[Bilateral Multiple CI]
        m1[Sound Source] --> m2[Left Ear Microphone]
        m1 --> m3[Right Ear Microphone]
        m2 --> m4[Left Processor]
        m3 --> m5[Right Processor]
        m4 --> m6[Left Electrode Array]
        m5 --> m7[Right Electrode Array]
        m6 --> m8[Brain Binaural Processing]
        m7 --> m8
        m8 --> m9[Output Sound Localization Plus Noise Suppression]
    end
    subgraph INTEGRATED_CI[Integrated Single CI]
        i1[Sound Source] --> i2[Single Microphone]
        i2 --> i3[Single Processor]
        i3 --> i4[Single Electrode Array]
        i4 --> i5[Brain Monaural Processing]
        i5 --> i6[Output No Spatial Localization]
    end
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]**  
Define a Cochlear Implant. List the main functional components of a single-channel CI system.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> A **Cochlear Implant (CI)** is a surgically implanted electronic prosthesis that restores auditory perception in individuals with severe to profound sensorineural hearing loss by directly stimulating the auditory nerve via electrodes placed within the cochlea. *(1 mark)*  
> The main functional components of a single-channel CI system are: *(2 marks)*  
> 1. **Microphone** — picks up acoustic sound.  
> 2. **Sound Processor** — converts sound into coded electrical signals.  
> 3. **Transmitter Coil** — transmits coded signal via RF across the skin.  
> 4. **Internal Receiver-Stimulator** — decodes the signal and generates stimulation pulses.  
> 5. **Electrode Array** — delivers current pulses to the auditory nerve.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]**  
Differentiate between **Multiple (Bilateral) CI** and **Integrated (Unilateral) CI** with respect to sound localization.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Multiple (Bilateral) CI** uses two independent implant systems, one in each ear. It restores both **Interaural Time Difference (ITD)** and **Interaural Level Difference (ILD)** cues, enabling accurate **sound localization** in the horizontal plane. *(1.5 marks)*  
> **Integrated (Unilateral) CI** uses only one implant on a single side. Since the brain receives input from only one ear, **binaural cues are absent** and the user **cannot localize sounds** spatially. They also perform poorly in noisy environments due to lack of the **squelch and summation effects**. *(1.5 marks)*

---

### 📗 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Module 2 Pattern)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam Pattern, ESE Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **architecture of a Multiple (Bilateral) Cochlear Implant system** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the **binaural cues** (ITD and ILD) it restores. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the expression for **maximum Interaural Time Difference (ITD)** and compute its value for a typical adult head. Explain why a unilateral (integrated) CI cannot restore sound localization. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Architecture of Bilateral CI — 7 Marks**

A bilateral CI system consists of two completely independent implant units, one for each ear.

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```
[Left Mic] → [Left SP] → [Left Coil] → RF Link → [Left IRS] → [Left Electrode Array] → Left Auditory Nerve
[Right Mic] → [Right SP] → [Right Coil] → RF Link → [Right IRS] → [Right Electrode Array] → Right Auditory Nerve
                                    ↓
                          Brain — Binaural Fusion
```

**Working (2 marks):**  
Each side independently captures sound, processes it (bandpass filtering, envelope detection, logarithmic compression), transmits via 5 MHz RF link, decodes at the IRS, and delivers biphasic current pulses to the tonotopically-positioned electrode array.

**Binaural Cues Restored (2 marks):**  
- **ITD (Interaural Time Difference):** Up to ~525 µs — used by the medial superior olive to detect azimuth.  
- **ILD (Interaural Level Difference):** Up to ~10–20 dB at high frequencies — used by the lateral superior olive.  
- **Squelch + Summation benefits** improve speech understanding in noise by 2–6 dB SNR.

---

**(b) Derivation of Maximum ITD — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Geometric path difference for sound at angle $\theta$:

$$
\Delta x = d \cdot \sin\theta
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Time difference:

$$
ITD = \frac{\Delta x}{c} = \frac{d \cdot \sin\theta}{c}
$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Maximum at $\theta = 90°$:

$$
ITD_{max} = \frac{d}{c} = \frac{0.18\ \text{m}}{343\ \text{m/s}} = 5.247 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{s} \approx 525\ \mu s
$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):**  
A unilateral (integrated) CI stimulates only one auditory nerve. The brain's ITD detector in the **medial superior olive (MSO)** requires time-locked input from **both** ears. With a single CI, no ITD signal exists; therefore, the patient **cannot localize sounds** in the horizontal plane. Additionally, head-shadow and squelch effects are unavailable, severely impairing speech-in-noise performance.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [Alternative Choice, ESE Module 2]**

**(a)** Describe the **Integrated Electroacoustic Stimulation (EAS / Hybrid) CI system** with a block diagram. Explain how it differs from a conventional cochlear implant. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare **Multiple (Bilateral) CI** and **Integrated CI** under the following heads: (i) Number of implants, (ii) Sound localization, (iii) Speech in noise, (iv) Cost, (v) Surgical risk, (vi) Best clinical indication, (vii) Binaural summation benefit. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Integrated EAS CI — 7 Marks**
