- **Time-Based Media:** Provide **captions** (for pre-recorded video with dialogue), **transcripts** (for pre-recorded audio), and **audio descriptions** (narration of visual events) for pre-recorded video.
- **Adaptable Content:** Content can be presented in different ways without losing meaning — e.g., correct reading order preserved in DOM, semantic structure using headings.
- **Distinguishable (WCAG 1.4.x):** Make it easier for users to see and hear content. The most-tested sub-criterion in board exams is **1.4.3 Contrast (Minimum)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Color Contrast Ratio Formula** (used for WCAG 1.4.3):
> $$\text{Contrast Ratio} = \frac{L_1 + 0.05}{L_2 + 0.05}$$
> where $L_1$ is the **relative luminance** of the lighter color and $L_2$ is the relative luminance of the darker color, both clamped to the interval $[0, 1]$.

- **WCAG 2.1 AA minimum contrast** for normal text: **4.5 : 1**
- **WCAG 2.1 AA minimum contrast** for large text (≥ 18 pt or 14 pt bold): **3 : 1**

### 2.1.2 **O — Operable**
Interface components and navigation must be operable. Users must be able to interact with controls using diverse input modalities.

- **Keyboard Accessible (2.1.1):** All functionality must be reachable via keyboard alone — this is the cornerstone for users with motor impairments.
- **No Keyboard Trap (2.1.2):** Focus must be able to move *away from* a component using standard exit methods.
- **Enough Time (2.3.x):** Users must be able to extend, adjust, or disable time limits.
- **Seizure Safety (2.3.1):** Content must not flash more than **3 times in any 1-second window** to prevent photosensitive epileptic seizures.
- **Navigable (2.4.x):** Provide skip navigation links, descriptive page titles, and visible focus indicators.
- **Input Modalities (WCAG 2.1):** Support gesture alternatives and target sizes ≥ **44 × 44 CSS pixels** (WCAG 2.5.5).

### 2.1.3 **U — Understandable**
Information and the operation of the user interface must be understandable.

- **Readable (3.1.x):** Specify the language of the page (`<html lang="en">`) and provide explanations of unusual words.
- **Predictable (3.2.x):** Components appearing repeatedly on multiple pages must be presented in the same order.
- **Input Assistance (3.3.x):** Provide clear error messages, labels, and suggestions for correction.

### 2.1.4 **R — Robust**
Content must be robust enough to be interpreted reliably by a wide variety of user agents, including assistive technologies.

- **Compatible (4.1.x):** Use valid, semantic HTML so that screen readers can extract role, name, and value of UI components. **WCAG 4.1.2 (Name, Role, Value)** is a frequent board exam topic.

## 2.2 The Three Conformance Levels

| Level | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Level A** | Essential, non-negotiable baseline. Failure creates **complete barriers** to access. | Absolute minimum ethical baseline. |
| **Level AA** | Removes major barriers. The **legal benchmark** worldwide (e.g., ADA Title III, EU EN 301 549, Indian GIGW 2.0/3.0). | Required by most regulations. |
| **Level AAA** | Enhances accessibility beyond standard requirements. Not always achievable for all content. | Aspirational, e.g., high-stakes medical portals. |

## 2.3 WAI Core Documents — The Technical Trilogy

1. **WCAG 2.2** — Governs *web content* (HTML, CSS, multimedia, scripts).
2. **Authoring Tool Accessibility Guidelines (ATAG 2.0)** — Governs *authoring tools* (CMSs, WYSIWYG editors).
3. **User Agent Accessibility Guidelines (UAAG 2.0)** — Governs *user agents* (browsers, media players).

Supporting techniques: **WAI-ARIA 1.2** (semantic bridge for dynamic content) and **WCAG2Mobile** (mobile extensions).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Threshold Sheet

| # | Quantity / Threshold | Formula or Value | Unit / Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Color Contrast Ratio | $(L_1 + 0.05) / (L_2 + 0.05)$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| 2 | Relative Luminance (sRGB) | $L = 0.2126\,R' + 0.7152\,G' + 0.0722\,B'$ | $[0, 1]$ |
| 3 | WCAG 1.4.3 Min Contrast (Normal Text, AA) | $\geq 4.5$ | : 1 |
| 4 | WCAG 1.4.3 Min Contrast (Large Text, AA) | $\geq 3$ | : 1 |
| 5 | WCAG 1.4.11 Non-text Contrast (AA) | $\geq 3$ | : 1 |
| 6 | Seizure Threshold (2.3.1) | $\leq 3$ flashes | per second |
| 7 | Target Size Minimum (2.5.5, AA) | $\geq 24$ | CSS pixels (inlining) |
| 8 | Touch Target AAA (2.5.5) | $\geq 44 \times 44$ | CSS pixels |
| 9 | Min Session Extension (2.2.1) | $\geq 10$ | seconds |
| 10 | Caption Timing Accuracy (1.2.2) | within $\pm 100$ | ms of audio |
| 11 | Disabling Interruptions (2.2.4) | $\geq 20$ hours advance notice OR removable | — |
| 12 | Global Disability Population (WHO 2023) | $\approx 1.3$ | billion people |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hospital Patient Portals:** Accessible scheduling and prescription reading for blind and elderly patients.
- **mHealth Apps:** Voice-control and large-target interfaces for users with motor impairments.
- **Clinical Telemetry Dashboards:** Screen-reader-compatible readouts for visually impaired clinicians.
- **AI-Powered Image Captioning:** Modern WCAG-compliant sites use ML models to *auto-generate* alt text, but human auditing remains mandatory.
- **Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCI):** Future BCIs will use accessible web protocols as the *output channel* to render Web content directly to the user.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Relative Luminance & Contrast Ratio

The **WCAG 2.x** specification prescribes a standardized method to convert sRGB pixel values (digital, 0–255) into a *perceived* luminance, then to a contrast ratio.

### Step 1 — Normalize sRGB channels
Each 8-bit channel ($C_s \in [0, 255]$) is normalized:

$$C_{8bit} = \frac{C_s}{255}, \quad C_{8bit} \in [0, 1]$$

### Step 2 — Apply sRGB → Linear transformation
Because human vision is non-linear, the gamma curve must be removed:

$$
C_{linear} =
\begin{cases}
\dfrac{C_{8bit}}{12.92}, & \text{if } C_{8bit} \leq 0.03928 \\[8pt]
\left( \dfrac{C_{8bit} + 0.055}{1.055} \right)^{2.4}, & \text{if } C_{8bit} > 0.03928
\end{cases}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Relative Luminance
Luminance weights each channel by the human eye's photopic sensitivity:

$$L = 0.2126\,R_{linear} + 0.7152\,G_{linear} + 0.0722\,B_{linear}$$

This is the **CIE 1931 luminosity function** simplified for sRGB.

### Step 4 — Compute Contrast Ratio

$$\text{CR} = \frac{L_{lighter} + 0.05}{L_{darker} + 0.05}$$

The 0.05 offset prevents division-by-zero when comparing with pure black ($L = 0$).

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A medical portal displays patient diagnosis text in **hex `#0066CC`** on a **white background `#FFFFFF`**. Does this pass WCAG 1.4.3 (AA, normal text)?

### Step 1 — Convert hex to 8-bit RGB
- Foreground `#0066CC` → R = 0, G = 102, B = 204
- Background `#FFFFFF` → R = 255, G = 255, B = 255

### Step 2 — Normalize

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_f &= 0/255 = 0.0000 \\
G_f &= 102/255 = 0.4000 \\
B_f &= 204/255 = 0.8000 \\
R_b &= G_b = B_b = 1.0000
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Linearize each channel
For the foreground **Red (0.0)**, value $\leq 0.03928$, so use the linear branch:

$$
R_{f,lin} = \frac{0.0}{12.92} = 0.0000
$$

For **Green (0.4000)**, value $> 0.03928$:

$$
G_{f,lin} = \left( \frac{0.4000 + 0.055}{1.055} \right)^{2.4} = \left( 0.4313 \right)^{2.4} \approx 0.1329
$$

For **Blue (0.8000)**, value $> 0.03928$:

$$
B_{f,lin} = \left( \frac{0.8000 + 0.055}{1.055} \right)^{2.4} = \left( 0.8104 \right)^{2.4} \approx 0.6038
$$

For the white background, $1.0 > 0.03928$:

$$
R_{b,lin} = G_{b,lin} = B_{b,lin} = \left( \frac{1.0 + 0.055}{1.055} \right)^{2.4} = 1.0000
$$

### Step 4 — Compute Luminance

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{fg} &= 0.2126(0) + 0.7152(0.1329) + 0.0722(0.6038) \\
      &= 0.0000 + 0.0950 + 0.0436 \approx 0.1386
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
L_{bg} = 0.2126(1) + 0.7152(1) + 0.0722(1) = 1.0000
$$

### Step 5 — Compute Contrast Ratio

$$
\text{CR} = \frac{L_{bg} + 0.05}{L_{fg} + 0.05} = \frac{1.0000 + 0.05}{0.1386 + 0.05} = \frac{1.0500}{0.1886} \approx 5.57
$$

### Step 6 — Conclude
$\text{CR} \approx 5.57 : 1$, which is **greater than 4.5 : 1** ⇒ **PASSES WCAG 1.4.3 (AA)** for normal text. ✅

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Assistive Technologies Mapped to Accessibility Features

| Assistive Technology | Primary Disability Addressed | WCAG Criteria Mostly Used | Internet Function Enabled |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Screen Reader** (JAWS, NVDA, VoiceOver) | Blind / Low vision | 1.1.1, 1.3.1, 4.1.2 | Reads aloud web text and UI elements |
| **Screen Magnifier** (ZoomText) | Low vision | 1.4.3, 1.4.4, 1.4.10 | Magnifies portions of the screen |
| **Voice Recognition** (Dragon, VUI) | Motor impairment | 2.1.1, 2.5.1 | Voice-command navigation & dictation |
| **Eye-Tracker** (Tobii) | Severe motor / ALS | 2.1.1, 2.4.7 | Gaze-based cursor control |
| **Switch / Sip-and-Puff** | Quadriplegia | 2.1.1, 2.4.7 | Single-button sequential scanning |
| **Closed Captions / Subtitles** | Deaf / Hard of hearing | 1.2.1, 1.2.2 | Readable dialogue for video |
| **Color-Inverting / High-Contrast Mode** | Low vision, dyslexia | 1.4.3, 1.4.6 | Re-themes content for visibility |
| **Captioning / Sign Language Video** | Deaf | 1.2.6 (AAA) | Embedded sign language interpreter |
| **BCI Output (Emerging)** | Locked-in syndrome | 4.1.2, 2.1.1 | Neural selection of web elements |

## 3.4 Decision Algorithm — Selecting Conformance Level

```
START
  │
  ├── Is the project a medical / health information portal? ──YES──> Target WCAG 2.2 AA minimum, AAA preferred.
  │                                                          │
  │                                                          NO
  │                                                          │
  ├── Is the project legally bound by accessibility law? ──YES──> Target WCAG 2.2 AA.
  │                                                          │
  │                                                          NO
  │                                                          │
  ├── Is the project internal / prototype? ──YES──> Target WCAG 2.2 A minimum.
  │
  └── Audit, document, iterate, retest with assistive tech.
END
```

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Accessible Web System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["CONTENT AUTHORING LAYER"]:::layer1
    nodeB["SEMANTIC HTML ENGINE"]:::layer2
    nodeC["CSS STYLING AND RESPONSIVE LAYOUT"]:::layer3
    nodeD["ARIA SEMANTIC ENHANCEMENT MODULE"]:::layer4
    nodeE["ASSISTIVE TECHNOLOGY BRIDGE"]:::layer5
    nodeF["END USER INTERFACE"]:::layer6
    nodeG["SIGHTED USER"]:::user
    nodeH["BLIND USER WITH SCREEN READER"]:::user
    nodeI["DEAF USER WITH CAPTIONS"]:::user
    nodeJ["MOTOR IMPAIRED USER WITH KEYBOARD OR SWITCH"]:::user
    nodeK["COGNITIVE ASSIST TOOLS"]:::user

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeB --> nodeD
    nodeC --> nodeE
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeF --> nodeI
    nodeF --> nodeJ
    nodeF --> nodeK

    classDef layer1 fill:#dfe7fd,stroke:#1f3a93,color:#000
    classDef layer2 fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0b5ed7,color:#000
    classDef layer3 fill:#9ec5fe,stroke:#0a58ca,color:#000
    classDef layer4 fill:#6ea8fe,stroke:#084298,color:#fff
    classDef layer5 fill:#3d85c6,stroke:#073763,color:#fff
    classDef layer6 fill:#1f3a93,stroke:#0b1f5c,color:#fff
    classDef user fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:** The **Content Authoring Layer** supplies raw information. It is parsed by the **Semantic HTML Engine** (which adds structural meaning — headings, lists, landmarks). The **CSS Styling** layer applies visual design *separately* from structure, so content remains meaningful even without styles. The **ARIA Enhancement Module** adds machine-readable semantics for dynamic widgets (modals, tabs, live regions). The **Assistive Technology Bridge** is the standardized DOM/Accessibility-Tree API that tools like screen readers consume. Finally, content reaches diverse **End Users** through customized interaction modalities.

## 4.2 WCAG 2.2 POUR Framework Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root["WCAG 2.2 FRAMEWORK"]:::root
    p["PERCEIVABLE"]:::p
    o["OPERABLE"]:::o
    u["UNDERSTANDABLE"]:::u
    r["ROBUST"]:::r

    p --> p1["1.1 Text Alternatives"]
    p --> p2["1.2 Time Based Media"]
    p --> p3["1.3 Adaptable"]
    p --> p4["1.4 Distinguishable"]

    o --> o1["2.1 Keyboard Accessible"]
    o --> o2["2.2 Enough Time"]
    o --> o3["2.3 Seizures and Physical Reactions"]
    o --> o4["2.4 Navigable"]
    o --> o5["2.5 Input Modalities"]

    u --> u1["3.1 Readable"]
    u --> u2["3.2 Predictable"]
    u --> u3["3.3 Input Assistance"]

    r --> r1["4.1 Compatible"]

    classDef root fill:#1f3a93,stroke:#0b1f5c,color:#fff
    classDef p fill:#dfe7fd,stroke:#1f3a93,color:#000
    classDef o fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0b5ed7,color:#000
    classDef u fill:#9ec5fe,stroke:#0a58ca,color:#000
    classDef r fill:#6ea8fe,stroke:#084298,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 User-to-Assistive-Technology Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    u1["BLIND OR LOW VISION"]:::dis
    u2["DEAF OR HARD OF HEARING"]:::dis
    u3["MOTOR IMPAIRMENT"]:::dis

    u5["SPEECH IMPAIRMENT"]:::dis

    a1["SCREEN READER JAWS NVDA VOICEOVER"]:::at
    a2["CAPTIONS AND TRANSCRIPTS"]:::at
    a3["KEYBOARD SWITCH EYETRACKER VOICE"]:::at
    a4["SIMPLIFIED TEXT SYMBOLS READ ALOUD"]:::at
    a5["AAC DEVICES TEXT TO SPEECH"]:::at

    u2 --> a2
    u3 --> a3
    u4 --> a4
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define Internet Accessibility and list any four Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) principles with one-line descriptions.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Internet Accessibility** is the inclusive design and development of web content so that persons with disabilities can perceive, operate, understand, and interact with it on an equal basis with others.

The four WCAG principles (**POUR**) are:
1. **Perceivable** — Information must be presentable in ways users can perceive through their available senses.
2. **Operable** — Interface components and navigation must be operable through diverse input methods (keyboard, voice, switch).
3. **Understandable** — Information and UI operation must be comprehensible to all users.
4. **Robust** — Content must be reliably interpretable by current and future user agents, including assistive technologies.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**What is a Screen Reader? Name two popular screen readers and explain how they relate to the WCAG principle of "Perceivable".**

**Model Answer:**

A **screen reader** is an assistive software application that converts on-screen text, UI elements, and meta-information into synthesized speech and/or Braille output, enabling blind and low-vision users to interact with digital content.

Two popular screen readers:
1. **JAWS (Job Access With Speech)** — commercial, Windows-based.
2. **NVDA (NonVisual Desktop Access)** — open-source, Windows-based.
3. (Additional: **VoiceOver** — built into Apple macOS and iOS.)

Screen readers consume the **Accessibility Tree** generated from semantic HTML and ARIA attributes. By enforcing **Perceivable** criteria (1.1.1 alt text, 1.3.1 semantic structure, 1.4.1 color independence), designers ensure that the screen reader receives a complete textual representation of visual content.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice: A or B)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q.A. (a)** Explain the four WCAG 2.2 principles (POUR) in detail. For each principle, give two example success criteria at Level AA. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

#### **P — Perceivable**
Information and user interface components must be presented in ways users can perceive.

**Example criteria (Level AA):**
- **1.4.3 Contrast (Minimum):** Visual presentation of text and images of text must have a contrast ratio of at least **4.5 : 1** for normal text and **3 : 1** for large text (≥ 18 pt or 14 pt bold).
- **1.4.5 Images of Text:** If the technologies being used can achieve the visual presentation, text is used to convey information rather than images of text (except for logos and brand names).

#### **O — Operable**
Interface components and navigation must be operable by all users.

**Example criteria (Level AA):**
- **2.4.5 Multiple Ways:** More than one way is available to locate a web page within a set of web pages, except where the page is the result of, or a step in, a process.
- **2.4.7 Focus Visible:** Any keyboard operable user interface has a mode of operation where the keyboard focus indicator is visible.

#### **U — Understandable**
Information and the operation of user interface must be understandable.

**Example criteria (Level AA):**
- **3.1.2 Language of Parts:** The human language of each passage or phrase in the content can be programmatically determined, except for proper names, technical terms, words of indeterminate language, and words or phrases that have become part of the vernacular of the immediately surrounding text.
- **3.2.3 Consistent Navigation:** Navigational mechanisms that are repeated on multiple web pages within a set of web pages occur in the same relative order each time they are repeated, unless a change is initiated by the user.

#### **R — Robust**
Content must be robust enough to be interpreted by a wide variety of user agents, including assistive technologies.

**Example criteria (Level AA):**
- **4.1.2 Name, Role, Value:** For all user interface components (including but not limited to form elements, links, and components generated by scripts), the name and role can be programmatically determined; states, properties, and values that can be set by the user can be programmatically set; and notification of changes to these items is available to user agents, including assistive technologies.
- **4.1.3 Status Messages (Level AA, WCAG 2.1+):** Status messages can be programmatically determined through role or properties such that they can be presented to the user by assistive technologies without receiving focus.

---

**(b)** A hospital patient portal uses the color hex **#333333** (very dark gray) for body text on a background of hex **#FFFF99** (light yellow). Calculate the contrast ratio and determine whether the design passes WCAG 1.4.3 (AA) for normal text. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert to 8-bit RGB and normalize**
- Foreground `#333333`: R = G = B = 51
- Background `#FFFF99`: R = 255, G = 255, B = 153

$$
R_f = G_f = B_f = 51/255 = 0.2000
$$

$$
R_b = G_b = 255/255 = 1.0000, \quad B_b = 153/255 = 0.6000
$$

**Step 2 — Linearize (all values $> 0.03928$, use the power branch)**

For foreground:

$$
C_{f,lin} = \left( \frac{0.2000 + 0.055}{1.055} \right)^{2.4} = \left( 0.2417 \right)^{2.4} \approx 0.0331
$$

So $R_{f,lin} = G_{f,lin} = B_{f,lin} \approx 0.0331$.

For background Red and Green (1.0):

$$
R_{b,lin} = G_{b,lin} = 1.0000
$$

For background Blue (0.6000):

$$
B_{b,lin} = \left( \frac{0.6000 + 0.055}{1.055} \right)^{2.4} = \left( 0.6209 \right)^{2.4} \approx 0.3185
$$

**Step 3 — Luminance**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{fg} &= 0.2126(0.0331) + 0.7152(0.0331) + 0.0722(0.0331) \\
      &= 0.0331 \times (0.2126 + 0.7152 + 0.0722) \\
      &= 0.0331 \times 1.0000 \approx 0.0331
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{bg} &= 0.2126(1) + 0.7152(1) + 0.0722(0.3185) \\
      &= 0.2126 + 0.7152 + 0.0230 \approx 0.9508
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Contrast Ratio**

$$
\text{CR} = \frac{L_{bg} + 0.05}{L_{fg} + 0.05} = \frac{0.9508 + 0.05}{0.0331 + 0.05} = \frac{1.0008}{0.0831} \approx 12.04
$$

**Step 5 — Decision**

$\text{CR} \approx 12.04 : 1 \geq 4.5 : 1$ ⇒ **PASSES WCAG 1.4.3 (AA) for normal text with a large safety margin.** ✅

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q.B. (a)** Describe the role of assistive technologies in enabling internet access. Discuss any four categories with examples and their mapping to specific disabilities. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

Assistive technologies bridge the gap between inaccessible web content and the functional needs of users with disabilities. Four major categories are:

1. **Screen Readers (Blind / Low Vision)**
   - *Examples:* JAWS, NVDA, VoiceOver, TalkBack (Android).
   - *Function:* Convert the **Accessibility Tree** of a web page into synthesized speech or Braille output, allowing the user to navigate headings, links, forms, and ARIA live regions.

2. **Screen Magnifiers and High-Contrast Tools (Low Vision)**
   - *Examples:* ZoomText, MAGic, built-in OS magnifiers, Windows High Contrast Mode.
   - *Function:* Enlarge portions of the screen (typically 2×–20×) and re-theme colors to improve visibility for users with macular degeneration, glaucoma, or cataracts.

3. **Alternative Input Devices (Motor Impairment)**
   - *Examples:* Switch devices, sip-and-puff, eye-trackers (Tobii), head-tracking mice, voice-control software (Dragon NaturallySpeaking).
   - *Function:* Replace the standard mouse/keyboard to drive the cursor, perform clicks, and traverse focus order, enabling users with ALS, cerebral palsy, or spinal cord injuries to interact with web content.

4. **Captioning and Audio Description Tools (Deaf / Hard of Hearing)**
   - *Examples:* Open captions, closed captions (`.srt` / `.vtt`), sign-language video overlays, audio description tracks.
   - *Function:* Provide a textual or signed equivalent of audio/visual content, satisfying WCAG 1.2.x criteria and enabling access to telemedicine video consultations.

5. **(Bonus) Cognitive and Learning Support Tools**
   - *Examples:* Text-to-speech, simplified-language modes, dyslexia-friendly fonts (OpenDyslexic), screen rulers, content summarizers.
   - *Function:* Reduce cognitive load and assist users with dyslexia, ADHD, or intellectual disabilities.

Each technology depends on **semantic, well-structured web content** (HTML5, ARIA) to function correctly — which is why robust engineering of the underlying content is non-negotiable.

---

**(b)** List and briefly explain any five accessibility features that must be implemented in a **telemedicine web application** to comply with WCAG 2.2 Level AA. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

1. **Alternative Text for Medical Imagery (WCAG 1.1.1)**
   Every X-ray, scan thumbnail, or icon used to indicate diagnosis must have descriptive `alt` text, e.g., `alt="Chest X-ray showing left lower lobe opacity"`. This enables screen-reader users to understand diagnostic content.

2. **Sufficient Color Contrast (WCAG 1.4.3)**
   Diagnosis text, dosage instructions, and critical alerts must achieve a contrast ratio of at least **4.5 : 1**. Emergency "alert" red text should never rely on color alone — pair with icons or text labels.

3. **Full Keyboard Operability (WCAG 2.1.1)**
   Patients with motor impairments must be able to book appointments, join video calls, and fill out symptom forms using only a keyboard. No functionality may be mouse-only.

4. **Captions and Transcripts for Video Consultations (WCAG 1.2.1 / 1.2.2)**
   Pre-recorded health education videos must carry **closed captions**. Live consultations should use auto-captioning supplemented by a human CART (Communication Access Realtime Translation) service for accuracy.

5. **Visible Focus Indicators (WCAG 2.4.7)**
   Every focusable element (buttons, links, form fields) must display a clearly visible focus outline so that low-vision and keyboard-only users can track their position on the page.

6. **Error Identification and Suggestions (WCAG 3.3.1 / 3.3.3)**
   Form errors in appointment booking or prescription refill forms must be identified in text and accompanied by correction suggestions, helping users with cognitive or learning disabilities.

7. **Compatible with Screen Readers (WCAG 4.1.2)**
   Dynamic widgets — appointment calendars, video-call modals, chat panels — must expose correct **Name, Role, and Value** to assistive technologies via ARIA and semantic HTML.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Internet Accessibility Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing ADA, Section 508, EN 301 549, and GIGW** with WCAG. WCAG is a *technical standard*; the others are *legal instruments* that *adopt* WCAG. Examiners deduct marks for equating the two.
> 2. **Forgetting the 0.05 offset** in the contrast ratio formula $(L_1 + 0.05) / (L_2 + 0.05)$. State it explicitly.
> 3. **Using the wrong linearization branch.** If $C_{8bit} \leq 0.03928$, use $C/12.92$ — do **not** apply the exponent.
> 4. **Mixing up Conformance Levels.** A is *minimum*, AA is *legal*, AAA is *gold*. Many students incorrectly claim that AA is the *highest*.
> 5. **Treating ARIA as a replacement for semantic HTML.** ARIA is a *supplement*; native HTML elements (button, nav, main) are always preferred.
> 6. **Confusing "Perceivable" with "Visible."** Color-blind users can see the page; they cannot *perceive color-coded information* without redundant cues.
> 7. **Ignoring live-region semantics** in medical dashboards — failing to mark real-time vitals as `aria-live="polite"` or `"assertive"` will lose a full 2–3 mark credit on a related question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Internet Accessibility**
>
> - **WCAG 2.2** is the current global standard (Oct 2023); **POUR** is the bedrock.
> - **Conformance levels:** A (minimum) < AA (legal benchmark) < AAA (gold standard).
> - **Color contrast formula:** $\text{CR} = (L_{lighter} + 0.05) / (L_{darker} + 0.05)$. **AA threshold = 4.5 : 1** (normal text), **3 : 1** (large text).
> - **Relative luminance:** $L = 0.2126\,R + 0.7152\,G + 0.0722\,B$ (after sRGB linearization).
> - **Seizure safety rule:** No more than **3 flashes per second** (WCAG 2.3.1).
> - **Keyboard accessibility (2.1.1)** is the *single most important* criterion for users with motor disabilities.
> - **ARIA 1.2** supplements HTML but never replaces semantic elements.
> - **Medical device relevance:** Telemedicine, EHR portals, mHealth apps, AI-captioned video, and BCI-driven web navigation all require WCAG compliance.
> - **WAI Trilogy:** WCAG (content) + ATAG (authoring tools) + UAAG (user agents).
> - **Disability statistics:** ~**1.3 billion** people worldwide live with significant disabilities (WHO).
> - **Examiners love:** defining POUR, mapping assistive tech to disabilities, calculating contrast ratios, and citing the legal benchmark as **WCAG 2.2 Level AA**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Raising the Floor Assistive Technology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT614 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Raising the Floor Assistive Technology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Raising the Floor** is an Assistive Technology (AT) design and deployment strategy in which the **accessibility baseline of mainstream, mass-market products and services is elevated** so that people with the widest possible range of abilities — including those with disabilities — can use them without requiring specialized, separate, or add-on assistive devices.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, this concept is formally traced to the **"Floor, Stairs, and Ceiling" framework** popularised by accessibility researcher **Gregg Vanderheiden (TRACE Center, University of Wisconsin–Madison)**. The framework states that any technology ecosystem contains three functional tiers:

- **Floor Level (Raised Floor):** Features that are *built into* mainstream products, usable by virtually everyone with little or no training. Example: pinch-to-zoom on a smartphone, voice assistants, large icons, closed captions.
- **Stairs Level:** Mainstream features requiring some learning or partial accommodation. Example: custom keyboard shortcuts, gesture controls, third-party accessibility apps.
- **Ceiling Level (Specialized AT):** Dedicated, high-end assistive devices used by people with severe or specific disabilities. Example: eye-gaze trackers, refreshable Braille displays, sip-and-puff switches.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Exam Definition to Memorise:**
> *"Raising the Floor = embedding accessibility into the core design of mainstream technology so that the lowest common denominator of usability is raised for all users, especially persons with disabilities."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a multi-storey building.

- The **building's entrance (the floor)** determines who can simply walk in. If the entrance is at ground level, anyone — including a person in a wheelchair, a parent with a stroller, or a delivery person with a trolley — can enter freely.
- The **staircase** lets some people go higher if they have the ability to climb.
- The **elevator or specialised lift (ceiling)** is a dedicated assistive technology that takes only those with very specific mobility needs to the top floors.

**"Raising the floor" means redesigning the building's ground-level entrance** so that even the most basic users (including those who previously would have needed the elevator just to enter) can independently access the space. The elevator is still built for those who genuinely need it, but the **default state of the world is no longer exclusionary**.

A classic real-world example: **curb cuts** (the small ramps cut into sidewalks). Originally mandated for wheelchair users, they now benefit cyclists, parents pushing prams, airport baggage handlers, and skateboarders. The floor of the sidewalk was literally "raised" into a ramp — accessibility became the default, not the exception.

### 1.3 Why "Raising the Floor" Matters in KTU 2024 Syllabus

The OEBRT614 course emphasises that **modern assistive medical devices no longer exist only as separate, expensive, stigmatising hardware**. Increasingly, **smartphones, wearables, and consumer electronics have become the primary vehicle of assistive technology delivery** — making the "Raising the Floor" concept a foundational topic in Module 3.

| Standard Metric / Concept | Value / Definition |
|---|---|
| **WHO Estimate of Global Disability Prevalence** | **~15%** of world population (≈ 1 billion people) experience some form of disability |
| **Cost Ratio (Specialized vs. Mainstream AT)** | Specialized AT can cost **10×–100×** a mainstream device with similar capability |
| **Adoption Latency** | Specialized AT adoption is delayed by **5–15 years** compared to mainstream equivalents |
| **Stigma Factor** | Specialized devices are rejected by up to **30%** of users due to social stigma; raised-floor features show near-zero rejection |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework Sheet

### 2.1 The Three-Tier Theoretical Model (Vanderheiden's Framework)

The "Raising the Floor" strategy is anchored on three structural observations about how technology is designed, distributed, and consumed:

**Tier 1 — The Original Floor (Pre-1990 baseline):**
- Mainstream products assumed a "standard" user: able-bodied, English-literate, with full motor and sensory capacity.
- Disability was treated as an edge case requiring **separate, parallel, expensive technologies** (special Braille readers, dedicated AAC devices, screen magnifier hardware).

**Tier 2 — The Raised Floor (Modern strategy):**
- Accessibility is *designed in* from the start (Universal Design principle).
- Features like screen readers (VoiceOver, TalkBack), voice control (Siri, Google Assistant), captioning, large-text modes, and switch control are **integrated into consumer products**.
- The result: a person who is blind can buy the same iPhone as a sighted person, and it works *out of the box*.

**Tier 3 — The Ceiling (Still essential, still needed):**
- For users with very severe or specific disabilities, **specialized AT remains irreplaceable**.
- Raised-floor features do *not* eliminate the ceiling; they shift the **distribution of effort** so that ceiling-level AT is reserved for those who truly need it.

### 2.2 Why Raise the Floor? The Underlying Logic

The "Raising the Floor" approach is justified by five structural advantages over specialized-AT-only models:

1. **Economies of Scale** — A single accessibility feature in a billion smartphones reaches a billion users at near-zero marginal cost. Specialized AT serves only niche markets.
2. **Reduced Stigma** — Users avoid devices that visibly mark them as "disabled." Mainstream devices with built-in accessibility features are *invisible* assistive technology.
3. **Faster Innovation Cycle** — Mainstream tech refreshes every 12–24 months; specialized AT often takes 5–10 years.
4. **Training and Support Infrastructure** — Mainstream devices come with retail support, YouTube tutorials, and peer communities; specialized AT often lacks these.
5. **Empowerment and Dignity** — Independence is preserved; users are not segregated into a separate "disability tech" ecosystem.

### 2.3 The "Why" and "How" — Operative Reasoning

| Step | Why It Works | How It Is Implemented |
|------|-------------|----------------------|
| **Identify the disability barrier** | Each disability (visual, auditory, motor, cognitive) maps to specific interaction failures (small text, audio-only cues, fine motor dependency, complex menus). | Conduct inclusive design research; involve users with disabilities in design teams. |
| **Design the inclusive baseline** | Many accessibility features (large text, high contrast, captions) benefit *all* users, not only those with disabilities (curb-cut effect). | Apply the **7 Principles of Universal Design** (Ron Mace). |
| **Embed into mainstream OS** | OS-level integration (iOS, Android, Windows) ensures reach and automatic updates. | Native APIs: UIAccessibility (iOS), AccessibilityService (Android), UI Automation (Windows). |
| **Layer ceiling-level AT on top** | Specialized AT still serves severe cases that raised-floor features cannot address. | Compatibility with switches, eye trackers, AAC devices. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Takeaway:** Raising the Floor does **NOT replace** specialized AT — it **complements and de-stigmatises** it. A correct exam answer must mention *both* that the floor is raised *and* that specialized ceiling-level devices remain necessary.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Framework Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Since "Raising the Floor" is a *conceptual* topic, the formula sheet below is presented as a **principles and metrics framework** — exactly how KTU 2024 Scheme valuation expects this content to be tabulated.

| Framework Element | Symbol / Metric | Description | Unit / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Floor Accessibility Index (FAI)** | $FAI$ | Proportion of mainstream product features usable without add-on AT | Dimensionless ratio, $0 \leq FAI \leq 1$ |
| **Coverage Gap** | $CG = 1 - FAI$ | Fraction of features still requiring specialized AT | Dimensionless ratio |
| **Adoption Multiplier** | $AM = \frac{N_{mainstream}}{N_{specialized}}$ | Ratio of users reached by mainstream vs. specialized AT | Pure number, $AM \gg 1$ |
| **Cost Reduction Factor** | $CRF = \frac{C_{specialized}}{C_{mainstream}}$ | How much cheaper mainstream-with-AT is than specialized AT | Pure number, $CRF \gg 1$ |
| **Latency Ratio** | $LR = \frac{T_{specialized}}{T_{mainstream}}$ | Time lag between mainstream release and equivalent specialized AT | Pure number, $LR \in [3, 15]$ typically |
| **Stigma Rejection Rate** | $SR$ | Fraction of users refusing to use visibly assistive devices | Dimensionless ratio, $SR \leq 0.30$ empirical |
| **Universal Design Principle Count** | $N_{UD}$ | Number of Ron Mace's 7 UD principles applied | Integer, $0 \leq N_{UD} \leq 7$ |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The "Raising the Floor" framework is the **design philosophy behind every modern consumer operating system and rehabilitation technology trend**:

- **iOS / iPadOS:** VoiceOver (screen reader), Magnifier, Sound Recognition, AssistiveTouch, Switch Control — all built-in.
- **Android:** TalkBack, Live Caption, Select to Speak, Voice Access.
- **Windows:** Narrator, Magnifier, Eye Control, Live Captions.
- **Wearables:** Apple Watch fall detection, fall detection on Pixel Watch, hearing-aid streaming protocols (ASHA, LE Audio).
- **Rehabilitation Engineering:** Modern prosthetic control apps (e.g., Ottobock, COAPT), AAC apps (Proloquo2Go, TouchChat) all run on consumer iPads — a textbook case of raised-floor assistive technology.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation — The "Floor Coverage" Computation

Although this topic is conceptual, KTU 2024 Scheme examiners may frame it as a **quantitative reasoning problem**. Below is a complete worked example showing how the *Coverage Gap* and *Adoption Multiplier* are computed.

> **Problem Statement (KTU-style):** A smartphone operating system ships with 120 user-facing features. Of these, 85 are usable by a person with low vision *without* any specialized add-on hardware. The remaining 35 require an external magnifier, screen reader dongle, or Braille display. A specialized low-vision device currently in the market supports all 120 features but costs ₹1,50,000 and is used by 8,000 people in India. The mainstream smartphone with built-in accessibility is sold to 40,000,000 users in India. Compute the **Coverage Gap (CG)**, **Adoption Multiplier (AM)**, and **Cost Reduction Factor (CRF)** for a low-vision user, assuming the smartphone costs ₹70,000.

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify Given Values**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{total} &= 120 \quad \text{(total features)} \\
F_{accessible} &= 85 \quad \text{(features usable without specialized AT)} \\
F_{gap} &= 35 \quad \text{(features still requiring specialized AT)} \\
N_{mainstream} &= 40{,}000{,}000 \quad \text{(mainstream users)} \\
N_{specialized} &= 8{,}000 \quad \text{(specialized AT users)} \\
C_{mainstream} &= ₹70{,}000 \\
C_{specialized} &= ₹1{,}50{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Floor Accessibility Index (FAI)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
FAI &= \frac{F_{accessible}}{F_{total}} \\
    &= \frac{85}{120} \\
    &= 0.7083
\end{aligned}
$$

So **70.83%** of the smartphone's features are usable by a low-vision user without specialized add-ons. This is the **raised floor** value.

**Step 3 — Compute the Coverage Gap (CG)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CG &= 1 - FAI \\
   &= 1 - 0.7083 \\
   &= 0.2917
\end{aligned}
$$

Interpretation: **29.17%** of features still need ceiling-level AT. This represents the *unmet* accessibility — the residual gap that the raised-floor strategy has not closed.

**Step 4 — Compute the Adoption Multiplier (AM)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
AM &= \frac{N_{mainstream}}{N_{specialized}} \\
   &= \frac{40{,}000{,}000}{8{,}000} \\
   &= 5{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

The mainstream smartphone reaches **5,000 times** more low-vision users than the specialized device. This is the **scale advantage** of the raised-floor model.

**Step 5 — Compute the Cost Reduction Factor (CRF)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CRF &= \frac{C_{specialized}}{C_{mainstream}} \\
    &= \frac{1{,}50{,}000}{70{,}000} \\
    &= 2.143
\end{aligned}
$$

The mainstream device is **~2.14× cheaper** than the specialized equivalent. (In many real-world scenarios, this ratio is far higher, since the mainstream device is multifunctional.)

**Step 6 — Synthesis Answer**

The "Raising the Floor" approach in this scenario yields:
- $FAI = 0.7083$ (70.83% of features accessible by default),
- $CG = 0.2917$ (29.17% residual gap, addressable by ceiling-level AT),
- $AM = 5{,}000$ (5,000× user reach),
- $CRF = 2.143$ (2.14× cost reduction).

This is the **quantitative justification** for the Raised Floor strategy.

### 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Raised Floor vs. Specialized AT

| Dimension | Raised-Floor (Mainstream) AT | Specialized (Ceiling) AT |
|---|---|---|
| **Cost per User** | Low (₹10K – ₹1L typical) | High (₹1L – ₹10L+ typical) |
| **User Base** | 10⁶ – 10⁹ | 10³ – 10⁵ |
| **Innovation Cycle** | 12–24 months | 5–10 years |
| **Social Visibility / Stigma** | Low (invisible features) | High (visible devices) |
| **Customisation Depth** | Limited to OS-level options | Very high, task-specific |
| **Repair & Support** | Retail network, global | Niche vendors, slow |
| **Best Use Case** | Mild–moderate disability, broad use | Severe / specific disability |
| **Failure Mode if Absent** | Many users excluded by default | Reduced dignity / independence |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Valuation Insight:** A high-scoring answer on this topic must explicitly contrast the *two* models and identify *when* ceiling-level AT is still required. Vague generalities score low; quantitative or comparative specificity scores high.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — The "Floor, Stairs, Ceiling" Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["USER POPULATION"] --> B["Can use anything mainstream"]
    A --> C["Need some accommodation"]
    A --> D["Need specialized AT"]

    B --> B1["RAISED FLOOR: Built-in mainstream accessibility"]
    B1 --> B1a["Large text / High contrast"]
    B1 --> B1b["Voice assistants"]
    B1 --> B1c["Closed captions"]
    B1 --> B1d["Touch gestures"]

    C --> C1["STAIRS: Add-on mainstream features"]
    C1 --> C1a["Third-party apps"]
    C1 --> C1b["Custom shortcuts"]
    C1 --> C1c["Switch control"]

    D --> D1["CEILING: Specialized AT devices"]
    D1 --> D1a["Eye-gaze trackers"]
    D1 --> D1b["Refreshable Braille displays"]
    D1 --> D1c["Sip-and-puff switches"]
    D1 --> D1d["Custom AAC devices"]

    B1 --> E["INDEPENDENT ACCESS FOR ALL"]
    C1 --> E
    D1 --> E

    style A fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#2d5a87,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#5a87b8,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#87b8d4,stroke:#000000
    style B1 fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    style C1 fill:#f9a825,stroke:#000000
    style D1 fill:#c62828,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Process Flow — How a "Floor" is Raised

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["STEP 1: Identify disability barrier"] --> P2["STEP 2: Apply Universal Design principles"]
    P2 --> P3["STEP 3: Embed accessibility into mainstream OS"]
    P3 --> P4["STEP 4: Test with users having disabilities"]
    P4 --> P5["STEP 5: Release as default feature"]
    P5 --> P6["STEP 6: Provide ceiling-level AT for residual gaps"]
    P6 --> P7["OUTCOME: Raised floor achieved"]

    style P1 fill:#1565c0,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    style P2 fill:#1976d2,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    style P3 fill:#1e88e5,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    style P4 fill:#2196f3,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    style P5 fill:#42a5f5,stroke:#000000
    style P6 fill:#64b5f6,stroke:#000000
    style P7 fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Architecture — Accessibility Feature Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    U["USER WITH DISABILITY"] --> UI["USER INTERFACE LAYER"]
    UI --> OS["OPERATING SYSTEM LAYER"]
    OS --> HW["HARDWARE LAYER"]

    UI --> F1["Screen reader"]
    UI --> F2["Voice input"]
    UI --> F3["Caption display"]
    UI --> F4["Magnification"]

    OS --> F5["Accessibility APIs"]
    OS --> F6["Switch control drivers"]
    OS --> F7["Text-to-speech engine"]

    HW --> F8["Haptic motors"]
    HW --> F9["High-resolution displays"]
    HW --> F10["Multi-mic arrays"]

    F1 --> OUT["ACCESSIBLE EXPERIENCE"]
    F2 --> OUT
    F3 --> OUT
    F4 --> OUT
    F5 --> OUT
    F6 --> OUT
    F7 --> OUT
    F8 --> OUT
    F9 --> OUT
    F10 --> OUT

    style U fill:#4a148c,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    style UI fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    style OS fill:#ad1457,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    style HW fill:#c62828,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    style OUT fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams above map the **functional architecture** of raised-floor AT delivery. The hardware → OS → UI → user stack ensures that accessibility is *systemic*, not *peripheral*. This is the same architecture found in iOS, Android, and Windows accessibility subsystems.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**
*Define the concept of "Raising the Floor" in Assistive Technology. Give two real-world examples.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> "Raising the Floor" is an AT design philosophy in which accessibility features are **embedded into mainstream, mass-market products** so that people with disabilities can use them without specialized add-on devices. It raises the *baseline* usability for all users.
> 
> **Example 1:** Curb cuts on sidewalks — originally built for wheelchair users, now used by parents with strollers, cyclists, and delivery carts. (1.5 marks)
> 
> **Example 2:** Built-in screen readers (VoiceOver on iPhone, TalkBack on Android) — a blind person can use a standard smartphone straight out of the box. (1 mark)
> 
> **Key phrase to include:** *"The floor of accessibility is raised so that the lowest common denominator of usability is no longer exclusionary."* (0.5 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]**
*Differentiate between the "Floor," "Stairs," and "Ceiling" levels in Vanderheiden's accessibility framework with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Level | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Floor** | Built-in mainstream features usable by almost everyone. | Large-text mode in iOS. (1 mark) |
| **Stairs** | Mainstream features requiring some accommodation or learning. | Customisable switch control mapping. (1 mark) |
| **Ceiling** | Specialized AT for severe/specific disabilities. | Refreshable Braille display. (1 mark) |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, with sub-parts typically **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students answer **one** of two alternatives.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **"Raising the Floor" concept** in Assistive Technology. Discuss its origins, objectives, and the Vanderheiden "Floor–Stairs–Ceiling" framework with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a **block diagram**, describe how a modern smartphone operating system implements the raised-floor accessibility strategy. List at least **six built-in accessibility features** and explain how each benefits users with different disabilities. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Answer

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** Raising the Floor is the design and policy strategy of embedding accessibility directly into mainstream, mass-market products and services so that the default usability level for people with disabilities is raised without requiring separate, specialized assistive devices.

2. **Origin and Context (1 mark):** The concept emerged in the 1990s and was formalised by Gregg Vanderheiden at the TRACE Center, University of Wisconsin–Madison, as part of a critique of the *exclusively-specialized* AT model. The shift was accelerated by the **Americans with Disabilities Act (1990)**, the **UN CRPD (2006)**, and the rise of consumer smartphones in the late 2000s.

3. **Three-Tier Framework (3 marks):**
   - **Floor:** Built-in mainstream features. Examples: closed captions, large-text mode, voice assistants, high-contrast themes.
   - **Stairs:** Add-on features / third-party apps. Examples: gesture customisation, third-party screen-magnifier apps, switch remapping.
   - **Ceiling:** Specialized AT for severe needs. Examples: eye-gaze trackers, sip-and-puff switches, dedicated AAC devices.

4. **Objectives (1 mark):** Reduce cost, reduce stigma, accelerate innovation, expand reach, preserve user dignity and independence.

5. **Real-World Significance (1 mark):** The curb-cut effect, smartphone screen readers, and the "Tap to Pay" contactless interfaces all demonstrate the strategy's reach.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Block Diagram Description (3 marks):**

```
USER → USER INTERFACE LAYER (touch, voice, gestures)
     → OPERATING SYSTEM (iOS / Android)
     → HARDWARE LAYER (display, mic, haptics, camera)
     → ACCESSIBILITY APIs (UIAccessibility, AccessibilityService)
     → ACCESSIBLE OUTPUT (screen reader audio, captions, magnification)
```

**Six Built-in Features (3 marks, 0.5 each):**

1. **Screen Reader (VoiceOver / TalkBack):** Benefits users with visual impairment.
2. **Magnifier:** Benefits users with low vision.
3. **Live Captions:** Benefits users with hearing impairment.
4. **Voice Control (Siri / Voice Access):** Benefits users with motor impairment.
5. **AssistiveTouch:** Benefits users with limited dexterity.
6. **Sound Recognition:** Benefits users with hearing impairment (alerts to doorbell, alarm, baby cry).

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The smartphone OS architecture is a textbook example of the raised-floor approach — a single mainstream device, sold to over a billion users, simultaneously serves as an AT platform for many disability categories.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Actual Past Paper Style]**

**(a)** Discuss the **advantages of the "Raising the Floor" approach** over the traditional specialized-AT model. Provide **at least four** distinct advantages with quantitative justification wherever possible. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Critically evaluate the **limitations of the Raised-Floor strategy**. Under what conditions is specialized (ceiling-level) AT still indispensable? Provide **three specific clinical or user scenarios**. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Answer

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

1. **Cost Reduction (2 marks):** Specialized AT costs ₹1L–₹10L; mainstream devices with built-in accessibility cost ₹10K–₹1L. The cost reduction factor $CRF$ is typically 2×–10×, sometimes higher.

2. **Mass Adoption / Scale (2 marks):** Adoption multiplier $AM$ is typically $10^3$ to $10^5$ — billions of users can access AT without ever buying a specialized device.

3. **Reduced Stigma (1 mark):** Built-in features are *invisible*. Stigma rejection rate $SR$ for specialized devices is empirically ≤ 0.30; raised-floor features have near-zero rejection.

4. **Faster Innovation (1 mark):** Latency ratio $LR$ is 3–15 in favour of mainstream devices. New features like Live Captions ship in months, not years.

5. **Repair and Support Network (1 mark):** Mainstream devices have global retail and online support; specialized AT often has none.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Limitations (4 marks):**

1. **Residual Coverage Gap:** Even with raised-floor features, $CG$ is rarely 0. Severe disabilities leave 20–40% gaps that mainstream features cannot close.
2. **Lack of Customisation:** OS-level accessibility is one-size-fits-all; specialized AT allows task-specific tuning.
3. **Battery and Processing Constraints:** Continuous screen readers, eye-tracking, and live captioning drain battery and require specific hardware.
4. **Digital Divide:** Mainstream devices require digital literacy, internet access, and electricity — all of which are barriers in low-resource settings.

**Three Clinical Scenarios Where Ceiling-Level AT Is Indispensable (3 marks, 1 each):**

1. **Locked-In Syndrome (e.g., post-stroke):** A patient with no voluntary movement except eye blinks requires an **eye-gaze AAC device** — a smartphone screen reader is insufficient because the user cannot reliably touch the screen.
2. **Total Blindness with Deafness (Deaf-Blind):** A deaf-blind user requires a **refreshable Braille display with tactile signing input** — no mainstream device currently supports this dual sensory substitution.
3. **Severe Speech Impairment (e.g., ALS in late stage):** The user needs a **dedicated AAC device with predictive eye-typing** calibrated to their specific gaze pattern — generic voice assistants cannot meet the throughput and accuracy needed for communication.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT claim** that raised-floor AT eliminates the need for specialized AT. Marks will be docked for absolutist language like "specialized AT is obsolete."
> 2. **Do NOT confuse** "Raising the Floor" with "Universal Design" — they overlap, but UD is a *design principle*, while Raising the Floor is a *deployment strategy* that may use UD as one of its tools.
> 3. **Always cite specific OS features** (VoiceOver, TalkBack, Live Captions, Switch Control) instead of vague phrases like "smartphone accessibility." Naming features earns full marks.
> 4. **In numerical problems, show the substitution step clearly** — writing only the final answer without intermediate algebra loses 50% of the allotted marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — Read Twice Before Exam:**

- [ ] **Raising the Floor** = *embedding* accessibility into mainstream products; *not* replacing specialized AT.
- [ ] Framework is **Vanderheiden's Floor–Stairs–Ceiling** model.
- [ ] **Floor examples:** VoiceOver, TalkBack, Live Captions, Magnifier, Voice Control, AssistiveTouch, high-contrast mode, closed captions.
- [ ] **Stairs examples:** Switch control remapping, third-party accessibility apps, custom keyboard shortcuts.
- [ ] **Ceiling examples:** Eye-gaze trackers, refreshable Braille displays, sip-and-puff switches, dedicated AAC devices.
- [ ] **Five advantages:** Cost, scale, stigma reduction, innovation speed, support infrastructure.
- [ ] **Three limitations:** Coverage gap $CG$, customisation deficit, hardware/digital-divide constraints.
- [ ] **Curb-cut effect** is the classic analogy — accessibility for disabled users ends up benefiting *all* users.
- [ ] **Universal Design (Ron Mace, 7 principles)** is a *tool* used by the Raised-Floor strategy, not a synonym.
- [ ] **Quantitative metrics** to memorise:
   - $FAI = \frac{F_{accessible}}{F_{total}}$
   - $CG = 1 - FAI$
   - $AM = \frac{N_{mainstream}}{N_{specialized}}$
   - $CRF = \frac{C_{specialized}}{C_{mainstream}}$
   - $LR = \frac{T_{specialized}}{T_{mainstream}}$
- [ ] **Disability prevalence (WHO):** ~**15%** of global population.
- [ ] Specialized AT is **still essential** for severe cases (locked-in syndrome, deaf-blind, advanced ALS, severe cerebral palsy).
- [ ] Exam-friendly phrasing: *"The floor is raised so the default state of the world is inclusive, while the ceiling remains for those who need specialised equipment."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cross-Platform Accessibility - Automatic Speech Recognition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT614 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cross-Platform Accessibility - Automatic Speech Recognition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR)** is a cross-disciplinary sub-field of computational linguistics and electrical engineering that develops methodologies and technologies enabling the **recognition, transcription, and translation of spoken language into machine-readable text or executable commands** by computers, without requiring manual human transcription.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme – OEBRT614 (Assistive Medical Devices)**, ASR is classified as a **Human-Computer Interaction (HCI)** modality that enables hands-free, eyes-free operation of biomedical equipment. It is a critical accessibility technology for patients with **motor disabilities, visual impairments, paralysis, or age-related dexterity loss**.

The mathematical objective of ASR is to find the most probable word sequence $\hat{W}$ given an observed acoustic signal $X$:

$$\hat{W} = \arg\max_{W} P(W \mid X)$$

By applying **Bayes' Theorem**, this is reformulated as:

$$\hat{W} = \arg\max_{W} \frac{P(X \mid W) \cdot P(W)}{P(X)}$$

Where:
- $P(X \mid W)$ = **Acoustic Model** probability (probability of the sound given the word).
- $P(W)$ = **Language Model** probability (probability of the word sequence in a given language).
- $P(X)$ = Evidence (constant for a given input, hence ignored in maximization).

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-Platform Accessibility** in ASR means the recognition engine can be deployed seamlessly across **hardware platforms** (Windows, macOS, Linux, Android, iOS, embedded medical-grade processors) and **input modalities** (microphone arrays, headsets, telephone channels, far-field microphones) while maintaining a consistent **Word Error Rate (WER)** below clinical usability thresholds.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition — "The Courtroom Stenographer" Analogy**
>
> Imagine a highly skilled court stenographer sitting in a noisy courtroom. They listen to a witness speaking in English (the acoustic signal), consult their knowledge of the English language and grammar (language model), and produce a written transcript of what was said (output text). The stenographer also has an "ear for accents" and filters out background noise (signal preprocessing).
>
> An ASR system works exactly like this stenographer, but with millions of statistical rules instead of one human ear. In a medical context, imagine a **quadriplegic patient** controlling their powered wheelchair, adjusting their hospital bed, calling a nurse, or dictating clinical notes — **all using their voice alone**. The ASR engine acts as the "digital stenographer" converting breath and voice into action.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics in ASR

- **Word Error Rate (WER):** Industry-standard accuracy metric (lower is better).
- **Word Accuracy:** $1 - \text{WER}$ (expressed as a percentage).
- **Real-Time Factor (RTF):** Ratio of processing time to audio duration; **RTF < 1.0** is required for real-time clinical use.
- **Latency:** End-to-end delay from speech end to text output, ideally **< 300 ms**.
- **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR):** Robustness measure in noisy hospital environments.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Architecture of an ASR Pipeline

A modern ASR system executes the following sequential stages:

1. **Audio Capture & Preprocessing**
   - The raw analog microphone signal is sampled (typically at **16 kHz** or **44.1 kHz**).
   - Pre-emphasis filtering is applied to boost high-frequency energy that is typically attenuated in human speech.
   - Noise reduction algorithms (e.g., spectral subtraction, Wiener filtering) suppress ambient hospital noise (ventilator beeps, IV pumps, conversations).

2. **Feature Extraction**
   - The audio is segmented into overlapping frames of **25 ms** with a **10 ms** shift.
   - Each frame is transformed into a compact numerical vector using **Mel-Frequency Cepstral Coefficients (MFCCs)** or **Filter Bank Energies (FBEs)**.
   - These features mimic the human cochlea's non-linear frequency resolution.

3. **Acoustic Modeling**
   - Maps acoustic features to **phonetic units** (smallest distinct sound units, e.g., /k/, /æ/, /t/ in "cat").
   - Traditional: **Hidden Markov Models (HMM)** combined with **Gaussian Mixture Models (GMM)**.
   - Modern: **Deep Neural Networks (DNN)**, **Recurrent Neural Networks (RNN)**, **Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)**, or **Conformer/Transformer** architectures.

4. **Language Modeling**
   - Assigns probabilities to sequences of words.
   - **Statistical N-gram models**: $P(w_i \mid w_{i-1}, w_{i-2}, ..., w_{i-n+1})$.
   - **Neural Language Models**: RNN-LM, Transformer-based LMs.
   - In medical ASR, domain-specific lexicons (e.g., "metformin", "tachycardia") are injected.

5. **Decoder / Search Algorithm**
   - Combines acoustic and language model scores using the **Viterbi algorithm** (a dynamic programming beam search).
   - Outputs the single most probable transcription or an N-best lattice of candidates.

6. **Post-Processing**
   - Punctuation restoration, capitalization, inverse text normalization ("twenty mg" → "20 mg"), and command interpretation.

### 2.2 Why Each Step Matters — The Engineering Rationale

> [!NOTE]
> **The 'Why' Behind the Pipeline**
> - **Preprocessing** is essential because hospital ICUs have **SNR often below 5 dB** — raw audio is unintelligible to any recognizer.
> - **MFCCs** compress thousands of audio samples into ~13 coefficients per frame, reducing computational load by 95%.
> - **HMMs** were historically favored because speech is a **time-varying stochastic process** with well-defined statistical behavior over short stationary segments.
> - **Language Models** resolve acoustic ambiguity — "two patients" vs. "to patients" vs. "too patience" are acoustically similar but contextually distinct.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **complete mathematical toolkit** for ASR-related examination problems in the OEBRT614 syllabus.

| **Formula / Concept** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Units / Boundary** | **Engineering Utility** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bayes' Decision Rule (ASR Core) | $\hat{W} = \arg\max_{W} P(X \mid W) \cdot P(W)$ | Probabilities are dimensionless $[0,1]$ | Foundation of statistical ASR decoders |
| Word Error Rate (WER) | $\text{WER} = \frac{S + D + I}{N} \times 100\%$ | $S$=Substitutions, $D$=Deletions, $I$=Insertions, $N$=Reference words | Clinical-grade ASR requires **WER < 5%** |
| Signal-to-Noise Ratio | $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}}\right)$ | Decibels (dB); $\geq 15$ dB for robust ASR | Hospital ICU baseline assessment |
| Real-Time Factor | $\text{RTF} = \frac{T_{\text{processing}}}{T_{\text{audio}}}$ | Dimensionless; $\text{RTF} < 1.0$ for real-time | Determines bedside usability |
| Pre-emphasis Filter | $y[n] = x[n] - \alpha \cdot x[n-1]$ | $\alpha \in [0.95, 0.97]$ typically | Boosts high-frequency formants |
| Frame Energy | $E = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x^2[n]$ | Joules / sample units | Voice Activity Detection (VAD) |
| MFCC (Conceptual) | $c_k = \text{DCT}\!\left(\log \vert \text{MelFilter}( \vert X(f) \vert ) \vert\right)$ | $k = 1, 2, \dots, 12$ coefficients | Spectral compact representation |
| HMM Likelihood (Forward Algorithm) | $P(X \mid \lambda) = \sum_{q} \pi_{q_0} \prod_{t=1}^{T} a_{q_{t-1}, q_t} \cdot b_{q_t}(x_t)$ | $\lambda$ = HMM parameters | Acoustic scoring in classical ASR |
| Shannon Entropy (LM perplexity) | $H = -\sum_{w} p(w) \log_2 p(w)$ | Bits per word | Language model strength indicator |

> [!WARNING]
> When writing WER expressions in the exam, **never write** `|x|` for absolute value inside a markdown table — use the **LaTeX vertical bar syntax** `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` to avoid breaking table formatting.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Medical Engineering

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** perspective, ASR is the cornerstone of:
- **Voice-controlled wheelchairs** for patients with **ALS, spinal cord injury, or cerebral palsy**.
- **Hands-free electronic health record (EHR) documentation** reducing physician burnout.
- **Emergency call systems** in geriatric care where patients cannot press a button.
- **Surgical robotics** where sterile hands cannot touch a console.
- **Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC)** devices for non-verbal patients (e.g., post-stroke aphasia).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: The ASR Bayes' Framework

The fundamental problem in ASR is the **inverse problem** — given a noisy acoustic observation, recover the most likely word sequence. Let us derive this rigorously.

**Step 1 — Statement of the Recognition Problem**

Let $X = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_T)$ be the observed sequence of acoustic feature vectors (e.g., MFCCs) of length $T$ frames, sampled at 100 frames/second (since frame shift is 10 ms).

Let $W = (w_1, w_2, \dots, w_N)$ be a candidate word sequence of length $N$ words drawn from vocabulary $\mathcal{V}$.

We seek:

$$
\hat{W} = \arg\max_{W \in \mathcal{V}^*} P(W \mid X)
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Bayes' Theorem**

By the definition of conditional probability:

$$
P(W \mid X) = \frac{P(X \mid W) \cdot P(W)}{P(X)}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplification via the Maximization Operation**

Since $P(X)$ does not depend on $W$, it is a **constant for a given input** and does not affect the argument of the maximum. We can safely drop it:

$$
\hat{W} = \arg\max_{W} P(X \mid W) \cdot P(W)
$$

**Step 4 — Take the Logarithm (Log-Domain Arithmetic)**

Floating-point underflow is a serious concern when multiplying many small probabilities (e.g., 100 frames × 0.001 each = $10^{-300}$). Taking the natural logarithm is a **monotonic transformation** that preserves the argmax:

$$
\hat{W} = \arg\max_{W} \left[ \log P(X \mid W) + \log P(W) \right]
$$

**Step 5 — Acoustic Model Decomposition**

Assuming conditional independence of frames (Markov assumption):

$$
\log P(X \mid W) = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \log P(x_t \mid w_i, \theta_{\text{AM}})
$$

Where $\theta_{\text{AM}}$ represents the trained acoustic model parameters.

**Step 6 — Language Model Decomposition (Bigram Truncation)**

For computational tractability, the language model is truncated to the previous $n-1$ words. For a bigram ($n=2$):

$$
\log P(W) \approx \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P(w_i \mid w_{i-1})
$$

**Step 7 — Weighted Combination (Decoder Score)**

The acoustic and language scores operate on different scales, so a tunable **language model weight** $\alpha$ and **insertion penalty** $\beta$ are introduced:

$$
\text{Score}(W) = \alpha \cdot \sum_{t=1}^{T} \log P(x_t \mid w_i) + \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) + \beta \cdot N
$$

**Final Recognition Rule:**

$$
\boxed{\;\hat{W} = \arg\max_{W} \left[ \alpha \cdot \log P_{\text{AM}}(X \mid W) + \log P_{\text{LM}}(W) + \beta \cdot \vert W \vert \right]\;}
$$

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Word Error Rate (WER)

The WER is computed using the **Levenshtein (Edit) Distance** algorithm at the word level.

**Step 1 — Define the Substitution, Deletion, and Insertion Counts**
- $S$ = number of words **substituted** (recognized as wrong word).
- $D$ = number of words **deleted** (missing from hypothesis).
- $I$ = number of words **inserted** (extra in hypothesis).
- $N$ = total words in the **reference (ground truth)** transcription.

**Step 2 — Dynamic Programming Recursion**

Let $D[i][j]$ be the edit distance between the first $i$ reference words and the first $j$ hypothesis words:

$$
D[i][j] = \min \begin{cases}
D[i-1][j] + 1 & \text{(deletion from reference)} \\
D[i][j-1] + 1 & \text{(insertion into hypothesis)} \\
D[i-1][j-1] + c(i,j) & \text{(match or substitution)}
\end{cases}
$$

Where $c(i,j) = 0$ if $w_i^{\text{ref}} = w_j^{\text{hyp}}$, else $c(i,j) = 1$.

**Step 3 — Final WER Formula**

$$
\boxed{\;\text{WER}(\%) = \frac{S + D + I}{N} \times 100\%\;}
$$

**Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example**

Suppose the reference is `"administer ten milligrams"` (3 words, $N=3$) and the ASR output is `"administer 10 ml"` (3 words).
- "administer" matches → 0 errors.
- "ten" → "10" → counted as **1 substitution**.
- "milligrams" → "ml" → counted as **1 substitution**.

$$
\text{WER} = \frac{1 + 0 + 1}{3} \times 100\% = 66.67\%
$$

This illustrates why **medical ASR systems** must include **inverse text normalization** (writing "10 mg" instead of "ten milligrams") to avoid such errors in clinical documentation.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — ASR Techniques by Generation

| **Generation** | **Acoustic Model** | **LM Type** | **Typical WER (Clean)** | **Medical Use Readiness** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1st (1980s) | HMM + Monophone GMM | N-gram (trigram) | 20–30% | Research only |
| 2nd (2000s) | HMM + Triphone GMM | N-gram + class-based | 10–20% | Limited pilot studies |
| 3rd (2010s) | HMM + DNN (TDNN) | RNN-LM | 5–10% | Clinical documentation |
| 4th (2018+) | End-to-End (CTC, Attention, RNN-T) | Transformer-LM | < 5% | Real-time bedside use |
| 5th (2023+) | Conformer + Whisper-style foundation models | LLM-augmented | 2–4% | Multilingual medical ASR |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Medical ASR System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Patient Voice Input] --> B[Microphone Array]
    B --> C[Analog-to-Digital Converter 16 kHz]
    C --> D[Pre-emphasis Filter alpha = 0.97]
    D --> E[Noise Suppression Wiener / Spectral Subtraction]
    E --> F[Frame Blocking 25 ms window, 10 ms shift]
    F --> G[MFCC Feature Extractor 13 Coefficients]
    G --> H{Acoustic Model Engine}
    H --> H1[HMM GMM Classical]
    H --> H2[Deep Neural Network Modern]
    H --> H3[Conformer Transformer SOTA]
    H1 --> I[Phonetic Probability Stream]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    I --> J[Decoder Viterbi Beam Search]
    K[Medical Language Model] --> J
    L[Pronunciation Lexicon CMUdict] --> J
    J --> M[N-Best Word Lattice]
    M --> N[Inverse Text Normalization]
    N --> O[Punctuation Restoration]
    O --> P[Command Interpreter / Text Output]
    P --> Q1[Wheelchair Motor Controller]
    P --> Q2[Hospital Bed Actuator]
    P --> Q3[Clinical EHR Documentation]
    P --> Q4[Emergency Nurse Call System]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Verification

> [!NOTE]
> The above diagram adheres to all Mermaid v10+ syntax rules:
> - All node IDs are alphanumeric (`A`, `B`, `C`, … `Q4`) and prefixed with letters.
> - No reserved keywords (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`) are used as node IDs.
> - All node labels are **raw, plain uppercase alphanumeric text** without bold, italic, or HTML tags inside the double quotes.
> - The flowchart direction is `TD` (top-down), avoiding syntax ambiguity.

### 4.3 Cross-Platform Deployment Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EdgeDevices[Edge Medical Devices]
        ED1[Wheelchair ECU ARM Cortex]
        ED2[Hospital Bed Controller]
        ED3[Ventilator HMI Panel]
    end
    subgraph MobilePlatforms[Mobile Platforms]
        MP1[Android Phone]
        MP2[iOS Tablet]
    end
    subgraph DesktopPlatforms[Desktop Platforms]
        DP1[Windows Clinician PC]
        DP2[macOS Workstation]
        DP3[Linux Medical Server]
    end
    ASRCore[ASR Core Engine ONNX Runtime] --> ED1
    ASRCore --> ED2
    ASRCore --> ED3
    ASRCore --> MP1
    ASRCore --> MP2
    ASRCore --> DP1
    ASRCore --> DP2
    ASRCore --> DP3
    ModelRepo[Model Repository Cloud / On-Premise] --> ASRCore
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **ONNX (Open Neural Network Exchange)** runtime enables a **single trained ASR model** to be deployed on heterogeneous hardware — from a **Raspberry Pi in a low-cost wheelchair** to a **multi-GPU hospital server** — fulfilling the **cross-platform** requirement of the KTU 2024 syllabus.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**
*Define Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR). List any two applications of ASR in assistive medical devices.*

**Model Answer:**

Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) is the technology that enables a machine to **identify, interpret, and transcribe human speech into text or machine-executable commands** by processing the acoustic signal through statistical or neural models.

**Two applications in assistive medical devices:**
1. **Voice-controlled powered wheelchairs** for patients with quadriplegia or ALS, where the user issues commands like "forward", "stop", or "turn left" to navigate.
2. **Hands-free clinical documentation** in EHR systems, allowing surgeons and physicians to dictate operative notes, prescriptions, and discharge summaries without touching a keyboard, thereby maintaining sterility and reducing burnout.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for two distinct applications)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**
*State the Bayes' rule formulation used in ASR and explain the role of the Acoustic Model and Language Model.*

**Model Answer:**

The Bayes' rule formulation for ASR is:

$$
\hat{W} = \arg\max_{W} P(X \mid W) \cdot P(W)
$$

Where:
- $P(X \mid W)$ is the **Acoustic Model (AM)** — it estimates the probability of observing the acoustic feature sequence $X$ given a hypothesized word sequence $W$. It captures the **phonetic and acoustic variability** of speech (e.g., accents, pitch, background noise).
- $P(W)$ is the **Language Model (LM)** — it estimates the prior probability of a word sequence occurring in a given language. It captures **syntactic and semantic structure** (e.g., "give patient medicine" is far more likely than "give medicine patient").

The decoder combines these two scores in the log domain to find the single most probable word sequence. *(3 marks: 1 for equation + 2 for AM and LM explanation)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the various stages involved in the **Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) pipeline** for an assistive medical device used by a paralytic patient. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the Bayes' decision rule used in ASR, explaining clearly the roles of the acoustic model, language model, and the effect of taking the logarithm on the recognition score. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

The ASR pipeline for a paralytic patient's assistive device consists of the following stages:

| **Stage** | **Function** | **Medical Context Relevance** |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Audio Capture | Microphone captures voice; sampled at 16 kHz, 16-bit | Patient's bedside microphone with far-field capability |
| 2. Pre-emphasis | $y[n] = x[n] - 0.97 \cdot x[n-1]$ | Boosts high-frequency consonants critical for medical terms |
| 3. Noise Suppression | Wiener filter / spectral subtraction | Removes ventilator, monitor alarms, staff conversations |
| 4. Framing | 25 ms window, 10 ms hop | Stationarity assumption for spectral analysis |
| 5. Feature Extraction | MFCC (13 coefficients + delta + delta-delta) | Compact representation of speech spectrum |
| 6. Acoustic Model | DNN/HMM or Conformer | Maps features to phonemes; trained on medical speech |
| 7. Pronunciation Lexicon | ARPAbet / CMUdict mapping | Maps words to phoneme sequences (e.g., "metformin" → /m ɛ t f ɔːr m ɪ n/) |
| 8. Language Model | Medical-domain trigram or neural LM | Predicts likely word sequences in clinical context |
| 9. Decoder | Weighted FSAs with Viterbi beam search | Finds optimal word sequence |
| 10. Post-processing | Inverse text normalization, command binding | Converts "wheelchair forward" to motor controller PWM signals |

*(Marks: 1 for block diagram + 6 for stage-wise explanation, 1 mark each for first 6 stages or proportional distribution.)*

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Problem Statement:** Given acoustic features $X$, find the most probable word sequence $W$.

$$
\hat{W} = \arg\max_{W} P(W \mid X) \quad \text{(1 mark)}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Bayes' Theorem:**

$$
P(W \mid X) = \frac{P(X \mid W) \cdot P(W)}{P(X)} \quad \text{(1 mark)}
$$

**Step 3 — Drop the Evidence Term:** $P(X)$ is independent of $W$, hence:

$$
\hat{W} = \arg\max_{W} P(X \mid W) \cdot P(W) \quad \text{(1 mark)}
$$

**Step 4 — Acoustic Model Role:** $P(X \mid W)$ quantifies how likely the observed spectral features are given a hypothesized word. It is computed by the **Acoustic Model** (HMM-GMM, DNN-HMM, or end-to-end neural network). It captures speaker variability, accents, and acoustic-phonetic mapping. *(1 mark)*

**Step 5 — Language Model Role:** $P(W)$ quantifies the prior probability of a word sequence in the target language. It is computed by the **Language Model** (N-gram or neural). It resolves ambiguity — e.g., "prescribe" vs. "proscribe" — and prevents grammatically absurd outputs. *(1 mark)*

**Step 6 — Log-Domain Transformation:** Since $T$ (frames) is often > 100, direct multiplication of small probabilities causes underflow. Taking the natural logarithm (a monotonic function preserving argmax):

$$
\hat{W} = \arg\max_{W} \left[ \log P(X \mid W) + \log P(W) \right] \quad \text{(1 mark)}
$$

This converts multiplications into additions, enabling efficient **Viterbi decoding** and avoiding floating-point underflow.

**Step 7 — Final Combined Score (with LM weight and word insertion penalty):**

$$
\hat{W} = \arg\max_{W} \left[ \alpha \cdot \log P_{\text{AM}}(X \mid W) + \log P_{\text{LM}}(W) + \beta \cdot \vert W \vert \right] \quad \text{(1 mark)}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Word Error Rate (WER)** in ASR. Compute the WER for the following scenario: A clinician dictates *"schedule MRI for patient tomorrow morning"* (5 words). The ASR system transcribes it as *"schedule MRI for patient in morning"*. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the role of **cross-platform accessibility** in deploying ASR for assistive medical devices. Mention at least four platform categories and the design challenges involved. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Definition of WER:** Word Error Rate is the standard ASR accuracy metric defined as the minimum number of word-level edits (substitutions, deletions, insertions) required to convert the ASR hypothesis into the reference, normalized by the number of reference words. *(2 marks)*

**Step 2 — Formula:**

$$
\text{WER} = \frac{S + D + I}{N} \times 100\%
$$

Where $S$ = substitutions, $D$ = deletions, $I$ = insertions, $N$ = reference word count. *(1 mark)*

**Step 3 — Compare Reference vs. Hypothesis:**

| Reference | Hypothesis | Match/Error |
|---|---|---|
| schedule | schedule | ✓ Match |
| MRI | MRI | ✓ Match |
| for | for | ✓ Match |
| patient | patient | ✓ Match |
| tomorrow | (missing) | ✗ Deletion |
| morning | in | ✗ Substitution |
| (no word) | morning | ✗ Insertion |

Wait — re-read: hypothesis is `"schedule MRI for patient in morning"` (6 words).

- "schedule" → "schedule" : Match
- "MRI" → "MRI" : Match
- "for" → "for" : Match
- "patient" → "patient" : Match
- "tomorrow" → missing : **1 Deletion**
- "morning" → "in morning" : "morning" is present but "in" is extra → **1 Insertion**

So $S = 0$, $D = 1$, $I = 1$, $N = 5$. *(1 mark)*

**Step 4 — Computation:**

$$
\text{WER} = \frac{0 + 1 + 1}{5} \times 100\% = \frac{2}{5} \times 100\% = 40\%
$$

*(1 mark for correct arithmetic + 2 marks for correct identification of S, D, I values.)*

**Conclusion:** A WER of 40% is clinically unacceptable; medical-grade ASR requires WER < 5%.

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**
