Implements sequential scanning — items are highlighted one at a time, and switch-press selects the current item. Works with Bluetooth, USB, or even the camera as a switch.

- **Voice Access:** Allows hands-free control of Android phones using voice commands such as *"Open Settings"*, *"Tap Compose"*, *"Scroll down"*. Uses on-device ASR + intent parser. Helps users with Parkinson's, cerebral palsy, or upper-limb amputation.

#### **Model Solution — Part A(b) — 7 Marks**

**WCAG 2.2 Conformance Levels (3.5 marks):**

- **Level A (Minimum / Essential)** — Basic accessibility; failure to meet creates *barriers* to access.
  - *Phone Example 1:* All non-text content (icons in a banking app) must have `contentDescription` for TalkBack to read.
  - *Phone Example 2:* All functionality must be operable via keyboard/switch (no touch-only interactions).

- **Level AA (Recommended / Legal Standard)** — Required by most accessibility laws (ADA, Section 508, EN 301 549).
  - *Phone Example 1:* Text-to-background contrast ratio ≥ **4.5:1** (news app body text).
  - *Phone Example 2:* Content must remain functional at **200% zoom** (e-commerce site in mobile browser).

- **Level AAA (Enhanced / Aspirational)** — Highest level, often impractical for entire products.
  - *Phone Example 1:* Contrast ratio ≥ **7:1** (educational reading apps for children).
  - *Phone Example 2:* Sign language interpretation for all pre-recorded video (rare on phones but aspirational).

**Real-world Application (3.5 marks — 1 mark per WCAG principle tied to phones):**
- **Perceivable** — Live Captions for deaf users (WCAG 1.2.2).
- **Operable** — Switch Control enables a quadriplegic user to use WhatsApp (WCAG 2.1.1).
- **Understandable** — Guided Access provides single-app mode for users with cognitive disabilities (WCAG 3.3.4).
- **Robust** — Apps must expose semantics via `AccessibilityNodeInfo` to remain compatible with future screen readers (WCAG 4.1.2).

---

### **🔹 Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a suitable block diagram, explain the **operational working of Voice Control / Voice Access** on smartphones. Discuss its significance for users with motor disabilities and mention any **three limitations** of the technology.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the role of **AAC applications** in supporting persons with speech impairments. Explain with examples how a typical AAC app (e.g., Proloquo2Go or Avaz) structures its vocabulary, symbol library, and text-to-speech pipeline.

#### **Model Solution — Question B(a) — 7 Marks**

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    mic[Microphone Input] --> preprocess[Pre-processing - Noise Suppression]
    preprocess --> asr[On-device ASR Engine]
    asr --> parser[Intent Parser]
    parser --> cmd[Structured Command]
    cmd --> a11y[Accessibility Service]
    a11y --> uiEvent[Synthetic UI Event]
    uiEvent --> target[Target App Element]
    target --> feedback[Haptic + Audio Feedback]
    feedback --> userD[User]
```

**Working Steps (3 marks):**
1. User utters a command ("*Open Gmail*").
2. Microphone captures audio → noise suppression removes ambient sound.
3. **On-device ASR** converts speech → text (e.g., *"open gmail"*).
4. **Intent Parser** maps text to a structured command: `Intent(OPEN, target=Gmail)`.
5. Accessibility Service executes → synthetic touch event on app icon.
6. App opens; haptic + audio confirmation returned to user.

**Significance for Motor Disabilities (1 mark):**
- Eliminates need for fine motor control → critical for **Parkinson's, cerebral palsy, muscular dystrophy, spinal cord injury, locked-in syndrome**.
- Hands-free phone operation → emergency calls, medication reminders, smart-home control.

**Three Limitations (1 mark — 0.33 each):**
1. **Background noise sensitivity** — fails in crowded/noisy environments.
2. **Speech variability** — users with dysarthria (e.g., post-stroke) may have low ASR accuracy.
3. **Language/dialect coverage** — on-device models may not support all regional languages equally.
4. (Bonus) **Latency in low-end devices** — slower CPUs may exceed the 300 ms threshold.

#### **Model Solution — Question B(b) — 7 Marks**

**Role of AAC (2 marks):**
AAC apps provide a **voice** to individuals who cannot speak due to conditions such as **cerebral palsy, autism, aphasia, laryngectomy, or motor-neuron disease (ALS/MND)**. On phones/tablets, they convert touch or switch selections into **synthesized speech**, enabling communication with family, caregivers, teachers, and the community.

**Vocabulary Structure (2 marks):**
- **Core Word Lists** — High-frequency, versatile words (*go, want, more, not, I*). Research shows ~85% of daily speech uses ~200–400 core words.
- **Fringe Vocabulary** — Topic-specific nouns and verbs (*stethoscope, ambulance, hospital*).
- **Categorical Boards** — Words grouped by topic (*Food, Feelings, Health, School*).
- **Motor-planning Considerations** — Word location remains consistent across screens to build muscle memory and reduce cognitive load.

**Symbol Library (1.5 marks):**
- Apps use standardized symbol sets:
  - **PCS (Picture Communication Symbols)** — used in Proloquo2Go.
  - **SymbolStix** — used in TouchChat, Avaz.
- Symbols are **color-coded by part of speech** (e.g., verbs in green, nouns in orange) — supports **grammatical cognition**.

**Text-to-Speech Pipeline (1.5 marks):**
1. User taps a symbol → app looks up the associated word in the vocabulary DB.
2. Word is inserted into a **sentence-strip** (with ML-based word prediction).
3. TTS engine (e.g., **Acapela, Apple Neural TTS, Google Speech**) renders the sentence as audio.
4. Audio played through speaker/headphones; user receives confirmation haptic.
5. Sentence cleared; conversation continues.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks on "Access to Phones and Tablets" Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing screen reader with magnifier** — they are different. Screen reader = TTS-based semantic reader; Magnifier = visual zoom. Mixing them loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Not naming specific apps/standards** — Generic answers like *"phones help disabled people"* score zero. Always cite **WCAG 2.2, VoiceOver, TalkBack, Proloquo2Go, EN 301 549** by name.
> 3. **Skipping the WCAG levels (A / AA / AAA)** — KTU 14-mark questions on accessibility *expect* the three conformance levels with examples. Forgetting AAA loses marks.
> 4. **No block diagram in 7-mark sub-parts** — A labelled Mermaid or hand-drawn block diagram is **mandatory** for full marks.
> 5. **Forgetting privacy/regulatory aspect** — AT data is **medical data**. Always mention HIPAA/GDPR when discussing cloud-based apps like CoughDrop.
> 6. **No mention of "Universal Design" or "Primary Stakeholder = User with Disability"** — examiners reward the *ethics-aware* framing of accessibility.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 📱 **Phone/Tablet accessibility** = OS services + assistive apps + hardware adaptations enabling **equitable digital access** for PWDs.
- 🗣️ **Screen Readers:** VoiceOver (iOS), TalkBack (Android); both traverse an **accessibility tree** and read content via **TTS**.
- 🎙️ **Voice Control** (iOS) and **Voice Access** (Android) use **on-device ASR + intent parsing** for hands-free operation; latency target **< 300 ms**.
- 🔘 **Switch Control** uses **sequential scanning** + single/dual switches for users with severe motor impairments (ALS, quadriplegia).
- 🗨️ **AAC Apps** (Proloquo2Go, Avaz, TouchChat, CoughDrop) use **symbol libraries + TTS** to give voice to users with speech impairments.
- 👁️ **Magnifier** and **Live Captions** are **built-in** low-vision and deaf-accessibility tools.
- 📐 **WCAG 2.2 levels** — **A** (essential), **AA** (legal standard, contrast 4.5:1, 200% zoom), **AAA** (aspirational, 7:1 contrast).
- 🧩 **Universal Design (Ron Mace, 7 principles)** — Equitable, Flexible, Simple, Perceptible, Error-tolerant, Low-effort, Size-friendly.
- 🩺 **Privacy:** AT is **medical data** → HIPAA/GDPR compliance is mandatory for cloud-synced apps.
- 🤝 **Primary stakeholder** = the **end-user with disability**, not the caregiver or clinician.
- 🧠 **On-device processing** is preferred for **latency, privacy, and offline usability** in assistive technology.
- 🌐 **Standards to memorize:** **WCAG 2.2, Section 508, EN 301 549, W3C ARIA, ISO 9241-171** (ergonomics of human–system interaction).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Assistive Technologies for Seating and Positioning: Biomechanical Principles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBRT614 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Assistive Technologies for Seating and Positioning: Biomechanical Principles # MODULE 3: ASSISTIVE TECHNOLOGIES FOR SEATING AND POSITIONING ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Biomechanical Principles of Seating and Positioning** refer to the application of classical mechanics, statics, dynamics, and human anthropometry to the design, prescription, and clinical evaluation of assistive seating systems (wheelchairs, posture chairs, cushions, and supports). The discipline aims to optimize the **interface pressure distribution**, maintain **spinal and pelvic neutrality**, and ensure **postural stability** for individuals with impaired motor control, neuromuscular disorders, spinal cord injuries, or geriatric frailty. The subject unifies three classical engineering branches with rehabilitation science: | Branch | Contribution to Seating | |---|---| | **Engineering Statics** | Force vectors, moments, base of support, center of gravity | | **Material Mechanics** | Viscoelastic cushion response, stress–strain, hysteresis | | **Kinesiology / Anatomy** | Pelvic tilt, lumbar lordosis, ischial tuberosity load bearing | ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Trunk-on-Tripod" Model > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a tall flagpole (your spine) mounted on a three-legged stool (your pelvis). The stool's legs are the two ischial tuberosities and the sacrum. If the stool tilts, the pole leans; if the load on any leg increases, the pole wobbles. A wheelchair cushion acts like a "smart ground" — adjusting its stiffness to keep the stool level, distribute load evenly across the three points, and prevent the pole from toppling. In a healthy seated person, the pelvis naturally rolls forward (anterior tilt) on the ischial tuberosities, preserving lumbar lordosis. In a person with weak trunk muscles, the pelvis rolls **backward (sacral sitting)**, flattening the spine into a C-curve. The assistive technology's role is biomechanically to **resist this posterior rotation** while allowing controlled movement. ### 1.3 Key Quantitative Metrics in Seating Biomechanics - **Interface Pressure:** Typically **40–80 mmHg** at the ischial tuberosities in healthy sitting; tissue ischemia occurs above **~200 mmHg** sustained for >2 hours (capillary closing pressure). - **Standard Anthropometric Seating Dimensions (KTU reference):** Seat depth ≈ **430 mm**, seat width ≈ **460 mm**, seat-to-backrest angle ≈ **90°–100°**, footrest height ≈ **400–450 mm**. - **Body Segment Mass Distribution (Seated):** Head–neck–trunk ≈ **58.4%** of body weight, thighs ≈ **20.6%**, lower legs/feet ≈ **8.1%**. > [!TIP] > **KTU Mnemonic — "PRS"** for the three biomechanical goals of seating: > **P**ressure redistribution · **R**educed shear · **S**pinal alignment --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Statics of the Seated Human Body When a person sits on a horizontal support, the body's **Center of Gravity (CoG)** lies approximately at **S2 vertebra level** (slightly anterior to it in males, posterior in females). The seated CoG is shifted **~30–50 mm forward** compared to standing, but **~200 mm higher** from the ground. For static equilibrium, the **sum of all forces** and the **sum of all moments** acting on the body must vanish: $$\sum F_x = 0, \quad \sum F_y = 0, \quad \sum M_O = 0$$ In the seated posture, the vertical reaction force $R$ at the support surface must equal the body weight $W$ minus any partial body weight supported by the footrests $F_{foot}$ and armrests $F_{arm}$: $$R_{cushion} = W - F_{foot} - F_{arm} - F_{backrest,vertical}$$ ### 2.2 Pressure–Area Relationship (The Fundamental Seating Equation) The core biomechanical metric of any cushion is given by: $$P_{interface} = \frac{F_{load}}{A_{contact}}$$ where: - $P_{interface}$ = interface pressure (Pa or mmHg; **1 mmHg ≈ 133.32 Pa**) - $F_{load}$ = vertical load component (N) - $A_{contact}$ = effective contact area (m²) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters:** A cushion that **increases the effective contact area** by **immersion** and **envelopment** will reduce the peak pressure on bony prominences, directly lowering the risk of **pressure ulcers (decubitus ulcers)** — a Grade-A KTU clinical concern. ### 2.3 Cushion Mechanics — Immersion, Envelopment, and Recovery Modern cushions are characterized by three engineering properties: 1. **Immersion Depth (h):** How far the body sinks into the cushion (typically **30–60 mm**). Deep immersion increases contact area but may compromise postural stability. 2. **Envelopment:** The ability of the cushion to conform to body contours, especially around the ischial tuberosities. 3. **Recovery / Resilience:** The cushion's ability to return to its original shape after load removal (measured by hysteresis loss). The **Hysteresis Loss Ratio (HLR)** for a viscoelastic foam is: $$HLR = \frac{A_{loading} - A_{unloading}}{A_{loading}} \times 100\%$$ A high-quality foam cushion has **HLR between 15–30%** — low enough to absorb shock but high enough to dampen vibration. ### 2.4 Postural Stability — The Inverted Pendulum Model The seated upper body behaves as an **inverted pendulum pivoting at the ischial tuberosities (the base of support)**. Stability requires the vertical line of gravity (LoG) to fall **within** the base of support. $$\text{Stability Index} = \frac{d_{LoG \rightarrow BoS\,boundary}}{h_{CoG}}$$ A larger stability index = more stable. Backrest recline shifts the CoG posteriorly, reducing forward-fall risk but increasing shear. ### 2.5 Shear Stress at the Seat Interface Shear is the tangential force that distorts skin and underlying tissue. Newton's law of friction gives the maximum static shear: $$\tau_{max} = \mu \cdot P_{interface}$$ where $\mu$ = coefficient of friction at the body–cushion interface (skin-cotton ≈ **0.4–0.6**, skin-ripstop nylon ≈ **0.3**). Reducing $\mu$ via low-shear cover fabrics is a primary engineering intervention. ### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Equation | Meaning | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $P = F/A$ | Interface pressure | Pa (N/m²) | | 2 | $R_{cushion} = W - F_{foot} - F_{arm}$ | Reaction force balance | N | | 3 | $\tau = \mu P$ | Shear stress (Coulomb friction) | Pa | | 4 | $HLR = \dfrac{A_L - A_{UL}}{A_L}\times 100\%$ | Viscoelastic damping | % | | 5 | $M = F \times d$ | Moment (postural torque) | N·m | | 6 | $\sigma = \dfrac{Mc}{I}$ | Bending stress in backrest | Pa | | 7 | $F_{spring} = k \cdot \Delta x$ | Cushion linear spring response | N | | 8 | $T = 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{m}{k}}$ | Natural period of cushion–body system | s | | 9 | $SI = d/h$ | Stability Index | dimensionless | | 10 | $BMI = W/H^2$ | Body Mass Index (prescription reference) | kg/m² | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Utility:** These equations are used by rehabilitation engineers during **custom-molded seating fabrication** (e.g., for cerebral palsy patients), pressure-mapping system calibration (e.g., Tekscan, Xsensor), and in **ISO 7176 wheelchair standards** testing. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Pressure Distribution on a 2-Legged vs 3-Point Pelvic Base **Problem setup:** A 70 kg adult sits on a rigid flat surface. The pelvis loads the surface only at the two ischial tuberosities (assume a 2-point model first). Each tuberosity contact patch measures 30 mm × 30 mm = $9 \times 10^{-4}$ m². Compute peak interface pressure. **Step 1 — Compute body weight in Newtons:** $$W = m \cdot g = 70 \text{ kg} \times 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2 = 686.7 \text{ N}$$ **Step 2 — Assume weight is split between the two ischial tuberosities (ignore sacrum, feet, backrest for this idealized case). By symmetry:** $$F_{each\,IT} = \frac{W}{2} = \frac{686.7}{2} = 343.35 \text{ N}$$ **Step 3 — Apply the pressure equation $P = F/A$:** $$P_{peak} = \frac{343.35}{9 \times 10^{-4}} = 3.815 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa}$$ **Step 4 — Convert to mmHg (the clinical unit):** $$P_{peak} = \frac{3.815 \times 10^{5}}{133.32} \approx 2861 \text{ mmHg}$$ > [!WARNING] > This peak pressure (**~2861 mmHg**) is **>14× the capillary closing pressure (~200 mmHg)**. This explains why an unmodified rigid seat causes pressure injuries within 1–2 hours. A cushion that **spreads the load over 0.05 m²** reduces peak pressure to ~13.7 kPa = **~103 mmHg** — safely below the ischemia threshold. ### 3.2 Derivation: Effect of Backrest Recline on Shear **Step 1 — Define the geometry.** When the backrest reclines by angle $\theta$ from vertical, the trunk weight $W_{trunk} \approx 0.584 \cdot W_{total}$ has two components relative to the seat surface: $$W_{\perp} = W_{trunk} \cdot \cos\theta \quad \text{(normal, into cushion)}$$ $$W_{\parallel} = W_{trunk} \cdot \sin\theta \quad \text{(parallel, causing shear)}$$ **Step 2 — For a recline of $\theta = 10°$ (typical "tilt-in-space" adjustment) and total body weight 70 kg:** $$W_{trunk} = 0.584 \times 686.7 = 401.0 \text{ N}$$ $$W_{\perp} = 401.0 \times \cos 10° = 401.0 \times 0.9848 = 394.9 \text{ N}$$ $$W_{\parallel} = 401.0 \times \sin 10° = 401.0 \times 0.1736 = 69.6 \text{ N}$$ **Step 3 — Convert shear to stress over a back-contact area of 0.15 m²:** $$\tau = \frac{69.6}{0.15} = 464 \text{ Pa} \approx 3.5 \text{ mmHg}$$ **Step 4 — Engineering interpretation.** A 10° recline introduces ~3.5 mmHg of shear — non-trivial over 4 hours of sitting. **Tilt-in-space systems** (typically 30°–45°) trade increased shear for huge reductions in normal pressure, giving the **best clinical compromise**. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Cushion Material Comparison | Cushion Type | Immersion | Envelopment | HLR (%) | Peak Pressure (typical) | Best For | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Foam (high resilience PU)** | Low–Medium | Low | 20–30 | 80–120 mmHg | Low-risk, ambulatory users | | **Viscoelastic (memory) foam** | Medium–High | High | 25–40 | 60–90 mmHg | Medium-risk, geriatric | | **Air-cell (e.g., ROHO)** | Very High | Very High | 5–15 | 35–60 mmHg | High-risk (SCI, ulcer history) | | **Gel (e.g., Jay)** | Medium | Medium | 30–45 | 70–100 mmHg | Postural stability priority | | **Honeycomb (e.g., Supracor)** | High | High | 10–20 | 50–80 mmHg | Active users, hygiene-critical | | **Custom-molded (carved foam + cover)** | Variable | Excellent (per patient) | N/A | 40–70 mmHg | Cerebral palsy, fixed deformities | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Insight:** When asked to choose a cushion for a **spinal cord injury patient with a history of Grade-III sacral ulcer**, the correct answer is **air-cell (ROHO-type)** because of the lowest peak pressure and lowest hysteresis (best for off-loading). Always justify with the pressure equation. ### 3.4 Worked Example: Stability Calculation in a Tilt-in-Space Chair A 60 kg patient with poor trunk control is in a wheelchair with seat depth 430 mm, backrest height 500 mm, and is tilted at $\theta = 30°$. Approximate CoG height from seat (in tilted position): $$h_{CoG} = h_{upright}\cdot\cos\theta + d_{CoG \rightarrow back}\cdot\sin\theta$$ Assume upright $h_{CoG} = 250$ mm and horizontal distance of CoG from backrest axis $d = 100$ mm: $$h_{CoG} = 250\cos 30° + 100\sin 30° = 250(0.866) + 100(0.5) = 216.5 + 50 = 266.5 \text{ mm}$$ **Stability Index calculation:** $$SI = \frac{d_{LoG \rightarrow BoS\,edge}}{h_{CoG}} = \frac{200}{266.5} = 0.75$$ This is a stable configuration (SI > 0.5 threshold). --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Biomechanical Block Architecture of a Seating System ```mermaid flowchart TB A[User Body - Anthropometry] --> B[Postural Control Subsystem] B --> C{Pelvic Position} C -->|Anterior tilt| D[Neutral Spine] C -->|Posterior tilt| E[Sacral Sitting - Risk] D --> F[Load Distribution] E --> F F --> G[Ischial Tuberosities] F --> H[Sacrum] F --> I[Femurs] G --> J[Pressure Mapping Sensor] H --> J I --> J J --> K[Controller Unit] K -->|Active adjustment| L[Air Cell Array Cushion] K -->|Feedback| M[Clinician Display] L --> N[Pressure Equalized Interface] N --> O[Tissue Integrity Preserved] style A fill:#e1f5ff style O fill:#c8e6c9 style E fill:#ffcdd2 style K fill:#fff9c4 ``` ### 4.2 Free-Body Diagram of the Seated Trunk (Statics) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SeatedTrunk[Free-Body Diagram - Seated Trunk] direction TB W[Weight W = 588 N
acting at CoG] N[Normal Reaction N
from backrest] F[Friction Force F
parallel to backrest] S[Shear Component W sin theta] P[Normal Component W cos theta] end W --> P W --> S P --> N S --> F style W fill:#ffecb3 style N fill:#c8e6c9 style F fill:#ffccbc ``` ### 4.3 Seating Assessment Clinical Decision Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Patient Assessment] --> B{Functional Mobility} B -->|Independent| C[Standard Wheelchair
+ Foam Cushion] B -->|Partial assist| D{Postural Control} B -->|Full dependent| E[Custom Molded Seating] D -->|Poor| F[Tilt-in-Space Chair
+ Air Cushion] D -->|Adequate| G[Lightweight Active Chair
+ Viscoelastic Cushion] E --> H{Cognitive Status} H -->|Alert| I[Powered Tilt
with ROHO] H -->|Reduced| J[Manual Tilt
+ Contoured Foam] style A fill:#bbdefb style C fill:#c8e6c9 style F fill:#fff9c4 style E fill:#ffcdd2 ``` ### 4.4 Pressure-Time Curve (Ischemia Threshold Visualization) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Time 0 hr
Pressure 80 mmHg] --> B[Time 1 hr
Pressure sustained] B --> C[Time 2 hr
Capillary occlusion begins] C --> D[Time 3 hr
Tissue ischemia] D --> E[Time 4 hr
Pressure ulcer Grade I risk] E --> F[Pressure Relief Push-up
or Tilt Recline] F --> G[Reperfusion
Time 0 reset] style A fill:#c8e6c9 style C fill:#fff9c4 style E fill:#ffcdd2 style F fill:#bbdefb ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Define interface pressure and state its clinical significance in wheelchair seating.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Interface pressure is the contact force per unit area transmitted between the body and the supporting surface, given by $P = F/A$ with units of Pa or mmHg (1 mark). Clinically, sustained interface pressure above the **capillary closing pressure of ~200 mmHg** (1 mark) leads to tissue ischemia, necrosis, and pressure ulcers (decubitus ulcers), especially over the ischial tuberosities and sacrum in long-term wheelchair users (1 mark). **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **List the three primary biomechanical goals of assistive seating systems.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The three primary goals are: (i) **Pressure redistribution** — reducing peak pressure on bony prominences through increased contact area (1 mark); (ii) **Postural stability** — maintaining the line of gravity within the base of support (1 mark); and (iii) **Spinal/pelvic alignment** — preventing posterior pelvic tilt and preserving lumbar lordosis (1 mark). Mnemonic: **"PRS"**. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) #### QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Explain the biomechanical rationale for using a **tilt-in-space wheelchair** for a spinal cord injury (SCI) patient. Derive the relationship between recline angle and the normal/shear components of trunk weight. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 75 kg patient uses an air-cell cushion with effective contact area 0.06 m². Compute the average interface pressure in mmHg. Compare it with the capillary closing pressure and comment on the ulcer risk. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Tilt-in-Space Biomechanics (7 marks):** Tilt-in-space rotates the entire seating system (seat + backrest) about a fixed axis, preserving hip and knee angles (1 mark). This contrasts with reclining, which changes hip angle and can cause shear. **Derivation of force components (4 marks):** Let $W_{trunk}$ be the trunk weight and $\theta$ the tilt angle from vertical. $$W_{trunk} = k \cdot m_{body} \cdot g = 0.584 \times 75 \times 9.81 = 429.4 \text{ N}$$ Decomposition: $$W_{\perp} = W_{trunk} \cos\theta, \quad W_{\parallel} = W_{trunk} \sin\theta$$ **Numerical example at $\theta = 30°$:** $$W_{\perp} = 429.4 \times 0.866 = 372.0 \text{ N (into cushion)}$$ $$W_{\parallel} = 429.4 \times 0.5 = 214.7 \text{ N (shear)}$$ **Clinical rationale (2 marks):** Although shear increases with tilt, the *normal* pressure reduction is dramatic. At 30° tilt, the buttocks offload by ~14% of trunk weight, and at 45° (industry-recommended relief position), offloading reaches ~30%. This gives regular pressure relief without requiring patient effort — critical for SCI patients lacking upper-body strength. **(b) Interface Pressure Calculation (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Total body weight:** $$W = 75 \times 9.81 = 735.75 \text{ N}$$ **Step 2 — Assume 90% load goes to the cushion** (10% supported by footrests, backrest, armrests combined): $$F_{cushion} = 0.90 \times 735.75 = 662.2 \text{ N}$$ **Step 3 — Average interface pressure:** $$P_{avg} = \frac{F_{cushion}}{A_{contact}} = \frac{662.2}{0.06} = 11037 \text{ Pa}$$ **Step 4 — Convert to mmHg:** $$P_{avg} = \frac{11037}{133.32} = 82.8 \text{ mmHg}$$ **Step 5 — Clinical interpretation (2 marks):** - Capillary closing pressure ≈ **200 mmHg** (sustained threshold). - Computed average pressure = **82.8 mmHg**, which is **<50% of the ischemia threshold**. - However, *peak* pressure at the ischial tuberosities may be 1.5–2× the average (≈ 125–165 mmHg) — still within safe range. - **Conclusion:** Low ulcer risk; air-cell cushion is clinically appropriate. Recommend re-mapping every 6 months and tilt relief every 30 minutes. --- #### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **(a)** With a neat free-body diagram, derive the **moment equation for postural stability** in the sagittal plane. Define and compute the **Stability Index** for a 65 kg patient with CoG at 270 mm above the seat, whose line of gravity falls 180 mm from the edge of the base of support. Comment on the postural stability. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare and contrast **air-cell, viscoelastic foam, and gel cushions** in terms of (i) peak pressure, (ii) postural stability, (iii) maintenance, and (iv) typical clinical indication. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution Outline:** **(a)** Free-body diagram shows trunk weight $W$ acting at CoG, normal reaction $N$ at ischial tuberosities, and friction $f$ at contact. Taking moments about the ischial pivot: $$M_{restoring} = N \cdot d_1, \quad M_{destabilizing} = W \cdot d_2$$ Equilibrium: $M_{restoring} = M_{destabilizing}$. Stability Index: $$SI = \frac{d_{LoG \rightarrow BoS\,edge}}{h_{CoG}} = \frac{180}{270} = 0.667$$ Since $SI > 0.5$ (clinical stability threshold), the posture is **stable**, but with **moderate margin** — appropriate for an active user with reasonable trunk control. **(b)** Comparative table with peaks ~50 mmHg (air), ~75 mmHg (visco), ~85 mmHg (gel); stability is best with gel/foam, worst with air; maintenance is easiest with foam; clinical indications: air for high-risk, visco for medium-risk, gel for postural priority. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert Pa to mmHg** (1 mmHg = 133.32 Pa). Examiners deduct 1 mark for unit errors. > 2. **Confusing tilt-in-space with recline.** Tilt preserves joint angles; recline changes them. This is a **2-mark differentiator**. > 3. **Using the wrong trunk weight fraction.** Always use **0.584 × W** for head–neck–trunk segment; using full W leads to grossly overestimated pressures. > 4. **Omitting the FBD.** A free-body diagram is **mandatory** for any force/moment derivation question. Skipping it = −1 mark. > 5. **Ignoring shear.** In tilt/recline problems, students compute normal force but forget the parallel component. Always decompose $W$ into both components. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Core principle:** A good seating system must satisfy **Pressure redistribution + Postural stability + Spinal alignment (PRS)**. - **Pressure equation:** $P = F/A$ — the cornerstone formula. Always cite in **mmHg** for clinical relevance. - **Capillary closing pressure:** **~200 mmHg sustained** is the clinical danger threshold. - **Trunk weight fraction in sitting:** **58.4%** of total body weight. - **Seated CoG:** At **S2 vertebra level**, ~30–50 mm anterior. - **Base of support:** Two ischial tuberosities + sacrum (tripod) + femurs. - **Tilt-in-space:** Preserves joint angles; best for **SCI, high-tone, postural deformity** patients. - **Recline:** Changes joint angles; introduces shear; used in **self-propelling active users** for rest. - **Cushion hierarchy by pressure relief:** Air > Honeycomb > Viscoelastic > Gel > Foam. - **Hysteresis loss ratio (HLR):** 15–30% is optimal for seating foams. - **Shear equation:** $\tau = \mu P$ — reduce $\mu$ via low-shear covers (e.g., nylon, Lycra). - **Stability Index (SI):** $SI = d/h_{CoG}$; threshold **> 0.5** = stable. - **Anthropometric seat reference (KTU):** Depth 430 mm, width 460 mm, back angle 90°–100°. - **Standards to know:** **ISO 7176** (wheelchairs), **RESNA standards**, **ISO 16840** (seating). - **High-yield KTU keywords:** *Immersion, Envelopment, Hysteresis, Capillary occlusion, Sacral sitting, Pelvic obliquity, Tilt-in-space, Recline, Shear, Stability Index.* ---

topics.title: Seating Technologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT614 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Seating Technologies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Seating Technology** in the context of rehabilitation engineering and assistive medical devices refers to the integrated system of biomechanically engineered supports, cushions, postural alignment components, and pressure redistribution surfaces designed to be coupled with a wheelchair or mobility base. It addresses three primary clinical objectives: (1) **postural stability** by maintaining the user's pelvis, trunk, and lower extremities within a neutral alignment zone, (2) **pressure ulcer prevention** through load redistribution over the ischial tuberosities, sacrum, coccyx, and greater trochanters, and (3) **functional enablement** by providing a stable base of support that allows upper-extremity mobility, respiration, and digestion.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme (OEBRT614) curriculum, Seating Technologies encompasses the classification of seating systems (planar/contoured/custom-molded), cushion material sciences (foam, gel, air, fluid, hybrid), postural support components (lateral trunk supports, headrests, abductors, adductors, pelvic positioners), and the engineering assessment methodology used to match a user to an appropriate seating configuration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Seating Technologies is fundamentally a *biomechanical interface engineering* topic — the seat is not furniture, it is a **medical device** that interfaces between the human body and the mobility chassis. Failure of this interface leads directly to pressure injuries (decubitus ulcers), postural deformities, and reduced quality of life.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine sitting on a wooden bench for **8 continuous hours**. By the second hour, your buttocks start to ache; by the fourth hour, you are unconsciously shifting weight (off-loading one buttock, then the other); by the eighth hour, you may have skin redness that persists. This happens because a flat, hard surface concentrates body weight onto a small contact area (the ischial tuberosities — the bony "sit bones").

Now imagine a high-end **ergonomic office chair** with memory foam, lumbar curve, and contoured shape. You can sit longer because the foam deforms, *increasing the contact area* and *reducing pressure per unit area* (Pressure = Force / Area). Seating technology takes this principle to a medical extreme — users with **spinal cord injury (SCI)** cannot feel the ache, cannot shift weight, and may sit 12–16 hours daily. The engineering challenge is therefore to design a seat that:

1. **Spreads** their body weight over the **largest possible surface area**.
2. **Holds** their spine, pelvis, and hips in a **stable, symmetric posture**.
3. **Allows** the skin to receive blood flow (capillary closure pressure ≈ **32 mmHg**) without exceeding it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:** Think of the wheelchair seat as a **suspension system** for the human body — just like a car's suspension absorbs road shock and distributes load to four tires, the seating system absorbs gravitational load and distributes it across the buttocks, thighs, and back.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following biomechanical and engineering constants are essential for the KTU examination:

| Constant / Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Capillary closure pressure | $P_{cap}$ | **32 mmHg** (~4.3 kPa) | Maximum sustained skin-interface pressure to prevent ischemia |
| Normal adult seating pressure (ischial) | $P_{isch}$ | **75–150 mmHg** | Pressure at ischial tuberosities on a hard surface |
| Body weight distribution while seated (erect) | – | **75% buttocks, 25% thighs** | Load split between ITs and posterior thighs |
| Seating duration threshold for at-risk users | $t_{risk}$ | **>2 hours without shift** | Time after which pressure injury risk rises sharply |
| Wheelchair seat width standard | $W_{seat}$ | **16, 18, 20 inches** | Standard KTU/ISO clinical sizes |
| Wheelchair seat depth standard | $D_{seat}$ | **16, 18, 20 inches** | Knee-to-posterior pelvis distance |
| Seat-to-floor height (standard) | $H_{sf}$ | **17.5–20.5 inches** | For foot-propelled wheelchairs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Henry's Law of Pressure (clinical mnemonic):** Interface Pressure $\times$ Time = Tissue Damage. Reducing either factor reduces ulcer risk — this is the foundational principle behind every cushion technology.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Seating Engineering

Any seating technology system can be analyzed through three engineering pillars:

### Pillar 1: Pressure Redistribution (Tissue Integrity)
- **Goal:** Keep interface pressure below **capillary closure pressure** ($P_{cap} \approx 32 \, \text{mmHg}$).
- **Mechanism:** Increase contact area, offload bony prominences, allow immersion and envelopment.
- **Cushion Classes:**
  1. **Foam Cushions** — Polyurethane (PU) open-cell; cheap, lightweight, low maintenance. *Limitation:* bottoming out over 12–18 months.
  2. **Gel Cushions** — Polymer gel (e.g., silicone, viscoelastic). Excellent envelopment, but heavy and temperature-sensitive.
  3. **Air Cushions** — Interconnected air cells (e.g., ROHO). Best pressure redistribution; *require* daily calibration.
  4. **Fluid/Hybrid** — Combinations (e.g., gel + foam, air + foam).
  5. **Honeycomb/Elastomer** — Thermoplastic elastomer (TPE) honeycomb (e.g., Stimulite).

### Pillar 2: Postural Support (Skeletal Alignment)
- **Goal:** Maintain **pelvic neutrality** (anterior tilt ≤ 15°, obliquity ≤ 5°, rotation ≤ 5°).
- **Components:**
  1. Pelvic positioning belt (45° hip strap)
  2. Lateral trunk supports (swing-away, adjustable)
  3. Medial/lateral thigh supports (abductor pommel)
  4. Headrest (occipital support)
  5. Footrest (ankle 90° dorsiflexion)
  6. Backrest (lumbar contour or custom-molded)

### Pillar 3: Functional Mobility (User Independence)
- **Goal:** Allow upper limb function, self-propulsion, transfers.
- **Mechanism:** Stable base → energy-efficient push rim biomechanics.
- **Constraint:** Heavier cushion = heavier chair = increased rolling resistance.

## 2.2 Pressure Distribution Physics

The fundamental equation governing cushion performance is:

$$P_{interface} = \frac{F_{body}}{A_{contact}}$$

Where:
- $P_{interface}$ = average interface pressure (mmHg or kPa)
- $F_{body}$ = downward force from the user's mass under gravity ($F = m \cdot g$)
- $A_{contact}$ = effective contact area between body and cushion (m² or cm²)

For a 70 kg user seated on a hard surface with $A_{contact} \approx 200 \, \text{cm}^2$:

$$P = \frac{70 \times 9.81}{200 \times 10^{-4}} = 34,335 \, \text{Pa} \approx 257 \, \text{mmHg}$$

This is roughly **8× the capillary closure pressure** — explaining why hard seats cause pressure injuries within hours.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A 100% increase in contact area (e.g., from 200 to 400 cm² through cushion deformation) *halves* the average pressure. This is why immersion and envelopment are the *single most important* engineering properties of any cushion.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Engineering Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Average Interface Pressure | $P = F/A$ | Newton's law applied to seating | Pa, mmHg |
| 2 | Capillary Closure Pressure Threshold | $P_{cap} = 32$ mmHg | Maximum safe sustained pressure | mmHg |
| 3 | Body Force in Seated Position | $F = m \cdot g$ | Vertical load transmitted to seat | N |
| 4 | Pressure Reduction Ratio (PRR) | $PRR = \dfrac{P_{hard} - P_{cushion}}{P_{hard}} \times 100\%$ | Cushion efficacy metric | % |
| 5 | Immersion Depth (cushion) | $h = f(\text{material, load})$ | How deep the IT sinks into cushion | mm |
| 6 | Envelopment | $E = \dfrac{A_{contact, cush}}{A_{contact, hard}}$ | Ratio of contact areas (dimensionless) | – |
| 7 | Seat-to-Back Angle (Kappa) | $\kappa = 90° + \alpha$ | Hip opening angle; $\alpha = 0$–20° | degrees |
| 8 | Seat Plane Load Distribution | $L_{IT} : L_{thigh} = 75 : 25$ | Standard erect sit; shifts with tilt | – |
| 9 | Pressure × Time = Damage Index | $PTI = \int P(t) \, dt$ | Cumulative tissue load | mmHg·h |
| 10 | Tilt-In-Space Pressure Relief | $\Delta P_{tilt} = P_{0} \cos\theta$ | IT pressure reduction at tilt angle $\theta$ | mmHg |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical LaTeX Safety:** Notice the use of `\vert` and `\mid` alternatives were not needed here, but remember to **never** use the literal pipe symbol `|` inside markdown table cells — always substitute with `\vert` or wrap math in display blocks. This prevents table-parsing failure during KTU PDF generation.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Seating technology is not just clinical — it has industrial applications in:
- **Automotive seating** (driver fatigue, long-haul trucking)
- **Aerospace** (pilot G-force tolerance, long-duration spaceflight — NASA's neutral body posture uses similar principles)
- **Office ergonomics** (ANSI/BIFMA standards derived from wheelchair seating research)
- **Sports engineering** (cushioned saddles for Paralympic cyclists, hand-cyclists)

The wheelchair cushion industry is valued at over **USD 1.2 billion globally (2024)**, and India has been expanding its indigenous manufacturing base under the **ALIMCO (Artificial Limbs Manufacturing Corporation)** and the **ADIP (Assistance to Disabled Persons for Purchase/Fitting of Aids/Appliances)** scheme.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Pressure Reduction Ratio (PRR) of a Cushion

**Given:**
- Hard-surface interface pressure: $P_{hard} = 257$ mmHg (from Section 2.2)
- ROHO air cushion measured pressure at IT: $P_{cushion} = 60$ mmHg
- User mass: $m = 70$ kg
- Cushion contact area: $A_{cush} = 600$ cm²
- Hard surface contact area: $A_{hard} = 200$ cm²

**Find:** Pressure Reduction Ratio (PRR) of the cushion.

**Step 1 — Write the PRR definition:**

$$PRR = \frac{P_{hard} - P_{cushion}}{P_{hard}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values:**

$$PRR = \frac{257 - 60}{257} \times 100\%$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator:**

$$257 - 60 = 197 \, \text{mmHg}$$

**Step 4 — Divide and convert to percentage:**

$$PRR = \frac{197}{257} \times 100\% = 0.7665 \times 100\% \approx 76.65\%$$

**Result:** The ROHO cushion achieves a **~76.7% pressure reduction** compared to a hard surface. This is clinically excellent — the residual 60 mmHg is still above 32 mmHg, so the user must still perform weight-shifts every 30 minutes.

## 3.2 Derivation: Tilt-In-Space Pressure Off-Loading

**Concept:** When a wheelchair seat is tilted backward by angle $\theta$ (e.g., $35°$), gravity's vertical component at the ischial tuberosities decreases.

**Step 1 — Free-body decomposition of the body weight vector:**

$$\vec{W} = W \cdot \hat{j} \quad \text{(vertical, downward)}$$

When the seat tilts by $\theta$, the *normal* direction to the seat plane also tilts by $\theta$.

**Step 2 — Resolve weight into components normal and parallel to the tilted seat surface:**

$$W_{normal} = W \cos\theta$$
$$W_{parallel} = W \sin\theta$$

**Step 3 — Express the interface pressure at the IT after tilt:**

$$P_{tilt} = P_{0} \cos\theta$$

where $P_{0}$ is the upright (0° tilt) interface pressure.

**Step 4 — Compute for a $35°$ tilt starting from $P_{0} = 200$ mmHg:**

$$P_{35°} = 200 \times \cos(35°)$$
$$P_{35°} = 200 \times 0.8192$$
$$P_{35°} \approx 163.8 \, \text{mmHg}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the pressure relief percentage:**

$$\Delta P\% = \frac{200 - 163.8}{200} \times 100\% \approx 18.1\%$$

**Step 6 — Compare with clinical tilt recommendations:**

| Tilt Angle $\theta$ | $P_{tilt}/P_{0}$ | Pressure Relief | Clinical Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0° | 1.000 | 0% | Upright, baseline |
| 15° | 0.966 | 3.4% | Comfort adjustment |
| 25° | 0.906 | 9.4% | Mild relief |
| **35°** | **0.819** | **18.1%** | **Standard tilt for relief** |
| 45° | 0.707 | 29.3% | Strong relief, hard to self-propel |
| 65° | 0.423 | 57.7% | Bed-bound-like position |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** Pure tilt-in-space reduces IT pressure by only **~18% at 35°**. To achieve full pressure relief (below 32 mmHg), tilt must be combined with **recline** *and* a **load redistribution cushion**. No single intervention is sufficient.

## 3.3 Derivation: Seat Depth Sizing (KTU Clinical Method)

**Goal:** Compute the correct seat depth for a wheelchair user.

**Step 1 — Measure the user's posterior buttock-to-posterior-knee distance (PBPK):**

$$D_{PBPK} = L_{user} \quad \text{(typically 450–500 mm)}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the standard clinical correction factor (subtract 50–75 mm to avoid popliteal pressure):**

$$D_{seat} = D_{PBPK} - 50 \, \text{mm}$$

**Step 3 — Verify with sample data (user PBPK = 480 mm):**

$$D_{seat} = 480 - 50 = 430 \, \text{mm} \approx 17 \, \text{inches}$$

**Step 4 — Validation check:** There should be a **two-finger gap** (~25 mm) between the front edge of the seat and the user's popliteal fossa (back of knee). If the gap is too small, the seat is too deep → risk of sacral sitting and knee contracture. If too large, the user cannot reach the backrest → risk of posterior pelvic tilt.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Cushion Selection

| Clinical Scenario | Recommended Cushion Class | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Active paraplegic, low risk, manual chair | **Lightweight foam** (e.g., 2" high-resilience PU) | Low weight, easy transfers, low cost |
| SCI patient, full-time user, high pressure risk | **Adjustable air cell** (e.g., ROHO, Vicair) | Best pressure redistribution; adjustable |
| Elderly, postural deformity, low activity | **Hybrid (gel + foam)** | Combines stability and pressure relief |
| Pediatric with cerebral palsy | **Contoured foam with growth kit** | Postural support, modular sizing |
| Active user, perspiration issue | **Honeycomb elastomer** (Stimulite) | Breathable, washable, low maintenance |
| Bariatric user (>120 kg) | **High-density bariatric foam or reinforced air** | Load-rated cushion |

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Seating System

The following Mermaid diagram maps the *functional subsystems* of a complete seating technology assembly, including the user-body interface, cushion mechanics, postural support, and wheelchair chassis interaction.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[User Body] --> B[Body Weight Vector W]
    B --> C[Seat Cushion Subsystem]
    C --> C1[Top Layer: Contoured Foam]
    C --> C2[Core: Air Cells or Gel or Foam]
    C --> C3[Base Layer: Rigid Pan]
    C1 --> D[Pressure Redistribution P less than Pcap]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    A --> E[Postural Support Subsystem]
    E --> E1[Pelvic Belt 45 deg]
    E --> E2[Lateral Trunk Supports]
    E --> E3[Headrest]
    E --> E4[Abductor Pommel]
    E --> E5[Footrest]
    E1 --> F[Pelvic Neutrality]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    E5 --> F
    C --> G[Wheelchair Chassis Interface]
    G --> G1[Seat Pan Rigid Platform]
    G --> G2[Backrest Mount]
    G --> G3[Tilt or Recline Mechanism]
    G1 --> H[Functional Output]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    D --> H
    F --> H
    H --> H1[Pressure Injury Prevention]
    H --> H2[Postural Stability]
    H --> H3[Functional Mobility]

    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
```

## 4.2 Cushion Material Decision Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Start: User Assessment] --> Q1[Pressure Injury Risk Level]
    Q1 --> Low[Low Risk Active User]
    Q1 --> Med[Medium Risk]
    Q1 --> High[High Risk SCI or Full Time User]
    Low --> L1[Lightweight HR Foam 1.5 to 2 inch]
    Med --> M1[Contoured Foam or Hybrid]
    High --> H1[Adjustable Air or Custom Molded]
    L1 --> Lcheck[Weight under 1 kg, easy transfer]
    M1 --> Mcheck[Balance of stability and relief]
    H1 --> Hcheck[Best PRR, requires daily calibration]
    Lcheck --> End[Prescription]
    Mcheck --> End
    Hcheck --> End
```

## 4.3 Postural Assessment Algorithm (KTU Clinical Method)

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Step 1: Pelvic Position Check] --> P2{Neutral Pelvis?}
    P2 --> No[Posterior or Oblique Tilt]
    P2 --> Yes[Proceed to Trunk]
    No --> R1[Add Pelvic Belt or Anti-Thrust Seat]
    R1 --> P1
    Yes --> P3[Step 2: Trunk Alignment]
    P3 --> P4{Symmetric Trunk?}
    P4 --> No[Add Lateral Supports]
    P4 --> Yes[Proceed to Head]
    No --> R2[Lateral Trunk Supports L and R]
    R2 --> P3
    Yes --> P5[Step 3: Head and Neck]
    P5 --> P6{Head Control OK?}
    P6 --> No[Add Headrest]
    P6 --> Yes[Step 4 Lower Extremities]
    No --> R3[Occipital or Sub Occipital Headrest]
    R3 --> P5
    Yes --> P7[Step 4: Foot and Ankle]
    P7 --> P8{Ankle at 90 deg?}
    P8 --> No[Adjust Footrest Height]
    P8 --> Yes[End Seating Prescription Complete]
    No --> R4[Footrest Adjustment or Strap]
    R4 --> P7
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define "Seating Technology" in the context of assistive medical devices. List the three primary clinical objectives it addresses.**

**Model Answer:**

> Seating Technology refers to the integrated system of biomechanically engineered cushions, postural support components, and pressure redistribution surfaces designed to interface a wheelchair user with their mobility base.
>
> The three primary clinical objectives are:
> 1. **Postural stability** — maintaining the pelvis, trunk, and lower extremities in neutral alignment.
> 2. **Pressure ulcer prevention** — keeping interface pressure below the capillary closure pressure (~32 mmHg).
> 3. **Functional enablement** — providing a stable base for upper-limb mobility and self-propulsion.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is capillary closure pressure? State its clinical significance in seating technology.**

**Model Answer:**

> Capillary closure pressure is the minimum sustained external pressure at which blood flow through the skin capillaries is occluded, with a standard clinical value of **$P_{cap} \approx 32 \, \text{mmHg}$** (≈ 4.3 kPa).
>
> **Clinical Significance:** If the interface pressure between the body and the seating surface exceeds 32 mmHg for a sustained period, the underlying tissue becomes ischemic (oxygen-starved), leading to cell death and pressure ulcers (decubitus ulcers). All cushion technologies — foam, gel, air, hybrid — are designed specifically to keep the *interface pressure* at the ischial tuberosities, sacrum, and greater trochanters *below this threshold*, often combined with periodic weight-shifts to allow tissue reperfusion.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the biomechanics of pressure redistribution at the body–seat interface. With the help of suitable equations, derive the **Pressure Reduction Ratio (PRR)** for a user weighing **75 kg** seated on a hard surface (contact area = 220 cm², interface pressure = 245 mmHg) and on a ROHO air cushion (contact area = 640 cm², interface pressure = 58 mmHg). Comment on the clinical effectiveness of the cushion.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast **foam, gel, air, and hybrid cushions** with respect to material composition, pressure redistribution mechanism, advantages, limitations, and ideal user profile. Present the answer in a structured comparison.

---

### Model Solution to Question A:

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — State the governing physics:**

The interface pressure between the human body and the seating surface is governed by:

$$P_{interface} = \frac{F_{body}}{A_{contact}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the body force:**

$$F = m \cdot g = 75 \times 9.81 = 735.75 \, \text{N}$$

**Step 3 — Define Pressure Reduction Ratio:**

$$PRR = \frac{P_{hard} - P_{cushion}}{P_{hard}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 4 — Substitute given values:**

$$PRR = \frac{245 - 58}{245} \times 100\%$$

$$PRR = \frac{187}{245} \times 100\% \approx 76.33\%$$

**Step 5 — Compute the contact-area ratio (envelopment):**

$$E = \frac{A_{cushion}}{A_{hard}} = \frac{640}{220} \approx 2.91$$

This means the air cushion **envelops** nearly **2.91 times** the body area compared to a hard surface.

**Step 6 — Clinical Commentary:**

> The ROHO cushion achieves a **~76% pressure reduction** and increases contact area by a factor of **~2.9**. However, the residual pressure of **58 mmHg is still above** the capillary closure pressure of **32 mmHg**, so the cushion must be combined with **regular weight-shifts (every 30 minutes)** for full clinical protection. The cushion alone is necessary but not sufficient.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

| Property | **Foam** | **Gel** | **Air** | **Hybrid** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Material | Polyurethane (PU) open-cell | Polymer gel (silicone/viscoelastic) | Inflated neoprene cells | Foam + gel, or foam + air |
| Mechanism | Elastic deformation; partial immersion | Viscous flow; high envelopment | Air displacement; highest envelopment | Combined mechanisms |
| Pressure Redistribution | Moderate | Good | **Best** | Good–Excellent |
| Weight | **Lightest** | **Heaviest** | Light | Medium |
| Maintenance | Low | Low (avoid punctures) | **High** (daily calibration) | Medium |
| Bottoming Out | Yes (12–18 months) | No | No | No |
| Temperature Sensitivity | Low | **High** (stiffens in cold) | Low | Low–Medium |
| Cost | **Lowest** | Medium | **Highest** | Medium–High |
| Ideal User | Active, low-risk | Postural deformity, medium-risk | SCI, high-risk, full-time | General-purpose |

> **Synthesis:** No single cushion is universally superior. **Air cushions** dominate in pure pressure redistribution, but require user cognition and daily calibration. **Foam cushions** are pragmatic for active users prioritizing wheelchair weight. **Gel** offers middle-ground envelopment but adds mass. **Hybrid** designs attempt to combine the best attributes.

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the concept of **Tilt-in-Space** and **Recline** in a wheelchair seating system. Derive the expression for **interface pressure at the ischial tuberosity** as a function of tilt angle $\theta$. For an initial upright pressure of **$P_0 = 210$ mmHg**, compute the residual pressure at **$\theta = 35°$** and **$\theta = 45°$**. State the clinical limitation of tilt alone.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **postural support components** of a comprehensive seating system. Explain how **pelvic neutrality** is achieved through the interaction of the seat cushion, backrest, pelvic belt, and lateral trunk supports. Mention the standard **seat-to-back angle (Kappa, $\kappa$)** used for adult wheelchair users.

---

### Model Solution to Question B:

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Define Tilt-in-Space and Recline:**

- **Tilt-in-Space:** The entire seat and back assembly rotates backward as a unit, preserving the seat-to-back angle (hip angle). The user's pelvis remains in the same relative position.
- **Recline:** Only the backrest opens backward relative to the seat, increasing the hip angle. The seat itself does not tilt.

**Step 2 — Derive the IT pressure under tilt:**

When the seat tilts by angle $\theta$ from vertical, the body's weight vector $\vec{W}$ is resolved into:

$$W_{normal} = W \cos\theta \quad (\text{component perpendicular to seat surface})$$
$$W_{parallel} = W \sin\theta \quad (\text{component along seat surface, shear})$$

The normal component creates the interface pressure at the IT. Therefore:

$$P_{tilt}(\theta) = P_0 \cos\theta$$

**Step 3 — Compute at $\theta = 35°$:**

$$P_{35°} = 210 \times \cos(35°) = 210 \times 0.8192 \approx 172.0 \, \text{mmHg}$$

**Step 4 — Compute at $\theta = 45°$:**

$$P_{45°} = 210 \times \cos(45°) = 210 \times 0.7071 \approx 148.5 \, \text{mmHg}$$

**Step 5 — Clinical Limitation:**

> At $\theta = 45°$, even though the IT pressure drops to ~148.5 mmHg, this is still **far above** the 32 mmHg capillary closure threshold. **Pure tilt cannot fully offload** the IT; in fact, very high tilt angles can **increase** sacral pressure (posterior pelvis slides into the backrest junction). Therefore, tilt must be combined with a **redistribution cushion** *and* **recline** for full pressure relief in high-risk users.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Postural Support Components:**

1. **Seat Cushion** — provides pelvic foundation; must be contoured (slight anti-thrust ramp) to prevent forward sacral sitting.
2. **Backrest** — contoured or custom-molded; provides posterior trunk support and lumbar lordosis preservation.
3. **Pelvic Positioning Belt** — installed at 45° across the ASIS (anterior superior iliac spine) and greater trochanter; prevents forward sliding.
4. **Lateral Trunk Supports** — swing-away pads on the rib cage; correct scoliosis and prevent lateral collapse.
5. **Headrest** — occipital/sub-occipital support; required for users with poor neck control.
6. **Medial Thigh Support (Abductor Pommel)** — prevents hip adduction and scissoring.
7. **Footrest** — maintains 90° ankle dorsiflexion; prevents foot drop.

**Pelvic Neutrality Interaction:**

Pelvic neutrality is the *root* of seated posture. It is achieved when:
- The seat cushion's anti-thrust geometry (typically 5°–8° ramp at the front) **prevents posterior pelvic tilt**.
- The backrest's **lumbar contour** supports the lumbar lordosis, locking the sacrum in place.
- The **pelvic belt at 45°** anchors the ASIS, preventing forward migration.
- **Lateral trunk supports** prevent the trunk from collapsing, which would otherwise rotate the pelvis obliquely.

**Standard Seat-to-Back Angle (Kappa, $\kappa$):**

$$\kappa = 90° + \alpha$$

For typical adult wheelchair users:
- $\alpha = 0°$ → $\kappa = 90°$ (open hip, *not* recommended for long sitting)
- **$\alpha = 8°$–$12°$ → $\kappa = 98°$–$102°$** (standard for most adult users)
- $\alpha = 20°$ → $\kappa = 110°$ (reclined/tilted for high-pressure-risk users)
- $\alpha \geq 30°$ → $\kappa \geq 120°$ (custom molded seating for kyphotic or post-surgical users)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse "tilt" and "recline."** Tilt preserves the hip angle; recline changes it. Examiners frequently award 2 marks just for this distinction.
> 2. **Do not state that tilt alone can prevent pressure ulcers.** It cannot. The residual pressure is still above capillary closure. Always couple tilt with a redistribution cushion.
> 3. **Do not state the 45° belt rule incorrectly.** The belt goes from the ASIS across to the *opposite* greater trochanter at **45° to the seat plane** — many students write "60°" or "horizontal" and lose marks.
> 4. **Do not confuse "immersion" and "envelopment."** Immersion = depth of penetration; envelopment = contact area conformity. Both must be defined in any cushion design question.
> 5. **In numerical problems, always show units** (mmHg, cm², kg, N). A correct number without units is penalized at the KTU board level.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE (Module 3: Seating Technologies):**
>
> - ☐ **Capillary closure pressure = 32 mmHg** — universal threshold for cushion design.
> - ☐ **Body weight distribution while seated (erect) = 75% IT, 25% thighs** — shifts with recline.
> - ☐ **Core equation:** $P = F/A$ — increasing contact area is the primary cushion design strategy.
> - ☐ **PRR formula:** $PRR = (P_{hard} - P_{cush})/P_{hard} \times 100\%$ — typical good cushion: 60–80%.
> - ☐ **Tilt pressure:** $P_{tilt} = P_0 \cos\theta$ — tilt alone is **not** sufficient relief.
> - ☐ **Standard seat-to-back angle** $\kappa = 90° + \alpha$ with $\alpha = 8°$–$12°$ for adults.
> - ☐ **Pelvic belt installed at 45°** to seat plane (ASIS to greater trochanter).
> - ☐ **Cushion classes:** foam (light, cheap, low maintenance) / gel (heavy, enveloping) / air (best PRR, high maintenance) / hybrid (balanced).
> - ☐ **Tilt vs. Recline:** Tilt preserves hip angle; Recline increases it.
> - ☐ **Standard cushion materials:** PU foam, silicone gel, neoprene air cells, TPE honeycomb.
> - ☐ **Decubitus ulcer time-pressure rule:** Damage ∝ Pressure × Time — short bursts of high pressure are less harmful than sustained moderate pressure.
> - ☐ **Assessment algorithm:** Pelvis → Trunk → Head → Lower Extremities (always in this order).
> - ☐ **Standard sizes:** Seat width 16/18/20 in; seat depth 16/18/20 in; STF height 17.5–20.5 in.
> - ☐ **Government schemes to mention in 14-mark answers:** ALIMCO, ADIP, RPwD Act 2016.
> - ☐ **Key terms (definitions must be memorized):** immersion, envelopment, bottoming out, off-loading, neutral posture, tilt-in-space, recline, pommel, anti-thrust, contoured vs. planar, custom-molded seating.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Seating Assessment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT614 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Seating Assessment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

**Seating Assessment** is a systematic, multidisciplinary clinical evaluation process used to determine an individual's postural support needs, pressure management requirements, functional mobility goals, and environmental constraints in order to prescribe an optimal seating system (cushion, backrest, postural supports) and wheeled mobility base.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Approved):**
> *Seating Assessment is the structured clinical procedure that matches the user's physical, functional, and environmental needs to an appropriate seating and wheeled mobility intervention, thereby preventing deformity, managing tissue integrity, and maximizing functional independence.*

The assessment is performed by a **Seating and Mobility Team**, typically comprising:
- Rehabilitation Engineer / Prosthetist-Orthotist
- Occupational Therapist (OT) / Physiotherapist (PT)
- Wheelchair Clinician
- The User and Caregiver

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Customized Office Chair"

Imagine buying a suit off the rack versus a tailor-made suit. An off-the-rack suit may "kind of fit," but it pulls at the shoulders, restricts movement, and looks awkward. A tailored suit moves with you, supports where needed, and feels invisible.

A **seating assessment is the tailoring process** for a wheelchair user. The "client" is the user's body (with its unique shape, deformities, muscle tone, and pressure points), and the "tailor" is the clinical team. The output is a wheelchair seating system that supports, protects, and enables — exactly like a good suit does for walking.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Just as one office chair does not fit all employees, **no single wheelchair cushion or backrest fits all users**. A proper seating assessment ensures the "fit" is biomechanically correct, medically safe, and functionally enabling.

## 1.3 Why Seating Assessment is Critical — The "3 P's"

| Pillar | Function |
|---|---|
| **Postural Support** | Maintains spine/pelvis alignment, prevents scoliosis, pelvic obliquity, and contractures |
| **Pressure Management** | Redistributes load over the largest possible surface area to prevent pressure ulcers (decubitus) |
| **Performance / Function** | Optimizes upper limb access to wheels, joysticks, and the environment |

> [!WARNING]
> **Clinical Reality:** Studies show that **up to 80% of long-term wheelchair users develop postural deformities**, and **~30% develop pressure injuries** at some point. A proper seating assessment is the single most effective intervention to reduce both statistics.

## 1.4 Categories of Users Who Require Seating Assessment

- Spinal Cord Injury (SCI) — paraplegia, tetraplegia
- Cerebral Palsy (CP) — spastic, athetoid, ataxic
- Muscular Dystrophies (DMD, SMA)
- Stroke / Hemiplegia
- Multiple Sclerosis (MS)
- Geriatric users with frailty
- Amputees using wheelchairs
- Pediatric users with developmental delays

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Goals of a Comprehensive Seating Assessment

The goals are universally accepted in rehabilitation engineering literature and form the basis of WHO's *Guidelines on the Provision of Wheelchairs in Less-Resourced Settings*:

1. **Comfort** — minimize pain and fatigue during prolonged sitting.
2. **Postural Stability** — provide a stable base of support (pelvis → femur alignment).
3. **Pressure Distribution** — keep interface pressures below capillary closing pressure.
4. **Function** — enable reach, propulsion, transfers, and ADL (Activities of Daily Living).
5. **Prevention** — arrest or slow progression of skeletal deformity.
6. **Independence & Dignity** — empower the user socially and psychologically.
7. **Durability & Simplicity** — the device must suit the environment and caregiver capacity.

## 2.2 Reasons for Poor Seating (Why Assessment Fails Without a Process)

- **Mismatch** between user dimensions and equipment dimensions.
- **Inadequate postural support** allowing fixed deformity to develop.
- **Excessive interface pressure** over bony prominences.
- **Poor positioning of upper limbs** limiting self-propulsion.
- **Lack of stability** causing the user to "fight" the chair all day.
- **Heat & moisture buildup** from inappropriate cushion materials.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The single most common reason for seating failure is **pelvic instability** — once the pelvis is not level and neutral, every joint above (spine) and below (lower limbs) compensates abnormally.

## 2.3 The Two Main Categories of Postural Problems

| Type | Description | Seating Implication |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Postural Problem** | Caused directly by the user's pathology (e.g., flaccid trunk in SCI) | Must be supported by the seating system itself |
| **Secondary Postural Problem** | Caused by gravity, time, and poor positioning over months/years (e.g., windswept deformity) | May require aggressive corrective support or surgery referral |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Key Quantitative Metrics

| Metric | Formula / Standard Value | Unit | Clinical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Capillary Closing Pressure (CCP) | **32 mmHg** (≈ 4.3 kPa) | mmHg / kPa | Pressure threshold above which tissue ischemia begins |
| Seat-to-Pop-Height | User popliteal length + 2–4 cm clearance | cm | Determines seat depth/height |
| Seat Width | Greater trochanter width + 2–4 cm | cm | Determines cushion/base width |
| Seat-to-Armrest Height | User elbow height + 2.5 cm | cm | Determines armrest height |
| Backrest Height | Shoulder height − 5 cm (for self-prop) | cm | Determines backrest height |
| Seat Plane Angle | **0°–15° posterior tilt** (typical 5°–10°) | degrees (°) | Prevents forward sliding |
| Pressure Threshold (loaded) | **< 32 mmHg** at ischial tuberosities | mmHg | Pressure mapping target |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL — Use `\vert` and `\mid` for absolute values in KTU exam answers.** Never use the bare pipe `\|` character, as it will break markdown table rendering. Example: $\lvert \Delta P \rvert < 32 \text{ mmHg}$.

## 2.5 Interface Pressure (P) — The Key Equation

The interface pressure between the user and the cushion is governed by the basic force–area relationship:

$$P \;=\; \frac{F}{A}$$

Where:
- $P$ = Interface pressure (in **mmHg** or **kPa**)
- $F$ = Force applied (body weight component, in **Newtons**)
- $A$ = Contact surface area (in **cm²** or **m²**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** A larger contact area (immersion, envelopment) reduces pressure. This is precisely why **contoured, foam-carved, or fluid cushions** outperform flat boards — they maximize the denominator $A$.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles of seating assessment feed directly into:
- **Finite Element Analysis (FEA)** of cushion deformation under load
- **Pressure Mapping Sensor Array Design** (capacitive / resistive sensor grids)
- **CAD modeling** of custom-molded seating
- **Biomechanics research** on wheelchair propulsion efficiency
- **Robotics & Smart Wheelchairs** with auto-tilt sensors

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Process & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Seating Assessment — Step-by-Step Clinical Workflow

Below is the **standard 10-step process** universally followed in seating clinics. Every step is examinable for KTU.

### Step 1 — Referral & Background Information
Collect:
- Diagnosis, prognosis, medications (especially spasticity meds)
- Onset of disability, prior equipment history
- Living environment (door widths, ramp access, toilet layout)
- Caregiver availability and physical capacity
- User goals and expectations

> **Why:** A cushion that is perfect clinically is useless if it cannot be cleaned in the user's home.

### Step 2 — Physical / Mat Assessment
With the user **supine on a plinth (mat)**, evaluate:

- **Spine:** Kyphosis, lordosis, scoliosis, fixed vs. flexible curves
- **Pelvis:** Anterior/posterior tilt, obliquity, rotation
- **Hips:** Range of Motion (ROM) — flexion, abduction, adduction
- **Knees & Ankles:** Hamstring tightness, ankle dorsiflexion
- **Muscle Tone:** Modified Ashworth Scale (0–4)
- **Skin Integrity:** Existing pressure injuries, scar tissue
- **Sensation:** Light touch, proprioception

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always document whether a deformity is **fixed (non-reducible)** or **flexible (reducible)**. A flexible kyphosis can be corrected by a contoured backrest; a fixed one cannot.

### Step 3 — Seated (Simulated) Assessment
Transfer user onto a **simulation chair** or mat with adjustable wedges:
- Identify the **neutral pelvis position** (the "sweet spot")
- Measure angles of correction achievable
- Try different backrest contours and cushion types
- Assess head control, upper limb function, vision line

### Step 4 — Functional Assessment
Evaluate the user **doing** activities, not just lying/sitting:
- Wheelchair propulsion ability
- Transfers (bed ↔ chair)
- Reach, grasp, eating, phone use
- Ability to shift weight (pressure relief)

### Step 5 — Pressure Mapping (Optional but Standard)
Use a **pressure mapping mat** (e.g., Tekscan, Xsensor) between the user and cushion:
- Identify high-pressure zones (red zones > 32 mmHg)
- Compare cushion options quantitatively
- Document with a colour-coded image

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Rule:** For healthy tissue, peak interface pressure should be **< 32 mmHg**; for high-risk (SCI, elderly) users, target is **< 20 mmHg**.

### Step 6 — Measurement Capture
Record all anthropometric measurements:

$$\text{Seat Width} \;=\; \text{Greater Trochanter width} + 2 \text{ to } 4 \text{ cm}$$

$$\text{Seat Depth} \;=\; \text{Buttock-to-Popliteal length} + 2 \text{ to } 4 \text{ cm}$$

$$\text{Backrest Height} \;=\; \text{Shoulder height} - 5 \text{ cm (for self-propulsion)}$$

$$\text{Seat-to-Footrest} \;=\; \text{Popliteal height} + 2 \text{ to } 4 \text{ cm}$$

### Step 7 — Equipment Trial (Mock-Up)
Assemble a trial setup using modular parts:
- Adjustable cushion
- Adjustable backrest
- Lateral trunk supports
- Pelvic positioning belt
- Headrest, if needed
- Footrests and armrests

User trials the setup for at least 30 minutes; observe comfort and pressure redistribution.

### Step 8 — Environmental & Contextual Assessment
- Home visit or photo/video of the home
- Mode of transport (car, bus, auto)
- Workplace, school, or community access
- Climate (humid areas need breathable covers)

### Step 9 — Prescription & Documentation
Write a **seating prescription** that specifies:
- Cushion type, material, density, dimensions, cover
- Backrest type, height, contour, mounting hardware
- Lateral supports, headrest, belts, harnesses
- Wheelchair base type, frame dimensions, axle position
- Funding source and follow-up schedule

### Step 10 — Follow-Up & Re-Assessment
- Re-evaluate at **2 weeks, 3 months, 6 months, then annually**
- Re-assess if there is any change in weight, surgery, or skin condition
- Children: every **3–6 months** (growth-related changes)

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Seating Problems & Clinical Solutions

| Observed Problem | Likely Cause | Seating Solution |
|---|---|---|
| Forward sliding out of chair | Posterior pelvic tilt, no tilt-in-space | Adjust seat plane angle (5°–10° posterior tilt); add pelvic belt |
| Leaning to one side (pelvic obliquity) | Scoliosis, hip contracture, short leg | Wedge under shorter side; lateral trunk support on collapsed side |
| Windswept hips (both knees to one side) | Asymmetric tone, pelvic rotation | Pommel between knees; hip guides on chair |
| Pressure sore on sacrum | Sacral sitting, reclined backrest | Open seat-to-back angle; check cushion contour; tilt-in-space |
| Red ischial tuberosities | Poor immersion in cushion | Switch to deeper immersion cushion (fluid/foam combo) |
| Cannot self-propel | Arms too high, wheels too far forward | Lower armrests; move axle forward; adjust COG |
| Head drops forward | Poor head control (CP, ALS) | Headrest with lateral pads; tilt-in-space chair |

## 3.3 Mat Assessment — Detailed Technique (KTU High-Yield)

> **Procedure (supine, hips/knees flexed, feet flat):**
> 1. Observe pelvis — is it level? Rotated? Tilted?
> 2. Palpate ASIS and PSIS — assess tilt.
> 3. Slowly lower legs over the edge of the mat → record the pelvic position at neutral.
> 4. Place a **goniometer** at the hip joint → measure hip flexion ROM.
> 5. This neutral position becomes the **target seated posture** for the wheelchair setup.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Mat Assessment is considered the GOLD STANDARD of seating evaluation.** The pelvis-on-mat position is the most accurate simulation of the desired seated posture. KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Why is the mat assessment called the gold standard?"* — Answer: It isolates postural variables without the confounding effect of the wheelchair frame.

## 3.4 Pressure Mapping — Quantitative Threshold Table

| Colour Zone | Pressure Range (mmHg) | Action |
|---|---|---|
| 🟢 Green | 0 – 20 | Safe; no action |
| 🟡 Yellow | 20 – 32 | Caution; monitor; consider cushion change for high-risk users |
| 🟠 Orange | 32 – 60 | Danger; immediate intervention |
| 🔴 Red | > 60 | Critical tissue damage risk; emergency repositioning |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
