<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define Seating Assessment. List its four primary goals.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Seating Assessment is a systematic clinical evaluation of a wheelchair user's postural, functional, pressure-management, and environmental needs to prescribe an appropriate seating and mobility system.

**Four Primary Goals:**
1. **Postural Support** — maintain neutral pelvis and spine alignment.
2. **Pressure Management** — keep interface pressure below capillary closing pressure (32 mmHg).
3. **Functional Performance** — enable upper-limb access for propulsion and ADL.
4. **Prevention of Complications** — prevent deformities and pressure injuries.

---

### **Q2. Why is the Mat Assessment considered the gold standard in seating evaluation?** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**Model Answer:**

The Mat Assessment is considered the gold standard because:
1. It allows the clinician to evaluate the user's posture **without the confounding influence** of the wheelchair frame.
2. The pelvis and spine can be palpated and observed directly.
3. Fixed versus flexible deformities can be clearly differentiated.
4. The **neutral pelvic position** achieved on the mat becomes the **target posture** for the final wheelchair setup.
5. It provides reproducible, comparable measurements across multiple visits.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay / Long Answer (14 Marks)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`

**"Explain in detail the step-by-step process of Seating Assessment. Discuss the role of pressure mapping in the assessment."**

#### **(a) Step-by-Step Process of Seating Assessment — 7 Marks**

The Seating Assessment follows a **10-step structured clinical workflow**:

1. **Referral and Background Information** — Diagnosis, prognosis, environment, caregiver.
2. **Physical/Mat Assessment** — Supine evaluation of spine, pelvis, hips, knees, tone, skin.
3. **Seated/Simulated Assessment** — Wedges and adjustable chairs to find neutral posture.
4. **Functional Assessment** — Propulsion, transfers, ADL observation.
5. **Pressure Mapping** — Quantitative load distribution analysis.
6. **Measurement Capture** — Anthropometric dimensions (seat width, depth, backrest height).
7. **Equipment Trial** — Mock-up with modular parts; user trials for 30+ minutes.
8. **Environmental Assessment** — Home, transport, workplace, climate.
9. **Prescription and Documentation** — Detailed written specifications.
10. **Follow-Up** — Re-evaluation at 2 weeks, 3 months, 6 months, then annually.

> **Key Equation:**
> $$\text{Seat Depth} = \text{Buttock-to-Popliteal} + 2 \text{ to } 4 \text{ cm}$$

> **Key Equation:**
> $$\text{Seat Width} = \text{Greater Trochanter width} + 2 \text{ to } 4 \text{ cm}$$

#### **(b) Role of Pressure Mapping in Seating Assessment — 7 Marks**

**Definition:** Pressure mapping is the use of a thin sensor mat (capacitive/resistive) placed between the user and the cushion to measure real-time interface pressure distribution.

**Key Roles:**
1. **Quantification:** Converts qualitative "feel" into objective mmHg values.
2. **Risk Identification:** Highlights red zones (> 60 mmHg) over ischial tuberosities and sacrum.
3. **Cushion Comparison:** Allows A/B testing of different cushion materials objectively.
4. **Documentation:** Generates a permanent visual record for the prescription file.
5. **Education:** Helps users visualize their own pressure patterns and learn pressure relief.
6. **Re-evaluation:** Compares post-cushion-change maps to confirm intervention success.

**Pressure–Risk Threshold Table:**

| Interface Pressure | Tissue Status |
|---|---|
| < 20 mmHg | Safe for most users |
| 20–32 mmHg | Caution — high-risk users |
| 32–60 mmHg | Tissue damage likely |
| > 60 mmHg | Emergency; necrosis risk |

> **Key Equation:**
> $$P = \frac{F}{A} \quad \text{where } P = \text{pressure, } F = \text{load force, } A = \text{contact area}$$

Pressure mapping directly supports the goal of keeping $P < 32 \text{ mmHg}$ at all bony prominences.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Internal Choice

**"Discuss the various postural problems encountered during seating assessment. Describe how seating solutions address pelvic obliquity, windsweeping, and sacral sitting."**

#### **(a) Common Postural Problems in Seating — 7 Marks**

| # | Postural Problem | Clinical Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Posterior Pelvic Tilt** | Pelvis tips backward; user slides forward |
| 2 | **Anterior Pelvic Tilt** | Pelvis tips forward; exaggerated lumbar lordosis |
| 3 | **Pelvic Obliquity** | Uneven pelvic height (left ≠ right) |
| 4 | **Pelvic Rotation** | One ASIS forward, one backward |
| 5 | **Windswept Deformity** | Both knees drift to one side; pelvis rotates |
| 6 | **Sacral Sitting** | User sits on sacrum, not ischial tuberosities |
| 7 | **Scoliosis** | Lateral curvature of the spine |
| 8 | **Kyphosis (fixed / flexible)** | Excessive thoracic curve |

**Cause:** Primary (from pathology) + Secondary (from gravity and time).

#### **(b) Seating Solutions for Three Specific Problems — 7 Marks**

**1. Pelvic Obliquity:**
- Place a **wedge** under the lower (collapsed) side of the pelvis.
- Use a **contoured cushion** with built-in pelvic obliquity correction.
- Add a **pelvic positioning belt** at 45°–60° to maintain neutral pelvis.

**2. Windsweeping:**
- Use a **pommel** (medial knee separator) between the knees.
- Add **hip guides / adductor pads** on the wheelchair.
- Provide a **contoured cushion** with an asymmetric well for the rotated pelvis.

**3. Sacral Sitting:**
- Open the **seat-to-back angle** to > 100° (e.g., 95°–100° is typical; 105° reduces sacral loading).
- Apply **5°–10° posterior seat tilt** to keep ischial tuberosities loaded.
- Use a **tilt-in-space wheelchair** for pressure redistribution.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. ❌ **Do not** confuse *seat-to-back angle* with *seat plane tilt*. They are different — one is the backrest angle, the other is the cushion angle.
> 2. ❌ **Do not** write "tilt-in-space" as "recline" — tilt keeps the seat-to-back angle constant; recline changes it.
> 3. ❌ **Do not** recommend aggressive corrections for **fixed deformities** — you must accept the deformity and support around it.
> 4. ❌ **Always** convert mmHg to kPa when both are required: $1 \text{ mmHg} \approx 0.133 \text{ kPa}$.
> 5. ❌ **Never** use the pipe character $\vert$ inside markdown table cells — it breaks table rendering. Use $\lvert \cdots \rvert$ in LaTeX.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Seating Assessment** = systematic clinical evaluation of posture, pressure, function, and environment.
- ✅ The **3 P's** to remember: **Posture, Pressure, Performance**.
- ✅ **Mat Assessment** is the gold standard for postural evaluation.
- ✅ **Capillary Closing Pressure (CCP) = 32 mmHg** is the critical threshold.
- ✅ **Pressure formula:** $P = \frac{F}{A}$ — larger contact area means lower pressure.
- ✅ **Seat plane angle** of 5°–10° posterior tilt prevents forward sliding.
- ✅ Always differentiate **fixed vs. flexible** deformities.
- ✅ **Pressure mapping** quantifies cushion performance objectively.
- ✅ **Follow-up schedule:** 2 weeks → 3 months → 6 months → annually.
- ✅ **Children** need re-assessment every 3–6 months due to growth.
- ✅ **Tilt-in-space** ≠ **Recline** — both redistribute pressure, but tilt preserves the seat-to-back angle.
- ✅ **Anthropometric tolerances:** add 2–4 cm clearance on all linear measurements.
- ✅ The **pelvis is the keystone** — once it is stable and neutral, all other corrections become easier.
- ✅ **WHO guidelines** mandate a multidisciplinary team for every comprehensive seating prescription.
- ✅ Always assess **caregiver capacity** before prescribing complex equipment.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principles of Intervention
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT614 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Principles of Intervention

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Assistive Medical Devices (AMD)** and rehabilitation engineering, **Principles of Intervention** refers to the foundational, evidence-based guidelines, ethical considerations, and systematic frameworks that govern the design, selection, prescription, application, and evaluation of assistive technologies intended to restore, augment, or compensate for impaired human function. These principles integrate the **biopsychosocial model of disability**, **user-centered design philosophy**, and the **World Health Organization's International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health (WHO-ICF)** framework to ensure that every technological intervention meaningfully addresses a person's lived experience of disability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Definition Anchor**
> An *intervention* in assistive technology is **NOT** merely the device itself. It is the **complete process** that encompasses assessment, prescription, fitting, training, follow-up, and outcome evaluation — of which the device is only one component.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a tailor making a bespoke suit for a customer. The tailor does not just hand the customer a pre-made jacket off the rack. Instead, the tailor first **measures** the customer, then **understands** the occasion (wedding vs. office), then **designs** the fabric pattern, then **stitches**, then **does fittings** and makes **alterations**, and finally follows up to ensure the customer is comfortable.

In the same way:

| Tailoring Step | Assistive Technology Intervention Step |
|---|---|
| Measuring the customer | **Assessment** of the user's body, function, environment |
| Understanding the occasion | **Goal-setting** with the user (what do they want to achieve?) |
| Choosing fabric and design | **Device selection** (manual wheelchair vs. powered, etc.) |
| Stitching the suit | **Fitting and customization** of the device |
| Final fitting | **Training** the user to use the device |
| Customer feedback | **Follow-up and outcome evaluation** |

If the tailor skips any step, the suit fails. Likewise, skipping any principle of intervention causes the assistive device to be **abandoned, misused, or harmful**. The standard statistic cited in rehabilitation literature is that approximately **one-third of assistive devices are abandoned** within a year of provision — almost always due to violation of one or more intervention principles.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The device is only the *visible* output. The *invisible* work — the principles — is what makes it work for the human.

## 1.3 Core Operational Metric in Assistive Intervention

Although this is a non-numerical topic, two universally cited KTU-relevant parameters are:

- **Device Acceptance Rate (DAR):** The percentage of users who continue to use a prescribed device after **6 to 12 months**. Industry benchmark for a well-designed intervention: **> 75%**.
- **Goal Attainment Scaling (GAS) Score:** A standardized metric measuring whether intervention goals are achieved. Each goal is rated on a 5-point scale from $-2$ (much worse) to $+2$ (much better than expected), aggregated as:

$$ \text{GAS} = 50 + \frac{10 \sum (w_i \cdot x_i)}{\sqrt{(1-\rho) \sum w_i^2 + \rho (\sum w_i)^2}} $$

where $w_i$ is the weight of goal $i$, $x_i$ is the attained score, and $\rho$ is the expected correlation among goal scores (commonly $\rho = 0.3$).

> [!TIP]
> The GAS formula is the **only quantitative formula** in this entire module that KTU examiners can ask you to write down. Memorize its structure and the meaning of the $50$ baseline (population mean) and the $10$ (population standard deviation multiplier).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

## 2.1 The Eight Core Principles of Intervention

The principles of intervention in assistive medical devices can be consolidated into **eight universally accepted pillars**. Each is presented in KTU-board-exam-friendly bullet structure with the "Why" and "How" explicitly stated.

### Principle 1: User-Centered Design (UCD)

- **Why:** Devices designed *for* the user often fail *with* the user. Disability is experienced personally; only the user knows their true goals.
- **How:** Involve the end-user in *every* stage — from needs assessment to prototype testing. Use tools like interviews, focus groups, contextual inquiry, and participatory design workshops.
- **KTU buzzword to remember:** *Nothing about us without us* — the disability rights slogan adopted into assistive technology policy.

### Principle 2: Holistic / Biopsychosocial Assessment

- **Why:** A person is not just a "broken body part." Disability is the interaction of **Body Structure/Function + Activity + Participation + Environmental Factors + Personal Factors**.
- **How:** Use the **WHO-ICF framework** (explained in Section 2.3) instead of the older purely *medical model* which focused only on impairment.

### Principle 3: Goal-Directed Intervention

- **Why:** Devices without clear goals are evaluated by intuition, not outcomes.
- **How:** Use the **SMART** criteria — **S**pecific, **M**easurable, **A**chievable, **R**elevant, **T**ime-bound. Document goals jointly with the user.

### Principle 4: Minimal Intervention / Least Restriction

- **Why:** Over-prescribing a complex device when a simple one suffices wastes resources, increases abandonment risk, and can create dependency.
- **How:** Apply the **"least assistive technology necessary"** heuristic. Start with the simplest effective solution and escalate only if required.

### Principle 5: Environmental and Contextual Fit

- **Why:** A wheelchair designed for a smooth city apartment will fail on rural unpaved paths. The same device behaves differently in different physical, social, and cultural environments.
- **How:** Conduct a **home and community assessment** — door widths, ramp availability, caregiver support, climate, stigma, and cultural acceptance.

### Principle 6: Interdisciplinary Team Approach

- **Why:** No single professional holds all the expertise. A user with a complex condition needs perspectives from multiple disciplines.
- **How:** Assemble a team including: **physiatrist, occupational therapist (OT), physiotherapist (PT), prosthetist-orthotist, biomedical engineer, social worker, and the user + family**.

### Principle 7: Evidence-Based Practice (EBP)

- **Why:** Clinical decisions must rest on validated research, not marketing claims or anecdotal experience.
- **How:** Integrate the three pillars of EBP: **(a) best available research evidence, (b) clinical expertise, (c) patient values and circumstances**.

### Principle 8: Outcome Evaluation and Follow-Up

- **Why:** Provision is not the endpoint; it is the *midpoint* of intervention. Devices need repair, re-fitting, and the user's condition may change.
- **How:** Schedule **structured follow-up** at 1 month, 3 months, 6 months, and 12 months post-provision. Use standardized outcome measures (GAS, FIM, COPM, QUEST 2.0).

## 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

| # | Principle | One-Line KTU Definition | Common KTU Exam Cue |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | User-Centered Design | User is co-designer, not passive recipient | "Participatory design" |
| 2 | Biopsychosocial Assessment | Assess body + activity + participation + environment | "ICF model" |
| 3 | Goal-Directed (SMART) | Every intervention must have measurable goals | "SMART goals" |
| 4 | Minimal Intervention | Start simple; escalate only if needed | "Least restrictive" |
| 5 | Environmental Fit | Device must match user's physical & social context | "Contextual assessment" |
| 6 | Interdisciplinary Team | Multiple professionals collaborate | "Team-based care" |
| 7 | Evidence-Based Practice | Research + expertise + patient values | "EBP triad" |
| 8 | Outcome Evaluation & Follow-up | Continuous monitoring, re-assessment, re-fitting | "Longitudinal follow-up" |

## 2.3 The WHO-ICF Framework (The Theoretical Backbone)

The **International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health (ICF)** is the single most important framework a KTU student must know for any disability or assistive-device question. It replaces the older *medical model* with the *biopsychosocial model*.

**ICF Components:**

1. **Body Functions and Structures** — physiological and anatomical parts (e.g., muscle strength, joint range).
2. **Activities** — execution of a task by an individual (e.g., walking, dressing).
3. **Participation** — involvement in life situations (e.g., going to school, working, socializing).
4. **Environmental Factors** — physical, social, and attitudinal environment (facilitators or barriers).
5. **Personal Factors** — age, gender, motivation, coping style, lifestyle (not classified in ICF but acknowledged).

The critical conceptual shift:

$$ \text{Medical Model (Old)}: \text{Disability} = f(\text{Impairment}) \quad \Rightarrow \text{Disability is IN the person} $$

$$ \text{Biopsychosocial / ICF Model (New)}: \text{Disability} = f(\text{Impairment}, \text{Activity}, \text{Participation}, \text{Environment}) \quad \Rightarrow \text{Disability is an INTERACTION} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU frequently asked distinction:** In the medical model, the *problem* is inside the person and must be "fixed." In the ICF/ biopsychosocial model, the *problem* is the **mismatch** between the person and their environment — which means the environment can be modified (curb cut, screen reader) just as legitimately as the person (surgery, device).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These principles are not abstract — they have direct engineering consequences:

- A **prosthetic limb** designed without user input may have cosmesis the engineer loves but weight the user rejects.
- A **hearing aid** prescribed without environmental assessment will be over-amplified in quiet offices and under-amplified in noisy markets.
- A **powered wheelchair** selected without team input may exceed the user's cognitive ability to safely operate it.
- A **screen reader** delivered without training becomes an expensive paperweight.

The phrase engineers must internalize: **"A device is only as good as the intervention system that delivers it."**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown of the Intervention Process

> **Note to students:** This module has no calculus-based derivations. Instead, the "derivation" here is the **logical sequence of decision-making** that an assistive technology professional follows — comparable in rigour to a multi-step engineering derivation, and equally important in KTU exam answers.

## 3.1 The Six-Stage Intervention Process (Step-by-Step Logical Deduction)

The intervention process is logically *deduced* from the eight principles in Section 2.1. Each stage *must* follow from the previous; skipping stages breaks the logical chain.

---

### **STAGE 1 — Referral and Initial Contact**

- **Logical starting point:** Someone (self, family, doctor, teacher) recognizes a need.
- **Action:** Document the referral, the referrer's concern, and the user's own stated concern (these may differ).
- **KTU exam phrase to use:** *"The intervention process is initiated upon identification of a functional need by the user, family, or referring professional."*

---

### **STAGE 2 — Multidisciplinary Assessment**

- **Logical deduction:** Because Principle 2 (holistic) and Principle 6 (team) demand it, this stage *cannot* be done by a single professional.
- **Three sub-assessments performed in parallel:**

  1. **Impairment-level assessment** (medical): diagnosis, prognosis, stability of condition.
  2. **Functional assessment** (OT/PT): what can the user currently do, and what *exactly* do they want to do? Standardized tools: **FIM (Functional Independence Measure), Barthel Index, COPM (Canadian Occupational Performance Measure)**.
  3. **Environmental assessment** (OT/social worker): physical barriers, social support, financial resources, cultural context.

- **Output of Stage 2:** A written *assessment report* containing:
  - User profile (age, diagnosis, prognosis, stability).
  - Current functional status (what they can / cannot do).
  - User-stated goals (in their own words).
  - Environmental constraints and facilitators.
  - Preliminary recommendation category (no device, simple device, complex device, or referral for further medical management).

---

### **STAGE 3 — Goal Setting Using SMART Framework**

- **Logical deduction:** From Principle 3 (goal-directed), vague wishes like *"I want to be more independent"* are operationally useless. They must be converted into SMART goals.

**SMART Criteria Table:**

| Letter | Meaning | Example (Imprecise → SMART) |
|---|---|---|
| S | Specific | "I want to walk" → *"I want to walk 50 metres to the bus stop"* |
| M | Measurable | Use distance, count, time, or standardized score |
| A | Achievable | Must be realistic given impairment and prognosis |
| R | Relevant | Must matter to the *user*, not just the clinician |
| T | Time-bound | Specify a deadline (e.g., *"by 3 months post-delivery"*) |

- **Document goals at three ICF levels:** body function (e.g., increase grip strength from 10 to 20 kg), activity (e.g., open a jar independently), participation (e.g., return to part-time work).

---

### **STAGE 4 — Device Selection and Prescription**

- **Logical deduction:** This is where Principles 1, 4, 5, and 7 converge. The "best" device is the one that **best matches the user + goal + environment**, supported by evidence, and is the *least* restrictive option that achieves the goal.

- **Decision algorithm (logical flow):**

  $$\text{If goal is achievable without any device} \rightarrow \text{No device (just training or environmental change)}$$

  $$\text{If goal is achievable with a simple, low-cost device} \rightarrow \text{Simple device}$$

  $$\text{If simple device fails or is insufficient} \rightarrow \text{Complex device with team consensus}$$

  $$\text{If no device fully meets the goal} \rightarrow \text{Custom fabrication / modification}$$

- **Matching tool:** The **Matching Person and Technology (MPT) Model** by Marcia Scherer is widely used. It evaluates three matches: *Person ↔ Technology*, *Person ↔ Environment*, *Technology ↔ Environment*. The device is appropriate only when **all three** matches are satisfactory.

---

### **STAGE 5 — Fitting, Customization, and Training**

- **Logical deduction:** Principles 1 and 8 demand that the device is not "handed over" but *fitted* to the user and accompanied by structured training.

- **Fitting steps:** physical measurement, alignment, adjustment of straps/cushions/controls, pressure mapping (for seating), trial use in clinical setting.
- **Training steps:** user training, caregiver training, training in realistic environments (not just the clinic), written instructions in user's language, troubleshooting guidance.
- **Why this stage is non-negotiable:** A study by Phillips & Zhao (1993, cited in WHO GATE initiative) reported that **training quality is the single strongest predictor of device non-abandonment** — stronger than the device's technical sophistication.

---

### **STAGE 6 — Follow-Up, Outcome Evaluation, and Re-Assessment**

- **Logical deduction:** Principle 8 demands longitudinal evaluation. The user's condition may change (progression of disease, recovery, ageing), the device may wear, and goals may evolve.

- **Standard follow-up schedule:**

  $$\text{Follow-up visits at: } t = 1 \text{ month, } 3 \text{ months, } 6 \text{ months, } 12 \text{ months (annually thereafter)}$$

- **Standardized outcome instruments to use:**

  | Instrument | What it Measures | KTU Use |
  |---|---|---|
  | GAS (Goal Attainment Scaling) | Extent to which personalized goals are met | Most flexible; the formula in §1.3 may be asked |
  | QUEST 2.0 (Quebec User Evaluation of Satisfaction with assistive Technology) | User satisfaction with device and service | Useful for service-quality questions |
  | FIM (Functional Independence Measure) | Burden of care / level of independence | For severe disability scenarios |
  | COPM (Canadian Occupational Performance Measure) | Self-perceived change in occupational performance | For goal-directed OT questions |
  | WHO-DAS 2.0 | Disability based on ICF | For ICF-aligned questions |

- **Decision tree at follow-up:**

  $$\text{Is goal achieved AND user satisfied AND device still functional?} \rightarrow \text{Continue, re-evaluate in 6-12 months}$$

  $$\text{Is goal partially achieved OR user has minor issues?} \rightarrow \text{Adjust / re-train / minor repair}$$

  $$\text{Is goal not achieved OR user dissatisfied OR device broken/unsafe?} \rightarrow \text{Re-enter Stage 4 (new prescription)}$$

---

## 3.2 Complete Logical Chain — Summary

The entire intervention process can therefore be written as a single logical sequence:

$$\text{Need} \rightarrow \text{Assessment} \rightarrow \text{Goal (SMART)} \rightarrow \text{Select} \rightarrow \text{Fit+Train} \rightarrow \text{Evaluate} \rightarrow \{\text{Continue} \mid \text{Adjust} \mid \text{Re-select}\}$$

This six-stage chain is the **single most high-yield structure** for any 14-mark KTU question on "principles of intervention." Memorize the stages, the tools used at each stage, and the underlying principle that justifies each stage.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Six-Stage Intervention Process (Flow Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[STAGE 1: Referral and Initial Contact] --> B[STAGE 2: Multidisciplinary Assessment]
    B --> B1[Medical: Diagnosis and Prognosis]
    B --> B2[Functional: FIM / COPM / Barthel]
    B --> B3[Environmental: Home and Community Audit]
    B1 --> C[STAGE 3: SMART Goal Setting]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> D[STAGE 4: Device Selection and Prescription]
    D --> D1{Least restrictive device that meets the goal?}
    D1 -- Yes, no device needed --> D2[No device: Training or environment change only]
    D1 -- Yes, simple device --> D3[Standard simple device]
    D1 -- Yes, complex device --> D4[Standard complex device with team consensus]
    D1 -- No match found --> D5[Custom fabrication or modification]
    D2 --> E[STAGE 5: Fitting Customization and Training]
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E
    E --> E1[Physical fitting and alignment]
    E --> E2[User training]
    E --> E3[Caregiver training]
    E --> E4[Training in real environments]
    E1 --> F[STAGE 6: Follow-Up and Outcome Evaluation]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    F --> F1{Goal achieved AND user satisfied AND device functional?}
    F1 -- Continue --> F2[Schedule next follow-up at 6 to 12 months]
    F1 -- Adjust --> F3[Re-train or minor repair then re-evaluate]
    F1 -- Re-select --> F4[Return to Stage 4 for new prescription]
```

## 4.2 The WHO-ICF Framework (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HEALTH_CONDITION["Health Condition: Disorder or Disease"]
        HC[Disorder or Disease]
    end
    subgraph BODY["Body Level"]
        BF[Body Functions]
        BS[Body Structures]
    end
    subgraph INDIVIDUAL["Individual Level"]
        ACT[Activities: Task Execution by Individual]
    end
    subgraph SOCIETY["Societal Level"]
        PAR[Participation: Involvement in Life Situations]
    end
    subgraph CONTEXT["Contextual Factors"]
        ENV[Environmental Factors: Facilitators or Barriers]
        PERS[Personal Factors: Motivation, Age, Lifestyle]
    end
    HC --> BF
    HC --> BS
    BF --> ACT
    BS --> ACT
    ACT --> PAR
    ENV -. modulates .-> ACT
    ENV -. modulates .-> PAR
    PERS -. modulates .-> ACT
    PERS -. modulates .-> PAR
```

> **Reading guide:** Solid arrows represent *direct influence*; dotted arrows represent *moderating influence*. The same Health Condition can produce vastly different levels of Activity limitation and Participation restriction depending on Environmental and Personal Factors — this is the *biopsychosocial* core idea.

## 4.3 The Matching Person and Technology (MPT) Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Person: Needs, Preferences, Skills, Expectations]
    T[Technology: Device Features, Functions, Aesthetics]
    E[Environment: Physical, Social, Cultural, Financial]
    P <--> T
    T <--> E
    P <--> E
    MATCH{All three matches satisfactory?}
    MATCH -- Yes --> USE[Successful device use and adoption]
    MATCH -- No, person technology mismatch --> AB1[Device abandoned: wrong device for user]
    MATCH -- No, person environment mismatch --> AB2[Device abandoned: wrong context for user]
    MATCH -- No, technology environment mismatch --> AB3[Device abandoned: wrong device for context]
```

## 4.4 Eight Principles ↔ Six Stages (Mapping Matrix Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Principle 1: User Centered Design] -.applies to.-> S1[Stage 1 Referral]
    P1 -.applies to.-> S5[Stage 5 Fitting and Training]
    P2[Principle 2: Biopsychosocial Assessment] -.applies to.-> S2[Stage 2 Assessment]
    P3[Principle 3: SMART Goals] -.applies to.-> S3[Stage 3 Goal Setting]
    P4[Principle 4: Minimal Intervention] -.applies to.-> S4[Stage 4 Device Selection]
    P5[Principle 5: Environmental Fit] -.applies to.-> S2[Stage 2 Assessment]
    P5 -.applies to.-> S4[Stage 4 Device Selection]
    P6[Principle 6: Interdisciplinary Team] -.applies to.-> S2[Stage 2 Assessment]
    P6 -.applies to.-> S4[Stage 4 Device Selection]
    P7[Principle 7: Evidence Based Practice] -.applies to.-> S4[Stage 4 Device Selection]
    P8[Principle 8: Outcome Evaluation and Follow-Up] -.applies to.-> S6[Stage 6 Follow-Up]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
*Define the term "intervention" in the context of assistive medical devices. Why is intervention considered broader than simply providing a device?*

**Model Answer (3 marks — keyword-mapped for KTU valuation):**

In the context of assistive medical devices, an **intervention** is the **complete, structured process** by which an assistive device is *assessed, prescribed, fitted, customized, trained upon, and followed up* for a person with functional limitations. **(1 mark)**

Intervention is broader than device provision because: **(2 marks)**

1. The **device is only one component** of a multi-step process that also includes assessment, goal setting, fitting, training, and outcome evaluation.
2. Without these surrounding steps, the device is likely to be **abandoned** — research indicates that roughly **one-third of all assistive devices are abandoned** within the first year of use, primarily due to inadequate assessment, training, or follow-up.
3. The intervention ensures the device is **matched to the user's goals, body, environment, and cultural context** — something mere device delivery cannot guarantee.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*List any **four** core principles of intervention in assistive technology and briefly explain the meaning of "least restrictive intervention."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Four core principles:** **(2 marks — 0.5 each)**

1. User-Centered Design
2. Goal-Directed (SMART) Intervention
3. Environmental and Contextual Fit
4. Outcome Evaluation and Follow-Up

*(Accept any four from the eight pillars listed in §2.1.)*

**Meaning of Least Restrictive Intervention:** **(1 mark)**

The *least restrictive intervention* principle states that the **simplest, least intrusive, lowest-cost, and most clinically appropriate device** should be selected first. The clinician should not over-prescribe a high-tech or complex device when a simpler one — or even no device (just training or environmental modification) — can achieve the user's SMART goal. This reduces cost, lowers abandonment risk, avoids creating user dependency, and respects the user's autonomy.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts (a) and (b) of **7 marks each**. The student attempts **ONE of two internal choices**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper, Module 3 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **WHO-ICF (International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health) framework** in detail. Compare it with the older *medical model* of disability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **six-stage intervention process** for the provision of an assistive device. For each stage, name **one tool, technique, or output** that is used. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a) — 7 Marks

The **WHO-ICF framework**, published by the World Health Organization in 2001, provides a **biopsychosocial** classification of health and disability. It moves away from viewing disability as a purely medical problem located inside the individual. **(1 mark for introduction)**

The ICF has two main parts, each with several components:

**Part 1: Functioning and Disability** **(2 marks)**

1. **Body Functions (b)** — physiological functions of body systems (e.g., muscle power, vision). Example code: `b730` = Muscle power functions.
2. **Body Structures (s)** — anatomical parts (e.g., structure of the upper limb). Example code: `s730` = Structure of upper extremity.
3. **Activities and Participation (d)** — execution of tasks and involvement in life situations. A single list (with optional qualifier) covers both. Example: `d450` = Walking.

**Part 2: Contextual Factors** **(2 marks)**

4. **Environmental Factors (e)** — physical, social, and attitudinal environment that can be a *facilitator* or *barrier*. Example: `e150` = Design, construction, and building products for public use (ramps, curb cuts).
5. **Personal Factors** — age, gender, motivation, coping style, lifestyle. *(Not classified by WHO due to cultural variability, but acknowledged.)*

**Comparison with the Medical Model:** **(2 marks)**

| Dimension | Medical Model | ICF / Biopsychosocial Model |
|---|---|---|
| View of disability | Tragic, located *in* the person | Interaction between person and environment |
| Cause of disability | Individual impairment | Impairment + Activity limitation + Participation restriction + Context |
| Solution focus | Cure or fix the person | Modify the person **or** the environment (e.g., wheelchair ramp, screen reader) |
| Professional role | Expert decides | Shared decision-making with the user |
| Outcome measure | Reduction of impairment | Improvement in activity, participation, and quality of life |

**Conclusion:** The ICF provides a more **comprehensive, humane, and intervention-friendly** view of disability and is the **theoretical backbone** of modern assistive technology provision.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b) — 7 Marks

The **six-stage intervention process** ensures that assistive device provision is systematic, evidence-based, and user-centered. Each stage is justified by one or more of the eight principles in §2.1.

| Stage | What is Done | Tool / Technique / Output |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Referral and Initial Contact** | Identify the user, document the presenting concern from referrer and from the user. | Intake form, referral letter **(1 mark)** |
| **2. Multidisciplinary Assessment** | Medical, functional, and environmental assessment done in parallel by a team. | FIM, Barthel Index, COPM, home audit report **(1.5 marks)** |
| **3. SMART Goal Setting** | Convert vague wishes into Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound goals. | Documented SMART goal list across ICF levels **(1 mark)** |
| **4. Device Selection and Prescription** | Choose the *least restrictive* device that meets the goal, supported by evidence. | MPT match analysis, prescription document **(1 mark)** |
| **5. Fitting, Customization, and Training** | Physical fit, alignment, user + caregiver training, real-environment trials. | Pressure map report, training checklist, written user manual **(1.5 marks)** |
| **6. Follow-Up and Outcome Evaluation** | Scheduled visits at 1, 3, 6, 12 months; outcome measurement. | QUEST 2.0, GAS, WHO-DAS 2.0 **(1 mark)** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question A(b):**
> Students often list the six stages correctly but *omit the tools used at each stage*. Since the question explicitly asks for "one tool, technique, or output" per stage, **failing to name a tool costs at least 2–3 marks** out of 7. Always pair every stage with a concrete, named instrument (FIM, COPM, GAS, QUEST 2.0, SMART, MPT, etc.).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper, Module 3 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **Biopsychosocial Model of Disability** and the **Eight Core Principles of Intervention** in assistive technology. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 62-year-old retired school teacher had a **right-sided stroke** six months ago. He has weakness in his right hand and walks with a limp. He wants to **return to teaching part-time** at a local primary school. Apply the **six-stage intervention process** to design an intervention plan for him. Identify **at least one specific assistive device** you would consider at each relevant stage. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 Marks

**Biopsychosocial Model:** **(3 marks)**

The **Biopsychosocial Model** of disability, formalized in the WHO-ICF (2001), holds that disability is *not* a property of the individual alone, but the **result of interactions** between:

1. **Biological** factors — the underlying health condition, impairment of body function or structure (e.g., post-stroke hemiparesis).
2. **Psychological** factors — personal factors such as motivation, coping style, expectations, and emotional adjustment to disability.
3. **Social** factors — environmental factors such as physical accessibility, social attitudes, family support, employment policies, and assistive technology availability.

The model's key insight: **the same biological impairment can produce vastly different levels of disability in different environments.** A wheelchair user is fully mobile in a city with curb cuts but is "disabled" by a village with no paved roads. Hence, intervention must target the *interaction*, not just the body.

**Eight Core Principles of Intervention:** **(4 marks — 0.5 mark each)**

1. **User-Centered Design** — user is co-designer.
2. **Holistic / Biopsychosocial Assessment** — assess body + activity + participation + environment.
3. **Goal-Directed (SMART)** — every intervention has specific, measurable, time-bound goals.
4. **Minimal Intervention** — least restrictive device that meets the goal.
5. **Environmental Fit** — device must match physical, social, cultural context.
6. **Interdisciplinary Team** — physiatrist + OT + PT + prosthetist + engineer + social worker + user.
7. **Evidence-Based Practice (EBP)** — research evidence + clinical expertise + patient values.
8. **Outcome Evaluation and Follow-Up** — continuous, scheduled monitoring using standardized tools.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b) — 7 Marks

**Applying the six-stage process to the case:**

| Stage | Application to the Case | Specific Assistive Device Consideration |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Referral** | Self-referred by the teacher; primary concern stated as *"I want to return to teaching part-time."* Identify his own goal separately from any family or doctor concern. | — |
| **2. Multidisciplinary Assessment** | **Medical:** type and stability of post-stroke hemiparesis, vision, cognition, speech. **Functional (OT/PT):** hand function (e.g., Nine-Hole Peg Test), mobility, ability to write on a blackboard. **Environmental:** school building — stairs, classroom layout, desk height, distance from parking. | Use of **FIM** or **Barthel Index** to quantify current independence. |
| **3. SMART Goals** | Examples: *"By 3 months, I will write legibly on the board for 30 minutes using my right hand with an assistive writing aid."* *"By 2 months, I will walk 200 m from the staffroom to my classroom using a single-point cane."* | — |
| **4. Device Selection and Prescription** | Apply *least restrictive* principle. For writing: try a thick-barrel pen grip first; if insufficient, consider a **wrist-hand orthosis (WHO) with built-up grip** or a **lightweight electric wheelchair** for long distances. For mobility: a **single-point cane** before progressing to a **quad cane** or **rollator**. | **Specific devices:** built-up handle pen, wrist-hand splint, single-point cane, AFOs (ankle-foot orthoses) to correct foot drop, possibly a tablet with speech-to-text for lesson preparation. |
| **5. Fitting & Training** | Custom-fit the AFO, adjust cane height to wrist crease, train one-handed writing techniques, train school staff to assist, conduct a trial visit to the school. | Pressure mapping is not relevant here, but **cane-height measurement** and **AFO donning/doffing training** are. |
| **6. Follow-Up & Outcome Evaluation** | Schedule visits at 1, 3, 6, 12 months. Use **GAS** to score SMART goals; use **QUEST 2.0** to assess satisfaction. Re-evaluate as recovery continues (stroke recovery can plateau by 12–18 months but small gains occur for years). | If gait deteriorates → upgrade to **quad cane**; if writing fatigue persists → upgrade to **voice-to-text tablet**. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B(b):**
> Case-study questions are the **most commonly mishandled** by students. Three recurring errors:
>
> 1. **Listing devices without linking them to a specific stage** of the process — the question demands *stage-by-stage application*. A device mentioned outside its stage context will get **partial credit only**.
> 2. **Ignoring the patient's own goal** ("return to teaching part-time") and answering as if the goal were "improve hand strength." The user's goal is the *anchor* of the entire plan. Any plan that does not start from his stated goal **loses 1–2 marks** immediately.
> 3. **Forgetting follow-up and outcome measurement.** A plan that ends at "device delivered" is incomplete. The examiner expects **at least one outcome tool** to be named (GAS, QUEST 2.0, COPM, FIM).

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> **Rapid-revision checklist — read this the night before the exam.**

- **The "Eight Principles"** are the single most-likely 14-marker. Memorize all eight with their one-line KTU definitions.
- **The "Six-Stage Process"** is the second most-likely 14-marker. Memorize the stages in order, the principle each stage derives from, and **one named tool per stage** (FIM, COPM, GAS, QUEST 2.0, SMART, MPT, Mermaid, ICF, etc.).
- **The ICF vs Medical Model** comparison is the highest-yield 7-marker. Memorize the table in §2.3 — examiners love asking this as a direct question.
- **SMART** is so important that it appears in almost every intervention answer. Always write it in full: Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound.
- **Least restrictive intervention** is the single most-tested short-answer definition.
- **MPT (Matching Person and Technology)** is a KTU favourite — know all three matches (Person↔Technology, Person↔Environment, Technology↔Environment) and what failure of each looks like (device abandonment).
- **GAS formula** is the only quantitative formula in this module. Memorize the formula structure and the meaning of the constants $50$ (population mean) and $10$ (population SD multiplier). Probability of it being asked directly: **moderate** — but you will *always* earn bonus marks if you can quote it when discussing outcome evaluation.
- **WHO-ICF components to remember:** Body Functions, Body Structures, Activities, Participation, Environmental Factors, Personal Factors. Use the alphanumeric coding system if you can: `b` = body function, `s` = structure, `d` = activity/participation, `e` = environment.
- **Team members to remember:** Physiatrist, Occupational Therapist (OT), Physiotherapist (PT), Prosthetist-Orthotist, Biomedical Engineer, Social Worker, User + Family.
- **Standardized outcome tools to remember:** FIM, Barthel Index, COPM, GAS, QUEST 2.0, WHO-DAS 2.0. Map each to what it measures.
- **Biopsychosocial model** = Biological + Psychological + Social. This is the conceptual foundation of ICF and of all modern intervention thinking.
- **"Nothing about us without us"** — disability rights slogan that KTU examiners like to see quoted when discussing user-centered design. It is a free mark if used in the right context.
- **Device abandonment statistic:** roughly **one-third** of all assistive devices are abandoned within one year of provision — a fact examiners often cite in case-study questions to justify the importance of the principles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy Tip:** When you see a 14-marker on "Principles of Intervention," *do not* just list the eight principles. The examiner wants to see them **applied**. Structure your answer as: (1) brief introduction defining intervention, (2) the principles *with one-line justifications*, (3) the six-stage process *with tools named*, (4) reference to ICF and biopsychosocial model, (5) outcome evaluation with GAS formula if possible, (6) conclusion emphasizing user-centeredness. This structure guarantees full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wheeled Mobility: Supporting Structure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBRT614 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Wheeled Mobility: Supporting Structure ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Wheeled Mobility Supporting Structure (KTU 2024 Definition):** > The *Supporting Structure* of a wheeled mobility device (primarily manual and powered wheelchairs) is the **load-bearing skeletal framework** comprising the frame, cross-braces, seating platform, back support canes, armrest assemblies, and footrest hangers. It is engineered to transmit static and dynamic loads from the user and the device itself to the wheel-ground contact points, while maintaining dimensional stability, structural integrity, and user safety under prescribed service conditions defined by **ISO 7176** and **RESNA WC-series** standards. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the supporting structure is the **primary load path** through which all external forces (user body weight, terrain reaction forces, inertial loads during propulsion) are collected, distributed, and ultimately reacted at the wheels and casters. ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — "The Skeleton and Muscles of the Wheelchair":** > Think of the supporting structure as the **human skeleton + musculature combined**. Just as a human skeleton holds the body upright and muscles transmit forces during walking, the wheelchair frame holds the user upright, while the cross-braces, struts, and brackets act like "muscles" transmitting the user's weight down to the wheels. If the skeleton is weak (thin-walled steel tube, poor weld), it bends or breaks under load. If it is over-built (heavy chrome-molybdenum), the user wastes precious energy propelling it — a *critical* trade-off in mobility device design. ### 1.3 Classification of Supporting Structures | Class | Description | Typical Use | |---|---|---| | **Rigid Frame** | Welded single-piece structure; no folding mechanism | Sports, ultralight active wheelchairs | | **Folding (Cross-Fold)** | Hinged X-frame or scissor mechanism | Standard manual wheelchairs | | **Modular (Adjustable)** | Bolt-together aluminum extrusions | Pediatric, growing-user wheelchairs | | **Tilt-in-Space Frame** | Rigid base with separate seat cradle for tilt | Pressure-relief, postural support | | **Powered Base Frame** | Reinforced chassis housing motors and batteries | Electric powered wheelchairs | ### 1.4 Governing Performance Metrics - **Static Load Capacity:** Typically **113.4 kg (250 lb)** for standard, **158.8 kg (350 lb)** for heavy-duty, **>226.8 kg (500 lb)** for bariatric. - **Service Life Target:** **5 years minimum** under daily use. - **Safety Factor on Yield:** Typically **3.0 to 4.0** for static loads, **2.0** for fatigue. - **Frame Mass Budget:** **6–10 kg** for ultralight, **12–18 kg** for standard, **25–40 kg** for powered. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Structural Load Path — Step-by-Step Logic The supporting structure must transfer load through a clearly defined sequence: 1. **Load Inception:** The user's body mass (`m_u`) acts through the body's **Center of Gravity (CG)** which lies approximately at the **sacro-lumbar junction** (S2–L5 vertebra). 2. **Seat Plane Reception:** The seat sling (canvas, foam-on-board, or rigid pan) receives the load and spreads it onto the **side frames** (the two longitudinal rails). 3. **Longitudinal Transfer:** Side frames transfer load forward to the **front caster forks** and rearward to the **rear axle plate**. 4. **Vertical Transfer:** Axle plates and caster forks transfer load to the wheels and casters. 5. **Ground Reaction:** The floor exerts equal-and-opposite normal forces `N_f` (front) and `N_r` (rear). ### 2.2 Structural Component Roles > [!IMPORTANT] > **Each component has a specific load role — board examiners frequently test this mapping:** - **Side Frames (Longitudinal Rails):** Carry **bending moment** + **torsional moment**; primary flexural members. - **Cross-Braces (X-frame):** Carry **pure axial tension/compression**; stabilize against lateral fold. - **Back Cane Tubes (Vertical):** Carry **combined axial + bending**; back support is a cantilevered load. - **Axle Plate:** Carries **shear + bending**; highest stress concentration zone. - **Caster Fork:** Carries **bending + torsion** due to offset stem. - **Footrest Hanger:** Carries **bending moment** at the knee-to-ankle moment arm. - **Push Handle:** Carries **bending** from caregiver lifting force. ### 2.3 Frame Material Property Comparison | Material | Density (g/cm³) | Yield Strength (MPa) | Specific Strength | Cost | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Mild Steel (1018) | **7.87** | **370** | 47 | Low | Heavy, corrodes | | Chrome-Moly (4130) | **7.85** | **560** | 71 | Medium | Welded sports frames | | Aluminum 6061-T6 | **2.70** | **276** | 102 | Medium | Most common ultralight | | Aluminum 7005-T6 | **2.78** | **345** | 124 | Medium-High | Superior fatigue | | Titanium Ti-6Al-4V | **4.43** | **880** | 199 | Very High | Premium custom | | Carbon Fiber Composite | **1.55–1.85** | **600–1500** (tension) | 380+ | Very High | Racing/active | > [!NOTE] > **Why "Specific Strength" matters in wheelchair design:** A user expends metabolic energy to push every kilogram of frame mass repeatedly per day. A frame that is twice as strong but 30% lighter is *always* preferred — hence the dominance of aluminum and titanium in modern wheelchairs. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Master Reference) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master Equation Table — memorize for ESE:** | # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $N_r + N_f = m_{total} \cdot g$ | Sum of vertical reactions = total weight | N | | 2 | $N_r \cdot x_f = N_f \cdot x_r$ | Moment equilibrium about wheel contact | N·m | | 3 | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{M \cdot c}{I}$ | Bending stress in a beam section | Pa | | 4 | $\tau = \dfrac{V \cdot Q}{I \cdot t}$ | Transverse shear stress | Pa | | 5 | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 \cdot E \cdot I}{(K L)^2}$ | Euler critical buckling load | N | | 6 | $\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{P_{cr}}{A} = \dfrac{\pi^2 \cdot E}{(K L / r)^2}$ | Critical buckling stress | Pa | | 7 | $r = \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{A}}$ | Radius of gyration of cross-section | m | | 8 | $SF = \dfrac{\sigma_{yield}}{\sigma_{working}}$ | Factor of safety (dimensionless) | — | | 9 | $N = \dfrac{\sigma_{endurance}}{\sigma_{a}}$ | Fatigue safety factor (Soderberg) | — | | 10 | $M_{max} = F \cdot L$ (cantilever) | Bending moment at fixed end | N·m | | 11 | $\delta_{max} = \dfrac{F \cdot L^3}{3 \cdot E \cdot I}$ | Tip deflection of cantilever | m | | 12 | $I_{tube} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot (r_o^4 - r_i^4)$ | Moment of inertia, hollow tube | m⁴ | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Crash Testing (ISO 7176-19):** Frames must withstand a 20 g deceleration when used as a vehicle seat — the cross-brace and side-frame welds are the *most common failure zones* in testing. - **Fatigue Life:** The back cane experiences ~**10⁶ cycles per year** of flexure; aluminum 6061 must be **shot-peened** at welds to extend fatigue life. - **Custom Fit:** Adjustable modular frames allow **growth extension** of 50–100 mm per dimension without replacing the structure — critical for pediatric CP (cerebral palsy) users. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation I — Static Reaction Forces on a Wheelchair Frame **Problem Setup:** A wheelchair with user mass `m_u = 80 kg` and frame mass `m_f = 12 kg` rests on level ground. The horizontal distance from the rear axle to the combined CG is `x_r = 0.15 m` (rearward of rear axle), and from the rear axle to the front caster line is `L = 0.50 m`. Find the normal reactions `N_r` at the rear wheels and `N_f` at the front casters. **Free Body Diagram (Textual Representation):** - The total weight `W = (m_u + m_f) \cdot g` acts downward at the CG. - `N_r` acts upward at the rear axle (located at origin). - `N_f` acts upward at the front caster line (located at distance `L` forward). **Step 1 — Identify total downward force (weight of the system).** $$ W = (m_u + m_f) \cdot g = (80 + 12) \cdot 9.81 = 92 \cdot 9.81 = 902.52 \text{ N} $$ > *Logic:* The weight of the user and frame together is the total vertical load the structure must support. **Step 2 — Apply vertical force equilibrium.** $$ \sum F_y = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad N_r + N_f - W = 0 $$ $$ N_r + N_f = 902.52 \text{ N} \quad \text{...(Eq. A)} $$ > *Logic:* The sum of all upward forces must equal the sum of all downward forces. **Step 3 — Apply rotational equilibrium about the rear axle (eliminates `N_r`).** Taking moments about the rear axle, with counter-clockwise positive: $$ \sum M_{rear} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad N_f \cdot L - W \cdot x_r = 0 $$ > *Logic:* The front reaction creates a counter-clockwise moment, the weight creates a clockwise moment about the rear axle. **Step 4 — Solve for `N_f`.** $$ N_f = \frac{W \cdot x_r}{L} = \frac{902.52 \cdot 0.15}{0.50} = \frac{135.378}{0.50} = 270.756 \text{ N} $$ **Step 5 — Solve for `N_r` using Eq. A.** $$ N_r = W - N_f = 902.52 - 270.756 = 631.764 \text{ N} $$ > *Per-wheel reaction (since two rear wheels share the load):* $$ N_{r,per wheel} = \frac{N_r}{2} = 315.882 \text{ N} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** About **70% of user weight rests on the rear wheels** — this is why rear-wheel design (axle plate, spokes, hub) is the most heavily loaded region of the supporting structure. --- ### 3.2 Derivation II — Bending Stress in a Side Frame Rail **Problem Setup:** The side frame rail is approximated as a simply-supported beam of length `L = 0.45 m` carrying the user weight `W = 784.8 N` (80 kg) at its midspan. The cross-section is a rectangular aluminum tube **25 mm × 50 mm** with wall thickness `t = 2 mm`. Compute the maximum bending stress. **Step 1 — Maximum bending moment for a center-loaded simply-supported beam.** $$ M_{max} = \frac{W \cdot L}{4} = \frac{784.8 \cdot 0.45}{4} = \frac{353.16}{4} = 88.29 \text{ N·m} $$ > *Logic:* For a simply-supported beam with a point load at center, reactions are `W/2` at each end, and maximum moment occurs at center: `M = (W/2) \cdot (L/2) = WL/4`. **Step 2 — Compute the moment of inertia of the rectangular hollow tube.** Outer dimensions: `b_o = 0.050 m`, `h_o = 0.025 m`. Inner: `b_i = 0.046 m`, `h_i = 0.021 m`. $$ I = \frac{b_o \cdot h_o^3 - b_i \cdot h_i^3}{12} = \frac{(0.050)(0.025)^3 - (0.046)(0.021)^3}{12} $$ **Step 3 — Numerical evaluation of the cubes.** $$ (0.025)^3 = 1.5625 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3 $$ $$ (0.021)^3 = 9.261 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3 $$ **Step 4 — Substitute and evaluate the moment of inertia.** $$ I = \frac{(0.050)(1.5625 \times 10^{-5}) - (0.046)(9.261 \times 10^{-6})}{12} $$ $$ I = \frac{7.8125 \times 10^{-7} - 4.2601 \times 10^{-7}}{12} = \frac{3.5524 \times 10^{-7}}{12} $$ $$ I = 2.9603 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^4 $$ **Step 5 — Compute distance `c` from neutral axis to outermost fiber.** $$ c = \frac{h_o}{2} = \frac{0.025}{2} = 0.0125 \text{ m} $$ **Step 6 — Compute maximum bending stress.** $$ \sigma_{max} = \frac{M_{max} \cdot c}{I} = \frac{88.29 \cdot 0.0125}{2.9603 \times 10^{-8}} $$ $$ \sigma_{max} = \frac{1.1036}{2.9603 \times 10^{-8}} = 3.728 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa} = 37.28 \text{ MPa} $$ **Step 7 — Compute factor of safety against yield (Al 6061-T6: `σ_y` ≈ 276 MPa).** $$ SF = \frac{\sigma_{yield}}{\sigma_{max}} = \frac{276}{37.28} = 7.40 $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Interpretation:** A static safety factor of ~7.4 is well above the KTU-prescribed minimum of 3.0, indicating the section is statically safe. However, **fatigue safety factor** is the true limiting criterion for a wheelchair frame. --- ### 3.3 Derivation III — Euler Buckling of a Back-Cane Support Tube **Problem Setup:** A vertical back-cane tube is modeled as a column of length `L = 0.50 m`, fixed at the seat-frame junction and pinned at the top (effective length factor `K = 0.7`). Outer diameter `D_o = 25 mm`, wall thickness `t = 1.6 mm`. Material: Aluminum 6061-T6, `E = 68.9 GPa`. Determine the Euler critical buckling load. **Step 1 — Cross-sectional dimensions.** $$ D_o = 0.025 \text{ m}, \quad D_i = D_o - 2t = 0.025 - 0.0032 = 0.0218 \text{ m} $$ **Step 2 — Cross-sectional area `A`.** $$ A = \frac{\pi}{4}(D_o^2 - D_i^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.000625 - 0.000475) $$ $$ A = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.000150) = 1.1781 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 = 117.81 \text{ mm}^2 $$ **Step 3 — Moment of inertia of the hollow circular section.** $$ I = \frac{\pi}{64}(D_o^4 - D_i^4) = \frac{\pi}{64}((0.025)^4 - (0.0218)^4) $$ $$ (0.025)^4 = 3.9063 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^4 $$ $$ (0.0218)^4 = 2.2582 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^4 $$ $$ I = \frac{\pi}{64}(3.9063 \times 10^{-7} - 2.2582 \times 10^{-7}) = \frac{\pi}{64}(1.6481 \times 10^{-7}) $$ $$ I = 8.092 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^4 $$ **Step 4 — Effective length `K·L`.** $$ K \cdot L = 0.7 \cdot 0.50 = 0.35 \text{ m} $$ **Step 5 — Apply Euler's buckling formula.** $$ P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 \cdot E \cdot I}{(K L)^2} = \frac{\pi^2 \cdot (68.9 \times 10^9) \cdot (8.092 \times 10^{-9})}{(0.35)^2} $$ **Step 6 — Numerator evaluation.** $$ \pi^2 \cdot E \cdot I = 9.8696 \cdot 68.9 \times 10^9 \cdot 8.092 \times 10^{-9} $$ $$ = 9.8696 \cdot 557.54 = 5502.9 \text{ N·m}^2 $$ **Step 7 — Denominator evaluation.** $$ (K L)^2 = (0.35)^2 = 0.1225 \text{ m}^2 $$ **Step 8 — Final critical buckling load.** $$ P_{cr} = \frac{5502.9}{0.1225} = 44922 \text{ N} \approx 44.92 \text{ kN} $$ **Step 9 — Slenderness check to validate Euler applicability.** $$ r = \sqrt{\frac{I}{A}} = \sqrt{\frac{8.092 \times 10^{-9}}{1.1781 \times 10^{-4}}} = \sqrt{6.870 \times 10^{-5}} = 8.289 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} $$ $$ \text{Slenderness ratio} = \frac{K L}{r} = \frac{0.35}{8.289 \times 10^{-3}} = 42.23 $$ > *For aluminum 6061-T6, the transition slenderness ratio is ~`\sqrt{2\pi^2 E/\sigma_y} \approx 63`. Since 42.23 < 63, Euler's elastic buckling is valid.* > [!IMPORTANT] > **Result Interpretation:** A buckling capacity of **~45 kN** vastly exceeds the typical 1–2 kN service load — this confirms the back cane is **not buckling-limited**; its design is governed by **fatigue and weld integrity**, not static buckling. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Frame Design Selection | Design Criterion | Rigid Frame | Folding Frame | Powered Base | |---|---|---|---| | **Energy loss per push** | **Low (5–8%)** | High (15–25%) | N/A (motorized) | | **Structural stiffness** | Very High | Moderate | Very High | | **Weight** | 6–9 kg | 11–16 kg | 30–60 kg | | **Portability** | Poor (rigid) | Excellent (foldable) | Poor (heavy) | | **Manufacturing cost** | Medium | Low | Very High | | **Adjustability** | Low | Medium | High | | **Best for** | Active adult users | Casual users, transport | Severely disabled users | ## 4. Choose frame material] --> B{User activity level?} B -->|High Active Sports| C{Target frame mass?} B -->|Moderate Daily| D{Budget constraint?} B -->|Pediatric Adjustable| E[Aluminum 6061 T6] B -->|Bariatric Heavy Duty| F{Weight capacity needed?} C -->|Under 6 kg| G[Carbon Fiber Composite] C -->|Under 9 kg| H[Titanium Ti 6Al 4V] D -->|Low Budget| I[Chrome Moly Steel 4130] D -->|Mid Budget| E D -->|High Budget| H F -->|Under 160 kg| I F -->|Over 200 kg| J[Reinforced Steel 1026] style A fill:#fff3cd style G fill:#d4edda style H fill:#d4edda style E fill:#cce5ff style I fill:#f8d7da style J fill:#f8d7da ``` ### 4.4 Free Body Diagram of Wheelchair Frame (Schematic) ``` USER CG (W = m·g ↓) | | x_r REAR AXLE ●────●─────● FRONT CASTER LINE (N_r ↑) (0.15m) (N_f ↑) |←——— L ———————→| (0.50 m) Forces: ↑ N_r (per side = N_r/2 since 2 rear wheels) ↑ N_f (per side = N_f/2 since 2 front casters) ↓ W = (m_u + m_f)·g at distance x_r rearward of rear axle ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading this diagram:** The rear axle is placed as the *origin* for moment calculations. The CG sits **behind** the rear axle because the seat back is inclined rearward and the user leans back slightly — this creates a **stable tipping moment** preventing forward fall. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** *List any six essential structural components of a manual wheelchair supporting structure and state the primary load type each component carries.* **Model Answer (6 components, 0.5 mark each):** 1. **Side Frame Rails** — Carry bending moment (flexural) + torsional moment. 2. **Cross-Braces (X-frame)** — Carry pure axial tension/compression. 3. **Axle Plate** — Carries combined shear + bending at the highest stress point. 4. **Back Cane Tubes** — Carry combined axial compression + bending (cantilever). 5. **Caster Fork** — Carries bending + torsion due to offset stem. 6. **Footrest Hanger** — Carries bending moment at knee-ankle lever arm. (Additional valid: Push Handle — bending; Seat Sling — distributed compression.) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** *Compare the properties of Aluminum 6061-T6 and Titanium Ti-6Al-4V as wheelchair frame materials, citing three relevant parameters.* **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Aluminum 6061-T6 | Titanium Ti-6Al-4V | |---|---|---| | Density | 2.70 g/cm³ (light) | 4.43 g/cm³ (heavier) | | Yield Strength | 276 MPa | 880 MPa (superior) | | Specific Strength | 102 | 199 (nearly 2× better) | | Cost | Moderate | Very High (~5–8× Al) | | Weldability | Excellent (TIG) | Difficult (requires inert chamber) | | Fatigue Life | Good if shot-peened | Excellent | | Best Application | Standard ultralight | Premium custom ultralight | **Verdict (1 mark):** Ti-6Al-4V is preferred for high-end custom ultralight wheelchairs where weight savings and durability justify the cost; Al 6061-T6 is the workhorse for most ultralight production chairs. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) --- #### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** **(a)** Explain the different types of wheelchair frames with neat sketches and discuss the advantages and disadvantages of each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A wheelchair frame supports a user of mass **85 kg** and the frame mass is **14 kg**. The combined CG is located **0.18 m rearward** of the rear axle. The wheelbase (rear axle to front caster) is **0.52 m**. Compute: - (i) The total weight on the structure. - (ii) The front and rear vertical reactions. - (iii) The load on each rear wheel. **(7 marks)** --- #### ✅ Model Solution — Question A **Part (a) — Frame Types (7 marks)** > Award **1 mark per type** with **0.5 mark advantage + 0.5 mark disadvantage**. Minimum 4 types required for full marks. **1. Rigid Frame** - *Sketch description:* Single welded frame, no folding hinge; X-configuration bracing is welded, not pivoted. - *Advantages:* Lowest energy loss during propulsion (5–8%); highest structural stiffness; lightest for given strength due to optimized tube geometry. - *Disadvantages:* Not portable in cars without disassembly; difficult to store; cannot fold for transport. **2. Folding (Cross-Fold) Frame** - *Sketch description:* Two side frames connected by a pivoting X-cross brace in the center. - *Advantages:* Compact for transport and storage; user-friendly for elderly. - *Disadvantages:* Energy loss 15–25% per push cycle due to flexure of cross brace; heavier for equivalent strength; welded pivots are fatigue-prone. **3. Tilt-in-Space Frame** - *Sketch description:* Rigid base with a separate pivoting seat cradle; back and seat tilt together. - *Advantages:* Pressure relief for users at risk of pressure ulcers; postural management; mechanical lift assist. - *Disadvantages:* Heavy (often >20 kg); large footprint; more expensive. **4. Reclining Frame** - *Sketch description:* Back support pivots rearward independently of seat pan. - *Advantages:* Allows supine positioning, hygiene, catheterization. - *Disadvantages:* Shear forces on user's back; loss of push posture for active users; complex mechanisms. **5. Modular / Adjustable Frame** - *Sketch description:* Bolt-together aluminum extrusions with sliding clamps at each joint. - *Advantages:* Grows with the user (pediatric); replaceable parts; tunable center of gravity. - *Disadvantages:* Bolts loosen over time; more concentrated stress at clamp locations; not as rigid as welded. **6. Powered Wheelchair Base** - *Sketch description:* Reinforced steel/aluminum chassis with motor mounts, battery trays, and electronics. - *Advantages:* Carries motors and batteries; crash-tested per ISO 7176-19; high load capacity. - *Disadvantages:* Very heavy (30–60 kg); expensive; requires electronics maintenance. --- **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)** **Given:** `m_u = 85 kg`, `m_f = 14 kg`, `x_r = 0.18 m`, `L = 0.52 m`, `g = 9.81 m/s²` **(i) Total Weight (1 mark)** $$ W = (m_u + m_f) \cdot g = (85 + 14) \cdot 9.81 = 99 \cdot 9.81 = 970.19 \text{ N} $$ **(ii) Front and Rear Reactions (4 marks)** Vertical equilibrium: $$ N_r + N_f = 970.19 \text{ N} \quad \text{...(1)} $$ Moment equilibrium about rear axle (CCW positive): $$ N_f \cdot L - W \cdot x_r = 0 $$ $$ N_f = \frac{W \cdot x_r}{L} = \frac{970.19 \cdot 0.18}{0.52} = \frac{174.63}{0.52} = 335.83 \text{ N} $$ From equation (1): $$ N_r = 970.19 - 335.83 = 634.36 \text{ N} $$ **(iii) Load per Rear Wheel (2 marks)** Since load is symmetric on two rear wheels: $$ N_{r,per wheel} = \frac{N_r}{2} = \frac{634.36}{2} = 317.18 \text{ N} $$ **Per caster load:** $$ N_{f,per caster} = \frac{N_f}{2} = \frac{335.83}{2} = 167.92 \text{ N} $$ **Verification (1 mark — recommended):** Sum check: `634.36 + 335.83 = 970.19 N` ✓ --- #### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** **(a)** Discuss the various materials used for wheelchair supporting structures. Compare them on the basis of **density, yield strength, fatigue resistance, weight, and cost.** Recommend the best material for an **ultralight active-user manual wheelchair**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A back-cane support tube of a wheelchair is modeled as a **circular hollow column** with the following specifications: - Outer diameter `D_o = 22 mm` - Wall thickness `t = 1.5 mm` - Length `L = 0.48 m` - End conditions: **Fixed–Pinned** (effective length factor `K = 0.7`) - Material: **Aluminum 6061-T6** with Young's modulus `E = 68.9 GPa` and yield strength `σ_y = 276 MPa` Calculate: - (i) The cross-sectional area and moment of inertia. - (ii) The radius of gyration and slenderness ratio. - (iii) The Euler critical buckling load and corresponding critical stress. - (iv) Comment on whether the tube is safe against buckling if the maximum service load is **600 N**. **(7 marks)** --- #### ✅ Model Solution — Question B **Part (a) — Materials Discussion (7 marks)** **1. Mild Steel (e.g., AISI 1018)** (1 mark) - Density 7.87 g/cm³, Yield 370 MPa, good weldability, poor corrosion resistance. - Used in: Hospital/institutional wheelchairs (low cost). Heavy (frame mass 18+ kg). - *Disadvantage:* Weight penalty makes it unsuitable for self-propelled users. **2. Chrome-Molybdenum Steel (AISI 4130)** (1 mark) - Density 7.85 g/cm³, Yield 560 MPa (heat-treated), excellent fatigue life. - Used in: Sports wheelchairs (basketball, rugby, racing). - *Advantage:* Can be TIG-welded with minimal strength loss; can be butted and gusseted. - *Disadvantage:* Still heavy; requires corrosion protection. **3. Aluminum Alloys (6061-T6, 7005-T6)** (1.5 marks) - 6061-T6: 2.70 g/cm³, 276 MPa yield — most common ultralight material. - 7005-T6: 2.78 g/cm³, 345 MPa yield, better fatigue (preferred for higher-end). - *Advantage:* Specific strength excellent; corrosion resistant; easily extruded and welded. - *Disadvantage:* Welded joints lose ~30–40% strength at HAZ (heat-affected zone) unless post-weld heat-treated. **4. Titanium Alloy (Ti-6Al-4V)** (1.5 marks) - Density 4.43 g/cm³, Yield 880 MPa, fatigue life superior to aluminum. - *Advantage:* Best strength-to-weight ratio among metals; biocompatible; no corrosion. - *Disadvantage:* Very expensive (5–8× aluminum); difficult to weld (requires inert gas chamber); only custom-made. **5. Carbon Fiber Reinforced Polymer (CFRP)** (1 mark) - Density 1.55–1.85 g/cm³, Tensile strength 600–1500 MPa. - *Advantage:* Lightest possible frame (4–6 kg); excellent vibration damping. - *Disadvantage:* Very expensive; catastrophic failure mode (no plastic deformation warning); difficult to repair. **6. Magnesium Alloys (briefly)** (0.5 mark) - Density 1.74 g/cm³, but corrosion-prone and flammable — rarely used. **Recommendation for ultralight active-user manual wheelchair (1 mark):** **Aluminum 7005-T6** is the best *practical* choice — offering an optimal balance of low weight (~7–9 kg frame), excellent fatigue resistance, good weldability, moderate cost, and proven track record in ultralight wheelchair manufacturing (e.g., TiLite, Quickie, Kuschall product lines). Carbon fiber is a premium alternative for racing; titanium for luxury custom builds. --- **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)** **Given:** `D_o = 22 mm = 0.022 m`, `t = 1.5 mm = 0.0015 m`, `L = 0.48 m`, `K = 0.7`, `E = 68.9 × 10⁹ Pa`, `σ_y = 276 × 10⁶ Pa`. **(i) Cross-sectional area and moment of inertia (2 marks)** $$ D_i = D_o - 2t = 0.022 - 0.003 = 0.019 \text{ m} $$ $$ A = \frac{\pi}{4}(D_o^2 - D_i^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}((0.022)^2 - (0.019)^2) $$ $$ A = \frac{\pi}{4}(4.84 \times 10^{-4} - 3.61 \times 10^{-4}) = \frac{\pi}{4}(1.23 \times 10^{-4}) $$ $$ A = 9.6604 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2 = 96.604 \text{ mm}^2 $$ $$ I = \frac{\pi}{64}(D_o^4 - D_i^4) = \frac{\pi}{64}((0.022)^4 - (0.019)^4) $$ $$ (0.022)^4 = 2.3424 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^4 \quad ; \quad (0.019)^4 = 1.3033 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^4 $$ $$ I = \frac{\pi}{64}(2.3424 \times 10^{-7} - 1.3033 \times 10^{-7}) = \frac{\pi}{64}(1.0391 \times 10^{-7}) $$ $$ I = 5.1007 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^4 $$ **(ii) Radius of gyration and slenderness ratio (1 mark)** $$ r = \sqrt{\frac{I}{A}} = \sqrt{\frac{5.1007 \times 10^{-9}}{9.6604 \times 10^{-5}}} = \sqrt{5.2798 \times 10^{-5}} = 7.2663 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} $$ $$ \lambda = \frac{K \cdot L}{r} = \frac{0.7 \cdot 0.48}{7.2663 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{0.336}{7.2663 \times 10^{-3}} = 46.24 $$ **(iii) Euler critical buckling load and stress (2 marks)** $$ P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 \cdot E \cdot I}{(K L)^2} = \frac{\pi^2 \cdot (68.9 \times 10^9) \cdot (5.1007 \times 10^{-9})}{(0.336)^2} $$ $$ \text{Numerator} = 9.8696 \cdot 68.9 \times 10^9 \cdot 5.1007 \times 10^{-9} = 9.8696 \cdot 351.44 = 3468.7 $$ $$ \text{Denominator} = (0.336)^2 = 0.11290 $$ $$ P_{cr} = \frac{3468.7}{0.11290} = 30723 \text{ N} \approx 30.72 \text{ kN} $$ $$ \sigma_{cr} = \frac{P_{cr}}{A} = \frac{30723}{9.6604 \times 10^{-5}} = 3.1804 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa} = 318.04 \text{ MPa} $$ **(iv) Safety assessment (2 marks)** Euler's elastic buckling formula is valid if the slenderness ratio exceeds the transition slenderness: $$ \lambda_{transition} = \pi \sqrt{\frac{2 E}{\sigma_y}} = \pi \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 68.9 \times 10^9}{276 \times 10^6}} = \pi \sqrt{499.28} = \pi \cdot 22.345 = 70.20 $$ Since `λ = 46.24 < λ_transition = 70.20`, **Euler's formula is not strictly valid** in the elastic range; the column would yield before buckling. Use the empirical **Johnson formula** or simply use yield as the limit: **Effective critical load ≈ σ_y · A:** $$ P_{yield} = \sigma_y \cdot A = 276 \times 10^6 \cdot 9.6604 \times 10^{-5} = 26662 \text{ N} \approx 26.66 \text{ kN} $$ **Factor of safety against service load (600 N):** $$ SF = \frac{P_{yield}}{P_{service}} = \frac{26662}{600} = 44.4 $$ > [!NOTE] > **Comment:** The back-cane tube has an enormous safety factor of **~44 against yielding** and even more against elastic buckling. The tube is **vastly over-designed for static compression** — the real design limiters in wheelchair back canes are **fatigue from cyclic flexure** and **weld heat-affected zone (HAZ) softening**, not buckling. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Forgetting to divide `N_r` by 2** when asking "load on each rear wheel" — examiners *deliberately* test this in 14-mark problems. **Always re-read the question wording.** > 2. **Confusing fixed-pinned with pinned-pinned:** K-constant is 1.0 for pinned-pinned, **0.7 for fixed-pinned**, 0.5 for fixed-fixed, 2.0 for fixed-free. Wrong K-value gives ~50% error in `P_cr`. > 3. **Using Euler when slenderness is low:** Euler's formula is for *long slender* columns. For `λ < λ_transition`, you must use the **Johnson parabolic formula** or the yield criterion. Markers deduct up to **2 marks** for this conceptual error. > 4. **Ignoring frame self-weight:** Some students compute reactions using *only user mass*. Always include frame mass in `W_total`. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist — Wheeled Mobility Supporting Structure** - ✅ The supporting structure is the **load-bearing skeleton** of the wheelchair — frame, cross-braces, back canes, axles, footrests, armrests, push handles. - ✅ Static load path: **User → Seat → Side Frames → Axle Plate / Caster Fork → Wheels / Casters → Ground.** - ✅ Approximately **70% of total weight** rests on the rear wheels; the rest on front casters. - ✅ **Cross-braces carry pure axial load**; **side frames carry bending + torsion**; **back canes carry combined axial + bending** (cantilever). - ✅ Most common materials: **Aluminum 6061-T6** (ultralight standard), **7005-T6** (high-end), **Ti-6Al-4V** (premium custom), **CFRP** (racing), **Chrome-Moly 4130** (sports). - ✅ **Specific strength** is the most important material selector for human-powered wheelchairs. - ✅ Euler buckling: `P_cr = π²EI/(KL)²` — valid only when slenderness `λ > λ_transition = π√(2E/σ_y)`. - ✅ **Safety factor ≥ 3.0** for static loads and **≥ 2.0 for fatigue** (Soderberg) — KTU 2024 standard. - ✅ Governing standards: **ISO 7176 series** (static stability, brakes, dimensions, crash safety), **RESNA WC-series**, **ISO 7176-19** for wheelchair-as-vehicle-seat. - ✅ Frame mass budget: **6–10 kg (ultralight)**, **12–18 kg (standard)**, **25–40 kg (powered)**. - ✅ Casters are **free to swivel**; the front wheel reaction `N_f` stabilizes the chair against rearward tipping. - ✅ The CG lies **behind the rear axle** for stability — this is intentional to prevent forward tip. - ✅ Fatigue (not static strength) is the **true design limit** for back canes — they undergo ~10⁶ cycles/year. - ✅ Bending stress formula `σ = Mc/I` is the single most-tested equation in this module — practice it until memorized. - ✅ Hollow tube sections are **preferred over solid rods** because `I` scales with the *fourth power* of diameter — same mass, vastly higher stiffness.

topics.title: Frame Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT614 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Frame Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Frame in Assistive Medical Devices?

A **frame** in an assistive medical device is the **primary load-bearing skeletal structure** that supports the user's body weight, provides geometric stability, and acts as the mounting platform for all functional subsystems (wheels, brakes, seating, supports, and electronic controls). In mobility aids, prosthetics, and orthotics, the frame dictates the device's **mechanical strength, weight, portability, and clinical suitability**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *A frame is the structural chassis of an assistive device that determines its rigidity, folding capability, adjustability, and biomechanical compatibility with the end user. The frame type is selected based on the user's disability, activity level, environment, and prescription.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **human skeleton**. Just as our skeleton (composed of rigid bones, flexible joints, and cartilage) provides shape, supports organs, and enables movement through articulated joints, the frame of a wheelchair or walker serves the same role. A **rigid frame** behaves like a fully fused skeleton (no flexibility), a **folding frame** works like joints that can bend for storage, and a **tilt-in-space frame** mimics the way a person leans back in a chair to redistribute weight.

Think of frame types as belonging to three broad families:
- **Stationary/Rigid** — engineered like a bicycle frame for maximum energy transfer.
- **Folding/Collapsible** — engineered like a folding ruler, sacrificing some rigidity for portability.
- **Adjustable/Modular** — engineered like modular furniture, allowing the frame to grow or adapt to the user.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Principle:** No single frame type is universally "best." The prescription is a function of the user's **clinical needs**, **lifestyle**, **transportation requirements**, and **budget**. KTU examiners frequently test whether students can match a frame type to a specific user condition.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following are the **industry-standard benchmarks** used to evaluate frames in assistive medical devices:

- **Maximum User Weight Capacity:** typically **100 kg to 150 kg** for standard frames; bariatric frames extend to **250 kg or higher**.
- **Frame Material Density:**
  - **Aluminum alloy (6061-T6):** ≈ **2.7 g/cm³**
  - **Titanium (Ti-6Al-4V):** ≈ **4.43 g/cm³**
  - **Chromoly steel (4130):** ≈ **7.85 g/cm³**
  - **Carbon fiber composite:** ≈ **1.55 g/cm³**
- **Standard Wheelchair Seat Width:** **16, 18, 20 inches** (≈ 406, 457, 508 mm).
