topics.title: Smart Wheelchairs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT614 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Smart Wheelchairs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Smart Wheelchair (KTU 2024 Definition):** A *Smart Wheelchair* (also termed an **Intelligent Powered Wheelchair** or **Autonomous Mobility Platform**) is a mechatronic assistive device that augments a conventional electric wheelchair with **embedded sensors, microcontrollers, actuators, and intelligent control algorithms** to provide **semi-autonomous or fully autonomous navigation, obstacle avoidance, environmental mapping, and adaptive human–machine interaction** for users with severe physical, neurological, or cognitive impairments.

In the KTU 2024 OEBRT614 syllabus context, a Smart Wheelchair is classified as a **Class II Active Medical Device** that operates on the principle of **sensor fusion + behavior-based control**, transforming a passive mobility aid into a **cognitive partner** for the user.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Self-Driving Car for the Indoor User"**
>
> Imagine you are blindfolded inside a large unfamiliar house. You have a walking stick, but you still bump into furniture. Now, suppose someone gives you **a small robotic guide dog** that:
> - **Whistles** when an obstacle is 2 m ahead,
> - **Pulls your hand** to the left when a wall is too close,
> - **Drives itself** to the kitchen when you say, *"Take me to the kitchen,"*
> - And **refuses to climb down a staircase** even if you push the joystick forward.
>
> That robotic guide dog is exactly what a **Smart Wheelchair** is — but in a 4-wheeled, motorized form. It does **not replace the user**; it **amplifies** the user's intent with environmental intelligence.

---

## 1.3 Core Differentiators from Conventional Powered Wheelchairs

| Feature | Conventional Powered Wheelchair | Smart Wheelchair |
|---|---|---|
| **Command Input** | Direct joystick voltage | Joystick, voice, head, eye, sip-and-puff, BCI |
| **Obstacle Handling** | **None** (bump-and-stop bumpers) | Active detection + avoidance |
| **Navigation** | User-driven only | Local maps, wall-following, goal-seeking |
| **Architecture** | Open-loop motor control | Closed-loop sensorimotor control |
| **Safety** | Mechanical brakes, current limit | Tilt sensors, anti-collision, speed scaling |
| **Intelligence** | **Zero on-board intelligence** | Microcontroller + algorithm layer |

---

## 1.4 Standard KTU Performance Metrics (Must Memorize)

The following **bolded metrics** are frequently asked in KTU exams and are part of the standard device specification envelope:

- **Maximum safe speed:** **1.5 m/s** (≈ 5.4 km/h, indoor) / **2.5 m/s** (outdoor)
- **Standard payload:** **100 kg – 150 kg**
- **Operating range per charge:** **15 km – 30 km**
- **Ultrasonic sensor range:** **0.02 m – 4.0 m**
- **Battery voltage:** **24 V DC** (typical 2 × 12 V sealed lead-acid or Li-ion)
- **Obstacle detection reaction time:** **≤ 100 ms**
- **Wheel encoder resolution:** **500 – 1024 pulses per revolution (PPR)**
- **International standard:** **ISO 7176** (Wheelchairs series)
- **Electromagnetic compatibility:** **IEC 60601-1-2**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 System Architecture — The 5-Layer Smart Wheelchair Model

A smart wheelchair is best understood as a **5-layer hierarchical mechatronic stack**:

### Layer 1 — **Physical / Mechanical Layer**
- Chassis (steel/aluminum/composite)
- 2 drive wheels + 2 caster wheels (**differential-drive** configuration is dominant)
- Drive motors: typically **24 V brushed/brushless DC, 150 W – 300 W each**
- Encoders mounted on drive-wheel shafts
- Tilt sensor (IMU) — usually **MPU6050 / MPU9250**

### Layer 2 — **Sensing / Perception Layer**
- **Ultrasonic sensors** (HC-SR04, Maxbotix) — proximity 0.02 m – 4.0 m
- **IR proximity sensors** (Sharp GP2Y0A21) — short range 0.10 m – 0.80 m
- **LiDAR** (2D sweep) — high-resolution mapping
- **RGB / Depth cameras** — object recognition (people, doors, stairs)
- **Wheel encoders** — odometry
- **IMU** — orientation, tilt, heading

### Layer 3 — **Processing / Decision Layer**
- **Microcontroller / SBC:** Arduino Mega, STM32, Raspberry Pi, NVIDIA Jetson Nano
- Runs the **sensor fusion algorithm** (e.g., complementary filter, Kalman filter)
- Hosts the **behavior-based arbiter** (subsumption architecture)
- Executes **local path planning** (DWA, A*, VFH+)

### Layer 4 — **Actuation / Motor-Driver Layer**
- **H-bridge motor drivers** (BTS7960, L298N, Cytron MDD10A)
- **PWM** speed control at 20 kHz to avoid audible noise
- **Closed-loop PID** on wheel velocity
- **Regenerative braking** (advanced models)

### Layer 5 — **Human–Machine Interface (HMI) Layer**
- Joystick, sip-and-puff, head-array switch
- Voice recognition (offline: Vosk, PocketSphinx)
- Eye-gaze tracker (Tobii)
- Brain-Computer Interface (OpenBCI, Emotiv EPOC)
- Tactile / haptic feedback display

---

## 2.2 Operating Principle (Why it Works)

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Operating Principle — "Sense → Think → Act" Loop**
> At a fixed cycle period **T (typically 50 ms – 100 ms)**, the wheelchair performs the closed loop:
>
> **SENSE** → Read ultrasonic, IR, encoder, IMU, camera values
> **THINK** → Sensor-fuse → estimate (x, y, θ) and obstacle map → choose behavior
> **ACT** → Output PWM duty cycle to left and right motors via PID controller
>
> This loop, repeated **10 – 20 times per second**, gives the smart wheelchair its **reactive autonomy**.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are the **highest-yield formulas** for KTU 2024 Scheme ESE questions on Smart Wheelchairs. Memorize units as well as symbols.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Differential-Drive Forward Velocity** | $v = \dfrac{v_R + v_L}{2}$ | $v_L, v_R$ : left / right wheel linear velocities (m/s) |
| 2 | **Angular Velocity (Yaw rate)** | $\omega = \dfrac{v_R - v_L}{b}$ | $b$ : wheelbase (distance between drive wheels), m |
| 3 | **Instantaneous Turning Radius** | $R = \dfrac{b}{2} \cdot \dfrac{v_R + v_L}{v_R - v_L}$ | $R$ : turning radius (m); $\vert v_R - v_L \vert > 0$ |
| 4 | **Ultrasonic Distance (Time of Flight)** | $d = \dfrac{v_{sound} \cdot t_{echo}}{2}$ | $v_{sound} \approx 343$ m/s; $t_{echo}$ in seconds |
| 5 | **Encoder-Based Odometry (Linear)** | $d_{wheel} = \dfrac{N_{pulses}}{N_{PPR}} \cdot 2\pi r$ | $r$ : wheel radius (m); $N_{PPR}$ : pulses per revolution |
| 6 | **Encoder-Based Odometry (Heading)** | $\Delta \theta = \dfrac{d_R - d_L}{b}$ | $d_R, d_L$ : incremental wheel distances (m) |
| 7 | **Battery Range (Wh basis)** | $R_{km} = \dfrac{V_{bat} \cdot C_{Ah} \cdot \eta}{P_{avg}}$ | $\eta$ : drivetrain efficiency ≈ 0.70; $P_{avg}$ in W |
| 8 | **Motor Mechanical Power** | $P = \tau \cdot \omega_m$ | $\tau$ : torque (N·m); $\omega_m$ : rad/s |
| 9 | **DC Motor Back-EMF** | $V_{emf} = K_e \cdot \omega$ | $K_e$ : back-EMF constant (V·s/rad) |
| 10 | **PID Controller Output** | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \displaystyle\int e(t)\,dt + K_d \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$ | $K_p, K_i, K_d$ : tuning gains; $e(t)$ : error |
| 11 | **Stair / Drop Detection Angle** | $\theta_{tilt} \geq \theta_{safe}$ (typically $15^\circ$) | IMU roll / pitch threshold |
| 12 | **Sensor Fusion (Complementary Filter)** | $\theta_{fused} = \alpha \cdot \theta_{gyro} + (1-\alpha) \cdot \theta_{accel}$ | $\alpha \approx 0.98$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL — Don't confuse the two radii:** A differential-drive wheelchair has a **geometric turning radius $R$** (its *path* radius) which is **infinite when going straight** ($v_R = v_L$). It is **not the same** as the wheel radius $r$ used in odometry.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Smart wheelchairs are not just academic curiosities; they are a **multi-billion-dollar assistive technology market** with applications in:

1. **Rehabilitation Engineering** — restoring mobility for spinal-cord-injury (SCI) patients
2. **Geriatric Care** — fall-prevention via tilt detection
3. **ALS / Locked-in Syndrome** — BCI-controlled variants
4. **Pediatric Cerebral Palsy** — shared-control learning wheelchairs
5. **Hospital Logistics** — autonomous patient transport in large hospitals
6. **Disaster-Rescue Robotics** — the same differential-drive + SLAM stack is used in ground-rescue robots

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every step is shown explicitly. No algebraic transition is skipped. All multi-line equations use `\begin{aligned}` to keep them readable.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Differential-Drive Kinematic Model (Forward Velocity & Yaw Rate)

A smart wheelchair in differential-drive mode has **two independently driven wheels** (left, right) of radius $r$ separated by axle length $b$, plus two passive casters.

### Step 1 — Define the angular velocities of the wheels
Let the left and right wheels spin at $\omega_L$ and $\omega_R$ (rad/s). The contact-point linear velocity of each wheel is:

$$v_L = r \cdot \omega_L \qquad v_R = r \cdot \omega_R$$

**Text row:** *The linear velocity at the contact patch equals wheel radius times its angular velocity, because pure rolling is assumed (no slip).*

### Step 2 — Express the chassis linear velocity
By rigid-body kinematics, the chassis centre moves with the **average** of the two wheel velocities:

$$\begin{aligned}
v &= \frac{v_L + v_R}{2} \\
  &= \frac{r\,\omega_L + r\,\omega_R}{2} \\
  &= \frac{r}{2}\,(\omega_L + \omega_R)
\end{aligned}$$

**Text row:** *The chassis centre is at the midpoint of the axle; its forward speed is the arithmetic mean of the left and right contact-point speeds.*

### Step 3 — Express the yaw rate (angular velocity about the vertical axis)
The differential between the two wheels produces rotation about the Instantaneous Centre of Rotation (ICR). From arc-length conservation on a circular path of radius $R$:

$$v_R = \omega \cdot \left(R + \tfrac{b}{2}\right), \qquad v_L = \omega \cdot \left(R - \tfrac{b}{2}\right)$$

Subtracting:

$$v_R - v_L = \omega \cdot b$$

Therefore:

$$\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{b} = \frac{r}{b}\,(\omega_R - \omega_L)$$

**Text row:** *The yaw rate is proportional to the **difference** of wheel speeds and inversely proportional to the wheelbase. If both wheels spin equally, the chair moves straight with zero yaw.*

### Step 4 — Express the turning radius $R$

Solving the two equations $v_R - v_L = \omega b$ and $v_R + v_L = 2v$:

$$\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{v}{\omega} \\
  &= \frac{(v_R + v_L)/2}{(v_R - v_L)/b} \\
  &= \frac{b}{2} \cdot \frac{v_R + v_L}{v_R - v_L}
\end{aligned}$$

**Text row:** *When $v_R = v_L$, denominator → 0 and the radius $R \rightarrow \infty$ (pure straight-line motion). When $v_L = 0$, the chair pivots about the left wheel and $R = b/2$.*

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Ultrasonic Time-of-Flight Distance Measurement

A typical ultrasonic sensor (HC-SR04) emits a 40 kHz burst and listens for the echo.

### Step 1 — Time of flight
The wave travels to the obstacle and back. Total distance covered by the wave = $2d$ where $d$ is the one-way distance:

$$2d = v_{sound} \cdot t_{echo}$$

### Step 2 — Solve for distance
$$d = \frac{v_{sound} \cdot t_{echo}}{2}$$

**Text row:** *The factor of 2 is mandatory — the wave traverses the gap **twice** (forward + return).*

### Step 3 — Numerical example (KTU-style)
Given: $t_{echo} = 11.66$ ms, $v_{sound} = 343$ m/s.

$$d = \frac{343 \cdot 11.66 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = \frac{3.998}{2} = 1.999 \;\text{m}$$

**Text row:** *Substitute, compute, and report units. KTU examiners deduct marks for missing units.*

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Battery Range Equation

### Step 1 — Total stored energy
A battery of voltage $V_{bat}$ (V) and capacity $C_{Ah}$ (A·h) stores:

$$E_{stored} = V_{bat} \cdot C_{Ah} \cdot 3600 \quad \text{(in Joules)}$$

**Text row:** *3600 converts A·h to A·s (Coulombs), giving J = V·C.*

### Step 2 — Usable energy
Not all energy is delivered to the wheels; some is lost as heat, controller inefficiency, and motor copper losses. Let $\eta$ = overall drivetrain efficiency.

$$E_{usable} = \eta \cdot V_{bat} \cdot C_{Ah} \cdot 3600$$

### Step 3 — Range in km
If the average propulsion power is $P_{avg}$ (W), the operating time is:

$$t_{run} = \frac{E_{usable}}{P_{avg}} \quad \text{(seconds)}$$

If average speed is $v_{avg}$ (m/s), the range is:

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{km} &= v_{avg} \cdot t_{run} \\
       &= v_{avg} \cdot \frac{\eta \cdot V_{bat} \cdot C_{Ah} \cdot 3600}{P_{avg}} \quad \text{(in metres)}
\end{aligned}$$

Convert to km:

$$R_{km} = \frac{v_{avg} \cdot \eta \cdot V_{bat} \cdot C_{Ah} \cdot 3600}{1000 \cdot P_{avg}} \quad \text{(km)}$$

**Text row:** *Final equation ties user velocity, battery chemistry, drivetrain efficiency, and propulsion power into a single deliverable — the operating range.*

### Step 4 — Numerical example
$V_{bat} = 24$ V, $C_{Ah} = 35$ A·h, $\eta = 0.70$, $v_{avg} = 1$ m/s, $P_{avg} = 80$ W.

$$R_{km} = \frac{1 \cdot 0.70 \cdot 24 \cdot 35 \cdot 3600}{1000 \cdot 80} = \frac{2.117 \times 10^6}{80\,000} = 26.46 \;\text{km}$$

**Text row:** *Realistic range for a mid-tier smart wheelchair.*

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Wheel Odometry Update Rule

### Step 1 — Incremental wheel rotation
In one sampling period $T$, the encoders emit $N_L$ and $N_R$ pulses for left and right wheels respectively.

### Step 2 — Incremental linear distance
$$\Delta d_L = \frac{N_L}{N_{PPR}} \cdot 2\pi r \qquad \Delta d_R = \frac{N_R}{N_{PPR}} \cdot 2\pi r$$

**Text row:** *The number of revolutions = pulses / pulses-per-revolution; multiplied by circumference $2\pi r$ gives the distance rolled.*

### Step 3 — Incremental heading change
$$\Delta \theta = \frac{\Delta d_R - \Delta d_L}{b}$$

### Step 4 — Pose update in 2-D plane
Using midpoint integration:

$$\begin{aligned}
x_{k+1} &= x_k + \Delta d \cdot \cos\!\left(\theta_k + \tfrac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right) \\
y_{k+1} &= y_k + \Delta d \cdot \sin\!\left(\theta_k + \tfrac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right) \\
\theta_{k+1} &= \theta_k + \Delta\theta
\end{aligned}$$

where $\Delta d = \dfrac{\Delta d_R + \Delta d_L}{2}$.

**Text row:** *This is the standard **dead-reckoning** update used in nearly every KTU lab experiment on smart wheelchairs.*

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Behavior Arbitration in Subsumption Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> The **Subsumption Architecture** (Rodney Brooks, 1986) is the dominant control framework for smart wheelchairs. Behaviors are layered, with higher layers **subsuming** (overriding) lower ones.

| Priority | Behavior | Trigger Condition | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 (Lowest) | **Wander** | No obstacles in any direction | Random / commanded motion |
| 1 | **Avoid Obstacles** | $\exists$ sensor $d < d_{safe}$ (e.g., 0.5 m) | Steer away from nearest obstacle |
| 2 | **Wall Follow** | User activates wall-follow mode | Maintain $d = d_{ref}$ from wall |
| 3 | **Approach Goal** | Goal coordinate set | Steer toward $(x_{goal}, y_{goal})$ |
| 4 (Highest) | **Emergency Stop** | Tilt $> 15^\circ$ or BCI panic signal | **Cut PWM to 0 immediately** |

**Working rule:** A higher-priority behavior **inhibits** (suppresses) the output of any lower-priority behavior for that control cycle.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All diagrams use **Mermaid** with strict compliance to the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE safeguards: pure-alphanumeric node IDs (letter-prefixed), no markdown formatting inside quoted labels, and no reserved-keyword collisions.

---

## 4.1 Smart Wheelchair — Top-Level System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[User Input Layer Joystick Voice BCI SipPuff]
    A2[Perception Layer Ultrasonic IR Lidar Camera Encoder IMU]
    A3[Decision Layer Microcontroller Behavior Arbiter Sensor Fusion]
    A4[Actuation Layer Motor Driver H-Bridge PWM Output]
    A5[Mechanical Layer Drive Wheels Casters Chassis]

    A1 --> A3
    A2 --> A3
    A3 --> A4
    A4 --> A5

    A5 -. Feedback .-> A2
    A1 -. User Intent Feedback .-> A3
    A4 -. Tachometer Feedback .-> A3

    classDef input fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef sense fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
    classDef think fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,color:#000
    classDef act fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000
    classDef mech fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#6c757d,color:#000

    class A1 input
    class A2 sense
    class A3 think
    class A4 act
    class A5 mech
```

**Reading the diagram:** Data flows upward from sensors to the decision layer, and control commands flow downward to the motors. **Dashed lines** denote feedback (encoder pulses, IMU, user confirmation).

---

## 4.2 Subsumption Behavior Arbitration Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    B0[Level 4 EMERGENCY STOP]
    B1[Level 3 APPROACH GOAL]
    B2[Level 2 WALL FOLLOW]
    B3[Level 1 AVOID OBSTACLES]
    B4[Level 0 WANDER]

    B0 -->|inhibits| B1
    B1 -->|inhibits| B2
    B2 -->|inhibits| B3
    B3 -->|inhibits| B4

    B4 --> M[Motor Command Output PWM to H-Bridge]
    B3 --> M
    B2 --> M
    B1 --> M
    B0 --> M

    S1[Tilt Sensor IMU] --> B0
    S2[Goal Setpoint from User] --> B1
    S3[Wall Distance Sensor] --> B2
    S4[Ultrasonic Array 6 Sensors] --> B3
    S5[Joystick or Voice Default] --> B4

    classDef emerg fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000
    classDef goal fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,color:#000
    classDef wall fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef avoid fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
    classDef wander fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#6c757d,color:#000

    class B0 emerg
    class B1 goal
    class B2 wall
    class B3 avoid
    class B4 wander
```

**Reading the diagram:** Higher-priority behaviors can **suppress** lower ones via *inhibit* arrows. The final motor command is the surviving (highest-active) behavior.

---

## 4.3 Differential-Drive Kinematic Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    W1((Left Wheel radius r)) --- C([Chassis Centre x y theta])
    C --- W2((Right Wheel radius r))
    W1 --- |axle b| W2
    W1 -->|vL = r wL| VEL[Chassis Velocity v]
    W2 -->|vR = r wR| VEL
    VEL --> YAW[Yaw Rate omega equals vR minus vL over b]
    YAW --> RAD[Turning Radius R equals v over omega]

    classDef wheel fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef center fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000

    class W1,W2 wheel
    class C center
    class VEL,YAW,RAD output
```

---

## 4.4 Sensor Fusion & Pose-Estimation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    E1[Wheel Encoder Left] --> F1[Encoder Processing]
    E2[Wheel Encoder Right] --> F1
    I1[IMU Gyroscope 3-axis] --> F2[Complementary Filter]
    I2[IMU Accelerometer 3-axis] --> F2
    L1[2D Lidar Sweep] --> F3[Local Occupancy Grid]
    U1[Ultrasonic Array] --> F3

    F1 --> P[Pose Estimator x y theta]
    F2 --> P
    F3 --> P

    P --> MAP[Local Cost Map]
    MAP --> PL[Path Planner A-star or DWA]
    PL --> BEH[Behavior Arbiter Subsumption]
    BEH --> PID[PID Velocity Controller Left and Right]
    PID --> MD[Motor Driver BTS7960 x 2]
    MD --> MTR[Drive Motors 24V DC x 2]

    classDef sense fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef fuse fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,color:#000

    classDef act fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000

    class E1,E2,I1,I2,L1,U1 sense
    class F1,F2,F3,P,MAP fuse

```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The questions below are modeled on the **KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Evaluation (ESE)** pattern. Each carries a **simulated KTU past-year tag** for realism. Mark distribution strictly follows KTU norms.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** — 3 Marks
**Define a Smart Wheelchair. List any four sensors commonly used in its perception layer.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Smart Wheelchair:** A motorised wheelchair augmented with embedded sensors, a microcontroller, and intelligent algorithms that enable **obstacle detection, autonomous or semi-autonomous navigation, and adaptive human–machine interaction** for users with severe physical or cognitive impairments.
>
> **Four sensors:**
> 1. **Ultrasonic sensor** (HC-SR04) — for medium-range obstacle detection (0.02 m – 4 m)
> 2. **IR proximity sensor** (Sharp GP2Y0A21) — for short-range floor-level detection
> 3. **Wheel encoder** (optical, 500 – 1024 PPR) — for odometry and speed feedback
> 4. **IMU** (MPU6050) — for tilt detection and heading estimation

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** — 3 Marks
**Differentiate between *subsumption* and *deliberative* control architectures in the context of a smart wheelchair.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Subsumption (Reactive) | Deliberative (Planning) |
|---|---|---|
| **Principle** | Layered behaviors; higher layers inhibit lower | Sense → Plan → Act sequence |
| **World Model** | **None** (sensor-driven) | Requires full **map / world model** |
| **Response Time** | **Very fast** (ms scale) | Slower (requires planning step) |
| **Computational Cost** | Low | High (A*, D*, RRT) |
| **Robustness to Map Errors** | **High** | Low (depends on map accuracy) |
| **KTU Example Use** | Obstacle avoidance, BCI panic-stop | Goal-reaching, autonomous docking |

**Conclusion:** Most real smart wheelchairs use a **hybrid** (3-tier) architecture that combines both for speed and intelligence.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do NOT** confuse **wheel radius $r$** with **turning radius $R$** — they are different parameters with different units.
> - **Do NOT** forget the factor of **2** in the ultrasonic time-of-flight formula. Half the batch loses 1 mark for this.
> - **Do NOT** list sensors without stating their **range and purpose** — generic listings are marked down 50%.
> - **Do NOT** skip the **subsumption priority order** in diagrams — arrows and priorities are explicitly checked.
> - **Do** use **standard SI units** consistently throughout numerical answers.
> - **Do** mention **ISO 7176** when discussing safety standards — KTU examiners value this.

---

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper, Module 3 Choice-1]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a Smart Wheelchair. Discuss the role of the perception, decision, and actuation layers.**

**Model Answer:**

A smart wheelchair architecture is organised as a **5-layer mechatronic stack**:

**1. Mechanical Layer:**
The base is a **differential-drive chassis** with two independently driven wheels (24 V DC, 150–300 W each) and two passive casters for stability.

**2. Perception Layer:**
This is the "senses" of the wheelchair. Typical sensors include:
- **Ultrasonic array** (5–6 sensors around the body) → obstacle map
- **IR sensors** (2–4 at the footplate) → drop / step detection
- **2D LiDAR** (optional) → high-resolution scanning
- **Wheel encoders** → odometry
- **IMU** → tilt, heading, acceleration

**3. Decision Layer:**
Hosts a **microcontroller (STM32/Arduino)** or **SBC (Raspberry Pi / Jetson Nano)**. It runs:
- **Sensor fusion** (e.g., complementary filter, Kalman filter) to estimate pose $(x, y, \theta)$
- **Behavior arbiter** (subsumption architecture) to resolve conflicts
- **Local path planner** (DWA, VFH+) to compute velocity commands

**4. Actuation Layer:**
Receives PWM duty cycles from the decision layer, drives **H-bridge motor drivers** (BTS7960 or Cytron MDD10A). Closed-loop **PID controllers** maintain target wheel velocity.

**5. HMI Layer:**
The user interface — joystick, voice, head-array, sip-and-puff, or BCI.

**Block Diagram** (refer to **Section 4.1** of these notes for the full Mermaid schematic).

**Role summary:**
- *Perception* **knows where the world is**
- *Decision* **decides what to do**
- *Actuation* **executes the decision**
- *HMI* **listens to the user**
- *Mechanical* **delivers the motion**

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A differential-drive smart wheelchair has a wheel radius $r = 0.15$ m, a wheelbase $b = 0.60$ m, and encoder resolution $N_{PPR} = 500$. In a sampling interval of $T = 100$ ms, the left encoder counts 25 pulses and the right encoder counts 35 pulses. Calculate (i) the linear velocity of each wheel, (ii) the chassis forward velocity and yaw rate, and (iii) the new heading after 5 sampling intervals (assume initial heading is zero).**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Linear velocity of each wheel**

Incremental distance rolled in $T = 0.10$ s:

$$\Delta d_L = \frac{25}{500} \cdot 2\pi \cdot 0.15 = 0.05 \cdot 0.9425 = 0.0471 \;\text{m}$$

$$\Delta d_R = \frac{35}{500} \cdot 2\pi \cdot 0.15 = 0.07 \cdot 0.9425 = 0.0660 \;\text{m}$$

Linear velocities (using $v = d / T$):

$$v_L = \frac{0.0471}{0.10} = 0.471 \;\text{m/s}$$

$$v_R = \frac{0.0660}{0.10} = 0.660 \;\text{m/s}$$

**(ii) Chassis forward velocity and yaw rate**

Using the formulas from Section 2.3:

$$v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{0.660 + 0.471}{2} = 0.5655 \;\text{m/s}$$

$$\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{b} = \frac{0.660 - 0.471}{0.60} = \frac{0.189}{0.60} = 0.315 \;\text{rad/s}$$

**(iii) Heading after 5 sampling intervals**

Per-interval heading change:

$$\Delta\theta = \frac{\Delta d_R - \Delta d_L}{b} = \frac{0.0660 - 0.0471}{0.60} = \frac{0.0189}{0.60} = 0.0315 \;\text{rad}$$

After 5 intervals:

$$\theta_5 = 5 \cdot 0.0315 = 0.1575 \;\text{rad} = 9.02^\circ$$

**Final Answers:**
- $v_L = 0.471$ m/s, $v_R = 0.660$ m/s
- $v = 0.5655$ m/s, $\omega = 0.315$ rad/s
- $\theta_5 \approx 9.02^\circ$ (turning left, since $v_R > v_L$)

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper, Module 3 Choice-2]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the working of an ultrasonic time-of-flight (ToF) sensor used for obstacle detection in a smart wheelchair. An HC-SR04 sensor returns an echo pulse width of $t_{echo} = 5.83$ ms. Compute the distance to the obstacle. What are the limitations of ultrasonic sensors in this application?**

**Model Answer:**

**Working Principle:**
The HC-SR04 ultrasonic sensor operates at **40 kHz**. The microcontroller sends a **10 µs trigger pulse** to the *Trig* pin. The sensor then emits **8 cycles of 40 kHz ultrasound**. The sound wave travels through air, reflects off the obstacle, and returns. The *Echo* pin goes HIGH for a duration $t_{echo}$ proportional to the round-trip time.

The distance is computed from the speed of sound in air:

$$d = \frac{v_{sound} \cdot t_{echo}}{2}$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

$$d = \frac{343 \cdot 5.83 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = \frac{1.9997}{2} = 0.9998 \;\text{m} \approx 1.00 \;\text{m}$$

**Limitations:**
1. **Specular reflection** — sound bounces away from angled surfaces
2. **Slow update rate** (≈ 40 Hz max) — limits cycle speed
3. **Poor angular resolution** — wide beam (~15°) misses narrow obstacles
4. **Cross-talk** between adjacent sensors
5. **Temperature dependence** — $v_{sound} = 331 + 0.6T$ (m/s), requires compensation
6. **Cannot detect soft / sound-absorbing materials** (cushions, curtains)

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Describe the various Human–Machine Interface (HMI) options for a smart wheelchair, with emphasis on how each is suited to specific categories of disability. Mention the relevance of the BCI-based interface.**

**Model Answer:**

A smart wheelchair must accommodate users with **widely varying residual abilities**. The HMI is therefore **modular and configurable**:

| User Condition | Recommended HMI | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **Standard user** (weak limbs) | Joystick (analog, 2-axis) | Direct, intuitive, low latency |
| **Quadriplegic** (no hand use) | **Sip-and-puff** switch | Uses breath control — always available |
| **High SCI / neck mobility only** | Head-array switches or head-tracker | Detects head tilt / rotation via IMU or camera |
| **ALS / advanced MD** | Eye-gaze tracker (Tobii) | Tracks pupil position; works when limbs fail |
| **Locked-in / late-stage ALS** | **BCI (Brain-Computer Interface)** | Uses EEG to decode motor intent |
| **Cognitive impairment** | Single-button touch UI with audio cues | Reduces cognitive load |

**BCI-Based Interface — Detailed Note:**

A **BCI-based** smart wheelchair (e.g., using **Emotiv EPOC** or **OpenBCI**) uses **electroencephalography (EEG)** to record brain activity from the scalp. **Motor-imagery** signals (imagining left-hand vs. right-hand movement) are decoded using **Common Spatial Patterns (CSP)** + **Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA)** or modern **deep-learning decoders (EEGNet, ConvNets)**.

**Advantages:**
- Last-resort option for **completely locked-in** users
- Non-invasive (no surgery)

**Limitations:**
- Low information transfer rate (ITR ≈ 25 bits/min)
- Susceptible to EMG / EOG artefacts
- Requires extensive **per-user calibration**
- Adds 5–15 seconds of command latency

**Conclusion:** BCI is a **promising but not yet mature** HMI for mass deployment. Most KTU lab projects use **joystick + voice + head-tracking** as a pragmatic compromise.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read 5 Minutes Before Exam)**

- ☐ **Definition:** Smart Wheelchair = Powered Wheelchair + Sensors + Microcontroller + Intelligent Algorithms
- ☐ **Standard KTU spec:** 24 V DC, 1.5 m/s indoor, 100–150 kg payload, 15–30 km range
- ☐ **5-layer architecture:** Mechanical → Perception → Decision → Actuation → HMI
- ☐ **Differential-drive formulas (MEMORIZE):**
  - $v = (v_L + v_R)/2$
  - $\omega = (v_R - v_L)/b$
  - $R = (b/2) \cdot (v_R + v_L)/(v_R - v_L)$
- ☐ **Ultrasonic distance (MEMORIZE):** $d = v_{sound} \cdot t_{echo} / 2$ → factor of 2 is **mandatory**
- ☐ **Encoder distance:** $\Delta d = (N/N_{PPR}) \cdot 2\pi r$
- ☐ **Pose update (midpoint integration):** $x_{k+1} = x_k + \Delta d \cos(\theta_k + \Delta\theta/2)$
- ☐ **Subsumption priority (top-down):** Emergency Stop > Approach Goal > Wall Follow > Avoid > Wander
- ☐ **PID controller:** $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \,de/dt$
- ☐ **Sensors & ranges:** Ultrasonic 0.02–4 m, IR 0.10–0.80 m, LiDAR up to 30 m, Camera = object recognition
- ☐ **HMI ladder:** Joystick → Sip-puff → Head-array → Eye-gaze → BCI
- ☐ **Safety standard:** ISO 7176; EMC: IEC 60601-1-2; tilt cutoff: ~15°
- ☐ **Operating loop period:** T = 50 – 100 ms (10–20 Hz)
- ☐ **Battery range formula:** $R_{km} = (v_{avg} \cdot \eta \cdot V_{bat} \cdot C_{Ah} \cdot 3600) / (1000 \cdot P_{avg})$
- ☐ **Two radii to NEVER confuse:** wheel radius $r$ (≈ 0.15 m) ≠ turning radius $R$ (depends on motion)
- ☐ **One behavior rule:** Higher subsumption layer **inhibits** all lower layers for that cycle.
- ☐ **One safety rule:** BCI "panic" signal and IMU tilt > 15° must cut PWM to **0** instantly.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Smart Wheelchairs**
> *(Prepared under KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10, 2024 Scheme, OEBRT614)*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Low-Technology Aids for Manipulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT614 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Low-Technology Aids for Manipulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme rehabilitation engineering, **Low-Technology Aids for Manipulation (LTAM)** are defined as **non-powered, mechanically simple, and economically affordable assistive devices** that compensate for limitations in a user's upper-limb (or occasionally lower-limb) range of motion, strength, dexterity, coordination, or grip function. They enable the user to perform **Activities of Daily Living (ADLs)** and **Instrumental Activities of Daily Living (IADLs)** such as dressing, eating, grooming, writing, reaching, and hygiene with greater independence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 4):**
> *Low-technology aids are non-electronic, non-motorized devices that rely on mechanical advantage, ergonomic geometry, and adaptive design to extend the functional reach and manipulation capabilities of users with physical impairments — particularly those resulting from spinal cord injury (SCI), arthritis, stroke (CVA), cerebral palsy, muscular dystrophy, or aging-related frailty.*

These devices sit at the **lowest tier** of the **Assistive Technology Pyramid (HAAT Model — Human, Activity, Assistive Technology)**, yet they are often the **most frequently prescribed** category of assistive devices globally because of their cost-effectiveness, durability, and zero power-dependence.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Extended Arm"**
>
> Imagine you dropped your house keys on the floor, but you are unable to bend down because of a stiff back. What is the simplest solution? You use a **stick with a hook** to scoop the keys up. That stick is a *low-technology aid for manipulation*. It does not have a battery, a motor, or a screen — it is just a clever mechanical extension of your hand. **All low-tech manipulation aids work on this exact principle:** they **redesign the geometry** of the task so that the user can accomplish it using the body movements they *do* have, instead of the movements they have *lost*.

A more technical analogy: think of a **crowbar** prying open a lid. The crowbar is a simple piece of shaped metal, but by changing the angle and length of force application, it multiplies the user's strength and reach. Low-tech manipulation aids operate on the same **Class 1 Lever** and **Class 3 Lever** biomechanical principles that physical therapists exploit in rehabilitation.

---

### 1.3 Key Characteristics (KTU Board-Weighted Terms)

| Characteristic | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Non-Powered** | No batteries, electricity, or motors required. |
| **Mechanically Simple** | Few or no moving parts; minimal maintenance. |
| **Low Cost** | Typically **₹ 200 – ₹ 5,000** in the Indian market; WHO essential-assistive-list pricing. |
| **Durable** | Service life often **5 – 10+ years**. |
| **User-Trained** | Requires short orientation; no specialist calibration. |
| **Task-Specific** | Often designed for a single ADL (e.g., sock donner, button hook). |
| **Portable** | Lightweight (typically **< 1.5 kg**), easy to carry. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU Term:** The phrase *"compensatory strategy device"* is the official KTU board phrase — examiners expect this exact wording in 3-mark and 14-mark answers.

---

### 1.4 Classification Snapshot (Preview)

For a 14-mark KTU answer, the examiner expects the student to classify these aids into **functional categories**. The canonical KTU 2024 classification is:

1. **Reaching Aids** (Reachers / Grabbers)
2. **Dressing Aids** (Dressing stick, sock/stocking aid, button hook, long-handled shoe horn)
3. **Eating & Drinking Aids** (Built-up handles, swivel spoon, plate guard, weighted cutlery, nosey cup)
4. **Grooming & Hygiene Aids** (Long-handled comb, long-handled sponge, toilet aid, bath brush)
5. **Writing & Communication Aids** (Pencil grips, handwriting aids, page turners)
6. **Holding & Stabilizing Aids** (Universal cuff, Dycem non-slip mat, suction cups, clamps)
7. **Kitchen & IADL Aids** (Jar opener, key turner, tap turner, one-handed cutting board)

> [!NOTE]
> All of these will be unpacked in **SECTION 2** with engineering rationale and in **SECTION 3** with analytical design matrices.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why Low-Tech Aids Work

The functional efficacy of any low-technology manipulation aid is governed by **three core biomechanical and ergonomic principles**. KTU examiners routinely test these principles for full 14 marks.

#### 2.1.1 Principle of Extended Lever Arm (Class 1 / Class 3 Lever)

Most reaching aids (e.g., a **reacher grabber**) function as a **Class 1 Lever**. The user applies a small force $F_{\text{in}}$ at the handle, which is transmitted through a rigid shaft to a gripping jaw at the far end. The mechanical relationship is:

$$
F_{\text{out}} = F_{\text{in}} \times \frac{L_{\text{effort}}}{L_{\text{load}}}
$$

where $L_{\text{effort}}$ is the length from the fulcrum (typically the trigger pivot) to the hand grip, and $L_{\text{load}}$ is the distance from the fulcrum to the gripping jaw.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** By making $L_{\text{effort}} \gg L_{\text{load}}$, the user can lift objects several times heavier than they could with a bare hand — *but* reach length and lifting force are an **engineering trade-off** (see Section 3).

#### 2.1.2 Principle of Ergonomic Handle Geometry

For users with **arthritis, cerebral palsy, or reduced grip strength**, the device's handle is the most critical interface. KTU follows the **hand anthropometry standards** (IS 14268 / ISO 7250):

$$
\text{Optimal Handle Diameter} = d_{\text{optimal}} \approx 30 \text{ to } 50 \text{ mm}
$$

Handles below 30 mm cause excessive grip force; handles above 50 mm cannot be fully closed by arthritic fingers. **Built-up handles** (often foam tubing, thermoplastic, or moulded silicone) enlarge the grip circumference to fall within this optimal range.

#### 2.1.3 Principle of Task Redirection (Compensatory Movement)

Low-tech aids do **not** restore lost function. They **redirect the task** to body parts that retain function. For example:

- A person with a **non-functional right hand** uses a **universal cuff** strapped to the palm to hold a spoon — eating is redirected to wrist/arm motion.
- A person who **cannot bend at the waist or knees** uses a **reacher** to pick up dropped items — bending is redirected to shoulder/elbow extension.

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **Nagi Disablement Model** in action: the aid compensates at the *functional limitation* level, not the *impairment* level.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

The following table is engineered for **rapid last-night revision**. Memorize it before any Part-B answer.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol / Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Lever Mechanical Advantage | $MA = \frac{L_{\text{effort}}}{L_{\text{load}}}$ (dimensionless) | Ratio of output force to input force in reachers and grabbers. |
| 2 | Grip Strength Required | $F_{\text{grip}} = \frac{W_{\text{object}}}{2 \cdot \mu \cdot r}$ (Newtons) | Force needed to hold an object; $W$ = weight, $\mu$ = friction coefficient, $r$ = handle radius. |
| 3 | Optimal Handle Diameter | $d_{\text{opt}} \in [30, 50]$ **mm** | Anthropometrically derived range to minimize grip effort. |
| 4 | Reacher Length Trade-off | $L_{\text{total}} \leq 0.75 \cdot h_{\text{user}}$ (metres) | Total reacher length should not exceed 75% of user's height to avoid toppling. |
| 5 | Dycem Coefficient of Friction | $\mu_{\text{Dycem}} \approx 1.5 \text{ to } 2.5$ | Non-slip mat friction; 3–5× higher than typical tablecloth. |
| 6 | Universal Cuff Strap Tension | $T_{\text{strap}} = k \cdot \Delta x$ (Newtons) | Hooke's law for elastic cuff; $k$ = elastic constant, $\Delta x$ = extension. |
| 7 | Dressing Stick Bending Stress | $\sigma = \frac{M \cdot c}{I}$ (Pa) | Where $M$ = bending moment from user's hand force, $c$ = radius, $I$ = 2nd moment of area. |
| 8 | Plate Guard Retention Torque | $\tau = F_{\text{clip}} \cdot r_{\text{plate}}$ (N·m) | Clipping force × plate radius; prevents plate guard from dislodging. |
| 9 | Weighted Cutlery Compensation | $m_{\text{cutlery}} = 80 \text{ to } 200$ **grams** | Tremor-suppression mass for users with Parkinson's or ataxia. |
| 10 | Cost Threshold (WHO Essential) | $C_{\text{LTAM}} < \text{0.5 \% of GNI per capita}$ | WHO affordability benchmark for essential assistive devices. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipe-Symbol Safety:** Notice the use of `$\vert$` style fences above (e.g., $d_{\text{opt}} \in [30, 50]$ **mm**). In KTU answer scripts, always write inequality fences as $\vert x \vert$ or use the word "modulus of" to avoid ambiguity.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Low-tech manipulation aids are not academic curiosities. They are deployed at scale in:

- **Rural Indian Primary Health Centres (PHCs)** — under the **ADIP Scheme (Assistance to Disabled Persons for Purchase/Fitting of Aids/Appliances)** of the Ministry of Social Justice & Empowerment.
- **WHO GATE Initiative (Global Cooperation on Assistive Technology, 2014–2020)** — listed in the **Priority Assistive Products List (APL)**.
- **Disaster-response settings** — low-tech aids function without power, critical in refugee camps.
- **School inclusion programs** — pencil grips and page turners enable children with cerebral palsy to participate in mainstream classrooms (aligned with **RPwD Act 2016, India**).

---

### 2.4 The HAAT Model Context (For 14-Mark Theory)

The **Human, Activity, Assistive Technology (HAAT)** model (Cook & Hussey, 1995/2002) is the canonical KTU theoretical framework. For low-tech manipulation aids, the model assigns:

- **Human** → the user with sensory, motor, or cognitive limitation.
- **Activity** → the specific ADL/IADL (eating, dressing, grooming).
- **Assistive Technology** → the low-tech aid itself.
- **Context** → physical environment (home, school, workplace).

The low-tech aid sits at the *interface* of all four components and is the **first-tier** prescription in the WHO **"5P" Assistive Technology Service Delivery Pathway** (People, Products, Personnel, Policy, Provision).

---

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module-Adaptive Notice:** This topic is more **engineering-design** oriented than purely mathematical. The "derivations" below are therefore presented as **analytical design derivations, ergonomic justification chains, and selection matrices** — all of which the KTU board accepts as full 14-mark analytical answers.

---

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation 1 — Optimal Reacher Length Using Lever Trade-off

A reacher grabber is essentially a **Class 1 Lever**. The user wants to maximize reach $L_{\text{load}}$ without losing too much lifting force. We can derive the **optimal total reacher length**.

**Step 1 — Define variables**
- $L_{\text{total}}$ = total reacher length (m)
- $L_{\text{effort}}$ = effort-arm length (handle to trigger pivot)
- $L_{\text{load}}$ = load-arm length (pivot to jaw)
- Therefore $L_{\text{total}} = L_{\text{effort}} + L_{\text{load}}$

**Step 2 — State the force balance**

Taking moments about the pivot (trigger):

$$
F_{\text{in}} \cdot L_{\text{effort}} = F_{\text{out}} \cdot L_{\text{load}}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for mechanical advantage**

$$
MA = \frac{F_{\text{out}}}{F_{\text{in}}} = \frac{L_{\text{effort}}}{L_{\text{load}}}
$$

**Step 4 — Express the trade-off**

A user with limited arm span (e.g., seated wheelchair user) requires a long $L_{\text{total}}$, but the lifting force $F_{\text{out}}$ drops linearly as $L_{\text{load}}$ grows:

$$
F_{\text{out}} = F_{\text{in}} \cdot \frac{L_{\text{total}} - L_{\text{load}}}{L_{\text{load}}}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the KTU constraint**

For a seated user of height $h_{\text{user}}$, the maximum usable reacher length is empirically:

$$
L_{\text{total, max}} = 0.75 \cdot h_{\text{user}}
$$

For a 1.6 m tall user:

$$
L_{\text{total, max}} = 0.75 \times 1.6 = 1.20 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 6 — Set the optimal pivot position**

For balanced performance, designers choose the pivot such that $L_{\text{effort}} \approx 1.5 \cdot L_{\text{load}}$, giving a mechanical advantage of **1.5×**. This means a 30 N hand squeeze lifts a 45 N object (≈ 4.5 kg mass).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering takeaway:** Reachability and liftability are **inverse** design objectives. KTU 14-mark answers must explicitly mention this trade-off.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Derivation 2 — Why Built-up Handles Reduce Grip Fatigue

A cylindrical handle is gripped by the fingers with a power grip (or pinch grip for small items). The torque balance is:

$$
T_{\text{finger}} = F_{\text{grip}} \cdot r_{\text{handle}}
$$

This torque must equal the **resisting torque** of the held object's weight (assuming horizontal hold):

$$
F_{\text{grip}} \cdot r = \mu \cdot F_{\text{grip}} \cdot R_{\text{object}} = W_{\text{object}} \cdot R_{\text{object}}
$$

Solving for the required grip force:

$$
F_{\text{grip}} = \frac{W_{\text{object}} \cdot R_{\text{object}}}{\mu \cdot r}
$$

**Numerical example:** Holding a 1.5 kg cup of tea ($W = 14.7$ N) with a small handle of radius $r = 10$ mm = 0.01 m, with skin friction $\mu = 0.3$:

$$
F_{\text{grip}} = \frac{14.7 \times 0.04}{0.3 \times 0.01} = \frac{0.588}{0.003} = 196 \text{ N}
$$

This exceeds the grip strength of a frail elderly person (often 80–120 N) → the cup slips.

**Solution — Built-up handle** with $r = 25$ mm = 0.025 m:

$$
F_{\text{grip, new}} = \frac{0.588}{0.3 \times 0.025} = 78.4 \text{ N}
$$

A **60% reduction** in required grip force → now within the user's capacity. This is why **built-up handles are the single most-prescribed low-tech eating aid** worldwide.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Phrasing:** Use the phrase *"build-up handle enlarges the moment arm, thereby reducing the muscular grip force required to counteract the gravitational torque of the object."*

---

### 3.3 Analytical Selection Matrix — Choosing the Right Low-Tech Aid

This matrix is a **high-yield 7-mark sub-answer** for any "selection / prescription" 14-mark question.

| User Limitation | Recommended LTAM Category | Specific Devices | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cannot bend at waist/knees** (SCI, post-hip surgery) | Reaching aids | Reacher, long-handled shoe horn, sock aid with straps | Compensates for lost trunk flexion by extending the arm's functional length. |
| **Weak grip (arthritis, muscular dystrophy)** | Built-up handles & universal cuff | Foam-tube handles, palm cuff, Dycem mats | Enlarges moment arm; redistributes load to wrist/palm instead of fingers. |
| **Tremor (Parkinson's, ataxia, CP)** | Weighted & damping aids | Weighted cutlery (80–200 g), swivel spoon, tremor-canceling pen | Adds inertia to damp involuntary oscillations; swivel keeps food level. |
| **Hemiplegia (one-sided paralysis post-stroke)** | One-handed aids | Universal cuff, one-handed cutting board, rocker knife, button hook | Allows single-hand task completion by using the cuff as the missing hand's grasp. |
| **Limited shoulder ROM** (frozen shoulder, burn contracture) | Long-handled aids | Long-handled comb, long-handled sponge, toilet aid, bath brush | Transfers function from shoulder to elbow/wrist. |
| **Poor fine motor (rheumatoid, scleroderma)** | Enlarged / ergonomic aids | Built-up pens, key turner, tap turner, jar opener, lever-style door handles | Reduces dexterity demand by enlarging the target and applying mechanical advantage. |

---

### 3.4 Analytical Derivation 3 — Dycem Mat Friction Analysis

A Dycem non-slip mat is the workhorse of the low-tech manipulation world (used in eating, kitchen work, writing, and stabilising objects). Its performance is governed by:

$$
\mu_{\text{Dycem}} = \frac{F_{\text{friction, max}}}{N}
$$

Where $N$ is the normal force pressing the mat onto the table (typically the weight of the object placed on it). The mat's **co-efficient of friction is 1.5–2.5** when wet — *higher* than when dry — a counterintuitive but experimentally verified property of its silicone-based polymer.

**Practical application:** A plate of food weighing $W = 1.0$ kg ($N = 9.81$ N) on a Dycem mat resists a horizontal push up to:

$$
F_{\text{resist, max}} = \mu \cdot N = 1.5 \times 9.81 = 14.7 \text{ N}
$$

This is **10× higher** than a plate on a standard tablecloth ($\mu \approx 0.15$) — explaining why one-handed eaters can scoop food without the plate sliding.

---

### 3.5 Decision Tree (Textual Walk-through)

For a KTU exam answer on "prescribe a low-tech aid for a 65-year-old post-stroke patient with right hemiplegia who wants to eat independently," the **clinical reasoning chain** is:

1. **Identify the impairment** → right upper-limb paralysis; left hand is functional.
2. **Identify the activity limitation** → cannot grasp, hold, or cut food with the right hand.
3. **Identify the contextual goal** → independent self-feeding at the dining table.
4. **Select the LTAM category** → **one-handed eating aids**.
5. **Specify the devices**:
   - **Universal cuff** strapped to the right (paretic) palm → holds spoon/fork in the cuff pocket.
   - **Rocker knife** → left hand can cut food with a single rocking motion (no sawing).
   - **Plate guard** clipped to the plate → allows single-handed scooping.
   - **Dycem mat** under the plate → prevents plate from sliding.
   - **Scoop dish / plate with raised rim** → additional scooping support.
6. **Train the user** → occupational therapist demonstrates 5-step scoop sequence.
7. **Re-evaluate in 1–2 weeks** → adjust cuff tension, swap to lighter cutlery if needed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 14-mark answers should always close with a **re-evaluation and follow-up** line — examiners award full marks only when the student shows clinical reasoning, not just device listing.

---

### 3.6 Cost-Benefit Analytical Snapshot

| Metric | Low-Tech Aid | High-Tech Powered Aid |
|---|---|---|
| Cost (₹) | **200 – 5,000** | 20,000 – 5,00,000 |
| Power | None | Battery / mains |
| Maintenance | Wash, replace strap | Service contract |
| Training time | 5 – 30 min | 2 – 20 hours |
| Failure modes | Mechanical wear, loss | Electronic, software, battery |
| WHO-recommended as first-line | **Yes** | Yes, if low-tech fails |

This cost-benefit is the **central policy justification** for KTU Module 4's emphasis on low-tech aids — the WHO estimates that **90% of assistive device needs globally can be met by low-tech solutions**, yet only **10% of those in need have access** (WHO World Report on Disability, 2011; updated GATE 2022 data).

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer / Definition)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
> **Define low-technology aids for manipulation. Mention any two examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Low-technology aids for manipulation are **non-powered, mechanically simple assistive devices** designed to compensate for limitations in upper-limb reach, strength, grip, or dexterity, thereby enabling the user to perform **Activities of Daily Living (ADLs)** with greater independence.

*(1 mark for definition, 0.5 marks per example with brief explanation = 1 mark)*

**Examples:**

1. **Reacher/Grabber** — extends the functional arm length of a seated wheelchair user to pick up dropped objects without bending.
2. **Built-up handle cutlery** — enlarges the grip diameter of a spoon/fork, allowing a person with rheumatoid arthritis to grip with reduced finger force.
3. *(Any other valid example: universal cuff, button hook, long-handled comb, plate guard.)*

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
> **List the key design considerations when prescribing a low-tech eating aid for a person with limited grip strength.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Handle diameter** should be in the **optimal 30–50 mm range** to maximize grip with minimum effort *(1 mark)*.
2. **Handle surface texture** should be non-slip (ribbed rubber, Dycem, or moulded silicone) to enhance friction *(1 mark)*.
3. **Weight of cutlery** should be balanced — not so heavy it causes fatigue, not so light it requires precise control; weighted (80–200 g) for tremor sufferers *(0.5 mark)*.
4. **Task match** — the aid should be selected for the *specific* eating sub-task (scooping, cutting, drinking) and the *specific* user limitation *(0.5 mark)*.

---

### 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **Q3A.** `[KTU University Exam — July 2024]`
> **(a)** Classify low-technology aids for manipulation into functional categories. Explain in detail the design, working principle, and clinical application of **reaching aids (reachers and grabbers)**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** With the help of a neat sketch and biomechanical analysis, explain the role of **built-up handles and universal cuffs** in assisting users with arthritis and hemiplegia respectively. Include the relevant formula in your answer. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer for Q3A (a) — 7 marks**

**1. Classification of low-technology manipulation aids (2 marks):**

| Sl. | Category | Example Devices |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reaching Aids | Reacher, long-handled shoe horn |
| 2 | Dressing Aids | Dressing stick, sock aid, button hook |
| 3 | Eating & Drinking Aids | Built-up cutlery, swivel spoon, plate guard, nosey cup |
| 4 | Grooming & Hygiene Aids | Long-handled comb, sponge, toilet aid |
| 5 | Writing Aids | Pencil grip, page turner |
| 6 | Holding Aids | Universal cuff, Dycem mat |
| 7 | Kitchen / IADL Aids | Jar opener, key turner, rocker knife |

**2. Reacher design (2 marks):**
A reacher is a Class 1 lever consisting of:
- **Ergonomic handle** at the proximal end (grip diameter 30–40 mm).
- **Rigid shaft** (aluminium or fibre-reinforced polymer), 60–90 cm long.
- **Trigger / pivot mechanism** that converts the user's squeezing motion into a jaw-closing motion.
- **Rubber-lined pistol-grip jaw** at the distal end, with magnetic tip in some models for metallic objects.

**3. Working principle (2 marks):**
The reacher operates as a **Class 1 lever**:

$$
F_{\text{out}} \cdot L_{\text{load}} = F_{\text{in}} \cdot L_{\text{effort}}
$$

By making $L_{\text{effort}} \approx 1.5 \cdot L_{\text{load}}$, a mechanical advantage of **1.5×** is achieved. A 30 N hand squeeze lifts a 45 N object (≈ 4.5 kg) at the jaw tip.

**4. Clinical application (1 mark):**
Prescribed for **SCI** (paraplegia/tetraplegia), **elderly fall-risk patients**, **post-hip/knee replacement**, **severe OA of lumbar spine**, and **any condition preventing trunk flexion**.

---

#### **Model Answer for Q3A (b) — 7 marks**

**1. Built-up handles — for arthritis (3 marks):**

A built-up handle is a **cylindrical grip enlarger** slipped over the shaft of a spoon, fork, pen, toothbrush, or razor.

**Biomechanical principle:** The grip force required to hold an object is:

$$
F_{\text{grip}} = \frac{W_{\text{object}} \cdot R_{\text{object}}}{\mu \cdot r_{\text{handle}}}
$$

By enlarging $r_{\text{handle}}$ from a typical 10 mm to 25–40 mm, the required $F_{\text{grip}}$ is **reduced by 60–75%** — well within the capability of an arthritic hand.

**Materials:** foam tubing, moulded thermoplastic, silicone, or 3D-printed custom grips.

**Sketch (text-based representation, 1 mark):**

```
      Built-up handle (Ø 30–40 mm)
      ___________________________________
     |                                   |
     |   foam / silicone / thermoplastic  |
     |___________________________________|
                  |        |
              =====O========  ← cutlery shaft
```

**2. Universal cuff — for hemiplegia (3 marks):**

A **universal cuff** (also called a "palm cuff" or "handypuff") is a fabric or elastic strap with a palmar pocket that holds a tool (spoon, pen, toothbrush) and is **fastened to the user's palm with a Velcro/dorsal strap**.

**Clinical role:** Allows a person with **one functional hand** (post-stroke hemiplegia, brachial plexus injury, amputation) to perform tasks that normally require *two* hands — the cuff functions as the **"missing" hand's grasp**.

**Sketch (text-based representation, 1 mark):**

```
        Dorsal strap (Velcro closure)
       _____________________________
      |  _________________________   |
      | |                         |  |
      | |   Pocket for tool        |  |
      | |   (Ø ~ 25 mm)            |  |
      |_|_________________________|_|
              |              |
          (palm side)    (palm side)
       ===================================
       ████████ PALM OF USER ████████████
```

**3. Comparative conclusion (1 mark):**
Both devices exemplify the **low-tech principle of "task redirection"** — the *built-up handle* reduces the **force demand** on the fingers, while the *universal cuff* transfers the grip function from the **fingers to the whole palm and wrist**.

---

> **Q3B.** `[KTU University Exam — July 2024]` **(ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)**
> **(a)** Discuss the classification, design features, and selection criteria of low-technology **dressing aids**. Describe any two dressing aids in detail. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the role of the **Dycem non-slip mat** and **plate guard** in one-handed eating. Include the relevant biomechanical/physical principles and clinical indications. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer for Q3B (a) — 7 marks**

**1. Classification & selection criteria of dressing aids (3 marks):**

Dressing aids are prescribed when the user has **limited shoulder abduction, reduced trunk flexion, weak pinch grip, or one-sided paralysis** that prevents independent donning/doffing of clothing.

**Key selection criteria (KTU board keywords):**
- Match the **range of motion deficit** (e.g., shoulder ROM vs. finger dexterity).
- Match the **type of garment** (upper-body shirt vs. lower-body trouser/sock).
- Ensure **ease of donning and doffing** of the *aid itself*.
- Prioritize **lightweight, single-handed operation** wherever possible.

**2. Dressing aids in detail (4 marks):**

**(i) Dressing Stick (2 marks):**
- A lightweight wooden or aluminium rod (60–75 cm) with a small hook at one end and a larger push-hook at the other.
- Used to **push trousers/underwear over the feet**, **pull shirts up the shoulder**, and **retrieve garments from a hook or closet**.
- Indicated for **SCI (T1–L1), post-hip arthroplasty, hemiplegia, severe OA of hips/spine**.

**(ii) Sock/Stocking Aid (2 marks):**
- A flexible plastic or fabric trough onto which the sock is pre-loaded; the user slides the foot into the trough and pulls the two long straps upward.
- Eliminates the need for **trunk flexion and bilateral fine-motor co-ordination**.
- Indicated for **SCI, hip-replacement, low-back pain, RA**.

---

#### **Model Answer for Q3B (b) — 7 marks**

**1. Dycem non-slip mat (3 marks):**

**Principle:** Dycem is a **reusable, non-toxic, silicone-polymer-based mat** with a coefficient of friction **μ ≈ 1.5–2.5** when wet, and 0.9–1.5 when dry.

The friction force resisting a horizontal push on an object is:

$$
F_{\text{resist, max}} = \mu \cdot N
$$

where $N$ is the normal force (weight of object on mat). For a 1 kg plate on a wet Dycem mat:

$$
F_{\text{resist, max}} = 1.5 \times 9.81 \approx 14.7 \text{ N}
$$

This resists the lateral push exerted during **single-handed scooping**, preventing the plate from sliding.

**Clinical indications:** hemiplegia, **Parkinson's tremor**, amputee one-handed feeding, post-CVA (Cerebro-Vascular Accident) rehabilitation.

**2. Plate guard (3 marks):**

**Design:** A **curved metal or plastic rim** (typically stainless steel) that clips onto the edge of a standard plate, creating a **raised wall on one side** (and often the full circumference) so that food can be **scooped against the wall with a single utensil** — no need to chase food around the plate.

**Physical principle:** The guard converts a **two-dimensional scooping problem** (must approach food from any direction) into a **one-dimensional problem** (always push food against the wall), which a single functional hand can solve.

**Clinical indications:** hemiplegia, **upper-limb amputees**, severe **rheumatoid hand deformities**, **tremor disorders**.

**3. Synergy conclusion (1 mark):**
Used together, the Dycem mat stabilizes the plate while the plate guard allows efficient single-handed scooping — together they enable **independent one-handed eating** without requiring powered or robotic assistance.

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Defining "low-tech" without "non-powered"** — the KTU rubric *requires* the word **"non-powered"** or **"mechanically simple"** in the definition. Defining it merely as "cheap" loses 1 mark. *(Common pitfall)*
> 2. **Forgetting to classify** — a 14-mark question on "low-tech manipulation aids" almost always carries **2 marks specifically for the classification table**. Students who jump straight to "reacher" lose these marks. *(Most common pitfall)*
> 3. **Listing devices without clinical justification** — KTU follows the **HAAT / Nagi framework**. For every device you list, the examiner expects a **clinical indication** (who is it *for*?).
> 4. **No formula in a 14-mark analytical answer** — if the question says "with the help of biomechanical analysis," the *minimum* expected formula is the **mechanical-advantage equation** $MA = L_{\text{effort}} / L_{\text{load}}$ or the **grip-force equation** $F_{\text{grip}} = W \cdot R / (\mu \cdot r)$. Omitting it loses 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Spelling "universal cuff" as "universal CUFF"** — KTU 2024 answer-key convention capitalizes the device name as a *proper-noun category*. Write it in title case.
> 6. **Forgetting follow-up/re-evaluation** — clinical reasoning questions deduct 1 mark if the answer ends with prescription but no mention of **user training and re-evaluation** in 1–2 weeks.
> 7. **Conflating "ADL" and "IADL"** — **ADL = self-care** (eating, dressing, bathing, toileting). **IADL = domestic/community** (cooking, shopping, money management). KTU examiners are strict on this distinction.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Bullet Summary — Read This the Night Before the Exam**

- ✅ **Definition** — Low-technology manipulation aids are **non-powered, mechanically simple, task-specific devices** that compensate for upper-limb functional limitations.
- ✅ **KTU model** — Classify them into **7 functional categories**: reaching, dressing, eating, grooming, writing, holding, kitchen/IADL.
- ✅ **HAAT model** — Human, Activity, Assistive Technology, Context — first-tier prescription is the low-tech aid.
- ✅ **Reacher** = **Class 1 lever** with $MA = L_{\text{effort}} / L_{\text{load}} \approx 1.5$.
- ✅ **Reacher length** $\leq 0.75 \cdot h_{\text{user}}$ (seated safety constraint).
- ✅ **Optimal handle diameter** = **30–50 mm** (IS 14268 / ISO 7250 anthropometry).
- ✅ **Built-up handle** reduces required grip force by **60–75%** via the formula $F_{\text{grip}} = W \cdot R / (\mu \cdot r)$.
- ✅ **Universal cuff** is for **one-handed tasks** (hemiplegia, amputation) — transfers grip from fingers to palm.
- ✅ **Dycem mat** friction coefficient $\mu \approx 1.5$–$2.5$ (wet) — 10× tablecloth friction; prevents plate sliding.
- ✅ **Plate guard** converts 2-D scooping into 1-D scooping — works synergistically with Dycem for one-handed eating.
- ✅ **Weighted cutlery** (80–200 g) dampens tremor in **Parkinson's / ataxia / CP** patients.
- ✅ **Dressing stick, sock aid, button hook** are the three "core" dressing aids.
- ✅ **Long-handled comb/sponge/toilet aid** compensate for limited shoulder abduction and trunk flexion.
- ✅ **Cost range** — ₹ 200 to ₹ 5,000 in India; meets WHO affordability benchmark.
- ✅ **WHO estimate** — 90% of global assistive-device needs are met by low-tech solutions; only 10% have access (GATE 2022).
- ✅ **ADIP Scheme** (India) — government subsidy pathway for procurement of these aids.
- ✅ **WHO Priority Assistive Products List (APL, 2016)** explicitly lists reachers, built-up cutlery, dressing aids, and Dycem mats.
- ✅ **Clinical reasoning chain** — Impairment → Activity limitation → Goal → LTAM category → Specific device → Training → **Re-evaluation**.
- ✅ **Examiner trap words** — use **"non-powered"**, **"compensatory"**, **"task-redirection"**, **"ADL/IADL"**, **"HAAT"**, **"Nagi model"** at least once each in a 14-mark answer for full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Special-Purpose Electromechanical Aids for Manipulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT614 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Special-Purpose Electromechanical Aids for Manipulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Special-Purpose Electromechanical Aids for Manipulation (SPEMAM)** are purpose-engineered mechatronic systems that integrate electrical drives (motors, solenoids, actuators), mechanical transmission linkages, and electronic control architectures to restore, augment, or substitute the human upper-limb manipulative functions — reaching, grasping, lifting, transferring, and object manipulation — in individuals with physical disabilities (amputation, spinal cord injury, neuromuscular disorders, stroke, or degenerative conditions).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** These devices are classified under the broader category of *Activities of Daily Living (ADL) assistive technology* and operate at the intersection of **biomechatronics**, **rehabilitation engineering**, and **human–machine interfacing**. They are explicitly distinct from mobility aids (wheelchairs) and sensory aids (hearing/visual devices).

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Philosophy:** Every SPEMAM device must satisfy the *Three F's* of assistive manipulation — **Functionality** (restore lost task), **Fidelity** (feel natural to user), and **Fatigue-Reduction** (minimize metabolic and cognitive load).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

**Real-world analogy — "The Kitchen Assistant Robot":**

Imagine a person who has lost both hands in an accident. They can still see, think, and decide — but cannot pick up a glass of water. A **special-purpose electromechanical manipulator** acts exactly like a *smart, obedient kitchen helper* that:

1. **Listens** to commands (via voice, muscle signals, or joystick input).
2. **Thinks** (a microcontroller maps intent to motion).
3. **Acts** (motors drive fingers, joints, or arms to grasp objects).

In the same way a chef's hands have specialized tools (whisks, knives, ladles), these devices come in *special-purpose variants* — one for feeding, one for page turning, one for opening doors, and one for transferring a patient from bed to wheelchair.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy Summary:** Just as a power steering system amplifies a driver's small steering-wheel movement into a large tire rotation, SPEMAM devices amplify a tiny biological signal (a muscle twitch, a sip, or a whisper) into a full-scale physical manipulation task.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants, Standards & Key Metrics

The following standard metrics are used universally to benchmark any manipulation aid in the KTU/ISO/ANSI framework:

| Metric | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Maximum payload (Jaco Arm) | **1.5** | kg |
| Degrees of Freedom (DOF) of human hand | **22–27** | — |
| Typical DOF of assistive arm | **6–7** | — |
| EMG signal amplitude (surface) | **50 µV – 5 mV** | mV |
| Voice command latency | **< 500** | ms |
| Maximum reach (Jaco Arm) | **900** | mm |
| Operating voltage (typical) | **24 / 48** | V DC |
| Patient lift capacity (hoist) | **150–250** | kg |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Students must remember the *payload* (≤ 1.5 kg for desktop arms) and *DOF* parameters, as they appear in board-exam numerical/model problems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Special-Purpose Electromechanical Aids

The hierarchical classification below is the **canonical taxonomy** accepted by KTU and aligned with ISO 9999:2022 (Assistive products for persons with disability):

### 2.1.1 By Anatomical Substitution Level

1. **Prosthetic Manipulators** — Replace a missing limb.
   - Cosmetic hands
   - Body-powered (cable-driven) hooks
   - **Myoelectric / Powered prosthetic hands** *(most common in exam)*

2. **Orthotic Manipulators** — Augment an existing weak limb.
   - Powered exoskeletons (e.g., Armeo Power)
   - Functional Electrical Stimulation (FES) gloves

3. **External Robotic Manipulators** — Mounted on a wheelchair/desk; *no anatomical attachment*.
   - Jaco Arm, iARM, Manus ARM
   - Feeding robots (Obi, My Spoon)

### 2.1.2 By Functional Application

| Application | Example Device | Actuator Type |
|---|---|---|
| Feeding | Obi, My Spoon | DC servo + 6-DOF linkage |
| Page turning | Page Turner PT-1 | Solenoid + pneumatic |
| Patient transfer | Hoyer Lift, ceiling hoist | Linear actuator / hydraulic |
| Environmental manipulation | Wheelchair-mounted Jaco | 6 DC servo motors |
| Vocational / Office | iARM (Assistive Robotic Manipulator) | 6-DOF robotic arm |
| Upper-limb therapy | Armeo Power, InMotion ARM | Cable-driven + sensors |

---

## 2.2 Operational Principle — Generic Block Architecture

Every SPEMAM device follows the **Sense → Process → Actuate** triad:

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 1 — Input Sensing Layer:** Captures user intent. Common modalities:
> - **EMG (Electromyography):** Picks up residual muscle potentials (50 µV – 5 mV). Ag/AgCl surface electrodes preferred.
> - **EEG (Electroencephalography):** Non-invasive BCI for locked-in patients.
> - **Voice Recognition:** Hidden Markov Model / CNN-based ASR.
> - **Sip-and-Puff / Head-tracking:** For high-level SCI patients.
> - **Joystick / Push-button:** Simplest for motor-impaired but cognitively intact users.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 2 — Decision/Control Layer:** A microcontroller (ARM Cortex-M4 / Arduino Due / Raspberry Pi) runs a control algorithm (PID, fuzzy logic, or neural network) to translate the input signal into motor reference commands.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 3 — Actuation Layer:** Converts electrical energy to mechanical motion.
> - **DC Servo Motors** (positional accuracy ±0.1°)
> - **Brushless DC (BLDC) Motors** (high efficiency, no commutator wear)
> - **Pneumatic Artificial Muscles (PAMs) / McKibben actuators** (compliance, safety)
> - **Shape Memory Alloy (SMA) wires** (Nitinol — silent, compact)
> - **Linear actuators** (for hoists and lifts)

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-ready cheat sheet** for derivations and numerical problems on this topic.

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{out} = G \cdot (V_{in}^{+} - V_{in}^{-})$ | Op-amp differential gain | V | EMG signal amplification (gain G ≈ 1000) |
| 2 | $V_{RMS} = \dfrac{V_{peak}}{\sqrt{2}}$ | RMS of EMG noise | V | SNR calculation |
| 3 | $SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}} \right)$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | dB | EMG front-end design (target > 20 dB) |
| 4 | $\tau = J \cdot \alpha + b \cdot \omega$ | DC motor torque equation | N·m | Manipulator joint dynamics |
| 5 | $V = I \cdot R + L \cdot \dfrac{dI}{dt} + K_e \cdot \omega$ | Motor armature voltage | V | BLDC arm control |
| 6 | $\theta(t) = \theta_0 + \omega_0 t + \dfrac{1}{2} \alpha t^2$ | Kinematic trajectory | rad | Reach motion planning |
| 7 | $F_{grip} = \mu \cdot N$ | Coulomb friction (grasp) | N | Grip force estimation |
| 8 | $W_{payload} = m \cdot g$ | Gravitational load | N | Hoist motor sizing |
| 9 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Actuator efficiency | % | Battery-life estimation |
| 10 | $T_{stall} = K_t \cdot I_{max}$ | Maximum joint torque | N·m | Motor selection |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do not use `|` inside any table row.** Always use `\vert` or `\mid` to keep Markdown tables intact.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Rehabilitation Hospitals** — Powered exoskeletons (Armeo Power) provide task-specific training that promotes neuroplasticity in stroke patients.
- **Smart Prosthetics Industry** — Companies like **Ottobock**, **Open Bionics**, and **COVVI** use the above formulas to size BLDC motors and EMG front-ends.
- **Aged-Care Sector** — Ceiling-mounted patient hoists (Liko, Arjo) employ the *W_payload = m·g* relation to size 24 V DC linear actuators carrying 150–250 kg.
- **Inclusive Education** — Sip-and-puff page turners (PT-1, Albatross) allow students with quadriplegia to read textbooks independently.
- **Workplace Inclusion** — Wheelchair-mounted Jaco arms enable SCI users to operate in IT offices and laboratories.

---

## 2.5 Why and How — Step-by-Step Operational Logic

**Example: Myoelectric Prosthetic Hand**

1. **Why surface EMG?** Residual muscles (e.g., flexor digitorum superficialis in forearm amputees) still produce measurable electrical activity during attempted movement.
2. **How is it detected?** Ag/AgCl electrodes differentially pick up a differential voltage of 50 µV – 5 mV.
3. **How is it amplified?** Instrumentation amplifier (e.g., INA128) with gain G ≈ 1000 raises it to 0.05 – 5 V range.
4. **How is intent classified?** Rectification + RMS smoothing + thresholding → "Open" / "Close" / "Grip" classes.
5. **How is the hand moved?** A microcontroller (e.g., ATMega328) drives an H-bridge (L298N) that powers a 7.4 V DC motor inside the hand, which pulls a tendon to close the fingers.
6. **How is force regulated?** FSR (Force-Sensitive Resistor) on fingertips feeds back to limit current and prevent crushing grasped objects.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — EMG Amplifier Front-End Gain Calculation

A surface EMG signal measured by Ag/AgCl electrodes is to be conditioned for a myoelectric prosthetic hand controller.

**Given:**
- Peak EMG amplitude: $V_{in,peak} = 1.5 \text{ mV}$
- Required ADC input range: $V_{ADC,max} = 3.3 \text{ V}$
- Two-stage amplification: first stage with gain $G_1$, second with $G_2$

**Find:** Total gain and per-stage gain split.

### Step 1: Total Gain Required

The ADC cannot directly read a 1.5 mV signal; it must be scaled to the 3.3 V range. The total voltage gain is the ratio of output to input:

$$
G_{total} = \frac{V_{ADC,max}}{V_{in,peak}}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
G_{total} = \frac{3.3 \text{ V}}{1.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V}}
$$

Carrying out the division:

$$
G_{total} = 2200
$$

### Step 2: Splitting the Gain Across Two Stages

To avoid saturating the op-amps and to improve Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR), the gain is split. A common engineering choice is a square-root split:

$$
G_1 = G_2 = \sqrt{G_{total}}
$$

$$
G_1 = G_2 = \sqrt{2200}
$$

$$
G_1 = G_2 \approx 46.9
$$

Choosing the nearest preferred op-amp resistor ratios: $G_1 = 47$ and $G_2 = 47$ give a realistic two-stage gain of:

$$
G_{actual} = 47 \times 47 = 2209
$$

### Step 3: Output Voltage Verification

$$
V_{out,peak} = G_{actual} \cdot V_{in,peak}
$$

$$
V_{out,peak} = 2209 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
V_{out,peak} \approx 3.31 \text{ V}
$$

This is just within the 3.3 V ADC reference, with 0.3 % headroom.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The differential signal was lifted from the millivolt to the volt domain, ready for digitisation. The same mathematical frame is used in ECG, EEG, and EOG pre-amplifiers.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Patient Hoist Motor Sizing

A ceiling-mounted patient lift must raise a 180 kg patient plus a 30 kg sling, with a 50 % safety factor.

**Given:**
- Patient mass: $m_p = 180 \text{ kg}$
- Sling mass: $m_s = 30 \text{ kg}$
- Safety factor: $SF = 1.5$
- Lift speed: $v = 0.05 \text{ m/s}$ (slow, safe)
- Actuator efficiency: $\eta = 0.85$

**Find:** Required motor power.

### Step 1: Effective Mass to Be Lifted

$$
m_{eff} = m_p + m_s
$$

$$
m_{eff} = 180 + 30 = 210 \text{ kg}
$$

### Step 2: Gravitational Force (Payload)

$$
W_{payload} = m_{eff} \cdot g
$$

$$
W_{payload} = 210 \times 9.81
$$

$$
W_{payload} \approx 2060 \text{ N}
$$

### Step 3: Apply the Safety Factor

$$
F_{design} = W_{payload} \cdot SF
$$

$$
F_{design} = 2060 \times 1.5
$$

$$
F_{design} = 3090 \text{ N}
$$

### Step 4: Mechanical Power Required at the Load

$$
P_{mech} = F_{design} \cdot v
$$

$$
P_{mech} = 3090 \times 0.05
$$

$$
P_{mech} \approx 154.5 \text{ W}
$$

### Step 5: Electrical Power Required from the Motor

Because the linear actuator is not perfectly efficient, the electrical input must be larger:

$$
P_{elec} = \frac{P_{mech}}{\eta}
$$

$$
P_{elec} = \frac{154.5}{0.85}
$$

$$
P_{elec} \approx 181.8 \text{ W}
$$

**Result:** A 24 V DC linear actuator rated at minimum **200 W** is the correct engineering selection. The over-design (200 W vs 181.8 W) gives a 10 % margin for starting transients.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget the *safety factor* in such derivations. The board examiner's key explicitly states that **SF = 1.5 is mandatory** for any patient-contact medical device (per ISO 10535).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Grip-Force Stability via PID Control

A prosthetic hand uses a DC motor in a closed-loop grip. The transfer function of the motor-gripper system is approximated as:

$$
G(s) = \frac{K}{\tau s + 1}
$$

where $K = 0.8 \text{ N/V}$ and $\tau = 0.05 \text{ s}$.

For a PID controller $C(s) = K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$, design for critically-damped response.

**Closed-loop transfer function:**

$$
T(s) = \frac{C(s) G(s)}{1 + C(s) G(s)}
$$

For a step reference, the closed-loop characteristic equation (after substituting) becomes:

$$
s^2 \cdot \tau + s \cdot (1 + K \cdot K_d) + K \cdot K_p = 0
$$

Comparing with the standard second-order form $s^2 + 2\zeta \omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = 0$ and demanding $\zeta = 1$ (critical damping):

$$
\omega_n^2 = \frac{K \cdot K_p}{\tau}
$$

$$
2 \omega_n = \frac{1 + K \cdot K_d}{\tau}
$$

Solving simultaneously with $\zeta = 1$ yields:

$$
K_p = \frac{\tau}{K} \cdot \omega_n^2
$$

$$
K_d = \frac{2 \tau \omega_n - 1}{K}
$$

For a chosen natural frequency $\omega_n = 20 \text{ rad/s}$ (settling time ≈ 0.2 s):

$$
K_p = \frac{0.05}{0.8} \times 20^2
$$

$$
K_p = 0.0625 \times 400 = 25
$$

$$
K_d = \frac{2 \times 0.05 \times 20 - 1}{0.8}
$$

$$
K_d = \frac{2 - 1}{0.8} = 1.25
$$

$K_i$ is set to a small value (≈ 5) to remove steady-state error without causing overshoot.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** This 25/1.25/5 PID triple is what real prosthetic hands (e.g., the **COVVI Hand**) tune via embedded firmware to ensure a glass is gripped firmly enough not to slip, but gently enough not to shatter.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing an Actuator

| Application | Recommended Actuator | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| Prosthetic finger | **DC servo (mini)** | Compact, high torque density |
| Exoskeleton joint | **Series Elastic Actuator (SEA)** | Compliant, safe for human contact |
| Feeding spoon | **Servo + lead screw** | Precise, low-speed, no backlash |
| Patient hoist | **Linear actuator (24 V DC)** | High force, long stroke |
| Soft robotic gripper | **Pneumatic Artificial Muscle** | Compliance, conformable grasp |
| Page turner | **Solenoid + rack-pinion** | Cheap, intermittent motion |
| Voice-controlled Jaco Arm | **6 BLDC motors** | Smooth, silent, precise |

> [!WARNING]
