
### 4.3 Wheelchair Hand-Rim Mechanical Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H[Hand force F h tangent to rim] --> T[Torque tau = F h times r rim]
    T --> W[Wheel of radius r wheel]
    W --> G[Ground reaction F g]
    G --> R[Rolling resistance opposing motion]
    R --> T

    H -.- M[Mechanical advantage MA = r rim over r wheel]
    G -.- M
    M --> V[Velocity ratio VR = r wheel over r rim]
```

### 4.4 Crutch Three-Point Gait Force Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    COM[Body center of mass weight W]:::w --> D[Distribution]
    D --> C1[Crutch 1 reaction F C]:::cr
    D --> C2[Crutch 2 reaction F C]:::cr
    D --> LEG[Unaffected leg F L equals 0 in strict NWB]:::cr
    C1 --> T1[Cane tip ground]
    C2 --> T2[Cane tip ground]

    classDef w fill:#8d99ae,stroke:#000
    classDef cr fill:#ef233c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Q1.** Define an *Electronic Travel Aid (ETA)*. List any two types of ETAs used by persons with visual impairment.

**Model Answer:**
An Electronic Travel Aid is a device that uses active sensing (ultrasonic, laser, infrared, or vision-based) to detect obstacles in the user's path and conveys the information through auditory or tactile feedback, thereby extending the user's perceptual range beyond the reach of the long cane.
**Types:** (i) Ultrasonic ETA (e.g., Sonicguide, Miniguide), (ii) Laser cane, (iii) Infrared-based (e.g., Mowat sensor), (iv) Vision-based wearable (e.g., OrCam).

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** Differentiate between *axillary* and *forearm (Lofstrand) crutches* in two points.

**Model Answer:**

| S. No. | Axillary Crutch | Forearm (Lofstrand) Crutch |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Padded top fits under the axilla; bears weight on the ribcage and hands. | Has a forearm cuff and handgrip; weight borne by hand and forearm. |
| 2 | Used for short-term, non-weight-bearing conditions. | Used for long-term / permanent locomotor disability. |
| 3 | Length rule: $L = 0.77 \, H$. | Length rule: handgrip at greater trochanter level. |
| 4 | Risk of axillary nerve damage with prolonged use. | Safer for long-term use. |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Wheelchairs

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified for 14 marks]**

**(a)** Classify wheelchairs based on drive and control mechanisms. Explain with neat sketches the working of a *manual self-propelled* wheelchair and a *powered wheelchair controlled by joystick*. (7 marks)

**(b)** Derive the **mechanical advantage of a hand-rim driven wheelchair wheel**, and show that for a 30 cm wheel and a 15 cm hand-rim, the velocity ratio is **2 : 1**. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification** (2 marks)

| Criterion | Category |
|---|---|
| Drive | Manual, Powered, Power-assist |
| Control | Hand-rim, Joystick, Sip-and-puff, Head-array, Chin-control |
| Use | Indoor, Outdoor, Sport, Standing |

**Working of Manual Self-Propelled Wheelchair (3 marks)**
The user grips the **hand-rim** (a smaller-diameter ring concentric with the rear wheel) and pushes forward in an arc. Friction between the hand and the rim generates a tangential force $F_h$, which produces a torque $\tau = F_h \cdot r_{rim}$ on the rear axle. This torque, transmitted via the axle to the larger wheel (radius $r_w$), creates a ground reaction $F_g = \tau / r_w$ at the contact patch, propelling the chair. A front **caster** wheel pivots freely for steering.

**Working of Powered Wheelchair with Joystick (2 marks)**
A DC or BLDC motor (typically 24 V, 200–450 W per wheel) is mounted on each rear wheel hub or coupled via gearboxes. The user displaces a **joystick** (Hall-effect or potentiometer based) producing voltage signals proportional to the desired **vector velocity** ($v_x$, $v_y$). The controller performs differential drive kinematics to compute individual wheel angular velocities and drives each motor through PWM-controlled H-bridge MOSFET drivers. Feedback is from encoders; safety is enforced by a watchdog and current limit.

---

**(b) Derivation of Mechanical Advantage (7 marks)**

**Step 1:** Consider the rear wheel of radius $r_w$ with a concentric hand-rim of radius $r_{rim}$, where $r_{rim} < r_w$.

**Step 2:** Apply a tangential force $F_h$ on the hand-rim. The torque on the axle is:

$$
\tau = F_h \cdot r_{rim}
$$

**Step 3:** This torque acts at the larger wheel radius, producing a tangential ground reaction $F_g$:

$$
\tau = F_g \cdot r_w
$$

**Step 4:** Equating and solving for $F_g$:

$$
F_g = F_h \cdot \frac{r_{rim}}{r_w}
$$

**Step 5:** The **Ideal Mechanical Advantage (IMA)** is defined as Load / Effort:

$$
\mathrm{IMA} = \frac{F_g}{F_h} = \frac{r_{rim}}{r_w}
$$

**Step 6:** The **Velocity Ratio (VR)** is the inverse (since work is conserved for an ideal machine):

$$
\mathrm{VR} = \frac{r_w}{r_{rim}}
$$

**Step 7:** Substituting $r_w = 30 \text{ cm}$ and $r_{rim} = 15 \text{ cm}$:

$$
\mathrm{VR} = \frac{30}{15} = 2
$$

Hence, the velocity ratio is **2 : 1** — the wheelchair moves 2 cm for every 1 cm of arc traced by the user's hand.

> [!NOTE]
> Since $r_{rim} < r_w$, the IMA $< 1$ (force-reduction, speed-amplification design). This is intentional in wheelchair design: rolling resistance is low, so trading force for speed yields a more ergonomic push stroke.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Smart Canes & ETAs (Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified for 14 marks]**

**(a)** With the help of a functional block diagram, describe the **working of an ultrasonic smart cane** for visually impaired users. List the major subsystems and their functions. (7 marks)

**(b)** Derive the **range equation** used in the smart cane, including temperature compensation of the speed of sound. A cane measures a round-trip time of 6.5 ms at 25 °C. Calculate the distance to the obstacle. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Smart Cane Working (7 marks)**

| Subsystem | Function |
|---|---|
| Power (Li-ion 3.7 V + regulator 3.3 V) | Energises the electronics. |
| Ultrasonic transducers (×2 to 3) | Emit and receive 40 kHz bursts to detect obstacles at chest, knee, and ground heights. |
| Microcontroller (e.g., STM32, ATmega328) | Computes range via TOF, classifies obstacle distance, and triggers alerts. |
| Vibration motors (handle + tip) | Provide non-auditory, discreet warning (intensity ∝ proximity). |
| Piezo buzzer / speaker | Provides audio tone or voice cue ("obstacle at 1 m"). |
| Bluetooth module (HC-05 / BLE) | Pairs with smartphone GPS app for wayfinding. |
| Push button | User input — power on/off, mode select. |

**Working principle (3 marks):** The ultrasonic transducers periodically emit pulses. The reflected echoes are received and the round-trip TOF is measured. The MCU computes $R = (c \cdot t)/2$ for each sensor. If $R < R_{threshold}$ (typically 1.5 m front, 0.6 m side), a corresponding vibration pattern or audio tone is activated. Bluetooth streaming of wayfinding data (e.g., turn-by-turn) is overlaid.

---

**(b) Derivation & Numerical Problem (7 marks)**

**Step 1:** Speed of sound in air, temperature-corrected (1 mark):

$$
c(T) = 331 + 0.6 \, T \quad [\text{m/s}]
$$

**Step 2:** At $T = 25^\circ C$:

$$
c(25) = 331 + 0.6 \cdot 25 = 331 + 15 = 346 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 3:** One-way range from round-trip time (2 marks):

$$
R = \frac{c \cdot t_{TOF}}{2}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute values, $t_{TOF} = 6.5 \text{ ms} = 6.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}$:

$$
R = \frac{346 \cdot 6.5 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = \frac{2.249}{2} = 1.1245 \text{ m}
$$

**Result:** The obstacle is at approximately **1.12 m** from the cane tip.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write $R = c \cdot t$ (forgetting the factor of 1/2). The acoustic wave travels **to the obstacle and back**, so the **round-trip distance is $c \cdot t$**, and the **one-way distance is half** of that.

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top reasons students lose marks in this module:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 1/2** in the ultrasonic range equation.
> 2. **Assuming all body weight** is borne by one crutch — the 3-point gait **shares** weight between the two crutches and the unaffected leg.
> 3. **Confusing MA and VR** in the wheelchair — they are reciprocals, not the same.
> 4. **Omitting temperature compensation** of the speed of sound in smart cane derivations.
> 5. Writing "white cane is an electronic device" — it is **passive / mechanical**, not electronic.
> 6. Not drawing the **Mermaid / block diagram** when the question says "with a neat sketch" — examiners expect at least a block diagram.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Mobility aids** compensate, augment, or replace the locomotor function; classified under **ISO 9999 Class 12**.
- **Wheelchairs**: manual vs. powered; the **hand-rim MA** is always $< 1$, but VR is $> 1$ for ergonomic propulsion.
- **Crutches**: axillary (short-term), forearm (long-term). Use $L = 0.77 H$ for axillary length.
- **Three-point gait**: each crutch supports $F_{ax} = W / (2 \cos \theta)$.
- **Walkers**: provide a **wide polygon of support**; rollators add wheels + brakes.
- **Prostheses vs. Orthoses**: prostheses **replace** a missing limb; orthoses **support** an existing limb.
- **White cane**: passive, **tactile**; the cane tip rolls along the ground — knee and head-height obstacles are **missed**.
- **Smart canes / ETAs**: use **ultrasonic / laser / IR** active sensing + **audio / haptic** feedback.
- **Ultrasonic range formula**: $R = (c \cdot t) / 2$ with $c = 331 + 0.6 T$.
- **Wayfinding**: GPS + smartphone apps (BlindSquare, Lazarillo) + Bluetooth beacons extend the orientation system to outdoor navigation.
- **Standard thresholds to memorize**: cane warning at 1.5 m, smart cane range 0.2 to 4 m, wheelchair stability angles 9° forward / 7° rear (RESNA).
- **Diagram etiquette**: always include a labelled block diagram for any device-based question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Assessment - Visual Function, Orientation and Mobility
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT614 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Assessment - Visual Function, Orientation and Mobility

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Visual Function Assessment

**Visual Function Assessment** is a systematic, multi-parametric clinical and functional evaluation of the human visual system used to quantify the residual visual capacity of an individual. It establishes the degree of impairment, identifies functional limitations, and guides the prescription of low-vision or assistive devices. The KTU 2024 syllabus treats this as the first step in the rehabilitation cascade for the visually impaired.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Standard):**
> Visual function assessment is the structured measurement of how well a person uses the available vision in everyday tasks. It combines **optical performance** (acuity, field, contrast) with **functional performance** (reading, navigation, face recognition).

The principal parameters measured are:

| Parameter | Clinical Tool | Functional Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Visual Acuity (VA)** | Snellen / LogMAR chart | Smallest resolvable detail |
| **Visual Field (VF)** | Perimeter / Confrontation | Spatial extent of vision |
| **Contrast Sensitivity (CS)** | Pelli-Robson, VCTS | Ability to detect low-contrast objects |
| **Color Vision** | Ishihara, Farnsworth D-15 | Hue discrimination ability |
| **Depth Perception** | Stereoacuity tests | 3D spatial judgment |
| **Refraction** | Retinoscope, Autorefractor | Optical correction needed |
| **Glare Recovery** | Brightness Acuity Tester | Light adaptation ability |

> [!NOTE]
> **WHO Classification of Visual Impairment (ICD-11 Standard, KTU Reference):**
> - **Normal vision:** Presenting VA $\geq 6/6$ (or $\geq 20/20$)
> - **Mild VI:** VA worse than $6/12$ to $6/18$
> - **Moderate VI:** VA worse than $6/18$ to $6/60$
> - **Severe VI:** VA worse than $6/60$ to $3/60$
> - **Blindness:** VA worse than $3/60$ in the better eye
> - **Total blindness:** No light perception (NLP)

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine your eye is a **camera**: visual function assessment is the technical diagnostic that tells the engineer (clinician) which lens components (cornea, lens, retina, optic nerve) are malfunctioning, by how much, and what image quality (functional vision) the camera is still capable of producing.

## 1.2 Orientation and Mobility (O&M)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Standard):**
> **Orientation** is the ability to know one's position in relation to surrounding environmental landmarks and to use that knowledge to plan and follow a route. **Mobility** is the physical ability to move safely, efficiently, and gracefully through the environment using residual senses or assistive devices.

**O&M Training** is a structured habilitation/rehabilitation discipline developed originally for war-blinded veterans, formalized by **Dr. Richard Hoover** (1946, Valley Forge Army Hospital) and later standardized by the **Academy for Certification of Vision Rehabilitation & Education Professionals (ACVREP)**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of O&M as the **"blind person's GPS + driving lessons combined."** Orientation is the "where am I on the map?" part, and mobility is the "how do I steer the wheelchair (or in this case, the body) safely along this path?" part. A person can have excellent orientation but poor mobility (afraid to walk) or vice versa.

### Core O&M Sub-Components

1. **Concept Development** – understanding spatial concepts (left/right, near/far, straight/curved).
2. **Sensory Training** – refining use of hearing, touch, smell, kinesthesia, and residual vision.
3. **Motor Skills** – posture, gait, balance, protective techniques.
4. **Orientation Skills** – using landmarks, compass directions, mental mapping.
5. **Mobility Aids Proficiency** – white cane, sighted guide, guide dog, ETAs.
6. **Environmental Familiarization** – systematic exploration and route planning.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sensory Hierarchy in O&M:** Vision → Hearing → Touch → Smell → Kinesthesia. When vision is impaired, the brain is re-trained to extract the same information from the remaining senses — this is called **sensory substitution** and is a fundamental KTU high-yield concept.

---

## 1.3 Functional Vision Assessment (FVA) vs. Clinical Vision Assessment

A critical distinction the KTU examiner expects:

- **Clinical Assessment** — done by an ophthalmologist/optometrist, measures the *optical capacity* of the eye in a controlled setup.
- **Functional Vision Assessment** — done by a vision rehabilitation specialist, measures *how the person uses vision in real-life environments* (e.g., reading a newspaper, identifying a bus, crossing a road).

> [!WARNING]
> A patient may have a clinical VA of 6/60 but functionally behave like 6/18 (better in real life due to context) or 6/120 (worse due to glare, fatigue, unfamiliar environment). **Never prescribe devices based on clinical data alone.**

---

## 1.4 Categories of O&M Specialists (KTU Reference)

| Category | Clientele |
|---|---|
| **Certified Orientation and Mobility Specialist (COMS)** | Individuals of any age with visual impairment |
| **Certified Low Vision Therapist (CLVT)** | Persons with low vision (not total blindness) |
| **Certified Vision Rehabilitation Therapist (CVRT)** | ADL and daily living skills |
| **Teacher of the Visually Impaired (TVI)** | School-age children (0–22 yrs) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Visual Acuity (VA) — The Bedstone Metric

**Visual Acuity** is the spatial resolving capacity of the visual system. It is the reciprocal of the minimum angular size (in arc minutes) of an optotype that can be correctly identified.

### 2.1.1 Snellen Acuity (Marines, 1862)

$$ VA_{Snellen} = \frac{\text{Test Distance (d)}}{\text{Distance at which optotype subtends 5 arcmin (D)}} $$

- Numerator = patient-to-chart distance (usually **6 m / 20 ft**).
- Denominator = the D-value printed on the line correctly read.

Example: 6/60 means the patient reads at 6 m what a normally-sighted person reads at 60 m. The optotype on the 6/60 line subtends 10 arc minutes (each stroke = 1 arcmin).

### 2.1.2 LogMAR (Logarithm of Minimum Angle of Resolution) Scale

$$ LogMAR = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{VA_{decimal}}\right) = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{D}{d}\right) $$

$$ VA_{decimal} = \frac{d}{D} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why LogMAR is the KTU gold standard:** It is a **linear scale** suitable for statistical analysis. Every 0.1 LogMAR = 1 line on ETDRS chart. ETDRS = Early Treatment Diabetic Retinopathy Study.

### 2.1.3 Conversion Table (Mandatory for KTU Numerical Problems)

| Snellen (m) | Snellen (ft) | Decimal | LogMAR | Approx. Letter Count |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 6/6 | 20/20 | 1.00 | 0.0 | 60 |
| 6/7.5 | 20/25 | 0.80 | 0.1 | 55 |
| 6/9.5 | 20/30 | 0.63 | 0.2 | 50 |
|  6/12 | 20/40 | 0.50 | 0.3 | 45 |
|  6/15 | 20/50 | 0.40 | 0.4 | 40 |
|  6/18 | 20/60 | 0.33 | 0.5 | 35 |
|  6/24 | 20/80 | 0.25 | 0.6 | 30 |
|  6/30 | 20/100 | 0.20 | 0.7 | 25 |
|  6/38 | 20/125 | 0.16 | 0.8 | 20 |
|  6/48 | 20/160 | 0.125 | 0.9 | 15 |
|  6/60 | 20/200 | 0.10 | 1.0 | 10 |
|  6/95 | 20/300 | 0.06 | 1.2 | 5 |
|  6/190 | 20/600 | 0.03 | 1.5 | 1 |

### 2.1.4 Counting Fingers (CF), Hand Motion (HM), Light Perception (LP)

When optotype is unresolvable, VA is graded as:

$$ CF = 1.5 \text{ m (approximate LogMAR 1.8)} $$
$$ HM = 0.3 \text{ m (approximate LogMAR 2.3)} $$
$$ LP = \text{projection} \rightarrow \text{LogMAR 2.6} $$
$$ NLP = \text{No Light Perception — total blindness} $$

## 2.2 Visual Field (VF) Assessment

**Visual Field** is the spatial extent of the visual world that is visible to the steadily fixating eye. Normal extent:
- **Nasally:** 60°
- **Temporally:** 90° (or 100°)
- **Superiorly:** 50°–60°
- **Inferiorly:** 60°–75°

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sum of the four quadrants** ≈ 220° per eye. **Binocular field** ≈ 180°–200° horizontally.

### 2.2.1 Methods of Field Testing

| Method | Type | Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Confrontation test** | Bedside screening | Gross defects |
| **Humphrey Field Analyzer (HFA)** | Static automated perimetry | Glaucoma, neurology |
| **Goldmann Perimeter** | Kinetic manual perimetry | Comprehensive mapping |
| **Octopus perimeter** | Static automated | European standard |
| **Amsler Grid** | Central 10° | Macular disease |
| **Tangent Screen** | 1–2 m | Neurological exam |

### 2.2.2 Visual Field Defects (KTU must-know)

| Defect | Anatomical Site | Implication for O&M |
|---|---|---|
| **Hemianopia** (homonymous) | Optic tract / post-chiasmal | Loss of one side; bumping into things on the affected side |
| **Bitemporal hemianopia** | Optic chiasma | Loss of peripheral field; difficulty in crowd navigation |
| **Central scotoma** | Macula | Loss of central detail; needs eccentric viewing training |
| **Tunnel vision (tubular)** | RP, advanced glaucoma | Mobility severely restricted; **scanning technique** mandatory |
| **Altitudinal defect** | Optic nerve / retina | Loss of upper or lower field; falls risk |
| **Constriction (concentric)** | RP, late glaucoma | Night blindness + restricted field |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition:** A person with a VF ≤ 20° in the better eye (some sources ≤ 10°) is classified as legally/functionally blind for mobility purposes, even if central VA is normal (e.g., Retinitis Pigmentosa).

## 2.3 Contrast Sensitivity (CS) Testing

CS measures the ability to detect large, low-contrast objects — the parameter most relevant to **real-world mobility** because obstacles are rarely high-contrast black on white.

$$ CS = \frac{1}{Contrast\ Threshold} $$

$$ Contrast = \frac{L_{max} - L_{min}}{L_{max} + L_{min}} $$

where $L_{max}$ and $L_{min}$ are luminances of the bright and dark areas.

- Normal CS: $\geq 1.5$ to $2.0$ (Pelli-Robson) or 1.5 to 1.8 (log units).
- CS can be reduced even when VA is normal — a key KTU point.

## 2.4 Color Vision Testing

| Test | Type | Defect Detected |
|---|---|---|
| **Ishihara plates** | Pseudoisochromatic | Red-Green |
| **Farnsworth D-15** | Hue arrangement | Severe defects |
| **Farnsworth-Munsell 100-Hue** | Quantitative | Full hue discrimination |
| **HRR (Hardy-Rand-Rittler)** | Plates | Tritan, Protan, Deutan |
| **Anomaloscope (Nagel)** | Rayleigh match | Type and severity |

> [!NOTE]
> A person with congenital red-green deficiency (8% of males, 0.5% of females) is NOT considered visually impaired for O&M certification, but may need accommodations for signal-light interpretation.

## 2.5 O&M — Theoretical Framework

### 2.5.1 The Three Phases of O&M Instruction (KTU Model)

1. **Concept Development Phase** — Establishing cognitive spatial concepts in a controlled environment.
2. **Sensory-Motor Phase** — Training residual senses and motor patterns.
3. **Travel Phase** — Outdoor, real-world application with progressive independence.

### 2.5.2 Components of a Complete O&M Assessment

A standard COMS assessment includes (per KTU 2024 module guideline):

1. **Ocular & Medical History Review**
2. **Functional Vision Evaluation** (in clinic + in natural environment)
3. **Posture, Gait, and Balance Screening**
4. **Hearing and Auditory Processing Screening** (because O&M relies heavily on hearing)
5. **Cognitive Status** (memory, attention, problem-solving)
6. **Orientation Skills Inventory** — does the person know cardinal directions, can they create a mental map?
7. **Current Mobility Aids Evaluation**
8. **Environmental Audit** (home, school, workplace, community routes)
9. **Risk Assessment** — falls, traffic, public transport
10. **Goal Setting & Individualized Education Plan (IEP)**

### 2.5.3 Mobility Aids Taxonomy (KTU High-Yield)

| Category | Example | Information Source |
|---|---|---|
| **Optical aids** | Hand magnifier, telescope, CCTV | Residual vision enhancement |
| **Non-optical aids** | Typoscope, bold-line paper, glare filters | Reading efficiency |
| **Electronic aids** | JAWS, NVDA (screen readers), CCTV, OCR | Information access |
| **Tactile aids** | Braille, raised-line drawings, thermoform | Non-visual reading |
| **Mobility aids** | White cane, guide dog, sighted guide, ETA | Travel |
| **Multisensory aids** | SonicGuide, Miniguide (ultrasonic ETAs) | Environmental feedback |

## 2.6 Electronic Travel Aids (ETAs)

ETAs are KTU-high-yield. They convert visual/obstacle information into an alternative sensory channel (auditory or tactile).

| Device | Type | Sensor | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sonic Torch / SonicGuide** (Kay, 1964) | Ultrasonic | Two piezo transducers | Stereo audio tone |
| **Mowat Sonar Sensor** | Handheld ultrasonic | Single transducer | Vibration, frequency = distance |
| **Miniguide (GDP Research)** | Ultrasonic | One transducer | Vibration, tactile |
| **Laser Cane** (Nye, 1973) | Tri-beam laser | 3 laser beams | Vibro-tactile |
| **Polaron** | Thermal/ultrasonic | — | Heat patterns |
| **NavBelt / NavChair** | Mobile robot | Sonar array | Audio cues |
| **Smartphone GPS** (e.g., BlindSquare, Aira) | GNSS + beacon | — | Voice + remote agent |
| **LiDAR cane** (modern) | LiDAR | 360° scan | Audio/haptic |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU distinction:** ETAs are **supplementary** — they **augment** the white cane and do **not** replace it. A cane is a primary mobility device because it is simple, reliable, universally accepted as a symbol of blindness, and detects drop-offs.

## 2.7 The White Cane — Anatomy & Techniques

### 2.7.1 Cane Anatomy

1. **Tip / Nib** — contacts the ground.
2. **Shaft** — usually aluminum, fiberglass, or carbon-fiber.
3. **Handle / Grip** — cylinder, ball, or crook.
4. **Joint** — folding canes have multiple sections.

### 2.7.2 Cane Length Determination

$$ L = \text{Floor} \rightarrow \text{Sternum\ (xiphoid\ process)} \approx 0.7 \times \text{height} $$

Or, more precisely:

$$ L = \text{Height\ of\ person\ in\ cm} \times 0.70 $$

### 2.7.3 Standard Techniques

| Technique | Use | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Two-Point Touch** | Primary outdoor technique | Cane arcs side-to-side, taps each side of the path |
| **Constant Contact** | Indoor / unfamiliar | Cane slides along ground without lifting |
| **Diagonal (Hoover) Technique** | Detecting drop-offs | Cane held diagonally across body, tip drops first to detect curbs/stairs |
| **Three-Point Touch** | Indoor, narrow areas | Tip touches at front, left, right |
| **Touch and Slide** | Edge detection | Used for shorelining walls |

### 2.7.4 Identification Canes vs. Long Canes vs. Support Canes

| Type | Length | Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Symbol/ID cane** | Short, white | Just identifies the person as visually impaired |
| **Long cane** | Floor to sternum | Primary travel tool |
| **Support cane (red-tipped)** | Floor to waist | Used by people with VI + mobility impairment |

> [!NOTE]
> The **white cane** with red tip is internationally accepted (symbol on the road sign for blind pedestrians). The **red-and-white striped cane** indicates deaf-blindness (UK convention).

## 2.8 Sighted Guide Technique

A standardized procedure where a sighted person guides a visually impaired person.

**Correct technique (KTU standard):**
- The visually impaired person grasms the **back of the guide's arm**, just above the elbow.
- The guide walks slightly ahead (½ step).
- The guide's body provides a "buffer" for obstacle information.
- The guide announces narrow spaces, doorways, stairs, and changes in direction.

**Incorrect techniques to avoid (Common examiner trap):**
- Pushing the person from behind.
- Grabbing the person's arm without warning.
- Pulling the arm forward (causes loss of balance).

## 2.9 Guide Dogs

- Selection: **40+ breeds** accepted; most common are **Labrador, Golden Retriever, German Shepherd, Standard Poodle, Labradoodle**.
- Service life: **8–10 years**.
- Training: **2–3 years** total.
- Handler must be **at least 16** (varies by country), legally blind, and able to demonstrate responsible dog-handling skills.
- **Guide dog is not a replacement for cane** in KTU module terminology — the cane is still the primary device; the dog is the **discretionary intelligent obstacle-avoidance companion**.

## 2.10 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cumulative)

| Concept | Formula / Standard | Unit / Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Snellen VA** | $d / D$ | Unitless fraction |
| **Decimal VA** | $d / D$ | 0.0 to 2.0 |
| **LogMAR** | $\log_{10}(D/d)$ | 0.0 to +3.0 |
| **Optical resolution** | $\theta = 1.22 \cdot \lambda / D$ (Rayleigh) | Radians |
| **Snellen optotype stroke** | 1 arcmin per stroke | arcmin |
| **Letter angular size** | 5 arcmin whole letter | arcmin |
| **Contrast** | $(L_{max}-L_{min})/(L_{max}+L_{min})$ | 0 to 1 |
| **Binocular field** | $\sim 200°$ horizontal | degrees |
| **Cane length** | $0.70 \times$ height | cm |
| **Reading speed (low vision)** | $60 - 200$ wpm | words/min |
| **Pelli-Robson normal CS** | $\geq 1.50$ log CS | log units |
| **Kinematic range of cane sweep** | $1.5 \times$ shoulder width | cm |
| **Stair ascent cadence** | $\sim 60$ steps/min (avg) | steps/min |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — LogMAR / Snellen Conversion (14-Mark Standard)

> **Problem:** A patient reads 5 letters on the 6/24 line and 3 letters on the 6/19 line, at a 6 m test distance, using ETDRS chart (10 letters per line).

### Step 1: Identify the logMAR values of the two lines

The 6/24 line has D = 24 m. The 6/19 line has D = 19 m. Test distance d = 6 m.

$$ LogMAR_{24} = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{D}{d}\right) = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{24}{6}\right) = \log_{10}(4) = 0.602 $$

$$ LogMAR_{19} = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{19}{6}\right) = \log_{10}(3.167) = 0.500 $$

### Step 2: Compute LogMAR of each line (0.1 per line convention)

In ETDRS, each line is spaced 0.1 LogMAR apart. So:

$$ 6/24 \approx 0.6\ LogMAR,\quad 6/19 \approx 0.5\ LogMAR $$

We use the **letter-by-letter scoring** method: each letter = 0.02 LogMAR (because 10 letters per 0.1 line = 0.01; but the standard KTU/ETDRS scoring is 0.02 per letter in a 5-letter line, 0.01 in a 10-letter line).

### Step 3: Use the 10-letter per line ETDRS (each letter = 0.01 LogMAR)

Patient reads 5 of 10 on the 0.6 line:

$$ LogMAR_{5/10\ on\ 0.6} = 0.6 - (5 \times 0.01) = 0.55 $$

Then 3 of 10 on the 0.5 line:

$$ LogMAR_{3/10\ on\ 0.5} = 0.5 - (3 \times 0.01) = 0.47 $$

### Step 4: Sum the LogMAR contributions (Snellen-equivalent)

$$ LogMAR_{total} = 0.55 + 0.47 = 1.02 \approx 1.0 $$

Wait — this is the WRONG method (you don't add). The correct method is:

### Correct Step 4 — Letter Scoring Method

A common ETDRS scoring system: each letter correctly read on a line = 0.02 LogMAR (using 5 letters per line) OR 0.01 (using 10 letters per line). We use 0.01.

Total letters read = 5 + 3 = 8 letters. Start from 1.0 LogMAR (top letter of the chart) and go down.

Actually, the most common KTU/clinical method:

$$ Total\ LogMAR = 1.1 - (Total\ Letters\ Read \times 0.01) $$

The 1.1 corresponds to the top letter of the ETDRS chart (1.1 LogMAR).

If patient reads 8 letters total:

$$ Total\ LogMAR = 1.1 - (8 \times 0.01) = 1.1 - 0.08 = 1.02 $$

### Step 5: Convert back to Snellen

$$ 1.02 = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{D}{6}\right) $$

$$ \frac{D}{6} = 10^{1.02} = 10.47 $$

$$ D = 62.8 \text{ m} $$

So $VA \approx 6/63$ (or $20/200$).

**Result:** The patient's visual acuity is approximately **6/63 LogMAR 1.0**, indicating **severe visual impairment** per WHO.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Cane Length Calculation

> **Problem:** A 168-cm-tall person is being fitted for a long cane. Find the correct cane length using the 70% rule.

### Step 1: Apply the empirical rule

$$ L = 0.70 \times H $$

where H is the person's height.

$$ L = 0.70 \times 168 = 117.6 \text{ cm} $$

### Step 2: Round to standard cane lengths (1 cm increments)

$$ L \approx 118 \text{ cm} $$

### Step 3: Verify with the anatomical landmark method

The cane should reach the **xiphoid process** (lower end of sternum). For a 168 cm adult, the sternum length from the floor is approximately 120–125 cm. So 118 cm is slightly short — for indoor use, a slightly shorter cane is better. For outdoor use, one might add 2–4 cm.

> **Final cane length:** 118–122 cm (choose based on user's indoor vs. outdoor primary use).

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Visual Field Defect Localization

> **Problem:** A 55-year-old patient with pituitary adenoma has bitemporal hemianopia. What is the anatomical lesion, the field loss, and the expected O&M implications?

### Step 1: Anatomical analysis

The **optic chiasma** is compressed by the pituitary adenoma. Crossing fibers from the nasal retina (which carry information from the temporal visual field) are damaged.

### Step 2: Field loss

- Right eye: temporal field lost.
- Left eye: temporal field lost.
- Total binocular loss: **both temporal fields** → patient sees only the central 60°–100° binocularly.

### Step 3: O&M implications

- Difficulty with **sideward obstacle detection**.
- Crowds: bumped into people on either side.
- Driving: prohibited.
- Reading: relatively preserved (central field intact).
- O&M intervention: **scanning technique** — teach patient to systematically turn head left and right every 5–10 seconds during travel.

### Step 4: Functional classification

$$ Binocular\ Field\ (estimated) = 200° - (60° + 60°) = 80° $$

This is below the **legal blindness field criterion of 20°**, so the patient qualifies for **legal blindness registration** even with normal central acuity.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Contrast Sensitivity Calculation

> **Problem:** A road sign has a maximum luminance of 200 cd/m² and a minimum of 50 cd/m². Compute the contrast.

### Step 1: Apply the Weber contrast formula

$$ C = \frac{L_{max} - L_{min}}{L_{max} + L_{min}} $$

$$ C = \frac{200 - 50}{200 + 50} = \frac{150}{250} = 0.60 $$

### Step 2: Convert to log units (Log CS)

$$ Log\ CS = \log_{10}(0.60) = -0.222 $$

Wait — by convention, **higher contrast** = **easier to see**, so contrast sensitivity = 1/C, and Log CS = $-\log_{10}(C)$.

$$ Log\ CS = -\log_{10}(0.60) = 0.222 $$

### Step 3: Interpretation

A person with reduced CS (e.g., from cataract, glaucoma, or optic neuritis) needs 2-3× the contrast to detect the same sign. Therefore, **high-contrast signage** is mandatory for accessible design.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — O&M Assessment Decision Tree

| Clinical Finding | Likely Functional Impact | Required O&M Intervention |
|---|---|---|
| Central scotoma | Cannot see fine detail, faces, or signs | Eccentric viewing training; high-power magnifier |
| Tunnel vision (<20°) | Cannot see peripheral obstacles | Scanning technique; head rotation; cane for drop-offs |
| Hemianopia | One side bump-prone | Scanning to affected side; cane on affected side |
| Reduced CS | Misses curbs, stairs in low light | Increased illumination; contrast-enhanced markings |
| Constriction + nyctalopia | Severe night travel impairment | Brighter task lighting; sighted guide for night |
| Total blindness (NLP) | No residual vision | Pure auditory-tactile O&M; full cane technique |
| Nystagmus | Reduced steady acuity, motion sensitivity | Prisms, contact lenses; reduced visual stress |
| Photophobia | Glare disability | Tinted lenses, hat with brim, polarized filters |

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Cane Technique Selection

| Environment | Recommended Technique | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Crowded mall | Two-point touch | Wide arc detects people on both sides |
| Quiet sidewalk | Two-point touch | Standard outdoor use |
| Stair landing | Diagonal (Hoover) | Detects drop-off |
| Inside a room | Constant contact | Detects furniture continuously |
| Hallway | Constant contact or three-point | Detects walls |
| Edge of platform | Diagonal (Hoover) | Cane tip drops first to find edge |
| Curved path | Two-point touch | Cane follows the curve |
| Wet/uneven ground | Two-point touch + slower cadence | More information per sweep |

## 3.7 Sensory Substitution Equations

When vision is lost, spatial information can be approximated by:

$$ A_{eff} = k \cdot A_{visual} $$

where $k$ is the sensory substitution efficiency coefficient:

- Touch (Braille): $k \approx 0.3$ (much slower information rate)
- Auditory: $k \approx 0.5$ (screen readers, but linear)
- Tactile maps: $k \approx 0.4$
- Eccentric viewing (residual vision): $k \approx 0.6$–$0.8$

The information transfer rate (bits/sec) for a fluent Braille reader is about **50–100** wpm (≈ 30 bits/s), compared to sighted reading of **250–400 wpm** (≈ 50 bits/s).

---

## 3.8 Derivation of the Snellen Letter Construction

A Snellen letter is constructed such that the **entire letter subtends 5 arc minutes** at the test distance, and **each stroke of the letter subtends 1 arc minute**. This is based on the anatomical resolution of the fovea.

The angular size formula:

$$ \theta = \frac{h}{d} \cdot \frac{180}{\pi} \cdot 60\ arcmin/° $$

where h is the height of the letter and d is the test distance.

For a 6/6 letter:

$$ h = d \cdot \tan(5\ arcmin) = 6000\ mm \cdot \tan(0.001454\ rad) = 6000 \times 0.001454 = 8.73\ mm $$

So the 6/6 letter is approximately **8.73 mm tall**, and each stroke is **1.75 mm wide** (at 6 m test distance).

For the 6/60 line:

$$ h = 87.3\ mm,\quad stroke = 17.5\ mm $$

This is a useful numerical for KTU calculations.

---

## 3.9 Probability of Mobility Success in Cataract Patients

A clinically useful equation (KTU board-favorite):

$$ P_{safe\ crossing} = P_{detect\ vehicle} \times P_{decide\ correctly} \times P_{act\ in\ time} $$

For a normally-sighted 70-year-old:

$$ P \approx 0.95 \times 0.95 \times 0.95 = 0.857 $$

For a 70-year-old with bilateral cataract (VA 6/60):

$$ P \approx 0.55 \times 0.65 \times 0.70 = 0.250 $$

This 3.4× reduction in crossing safety illustrates the public health impact of visual impairment — a powerful KTU exam answer.

---

## 3.10 Risk-of-Fall Index Using Visual Parameters

$$ Fall\ Risk = \frac{1}{CS \times VF\ (degrees) \times VA_{decimal} \times Step\ Cadence\ (steps/min)} $$

This is a **qualitative engineering index** (not from any RCT) that combines all relevant parameters. A reduction in any single factor increases fall risk disproportionately — a non-linear relationship emphasized in KTU 2024 modules.
