<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**Q1. Define the term "Presbycusis" and state the type of hearing loss it produces.**

> **Model Answer:**
> **Presbycusis** is the age-related, progressive, bilateral, symmetrical sensorineural hearing loss caused by the cumulative degeneration of the **cochlear hair cells** (particularly outer hair cells at the basal turn) and the **stria vascularis** within the inner ear. It is the most common form of hearing impairment in adults above 65 years of age.
> - **Type:** **Sensorineural** (cochlear, specifically).
> - **Audiometric signature:** High-frequency sloping loss with reduced speech discrimination out of proportion to the audiogram.
> - **Typical management:** Bilateral digital BTE hearing aids with directional microphones and WDRC.

---

**Q2. Differentiate between air conduction and bone conduction pathways in hearing aid fitting.**

> **Model Answer:**
> | Aspect | Air Conduction (AC) | Bone Conduction (BC) |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Pathway** | Sound travels via the ear canal → tympanic membrane → ossicles → cochlea | Sound vibrations delivered directly to the skull → cochlea |
> | **Device** | Conventional air-conduction hearing aid | Bone-anchored hearing aid (BAHA) or BC headset |
> | **Indication** | Mild–severe SNHL, conductive loss | Chronic otitis media, atresia, single-sided deafness |
> | **Coupling** | Earmold or dome in the canal | Percutaneous abutment or transcutaneous magnetic attachment |
> | **Comfort** | High (occlusion effect may occur) | Avoids canal blockage |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

> **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

### ⭐ Choice 1 — Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **signal processing chain of a digital hearing aid**. Explain the function of each block in detail. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> 1. **Microphone (Acoustic-to-Electric Transducer):** A miniature electret or MEMS microphone converts incoming acoustic pressure waves into a low-voltage analog signal. Typical bandwidth is 100 Hz – 8 kHz; sensitivity is around −44 dBV/Pa.
> 2. **Pre-Amplifier + Anti-Aliasing Filter:** A low-noise preamplifier raises the signal level to a usable voltage for the ADC. The anti-aliasing low-pass filter (Butterworth, 4th order, fc ≈ 8 kHz) restricts bandwidth to satisfy the Nyquist criterion.
> 3. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC):** A 16-bit sigma-delta (ΣΔ) ADC samples the bandlimited signal at fs = 32 kHz, providing a dynamic range > 96 dB.
> 4. **DSP Core (Heart of the Device):** This is the programmable processor (e.g., a low-power digital signal controller). It executes:
>    - Channel-splitting filter bank (e.g., 8–16 bands).
>    - Wide Dynamic Range Compression (WDRC).
>    - Digital noise reduction (spectral subtraction).
>    - Adaptive directional microphone processing.
>    - Feedback cancellation via adaptive FIR filtering.
> 5. **Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC) + Output Amplifier:** Reconstructs the processed waveform and drives the receiver at the prescribed gain.
> 6. **Receiver (Electric-to-Acoustic Transducer):** A balanced armature or dynamic micro-speaker delivers the amplified acoustic signal to the ear canal via a custom earmold or open-fit dome.
>
> *Block diagram (as shown in Section 4.1) carries 2 marks; functional explanation of each block carries 5 marks.*

**(b)** A patient presents with a **bilateral moderate sensorineural hearing loss** of **50 dB HL** (PTA). The audiologist prescribes a target aided threshold of **25 dB HL** at 1 kHz. Calculate the **functional gain** required and explain how a **WDRC algorithm** would handle the patient's reduced dynamic range. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> **Step 1 — Functional gain computation:**
>
> $$G_{func} = HTL_{unaided} - HTL_{aided} = 50 - 25 = 25\ dB$$
>
> **Step 2 — Identify the dynamic range problem:**
>
> A normal listener has UCL ≈ 100 dB HL and threshold ≈ 0 dB HL, giving a dynamic range of 100 dB. The patient's threshold is 50 dB HL but UCL remains near 100 dB HL (no recruitment in conductive loss; in SNHL, UCL may drop to 80 dB HL, so DR ≈ 30 dB).
>
> **Step 3 — WDRC solution:**
>
> Wide Dynamic Range Compression squeezes the wide input range (0–100 dB SPL) into the patient's narrow residual range (50–80 dB HL).
> - **Below threshold (input < 30 dB SPL):** Linear gain of 50 dB is applied (high gain for soft sounds).
> - **At conversational level (input 50–65 dB SPL):** Compression knee; the gain is reduced to 25 dB.
> - **Above comfort (input > 80 dB SPL):** Compression ratio of 3:1 or higher limits output to safe levels.
>
> **Step 4 — Compression ratio calculation:**
>
> $$CR = \frac{\Delta \text{Input}}{\Delta \text{Output}} = \frac{100 - 30}{80 - 50} = \frac{70}{30} \approx 2.33 : 1$$
>
> **Final Result:** The hearing aid must provide **25 dB functional gain** at 1 kHz and use WDRC with a **compression ratio of ≈ 2.3:1** to map conversational speech into the patient's residual auditory area.

---

### ⭐ Choice 2 — Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the **working principle, components, and coding strategies** of a **cochlear implant system**. Compare ACE and CIS strategies. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> **Working Principle:** A cochlear implant (CI) bypasses the damaged hair cells of the cochlea and directly stimulates the **spiral ganglion neurons** of the auditory nerve with patterned electrical pulse trains derived from acoustic analysis.
>
> **Components:**
>
> *External Parts:*
> 1. **Microphone** (head-level or in ear-hook): Picks up acoustic signal.
> 2. **Speech Processor** (body-worn or behind-the-ear): Performs band-pass filtering, envelope extraction, and strategy-specific mapping.
> 3. **Transmitter coil**: Sends power + data across the skin via **inductive radio-frequency coupling** (typically 5 MHz carrier).
>
> *Internal (Implanted) Parts:*
> 4. **Receiver-stimulator**: Demodulates the RF signal, decodes the digital frame, and generates biphasic current pulses.
> 5. **Electrode array**: A silastic carrier with 12–22 platinum-iridium electrode contacts inserted into the scala tympani, typically reaching a depth of 25–31 mm.
>
> **Coding Strategies:**
>
> | Feature | CIS (Continuous Interleaved Sampling) | ACE (Advanced Combination Encoder) |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Bands selected** | All (e.g., 22) | Maxima (e.g., 8–12 of 22) per cycle |
> | **Stim. rate** | High (per channel) | High overall, lower per channel |
> | **Power** | High | Lower (only active bands) |
> | **Speech in noise** | Moderate | Better (spectral peaking) |
> | **Channel interaction** | Reduced (non-simultaneous) | Reduced, focused |
> | **Processor load** | Higher | Lower |
> | **Used in** | Older Cochlear devices | Cochlear, modern devices |
>
> **CIS** scans channels sequentially (non-overlapping) with biphasic pulses → minimizes channel interaction.
> **ACE** selects the N highest-energy channels per frame → efficient and spectrally accurate.
>
> *(Working principle 2 marks, components 2 marks, strategy comparison 3 marks.)*

**(b)** A bilateral cochlear implant candidate has a **PTA of 95 dB HL** with **0% speech discrimination** even with a high-power BTE hearing aid. Justify the candidacy for cochlear implantation, mention the **pre-implant assessments** required, and explain the **frequency-to-place mapping** (tonotopic organization) of the electrode array. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> **Candidacy Justification:**
> - PTA of 95 dB HL falls in the **profound hearing loss** category.
> - **0% speech discrimination** indicates that even with maximum acoustic amplification, the auditory nerve is not receiving usable temporal-envelope cues → the patient is functionally deaf.
> - Conventional hearing aids cannot restore intelligibility because the cochlear transducers (hair cells) are non-functional.
> - Therefore, a **cochlear implant** is medically justified as it bypasses the cochlea and directly stimulates the auditory nerve.
>
> **Pre-Implant Assessment Battery:**
> 1. **Audiological:** PTA, aided audiogram, speech discrimination tests, tympanometry, ABR (Auditory Brainstem Response), OAE (Otoacoustic Emissions).
> 2. **Radiological:** High-resolution CT and MRI of temporal bones to confirm cochlear patency and exclude eighth nerve aplasia.
> 3. **Medical:** General anaesthesia fitness, vaccination (pneumococcal, meningitis), chronic-ear evaluation.
> 4. **Psychological/Cognitive:** Motivation, realistic expectations, family counselling.
> 5. **Speech-Language Baseline:** Pre-implant speech, language, and communication mode assessment.
>
> **Frequency-to-Place (Tonotopic) Mapping:**
> The basilar membrane exhibits a **tonotopic gradient**: the **base** resonates at high frequencies and the **apex** at low frequencies. The electrode array is designed to exploit this:
>
> | Electrode Location | Cochlear Region | Frequency Assigned |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Most basal** (E1) | Base of cochlea | **~8000 Hz** (high) |
> | **Middle** (E11) | Mid turn | **~1000 Hz** (mid) |
> | **Most apical** (E22) | Apex / helicotrema | **~100 Hz** (low) |
>
> The speech processor's filter bank inverts the microphone's frequency analysis and assigns each frequency band to the electrode whose position best matches the natural tonotopic place. This mimics the natural cochlear encoding, allowing the brain to interpret the resulting neural pattern as meaningful sound.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU Auditory Devices Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the reference 20 µPa** when computing dB SPL — examiners specifically allocate **½ mark** for writing the correct reference. Never write "dB" without a qualifier (SPL, HL, or SIL).
> 2. **Confusing insertion gain with functional gain.** Insertion gain = HA-in-ear minus unaided. Functional gain = aided HTL minus unaided HTL.
> 3. **Misclassifying conductive vs sensorineural loss** in the first paragraph of a 7-mark sub-question. The clinical classification drives the device selection — getting it wrong costs 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Writing "Cochlear implant directly stimulates the cochlea"** — this is **WRONG**. The CI stimulates the **auditory nerve** (spiral ganglion); the cochlear hair cells are bypassed because they are damaged.
> 5. **In compression-ratio questions**, students often invert the ratio. Always remember: $CR = \Delta \text{Input (dB)} / \Delta \text{Output (dB)}$.
> 6. **Skipping units** in numerical answers is a guaranteed **½–1 mark deduction** per sub-part.
> 7. **Forgetting the tonotopic map direction** — high frequencies are encoded at the **base**, low frequencies at the **apex** of the cochlea. Reversing this is a common error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (High-Density Summary)**

- [x] **Hearing loss types:** Conductive (mechanical), Sensorineural (cochlear/neural), Mixed.
- [x] **Standard reference pressure:** **20 µPa** for dB SPL.
- [x] **HTL categories:** Mild 26–40, Moderate 41–60, Severe 61–80, Profound >81 dB HL.
- [x] **Hearing aid styles (power order):** BTE > ITE > ITC > CIC > IIC.
- [x] **Signal chain:** Mic → Preamp → Anti-alias → ADC → DSP (WDRC) → DAC → Receiver.
- [x] **WDRC:** Maps wide input range into the patient's reduced dynamic range; solves the recruitment problem.
- [x] **Compression Ratio:** $CR = \Delta \text{In (dB)} / \Delta \text{Out (dB)}$; CR > 1 means compression.
- [x] **Insertion Gain:** $G_{ins} = G_{occluded} - G_{unoccluded}$.
- [x] **Functional Gain:** $G_{func} = HTL_{unaided} - HTL_{aided}$.
- [x] **Cochlear Implant bypasses the cochlea** and stimulates the **spiral ganglion / auditory nerve** directly.
- [x] **External parts of CI:** Microphone + Speech Processor + Transmitter Coil.
- [x] **Internal parts of CI:** Receiver-Stimulator + Electrode Array (12–22 electrodes).
- [x] **Tonotopic map:** **Base = High Frequency (~8 kHz), Apex = Low Frequency (~100 Hz)**.
- [x] **Coding strategies:** CIS (sequential, all channels) and ACE (max-selection, N channels per frame).
- [x] **Bone-Anchored Hearing Aid (BAHA):** Used for conductive loss, single-sided deafness, chronic otitis media.
- [x] **Middle Ear Implants (MEI):** Vibrant Soundbridge — electromechanical transducer coupled to ossicles.
- [x] **Auditory Brainstem Implant (ABI):** For neurofibromatosis type 2 patients without a functional auditory nerve.
- [x] **ALC / ALD systems:** Assistive Listening Devices — FM, infrared, induction loop (telecoil) for classrooms and public spaces.
- [x] **Battery life formula:** $t = C_{battery} / I_{draw}$ (in hours, mAh/mA).
- [x] **Nyquist:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$; HA ADC uses ~32 kHz sample rate.
- [x] **PTA formula:** Average of HTL at **0.5, 1, 2, 4 kHz** — the four-frequency clinical standard.
- [x] **Speech Intelligibility Index (SII):** Weighted sum of band audibilities, 0 to 1, predicts speech understanding.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Aids for Auditory Impairments | KTU 2024 Scheme**

topics.title: Face-to-Face Communication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT614 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Face-to-Face Communication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Face-to-Face (F2F) Communication Aids** are a specialized class of **Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC)** devices that enable, supplement, or replace natural speech and writing during direct interpersonal interaction between a user and a communication partner who are physically co-present in the same environment.

As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme** classification, F2F communication aids operate within the **Human–Device–Human (H–D–H) communication loop**, where the device functions as a real-time transducer between the user's expressive intent and the partner's sensory reception channel (auditory, visual, or tactile).

> [!NOTE]
> **AAC – Augmentative and Alternative Communication (ASHA Definition):**
> AAC refers to all forms of communication (other than oral speech) used to express thoughts, needs, wants, and ideas. It includes **unaided systems** (gestures, sign language) and **aided systems** (pictures, communication boards, speech-generating devices).

### 1.2 Target User Population

| Impairment Category | Communication Challenge | F2F Aid Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Dysarthria / Anarthria** (e.g., post-stroke, ALS) | Weak or absent speech motor control | Speech-generating device (SGD) |
| **Aphasia** (Broca's, Global) | Loss of language formulation | Picture/word boards, language apps |
| **Hearing impairment** (profound) | Cannot perceive partner's speech | Lip-reading AI, sign language, captioned speech |
| **Laryngectomy** | Removed vocal apparatus | Electrolarynx, TEP voice prosthesis |
| **Locked-in Syndrome** | Total paralysis except eye muscles | Eye-tracking AAC, BCI |
| **Cerebral Palsy** | Motor + speech impairment | Switch-scanning AAC, partner-assisted scanning |
| **Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD)** | Pragmatic & social-communication deficits | PECS, visual scene displays |
| **Intellectual Disability** | Limited expressive vocabulary | Symbol-based boards (Pictogram, Makaton) |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition Builder)

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Universal Remote Control" Analogy for AAC:**
> Imagine your mouth as a *remote control* that normally sends voice signals to a *TV* (the listener). When the remote breaks (e.g., due to stroke), you cannot change channels (communicate). A F2F communication aid acts as an **alternate remote** — you press buttons (select symbols) and the alternate remote produces the output (synthesized voice or text) that the TV (listener) can still receive. The original "remote" (natural speech) is augmented or replaced, but the channel (face-to-face interaction) remains intact.

### 1.4 Why This Topic Matters (KTU Outcome Alignment)

- **CO4 (Module 4 typical):** *Design and evaluate assistive solutions for communication and sensory rehabilitation.*
- F2F communication is the **highest-frequency, lowest-latency** form of human communication — assistive failures here cause the greatest social isolation and economic loss.
- The **WHO ICF framework** (International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health) identifies *Communication* as a core *Activity and Participation* domain, making F2F aids a public health priority.

### 1.5 Classification of F2F Communication Aids (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three-Tier Classification of F2F Aids (Beukelman & Mirenda, 2013 framework adopted by KTU):**
> 1. **Unaided AAC** – No external tool; relies on the body (gestures, manual signs, facial expression).
> 2. **Low-Tech Aided AAC** – No batteries/electronics; paper-based boards, picture cards, alphabet charts.
> 3. **High-Tech Aided AAC** – Electronic devices with synthesized/recorded speech, eye-tracking, AI-driven input.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Shannon–Weaver Communication Model Applied to F2F Aids

Every F2F communication aid can be mapped onto the classic **Shannon–Weaver (1948) model** of communication. Understanding this mapping is essential for design analysis and forms the backbone of Module 4 questions.

| Shannon Element | Natural Speech | F2F Communication Aid |
|---|---|---|
| **Information Source** | Speaker's intent | User's intent (via selection) |
| **Transmitter** | Larynx, tongue, lips | Selection mechanism (switch, touch, eye-tracker) |
| **Channel** | Air (acoustic) | Electrical signals, IR/EM spectrum, screen pixels |
| **Noise** | Ambient sound | EMG artifacts, glare, MOCAP errors |
| **Receiver** | Partner's ear/cochlea | Loudspeaker, display, haptic vibrator |
| **Destination** | Partner's language cortex | Partner's comprehension |

The **channel capacity** $C$ (bits/sec) achievable in an aided F2F system is given by:

$$
C = B \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)
$$

where:
- $B$ = bandwidth of the input modality (e.g., for an eye-tracker, $B \approx 30\text{–}120$ Hz sampling)
- $\dfrac{S}{N}$ = signal-to-noise ratio of the user's input signal (e.g., clean EOG vs. blink artifact)

### 2.2 Selection Methods in F2F Communication Aids

> [!NOTE]
> **Selection Method** = the procedure by which the user chooses a target symbol/message from the available vocabulary on the device.

The four canonical selection methods are:

1. **Direct Selection** – User points, touches, or dwells on the target. *Fastest* but requires fine motor control.
2. **Scanning** – Items are highlighted sequentially (row/column, linear, or circular); user activates a switch when the target is highlighted. *Slowest* but works with a single switch.
3. **Encoding** – Each item is assigned a code (e.g., Morse-like); user enters the code via one or two switches. *Intermediate speed, low motor demand*.
4. **Predictive / Linguistic Selection** – Word prediction, semantic clustering, frequency-based sorting. *Augments* any of the above.

The **theoretical maximum communication rate** (in words per minute, WPM) is bounded by:

$$
R_{\text{wpm}} = \dfrac{60}{T_s} \cdot \dfrac{1}{L_{\text{eff}}}
$$

where $T_s$ = average selection time per item (seconds) and $L_{\text{eff}}$ = effective code length (symbols/word, after prediction & linguistic compression).

### 2.3 Working Principles of Core F2F Sub-Systems

#### 2.3.1 Speech-Generating Devices (SGDs)

A SGD replaces or augments natural speech using one of three synthesis strategies:

| Synthesis Strategy | Principle | Quality | Computational Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Recorded (Diphone) Playback** | Concatenates pre-recorded human diphones | High naturalness | Low |
| **Formant Synthesis** (Klatt) | Models vocal tract via formant frequencies $F_1, F_2, F_3$ | Robotic but intelligible | Very low |
| **Concatenative Unit Selection** | Picks optimal units from a large corpus | Very high | High |
| **Neural TTS** (Tacotron, WaveNet) | Deep learning end-to-end | State-of-the-art naturalness | Very high |

The fundamental formant relation (source–filter model):

$$
F_n = \dfrac{(2n - 1) \cdot v}{4L}, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots
$$

where:
- $F_n$ = $n$-th formant frequency (Hz)
- $v$ = speed of sound in the vocal tract medium ($\approx 353$ m/s in air at 37 °C)
- $L$ = effective vocal tract length (m, $\approx 0.17$ m for adult males, $0.15$ m for adult females)

#### 2.3.2 Visual Speech Recognition (Lip Reading) Systems

Used to assist hearing-impaired users in F2F settings. The system pipeline:

1. **Face detection** (Viola–Jones / CNN) → locate mouth ROI.
2. **Lip localization & tracking** (Active Shape Model, dlib 68-point landmark, or MediaPipe).
3. **Feature extraction**:
   - Geometric: lip width $W(t)$, height $H(t)$, area $A(t)$, aspect ratio $\rho = W/H$.
   - Appearance-based: LBP, HOG, or CNN features.
4. **Classification** → phoneme or word recognition (HMM, LSTM, or Transformer).
5. **Output** → real-time captioning on screen, or vibration cue on a wearable.

The **geometric feature vector** at frame $t$ is:

$$
\mathbf{g}(t) = \left[ W(t),\; H(t),\; A(t),\; \rho(t),\; \theta(t) \right]^T
$$

where $\theta(t)$ is the lip-corner angle indicating mouth openness direction.

#### 2.3.3 Eye-Tracking Based AAC (ET-AAC)

Used for users with severe motor impairment (ALS, locked-in syndrome). The eye gaze vector is:

$$
\mathbf{g}_{\text{gaze}}(t) = \left[ \alpha(t),\; \beta(t) \right]
$$

where $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are pitch and yaw angles of the eye relative to the head frame, estimated from pupil-center and corneal-reflection (PCCR) geometry:

$$
\alpha = \arctan\!\left( \dfrac{x_p - x_c}{f} \right), \qquad
\beta = \arctan\!\left( \dfrac{y_p - y_c}{f} \right)
$$

- $(x_p, y_p)$ = pupil center in image plane
- $(x_c, y_c)$ = corneal reflection (glint) position
- $f$ = camera focal length (mm)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dwell-Time Selection Rule:**
> A target is *committed* when the gaze remains within a circular region of radius $r$ (typically $r \approx 1°$ visual angle) for a duration $T_d \geq 600\text{–}1000$ ms. This prevents accidental selections during saccades.

#### 2.3.4 Sign Language Recognition (SLR) / Translation Gloves

Two implementation paths:

- **Vision-based SLR:** RGB or depth camera captures hand shapes, then a CNN/LSTM classifies signs. Used in **F2F kiosks** and **educational apps**.
- **Sensor-based (Wearable) SLR:** Flex sensors on fingers + IMU (9-DoF) on the wrist → gesture classification.

The **flex sensor** converts finger bend to resistance:

$$
R(\phi) = R_0 \left( 1 + k \phi \right)
$$

where:
- $R_0$ = nominal resistance (typically $10\text{ k}\Omega$ at $\phi = 0°$)
- $k$ = bend sensitivity factor (per degree)
- $\phi$ = bend angle (degrees)

Voltage divider output:

$$
V_{\text{out}} = V_{\text{cc}} \cdot \dfrac{R(\phi)}{R(\phi) + R_{\text{ref}}}
$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comprehensive Formula Sheet – F2F Communication Aids (Module 4)**

| # | Equation | Meaning | Typical Units | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + S/N)$ | Shannon channel capacity | bits/s | All aided AAC systems |
| 2 | $R_{\text{wpm}} = \dfrac{60}{T_s \cdot L_{\text{eff}}}$ | Communication rate | words/min | Selection method analysis |
| 3 | $F_n = \dfrac{(2n-1)v}{4L}$ | Formant frequency (source-filter) | Hz | Speech synthesis |
| 4 | $\rho = W/H$ | Lip aspect ratio (openness) | dimensionless | Lip reading |
| 5 | $R(\phi) = R_0(1 + k\phi)$ | Flex sensor resistance vs. bend | $\Omega$ | Sign language gloves |
| 6 | $V_{\text{out}} = V_{cc} \cdot \dfrac{R(\phi)}{R(\phi) + R_{\text{ref}}}$ | Voltage divider output | V | Sensor conditioning |
| 7 | $\alpha = \arctan\!\left( \dfrac{x_p - x_c}{f} \right)$ | Gaze pitch angle | rad / deg | Eye-tracking AAC |
| 8 | $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left( \dfrac{P_s}{P_n} \right)$ | Signal-to-noise ratio (dB) | dB | Speech/audio input quality |
| 9 | $H = -\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Shannon entropy of vocabulary | bits/symbol | AAC vocabulary design |
| 10 | $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$ | Nyquist sampling rate (for audio) | Hz | Speech digitization (typically $f_s = 16$ kHz) |

> **Units & Constants Quick Reference:** $v_{\text{sound, body}} \approx 353$ m/s; $L_{\text{tract}} \approx 0.17$ m; standard speech $f_s = 16$ kHz; bandwidth for telephony = 300–3400 Hz; bandwidth for wideband speech = 50–7000 Hz.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of F2F Aids |
|---|---|
| **Hospitals / ICUs** | Ventilator/tracheostomy patients use ET-AAC to communicate with nurses within seconds. |
| **Rehabilitation Clinics** | Stroke survivors regain communication via AAC apps (Proloquo2Go, TouchChat). |
| **Education** | Classrooms for deaf children use real-time captioning & sign language avatars. |
| **Public Services** | Banks, airports deploy video remote interpreting (VRI) kiosks — a high-tech F2F aid. |
| **Smart Home / IoT** | Voice-first AAC integrated with Alexa/Google Assistant for ambient control. |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Communication Rate of a Row–Column Scanning AAC System

> **Problem Setup:** A user with severe motor impairment (single switch, e.g., a sip-and-puff switch) needs to select one symbol from a grid of size $r \times c$ (rows × columns) on a communication board. The system scans row by row; the user presses the switch once to select a row, then again to select a column. The user can produce an average of $P$ presses per second (limited by fatigue). What is the maximum communication rate in words per minute, if the user must select $L$ symbols to spell a word on average?

**Step 1 — Time per switch press:**
The user requires an average time $T_p = 1/P$ seconds per press (including physical reaction + motor delay).

**Step 2 — Total presses to select one symbol from an $r \times c$ grid:**
The system first scans all $r$ rows sequentially; the user presses when the desired row is highlighted (one press). Then the system scans the $c$ columns of that row; the user presses again to commit (second press). 
Therefore:

$$
N_{\text{presses/symbol}} = 2
$$

**Step 3 — Time to select one symbol:**

$$
T_s = N_{\text{presses/symbol}} \times T_p = \dfrac{2}{P}
$$

**Step 4 — Time to spell a word (assuming $L$ symbols/word, no prediction):**

$$
T_{\text{word}} = L \cdot T_s = \dfrac{2L}{P}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to words per minute:**

$$
R_{\text{wpm}} = \dfrac{60}{T_{\text{word}}} = \dfrac{60 P}{2 L} = \dfrac{30 P}{L}
$$

**Step 6 — Numerical example:**

If the user can press the switch at $P = 0.5$ presses/s (1 press every 2 s, typical for fatigued users) and uses a 6-letter word average ($L = 6$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{wpm}} &= \dfrac{30 \times 0.5}{6} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{15}{6} \\[4pt]
&= 2.5 \text{ WPM}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** This is **2.5 words per minute** — about 100× slower than natural speech (~150 WPM). This explains why *prediction* and *word-completion* features are critical design requirements in any F2F aid for motor-impaired users.

**Step 7 — Improvement with linguistic prediction (effective length $L_{\text{eff}} < L$):**
With a 50% keystroke savings predictor, $L_{\text{eff}} = 3$, giving:

$$
R_{\text{wpm}} = \dfrac{30 \times 0.5}{3} = 5 \text{ WPM} \quad \text{(a 100% increase)}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Gaze Angle from Pupil–Glint Vector (ET-AAC Calibration)

> **Problem Setup:** A low-cost webcam-based eye tracker has focal length $f = 6$ mm. After image processing, the pupil center is detected at image coordinates $(x_p, y_p) = (320, 240)$ pixels, and the corneal glint (infrared reflection) is at $(x_c, y_c) = (310, 238)$ pixels, with pixel pitch $p = 10\ \mu\text{m/pixel}$. Compute the gaze pitch and yaw angles.

**Step 1 — Convert pixel offsets to millimeters:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta x &= (x_p - x_c) \cdot p = (320 - 310) \times 10 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} \\
&= 10 \times 10^{-5} = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.1 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta y &= (y_p - y_c) \cdot p = (240 - 238) \times 10 \times 10^{-6} \\
&= 2 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} = 0.02 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute gaze angles using PCCR model:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha &= \arctan\!\left( \dfrac{\Delta x}{f} \right) = \arctan\!\left( \dfrac{0.1}{6} \right) \\[4pt]
&= \arctan(0.01667) \approx 0.9549° \;\; (\text{horizontal yaw})
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta &= \arctan\!\left( \dfrac{\Delta y}{f} \right) = \arctan\!\left( \dfrac{0.02}{6} \right) \\[4pt]
&= \arctan(0.00333) \approx 0.1910° \;\; (\text{vertical pitch})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**
The user is gazing approximately **1° right and 0.2° down** from the camera optical axis — corresponding to a screen region slightly right-of-center. The ET-AAC system would highlight the on-screen key at the corresponding $\alpha, \beta$ location.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Sign Convention:** In screen coordinates, the X-axis typically increases to the *right* and Y-axis *down* in image space. A positive $\Delta x$ (pupil to the right of glint) means the user is looking to the *left* (because the eye rotates to bring the pupil toward the visual target, opposite to the camera's perspective). Always verify sign convention in the device datasheet.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: F2F Aid Selection Methods

| Parameter | Direct Selection (Touch) | Switch Scanning (Row-Col) | Encoding (Morse) | Eye-Tracking (Dwell) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Motor Demand** | High (full arm/hand) | Very Low (1 switch) | Low (1–2 switches) | Very Low (eye) |
| **Speed (WPM, typical)** | 30–60 | 2–10 | 8–15 | 10–30 |
| **Cognitive Load** | Low | High (must wait for target) | High (must know code) | Medium |
| **Fatigue** | Medium | High (repetitive presses) | Medium | High (eye strain) |
| **Accuracy** | High | High | Error-prone | Midas-touch problem |
| **Cost (USD)** | $50–$2000 | $50–$500 | $100–$800 | $200–$10000+ |
| **Best For** | CP (mild), autism, aphasia | ALS, high SCI, locked-in | Experienced users, deaf-blind | ALS, late-stage, locked-in |
| **Example Device** | iPad + Proloquo2Go | LightWriter, BIGmack | TASH Swift | Tobii Dynavox, EyeGaze |

### 3.4 Derivation: Vocabulary Design Using Shannon Entropy

> **Problem Setup:** A speech-generating device offers a 1000-symbol core vocabulary for a child with autism. Frequency analysis shows that 20 symbols account for 80% of daily communication (Pareto / Zipf-like distribution). The remaining 980 symbols follow a long tail. Design a *frequency-weighted* keyboard layout that minimizes average selection time.

**Step 1 — Compute entropy of the uniform distribution (no weighting):**

$$
H_{\text{uniform}} = \log_2(1000) \approx 9.97 \text{ bits/symbol}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Zipf's law approximation:**
For a vocabulary of $N$ words with ranks $1, 2, \ldots, N$, the probability of the $i$-th most frequent word is:

$$
p_i = \dfrac{1/i^s}{\displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^{N} 1/j^s}
$$

For $s = 1$ (typical for English), the partition function approximates $\sum_{j=1}^{N} 1/j \approx \ln N + \gamma$ (Euler–Mascheroni constant $\gamma \approx 0.5772$).

**Step 3 — Compute entropy of the Zipf distribution:**

$$
H_{\text{Zipf}} = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i \log_2 p_i
$$

For $N = 1000$ and $s = 1$, $H_{\text{Zipf}} \approx 7.05$ bits/symbol — a **30% reduction** in information per selection, which directly speeds up communication.

**Step 4 — Engineering implication:**
Place the top-20 most frequent symbols in the **first position of row 1, columns 1–4** (lowest selection time, top-left of the grid for right-handed users). This is the principle behind **Minspeak**, **Unity**, and **PECS** layouts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design takeaway for KTU:** A F2F aid that uses *frequency-optimized* layout can effectively double the user's WPM without any change in hardware — purely by intelligent software design.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Generic F2F Communication Aid

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the complete signal/data flow of a high-tech F2F communication aid (e.g., a tablet-based AAC app with optional eye-tracking or switch input).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Intent Source] --> B{Input Modality Selector}
    B -->|Touch| C1[Capacitive Touch Panel]
    B -->|Switch| C2[Mechanical Switch Input]
    B -->|Gaze| C3[Eye Tracker Module]
    B -->|Brain Signal| C4[EEG Headset BCI]
    C1 --> D[Input Decoder Firmware]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    D --> E[Symbol / Letter Selection Logic]
    E --> F[Language Prediction Engine]
    F --> G[Message Formulation Buffer]
    G --> H{Output Modality Selector}
    H -->|Audible| I1[Text To Speech Synthesizer]
    H -->|Visual| I2[Display Screen Text]
    H -->|Tactile| I3[Haptic Vibrator Array]
    H -->|Printed| I4[Thermal Printer]
    I1 --> J[Communication Partner]
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
    I4 --> J
    K[Vocabulary Database] --> E
    L[Word Prediction Library] --> F
    M[User Profile and Settings] --> D
    M --> E
    N[Power Management BMS] --> A
    N --> D
    N --> I1
    N --> I2
```

**Block-Level Functional Walkthrough (Reading the Diagram):**

1. **`A → B`**: The user's expressive intent (the *information source*) enters the system. The `Input Modality Selector` adapts the device to the user's residual abilities.
2. **`C1–C4 → D`**: The chosen input is digitized and decoded into discrete selection events.
3. **`D → E`**: Each selection event is mapped to a symbol, letter, or word from the **`K` (Vocabulary Database)**.
4. **`E → F`**: The **`F` (Language Prediction Engine)**, fed by the **`L` (Word Prediction Library)**, refines the selection in real time.
5. **`F → G`**: The message is assembled in the **`G` (Message Formulation Buffer)**.
6. **`G → H`**: The output modality is chosen based on the partner's needs (audible for sighted partners in quiet rooms, visual for noisy rooms or deaf partners, tactile for deaf-blind partners, printed for written record).
7. **`H → I1–I4`**: The output is rendered through the appropriate transducer.
8. **`I1–I4 → J`**: The communication partner receives the message, closing the F2F interaction loop.
9. **`M` and `N`**: User customization (voice type, vocabulary set) and power management run as cross-cutting concerns.

### 4.2 Architecture of a Lip-Reading / Visual Speech Recognition Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Camera RGB or IR] --> A2[Face Detection CNN]
    A2 --> A3[Mouth ROI Extraction]
    A3 --> A4[Lip Landmark 68 Points]
    A4 --> A5[Geometric Feature Vector]
    A4 --> A6[Appearance CNN Features]
    A5 --> A7[Feature Fusion Layer]
    A6 --> A7
    A7 --> A8[Temporal Model LSTM or Transformer]
    A8 --> A9[Phoneme Decoder CTC]
    A9 --> A10[Word Output Display Caption]
    A10 --> A11[User Hearing Aid Visual]
```

### 4.3 Decision Flow: Choosing the Right F2F Aid (Clinician's Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Patient Assessment]) --> Q1{Can patient speak intelligibly?}
    Q1 -->|Yes, partially| P1[Low Tech Picture Board]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Can patient use hands reliably?}
    Q2 -->|Yes, with effort| P2[Touch Screen SGD iPad App]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Can patient move head eyes?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| P3[Eye Tracker AAC Tobii]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Can patient blink or sip puff?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| P4[Single Switch Scanning AAC]
    Q4 -->|No| P5[BCI EEG based AAC Research Only]
    P1 --> End([F2F Aid Deployed])
    P2 --> End
    P3 --> End
    P4 --> End
    P5 --> End
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Decision Diagram:** This is a clinical triage algorithm. In KTU examinations, a similar flowchart may be asked under *Design* or *Case Study* type questions. Always justify the choice with the user's **motor, cognitive, linguistic, and sensory** profile.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
**Define Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC). List the four main categories of aided AAC devices with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 mark):** AAC is a set of tools, strategies, and technologies that supplement or replace natural speech and writing for individuals with communication impairments. (ASHA definition adapted.)

> **Four Categories (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
> 1. **Manual Communication Boards / Picture Cards** – e.g., a printed alphabet chart.
> 2. **Electronic Single-Message Devices** – e.g., BIGmack (records & plays one message).
> 3. **Speech-Generating Devices (SGDs)** – e.g., Tobii Dynavox, iPad with Proloquo2Go.
> 4. **Eye-Tracking or BCI-Based AAC** – e.g., Tobii Eye Tracker, Emotiv EPOC X BCI.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
**Explain the difference between *Direct Selection* and *Scanning* methods in F2F communication aids. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Direct Selection | Scanning |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | User points/touches/dwells on target directly. | System highlights items sequentially; user activates switch. |
| **Speed** | Fast (15–60 WPM) | Slow (2–10 WPM) |
| **Advantage** | High speed, intuitive, low cognitive load. | Works for users with severe motor impairment (single switch). |
| **Disadvantage** | Requires precise motor control. | Time-consuming, high fatigue, requires good attention/timing. |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks – Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT** confuse **AAC** (Augmentative & Alternative Communication) with **ALS** (Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis) — both abbreviated similarly. Spell out the first use.
> - **Do NOT** omit the **shannon channel capacity** equation when asked to "analyse" a F2F system — examiners specifically look for the link between input modality, bandwidth, and SNR.
> - **Do NOT** write vague answers like "AAC helps disabled people" — always specify *which impairment*, *which device class*, and *which engineering principle*.
> - **Do NOT** skip the formula for communication rate; even if not asked numerically, showing the rate equation earns methodology marks.
> - **Diagrams are mandatory** for 14-mark questions — at least one labelled block diagram earns 1–2 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Design & Analysis Oriented*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme, Module 4]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the major components of a **Speech-Generating Device (SGD)** used in Face-to-Face communication. Explain the working of its **text-to-speech synthesis** subsystem. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 35-year-old post-stroke patient with severe dysarthria can only move her right index finger. Design a F2F communication aid for her, justifying your choice of input modality, selection method, and output. Estimate her **maximum communication rate** if she can perform 1 selection every 2 seconds and her average word length is 5 characters. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram and Working (7 marks):**

A Speech-Generating Device (SGD) consists of the following blocks (refer to Section 4.1 block diagram):

1. **Input Interface** – Touchscreen / switch / keyboard / eye-tracker (1 mark)
2. **Vocabulary Database** – Stores pre-organized symbol–speech mappings (Minspeak, Unity, PODD) (1 mark)
3. **Message Buffer** – Temporary RAM area where selected words are accumulated (0.5 mark)
4. **Language Processing Engine** – Performs word prediction, grammar correction, abbreviation expansion (0.5 mark)
5. **Text-to-Speech (TTS) Synthesizer** – Converts text buffer into acoustic output (1.5 marks)
6. **Output Transducer** – Loudspeaker, headphone jack, or Bluetooth streaming (0.5 mark)
7. **Power & User Interface** – Battery, settings, volume, voice selection (0.5 mark)
8. **Connectivity** – Wi-Fi for remote updates, caregiver apps, cloud TTS (0.5 mark)

**Working of TTS subsystem (1.5 marks for explanation):**

- **Stage 1 – Text Normalization:** Expands abbreviations ("Dr." → "Doctor"), numbers, dates.
- **Stage 2 – Linguistic Analysis:** Assigns phonemes, stress, and intonation using a pronunciation dictionary (CMUdict) and prosody rules.
- **Stage 3 – Acoustic Synthesis:** Modern systems use **neural TTS** (Tacotron 2 + WaveNet) or **concatenative unit selection** to produce the audio waveform. For formant-based TTS, the source–filter model generates formants $F_1, F_2, F_3$ per Equation 3 of the Formula Sheet.
- **Stage 4 – Output:** Digital-to-analog conversion → amplifier → loudspeaker.

**(b) F2F Aid Design and Communication Rate (7 marks):**

**Impairment analysis:** Severe dysarthria, retained right index-finger movement → motor capability is *limited but functional*. This rules out BCI and eye-tracking (over-engineered, expensive, slow to set up). Single-switch scanning is also suboptimal because her *hand* is still functional. (1 mark)

**Recommended design (4 marks):**

| Subsystem | Choice | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **Input Modality** | Single-finger **capacitive touch** on a tablet | Uses her residual fine motor control; no headset or eye-tracker needed |
| **Selection Method** | **Direct Selection** with on-screen **QWERTY or symbol grid** | Maximum speed (~30 WPM) with one finger |
| **Layout** | **Frequency-optimized** (top-50 words in row 1) | Boosts effective WPM via prediction |
| **Output** | **Neural TTS** (clear female voice matching her age) with **on-screen text** | Sighted partners; natural-sounding voice reduces stigma |
| **Device** | iPad / Android tablet with **Proloquo4Text** or **TouchChat** app | Low cost, high availability, caregiver-friendly |
| **Accessories** | Ruggedized case, stylus with rubber tip, wheelchair mount | Daily-use durability; mounts to her powered wheelchair |

**Maximum Communication Rate Calculation (2 marks):**

Given: $T_s = 2$ s/selection, $L = 5$ symbols/word.

$$
R_{\text{wpm}} = \dfrac{60}{T_s \cdot L} = \dfrac{60}{2 \times 5} = 6 \text{ WPM}
$$

If word prediction gives 50% keystroke savings ($L_{\text{eff}} = 2.5$):

$$
R_{\text{wpm, predicted}} = \dfrac{60}{2 \times 2.5} = 12 \text{ WPM}
$$

This is **sufficient for basic conversational needs** (greetings, requests, short answers) and can be supplemented with **pre-stored phrases** ("I need water", "Call the nurse") for one-tap speed.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice (Comparative & Application Oriented)*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme, Module 4]**

**(a)** Compare **Eye-Tracking AAC**, **Switch-Scanning AAC**, and **BCI-based AAC** as F2F communication aids. Your answer must include: working principle, typical user population, speed, cost, and one limitation of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A deaf patient visits a busy hospital reception. Design a **F2F communication kiosk** that allows the patient to communicate face-to-face with the receptionist. Specify hardware, software, and accessibility features. Justify each design choice. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Eye-Tracking AAC | Switch-Scanning AAC | BCI-based AAC |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Working Principle** | Infrared camera tracks pupil & corneal reflection (PCCR) to determine gaze vector; dwell or blink triggers selection. | Items highlighted sequentially on screen; user presses a single switch when target is reached. | EEG electrodes detect P300 event-related potentials or motor imagery; classifier picks target. |
| **Typical User** | Late-stage ALS, locked-in syndrome, high SCI | Severe CP, advanced MS, early ALS | Total locked-in, advanced neurodegenerative disease |
| **Speed (WPM)** | 10–30 WPM | 2–10 WPM | 2–8 WPM |
| **Cost (USD)** | $5,000 – $15,000 | $200 – $2,000 | $10,000 – $50,000+ (research) |
| **Limitation** | "Midas touch" problem (unintended selections); requires good vision; expensive | Slow, fatigue-inducing, high cognitive load | Requires gel electrodes, controlled environment, low accuracy, long calibration |
| **Example Device** | Tobii Dynavox TD-I-13 | Origin Instruments Hitch | g.tec intendiX, Neurable |

**Justification note (1 mark):** Eye-tracking is the *clinical gold standard* for motor-impaired AAC; switch-scanning is the *lowest-cost, most universally compatible*; BCI is the *last resort* when all motor channels are lost.

**(b) F2F Hospital Reception Kiosk Design (7 marks):**

**Scenario constraints:** Deaf patient → must use **visual** (not auditory) output; reception is busy → kiosk must be **fast and standalone**; patient unfamiliar with device → must be **intuitive**.

**Recommended Design:**

| Subsystem | Specification | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **Display** | 21" multi-touch capacitive screen, anti-glare, ≥250 nits | Large enough for two people to read simultaneously from opposite sides; touch is universal |
| **Input** | Touch keyboard with **sign language avatar** (e.g., SigningAvatar or SiSi) | User types; avatar signs in real time to the receptionist who may not know sign language |
| **Output to Receptionist** | Large clear text + speech synthesis (so hearing receptionist can also hear if needed) | Dual-modality output ensures message comprehension |
| **Input from Receptionist** | Speech-recognition microphone that converts receptionist's voice to **live on-screen captions** | Closes the loop for the deaf patient |
| **Connectivity** | Wi-Fi, VPN to hospital EMR for patient lookup | Reduces repetitive typing of name, ID |
| **Camera** | Front-facing HD camera for **Video Remote Interpreting (VRI)** to a human sign interpreter on call | Handles complex/emotional conversations |
| **Accessibility** | Height-adjustable mount, wheelchair-accessible, induction loop for hearing-aid users, multilingual | Universal Design (UD) compliance |
| **Power & Housing** | Rugged antimicrobial enclosure, UPS backup | Hospital hygiene & 24/7 operation |
| **Software** | Android/iOS-based with custom AAC app (e.g., CoughDrop, Proloquo2Go) | Mature AAC ecosystem |

**Justification (1 mark):** This design uses the **dual-channel redundancy** principle (text + speech + sign) to maximize communication robustness in a noisy public setting, satisfying **Section 508** and **WCAG 2.1** accessibility standards.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **⚡ Rapid Revision Checklist — Face-to-Face Communication Aids (Module 4)**

- [ ] **F2F aids** belong to the broader class of **Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC)** devices.
- [ ] Three classes: **Unaided** (sign, gesture), **Low-tech aided** (boards, cards), **High-tech aided** (SGDs, eye-trackers, BCI).
- [ ] **Impairment → Aid mapping:** Dysarthria → SGD; Aphasia → Picture boards; Hearing loss → Captioning/Lip-reading; Locked-in → Eye-tracker/BCI.
- [ ] **Shannon channel capacity** $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ governs the upper limit of any AAC data rate.
- [ ] **Communication rate** $R_{\text{wpm}} = \dfrac{60}{T_s \cdot L_{\text{eff}}}$ — always *increase* by reducing $T_s$ (faster input) or $L_{\text{eff}}$ (better prediction).
- [ ] **Selection methods:** Direct, Scanning, Encoding, Predictive — choice depends on motor & cognitive ability.
- [ ] **Eye-tracking PCCR model** uses pupil + glint offset; **dwell time** $\approx$ 600–1000 ms prevents Midas-touch.
- [ ] **Sign language gloves** use **flex sensors** $R(\phi) = R_0(1 + k\phi)$ plus **IMU** for hand orientation.
- [ ] **TTS strategies:** Recorded, Formant (Klatt), Concatenative, Neural (Tacotron/WaveNet) — trade off naturalness vs. compute.
- [ ] **Formant relation:** $F_n = (2n-1)v/(4L)$; $L \approx 0.17$ m for adult male tract.
- [ ] **Nyquist rule** for speech digitization: $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$ → typical $f_s = 16$ kHz for speech.
- [ ] **Shannon entropy** of vocabulary guides *frequency-optimized* layouts (Minspeak, Unity).
- [ ] **Universal Design** principles: multi-modal output (text + audio + haptic), wheelchair-accessible, multilingual.
- [ ] **Clinical triage order:** Hand → Eye → Head → Single switch → BCI (from least to most severe motor impairment).
- [ ] **KTU exam hot keywords:** *AAC, SGD, dysarthria, scanning, dwell time, formant, PCCR, BCI, PECS, Minspeak, Midas-touch, ICF framework, Universal Design.*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Alerting Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT614 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Alerting Devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Alerting Devices** are a specialized class of **assistive medical technology** designed to convert ambient environmental auditory signals (sounds, alarms, and notifications) into alternative sensory modalities — typically **visual (light)**, **tactile (vibration)**, or **enhanced auditory** outputs — so that individuals with **hearing impairment, deafness, or auditory processing disorders** can perceive critical safety, communication, and daily-living information that they would otherwise miss.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **OEBRT614 (Module 4)**, alerting devices constitute a primary sub-category within the broader classification of **Sensory Substitution Devices (SSDs)**, sitting alongside *signaling devices* and *communication aids* in the assistive technology taxonomy.

### 1.2 Classification of Alerting Devices (KTU Framework)

| Category | Primary Output Modality | Target User Group |
|---|---|---|
| **Visual Alerting Devices** | Flashing light / Strobe | Deaf, Hard-of-Hearing |
| **Tactile Alerting Devices** | Vibration / Mechanical motion | Deaf-Blind, Deeply deaf |
| **Auditory Alerting Devices** | Amplified / Low-frequency sound | Mild-moderate hearing loss |
| **Multimodal Alerting Devices** | Combined light + vibration + sound | Universal design users |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Translator Phone" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you are in a foreign country where you cannot understand the spoken language. An **alerting device is essentially a "universal translator"** for your ears. Just as a translator app converts spoken words into written text on your phone screen, an alerting device converts an incoming *doorbell ring* (auditory) into a *flashing light* (visual) or a *pillow vibration* (tactile). The information content is identical — only the delivery channel is changed to match what the user can perceive.

**Key Design Principle — Sensory Substitution:** When one sensory channel is impaired, the brain is remarkably capable of *rewiring* itself to interpret information delivered through a *different* channel. Alerting devices exploit this **neuroplasticity** to maintain the user's situational awareness and safety.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Specifications

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Core engineering parameters (per KTU 2024 syllabus and IEC 60118 / IEC 60601 assistive-device standards):
> - **Sound Detection Threshold:** typically **30 dB SPL** to **120 dB SPL**
> - **Visual Flash Frequency:** **1 Hz to 5 Hz** (regulated to avoid photosensitive epilepsy risk at > 3 Hz)
> - **Tactile Vibration Frequency:** **150 Hz to 300 Hz** (optimal mechanoreceptor sensitivity)
> - **Wireless Operating Frequency:** **2.4 GHz, 433 MHz, 315 MHz** (ISM bands)
> - **Effective Range (wireless systems):** **30 m to 200 m** (open field)
> - **Battery Life:** **24 hours to 7 days** depending on duty cycle

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Theory — The Three-Stage Processing Pipeline

Every alerting device, regardless of physical form, follows a universal **three-stage signal-processing architecture**:

### Stage 1: Acoustic Sensing (Input Transduction)
- A **microphone** (electret condenser or MEMS type) captures ambient sound pressure waves.
- The microphone converts acoustic energy (P, in Pascal) into an electrical voltage signal (V, in Volts).
- Sensitivity is rated in **dB SPL** (Sound Pressure Level relative to 20 µPa reference).

### Stage 2: Signal Classification & Decision (Processing)
- The analog signal is amplified, filtered (typically a **band-pass filter** centered on the target alert frequency), and digitized via an **ADC**.
- A microcontroller or DSP chip runs a **pattern-recognition algorithm** to distinguish between target alerts (doorbell, smoke alarm, baby cry) and ambient noise.
- **Decision logic:** If the detected signal's spectral signature, duration, and amplitude match a pre-trained template → trigger the output stage.

### Stage 3: Multi-Modal Output (Alert Generation)
- A **visual driver circuit** activates high-intensity LEDs or xenon strobes.
- A **tactile driver circuit** energizes an **eccentric rotating mass (ERM)** motor or a **linear resonant actuator (LRA)**.
- An **auditory driver circuit** may amplify and pitch-shift the signal into the user's residual hearing range.

## 2.2 Underlying Engineering Principles

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" behind the Design:**
> 1. **Why visual?** Light has a 100% detection rate in sleeping individuals when flash intensity exceeds **75 candela** at eye level.
> 2. **Why tactile?** Vibration bypasses the auditory cortex entirely, delivering information directly to **Pacinian corpuscles** in the skin.
> 3. **Why multimodal?** Redundant signaling ensures the alert is perceived even if one channel is occluded (e.g., lights off → vibration works; user asleep → vibration works).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every formula, conversion, and engineering relationship required for KTU 2024 Scheme examination questions on Alerting Devices.

| # | Formula / Relationship | Meaning of Variables | Standard Units | Real-World Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P}{P_0}\right)$ | $L_p$ = Sound Pressure Level, $P$ = measured pressure, $P_0 = 20\,\mu\text{Pa}$ | dB SPL | Converting microphone voltage to perceived loudness |
| 2 | $S = \dfrac{V_{out}}{P_{in}}$ | $S$ = microphone sensitivity | V/Pa | Specifying microphone performance |
| 3 | $f_{flash} = \dfrac{1}{T_{period}}$ | $f_{flash}$ = visual flash rate, $T_{period}$ = time between flashes | Hz | Setting strobe frequency to avoid seizure risk |
| 4 | $a_{tactile} = \omega^{2} \cdot r$ | $a$ = peak acceleration of ERM, $\omega$ = angular velocity, $r$ = offset radius | m/s² | Sizing vibration motor for perceptible alert |
| 5 | $P_{LED} = V_f \cdot I_f$ | $P_{LED}$ = power dissipation, $V_f$ = forward voltage, $I_f$ = forward current | W | Designing LED driver circuit |
| 6 | $R_{range} = \dfrac{c}{4\pi f}$ | $R_{range}$ ≈ free-space path-loss reference, $c$ = speed of light, $f$ = frequency | m | Estimating wireless transmitter range |
| 7 | $SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | SNR = signal-to-noise ratio | dB | Setting detection threshold in DSP |
| 8 | $T_{latency} = T_{sense} + T_{process} + T_{output}$ | Total alert delay budget | seconds (typically < 1 s) | Real-time alert performance metric |
| 9 | $\eta_{alert} = \dfrac{N_{perceived}}{N_{emitted}} \times 100\%$ | Alert effectiveness ratio | % | Clinical validation metric |
| 10 | $V_{battery} = \dfrac{E_{total}}{I_{avg} \cdot t_{runtime}}$ | Sizing battery for desired runtime | V | Power-budget engineering |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Alerting devices are deployed across **five primary domains**:
1. **Home safety** — smoke, CO, and gas-leak detection with strobe + bed-shaker outputs
2. **Healthcare facilities** — patient call systems, infusion pump alarms
3. **Workplace accessibility** — fire alarm compliance (ADA / RPwD Act 2016)
4. **Public infrastructure** — airport, railway, and emergency-vehicle alerting
5. **Personal communication** — baby monitors, doorbell notification, phone call flashing

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Sound Pressure Level (SPL) from Microphone Output

> **Problem:** A MEMS microphone outputs $V_{out} = 0.215\,\text{V}$ when exposed to an ambient doorbell sound. The microphone sensitivity rating is $S = -38\,\text{dBV/Pa}$. Calculate the SPL of the doorbell and verify whether it exceeds the alerting-device trigger threshold of **65 dB SPL**.

### Step 1 — Convert sensitivity from dBV/Pa to linear V/Pa
$$
S_{linear} = 10^{\,S_{dB}/20} = 10^{-38/20}
$$

$$
S_{linear} = 10^{-1.9} = 0.01259\,\text{V/Pa}
$$

*Logic:* The decibel-to-linear conversion for voltage quantities uses the factor **20 log₁₀** (not 10), because decibels are defined on a power-proportional scale, and voltage squared is proportional to power across a fixed impedance.

### Step 2 — Solve for the acoustic pressure $P$
$$
P = \dfrac{V_{out}}{S_{linear}} = \dfrac{0.215}{0.01259}
$$

$$
P = 17.08\,\text{Pa}
$$

*Logic:* Rearranging $S = V_{out} / P$ gives $P = V_{out} / S$. The pressure is approximately **17 Pa**, which is a moderately loud sound (atmospheric pressure is ~100,000 Pa for scale).

### Step 3 — Compute Sound Pressure Level $L_p$ relative to reference $P_0 = 20\,\mu\text{Pa}$
$$
L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P}{P_0}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{17.08}{20 \times 10^{-6}}\right)
$$

$$
L_p = 20 \log_{10}(854{,}000)
$$

$$
L_p = 20 \times 5.9314 = 118.63\,\text{dB SPL}
$$

*Logic:* This doorbell is extremely loud (~118 dB SPL, comparable to a rock concert), which is far above the trigger threshold.

### Step 4 — Threshold verification
$$
L_p = 118.63\,\text{dB SPL} \;\; > \;\; L_{threshold} = 65\,\text{dB SPL}
$$

*Conclusion:* The detection algorithm **WILL trigger** the alerting device. The alert is initiated with a margin of **53.63 dB**, providing robust headroom against background noise.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Battery Sizing for a Wearable Alerting Bracelet

> **Problem:** A wearable tactile alerting bracelet has the following duty cycle: idle current $I_{idle} = 5\,\text{mA}$, alerting current $I_{alert} = 80\,\text{mA}$, with 5 alerts per day, each lasting 10 seconds. The required runtime is **$t_{runtime} = 7$ days**. Size the required battery capacity.

### Step 1 — Calculate daily alert time
$$
t_{alert/day} = 5 \times 10\,\text{s} = 50\,\text{s/day}
$$

*Logic:* Total seconds the device actively vibrates per 24-hour period.

### Step 2 — Calculate daily alert-mode charge consumption
$$
Q_{alert/day} = I_{alert} \times t_{alert/day} = 0.080\,\text{A} \times 50\,\text{s}
$$

$$
Q_{alert/day} = 4.0\,\text{A·s} = \dfrac{4.0}{3600}\,\text{Ah} = 1.111\,\text{mAh}
$$

*Logic:* The alert-mode contribution is negligible (1.1 mAh/day) because the active duration is so short.

### Step 3 — Calculate daily idle-mode charge consumption
$$
t_{idle/day} = 24 \times 3600 - 50 = 86{,}350\,\text{s}
$$

$$
Q_{idle/day} = 0.005\,\text{A} \times 86{,}350\,\text{s} = 431.75\,\text{A·s}
$$

$$
Q_{idle/day} = \dfrac{431.75}{3600} = 0.1199\,\text{Ah} = 119.9\,\text{mAh}
$$

*Logic:* The idle current dominates the energy budget, accounting for > 99% of daily consumption.

### Step 4 — Total required capacity over 7 days
$$
Q_{total} = 7 \times (Q_{idle/day} + Q_{alert/day})
$$

$$
Q_{total} = 7 \times (119.9 + 1.111) = 7 \times 121.01 = 847.1\,\text{mAh}
$$

### Step 5 — Apply derating factor (safety margin of 25%)
$$
Q_{design} = Q_{total} \times 1.25 = 847.1 \times 1.25 = 1058.9\,\text{mAh}
$$

*Logic:* Real batteries lose capacity with temperature, age, and discharge rate, so a 25% margin is standard engineering practice.

### Step 6 — Select standard battery size
$$
\boxed{Q_{battery} = 1100\,\text{mAh} \;\; (\text{standard 3.7 V Li-Po cell})}
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: Choosing Alert Modality

| User Condition | Recommended Modality | Engineering Rationale | Example Product |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Deaf, sighted, mobile** | Visual (strobe) + Smartphone push | High conspicuity, social acceptance | Bellman Visit flash receiver |
| **Deaf, sighted, sleeping** | Tactile (bed-shaker) + Strobe | Reaches user in deep sleep | Serenity Sweet Dreams alarm clock |
| **Deaf-blind** | Tactile only (vibration) | Vision channel unavailable | Haptic wristband (neonatal ICU) |
| **Hard-of-hearing, elderly** | Enhanced low-frequency auditory + Visual | Leverages residual hearing (presbycusis) | Amplified phone ringer (95 dB) |
| **Hospital patient** | Multimodal (light + tone + tactile) | Safety-critical redundancy | ICU nurse-call pillow speaker |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Generic Alerting Device

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the **end-to-end signal flow** of a representative wireless alerting device system, from acoustic event capture to multi-modal user perception.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Acoustic Event Source: Doorbell or Smoke Alarm]
    A2[Microphone: MEMS or Electret Transducer]
    A3[Preamplifier: 40 dB Gain Stage]
    A4[Band-Pass Filter: 500 Hz to 4 kHz]
    A5[ADC: 16-bit, 44.1 kS/s]
    A6[Microcontroller DSP: Pattern Recognition Algorithm]
    D1[Decision: Signal Matches Alert Template]
    O1[Visual Output Driver: High-Intensity LED Array 75 cd]
    O2[Tactile Output Driver: ERM Motor 175 Hz, 1.5 g]
    O3[Auditory Output Driver: Amplified Speaker 95 dB SPL]
    R1[User Receives Alert: Light or Vibration or Sound]
    W1[Wireless Link: 2.4 GHz Transmitter]
    W2[Wireless Receiver at Pager or Wristband]

    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> A3
    A3 --> A4
    A4 --> A5
    A5 --> A6
    A6 --> D1
    D1 -->|Trigger Visual| O1
    D1 -->|Trigger Tactile| O2
    D1 -->|Trigger Auditory| O3
    O1 --> R1
    O2 --> R1
    O3 --> R1
    A6 --> W1
    W1 --> W2

```

    S5 --> S9
